topics.title: Properties of Fluid - Density
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties of Fluid - Density

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Density (ρ – rho)** is defined as the mass of a fluid per unit volume. It is a fundamental intrinsic property of a fluid that quantifies how tightly the matter (mass) is packed into a given space. Mathematically, it is the ratio of mass ($m$) to volume ($V$).

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V}$$

The SI unit of density is **kg/m³** (kilogram per cubic metre), and in the CGS system, it is expressed in **g/cm³** or **g/cc**. Density is a **scalar quantity** and a **point function** in fluid mechanics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Density is the foundational property upon which *all other fluid properties* (specific weight, specific gravity, specific volume, kinematic viscosity) are mathematically derived. Examiners frequently link this topic to problems on manometry, buoyancy, and fluid statics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding **two identical-sized boxes** — one filled with **steel balls** and the other filled with **cotton balls**. Both boxes have the *same volume* (say, 1 m³), but the box of steel will be **massively heavier** because steel is *denser* than cotton. Density, therefore, tells you **"how much stuff is squeezed into a given space."**

In fluid terms:
- **Mercury** is a very dense liquid (≈ 13,600 kg/m³) — that is why it is used in **barometers** and **manometers**; a small column can balance atmospheric pressure.
- **Air** at sea level is sparse (≈ 1.225 kg/m³) — a huge volume is needed to obtain a meaningful mass.
- **Water** at 4°C has the benchmark density of **1000 kg/m³** — this serves as the **reference standard** for computing **specific gravity**.

### 1.3 Related Fluid Properties (Family of Density-Derived Quantities)

| Property | Symbol | Definition | SI Unit |
|----------|--------|------------|---------|
| Density | $\rho$ | Mass per unit volume | kg/m³ |
| Specific Weight | $\gamma$ | Weight per unit volume | N/m³ |
| Specific Gravity | $S$ or $SG$ | Ratio of density of fluid to density of water | Dimensionless |
| Specific Volume | $v$ | Volume per unit mass | m³/kg |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Remember:**
> - Density of **water** at **4°C**: $\rho_{w} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - Density of **mercury** at 20°C: $\rho_{Hg} \approx 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Density of **air** at STP: $\rho_{air} \approx 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structured Logical Breakdown of Density Concepts

**Step 1 — Primary Definition:**
Mass per unit volume. The denser the fluid, the more mass is contained in a unit cube of that fluid.

**Step 2 — Variation of Density:**
- For **liquids**, density is practically **incompressible** — it remains nearly constant with moderate pressure changes.
- For **gases**, density varies **significantly** with both pressure and temperature (governed by the Ideal Gas Law).
- Density of water reaches a **maximum at 4°C** (an anomalous property — a key KTU 2-mark question!).

**Step 3 — Why Density Matters in Engineering:**
- Determines the **weight** of a fluid column (used in manometer design).
- Governs **buoyancy forces** (Archimedes' Principle).
- Affects **Reynolds Number** (via kinematic viscosity $\nu = \mu/\rho$).
- Controls **inertia** of moving fluid (momentum in jets, turbines).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Derived Property

- **Specific Weight ($\gamma$):** Why introduce weight instead of mass? Because in *fluid statics* problems, we deal with **forces** (which involve weight). $\gamma = \rho g$ converts mass-density into a **force-based** density.
- **Specific Gravity ($S$):** Why dimensionless? Because it is a **ratio** — it gives a quick mental shortcut. "Oil has SG = 0.8" instantly tells you oil is **lighter than water** without writing units.
- **Specific Volume ($v$):** Used predominantly in **thermodynamics** and **gas dynamics** where volume per unit mass is more convenient (e.g., steam tables).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | Fundamental definition of density | kg/m³ |
| 2 | $\gamma = \rho \cdot g$ | Specific weight from density | N/m³ |
| 3 | $S = \dfrac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Specific gravity (dimensionless) | — |
| 4 | $v = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | Specific volume (reciprocal of density) | m³/kg |
| 5 | $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | Reference density of water at 4°C | kg/m³ |
| 6 | $\gamma_{water} = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$ | Specific weight of water | N/m³ |
| 7 | $\rho = \dfrac{\gamma}{g}$ | Density from specific weight | kg/m³ |
| 8 | $\gamma = S \cdot \gamma_{water}$ | Specific weight using specific gravity | N/m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Civil Engineering:** Designing water supply systems, dam pressure calculations.
> - **Mechanical Engineering:** Sizing hydraulic turbines (impulse/reaction), selecting pump fluids.
> - **Aerospace:** Calculating lift and drag, where **air density** at altitude is critical.
> - **Chemical Engineering:** Distillation column design, where fluid densities govern separation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between Specific Weight and Density

**Starting Point:** Specific weight ($\gamma$) is defined as weight per unit volume.

$$\gamma = \frac{W}{V}$$

**Step 1 — Express Weight in terms of Mass:**
By Newton's Second Law, weight $W$ is the gravitational force acting on a mass $m$:

$$W = m \cdot g$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Weight into the Specific Weight Equation:**
Replace $W$ in the numerator with $m \cdot g$:

$$\gamma = \frac{m \cdot g}{V}$$

**Step 3 — Re-arrange to Recognise Density:**
Group $m/V$ together. By definition, $m/V$ is exactly density $\rho$:

$$\gamma = \left(\frac{m}{V}\right) \cdot g$$

**Step 4 — Final Compact Form:**

$$\boxed{\gamma = \rho \cdot g}$$

This is the **most fundamental relation** in fluid statics, frequently quoted in KTU examinations.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship Between Specific Gravity and Density

**Starting Point:** By definition, specific gravity is the ratio of the density of a given fluid to the density of pure water at 4°C.

$$S = \frac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water,\,4°C}}$$

**Step 1 — Cross-Multiply to Solve for Fluid Density:**

$$\rho_{fluid} = S \cdot \rho_{water}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Standard Value of Water Density (1000 kg/m³):**

$$\boxed{\rho_{fluid} = 1000 \cdot S \quad \text{(in kg/m}^3\text{)}}$$

**Interpretation:** If you are told "an oil has a specific gravity of 0.85", you can immediately compute its density as $\rho_{oil} = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$ — no further data needed.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Specific Weight Using Specific Gravity

**Starting Point:** Combine $\gamma = \rho g$ with $\rho = S \cdot \rho_{w}$:

**Step 1 — Substitute Density Expression:**

$$\gamma_{fluid} = (S \cdot \rho_{water}) \cdot g$$

**Step 2 — Recognise $\rho_{water} \cdot g$ as the Specific Weight of Water ($\gamma_{w} = 9810$ N/m³):**

$$\boxed{\gamma_{fluid} = S \cdot \gamma_{water}}$$

**Numerical Example (Worked):**
For kerosene with $S = 0.78$:

$$\gamma_{kerosene} = 0.78 \times 9810 = 7651.8 \text{ N/m}^3$$

Correspondingly:

$$\rho_{kerosene} = 0.78 \times 1000 = 780 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Specific Volume as the Reciprocal of Density

**Starting Point:** Specific volume is volume per unit mass.

$$v = \frac{V}{m}$$

**Step 1 — Invert the Density Formula:**
Since $\rho = m/V$, taking the reciprocal:

$$v = \frac{1}{\rho}$$

**Step 2 — Final Form:**

$$\boxed{v = \frac{1}{\rho}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** When asked *"A liquid of density 850 kg/m³ occupies a tank of 2 m³. Find its (i) mass, (ii) specific weight, (iii) specific volume, (iv) specific gravity."* — this is a **classic 7-mark Part B sub-question**. Always show units *throughout* the calculation.

### 3.5 Numerical Problem — Density to Mass Conversion

**Problem:** A cylindrical tank of diameter 1.5 m and height 3 m is completely filled with oil of specific gravity 0.86. Calculate: (a) the mass of oil, (b) the specific weight of oil.

**Given:**
- Diameter $D = 1.5$ m → Radius $r = 0.75$ m
- Height $h = 3$ m
- Specific gravity $S = 0.86$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Solution:**

**Step (a) — Mass of Oil:**

$$V = \pi r^2 h = \pi \times (0.75)^2 \times 3$$

$$V = \pi \times 0.5625 \times 3 = \pi \times 1.6875$$

$$V = 5.3014 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\rho_{oil} = S \times 1000 = 0.86 \times 1000 = 860 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$m = \rho \times V = 860 \times 5.3014 = 4559.2 \text{ kg}$$

**Step (b) — Specific Weight of Oil:**

$$\gamma_{oil} = \rho \cdot g = 860 \times 9.81 = 8436.6 \text{ N/m}^3$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Relationship Map — The "Density Family" of Fluid Properties

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates how density ($\rho$) acts as the **central hub** from which all other density-derived fluid properties are computed. This is the mental model that KTU examiners expect students to internalise.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mass m kg] --> C[Density rho = m / V]
    B[Volume V m3] --> C
    C --> D[Specific Weight gamma = rho x g]
    C --> E[Specific Volume nu = 1 / rho]
    C --> F[Specific Gravity S = rho / rho_water]
    G[Reference: rho_water = 1000 kg per m3] --> F
    H[Reference: gamma_water = 9810 N per m3] --> D
    D --> I[Engineering Use: Manometry, Hydrostatic Pressure]
    F --> J[Engineering Use: Quick fluid identification]
    E --> K[Engineering Use: Thermodynamics, Gas dynamics]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Density in a Real Hydraulic System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input["INPUT PARAMETERS"]
        P1[Mass m]
        P2[Volume V]
        P3[Temperature T]
        P4[Specific Gravity S]
    end

    subgraph Processing["CALCULATION ENGINE"]
        R1[rho = m / V]
        R2[gamma = rho x g]
        R3[rho_fluid = S x 1000]
    end

    subgraph Output["ENGINEERING OUTPUTS"]
        O1[Mass of fluid in tank]
        O2[Hydrostatic force on submerged body]
        O3[Buoyancy force via Archimedes]
        O4[Weight of fluid column in manometer]
    end

    P1 --> R1
    P2 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    P4 --> R3
    R3 --> R2
    R1 --> O1
    R2 --> O2
    R2 --> O3
    R2 --> O4
    P3 -.-> R1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Notice the *two parallel paths* to compute specific weight — (1) directly from measured mass-volume, or (2) indirectly from specific gravity. In KTU exam problems, the second path is **faster and preferred** when specific gravity is given.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the following terms: (i) Density, (ii) Specific Weight, (iii) Specific Gravity. State their SI units.

**Model Answer:**
**(i) Density ($\rho$):** It is defined as the mass per unit volume of a fluid.
$$\rho = \frac{m}{V} \quad \text{with SI unit: kg/m}^3$$
**(ii) Specific Weight ($\gamma$):** It is the weight per unit volume of a fluid.
$$\gamma = \rho g \quad \text{with SI unit: N/m}^3$$
**(iii) Specific Gravity ($S$):** It is the ratio of the density of a given fluid to the density of pure water at 4°C.
$$S = \frac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water}} \quad \text{— Dimensionless (no units)}$$

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
State the numerical value of the density and specific weight of water at 4°C. Why is water taken as the reference fluid for specific gravity?

**Model Answer:**
- Density of water at 4°C: $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Specific weight of water at 4°C: $\gamma_w = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$
- **Reason for water as reference:** Water is the most abundant, easily available, and chemically stable liquid on Earth. Its density at 4°C is a fixed, reproducible constant (1000 kg/m³ exactly), making it an ideal universal standard. Additionally, water has its **maximum density at 4°C**, ensuring a stable benchmark regardless of minor temperature variations in laboratory conditions.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** A liquid of specific gravity 0.85 occupies a volume of 0.6 m³. Find its (i) mass, (ii) weight, (iii) specific weight, and (iv) specific volume. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A vessel contains 500 kg of a liquid. If the volume of the liquid is 0.625 m³, calculate the density, specific weight, and specific gravity of the liquid. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Given:** $S = 0.85$, $V = 0.6 \text{ m}^3$

**(i) Mass of the liquid:**

$$\rho = S \times 1000 = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$m = \rho \times V = 850 \times 0.6 = \boxed{510 \text{ kg}}$$

**(ii) Weight of the liquid:**

$$W = m \times g = 510 \times 9.81 = \boxed{5003.1 \text{ N}}$$

**(iii) Specific weight:**

$$\gamma = \rho \times g = 850 \times 9.81 = \boxed{8338.5 \text{ N/m}^3}$$

**(iv) Specific volume:**

$$v = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{1}{850} = \boxed{1.176 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}}$$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $m = 500 \text{ kg}$, $V = 0.625 \text{ m}^3$

**Density:**

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{500}{0.625} = \boxed{800 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

**Specific weight:**

$$\gamma = \rho \times g = 800 \times 9.81 = \boxed{7848 \text{ N/m}^3}$$

**Specific gravity:**

$$S = \frac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water}} = \frac{800}{1000} = \boxed{0.8 \text{ (dimensionless)}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the difference between density and specific weight of a fluid. A fluid has a specific gravity of 1.2. Find its specific weight in N/m³ and density in kg/m³. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two fluids, A and B, have densities 1200 kg/m³ and 850 kg/m³ respectively, at 20°C. Determine: (i) the specific gravity of each fluid, (ii) the specific weight of each fluid, and (iii) which fluid would float on the other when poured in the same container. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Difference (with sketch):**
- **Density ($\rho$):** Mass per unit volume. Depends only on the fluid's composition and state.
- **Specific Weight ($\gamma$):** Weight per unit volume. Depends on both fluid composition *and* local gravity.

A simple schematic:
- Cube of side 1 m → Volume = 1 m³
- Mass = $\rho$ kg → Weight = $\rho g$ N
- Density points to *how much matter is inside*; specific weight points to *how much the Earth pulls on that matter*.

**Numerical:**
$$S = 1.2 \Rightarrow \rho = 1.2 \times 1000 = \boxed{1200 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

$$\gamma = \rho g = 1200 \times 9.81 = \boxed{11772 \text{ N/m}^3}$$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Specific gravity:**

$$S_A = \frac{1200}{1000} = 1.2, \quad S_B = \frac{850}{1000} = 0.85$$

**(ii) Specific weight:**

$$\gamma_A = 1200 \times 9.81 = 11772 \text{ N/m}^3$$

$$\gamma_B = 850 \times 9.81 = 8338.5 \text{ N/m}^3$$

**(iii) Floatation rule:** The fluid with **lower density floats** on the fluid with **higher density**. Since $\rho_B (850) < \rho_A (1200)$, **fluid B floats on fluid A**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — Leaving density in g/cm³ instead of converting to kg/m³ (multiply by 1000).
> 2. **Using $g = 9.8$ instead of $g = 9.81$** — KTU officially uses **9.81 m/s²**; using 9.8 may be penalised in strict valuation.
> 3. **Adding units to Specific Gravity** — SG is *dimensionless*. Writing "SG = 0.85 kg/m³" is a **mark-deduction error**.
> 4. **Confusing $\rho$ and $\gamma$** — Density uses $m$ (mass); specific weight uses $W$ (weight). Mixing them is a common 2-mark loss.
> 5. **Skipping the final answer box / underline** — KTU scripts value presentation; a clearly boxed final answer earns full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Density ($\rho$):** Mass per unit volume — SI unit: **kg/m³** — Formula: $\rho = m/V$.
- **Specific Weight ($\gamma$):** Weight per unit volume — SI unit: **N/m³** — Formula: $\gamma = \rho g$.
- **Specific Gravity ($S$):** Ratio, **dimensionless** — Formula: $S = \rho_{fluid} / 1000$.
- **Specific Volume ($v$):** Reciprocal of density — SI unit: **m³/kg** — Formula: $v = 1/\rho$.
- **Water Reference Values (MUST MEMORISE):** $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\gamma_w = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.
- Water has **maximum density at 4°C** — a frequently asked 2-mark KTU question.
- **Conversion shortcut:** SG × 1000 = density in kg/m³; SG × 9810 = specific weight in N/m³.
- **Floatation rule (Archimedes link):** Lower density fluid **floats** on higher density fluid.
- Density is a **point property** in fluid mechanics — it can vary in compressible flow.
- **Unit conversion memory hook:** $1 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (numerically identical, just SI re-scaling).
- Density of mercury (~13,600 kg/m³) is what makes **barometers** practical with short columns.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specific Weight
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Specific Weight

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Specific Weight (γ):** The specific weight of a fluid is defined as the **weight per unit volume** of the fluid. It is a measure of the heaviness of a fluid and is also commonly referred to as the **weight density** of the fluid.

Mathematically, it is expressed as the ratio of the weight ($W$) of a fluid to its volume ($V$):

$$\gamma = \frac{W}{V}$$

Since weight $W = m \cdot g$ and mass $m = \rho \cdot V$, the specific weight can also be expressed in terms of density and acceleration due to gravity.

### 1.2 SI Units and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The **standard SI unit** of specific weight is **Newton per cubic meter (N/m³)**. In the FPS (Foot-Pound-Second) system, it is expressed as **pounds per cubic foot (lb/ft³)**. The **standard value of acceleration due to gravity** used in KTU numerical problems is **g = 9.81 m/s²**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are carrying two identical buckets — one filled with **water** and the other with **honey**. Although both buckets occupy the same volume, the bucket of honey feels significantly heavier when you lift it. Why? Because the **particles of honey are packed more tightly per unit volume**, meaning each cubic meter of honey contains more mass and therefore more weight than the same cubic meter of water.

This intuitive difference in "heaviness per unit volume" is exactly what **specific weight** quantifies. It is the reason why heavy fluids (like mercury) sink below lighter fluids (like oil) in a layered container.

### 1.4 Physical Significance in Fluid Statics

> [!NOTE]
> Specific weight is a **direct indicator of the gravitational body force** acting on a fluid element. In KTU 2024 Module 1 problems, specific weight is the cornerstone for deriving **hydrostatic pressure**, **manometer equations**, and **total pressure on submerged surfaces**. Without specific weight, you cannot evaluate pressure variation with depth in static fluids.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Core Relationship Chain

The specific weight is fundamentally derived from three primary physical quantities:

- **Mass (m):** The amount of matter contained in a fluid body, measured in **kilograms (kg)**.
- **Volume (V):** The three-dimensional space occupied by the fluid, measured in **cubic meters (m³)**.
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** The constant gravitational pull acting on the fluid mass, equal to **9.81 m/s²** at sea level on Earth.

The operational logic flows as follows:

1. The **density ($\rho$)** is determined by dividing the mass of the fluid by its volume ($\rho = m/V$).
2. The **weight (W)** is determined by multiplying the mass by gravitational acceleration ($W = m \cdot g$).
3. The **specific weight ($\gamma$)** is the weight per unit volume, so we divide the weight by the volume.
4. Combining these relationships, we arrive at the master equation $\gamma = \rho \cdot g$.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Specific Weight

In fluid mechanics, we frequently deal with **pressure heads** and **pressure variations** in static fluid columns. These pressure variations are not dependent on mass — they are dependent on the **gravitational pull on the fluid mass per unit volume**, which is precisely the specific weight. Hence, $\gamma$ is the most natural property to use in **hydrostatic pressure equations** of the form $P = \gamma \cdot h$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula sheet** for Specific Weight in KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) questions.

| **Formula** | **Description** | **SI Unit** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\gamma = \dfrac{W}{V}$ | Specific weight as weight per unit volume | $\text{N/m}^3$ |
| $\gamma = \rho \cdot g$ | Specific weight in terms of density and gravity | $\text{N/m}^3$ |
| $\gamma_{rel} = \dfrac{\gamma_{substance}}{\gamma_{water}}$ | Relative or specific gravity (dimensionless) | No unit |
| $\gamma = \dfrac{\rho_{water} \cdot g}{S}$ | Specific weight from specific gravity $S$ | $\text{N/m}^3$ |
| $W = \gamma \cdot V$ | Weight from specific weight and volume | $\text{N}$ |
| $m = \rho \cdot V$ | Mass from density and volume | $\text{kg}$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Specific weight is not merely an academic definition. It plays a **critical role** in the following engineering domains:

- **Dam Engineering:** Calculating the hydrostatic thrust on the vertical upstream face of gravity dams.
- **Pipeline Design:** Determining the static pressure head at the base of elevated water storage tanks.
- **Hydraulic Machinery:** Evaluating the inlet and outlet pressure heads in pumps and turbines.
- **Submarine Design:** Analyzing buoyancy and stability of marine vessels.
- **Manometer Calibration:** Converting fluid column heights to equivalent pressure readings.

### 2.5 Standard Values for KTU 2024 Examination

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU numerical problems, you are expected to **memorize** the standard specific weights of common fluids at **4°C** (where water has its maximum density).

- **Water at 4°C:** $\gamma_{water} = 9810 \ \text{N/m}^3$
- **Mercury (Hg) at 20°C:** $\gamma_{Hg} \approx 132840 \ \text{N/m}^3$
- **Air at standard conditions:** $\gamma_{air} \approx 11.81 \ \text{N/m}^3$
- **Sea Water:** $\gamma_{sw} \approx 10050 \ \text{N/m}^3$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From First Principles (Weight per Unit Volume)

We begin with the most fundamental physical quantities.

**Step 1:** Consider a fluid element of volume $V$ and mass $m$. The weight of this fluid element is given by Newton's second law of motion:

$$W = m \cdot g$$

**Step 2:** By definition, the mass can be expressed in terms of the fluid's density and its volume:

$$m = \rho \cdot V$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the expression for mass into the weight equation:

$$W = (\rho \cdot V) \cdot g$$

$$W = \rho \cdot g \cdot V$$

**Step 4:** The specific weight is defined as the weight per unit volume. We therefore divide both sides of the equation by the volume $V$:

$$\frac{W}{V} = \rho \cdot g$$

**Step 5:** Recognizing that the left-hand side is the definition of specific weight, we obtain the master relationship:

$$\boxed{\gamma = \rho \cdot g}$$

This completes the derivation. The specific weight is directly proportional to both the density of the fluid and the local acceleration due to gravity.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Linking Specific Weight to Hydrostatic Pressure

The most important application of specific weight in Module 1 is the derivation of hydrostatic pressure.

**Step 1:** Consider a static fluid column of height $h$ and uniform cross-sectional area $A$ resting on a horizontal surface.

**Step 2:** The volume of the fluid column is:

$$V = A \cdot h$$

**Step 3:** The weight of the fluid column acting vertically downward is:

$$W = \gamma \cdot V = \gamma \cdot A \cdot h$$

**Step 4:** The pressure exerted on the horizontal surface at the base is the force per unit area. The force here is the weight of the fluid column acting on the area $A$:

$$P = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{W}{A}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting the expression for $W$:

$$P = \frac{\gamma \cdot A \cdot h}{A}$$

**Step 6:** The cross-sectional area cancels out, leaving the celebrated hydrostatic pressure equation:

$$\boxed{P = \gamma \cdot h}$$

This shows that hydrostatic pressure is **directly proportional to the specific weight** of the fluid and the depth of submersion.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Specific Weight in Terms of Specific Gravity

The **specific gravity (S)** of a fluid is defined as the ratio of the density of the fluid to the density of water at 4°C.

$$S = \frac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water}}$$

**Step 1:** Rearrange the specific gravity equation to express the density of the fluid:

$$\rho_{fluid} = S \cdot \rho_{water}$$

**Step 2:** Multiply both sides by the acceleration due to gravity $g$:

$$\rho_{fluid} \cdot g = S \cdot \rho_{water} \cdot g$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that the left-hand side is the specific weight of the fluid, while the right-hand side contains the specific weight of water:

$$\boxed{\gamma_{fluid} = S \cdot \gamma_{water}}$$

This relationship is extremely useful in KTU 2024 problems where the specific gravity of an oil or liquid is given, and the absolute specific weight must be calculated.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A storage tank contains **0.4 m³ of oil** with a specific gravity of **0.85**. Calculate the **weight of the oil** in the tank. Take the acceleration due to gravity as $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$.

**Step 1 — Identify the given data:**

- Volume of oil, $V = 0.4 \ \text{m}^3$
- Specific gravity, $S = 0.85$
- Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$

**Step 2 — Calculate the specific weight of water:**

The standard specific weight of water at 4°C is computed as:

$$\gamma_{water} = \rho_{water} \cdot g = 1000 \ \text{kg/m}^3 \times 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$$

$$\gamma_{water} = 9810 \ \text{N/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the specific weight of the oil:**

Using the specific gravity relationship derived above:

$$\gamma_{oil} = S \cdot \gamma_{water} = 0.85 \times 9810$$

$$\gamma_{oil} = 8338.5 \ \text{N/m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the weight of the oil:**

$$W = \gamma_{oil} \cdot V = 8338.5 \times 0.4$$

$$\boxed{W = 3335.4 \ \text{N} \approx 3.335 \ \text{kN}}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow Diagram: Derivation Chain of Specific Weight

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the logical derivation chain from fundamental quantities to the specific weight, showing the interconnections between mass, weight, density, and gravity.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Mass of Fluid: m in kg] --> B[Volume of Fluid: V in m³]
    B --> C[Density: rho equals m divided by V in kg per m³]
    A --> D[Weight: W equals m times g in N]
    D --> E[Specific Weight: gamma equals W divided by V in N per m³]
    C --> E
    G[Acceleration due to gravity: g equals 9.81 m per s²] --> D
    G --> E
    F[Specific Gravity: S equals rho_fluid divided by rho_water] --> H[gamma_fluid equals S times gamma_water]
    E --> I[Hydrostatic Pressure: P equals gamma times h in Pa]
    E --> H
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Application of Specific Weight in Fluid Systems

The following block diagram maps the **functional role** of specific weight across the various sub-systems of a typical hydraulic engineering workflow.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Fluid Property Input: Specific Weight gamma] --> nodeB[Hydrostatic Pressure Calculation]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Manometric Pressure Head Conversion]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Buoyancy and Floatation Analysis]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Total Force on Submerged Surfaces]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Dam Wall Design and Stability]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Pipeline Pressure Drop Estimation]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Calibration of U-tube and Inclined Manometers]
    nodeD --> nodeI[Submarine and Ship Stability Design]
    nodeE --> nodeJ[Design of Storage Tank Walls and Gates]
    nodeF --> nodeK[Final Civil Engineering Output]
    nodeG --> nodeK
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
```

### 4.3 Interpretation of the Diagrams

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram 1** establishes the **mathematical lineage** of specific weight — from the base physical quantities of mass, volume, and gravity, through the intermediate property of density, to the final derived quantity $\gamma$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram 2** demonstrates the **engineering reach** of specific weight. A single property feeds into four major analytical branches in fluid mechanics, which in turn support critical design decisions in dams, pipelines, marine vessels, and storage systems. For KTU 2024, this illustrates why specific weight is a **Module 1 foundational topic** that reappears in every subsequent module.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Define the term "Specific Weight" of a fluid. State its SI unit.**

**Model Answer:**

The specific weight of a fluid is defined as the **weight per unit volume** of the fluid. It is denoted by the symbol $\gamma$ (gamma) and is mathematically expressed as $\gamma = W / V = \rho \cdot g$, where $W$ is the weight, $V$ is the volume, $\rho$ is the density, and $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity. The **SI unit** of specific weight is **Newton per cubic meter (N/m³)**.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Distinguish between density and specific weight of a fluid.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **Density ($\rho$)** | **Specific Weight ($\gamma$)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Mass per unit volume | Weight per unit volume |
| Formula | $\rho = m / V$ | $\gamma = W / V = \rho \cdot g$ |
| SI Unit | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | $\text{N/m}^3$ |
| Dependency on $g$ | Independent of gravity | Depends on local gravity |
| Nature | Intrinsic material property | Varies with location on Earth |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Students frequently confuse **specific weight ($\gamma$)** with **specific gravity ($S$)** and write $\gamma = S$ directly. Remember: $S$ is **dimensionless**, while $\gamma$ has units of $\text{N/m}^3$. Always compute $\gamma_{water} = 9810 \ \text{N/m}^3$ first, then multiply by $S$. Also, do not forget to use $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$ — using $g = 10$ will cause a **1.9% error** that examiners penalize.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

> A cylindrical storage tank of diameter **1.2 m** and height **3 m** is filled with a liquid having a specific gravity of **1.25**.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the specific weight, mass, and total weight of the liquid in the tank. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** Determine the hydrostatic pressure at the base of the tank and the total force exerted by the liquid on the bottom of the tank. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Given Data:**

- Diameter of tank, $D = 1.2 \ \text{m}$
- Height of tank, $h_{tank} = 3 \ \text{m}$
- Specific gravity, $S = 1.25$
- Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Specific Weight:**

$$\gamma_{water} = 1000 \times 9.81 = 9810 \ \text{N/m}^3$$

$$\gamma_{liquid} = S \times \gamma_{water} = 1.25 \times 9810 = 12262.5 \ \text{N/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Volume of the Cylindrical Tank:**

$$V = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^2 \times h_{tank}$$

$$V = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (1.2)^2 \times 3$$

$$V = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 1.44 \times 3 = 3.3929 \ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the Mass of the Liquid:**

$$m = \rho_{liquid} \cdot V$$

Since $\rho_{liquid} = S \times \rho_{water} = 1.25 \times 1000 = 1250 \ \text{kg/m}^3$:

$$m = 1250 \times 3.3929 = 4241.15 \ \text{kg}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the Total Weight:**

$$W = m \cdot g = 4241.15 \times 9.81 = 41605.5 \ \text{N} \approx 41.6 \ \text{kN}$$

Alternatively, $W = \gamma_{liquid} \times V = 12262.5 \times 3.3929 = 41605.5 \ \text{N}$. ✓

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure at the Base:**

Since the tank is open and full, the depth of liquid $h = 3 \ \text{m}$:

$$P = \gamma_{liquid} \times h = 12262.5 \times 3 = 36787.5 \ \text{Pa}$$

$$P \approx 36.79 \ \text{kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Base Area of the Tank:**

$$A_{base} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 1.44 = 1.131 \ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Total Force on the Bottom:**

$$F = P \times A_{base} = 36787.5 \times 1.131 = 41605.5 \ \text{N}$$

$$F \approx 41.6 \ \text{kN}$$

> This force equals the total weight of the liquid, confirming the hydrostatic principle.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

> **(a)** Derive the relationship between specific weight, density, and specific gravity of a fluid. Explain how the specific weight of a fluid varies with temperature and pressure. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A U-tube manometer is connected to a pipeline carrying oil of specific gravity **0.86**. The manometer uses mercury (specific gravity **13.6**) as the indicating fluid. If the difference in mercury levels is **20 cm**, determine the gauge pressure at the center of the pipeline. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Relationship between Specific Weight, Density, and Specific Gravity:**

The specific weight $\gamma$ is related to density $\rho$ by:

$$\gamma = \rho \cdot g$$

The specific gravity $S$ is defined as the ratio of fluid density to water density:

$$S = \frac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water}}$$

Therefore, $\rho_{fluid} = S \times \rho_{water}$. Substituting into the specific weight equation:

$$\gamma_{fluid} = \rho_{fluid} \cdot g = S \cdot \rho_{water} \cdot g = S \cdot \gamma_{water}$$

Hence the master relationship: $\boxed{\gamma_{fluid} = S \times \gamma_{water}}$.

**Step 2 — Variation of Specific Weight with Temperature:**

- As temperature increases, the fluid **expands**, causing its volume to increase while its mass remains constant.
- Since $\rho = m/V$, the density **decreases** with rising temperature.
- Consequently, $\gamma = \rho \cdot g$ also **decreases** with rising temperature.

**Step 3 — Variation of Specific Weight with Pressure:**

- For **liquids**, which are nearly incompressible, an increase in pressure causes only a marginal increase in density, and hence a negligible change in specific weight.
- For **gases**, which are highly compressible, an increase in pressure causes a significant increase in density (in accordance with the ideal gas law), and hence a substantial increase in specific weight.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Given Data:**

- Specific gravity of oil, $S_{oil} = 0.86$
- Specific gravity of mercury, $S_{Hg} = 13.6$
- Mercury level difference, $h = 20 \ \text{cm} = 0.20 \ \text{m}$
- Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$

**Step 2 — Standard U-tube Manometer Equation:**

For a simple U-tube manometer connected to a pipeline containing oil and using mercury as the manometric fluid, the gauge pressure at the pipe center is given by:

$$P = h \times (\gamma_{Hg} - \gamma_{oil})$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Specific Weights:**

$$\gamma_{oil} = 0.86 \times 9810 = 8436.6 \ \text{N/m}^3$$

$$\gamma_{Hg} = 13.6 \times 9810 = 133416 \ \text{N/m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the Differential Specific Weight:**

$$\gamma_{Hg} - \gamma_{oil} = 133416 - 8436.6 = 124979.4 \ \text{N/m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the Gauge Pressure:**

$$P = 0.20 \times 124979.4$$

$$P = 24995.88 \ \text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 25.0 \ \text{kPa (gauge)}}$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Specific Weight**

- **Definition:** Specific weight $\gamma$ is the **weight per unit volume** of a fluid. Symbol: **gamma ($\gamma$)**.
- **Master Formula:** $\gamma = \rho \cdot g$ where $\rho$ is density and $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$.
- **Alternate Form:** $\gamma = W / V$ where $W$ is weight and $V$ is volume.
- **SI Unit:** **N/m³** (Newton per cubic meter). FPS unit: lb/ft³.
- **Standard Value:** $\gamma_{water} = 9810 \ \text{N/m}^3$ at **4°C** (memorize this).
- **Specific Gravity Link:** $\gamma_{fluid} = S \times \gamma_{water}$, where $S$ is dimensionless.
- **Hydrostatic Pressure Link:** $P = \gamma \cdot h$ — the most critical application in Module 1.
- **Temperature Effect:** $\gamma$ **decreases** with rising temperature (density drops).
- **Pressure Effect:** For liquids, $\gamma$ is nearly **constant** with pressure (incompressibility). For gases, $\gamma$ is **highly sensitive** to pressure.
- **Common Mistake:** Do not confuse $\gamma$ (specific weight, N/m³) with $S$ (specific gravity, dimensionless).
- **Use $g = 9.81$ m/s²** unless the problem explicitly states a different value. Using $g = 10$ will lead to mark deductions.
- **Always show units** in every step of the KTU examination solution to earn full marks.
- **Mercury Standard:** $\gamma_{Hg} = 132840$ to $133416 \ \text{N/m}^3$ depending on temperature. Use $13.6 \times 9810$ in calculations.
- **Linkage to Other Modules:** Specific weight reappears in **Module 2** (fluid kinematics), **Module 3** (dimensional analysis), and **Module 4** (turbomachinery) — master it now.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Viscosity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Viscosity # 🔬 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Viscosity > [!IMPORTANT] > **Viscosity** is the property of a fluid that offers resistance to the relative motion (deformation) of its fluid layers. It is the internal friction of a fluid in motion, which opposes the tangential (shearing) motion between adjacent layers. Mathematically, **Newton's Law of Viscosity** states that the shear stress ($\tau$) between two adjacent fluid layers is directly proportional to the rate of change of velocity with respect to the perpendicular distance from the fixed surface (velocity gradient). $$ \tau \propto \frac{du}{dy} $$ $$ \tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} $$ Where: - $\tau$ = Shear stress (N/m² or Pa) - $\mu$ = Coefficient of dynamic viscosity (N·s/m² or Pa·s) - $\frac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient (1/s or s⁻¹) ## 1.2 Two Key Coefficients of Viscosity ### 🔹 Dynamic Viscosity (μ) — *Also called Absolute Viscosity* It is the ratio of shear stress to the velocity gradient. It represents the actual internal friction between fluid layers. $$ \mu = \frac{\tau}{\left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)} $$ ### 🔹 Kinematic Viscosity (ν) It is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to the mass density of the fluid. It is a measure of a fluid's resistance to flow under the influence of gravity alone. $$ \nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} $$ Where $\rho$ is the mass density of the fluid (kg/m³). > [!NOTE] > **Memory Tip:** *Dynamic* → units involve **mass** (kg/m·s). *Kinematic* → units involve only **length and time** (m²/s). The term "Kinematic" comes from the Greek word *kinema* meaning motion — so no force, no mass! ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🧈 Imagine a thick honey jar 🍯. When you tilt the jar, the honey pours very slowly compared to water 💧. Why? Because the molecules of honey cling more tightly to each other, resisting the relative sliding of layers — this internal "stickiness" is what we call **viscosity**. **Visualize it as a deck of cards:** Take a deck of playing cards placed flat on a table. Push the top card horizontally — it slides easily while the bottom card stays put (held by friction with the table). The cards in between slide progressively slower. This *layered sliding behavior* is exactly how a viscous fluid flows near a solid boundary — the layer in contact sticks (no-slip condition), and layers above slide over it with progressively higher velocities. ## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants (Water at 20°C) | Property | Symbol | Value | |----------|--------|-------| | Dynamic Viscosity of Water | $\mu_{water}$ | **1.002 × 10⁻³ N·s/m²** | | Kinematic Viscosity of Water | $\nu_{water}$ | **1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** | | Density of Water | $\rho_{water}$ | **998 kg/m³** | > [!TIP] > In KTU exams, when not specified, assume standard values at **20°C** unless the question states otherwise. # 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Newton's Law of Viscosity — The Underlying Logic When a fluid flows over a solid surface, the layer in immediate contact with the surface adheres to it (the famous **no-slip condition**). As we move away from the surface, each successive layer moves faster than the layer beneath it. **Why does this resistance occur?** - Fluid molecules experience attractive intermolecular forces (cohesive forces). - When two adjacent layers move at different velocities, momentum transfer occurs between them. - The faster-moving layer drags the slower one, and vice versa. - This momentum exchange manifests as **shear stress** that opposes the motion. ## 2.2 Newton's Hypothesis — Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1:** Consider a fluid element between two parallel plates. **Step 2:** The lower plate is fixed; the upper plate moves with velocity $U$. **Step 3:** A linear velocity distribution develops across the fluid gap $h$: $$ u(y) = U \cdot \frac{y}{h} $$ **Step 4:** The velocity gradient (also called **shear rate** or **rate of shear strain**) is constant: $$ \frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{h} $$ **Step 5:** By Newton's hypothesis, the shear stress is proportional to this gradient, giving: $$ \tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} $$ ## 2.3 Classification of Fluids Based on Viscosity | Fluid Type | Shear Stress Relation | Curve Shape | |------------|----------------------|-------------| | **Newtonian Fluid** | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Straight line through origin | | **Non-Newtonian Fluid** | $\tau \neq \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Curved or yield-stress behavior | ### Sub-categories of Non-Newtonian Fluids: - **Pseudo-plastic** (e.g., blood, paint): viscosity decreases with shear rate. - **Dilatant** (e.g., corn starch slurry): viscosity increases with shear rate. - **Bingham Plastic** (e.g., toothpaste, mud): requires a yield stress before flow begins. - **Thixotropic / Rheopectic**: time-dependent behavior. > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme specifically tests the distinction between Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids. Water, air, oils, and most simple fluids are Newtonian. ## 2.4 Effect of Temperature on Viscosity 🌡️ | Fluid Type | Viscosity behavior with ↑ Temperature | |------------|----------------------------------------| | **Liquids** (water, oil) | Viscosity **decreases** sharply | | **Gases** (air, steam) | Viscosity **increases** slightly | ### Why this difference? - In **liquids**, molecular cohesion is dominant. Heating loosens bonds → less internal friction → viscosity drops. - In **gases**, molecules are far apart. Heating increases random molecular motion → more momentum transfer between layers → viscosity rises. ### Approximate Empirical Relations: - For liquids: $\mu_T = \mu_0 \left(\dfrac{1}{1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2}\right)$ (Sutherland-type relation) - For gases: $\mu_T = \mu_0 \left(\dfrac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_0 + S}{T + S}\right)$ (Sutherland's formula) ## 2.5 📋 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET | # | Formula | Description | Units / Dimensions | |---|---------|-------------|-------------------| | 1 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's Law of Viscosity | $\tau$: N/m² or Pa | | 2 | $\mu = \dfrac{\tau}{du/dy}$ | Dynamic viscosity definition | N·s/m² or Pa·s; $[ML^{-1}T^{-1}]$ | | 3 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity | m²/s; $[L^{2}T^{-1}]$ | | 4 | $\mu_{water}(20°C) = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s | Reference value | Pa·s | | 5 | $\nu_{water}(20°C) = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s | Reference value | m²/s | | 6 | $\tau \cdot \Delta t = \mu \cdot \Delta u$ | Impulse-momentum form (per unit area) | Pa·s | | 7 | $\dfrac{\mu}{\rho} = \nu$ | Density-related conversion | — | > [!WARNING] > **Units Trap:** Always check if the problem uses **CGS (Poise, Stokes)** or **SI (Pa·s, m²/s)**. Conversions: > - $1 \text{ Pa·s} = 10 \text{ Poise}$ > - $1 \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 10^4 \text{ Stokes}$ > - $1 \text{ cP} = 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$ (centipoise is common in engineering) ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility 🏗️ - **Hydraulic Machinery:** Bearing design, pump performance, and turbine lubrication depend heavily on oil viscosity. - **Pipeline Design:** Viscosity dictates the pressure drop across long-distance oil pipelines (Hagen-Poiseuille flow). - **Automotive Engineering:** Engine oil grade (SAE 10W-30, etc.) is a direct specification of viscosity at given temperatures. - **Aerodynamics:** Air viscosity governs boundary layer formation and drag on aircraft wings. - **Food and Pharma Industry:** Viscosity determines flow behavior of syrups, blood, and pharmaceutical fluids. # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Newton's Law of Viscosity from Shear Stress Definition Consider a fluid element of thickness $dy$ and area $dA$, sandwiched between two parallel plates. **Step 1 — Identify the shearing force:** A shearing force $dF$ is applied on the top surface of the element. This produces a shear stress: $$ \tau = \frac{dF}{dA} $$ **Step 2 — Identify the deformation:** The top surface displaces by $du$ over time $dt$, while the bottom remains stationary. **Step 3 — Compute shear strain rate:** The deformation (shear strain) is: $$ \gamma = \frac{du}{dy} $$ The rate of shear strain is: $$ \dot{\gamma} = \frac{d\gamma}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{du}{dy}\right) = \frac{1}{dy}\frac{du}{dt} $$ **Step 4 — Apply Newton's Hypothesis (postulate):** Newton proposed that for a fluid: $$ \tau \propto \dot{\gamma} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau = \mu \cdot \dot{\gamma} $$ **Step 5 — Final form (steady flow):** Since for steady flow $\dfrac{du}{dt}$ is constant at a point, we write: $$ \tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} $$ This is **Newton's Law of Viscosity** — a fundamental constitutive equation of fluid mechanics. ## 3.2 Derivation: Dimensions of Dynamic Viscosity [μ] Starting from: $$ \mu = \frac{\tau}{(du/dy)} $$ **Step 1 — Express $\tau$ in basic dimensions:** $$ [\tau] = \frac{[F]}{[A]} = \frac{MLT^{-2}}{L^{2}} = ML^{-1}T^{-2} $$ **Step 2 — Express velocity gradient in basic dimensions:** $$ \left[\frac{du}{dy}\right] = \frac{LT^{-1}}{L} = T^{-1} $$ **Step 3 — Divide dimensions:** $$ [\mu] = \frac{ML^{-1}T^{-2}}{T^{-1}} = ML^{-1}T^{-1} $$ **Final:** $[\mu] = ML^{-1}T^{-1}$ (in SI base units: **kg·m⁻¹·s⁻¹**) ## 3.3 Derivation: Dimensions of Kinematic Viscosity [ν] Starting from: $$ \nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} $$ **Step 1 — Dimensions of $\mu$:** $ML^{-1}T^{-1}$ **Step 2 — Dimensions of $\rho$:** $ML^{-3}$ **Step 3 — Divide:** $$ [\nu] = \frac{ML^{-1}T^{-1}}{ML^{-3}} = L^{2}T^{-1} $$ **Final:** $[\nu] = L^{2}T^{-1}$ (in SI base units: **m²·s⁻¹**) ## 3.4 Analytical Worked Problem — Typical KTU Pattern ### 📝 Problem Statement: A Newtonian fluid is placed between two parallel plates separated by a distance of **5 mm**. The lower plate is fixed and the upper plate moves with a velocity of **0.25 m/s**. The shear stress developed is **0.2 N/m²**. Calculate: 1. The dynamic viscosity $\mu$ of the fluid. 2. The kinematic viscosity $\nu$, given that fluid density = **900 kg/m³**. 3. The velocity at a distance of **2 mm** from the fixed plate. ### ✅ Solution: **Given Data:** - Plate gap: $h = 5 \text{ mm} = 0.005 \text{ m}$ - Upper plate velocity: $U = 0.25 \text{ m/s}$ - Shear stress: $\tau = 0.2 \text{ N/m}^2$ - Density: $\rho = 900 \text{ kg/m}^3$ **Part (a): Dynamic Viscosity** From Newton's Law: $$ \tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} $$ For a linear velocity profile (Couette flow): $$ \frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{h} = \frac{0.25}{0.005} = 50 \text{ s}^{-1} $$ Therefore: $$ \mu = \frac{\tau}{du/dy} = \frac{0.2}{50} = 0.004 \text{ N·s/m}^2 = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s} $$ **Part (b): Kinematic Viscosity** $$ \nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{900} = 4.44 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} $$ **Part (c): Velocity at y = 2 mm** For linear velocity distribution: $$ u(y) = U \cdot \frac{y}{h} = 0.25 \times \frac{0.002}{0.005} = 0.10 \text{ m/s} $$ ### 🧾 Summary of Results: | Quantity | Symbol | Value | |----------|--------|-------| | Dynamic viscosity | $\mu$ | $4 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s | | Kinematic viscosity | $\nu$ | $4.44 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s | | Velocity at 2 mm | $u$ | $0.10$ m/s | # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Newton's Law of Viscosity — Physical Setup ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["FIXED LOWER PLATE
Velocity = 0 m/s
y = 0"]:::plateStyle node2["FLUID LAYER
dy thickness
Linear Velocity Profile"]:::fluidStyle node3["MOVING UPPER PLATE
Velocity = U m/s
y = h"]:::plateStyle node4["Shear Stress tau acts
on upper plate
opposing motion"]:::stressStyle node5["Velocity Profile u(y) = U y / h"]:::profileStyle node6["Velocity Gradient du/dy = U/h"]:::gradStyle node1 -->|"y = 0"| node2 node2 -->|"y = h"| node3 node3 -.->|"tau opposes U"| node4 node2 -.->|"Linear variation"| node5 node5 -.->|"Constant slope"| node6 node6 -.->|"Newton's Law"| node7["tau = mu times du/dy"]:::lawStyle classDef plateStyle fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef fluidStyle fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#0b2545,stroke-width:2px classDef stressStyle fill:#f1948a,stroke:#922b21,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef profileStyle fill:#abebc6,stroke:#196f3d,color:#0b2545,stroke-width:2px classDef gradStyle fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,color:#0b2545,stroke-width:2px classDef lawStyle fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#5b2c6f,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fluid Flow & Viscosity Interaction ```mermaid flowchart LR A["INPUT FORCES
Applied Shear Force F
Plate Velocity U"]:::inputBlock B["FLUID MEDIUM
Newtonian or Non-Newtonian
Density rho, Viscosity mu"]:::fluidBlock C["MOMENTUM TRANSFER
Between Adjacent Layers
Faster layer drags slower"]:::transferBlock D["VELOCITY GRADIENT
du by dy = U over h
Shear Rate"]:::gradBlock E["OUTPUT RESPONSE
Shear Stress tau
Resistive Force"]:::outputBlock F["GOVERNING EQUATION
tau equals mu times du by dy"]:::lawBlock A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> F F --> E B -.->|"Controls magnitude"| F classDef inputBlock fill:#3498db,stroke:#1b4f72,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef fluidBlock fill:#48c9b0,stroke:#117864,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef transferBlock fill:#f39c12,stroke:#9c640c,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef gradBlock fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#78281f,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef outputBlock fill:#9b59b6,stroke:#4a235a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef lawBlock fill:#1abc9c,stroke:#0e6251,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px ``` ## 4.3 Decision Flowchart: Newtonian vs Non-Newtonian Classification ```mermaid flowchart TB start["START
Observe Fluid Behavior
Under Applied Shear"]:::startStyle q1{"Does tau vary
linearly with
du by dy?"}:::qStyle n1["NEWTONIAN FLUID
tau = mu du by dy
mu is constant"]:::newtStyle q2{"Viscosity changes
with shear rate?"}:::qStyle n2["BINGHAM PLASTIC
Yields first, then flows
tau = tau_yield + mu du by dy"]:::bingStyle n3["PSEUDOPLASTIC
mu decreases with shear
e.g., blood, paint"]:::pseudoStyle n4["DILATANT
mu increases with shear
e.g., starch slurry"]:::dilStyle start --> q1 q1 -->|"YES"| n1 q1 -->|"NO"| q2 q2 -->|"Needs yield stress"| n2 q2 -->|"No yield stress"| q3{"mu increases
or decreases?"}:::qStyle q3 -->|"Decreases"| n3 q3 -->|"Increases"| n4 classDef startStyle fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#1b2631,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px classDef qStyle fill:#fdfd96,stroke:#7d6608,color:#0b2545,stroke-width:2px classDef newtStyle fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#1b4f72,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef bingStyle fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef pseudoStyle fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef dilStyle fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,color:#0b2545,stroke-width:2px ``` # 🎓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📚 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define viscosity. Differentiate between dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity.** #### ✅ Model Answer: **Viscosity** is the property of a fluid that resists the relative motion of its internal layers, manifesting as internal friction during flow. **Dynamic Viscosity (μ):** - It is the ratio of shear stress to velocity gradient. - Represents the actual internal friction. - Units: N·s/m² (Pa·s) | Dimensions: $ML^{-1}T^{-1}$ **Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** - It is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to mass density. - Represents the fluid's resistance to flow under gravity. - Units: m²/s | Dimensions: $L^{2}T^{-1}$ **Relationship:** $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State Newton's law of viscosity. Explain why viscosity of liquids decreases with temperature while that of gases increases.** #### ✅ Model Answer: **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** The shear stress developed in a fluid is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain (velocity gradient): $$ \tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} $$ **For Liquids:** Cohesive (intermolecular) forces dominate. As temperature rises, these cohesive forces weaken significantly → molecules slide past each other more easily → viscosity **decreases**. **For Gases:** Molecular spacing is large; cohesive forces are negligible. As temperature rises, molecular agitation and random motion increase → more momentum exchange between layers → viscosity **increases**. --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice) ### 🔷 Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **A Newtonian fluid is contained between two horizontal parallel plates 8 mm apart. The lower plate is fixed and the upper plate moves at 0.4 m/s. The shear stress observed is 0.24 N/m². Determine:** **(a)** The dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity of the fluid, given density = 850 kg/m³. **(b)** The shear stress required if the velocity of the upper plate is doubled. Also determine the velocity gradient and comment on the type of flow profile. #### ✅ Solution: **Given:** - Plate gap: $h = 8 \text{ mm} = 0.008 \text{ m}$ - Upper plate velocity: $U = 0.4 \text{ m/s}$ - Shear stress: $\tau = 0.24 \text{ N/m}^2$ - Density: $\rho = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$ ### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Compute velocity gradient:** $$ \frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{h} = \frac{0.4}{0.008} = 50 \text{ s}^{-1} $$ **Step 2 — Dynamic Viscosity:** $$ \mu = \frac{\tau}{(du/dy)} = \frac{0.24}{50} = 0.0048 \text{ N·s/m}^2 = 4.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s} $$ **Step 3 — Kinematic Viscosity:** $$ \nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{4.8 \times 10^{-3}}{850} = 5.647 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} $$ ### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — New velocity:** $U' = 2 \times 0.4 = 0.8 \text{ m/s}$ **Step 2 — New velocity gradient:** $$ \frac{du}{dy}' = \frac{U'}{h} = \frac{0.8}{0.008} = 100 \text{ s}^{-1} $$ **Step 3 — New shear stress:** $$ \tau' = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}' = 0.0048 \times 100 = 0.48 \text{ N/m}^2 $$ **Step 4 — Comment on flow profile:** Since the relationship between $\tau$ and $du/dy$ remains linear (through origin), the fluid continues to exhibit **Newtonian behavior**. The flow profile remains **linear** (Couette flow), with a doubled velocity gradient and doubled shear stress as compared to the original case. | Quantity | Original Case | Doubled Velocity Case | |----------|---------------|----------------------| | Velocity (m/s) | 0.4 | 0.8 | | Velocity gradient (1/s) | 50 | 100 | | Shear stress (N/m²) | 0.24 | 0.48 | --- ### 🔷 Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` (Alternative Choice) **(a)** Derive the dimensions of dynamic viscosity ($\mu$) and kinematic viscosity ($\nu$). Explain their significance in fluid mechanics. **(b)** A thin plate of area 0.5 m² is pulled with a velocity of 0.3 m/s through an oil layer of thickness 6 mm placed on a fixed horizontal surface. The force required to maintain this motion is 5 N. Compute the viscosity of the oil. If the oil density is 920 kg/m³, determine the kinematic viscosity. #### ✅ Solution: ### Part (a) — 7 Marks (Derivation of Dimensions) **Dynamic Viscosity:** $$ \mu = \frac{\tau}{(du/dy)} = \frac{F/A}{u/y} $$ **Substituting dimensions:** - $[F] = MLT^{-2}$ - $[A] = L^{2}$ - $[u] = LT^{-1}$ - $[y] = L$ Therefore: $$ [\mu] = \frac{MLT^{-2}/L^{2}}{LT^{-1}/L} = \frac{ML^{-1}T^{-2}}{T^{-1}} = ML^{-1}T^{-1} $$ **Final Dimension:** $[\mu] = M^{1}L^{-1}T^{-1}$ (SI: kg·m⁻¹·s⁻¹) **Kinematic Viscosity:** $$ \nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} $$ **Substituting dimensions:** $$ [\nu] = \frac{ML^{-1}T^{-1}}{ML^{-3}} = L^{2}T^{-1} $$ **Final Dimension:** $[\nu] = L^{2}T^{-1}$ (SI: m²·s⁻¹) **Significance:** - Dynamic viscosity quantifies internal friction forces between layers. - Kinematic viscosity is used in dimensionless numbers like **Reynolds number ($Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{VL}{\nu}$)** to predict flow regime (laminar/turbulent). ### Part (b) — 7 Marks (Numerical) **Given:** - Plate area: $A = 0.5 \text{ m}^2$ - Plate velocity: $U = 0.3 \text{ m/s}$ - Oil layer thickness: $h = 6 \text{ mm} = 0.006 \text{ m}$ - Pulling force: $F = 5 \text{ N}$ - Oil density: $\rho = 920 \text{ kg/m}^3$ **Step 1 — Compute shear stress:** $$ \tau = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{5}{0.5} = 10 \text{ N/m}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Compute velocity gradient:** $$ \frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{h} = \frac{0.3}{0.006} = 50 \text{ s}^{-1} $$ **Step 3 — Dynamic Viscosity:** $$ \mu = \frac{\tau}{du/dy} = \frac{10}{50} = 0.20 \text{ N·s/m}^2 = 0.20 \text{ Pa·s} $$ **Step 4 — Kinematic Viscosity:** $$ \nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.20}{920} = 2.174 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > > **Common mistakes that cost marks:** > 1. **Unit confusion:** Many students write viscosity in N·s/m² but forget it's equivalent to Pa·s. KTU strictly expects SI base or derived SI units. **MISTAKE:** Writing "N/m²·s" — INCORRECT. > 2. **Forgetting linear velocity profile assumption:** KTU Part B derivations *assume* linear velocity distribution in parallel-plate Couette flow. If a student writes any other profile, they lose 2 marks. > 3. **Mixing up dynamic and kinematic:** Dynamic viscosity involves $\rho$ only for converting to kinematic; never the other way around. > 4. **Dimensional analysis errors:** The exponent for $L$ in $[\mu]$ is $-1$, not $-2$. Memorize it: **"M-L-to-the-minus-one-T-to-the-minus-one"**. > 5. **Skipping intermediate steps:** Always show $du/dy$ computation explicitly before using it — KTU awards step marks. --- ## ⚡ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > ✅ **Rapid Revision Checklist** - 🔹 Viscosity = internal friction of fluid resisting layer-to-layer motion. - 🔹 **Newton's Law:** $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ — the cornerstone of fluid mechanics. - 🔹 Dynamic viscosity $\mu$: units = **Pa·s** or **N·s/m²**; dimensions = $ML^{-1}T^{-1}$. - 🔹 Kinematic viscosity $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$: units = **m²/s**; dimensions = $L^{2}T^{-1}$. - 🔹 Liquids → $\mu$ **decreases** with temperature; Gases → $\mu$ **increases** with temperature. - 🔹 **Newtonian fluids** obey $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ with constant $\mu$. Examples: water, air, oils. - 🔹 **Non-Newtonian fluids** include pseudoplastic, dilatant, Bingham plastic, thixotropic, rheopectic. - 🔹 No-slip condition: fluid layer at solid boundary has **zero velocity** relative to that boundary. - 🔹 Couette flow (linear profile): velocity gradient = $\dfrac{U}{h}$. - 🔹 Conversions: $1 \text{ Pa·s} = 10 \text{ Poise} = 1000 \text{ cP}$. - 🔹 Reynolds number uses kinematic viscosity: $Re = \dfrac{VL}{\nu}$. - 🔹 KTU 2024 expects SI units throughout — avoid CGS unless explicitly asked. > [!TIP] > 🎯 **High-Yield Insight:** Almost every KTU Part B question on viscosity couples it with **Reynolds Number** or **boundary layer concepts** in later modules. Master $\mu$ vs $\nu$ now — it pays dividends throughout the course!

topics.title: Surface Tension
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Surface Tension

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Surface Tension (σ):** Surface tension is defined as the tensile force per unit length acting on an imaginary line drawn on the surface of a liquid, the force being normal to the line and tangential to the liquid surface. Mathematically, it is expressed as:
>
> $$\sigma = \frac{F}{L}$$
>
> where **F** is the tensile force (in Newtons) acting perpendicular to an imaginary line of length **L** (in meters) drawn on the liquid surface. The standard unit of surface tension is **N/m** (Newton per meter), and the corresponding CGS unit is **dyne/cm** (1 N/m = 1000 dyne/cm).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, surface tension belongs to the family of **fluid static properties** and is treated as a fundamental **intensive property** of liquids at a given temperature and pressure. For a clean water-air interface at **20 °C**, the surface tension is approximately **σ = 0.0728 N/m**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine placing a thin, tightly stretched **transparent membrane** (like a soap film) over the open mouth of a glass tumbler. If you place a small dry needle gently on this membrane, it does not sink — it floats. The membrane appears to be in a state of tension, resisting any attempt to break it. A liquid surface behaves in an exactly similar manner.

- The molecules **inside** the liquid are pulled equally in **all directions** by neighboring molecules (cohesive forces).
- The molecules at the **surface**, however, experience a **net downward pull** because there are far fewer molecules in the air above to pull them upward.
- This net inward pull causes the surface molecules to **pack tightly together**, behaving like a stretched elastic skin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Surface tension is **not** a force in the conventional sense. It is a **force per unit length** along a free surface. The energy equivalent of surface tension is **J/m²** (energy per unit area), since work must be done to bring additional molecules from the bulk to the surface.

### 1.3 Cohesive and Adhesive Forces

The molecular explanation of surface tension rests on two fundamental intermolecular forces:

| Force Type | Definition | Example |
|------------|------------|---------|
| **Cohesive Force** | Force of attraction between molecules of the **same** substance | Water-to-water molecular attraction |
| **Adhesive Force** | Force of attraction between molecules of **different** substances | Water-to-glass molecular attraction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Wetting Condition:** When **adhesive force > cohesive force**, the liquid spreads on the solid surface and wets it (e.g., water on clean glass). When **cohesive force > adhesive force**, the liquid forms droplets and does not wet the surface (e.g., mercury on glass).

### 1.4 The Contact Angle (θ)

The **angle** formed between the tangent to the liquid surface and the solid surface at the point of contact is known as the **contact angle**, denoted by **θ**. It is a measurable indicator of the degree of wetting.

- **θ < 90°** → Liquid wets the surface (e.g., water on glass, θ ≈ 0°)
- **θ > 90°** → Liquid does not wet the surface (e.g., mercury on glass, θ ≈ 140°)
- **θ = 0°** → Perfect wetting (complete spreading)
- **θ = 180°** → Perfect non-wetting (theoretically)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Molecular Theory of Surface Tension — Step-by-Step Logic

Let us dissect the operational mechanics of why a liquid surface behaves like a stretched membrane.

1. **Molecules in the Bulk (Interior):** Every molecule located deep inside the liquid is surrounded on all sides by other liquid molecules. The **symmetrical** radial pull from all directions results in a **net zero force** on such a molecule.
2. **Molecules at the Surface:** A molecule lying at the free surface has neighboring liquid molecules only below and to the sides. The attractive pull from above (air molecules) is **negligible** due to the much lower density of gas. This creates a **net inward (downward) pull**.
3. **Energy Minimization:** To minimize the potential energy of the system, the surface molecules are pulled as close together as possible. This effectively **reduces the surface area** to a minimum — which is why free liquid drops assume a **spherical shape** (a sphere has the minimum surface area for a given volume).
4. **Tension Manifestation:** The surface therefore behaves like a stretched elastic film, always trying to contract. Any force applied to break this film must overcome this molecular pull, which is quantified as surface tension **σ**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Surface tension governs droplet formation in fuel injectors, capillary action in wicks and oil reservoirs, the operation of manometers using liquid columns, and the design of heat pipes. It is also critical in the analysis of **bubbles**, **droplets**, and **thin films** in chemical process equipment.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles **all high-yield formulas** for the topic of Surface Tension. These are the equations most frequently tested in KTU University Examinations and internal assessments under the 2024 Scheme.

| # | Phenomenon | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|------------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Basic Definition** | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{L}$ | $F$ = force (N), $L$ = length (m), $\sigma$ = N/m |
| 2 | **Energy Equivalence** | $W = \sigma \cdot \Delta A$ | $W$ = work (J), $\Delta A$ = change in area (m²) |
| 3 | **Pressure in a Liquid Droplet** | $p = \dfrac{2\sigma}{R}$ | $p$ = gauge pressure (N/m²), $R$ = radius (m) |
| 4 | **Pressure in a Soap Bubble** (hollow with air inside) | $p = \dfrac{4\sigma}{R}$ | $p$ = gauge pressure (N/m²), $R$ = radius (m) |
| 5 | **Pressure in a Liquid Jet / Cylinder** | $p = \dfrac{\sigma}{R}$ | $p$ = gauge pressure (N/m²), $R$ = radius (m) |
| 6 | **Capillary Rise (General)** | $h = \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g R}$ | $h$ = rise (m), $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), $R$ = tube radius (m) |
| 7 | **Capillary Rise (Water-Glass, θ ≈ 0°)** | $h = \dfrac{2\sigma}{\rho g R}$ | Special case of (6) with $\cos 0° = 1$ |
| 8 | **Capillary Depression (Mercury-Glass)** | $h = \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g R}$ | $h$ is negative (depression); for mercury $\cos\theta$ is negative |
| 9 | **Force on a Submerged Rectangular Plate** | $F = \sigma \cdot (2 \cdot (b + d))$ | $F$ = pull-out force (N), $b$ = width, $d$ = depth of plate (m) |
| 10 | **Total Pull on a Ring (Wilhelmy Plate Method)** | $F = \sigma \cdot 2 \cdot 2\pi R = 4\pi R \sigma$ | $F$ = force (N), $R$ = ring radius (m) — two surfaces (inner + outer) |
| 11 | **Capillary Rise in terms of tube diameter d** | $h = \dfrac{4\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d}$ | $d$ = diameter of tube (m) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Memorization Trap:** The factor is **2σ/R** for a liquid droplet (one surface) and **4σ/R** for a soap bubble (two surfaces). Many students mistakenly apply the droplet formula to bubbles. Always count the **number of liquid surfaces** enclosing the gas!

### 2.3 Effect of Temperature and Impurities on Surface Tension

- **Temperature:** Surface tension **decreases** with an increase in temperature. At the critical temperature, surface tension becomes **zero** (the liquid and gas phases become indistinguishable).
- **Impurities:** Soluble impurities like **salt (NaCl)** typically **increase** surface tension. **Insoluble impurities** like oil and soap, however, **decrease** surface tension significantly — this is why soap is used as a detergent.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every algebraic transition in this section is shown explicitly. Each step carries an accompanying explanation in plain English to ensure complete clarity for KTU examination preparation.

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Pressure Difference Across a Curved Liquid Surface (Laplace's Law)

**Statement:** The pressure difference across a spherical liquid surface is given by $\Delta p = \dfrac{2\sigma}{R}$ for a single surface (droplet) and $\Delta p = \dfrac{4\sigma}{R}$ for a soap bubble.

**Step 1 — Consider an Elementary Surface Element**

Let us isolate a small spherical droplet of radius **R**. Consider a small element of the surface bounded by a curve. The two principal radii of curvature are both equal to **R** since the surface is spherical.

**Step 2 — Force Balance on the Surface Element**

Imagine a small circular element of the droplet of radius **a** (where $a \ll R$). The surface tension force acts tangentially around the perimeter of this element.

- The **perimeter** of the small element = $2\pi a$
- The **surface tension force** pulling the element around its edge = $\sigma \cdot 2\pi a$
- This force acts **tangentially** to the surface.

**Step 3 — Resolve the Force Normal to the Surface**

When the surface is curved, the tangential force has a small **radial (normal) component** that pushes the element inward. By geometry, for a small element on a sphere of radius **R**, the angle of the tangent with the horizontal is $\phi = \dfrac{a}{2R}$ (small angle approximation).

The **normal component** of the total surface tension force is:

$$F_{normal} = (\sigma \cdot 2\pi a) \cdot \sin\phi \approx (\sigma \cdot 2\pi a) \cdot \frac{a}{2R} = \frac{\pi \sigma a^{2}}{R}$$

**Step 4 — Equate to the Net Pressure Force**

The net inward pressure force on the element due to the pressure difference $\Delta p$ is:

$$F_{pressure} = \Delta p \cdot \pi a^{2}$$

For mechanical equilibrium, $F_{pressure} = F_{normal}$:

$$\Delta p \cdot \pi a^{2} = \frac{\pi \sigma a^{2}}{R}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify**

Cancelling $\pi a^{2}$ from both sides:

$$\Delta p = \frac{2\sigma}{R}$$

This is the **pressure difference across a liquid droplet**.

**Step 6 — Extend to a Soap Bubble**

A soap bubble has **two surfaces** (inner and outer), each of which contributes $\dfrac{2\sigma}{R}$:

$$\Delta p = \frac{4\sigma}{R}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Capillary Rise

**Statement:** When a glass capillary tube of radius **R** is dipped in a liquid that wets it (e.g., water), the liquid rises inside the tube to a height **h** given by:

$$h = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g R}$$

**Step 1 — Define the Geometry**

Consider a vertical capillary tube of internal radius **R** dipped into a liquid of density **ρ** and surface tension **σ**. The contact angle at the meniscus is **θ**.

**Step 2 — Identify the Forces**

Three vertical forces act on the column of liquid of height **h** that rises inside the tube:

1. **Upward force due to surface tension:** $F_{up} = \sigma \cdot 2\pi R \cdot \cos\theta$
   - The contact length around the meniscus = $2\pi R$.
   - Only the **vertical component** of the surface tension contributes, hence $\cos\theta$.

2. **Downward force due to weight of the liquid column:** $F_{down} = \rho g h \cdot \pi R^{2}$
   - Volume of liquid column = $\pi R^{2} h$
   - Weight = $\rho g \cdot \pi R^{2} h$

3. **Pressure forces on top and bottom of the column cancel** (both atmospheric).

**Step 3 — Apply Equilibrium**

For the column to be in static equilibrium:

$$F_{up} = F_{down}$$

$$\sigma \cdot 2\pi R \cdot \cos\theta = \rho g h \cdot \pi R^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for h**

Dividing both sides by $\pi R$:

$$2\sigma \cos\theta = \rho g h R$$

Solving for **h**:

$$h = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g R}$$

**Step 5 — Special Case for Water-Glass**

For water on clean glass, $\theta \approx 0°$, so $\cos\theta = 1$:

$$h = \frac{2\sigma}{\rho g R}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Force Required to Pull a Rectangular Plate from a Liquid Surface

**Statement:** The force required to detach a thin rectangular plate of width **b** and depth **d** from a liquid surface is $F = 2\sigma(b + d)$.

**Step 1 — Setup**

A thin plate is held vertically and pulled out of a liquid. The liquid clings to the plate due to surface tension along the **perimeter of contact** between the plate and the liquid surface.

**Step 2 — Compute the Contact Perimeter**

The plate has **two faces** (front and back) and **two side edges** (top and bottom) where it contacts the liquid. The contact perimeter (around the cross-section of the plate immersed at the surface) is:

$$P = 2(b + d)$$

(The factor of 2 accounts for the two faces of the plate.)

**Step 3 — Write the Force**

The surface tension acts along the entire contact length, and the force is:

$$F = \sigma \cdot P = \sigma \cdot 2(b + d) = 2\sigma(b + d)$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following **Mermaid block diagrams** provide a block-level functional representation of the molecular mechanisms and engineering phenomena discussed above. These are designed to render correctly in Mermaid v10+.

### 4.1 Molecular Force Distribution at the Liquid Surface

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A1["BULK LIQUID REGION"]
        node1["INNER MOLECULE A"]
        node2["SURFACE MOLECULE B"]
        node3["SURFACE MOLECULE C"]
        node4["INTERFACE ZONE"]
    end
    subgraph A2["GAS PHASE ABOVE"]
        node5["RARE AIR MOLECULES"]
    end
    node1 -->|Equal Pull All Sides| node1
    node2 -->|Strong Downward Pull| node4
    node3 -->|Lateral Pull Only| node4
    node5 -->|Negligible Pull| node4
    node4 -->|NET INWARD FORCE| node6["SURFACE CONTRACTION TENSION"]
    node6 --> node7["LIQUID ACTS AS STRETCHED MEMBRANE"]
```

### 4.2 Capillary Rise — Functional Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph B1["CAPILLARY TUBE IN CONTACT WITH LIQUID"]
        step1["LIQUID SURFACE INSIDE TUBE FORMS MENISCUS"]
        step2["CONTACT ANGLE THETA MEASURED AT WALL"]
        step3["SURFACE TENSION ACTS TANGENTIALLY"]
    end
    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
    step3 --> step4["VERTICAL COMPONENT SIGMA COS THETA PULLS COLUMN UPWARD"]
    step4 --> step5["GRAVITATIONAL WEIGHT RHO G H PI R SQUARED PULLS DOWNWARD"]
    step5 --> step6{"EQUILIBRIUM ESTABLISHED"}
    step6 -->|YES| step7["HEIGHT H EQUALS 2 SIGMA COS THETA DIVIDED BY RHO G R"]
    step6 -->|NO| step3
```

### 4.3 Decision Matrix — Droplet, Bubble, or Jet

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["IDENTIFY GEOMETRY OF LIQUID SURFACE"] --> q1{"HOW MANY LIQUID SURFACES ENCLOSE THE GAS?"}
    q1 -->|ONE SURFACE| drop["LIQUID DROPLET FORMULA P EQUALS 2 SIGMA DIVIDED BY R"]
    q1 -->|TWO SURFACES INNER AND OUTER| bub["SOAP BUBBLE FORMULA P EQUALS 4 SIGMA DIVIDED BY R"]
    q1 -->|ONE CYLINDRICAL SURFACE| jet["LIQUID JET FORMULA P EQUALS SIGMA DIVIDED BY R"]
```

### 4.4 Wetting vs Non-Wetting — Behavioural Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cond{"ADHESIVE FORCE vs COHESIVE FORCE"}
    cond -->|ADHESIVE GREATER| wet["WETTING BEHAVIOUR"]
    cond -->|COHESIVE GREATER| dry["NON WETTING BEHAVIOUR"]
    wet --> w1["CONTACT ANGLE LESS THAN 90 DEGREES"]
    wet --> w2["CONCAVE MENISCUS INSIDE TUBE"]
    wet --> w3["CAPILLARY RISE EXAMPLE WATER IN GLASS"]
    dry --> d1["CONTACT ANGLE GREATER THAN 90 DEGREES"]
    dry --> d2["CONVEX MENISCUS INSIDE TUBE"]
    dry --> d3["CAPILLARY DEPRESSION EXAMPLE MERCURY IN GLASS"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modeled strictly on **KTU 2024 Scheme** assessment patterns. Marks are distributed according to the standard **ESE (End Semester Evaluation)** pattern: **Part A = 3 marks**, **Part B = 14 marks** (with internal choice and 7+7 sub-parts). Each solution is written in valuation-key style with clear stepwise logic.

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define surface tension. What are its units? Explain in one sentence why a liquid drop is spherical in shape.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Surface tension is the tensile force per unit length acting on an imaginary line drawn on a free liquid surface, the force being normal to the line and tangential to the surface.
- **Units (1 mark):** The SI unit is **N/m** (Newton per meter); the CGS unit is **dyne/cm**.
- **Spherical shape (1 mark):** A liquid drop is spherical because surface tension tends to minimize the surface area for a given volume, and a sphere has the **minimum surface area** to volume ratio of any geometric shape.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
Differentiate between cohesive and adhesive forces. What is the contact angle?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Cohesive Force (1 mark):** The force of attraction between molecules of the **same** substance (e.g., water-to-water).
- **Adhesive Force (1 mark):** The force of attraction between molecules of **different** substances (e.g., water-to-glass).
- **Contact Angle (1 mark):** The **angle θ** formed between the tangent plane to the liquid surface and the tangent plane to the solid surface at the point of contact. It is a measure of the degree of wetting.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) Derive an expression for the pressure difference across a curved liquid surface. (7 marks)**
**(b) A soap bubble of diameter 20 mm is formed with a liquid of surface tension 0.05 N/m. Calculate the pressure difference between the inside and the outside of the bubble. If the same liquid forms a droplet of the same diameter, what will be the pressure difference? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**
