- **Non-Newtonian fluids** show **variable apparent viscosity** depending on shear rate or time.
- **Power-law (Ostwald–de Waele) model:** $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ where $K$ is consistency index and $n$ is flow behavior index.
- **$n = 1$** → Newtonian (special case where $K = \mu$).
- **$n < 1$** → Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning) — examples: blood, paint, ketchup.
- **$n > 1$** → Dilatant (shear-thickening) — examples: cornstarch slurry, wet sand.
- **Bingham Plastic:** $\tau = \tau_0 + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$ — has a **yield stress** below which no flow occurs; examples: toothpaste, drilling mud.
- **Herschel–Bulkley:** $\tau = \tau_0 + K \dot{\gamma}^n$ — combines yield stress with power-law behavior.
- **Thixotropic** fluids show **decreasing viscosity over time** at constant shear rate (e.g., gelatin, peanut butter, printing ink).
- **Rheopectic** fluids show **increasing viscosity over time** at constant shear rate (e.g., gypsum paste).
- **Viscoelastic** fluids show both **viscous flow and elastic recoil** (e.g., egg white, polymer melts, Silly Putty).
- **Apparent viscosity** for power-law fluid: $\mu_{app} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$.
- **Velocity profile in pipe** for power-law fluid:
  $$u(r) = \frac{n}{n+1} \left(\frac{\Delta p}{2LK}\right)^{1/n} \left[R^{\frac{n+1}{n}} - r^{\frac{n+1}{n}}\right]$$
- **Volumetric flow rate** for power-law fluid:
  $$Q = \frac{\pi n}{3n + 1} \left(\frac{\Delta p}{2LK}\right)^{1/n} R^{\frac{3n+1}{n}}$$
- For $n = 1$, both expressions **reduce to the classical Hagen–Poiseuille result** — always verify this as a check.
- **Generalized Reynolds Number** for power-law fluid: $\text{Re}_{gen} = \dfrac{D^n \bar{u}^{2-n} \rho}{8^{n-1} K \left(\dfrac{3n+1}{4n}\right)^n}$.
- **Bingham plastics** exhibit **plug flow** in the central core region ($r < r_0$) where shear stress is below the yield stress.
- **SI unit of dynamic viscosity** $\mu$ is **Pa·s** (or N·s/m²); CGS unit is **poise (P)** where $1 \text{ P} = 0.1 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$.
- A plot of $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ is called a **rheogram** (also consistency curve or flow curve).
- The science of deformation and flow of matter is called **Rheology**.
- For **board exam writing**: always include (1) constitutive equation, (2) rheogram sketch, (3) physical explanation, (4) examples — this fetches **full marks**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real and Ideal Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Real and Ideal Fluids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Ideal Fluid:**
An *ideal fluid* is a hypothetical fluid that possesses the following idealized properties:
1. It is **inviscid** (zero viscosity — no internal resistance to flow)
2. It is **incompressible** (constant density irrespective of pressure)
3. It possesses **zero surface tension**
4. It has **zero vapor pressure**
5. It exhibits **homogeneous and isotropic** behavior
6. Flow is assumed **steady, laminar, and irrotational**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** An ideal fluid does **NOT** exist in nature. It is a *mathematical abstraction* used to simplify the governing equations of motion (Euler's equations are derived under these assumptions).

**Real Fluid:**
A *real fluid* is any fluid that actually exists in nature. It possesses the following practical properties:
1. It exhibits **viscosity** (internal friction between fluid layers)
2. It is **compressible** to some degree (density changes with pressure/temperature)
3. It has finite **surface tension**
4. It has measurable **vapor pressure**
5. It may be **Newtonian or Non-Newtonian**
6. Flow may be **steady or unsteady, laminar or turbulent**

> [!NOTE]
> **Examples of Real Fluids:** Water, air, oil, blood, mercury, glycerin, kerosene.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

**Imagine a busy highway:**
- An **ideal fluid** behaves like cars on a *frictionless ice highway* — every car moves without any resistance from the road or other cars. Cars can glide past each other effortlessly with no energy loss.
- A **real fluid** behaves like cars on a *normal asphalt road* — there is friction (viscosity) between tires and the road, drag between cars moving at different speeds, and energy is continuously lost as heat.

**Another analogy — a stack of playing cards:**
- If you push the top card, the bottom card stays perfectly still in an *ideal fluid* (no shear transmission).
- In a *real fluid*, the bottom card moves slightly because shear stress is transmitted from the top to the bottom layer by layer.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Properties

| Property | Symbol | Unit | Ideal Fluid | Real Fluid |
|----------|--------|------|-------------|------------|
| Viscosity (dynamic) | $\mu$ | Pa·s or N·s/m² | $\mu = 0$ | $\mu > 0$ |
| Density | $\rho$ | kg/m³ | Constant | Variable |
| Surface tension | $\sigma$ | N/m | $\sigma = 0$ | $\sigma > 0$ |
| Vapor pressure | $p_v$ | Pa | $p_v = 0$ | $p_v > 0$ |
| Bulk modulus | $K$ | Pa | $K = \infty$ | Finite |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **kinematic viscosity** $\nu = \mu / \rho$ has units of **m²/s** and plays a critical role in Reynolds number calculations for distinguishing laminar vs. turbulent flow.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Real & Ideal Fluids Differ

The classification of fluids in KTU Module 1 is structured as follows:

- **Step 1 — Viscosity Check:** Does the fluid offer internal resistance to flow?
  - *No* → Ideal fluid (theoretical)
  - *Yes* → Real fluid (practical)
- **Step 2 — Viscosity Variation Check (for real fluids):** Does viscosity vary with the rate of shear?
  - *Constant* → **Newtonian fluid** (obeys Newton's law of viscosity)
  - *Variable* → **Non-Newtonian fluid**
- **Step 3 — Compressibility Check:** Does density change under applied pressure?
  - *Negligibly small change* → Treat as incompressible (e.g., water below 100 m head)
  - *Significant change* → Compressible fluid (e.g., air, gases at high speeds)

## 2.2 Newton's Law of Viscosity (Foundation Concept)

For a real fluid in laminar flow, the **shear stress** $\tau$ between adjacent fluid layers is directly proportional to the **velocity gradient** $du/dy$ perpendicular to the flow direction.

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress at the fluid layer (Pa or N/m²)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\frac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient (s⁻¹) — change of velocity $u$ with respect to perpendicular distance $y$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Fluids that obey this linear relationship are called **Newtonian fluids** (e.g., water, air, oil, gasoline). Fluids that do not obey this law (e.g., blood, mud, toothpaste, paint) are **Non-Newtonian fluids** and follow power-law or other rheological models.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Unit |
|---|-------------------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's law of viscosity | Pa |
| 2 | $\mu = \rho \cdot \nu$ | Dynamic viscosity = density × kinematic viscosity | Pa·s |
| 3 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity definition | m²/s |
| 4 | $F = \mu \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Viscous force on a fluid layer (Newton's viscous force) | N |
| 5 | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | Mass density (mass per unit volume) | kg/m³ |
| 6 | $S = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$ | Specific gravity (dimensionless ratio) | — |
| 7 | $\gamma = \rho \cdot g$ | Specific weight (weight per unit volume) | N/m³ |
| 8 | $\beta = -\dfrac{1}{V} \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial p}$ | Compressibility coefficient | Pa⁻¹ |
| 9 | $K = \dfrac{1}{\beta} = -V \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial V}$ | Bulk modulus of elasticity | Pa |
| 10 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number (laminar/turbulent indicator) | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Exam Pitfall:** Students often confuse **dynamic viscosity $\mu$** with **kinematic viscosity $\nu$**. Remember: $\mu$ has units **Pa·s** (resistance to flow), while $\nu$ has units **m²/s** (ratio used in momentum diffusion problems).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding real vs. ideal fluid behavior is **fundamental** in:
- **Civil Engineering:** Open channel flow design (real fluids), spillway hydraulics
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Lubrication systems (viscous oils), pipe flow networks
- **Aerospace Engineering:** Airfoil design (low-viscosity air approximated as ideal)
- **Chemical Engineering:** Pumping of slurries (non-Newtonian fluids)
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Blood flow modeling in arteries (non-Newtonian)
- **Hydraulic Machines:** Turbine blade design, pump performance curves

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study ideal fluids if they don't exist?** Because they allow us to derive the **Bernoulli's equation** and **Euler's equation of motion** without the complex viscous term. The results are then corrected for real-fluid effects using empirical coefficients (e.g., friction factor $f$ in Darcy-Weisbach equation).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Deriving the Viscous Shear Force from Newton's Law

We will derive the expression for the **viscous force on a fluid element** between two parallel layers, which is the foundation of real fluid analysis.

**Given:**
- Two parallel fluid layers separated by a perpendicular distance $dy$
- Lower layer has velocity $u$ and upper layer has velocity $u + du$
- Area of contact between layers = $A$

**Step 1:** Apply Newton's law of viscosity at the interface:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

This text row explains: Shear stress equals dynamic viscosity times the velocity gradient between the two layers.

**Step 2:** The shear force $F$ acting on the area $A$ is the shear stress times the area:

$$F = \tau \cdot A$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the expression for $\tau$ from Step 1 into the equation:

$$F = \mu \frac{du}{dy} \cdot A$$

**Step 4:** Express the velocity difference $du$ in terms of small finite increment (for practical calculations):

$$du = \left( \frac{du}{dy} \right) dy$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into the force expression:

$$F = \mu A \frac{du}{dy}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** The viscous (shear) force transmitted between two parallel fluid layers is **directly proportional** to:
> (a) Dynamic viscosity $\mu$
> (b) Contact area $A$
> (c) Velocity gradient $du/dy$

This force always **opposes** the relative motion between layers.

## 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between Dynamic and Kinematic Viscosity

**Starting Definition:** Kinematic viscosity is defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity to mass density:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**Cross-multiplying** the above expression to obtain dynamic viscosity in terms of kinematic viscosity:

$$\mu = \rho \cdot \nu$$

**Verification of Units:**
- $\rho$ has units $\text{kg/m}^3$
- $\nu$ has units $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- Product $\rho \cdot \nu$ has units $\dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3} \cdot \dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}} = \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s}} = \text{Pa·s}$ ✓

This confirms that $\mu$ indeed has units of **Pa·s**, as required for the viscous force equation.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Summary Table

| Property | Ideal Fluid | Real Fluid (Newtonian) | Real Fluid (Non-Newtonian) |
|----------|-------------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| Viscosity | $\mu = 0$ | $\mu = \text{constant} > 0$ | $\mu = f\left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)$ |
| Density | Constant | Nearly constant (often assumed) | Variable in some cases |
| Shear stress | $\tau = 0$ | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | $\tau = K \left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)^n$ |
| Compressibility | Zero | Small but finite | Varies |
| Surface tension | Zero | Finite | Finite |
| Examples | Mathematical construct | Water, air, oil | Blood, paint, mud |
| Governing equation | Euler's equation | Navier-Stokes equation | Modified constitutive models |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The transition from *ideal* to *real* fluid analysis requires adding the **viscous term** to Euler's equation to get the **Navier-Stokes equation**. This is a frequently asked derivation in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fluid Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["FLUIDS"] --> nodeB["IDEAL FLUID"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["REAL FLUIDS"]
    nodeB --> nodeB1["Zero Viscosity"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["Incompressible"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["No Surface Tension"]
    nodeB --> nodeB4["Theoretical Construct"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["NEWTONIAN FLUIDS"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["NON NEWTONIAN FLUIDS"]
    nodeD --> nodeD1["Water"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["Air"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3["Oil"]
    nodeD --> nodeD4["Gasoline"]
    nodeE --> nodeE1["Pseudoplastic"]
    nodeE --> nodeE2["Dilatant"]
    nodeE --> nodeE3["Bingham Plastic"]
    nodeE --> nodeE4["Thixotropic"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeApp["Applications: Pipe flow, turbines, pumps"]
    nodeD2 --> nodeApp
    nodeD3 --> nodeApp
    nodeD4 --> nodeApp
    nodeE1 --> nodeApp
    nodeE2 --> nodeApp
    nodeE3 --> nodeApp
    nodeE4 --> nodeApp
    nodeB4 --> nodeTheory["Used to derive Euler Equation and Bernoulli Equation"]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Newton's Law of Viscosity — Velocity Profile Between Parallel Plates

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    plateTop["Upper Plate Moving with Velocity U"]
    plateBot["Lower Plate Stationary"]
    yAxis["y axis perpendicular to flow"]
    uAxis["u axis along flow direction"]
    fluidLayer["Fluid Layers with Linear Velocity Profile"]
    shearForce["Shear Stress tau equals mu times du over dy"]
    plateTop --> fluidLayer
    plateBot --> fluidLayer
    fluidLayer --> yAxis
    fluidLayer --> uAxis
    fluidLayer --> shearForce
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: From Ideal to Real Fluid Modeling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1["Step 1: Assume Ideal Fluid"] --> step2["Step 2: Apply Euler Equation of Motion"]
    step2 --> step3["Step 3: Integrate to get Bernoulli Equation"]
    step3 --> step4["Step 4: Add Viscous Term to get Navier Stokes Equation"]
    step4 --> step5["Step 5: Solve for Real Fluid with Empirical Friction Factors"]
    step5 --> step6["Step 6: Validate using Experimental Data"]
    step1 --> stepA["Assumptions: Incompressible, Inviscid, Steady"]
    step4 --> stepB["Add term mu times Laplacian of velocity"]
    step5 --> stepC["Use Darcy Weisbach or Hazen Williams formula"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The Mermaid flowcharts above are designed to be mentally visualizable. Block 1 shows the fluid classification tree. Block 2 illustrates the physical setup of Newton's viscosity experiment. Block 3 shows the engineering workflow of progressing from ideal to real fluid analysis — exactly as taught in KTU Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define an ideal fluid. List any four of its assumed properties.**

**Model Answer:**
An ideal fluid is a hypothetical fluid that does not actually exist but is assumed for the purpose of mathematical analysis of fluid flow problems. The four assumed properties of an ideal fluid are:
1. It is **inviscid** (zero viscosity, no internal friction).
2. It is **incompressible** (density is constant regardless of pressure).
3. It has **zero surface tension**.
4. It possesses **zero vapor pressure**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Word limit tip:** KTU expects 4 distinct points for full 3 marks. Avoid repeating the same idea in different words.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Newtonian Fluid | Non-Newtonian Fluid |
|---------|----------------|---------------------|
| Viscosity behavior | Constant, independent of shear rate | Variable, depends on shear rate |
| Shear stress relation | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ (linear) | $\tau = K \left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)^n$ (non-linear) |
| Example | Water, air, oil | Blood, toothpaste, paint |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** State Newton's law of viscosity. Derive the expression for viscous force between two parallel fluid layers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flat plate of area $0.5 \text{ m}^2$ is pulled with a velocity of $0.3 \text{ m/s}$ through a thin oil film of thickness $1.5 \text{ mm}$ sandwiched between the plate and a fixed surface. The dynamic viscosity of oil is $0.9 \text{ Pa·s}$. Calculate: (i) the shear stress developed, and (ii) the viscous drag force on the plate. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Statement of Newton's Law of Viscosity:**
The shear stress $\tau$ developed in a real fluid is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain (velocity gradient) and is given by:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where $\mu$ is the **dynamic viscosity** of the fluid (a constant of proportionality for Newtonian fluids).

**Derivation of Viscous Force:**

Consider two parallel fluid layers separated by an infinitesimal distance $dy$. The lower layer is stationary and the upper layer moves with velocity $u$ relative to the lower layer.

The shear stress at the interface (from Newton's law):

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

The viscous force on area $A$ is:

$$F = \tau \cdot A = \mu A \frac{du}{dy}$$

For a finite thickness $h$ between the plates, with the upper plate moving at velocity $U$:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{h}$$

Therefore:

$$F = \mu A \frac{U}{h}$$

This is the expression for the **viscous drag force** between two parallel fluid layers.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Area of plate, $A = 0.5 \text{ m}^2$
- Velocity of plate, $U = 0.3 \text{ m/s}$
- Oil film thickness, $h = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 0.0015 \text{ m}$
- Dynamic viscosity, $\mu = 0.9 \text{ Pa·s}$

**(i) Shear Stress:**

Velocity gradient:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{h} = \frac{0.3}{0.0015} = 200 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

Shear stress:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = 0.9 \times 200 = 180 \text{ Pa}$$

**(ii) Viscous Drag Force:**

$$F = \tau \cdot A = 180 \times 0.5 = 90 \text{ N}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** Shear stress $\tau = 180 \text{ Pa}$; Viscous drag force $F = 90 \text{ N}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative to Question A)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Define and explain the terms: (i) Dynamic viscosity, (ii) Kinematic viscosity, and (iii) Specific gravity. State their SI units. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A liquid has a kinematic viscosity of $4.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ and a specific gravity of $0.86$. Calculate: (i) the dynamic viscosity in Pa·s, and (ii) the dynamic viscosity in poise. Given: density of water = $1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $1 \text{ poise} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s}$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**(i) Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$):**
It is defined as the shear stress developed per unit velocity gradient in a Newtonian fluid. It represents the fluid's internal resistance to flow.

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{du/dy}$$

**SI Unit:** Pa·s (or N·s/m²)

**(ii) Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):**
It is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to the mass density of the fluid. It represents the diffusion of momentum in the fluid.

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**SI Unit:** m²/s

**(iii) Specific Gravity ($S$):**
It is the dimensionless ratio of the density of a fluid to the density of water at standard conditions (4°C).

$$S = \frac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$$

**Unit:** Dimensionless (no SI unit)

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Kinematic viscosity, $\nu = 4.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- Specific gravity, $S = 0.86$
- Density of water, $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Calculate density of the liquid:**

$$\rho_{\text{fluid}} = S \times \rho_w = 0.86 \times 1000 = 860 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Calculate dynamic viscosity in Pa·s:**

Using $\mu = \rho \cdot \nu$:

$$\mu = 860 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-5} = 0.0387 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to poise:**

$$1 \text{ poise} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s}$$

$$\mu = \frac{0.0387}{0.1} = 0.387 \text{ poise}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $\mu = 0.0387 \text{ Pa·s} = 0.387 \text{ poise}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Always convert mm to m and poise to Pa·s before substituting. Marks are deducted heavily for inconsistent units.
> 2. **Confusing $\mu$ and $\nu$:** Many students write $\mu = \nu \cdot \rho$ incorrectly. The correct relation is $\mu = \rho \cdot \nu$ (memorize this).
> 3. **Forgetting to state the law in part (a):** Just writing the formula is not enough; the **statement** of Newton's law is mandatory for full marks.
> 4. **No intermediate steps:** The KTU board examiner expects clear step-by-step calculations. Skipping steps in numerical problems can cost 2–3 marks easily.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Ideal fluid** is a *theoretical* fluid with **zero viscosity, incompressible, zero surface tension**, and **zero vapor pressure**. It **does not exist** in nature.
- ✅ **Real fluid** is what we actually deal with — water, air, oil, etc. It has **viscosity, finite compressibility, surface tension**, and **vapor pressure**.
- ✅ **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ — applicable only to **Newtonian fluids**.
- ✅ **Dynamic viscosity $\mu$** has units **Pa·s**; **Kinematic viscosity $\nu = \mu/\rho$** has units **m²/s**.
- ✅ **1 poise = 0.1 Pa·s**; **1 stoke = 10⁻⁴ m²/s** (CGS units — be ready for conversion questions).
- ✅ **Newtonian fluids** = water, air, oil, gasoline (constant viscosity).
- ✅ **Non-Newtonian fluids** = blood, paint, mud, toothpaste (variable viscosity).
- ✅ **Specific gravity** is dimensionless; **density** has unit **kg/m³**; **specific weight** $\gamma = \rho g$ has unit **N/m³**.
- ✅ **Reynolds number** $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ — distinguishes laminar ($Re < 2000$) from turbulent ($Re > 4000$) flow in pipes.
- ✅ For KTU numericals, always **draw a free-body diagram or schematic**, then **list the givens**, then **substitute with units**.
- ✅ The **continuum hypothesis** assumes fluid properties vary continuously in space and time — fundamental to all fluid mechanics derivations.
- ✅ The transition from **Euler's equation** (ideal) to **Navier-Stokes equation** (real) is achieved by **adding the viscous term** — a high-yield KTU question.
- ✅ For a moving plate problem: $F = \mu A \dfrac{U}{h}$ — memorize this formula pattern.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incompressible and Compressible Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Incompressible and Compressible Fluids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In fluid mechanics, every real fluid is, to some extent, **compressible** — meaning its density changes when pressure is applied. However, for engineering analysis, fluids are broadly classified into two practical categories based on how significantly their density changes during flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A fluid is classified as **incompressible** if its density remains essentially constant during flow ($\rho = \text{constant}$). It is classified as **compressible** if its density varies appreciably with pressure ($\rho \neq \text{constant}$).

### Incompressible Fluid
A fluid whose density does **not change** appreciably with a change in pressure or temperature during the flow process. Mathematically:
$$\rho = \text{constant} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{D\rho}{Dt} = 0$$

Typical examples: **water** ($\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$), oils, mercury, and other liquids under normal flow conditions.

### Compressible Fluid
A fluid whose density **varies significantly** with pressure and/or temperature during the flow process. Mathematically:
$$\rho \neq \text{constant} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{D\rho}{Dt} \neq 0$$

Typical examples: **air** ($\rho \approx 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at STP), all gases, steam, natural gas, and high-velocity vapours.

## 1.2 The Mach Number Criterion (The Real Engineering Rule)

In practice, we cannot decide compressibility just by saying "it's a gas" or "it's a liquid." The universally accepted **KTU Board** and industry criterion uses the **Mach Number ($M$)** — the ratio of flow velocity to the speed of sound in that fluid:

$$M = \frac{V}{c}$$

where $V$ = velocity of the fluid flow, and $c$ = speed of sound in the fluid medium.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule (valid in 99% of KTU numericals):**
> - If $M < 0.3$ → treat the flow as **incompressible** (density change < 5%).
> - If $M \geq 0.3$ → treat the flow as **compressible**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Balloon Analogy:**
> Imagine trying to squeeze two balloons — one filled with **water** and one filled with **air**.
> - The **water-filled balloon** feels rock-hard. Even with enormous hand pressure, its volume barely changes. This is an **incompressible fluid** in action.
> - The **air-filled balloon** compresses very easily — push gently and it deforms significantly. This is a **compressible fluid**.
>
> **Why does this matter?** When designing a pipe carrying water (incompressible), we assume volume in = volume out, so $A_1V_1 = A_2V_2$. For a jet engine's air intake (compressible), this assumption fails — we must account for density changes using $A_1 \rho_1 V_1 = A_2 \rho_2 V_2$.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Properties

| Property | Symbol | Typical Value (Water at 20°C) | Typical Value (Air at STP) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density | $\rho$ | **1000 kg/m³** | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| Bulk Modulus | $K$ | **2.1 × 10⁹ Pa** | **1.01 × 10⁵ Pa** (isothermal) |
| Speed of Sound | $c$ | **≈ 1480 m/s** | **≈ 343 m/s** |

The stark contrast in bulk modulus (a factor of **~20,000×**) explains why liquids behave as nearly incompressible while gases do not.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bulk Modulus of Elasticity ($K$) — The Compressibility Indicator

The **bulk modulus of elasticity** ($K$ or $E_v$) quantifies a fluid's resistance to uniform compression. It is defined as the ratio of incremental pressure to the corresponding fractional decrease in volume:

$$K = -V \frac{dP}{dV} = \rho \frac{dP}{d\rho}$$

- A **high $K$** → fluid is **stiff** (hard to compress) → e.g., water.
- A **low $K$** → fluid is **soft** (easy to compress) → e.g., air.

The reciprocal of bulk modulus is called the **compressibility coefficient** ($\tau$):
$$\tau = \frac{1}{K}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Distinction:**
> - $K_{\text{water}} = 2.1 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$ → practically incompressible.
> - $K_{\text{air}} = 1.01 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$ → highly compressible.

## 2.2 Bulk Modulus for Ideal Gases

For an ideal gas ($p = \rho R T$), the bulk modulus depends on the **thermodynamic process**:

| Process | Relation | Bulk Modulus $K$ | Speed of Sound $c$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Isothermal** (slow, heat exchanged) | $pV = \text{constant}$ | $K = p$ | $c = \sqrt{RT}$ |
| **Adiabatic/Isentropic** (fast, no heat exchange) | $pV^\gamma = \text{constant}$ | $K = \gamma p$ | $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ |

For air: $\gamma = 1.4$ (ratio of specific heats), $R = 287 \text{ J/kg·K}$.

## 2.3 Speed of Sound in a Fluid

The speed of sound $c$ in any fluid is given by Newton's classical formula:
$$c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}}$$

This is a **fundamental relationship** — it connects compressibility directly to wave propagation speed.

## 2.4 Classification of Flow Based on Mach Number

| Mach Number Range | Flow Regime | Compressibility Treatment |
|---|---|---|
| $M < 0.3$ | Subsonic (low-speed) | **Incompressible** flow |
| $0.3 \leq M < 1$ | Subsonic (high-speed) | Compressible flow |
| $M = 1$ | Sonic (Mach 1) | Compressible flow |
| $1 < M < 5$ | Supersonic | Compressible flow |
| $M > 5$ | Hypersonic | Compressible flow |

## 2.5 Continuity Equation — Incompressible vs Compressible

| Form | Incompressible | Compressible (Steady) |
|---|---|---|
| Differential | $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ | $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ |
| Pipe Flow | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic Machines (Turbines, Pumps)**: Water is treated as incompressible. This simplifies Bernoulli's equation and continuity.
- **Aerospace & Jet Engines**: Air at high speeds (M > 0.3) must be treated as compressible — shocks, expansion fans, and density waves appear.
- **Gas Pipelines (ONGC, GAIL projects)**: Natural gas is highly compressible; pipeline design uses the Weymouth/Panhandle equations.
- **Weather Balloons & Rocket Nozzles**: Density changes must be modelled precisely for accurate trajectories.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:**
> Most B.Tech-level problems in Fluid Mechanics and Hydraulic Machines deal with **water** and therefore use the **incompressible** assumption. The moment the problem mentions **gas, steam, or high-velocity air**, switch to compressible analysis using Mach number.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K = -V \dfrac{dP}{dV}$ | Bulk modulus definition | Pa or N/m² |
| 2 | $K = \rho \dfrac{dP}{d\rho}$ | Bulk modulus in terms of density | Pa |
| 3 | $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\rho}}$ | Speed of sound in fluid | m/s |
| 4 | $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | Speed of sound in ideal gas (adiabatic) | m/s |
| 5 | $M = \dfrac{V}{c}$ | Mach number | dimensionless |
| 6 | $M < 0.3$ | Incompressible flow criterion | — |
| 7 | $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ | Continuity for compressible flow | kg/s |
| 8 | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | Continuity for incompressible flow | m³/s |
| 9 | $p = \rho R T$ | Ideal gas equation | Pa |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Speed of Sound in a Fluid (Newton–Laplace Equation)

This is a **favourite 7-mark derivation** in KTU exams.

### Step 1 — Set Up the Physical Picture
Consider a sound wave travelling through a fluid at speed $c$. Inside the wave, a fluid element of volume $V$ and density $\rho$ experiences a small pressure change $dP$, which causes a corresponding density change $d\rho$.

By the definition of bulk modulus:
$$K = \frac{dP}{-\dfrac{dV}{V}}$$

### Step 2 — Relate Volume Change to Density Change
For a fluid element of mass $m = \rho V = \text{constant}$ (mass is conserved within the element):
$$\rho V = \text{constant}$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to time:
$$\rho \, dV + V \, d\rho = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dV}{V} = -\frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Back into Bulk Modulus Expression
$$K = \frac{dP}{-\left(-\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho}\right)} = \rho \frac{dP}{d\rho}$$

This is a **key identity** in fluid mechanics — the bulk modulus in terms of density:
$$\boxed{K = \rho \frac{dP}{d\rho}}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Continuity to a Sound Wave
Consider a sound wave moving through a stationary fluid with velocity $c$. In a reference frame moving with the wave, the fluid appears to enter the wave at velocity $c$ and leave at velocity $c + dV$, with the density changing from $\rho$ to $\rho + d\rho$.

Mass flow in = Mass flow out:
$$\rho \, c \, A = (\rho + d\rho)(c + dV)A$$

Expanding (neglecting higher-order infinitesimal $d\rho \, dV$):
$$\rho c = \rho c + \rho \, dV + c \, d\rho$$
$$0 = \rho \, dV + c \, d\rho$$
$$dV = -\frac{c \, d\rho}{\rho}$$

### Step 5 — Apply Momentum (Newton's Second Law) to the Wave
The pressure difference across the wave front accelerates the fluid:
$$-dP \cdot A = (\rho A \, c) \cdot dV$$

The negative sign indicates that pressure decreases as velocity increases (expansion). Substituting the value of $dV$:
$$-dP \cdot A = \rho A c \left(-\frac{c \, d\rho}{\rho}\right) = -A c^2 \, d\rho$$

$$dP = c^2 \, d\rho$$

### Step 6 — Combine the Two Expressions for $dP$
From Step 3: $dP = \dfrac{K}{\rho} d\rho$
From Step 5: $dP = c^2 \, d\rho$

Equating:
$$c^2 = \frac{K}{\rho}$$

$$\boxed{c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}}}$$

This is the **Newton–Laplace equation** for the speed of sound.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Speed of Sound in an Ideal Gas (Adiabatic Process)

### Step 1 — Start from the Adiabatic Equation
For an adiabatic (isentropic) process in an ideal gas:
$$pV^\gamma = \text{constant}$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate Both Sides
$$V^\gamma \, dP + \gamma p V^{\gamma-1} \, dV = 0$$

Dividing by $V^{\gamma-1}$:
$$V \, dP + \gamma p \, dV = 0$$
$$\frac{dP}{dV} = -\frac{\gamma p}{V}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Density Form
Since $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$, we have $\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} = -\dfrac{dV}{V}$. Therefore:
$$\frac{dP}{d\rho} = \gamma p$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Speed of Sound Formula
$$c^2 = \frac{dP}{d\rho} = \gamma p$$

$$\boxed{c = \sqrt{\gamma p / \rho}}$$

### Step 5 — Use the Ideal Gas Law $p = \rho R T$
$$c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma \rho R T}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$$

$$\boxed{c = \sqrt{\gamma R T} \quad \text{(Speed of sound in an ideal gas)}}$$

**Numerical Check for Air at 15°C (288 K):**
$$c = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 288} = \sqrt{115{,}718} \approx 340.2 \text{ m/s} \checkmark$$

This matches the experimentally measured value of ~340 m/s — a critical validation.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** Air flows through a duct at $V = 180 \text{ m/s}$. The static temperature is $T = 40^\circ\text{C}$. Determine whether the flow can be treated as incompressible. Given: $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287 \text{ J/kg·K}$.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Convert temperature to Kelvin.
$$T = 40 + 273 = 313 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the speed of sound in air (adiabatic process).
$$c = \sqrt{\gamma R T} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 313}$$

$$c = \sqrt{125{,}751.4} \approx 354.6 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the Mach number.
$$M = \frac{V}{c} = \frac{180}{354.6} \approx 0.508$$

**Step 4:** Apply the criterion.
Since $M = 0.508 > 0.3$, the flow **cannot** be treated as incompressible. It must be analysed using **compressible flow** equations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Students often forget to convert °C to Kelvin — this single error costs 1–2 marks in KTU valuation.

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Property | Incompressible Fluid | Compressible Fluid |
|---|---|---|
| Density $\rho$ | Constant | Varies with $P, T$ |
| Bulk Modulus $K$ | Very large ($\sim 10^9$ Pa) | Small ($\sim 10^5$ Pa for gases) |
| Speed of Sound $c$ | Very high ($\sim 1500$ m/s for water) | Lower ($\sim 340$ m/s for air) |
| Mach Number $M$ | $M < 0.3$ | $M \geq 0.3$ |
| Continuity | $AV = \text{constant}$ | $\rho AV = \text{constant}$ |
| Examples | Water, oil, mercury | Air, steam, natural gas |
| Governing Equations | Bernoulli + continuity | Compressible Euler/Navier–Stokes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fluid Classification Flowchart

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **complete decision logic** for classifying a fluid flow as compressible or incompressible — a critical skill tested in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["START: Given a fluid flow problem"] --> propA["Identify the fluid: liquid or gas?"]
    propA --> liquidCheck{"Is the fluid a liquid?"}
    propA --> gasCheck{"Is the fluid a gas?"}

    liquidCheck --> liqCheck{"Velocity V less than 0.3 times speed of sound?"}
    liqCheck -- YES --> liqResult["INCOMPRESSIBLE FLOW: Density constant, rho equals 1000 kg per cubic metre for water"]
    liqCheck -- NO --> liqComp["Treat as COMPRESSIBLE only at extreme shock conditions"]

    gasCheck --> velCheck{"Compute Mach Number M equals V over c"}
    velCheck --> machVal{"Compare M with 0.3 threshold"}

    machVal -- M LESS THAN 0.3 --> subResult["SUBSONIC: Practically INCOMPRESSIBLE FLOW: Use A1 V1 equals A2 V2"]
    machVal -- M EQUALS 0.3 TO 1 --> subCom["SUBSONIC COMPRESSIBLE: Use rho1 A1 V1 equals rho2 A2 V2"]
    machVal -- M EQUALS 1 --> sonicRes["SONIC FLOW: Choked flow condition, use compressible isentropic relations"]
    machVal -- M GREATER THAN 1 --> superRes["SUPERSONIC FLOW: Shock waves and expansion fans present, requires compressible analysis"]
    machVal -- M GREATER THAN 5 --> hyperRes["HYPERSONIC FLOW: Real gas effects and high temperature phenomena dominate"]

    liqResult --> endA["END: Choose Bernoulli plus continuity equations"]
    liqComp --> endA
    subResult --> endA
    subCom --> endB["END: Use compressible flow tables and isentropic relations"]
    sonicRes --> endB
    superRes --> endB
    hyperRes --> endB

    style startA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style endA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style endB fill:#FF7F7F,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style liqResult fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style subResult fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Speed of Sound — Concept Architecture

This diagram explains **how bulk modulus and density jointly determine** the speed of sound, which in turn sets the threshold for the compressibility decision.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpA["Bulk Modulus K of fluid"] --> speedCalc["Speed of Sound c equals sqrt K over rho"]
    inpB["Density rho of fluid"] --> speedCalc

    speedCalc --> machOut["Mach Number M equals V over c"]

    machOut --> critA{"M less than 0.3?"}
    critA -- YES --> outA["INCOMPRESSIBLE FLOW REGIME: Bernoulli equation valid"]
    critA -- NO --> outB["COMPRESSIBLE FLOW REGIME: Use compressible Euler equations"]

    outA --> applA["APPLICATION: Water supply, turbines, pumps, hydraulic presses"]
    outB --> applB["APPLICATION: Jet engines, rockets, gas pipelines, supersonic nozzles"]

    style inpA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style inpB fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style speedCalc fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style outA fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style outB fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style applA fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style applB fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Similar Pattern]`

**Define Mach number. State its significance in classifying a fluid flow as compressible or incompressible.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Mach number ($M$)** is defined as the ratio of the velocity of the fluid ($V$) to the local speed of sound ($c$) in that fluid:
$$M = \frac{V}{c}$$

**Significance:**
- It is a **dimensionless number** that quantifies how compressible a flow is.
- When $M < 0.3$, the density variation in the flow is less than 5%, and the flow is treated as **incompressible**.
- When $M \geq 0.3$, density changes become significant, and the flow must be treated as **compressible** (subsonic, sonic, supersonic, or hypersonic).
- It is the primary criterion used in aerodynamics, jet propulsion, and gas dynamics.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]`

**Define bulk modulus of elasticity. What is its value for water and air?**

**Model Answer:**

The **bulk modulus of elasticity ($K$)** of a fluid is defined as the ratio of the change in pressure ($dP$) to the corresponding volumetric strain ($-\dfrac{dV}{V}$):

$$K = -\frac{dP}{dV/V} = \rho \frac{dP}{d\rho}$$

**Values:**
- For **water** at 20°C: $K \approx 2.1 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$ (very high — water is nearly incompressible).
- For **air** at STP (isothermal): $K \approx 1.01 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$ (very low — air is highly compressible).

A high bulk modulus means the fluid strongly resists compression.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1 Pattern]`

**(a) Derive an expression for the speed of sound in a fluid medium. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The detailed derivation is already presented in Section 3.1. Key steps to write:

1. Write bulk modulus definition: $K = -V \dfrac{dP}{dV}$.
2. Use mass conservation $\rho V = \text{constant}$ to get $\dfrac{dV}{V} = -\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho}$.
3. Substitute to obtain $K = \rho \dfrac{dP}{d\rho}$.
4. Apply continuity across a sound wave front: $dV = -\dfrac{c \, d\rho}{\rho}$.
5. Apply momentum equation: $-dP \cdot A = \rho A c \, dV$.
6. Combine to obtain $dP = c^2 \, d\rho$.
7. Equate with Step 3: $c^2 = \dfrac{K}{\rho}$.

$$\boxed{c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}}}$$

**(b) Air at 27°C flows through a duct at 200 m/s. Calculate the Mach number and comment on the compressibility of the flow. Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/kg·K. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert temperature to Kelvin.
$$T = 27 + 273 = 300 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the speed of sound in air (adiabatic process).
$$c = \sqrt{\gamma R T} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 300}$$

Performing the multiplication:
$$1.4 \times 287 = 401.8$$
$$401.8 \times 300 = 120{,}540$$

$$c = \sqrt{120{,}540} \approx 347.19 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the Mach number.
$$M = \frac{V}{c} = \frac{200}{347.19} \approx 0.576$$

**Step 4:** Comment on compressibility.
Since $M = 0.576 > 0.3$, the flow **cannot be treated as incompressible**. It lies in the **subsonic compressible** regime and must be analysed using compressible flow equations. The density change is significant enough to invalidate the constant-density assumption.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. Many students forget to convert °C to Kelvin. This loses 1 mark immediately.
> 2. Do not use isothermal formula $c = \sqrt{RT}$ for sound — sound waves propagate adiabatically. Always use $c = \sqrt{\gamma RT}$.
> 3. Students often write $M = 0.576$ but forget to compare with the 0.3 threshold and conclude compressibility — losing the final 1 mark.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1 Pattern]` **(ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)**

**(a) Explain the difference between compressible and incompressible fluids with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Tabular Comparison in Descriptive Form):**

| Aspect | Incompressible Fluid | Compressible Fluid |
|---|---|---|
| Density | Constant during flow | Varies with pressure and temperature |
| Bulk Modulus | Very high (water: $2.1 \times 10^9$ Pa) | Low (air: $1.01 \times 10^5$ Pa) |
| Mach Number | $M < 0.3$ | $M \geq 0.3$ |
| Continuity | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ |
| Typical Examples | Water, oil, mercury, hydraulic fluids | Air, steam, natural gas, nitrogen |
| Engineering Use | Hydraulic turbines, pumps, dams | Jet engines, rockets, gas pipelines |
| Governing Equations | Simple Bernoulli + continuity | Compressible Euler/isentropic flow |

**Examples:**
- **Incompressible:** Water flowing through a penstock to a Pelton wheel turbine in a hydroelectric power station. Density remains $1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
- **Compressible:** Air entering the diffuser of a jet engine at high altitude and speed. Density changes significantly due to pressure and temperature variations.

**(b) The bulk modulus of elasticity of a liquid is $2.5 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$. Find the speed of sound in the liquid if its density is 1000 kg/m³. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the given data.
$$K = 2.5 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2, \quad \rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Apply the speed of sound formula.
$$c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values.
$$c = \sqrt{\frac{2.5 \times 10^9}{1000}} = \sqrt{2.5 \times 10^6}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the numerical result.
$$c = \sqrt{2{,}500{,}000} \approx 1581.14 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{c \approx 1581 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation.
This speed is significantly higher than that in air (~340 m/s), confirming that liquids are much stiffer media for sound propagation. Such a fluid can be confidently treated as **incompressible** in any hydraulic machinery problem.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. Unit mismatch — bulk modulus must be in Pa (= N/m²), not kPa or MPa. Convert before substitution.
> 2. Don't confuse density in g/cm³ with kg/m³ — water density is **1000 kg/m³**, not 1.
> 3. Don't forget the square root at the end — a common last-step slip that costs full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Incompressible and Compressible Fluids**

- [x] **Definition:** Incompressible fluid → $\rho = \text{constant}$; Compressible fluid → $\rho \neq \text{constant}$.
- [x] **Bulk Modulus:** $K = -V \dfrac{dP}{dV} = \rho \dfrac{dP}{d\rho}$; water $\approx 2.1 \times 10^9$ Pa, air $\approx 10^5$ Pa.
- [x] **Compressibility Coefficient:** $\tau = \dfrac{1}{K}$.
- [x] **Speed of Sound (general fluid):** $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\rho}}$.
- [x] **Speed of Sound (ideal gas, adiabatic):** $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$.
- [x] **Speed of Sound (ideal gas, isothermal):** $c = \sqrt{R T}$ (rarely used — Newton originally used this incorrectly).
- [x] **Mach Number:** $M = \dfrac{V}{c}$ — dimensionless, the universal compressibility indicator.
- [x] **Engineering Criterion:** $M < 0.3 \rightarrow$ incompressible; $M \geq 0.3 \rightarrow$ compressible.
- [x] **Flow Regimes:** Subsonic ($M < 1$), Sonic ($M = 1$), Supersonic ($1 < M < 5$), Hypersonic ($M > 5$).
- [x] **Continuity (incompressible):** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$.
- [x] **Continuity (compressible):** $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$.
- [x] **Ideal Gas Law:** $p = \rho R T$.
- [x] **Always convert °C to Kelvin** in gas problems: $T(\text{K}) = T(^\circ\text{C}) + 273$.
- [x] **Use $\gamma = 1.4$ for air**, $R = 287$ J/kg·K.
- [x] **Speed of sound in water** ≈ 1480 m/s; **in air** ≈ 340 m/s — remember these for quick MCQ elimination.
- [x] **Hydraulic machinery problems assume incompressible flow** unless explicitly stated otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid Statics: Atmospheric Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fluid Statics: Atmospheric Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Atmospheric Pressure** is defined as the hydrostatic pressure exerted by the weight of the entire column of air (the Earth's atmosphere) standing vertically above a unit area at any given point. It is the force per unit area exerted by the cumulative weight of all the gas molecules in the atmosphere above the reference surface, pulled downward by gravity.

Mathematically expressed at its core:

$$P_{atm} = \int_{z}^{\infty} \rho(h) \cdot g \, dh$$

where:
- $\rho(h)$ is the air density varying with altitude $h$
- $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $z$ is the elevation of the reference point

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Atmospheric Pressure (ISA - International Standard Atmosphere):**
> $P_{atm} = \mathbf{101.325 \, kPa} = 1.01325 \, \text{bar} = 760 \, \text{mmHg} = 14.696 \, \text{psi} = 1 \, \text{atm}$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the atmosphere as a **massive, invisible ocean of air** that surrounds our planet. Just as a diver at the bottom of a swimming pool feels immense water pressure on their ears due to the weight of water above them, every object on Earth (and every person) is constantly being squeezed by the weight of the air column extending from the ground all the way to the top of the atmosphere (~100 km up).

**Real-world examples of atmospheric pressure effects:**

| Phenomenon | Atmospheric Pressure Effect |
|------------|----------------------------|
| Drinking with a straw | Creating a partial vacuum inside the straw; atmospheric pressure pushes the liquid up |
| Suction cups sticking to walls | External atmospheric pressure pushes the cup against the wall |
| Ear "popping" at high altitudes | Pressure differential between middle ear and surroundings |
| Vacuum-sealed food packaging | External atmospheric pressure compresses the package |
| Mercury barometer reading | 760 mm column held up by atmospheric force |

> [!NOTE]
> At sea level, every square centimeter of your body experiences approximately **1.033 kgf** of air pushing down on it. We don't feel crushed because the pressure inside our body (internal fluid pressure) balances it — this is the principle of **pressure equilibrium** in living systems.

### 1.3 Why Atmospheric Pressure Matters in Engineering

- **Pump design**: Suction head of pumps is limited by atmospheric pressure (theoretical max suction lift ≈ 10.33 m of water at sea level).
- **Aerospace engineering**: Aircraft cabin pressurization compensates for low external atmospheric pressure at cruising altitudes.
- **Hydraulic systems**: Atmospheric pressure acts on fluid surfaces and must be accounted for in manometer and reservoir design.
- **Meteorology**: Weather patterns are driven by **atmospheric pressure differentials** between high and low pressure zones.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Principles Governing Atmospheric Pressure

The study of atmospheric pressure relies on three core pillars of fluid statics:

**Pillar 1: Hydrostatic Pressure Equation**
For any static fluid (including air in static equilibrium), the pressure variation with depth follows:

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$$

This is derived from force balance on a static fluid element. The negative sign indicates that pressure **decreases** as we move upward (increase in z).

**Pillar 2: Pascal's Law (Pressure Transmission)**
Pressure applied to an enclosed static fluid is transmitted undiminished to every portion of the fluid and the container walls. This law underpins the operation of all pressure-measuring devices.

**Pillar 3: Compressibility of Air**
Unlike liquids, air is highly compressible. Its density varies significantly with pressure and temperature, which means atmospheric pressure does **not** vary linearly with altitude (unlike hydrostatic water pressure).

### 2.2 Variation of Atmospheric Pressure with Altitude

The variation depends on the assumed thermal model of the atmosphere:

**Case A: Isothermal Atmosphere (Temperature constant with altitude)**
This is the simpler model, often used in basic KTU problems.

$$P = P_0 \cdot e^{-\frac{\rho_0 g (z - z_0)}{P_0}}$$

Or using universal gas law substitution:

$$P = P_0 \cdot e^{-\frac{M g (z - z_0)}{R T}}$$

**Case B: Adiabatic/Real Atmosphere (Temperature decreases linearly with altitude)**
Used in the **International Standard Atmosphere (ISA)** model. Temperature varies as:

$$T = T_0 - L \cdot z$$

where $L$ is the **temperature lapse rate** = **6.5 K/km** (troposphere).

$$P = P_0 \left[1 - \frac{L(z - z_0)}{T_0}\right]^{\frac{gM}{RL}}$$

### 2.3 Measurement of Atmospheric Pressure

**Primary Instrument: Mercury Barometer (Invented by Torricelli, 1643)**
- A glass tube (≥ 760 mm long) filled with mercury, inverted into a mercury reservoir.
- The mercury column height represents atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm} = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$
- At standard conditions: $h = 760$ mm = 0.76 m

**Secondary Instrument: Aneroid Barometer**
- Uses a sealed, partially evacuated metal capsule that deforms with pressure changes.
- Mechanical linkage converts deformation to pointer movement.

**Working Fluid Equivalents** (for manometer-based pressure measurement):

| Fluid | Density (kg/m³) | Height of 1 atm column |
|-------|-----------------|------------------------|
| Mercury (Hg) | 13,600 | 760 mm |
| Water (H₂O) | 1,000 | 10.33 m |
| Oil (typical) | 850 | 12.15 m |
| Glycerin | 1,260 | 8.20 m |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $P_{atm} = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ (incompressible) | Pa |
| 2 | $\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$ | Hydrostatic pressure gradient | Pa/m |
| 3 | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | Absolute pressure at depth $h$ below free surface | Pa |
| 4 | $P_{gage} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ | Gage pressure relationship | Pa |
| 5 | $P_v = P_{abs} - P_{gage}$ | Vacuum pressure | Pa |
| 6 | $P = P_0 \, e^{-\rho_0 g z / P_0}$ | Isothermal atmosphere (variable $\rho$ model) | Pa |
| 7 | $P = P_0 \, e^{-Mgz/(RT)}$ | Isothermal barometric formula | Pa |
| 8 | $P = P_0 \left[1 - \frac{Lz}{T_0}\right]^{gM/(RL)}$ | Adiabatic (ISA) atmospheric model | Pa |
| 9 | $P_{atm} = \rho_{Hg} \, g \, h_{Hg}$ | Barometric height relation | Pa |
| 10 | $h_{water} = 13.6 \times h_{Hg}$ | Fluid column height conversion | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Constants for KTU Problems:**
> - $g = 9.81$ m/s²
> - $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³
> - $\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600$ kg/m³
> - $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) (Universal gas constant)
> - $M_{air} = 0.02896$ kg/mol (Molar mass of air)
> - $T_0 = 288.15$ K (ISA sea level temperature = 15°C)
> - $P_0 = 101.325$ kPa (ISA sea level pressure)
> - $L = 0.0065$ K/m (Lapse rate)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Civil Engineering**: Design of water supply systems, dams, and water towers — atmospheric pressure determines the maximum theoretical suction lift.
- **Mechanical Engineering**: HVAC system design, internal combustion engine intake manifolds.
- **Aerospace Engineering**: Altimeter calibration, cabin pressurization, parachute design.
- **Chemical Engineering**: Distillation columns, vacuum systems, gas storage tanks.
- **Environmental Engineering**: Stack design for pollution dispersal (uses pressure differentials).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Hydrostatic Pressure Equation ($\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$)

**Step 1: Consider a static fluid element**
Take a small fluid element in the form of a thin slab of cross-sectional area $dA$ and thickness $dz$, located at a depth $z$ in a static fluid.

**Step 2: Identify all forces acting on the element**

Forces acting in the vertical ($z$) direction on the element:

- Pressure force on the top face: $P \cdot dA$ (acting downward)
- Pressure force on the bottom face: $(P + dP) \cdot dA$ (acting upward)
- Weight of the element: $dW = \rho g \, dA \, dz$ (acting downward)

**Step 3: Apply equilibrium condition**
For a static fluid, the net force in any direction must equal zero (Newton's first law):

$$\sum F_z = 0$$

$$(P + dP) \cdot dA - P \cdot dA - \rho g \, dA \, dz = 0$$

**Step 4: Simplify the equation**

$$P \cdot dA + dP \cdot dA - P \cdot dA - \rho g \, dA \, dz = 0$$

The $P \cdot dA$ terms cancel out:

$$dP \cdot dA - \rho g \, dA \, dz = 0$$

Dividing both sides by $dA$:

$$dP - \rho g \, dz = 0$$

**Step 5: Final form of the hydrostatic equation**

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **negative sign** is crucial: it tells us that pressure **decreases** as elevation $z$ increases. This is the foundational equation used to derive all atmospheric pressure models.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Pressure Variation in an Incompressible Fluid (Constant Density)

**Step 1: Starting equation**

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$$

**Step 2: Apply incompressibility condition**
For an incompressible fluid, $\rho$ = constant. So we can separate variables and integrate.

$$dP = -\rho g \, dz$$

**Step 3: Integrate from the free surface (point 0) to depth $h$**

$$\int_{P_0}^{P} dP = -\rho g \int_{0}^{h} dz$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the integrals**

$$P - P_0 = -\rho g (h - 0)$$

**Step 5: Final result**

$$P = P_0 - \rho g h$$

But since depth $h$ is conventionally measured **downward** (so $z = -h$ in the original equation):

$$\boxed{P = P_0 + \rho g h} \quad \blacksquare$$

where $P_0$ is the surface pressure (e.g., atmospheric pressure at the free surface) and $P$ is the absolute pressure at depth $h$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Barometric Formula for Isothermal Atmosphere

**Step 1: Starting equation**

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$$

**Step 2: Apply the ideal gas law to express $\rho$ in terms of $P$**
For an isothermal ideal gas:

$$P = \rho R_s T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho = \frac{P}{R_s T}$$

where $R_s = R/M$ is the **specific gas constant** for air.

**Step 3: Substitute $\rho$ into the hydrostatic equation**

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\left(\frac{P}{R_s T}\right) g = -\frac{P g}{R_s T}$$

**Step 4: Separate variables**

$$\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{g}{R_s T} dz$$

**Step 5: Integrate from sea level ($z = 0$, $P = P_0$) to elevation $z$ (pressure $P$)**

$$\int_{P_0}^{P} \frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{g}{R_s T} \int_{0}^{z} dz$$

**Step 6: Evaluate the integrals**

$$\ln\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = -\frac{g z}{R_s T}$$

**Step 7: Exponentiate both sides**

$$\frac{P}{P_0} = e^{-gz/(R_s T)}$$

**Step 8: Final result**

$$\boxed{P = P_0 \, e^{-Mgz/(RT)}} \quad \blacksquare$$

This is the **Barometric Formula** for an isothermal atmosphere, valid for moderate altitudes (up to ~10 km).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Pressure Variation in Adiabatic (ISA) Atmosphere

**Step 1: Assume temperature varies linearly with altitude**

$$T = T_0 - L z$$

where $L$ is the lapse rate (positive number representing the rate of temperature **decrease** with altitude).

**Step 2: Starting from ideal gas law substitution**

$$\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{g}{R_s T} dz = -\frac{Mg}{R T} dz$$

**Step 3: Substitute $T = T_0 - L z$**

$$\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{Mg}{R(T_0 - L z)} dz$$

**Step 4: Integrate from sea level ($z = 0$, $P = P_0$) to altitude $z$**

$$\int_{P_0}^{P} \frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{Mg}{R} \int_{0}^{z} \frac{dz}{T_0 - L z}$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the right-hand integral using substitution $u = T_0 - L z$, $du = -L \, dz$**

$$\int_{0}^{z} \frac{dz}{T_0 - L z} = \left[-\frac{1}{L} \ln(T_0 - L z)\right]_{0}^{z} = -\frac{1}{L} \ln\left(\frac{T_0 - L z}{T_0}\right)$$

**Step 6: Substitute back into the equation**

$$\ln\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = -\frac{Mg}{R} \cdot \left[-\frac{1}{L} \ln\left(\frac{T_0 - L z}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = \frac{Mg}{RL} \ln\left(\frac{T_0 - L z}{T_0}\right)$$

**Step 7: Combine the logarithms**

$$\ln\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = \ln\left[\left(\frac{T_0 - L z}{T_0}\right)^{Mg/(RL)}\right]$$

**Step 8: Exponentiate both sides**

$$\frac{P}{P_0} = \left(\frac{T_0 - L z}{T_0}\right)^{Mg/(RL)}$$

**Step 9: Final result**

$$\boxed{P = P_0 \left[1 - \frac{L z}{T_0}\right]^{gM/(RL)}} \quad \blacksquare$$

This is the **ISA pressure formula**, used in aerospace and meteorology for accurate atmospheric modeling in the troposphere (0–11 km).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Model

| Scenario | Applicable Model | Reason |
|----------|------------------|--------|
| Water column in a tank | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | Water is essentially incompressible |
| Atmospheric pressure up to 1 km altitude | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ (approx.) | Air density changes are negligible at low altitude |
| Atmospheric pressure at cruising altitude (10+ km) | Isothermal barometric | Simplification, reasonable accuracy |
| Aircraft altimeter calibration | ISA/Adiabatic | Most accurate model for engineering |
| Ocean depth pressure (water) | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | Water is incompressible, density constant |
| Storm prediction/meteorology | Real-gas equations + humidity correction | High accuracy required |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Atmospheric Pressure Measurement & Variation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Atmospheric Air Column] --> B[Weight of Air Above]
    B --> C[Hydrostatic Force dF = ρ g dA dz]
    C --> D[Pressure at Reference Point P_atm]
    D --> E{Pressure Type}
    E -->|Absolute| F[P_abs = P_atm + P_gage]
    E -->|Gage| G[P_gage = P_abs - P_atm]
    E -->|Vacuum| H[P_vac = P_atm - P_abs]
    D --> I{Measurement Device}
    I -->|Mercury Barometer| J[h_Hg = P_atm / ρ_Hg g]
    I -->|Aneroid Barometer| K[Capsule Deformation]
    I -->|Manometer| L[Fluid Column Height]
    D --> M{Altitude Range}
    M -->|0 to 1 km| N[Linear Approx P = P0 - ρgh]
    M -->|1 to 11 km| O[ISA Model with Lapse Rate]
    M -->|Above 11 km| P[Isothermal Model]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#9a3412,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#14532d,stroke:#166534,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#581c87,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#854d0e,stroke:#a16207,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Pressure Variation with Altitude Models

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Define Atmospheric Model]) --> Q1{Is air density constant?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| M1[Use Hydrostatic Equation: P = P0 + ρgh]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is temperature constant?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| M2[Isothermal Barometric Formula: P = P0 e^-Mgz/RT]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Linear temp decrease with altitude?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| M4[ISA Adiabatic Model: P = P0 1-Lz/T0^gM/RL]
    Q3 -->|No| M5[Use Real-Gas Equations with Numerical Integration]

    M1 --> Output1[Result: P linear in z]
    M2 --> Output2[Result: P exponential decay in z]
    M4 --> Output3[Result: P power-law decay in z]
    M5 --> Output4[Result: Numerical solution required]

    style Start fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#075985,color:#ffffff
    style Q1 fill:#7e22ce,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#ffffff
    style Q2 fill:#7e22ce,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#ffffff
    style Q3 fill:#7e22ce,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#ffffff
    style M1 fill:#166534,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    style M2 fill:#166534,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    style M4 fill:#166534,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    style M5 fill:#166534,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Mercury Barometer Setup (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Open Mercury Reservoir] -->|Atmospheric Pressure P_atm pushes down| B[Mercury Surface in Reservoir]
    B -->|Rises up the tube| C[Evacuated Glass Tube 1 m long]
    C -->|Mercury column height h_Hg| D[Vacuum at Top Vapour Pressure negligible]
    D -->|Force balance| E[P_atm × A = ρ_Hg × g × h_Hg × A]
    E -->|Solving| F[h_Hg = P_atm / ρ_Hg × g]
    F -->|At sea level| G[h_Hg = 760 mm]

    style A fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#075985,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#9a3412,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#1f2937,stroke:#111827,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#14532d,stroke:#166534,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define atmospheric pressure. State its standard value in (i) Pascal, (ii) mm of Hg, and (iii) bar.**

**Model Answer:**

Atmospheric pressure is the pressure exerted at any point on Earth's surface due to the weight of the column of air standing vertically above that point.

Standard value:
- **(i) Pascal**: $P_{atm} = \mathbf{101{,}325 \, Pa = 101.325 \, kPa}$
- **(ii) mm of Hg**: $h_{Hg} = \mathbf{760 \, mm}$
- **(iii) bar**: $P_{atm} = \mathbf{1.01325 \, bar}$

*Verification*: $P = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h = 13{,}600 \times 9.81 \times 0.760 = 101{,}396 \approx 101.3$ kPa ✓

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between absolute pressure, gage pressure, and vacuum pressure with a suitable sketch.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Definition | Relation |
|-----------|------------|----------|
| **Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$)** | Pressure measured from perfect vacuum (zero reference) | $P_{abs} \geq 0$ always |
| **Gage Pressure ($P_{gage}$)** | Pressure measured **above** atmospheric pressure | $P_{gage} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ |
| **Vacuum Pressure ($P_{vac}$)** | Pressure measured **below** atmospheric pressure | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ |

**Sketch (described textually):**

A vertical number line representing pressure magnitude:
- Bottom: **0 Pa (Perfect Vacuum)** — Absolute zero
- Middle: **$P_{atm}$ = 101.325 kPa** — Atmospheric line
- Top: Any positive pressure value

- **Gage pressure** = reading **above** the atmospheric line
- **Vacuum pressure** = reading **below** the atmospheric line (down to 0)
- **Absolute pressure** = reading **from the bottom** (perfect vacuum reference)

Note: $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gage} = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$

---

### PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship for the variation of pressure with altitude assuming the atmosphere behaves as an **isothermal ideal gas**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An aircraft flies at an altitude of 8000 m. If the sea-level pressure is 101.325 kPa and the air temperature is 15°C (assumed constant), determine the **atmospheric pressure** at this altitude. Take $M = 0.029$ kg/mol and $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a): Derivation of Isothermal Barometric Formula

**Step 1**: Consider a thin horizontal slab of air of thickness $dz$ at elevation $z$ in static equilibrium.

**Step 2**: Force balance on the slab gives the hydrostatic equation:

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$$

**Step 3**: Using the ideal gas law for air (isothermal):

$$P = \rho R_s T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho = \frac{P}{R_s T}$$

**Step 4**: Substituting $\rho$:

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\frac{P g}{R_s T}$$

**Step 5**: Separating variables:

$$\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{g}{R_s T} dz$$

**Step 6**: Integrating from $z = 0$ (where $P = P_0$) to $z = z$ (where $P = P$):

$$\int_{P_0}^{P} \frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{g}{R_s T} \int_{0}^{z} dz$$

**Step 7**: Evaluating:

$$\ln\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = -\frac{g z}{R_s T}$$

**Step 8**: Exponentiating:

$$\boxed{P = P_0 \, e^{-Mg z / (R T)}}$$

This is the **isothermal barometric formula**, valid for moderate altitudes.

---

#### Part (b): Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $z = 8000$ m
- $P_0 = 101.325$ kPa
- $T = 15°C = 288.15$ K
- $M = 0.029$ kg/mol
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1**: Calculate the exponent:

$$\frac{Mg z}{R T} = \frac{0.029 \times 9.81 \times 8000}{8.314 \times 288.15}$$

**Step 2**: Compute numerator:

$$0.029 \times 9.81 = 0.28449$$

$$0.28449 \times 8000 = 2275.92$$

**Step 3**: Compute denominator:

$$8.314 \times 288.15 = 2395.68$$

**Step 4**: Compute the ratio:

$$\frac{Mg z}{R T} = \frac{2275.92}{2395.68} = 0.9499$$

**Step 5**: Compute the exponential term:

$$e^{-0.9499} = 0.3867$$

**Step 6**: Compute final pressure:

$$P = 101.325 \times 0.3867 = 39.18 \text{ kPa}$$

**Result:** The atmospheric pressure at 8000 m altitude is approximately **$P \approx 39.18$ kPa** (about 0.387 atm or 297 mmHg).

---

### **QUESTION B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** A **mercury barometer** reads 750 mm Hg at a location. If the local air temperature is 30°C and gravitational acceleration is 9.8 m/s², calculate: (i) the local atmospheric pressure in kPa, and (ii) the equivalent height of a **water column** that would balance this pressure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the construction and working of a **mercury barometer** with a neat sketch. State why mercury is preferred over water as the barometric fluid. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a): Numerical Calculations

**Given:**
- $h_{Hg} = 750$ mm = 0.750 m
- $T = 30°C$ (not required for the calculation, since we use $\rho_{Hg}$ at the given temperature implicitly)
- $g = 9.8$ m/s²
- $\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600$ kg/m³
- $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³

**Part (i): Atmospheric pressure in kPa**

**Step 1**: Apply the barometric equation:

$$P_{atm} = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h_{Hg}$$

**Step 2**: Substitute values:

$$P_{atm} = 13{,}600 \times 9.8 \times 0.750$$

**Step 3**: Compute step by step:

$$13{,}600 \times 9.8 = 133{,}280$$

$$133{,}280 \times 0.750 = 99{,}960 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4**: Convert to kPa:

$$P_{atm} = \frac{99{,}960}{1000} = 99.96 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{atm} \approx 99.96 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Part (ii): Equivalent water column height**

**Step 1**: Equate pressures:

$$\rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h_{Hg} = \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot h_{water}$$

**Step 2**: Cancel $g$:

$$\rho_{Hg} \cdot h_{Hg} = \rho_{water} \cdot h_{water}$$

**Step 3**: Solve for $h_{water}$:

$$h_{water} = \frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_{water}} \cdot h_{Hg} = 13.6 \times 0.750$$

$$\boxed{h_{water} = 10.2 \text{ m}}$$

---

#### Part (b): Mercury Barometer – Construction, Working, and Justification

**Construction:**
A mercury barometer consists of:
1. A **glass tube** of length ~1 meter, closed at one end.
2. The tube is completely filled with **pure, clean mercury**.
3. The open end is covered with a finger and the tube is **inverted** into an open **mercury reservoir** (cistern).
4. The finger is removed, and mercury flows down until the column is balanced by atmospheric pressure.
5. A **vacuum** (Torricellian vacuum) forms at the top of the tube.

**Working Principle:**
The atmospheric pressure acts on the exposed mercury surface in the reservoir, pushing mercury up the tube. The column stabilizes when the weight of the mercury column equals the atmospheric force:

$$P_{atm} \cdot A = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h_{Hg} \cdot A$$

$$\Rightarrow P_{atm} = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h_{Hg}$$

The reading $h_{Hg}$ directly gives the atmospheric pressure in mm Hg.

**Why Mercury is Preferred Over Water:**

| Property | Mercury Advantage |
|----------|-------------------|
| **High density** ($\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600$ kg/m³) | Column height is only 760 mm instead of 10.33 m (water) — practical and portable |
| **Low vapor pressure** (0.00016 mmHg at 20°C) | Accurate vacuum at top, no vapor contamination of reading |
| **Doesn't wet glass** (high surface tension) | Forms a clean meniscus, accurate reading; water would cling to walls |
| **Visible silvery color** | Easy to read the meniscus position |
| **Chemically stable** | Doesn't react with air, long-term reliability |
| **Wide liquid range** ($-39°C$ to $357°C$) | Works in varied climatic conditions |

If water were used, the barometer would need to be over **10 meters tall** — practically unfeasible.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Hydrostatic Equation**: Forgetting the negative sign in $\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$ and reporting pressure *increases* with altitude. **(–1 mark)**
>
> 2. **Confusing Gage and Absolute Pressure**: Reporting atmospheric pressure in kPa(g) instead of kPa(a). Always clarify: $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa **absolute**. **(–1 mark)**
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Convert Units**: Using $g = 9.81$ m/s² but leaving height in cm, or vice versa. Standardize everything to SI units (m, kg, s) before substituting. **(–1 to –2 marks)**
>
> 4. **Using $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³ for air**: Air density is ~1.225 kg/m³ at sea level. Don't mix water constants with air problems. **(–1 mark)**
>
> 5. **Barometric Formula Misapplication**: The isothermal barometric formula $P = P_0 e^{-Mgz/RT}$ assumes **constant temperature**. If the problem gives a lapse rate, you MUST use the ISA/adiabatic model. **(–2 marks)**
>
> 6. **Skipping Intermediate Steps**: KTU examiners want to see the derivation step-by-step. A direct jump to the final formula gets 0 marks for derivation questions.
>
> 7. **Numerical Calculation Errors**: Always recompute $e^{-x}$ and powers carefully. One arithmetic slip can lose 2–3 marks on a 7-mark problem.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Atmospheric pressure is the weight of the air column above a reference point per unit area.
- ✅ Standard atmospheric pressure = **101.325 kPa = 760 mmHg = 1 atm = 1.01325 bar**.
- ✅ Hydrostatic equation: $\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$ (negative sign is critical).
- ✅ Incompressible fluid pressure: $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ (used for water/liquids).
- ✅ Compressible (isothermal air): $P = P_0 e^{-Mgz/RT}$ (Barometric Formula).
- ✅ Compressible (ISA/Adiabatic): $P = P_0 \left[1 - \frac{Lz}{T_0}\right]^{gM/(RL)}$ with $L = 0.0065$ K/m.
- ✅ Three pressure scales: $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gage} = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$.
- ✅ Barometer height relation: $P_{atm} = \rho g h$, so $h = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g}$.
- ✅ Water column equivalent: 1 atm ↔ 10.33 m of water ↔ 760 mm of mercury.
- ✅ Mercury is preferred due to high density, low vapor pressure, and non-wetting property.
- ✅ Standard values: $g = 9.81$ m/s², $M_{air} = 0.029$ kg/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T_0 = 288.15$ K.
- ✅ Practical applications: pump suction limits, altimeter calibration, barometric altimetry, weather forecasting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gauge Pressure and Absolute Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Gauge Pressure and Absolute Pressure ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In **Fluid Statics**, pressure at any point in a fluid is defined as the normal force per unit area exerted on an infinitesimal fluid element. However, the **numerical value** of this pressure depends entirely on the **reference datum** chosen by the observer. This leads to the two universally adopted reference frameworks in engineering practice: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$)** is the total pressure measured with respect to a **perfect vacuum** (the complete absence of any molecular pressure). It is the **true thermodynamic pressure** of a fluid and is **always positive** (or zero in a perfect vacuum). It can never be negative because it is referenced to the lowest possible physical state. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Gauge Pressure ($P_{gauge}$)** is the pressure measured with respect to the **local atmospheric pressure** as the datum. It is the reading indicated by most mechanical pressure instruments (Bourdon gauges, manometers in everyday use). Gauge pressure can be **positive** (above atmosphere) or **negative** (below atmosphere). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sea Level" Intuition Imagine you are **scuba diving** in the ocean: - **Absolute Pressure** = The total weight of everything above you (water + the entire atmosphere from sea level to space). This is your **true** physical pressure environment. - **Gauge Pressure** = The extra pressure **beyond what you would feel standing at the beach** breathing normal air. Your dive computer subtracts out the 1 atmosphere you already had at the surface. Now imagine you are climbing a **high mountain** with a sealed bag of chips: - The chips bag **puffs up** because the air **inside** the bag is at the original (higher) absolute pressure, but the **outside** atmospheric pressure is now lower. - The bag's internal absolute pressure has **not changed** — it is still ~101.3 kPa. But the gauge pressure (relative to the new local atmosphere) has become **positive**. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Atmospheric Pressure (1 atm) = 101.325 kPa = 101.325 kN/m² = 1.01325 bar = 760 mm of Hg = 14.696 psi** > This value is defined by the **ISC (International Standard Atmosphere)** at sea level at 15°C. ### 1.3 Vacuum Pressure — The "Third State" When the local absolute pressure drops **below** atmospheric pressure, the gauge reading becomes **negative**. Engineers flip the sign and call this **Vacuum Pressure**: $$P_{vac} = -P_{gauge} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$$ A "**partial vacuum**" exists whenever $P_{abs} < P_{atm}$. A "**perfect vacuum**" is reached when $P_{abs} = 0$, meaning the gauge reads **-101.325 kPa** (i.e., a full vacuum gauge of -1 atm). ### 1.4 The Reference Datum Visualization > [!NOTE] > Think of a number line. The **Perfect Vacuum** is the absolute mathematical zero (left end). The **Local Atmosphere** sits at +1 atm (or 101.325 kPa) somewhere in the middle. **Absolute pressure** is always measured from the left end. **Gauge pressure** is always measured from the "Atmosphere" mark. **Vacuum pressure** is also measured from the "Atmosphere" mark, but pointing **leftward** (towards vacuum). ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Fundamental Pressure Relationships The relationship between these three pressure concepts can be derived from the simple idea of shifting the reference point on a number line: **Step 1 — Define each term clearly:** - Let $P_{atm}$ = Local atmospheric pressure (the "zero" for any gauge instrument) - Let $P_{abs}$ = True total pressure above perfect vacuum - Let $P_{gauge}$ = Pressure reading above (or below) local atmosphere - Let $P_{vac}$ = Magnitude of pressure below local atmosphere (always ≥ 0) **Step 2 — Apply the definition of "gauge":** Since gauge pressure is defined as the *excess* (or *deficit*) over atmospheric: $$P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange for absolute pressure (the workhorse equation):** $$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$$ **Step 4 — Establish the vacuum relationship:** By definition, vacuum pressure is positive when $P_{abs} < P_{atm}$: $$P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs} = -P_{gauge}$$ ### 2.2 Sign Convention Summary | Condition | Absolute Pressure | Gauge Pressure | Vacuum Pressure | |---|---|---|---| | Perfect Vacuum | $0$ | $-P_{atm}$ | $P_{atm}$ (full vacuum) | | Below Atmosphere | $0 < P_{abs} < P_{atm}$ | **Negative** | **Positive** | | Exactly Atmospheric | $P_{atm}$ | $0$ | $0$ | | Above Atmosphere | $P_{abs} > P_{atm}$ | **Positive** | Not defined (= 0) | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | SI Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | Absolute from gauge + atmosphere | Pa or N/m² | | 2 | $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ | Gauge from absolute | Pa | | 3 | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ | Vacuum pressure (always ≥ 0) | Pa | | 4 | $P_{gauge} = -P_{vac}$ | Sign relationship | Pa | | 5 | $1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ | Standard atmospheric | kPa | | 6 | $1 \text{ atm} = 760 \text{ mm Hg} = 10.33 \text{ m of water}$ | Mercury/Water column | mm / m | | 7 | $1 \text{ bar} = 100 \text{ kPa} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$ | Metric bar | Pa | | 8 | $P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure from depth | Pa | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TIP:** In numerical problems, the phrase "**gauge pressure**" means the *given* pressure value is **already relative to atmosphere**. You only add $P_{atm}$ when the problem asks for **absolute** pressure. Conversely, "**absolute pressure**" means you must subtract $P_{atm}$ to get the gauge reading. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Why This Distinction Matters | |---|---| | **Tire Pressure Gauges** | Always read **gauge** (over atmospheric).
