Tire specs like "32 psi" are gauge values. | | **Boiler Safety** | Relief valves set on **gauge** pressure. ASME code is written in gauge terms. | | **Aircraft Altimeters** | They actually measure **absolute** pressure to deduce altitude. | | **Vacuum Pump Sizing** | Specified in terms of "**vacuum**" or "**Torr**" (mm of Hg below 760 mm). | | **Beverage Carbonation** | CO₂ tanks regulated in **gauge** (typically 50–60 psi gauge). | | **Thermodynamics / Gas Laws** | Ideal Gas Law ($PV = nRT$) **always** uses **absolute** pressure and **absolute** temperature. Using gauge values here will give catastrophically wrong answers. | > [!WARNING] > **The #1 KTU Mistake:** Substituting gauge pressure into $PV = nRT$ or any gas law. This will silently produce an absurdly wrong answer because the gas law has a **hard zero** at perfect vacuum, not at atmospheric. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: The Master Pressure Equation **Starting Point:** By definition, gauge pressure is the difference between the true (absolute) pressure and the ambient atmospheric reference. $$ \begin{aligned} P_{gauge} &\equiv P_{abs} - P_{atm} \\ \text{Adding } P_{atm} \text{ to both sides:} \quad P_{atm} + P_{gauge} &= P_{abs} \\ \therefore \quad P_{abs} &= P_{atm} + P_{gauge} \end{aligned} $$ **Vacuum Relation:** When $P_{abs} < P_{atm}$, we define vacuum as the *magnitude* of the deficit: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{vac} &\equiv P_{atm} - P_{abs} \\ \text{Substituting } P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}: \quad P_{vac} &= P_{atm} - (P_{atm} + P_{gauge}) \\ P_{vac} &= -P_{gauge} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2 Solved Numerical Example 1 (Standard KTU Style) **Problem:** A pressure gauge fitted on a pipeline reads **350 kPa**. The local barometer reads **755 mm of Hg**. Determine: (a) The absolute pressure in the pipeline. (b) The vacuum pressure (if applicable, otherwise state NIL). *Take $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.* **Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert atmospheric pressure from mm of Hg to kPa:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{atm} &= \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h \\ P_{atm} &= 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \times 0.755 \text{ m} \\ P_{atm} &= 13600 \times 9.81 \times 0.755 \\ P_{atm} &= 100,724.28 \text{ Pa} \\ P_{atm} &\approx 100.72 \text{ kPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute absolute pressure using the master equation:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{abs} &= P_{atm} + P_{gauge} \\ P_{abs} &= 100.72 \text{ kPa} + 350 \text{ kPa} \\ P_{abs} &= 450.72 \text{ kPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Decide on vacuum pressure:** Since $P_{gauge} = +350 \text{ kPa} > 0$, the pipeline pressure is **above** atmosphere. > **∴ Vacuum pressure = NIL** (Not Applicable; vacuum is defined only when gauge is negative). **Final Answer (boxed for exam):** - $P_{abs} = 450.72 \text{ kPa}$ - $P_{vac} = \text{NIL}$ ### 3.3 Solved Numerical Example 2 (Vacuum Application) **Problem:** A condenser in a steam power plant has an absolute pressure of **8.5 kPa**. The local atmospheric pressure is **101.3 kPa**. Find: (a) The gauge pressure in kPa. (b) The vacuum pressure in kPa and in mm of Hg. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute gauge pressure:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{gauge} &= P_{abs} - P_{atm} \\ P_{gauge} &= 8.5 - 101.3 \\ P_{gauge} &= -92.8 \text{ kPa} \end{aligned} $$ The negative sign confirms the condenser operates **below** atmospheric (a partial vacuum, as expected for steam condensation). **Step 2 — Compute vacuum pressure (take magnitude):** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{vac} &= P_{atm} - P_{abs} \\ P_{vac} &= 101.3 - 8.5 \\ P_{vac} &= 92.8 \text{ kPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Convert vacuum to mm of Hg:** $$ \begin{aligned} h_{Hg} &= \frac{P_{vac}}{\rho_{Hg} \cdot g} \\ h_{Hg} &= \frac{92800 \text{ Pa}}{13600 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2} \\ h_{Hg} &= \frac{92800}{133416} \\ h_{Hg} &\approx 0.6956 \text{ m} \\ h_{Hg} &\approx 695.6 \text{ mm of Hg} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** - $P_{gauge} = -92.8 \text{ kPa}$ - $P_{vac} = 92.8 \text{ kPa} = 695.6 \text{ mm Hg}$ ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Quantity | Symbol | Reference Datum | Sign Range | Common Instrument | |---|---|---|---|---| | Absolute Pressure | $P_{abs}$ | Perfect Vacuum (0 Pa) | $P_{abs} \geq 0$ | Barometer, Absolute Transducer | | Atmospheric Pressure | $P_{atm}$ | Perfect Vacuum | $\approx 101.325$ kPa | Mercury Barometer | | Gauge Pressure | $P_{gauge}$ | Local Atmosphere | $-\infty$ to $+\infty$ | Bourdon Gauge, Manometer | | Vacuum Pressure | $P_{vac}$ | Local Atmosphere (deficit) | $0 \leq P_{vac} \leq P_{atm}$ | Vacuum Gauge, McLeod Gauge | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Conceptual Number Line (Pressure Reference Frame) The following block diagram illustrates how the four pressure states relate to a common reference axis, mapping the datum logic behind gauge, absolute, atmospheric, and vacuum readings. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RefLine["PRESSURE REFERENCE AXIS (Number Line)"] direction LR Vacuum["PERFECT VACUUM
P_abs = 0 Pa
P_gauge = -101.325 kPa"] BelowAtm["PARTIAL VACUUM REGION
0 less than P_abs less than P_atm
P_gauge is NEGATIVE
P_vac is POSITIVE"] Atmosphere["LOCAL ATMOSPHERE
P_atm approx 101.325 kPa
P_gauge = 0
P_vac = 0"] AboveAtm["PRESSURIZED REGION
P_abs greater than P_atm
P_gauge is POSITIVE
P_vac = NIL"] end Vacuum -->|"0 Pa Datum"| BelowAtm BelowAtm -->|"P_atm Datum"| Atmosphere Atmosphere -->|"Pressure Increases"| AboveAtm AtmArrow["Atmospheric Datum (P_atm)"] -.->|"Gauge / Vacuum Instruments
use this as ZERO"| Atmosphere AbsArrow["Absolute Datum (0 Pa)"] -.->|"Thermodynamic / Gas Law
use this as ZERO"| Vacuum ``` ### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Pressure Conversion Engine This block-level functional architecture maps the *information flow* when converting between pressure readings — the kind of logic a KTU student should mentally execute when solving exam problems. ```mermaid flowchart TB Input1["Input Channel 1
P_gauge given
(Bourdon dial reading)"] Input2["Input Channel 2
P_atm given
(Barometer reading)"] Input3["Input Channel 3
P_abs given
(Absolute transducer)"] Input4["Input Channel 4
P_vac given
(Vacuum gauge)"] Hub["CENTRAL CONVERSION HUB
P_abs = P_atm + P_gauge
P_vac = P_atm - P_abs
P_gauge = -P_vac"] Out1["Output 1
P_abs in kPa, bar, psi"] Out2["Output 2
P_gauge sign and value"] Out3["Output 3
P_vac magnitude and mm Hg"] Out4["Output 4
Hydrostatic head h = P over rho times g"] Input1 --> Hub Input2 --> Hub Input3 --> Hub Input4 --> Hub Hub --> Out1 Hub --> Out2 Hub --> Out3 Hub --> Out4 Validity["VALIDITY CHECKER
If P_gauge greater than 0 then Above Atmosphere
If P_gauge less than 0 then Below Atmosphere
If P_gauge = 0 then Atmospheric Equilibrium"] -.-> Hub ``` ### 4.3 Manometer Schematic (Description) > Since physical instrument drawings are difficult to render in Mermaid, the following textual block schematic describes a classic **U-tube manometer** measuring gauge pressure: **Schematic — U-Tube Manometer (Open Type):** - **Left Limb:** Connected to the pipe/vessel whose pressure is to be measured. The fluid in the pipe (say water) pushes down on the manometric fluid. - **Right Limb:** Open to the local atmosphere. - **Manometric Fluid:** Mercury ($\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$) or a heavier immiscible liquid, chosen so the level difference $h$ is readable. - **Reading Method:** The mercury level on the **pipe-side** falls, while the **atmosphere-side** rises. The vertical difference $h$ is measured with a scale. - **Gauge Pressure Formula (for gas in pipe):** $P_{gauge} = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_{gas}) \cdot g \cdot h$. Since $\rho_{gas} \ll \rho_{Hg}$, this simplifies to $P_{gauge} \approx \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$. - **Absolute Pressure:** $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]` **Q: Differentiate between absolute pressure and gauge pressure. Why is absolute pressure used in thermodynamic calculations?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Absolute Pressure | Gauge Pressure | |---|---|---| | Reference | Perfect vacuum (0 Pa) | Local atmospheric pressure | | Range | Always ≥ 0 | Can be positive, zero, or negative | | Reading | Barometer / absolute transducer | Bourdon gauge / manometer | | Used in | Gas laws, thermodynamics, altitude | Industrial process, safety valves | **Why absolute is used in thermodynamics:** The Ideal Gas Law $PV = nRT$ and all thermodynamic state equations are derived assuming pressure is measured from a **true zero** (perfect vacuum). Atmospheric pressure is a *variable* (changes with weather, altitude, temperature), so it cannot serve as a universal zero. Using gauge pressure would give wrong values for $V$, work done, and energy calculations. **(1 mark)** #### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]` **Q: Define vacuum pressure. A condenser operates at an absolute pressure of 0.085 bar. If atmospheric pressure is 1.013 bar, calculate the vacuum pressure in kPa.** **Model Answer:** Vacuum pressure is defined as the pressure **below atmospheric**; it is the difference $P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ when $P_{abs} < P_{atm}$. **(1 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{vac} &= P_{atm} - P_{abs} \\ P_{vac} &= (1.013 - 0.085) \text{ bar} \\ P_{vac} &= 0.928 \text{ bar} \\ P_{vac} &= 0.928 \times 100 \text{ kPa} \\ P_{vac} &= 92.8 \text{ kPa} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) #### **Question B.A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern]` **(a)** Derive the relationship between absolute pressure, gauge pressure, and vacuum pressure. Explain the significance of each with a neat sketch. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A pipe carrying water has a Bourdon gauge reading of **4.2 bar** at a point. The local barometer reads **750 mm of Hg**. Determine the absolute pressure in the pipe in (i) kPa, (ii) m of water column, and (iii) N/m². Take $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution to B.A:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Definitions (2 marks):** - $P_{abs}$: Pressure measured above perfect vacuum (true pressure). - $P_{gauge}$: Pressure measured above local atmosphere. - $P_{vac}$: Magnitude of pressure below local atmosphere (always positive). - $P_{atm}$: Local barometric pressure (taken as $\approx 101.325$ kPa at standard conditions). **Step 2 — Master Relation (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Since gauge is the excess over atmosphere:} \quad P_{gauge} &= P_{abs} - P_{atm} \\ \text{Therefore:} \quad \boxed{P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}} \\ \text{When } P_{abs} < P_{atm}: \quad \boxed{P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs} = -P_{gauge}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Significance + Sketch Description (2 marks):** The sketch should show a number line with the four reference points (Vacuum → Below Atm → Atm → Above Atm). Each pressure is shown as a vector from its respective datum. --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Atmospheric pressure in bar:** $$ P_{atm} = 750 \text{ mm Hg} = 0.750 \text{ m Hg} $$ $$ P_{atm} = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h = 13600 \times 9.81 \times 0.750 = 100,062 \text{ Pa} \approx 100.062 \text{ kPa} $$ Converting to bar: $P_{atm} \approx 1.00062 \text{ bar}$ **Step 2 — Absolute pressure in bar (1 mark):** $$ P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge} = 1.00062 + 4.2 = 5.20062 \text{ bar} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to kPa (2 marks):** $$ P_{abs} = 5.20062 \times 100 = 520.062 \text{ kPa} \quad \boxed{\approx 520.06 \text{ kPa}} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to m of water (2 marks):** $$ h_{water} = \frac{P_{abs}}{\rho_{water} \cdot g} = \frac{520062}{1000 \times 9.81} = 53.01 \text{ m} \quad \boxed{\approx 53.01 \text{ m of water}} $$ **Step 5 — Express in N/m² (2 marks):** $$ \boxed{P_{abs} = 520062 \text{ N/m}^2 = 5.20062 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2} $$ --- #### **Question B.B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Pattern]` **(a)** With the help of a U-tube manometer, explain how gauge pressure of a gas in a closed vessel is measured. Derive the expression for gauge pressure in terms of manometer reading. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A closed tank contains a gas at an absolute pressure of **250 kPa**. The atmospheric pressure is **98 kPa**. If the tank is cooled, the gauge pressure drops to **120 kPa**. Determine: (i) the new absolute pressure, (ii) the new vacuum pressure (if any), and (iii) the change in absolute pressure. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution to B.B:** **(a) U-tube manometer explanation (7 marks):** **Diagram description (3 marks):** A U-tube manometer consists of a U-shaped glass tube with one end connected to the gas vessel (whose pressure $P_{gas}$ is to be measured) and the other end **open to atmosphere** ($P_{atm}$). The U-tube is partially filled with mercury (or any heavy immiscible liquid). When the gas pressure exceeds atmospheric, the mercury on the gas-side is pushed down by a height $h$ relative to the atmosphere-side. **Derivation (4 marks):** Apply hydrostatic balance. At the **lower mercury level** (the datum), the pressure from both limbs must be equal (Pascal's principle for a continuous static fluid): $$ P_{gas} + \rho_{gas} \cdot g \cdot a = P_{atm} + \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h $$ where $a$ is the gas column height difference (negligible for gas). Simplifying: $$ P_{gas} - P_{atm} = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h $$ The LHS is the **gauge pressure** by definition: $$ \boxed{P_{gauge} = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h} $$ --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** - Initial absolute pressure: $P_{abs,1} = 250$ kPa - Atmospheric: $P_{atm} = 98$ kPa - Final gauge pressure: $P_{gauge,2} = 120$ kPa **Step 1 — Initial gauge pressure (1 mark):** $$ P_{gauge,1} = P_{abs,1} - P_{atm} = 250 - 98 = 152 \text{ kPa} $$ **Step 2 — New absolute pressure (2 marks):** $$ P_{abs,2} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge,2} = 98 + 120 = \boxed{218 \text{ kPa}} $$ **Step 3 — New vacuum pressure (2 marks):** Since $P_{gauge,2} = 120 \text{ kPa} > 0$, the tank is still **above** atmosphere. Therefore, **vacuum pressure = NIL**. **Step 4 — Change in absolute pressure (2 marks):** $$ \Delta P_{abs} = P_{abs,1} - P_{abs,2} = 250 - 218 = \boxed{32 \text{ kPa (decrease)}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Sign Confusion (–2 marks):** Writing $P_{gauge}$ as positive when it should be negative (or vice versa) for sub-atmospheric conditions. Always state the sign explicitly. > 2. **Forgetting to Add Atmosphere (–2 marks):** Reporting the gauge reading as the "absolute pressure" without adding $P_{atm}$. This is the single most common error in this topic. > 3. **Unit Mismatch (–1 mark):** Mixing bar with kPa without explicit conversion factor. Always write: "$1 \text{ bar} = 100 \text{ kPa}$". > 4. **Using $g = 9.8$ vs $g = 9.81$:** Either is acceptable if used consistently. But mixing within the same solution loses 0.5 marks. > 5. **Vacuum Pressure Reporting Negative:** Vacuum is defined as a *magnitude*, so it is **always positive or zero**. A negative vacuum pressure is physically meaningless. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Revision — Print This Section Before Your Exam:** - ✅ **Absolute pressure** is referenced to **perfect vacuum** (true zero). It is **always ≥ 0**. - ✅ **Gauge pressure** is referenced to **local atmosphere**. It **can be negative**. - ✅ **Vacuum pressure** = magnitude of pressure below atmosphere. It is **always ≥ 0**, and is **defined only when gauge is negative**. - ✅ **Master Equation:** $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ - ✅ **Vacuum Equation:** $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs} = -P_{gauge}$ - ✅ **Standard Atmosphere:** $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa $= 1.01325$ bar $= 760$ mm Hg $= 10.33$ m of water. - ✅ **1 bar = 100 kPa = $10^5$ Pa** (exactly, by SI definition). - ✅ **Use ABSOLUTE pressure** in **all** gas laws ($PV = nRT$) and **all** thermodynamic calculations. - ✅ **Use GAUGE pressure** for **practical/industrial** measurements (tires, boilers, pipe pressures). - ✅ **Use VACUUM pressure** when describing **condensers, vacuum pumps, suction lines**. - ✅ A Bourdon gauge reads **gauge**; a barometer reads **atmospheric**; a sealed absolute transducer reads **absolute**. - ✅ Mercury density: $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³. Water density: $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³. $g = 9.81$ m/s² (use 9.81 unless told otherwise). - ✅ **Negative gauge ≠ negative absolute.** Absolute pressure can NEVER be negative — that would imply a physical state below perfect vacuum, which is impossible. - ✅ If a problem says "pressure in the pipe is X," **without** specifying gauge or absolute, ask yourself: is the pressure sensor open to atmosphere? If yes → **gauge**. If the sensor is sealed → **absolute**. > **End of Module 1 — Gauge Pressure and Absolute Pressure Notes**

topics.title: Pascal's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pascal's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Pascal's Law** (also called the **Principle of Transmission of Fluid Pressure**) is a fundamental principle in fluid statics that states:

> *"A change in pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished to every portion of the fluid and to the walls of the containing vessel."*

In more precise KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Pascal's Law is articulated as: **The intensity of pressure at any point in a fluid at rest is the same in all directions, and any change in pressure applied at one point is transmitted equally and undiminished to all other points within the fluid.**

The law was formulated by the French mathematician and physicist **Blaise Pascal** in **1653** and forms the foundational basis for the operation of all hydraulic systems, including hydraulic presses, lifts, brakes, and jacks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Pascal's Law is exclusively valid for:
> 1. **Fluids at rest** (static condition only)
> 2. **Incompressible fluids** (liquids; gases require modified treatment)
> 3. The pressure transmission occurs **perpendicular** to the containing surfaces.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

Imagine you have a **sealed water balloon** filled completely with water (no air bubbles). Now, if you press your thumb on one side of the balloon, what happens?

The water inside cannot be compressed (since liquids are nearly incompressible), so the pressure from your thumb is instantly felt on **every other part** of the balloon's inner surface — the top, bottom, sides, and back — with exactly the **same magnitude**. The balloon bulges uniformly in all directions, not just where you pressed it.

This is Pascal's Law in action! The fluid acts as a "pressure messenger," transmitting your push to every wall simultaneously.

Another everyday example: when you squeeze one end of a **fully filled water tube** (like a toothpaste tube filled with water), water squirts out from the other end with the same pressure you applied.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Think of fluid molecules as a chain of tiny billiard balls connected by springs. A push at one end instantly transmits a force throughout the entire chain because the fluid cannot be compressed or absorb the energy.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$
- **Density of water (at 4°C):** $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Bulk modulus of water:** $K \approx 2.2 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$ (this large value justifies the incompressibility assumption)

### 1.4 Pressure — The Fundamental Quantity

Before diving deeper, let's recall that **pressure** is defined as the normal force per unit area:

$$P = \frac{F}{A}$$

where $F$ is the normal force (in Newtons, **N**) and $A$ is the area (in m²). The SI unit of pressure is **Pascal (Pa)**, where $1 \text{ Pa} = 1 \text{ N/m}^2$.

In a fluid at rest, the pressure at any depth $h$ below the free surface is given by the **hydrostatic pressure equation**:

$$P = P_0 + \rho g h$$

where $P_0$ is the pressure at the free surface (often atmospheric), $\rho$ is the fluid density, and $g$ is gravitational acceleration.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The 'Why' and 'How'

To understand Pascal's Law from first principles, consider an elemental fluid parcel (an infinitesimally small cube of fluid) in equilibrium within a static fluid. For this fluid element to remain stationary, all forces acting on it must balance.

#### Key Logical Steps:

**Step 1: Equilibrium of a Fluid Element**
Consider a small fluid element with dimensions $dx \times dy \times dz$ inside a static fluid. The forces acting on it are:
- Pressure forces on all six faces
- Body force (gravity) acting on the volume

**Step 2: Force Balance in Each Direction**
For equilibrium in the x-direction:
$$P \cdot dy \cdot dz - \left(P + \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} dx\right) dy \cdot dz = 0$$

This simplifies to $\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} = 0$. Similarly, by symmetry:
$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial y} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial P}{\partial z} = 0$$

**Step 3: Conclusion**
Since pressure gradients in all horizontal directions are zero, **pressure is the same at all points at the same elevation** — this is one form of Pascal's Law.

**Step 4: Pressure Transmission Principle**
Now suppose an external pressure $dP$ is applied at any point in the confined fluid. Due to the incompressibility (zero volume change) and the equilibrium condition, this pressure increment $dP$ must appear at **every other point** in the fluid. Hence:

$$P_{point\ 1} = P_{point\ 2} = P_{point\ 3} = \ldots = P_{point\ n}$$

### 2.2 Mathematical Statement of Pascal's Law

The most general mathematical form used in KTU examinations is:

$$P_1 = P_2 = P_3 = \ldots = P_n$$

For the common engineering application (hydraulic systems):

$$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

This means the pressure at the input piston equals the pressure at the output piston.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility and Real-World Applications

| Application | Working Principle | Real-World Example |
|-------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Hydraulic Lift** | Small force on small piston creates pressure that lifts heavy load on large piston | Car service station lifts, elevator systems |
| **Hydraulic Press** | Multiplication of force through area difference | Forging presses, metal forming machines |
| **Hydraulic Brakes** | Pedal force transmitted via fluid to brake pads | All modern vehicles (cars, bikes, trucks) |
| **Hydraulic Jack** | Used to lift vehicles for tire changes | Garage jacks |
| **Hydraulic Accumulator** | Stores pressure energy for later use | Industrial hydraulic systems |
| **Dentist's Chair** | Foot pedal creates pressure to adjust chair height | Medical/dental equipment |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Pressure Definition | $P = F/A$ | Pa or N/m² | Fundamental |
| 2 | Pascal's Law (Pressure Equality) | $P_1 = P_2 = P_3$ | Pa | Core principle |
| 3 | Hydraulic System Equation | $F_1/A_1 = F_2/A_2$ | Pa | Force multiplication |
| 4 | Force Multiplication (Mechanical Advantage) | $F_2 = F_1 \cdot (A_2/A_1)$ | N | Hydraulic lift, press |
| 5 | Displacement Relationship | $A_1 \cdot x_1 = A_2 \cdot x_2$ | m³ (volume conservation) | Hydraulic system kinematics |
| 6 | Velocity Ratio | $V_1/V_2 = A_2/A_1$ | dimensionless | Energy conservation |
| 7 | Work/Energy Balance | $F_1 \cdot x_1 = F_2 \cdot x_2$ | J (joules) | Energy conservation proof |
| 8 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $P = \rho g h$ | Pa | Pressure due to fluid column |
| 9 | Gauge Pressure | $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ | Pa | Pressure measurement |
| 10 | Absolute Pressure | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | Pa | Reference pressure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** The displacement relationship $A_1 x_1 = A_2 x_2$ comes from the **conservation of volume** for an incompressible fluid. This means a small piston moving a large distance produces a large piston moving a small distance — illustrating the **trade-off between force and distance** in hydraulic systems.

### 2.5 Limitations of Pascal's Law

- **Valid only for static fluids** — does not apply to fluids in motion (where dynamic effects become significant).
- **Applicable only to incompressible fluids** — for gases, density changes must be considered.
- **Neglects fluid friction** — real fluids have viscosity, causing minor pressure losses not predicted by the law.
- **Assumes rigid container walls** — elastic walls absorb some energy, reducing transmission efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Pressure Equality in a Static Fluid (Pascal's Law Proof)

**Objective:** Prove that pressure at any two points in a connected static fluid at the same elevation is equal.

**Setup:** Consider two points A and B inside a stationary fluid, connected by an arbitrary path. The fluid is in static equilibrium.

**Step 1:** Consider an infinitesimal fluid element along the path between A and B with cross-sectional area $dA$ and length $ds$.

The force on one face due to pressure $P$ is:
$$dF = P \cdot dA$$

**Step 2:** Along the path element $ds$, the pressure may vary. The net force in the direction of the path is:
$$dF_{net} = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} \cdot dA \cdot ds$$

**Step 3:** For the fluid element to be in equilibrium (no acceleration since fluid is static):
$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Integrating along the entire path from A to B:
$$P_B - P_A = \int_A^B \frac{\partial P}{\partial s} ds = 0$$

**Step 5:** Therefore:
$$P_A = P_B$$

This proves that **pressure is the same at all points at the same elevation in a connected static fluid** — the foundational statement of Pascal's Law.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Force Multiplication in a Hydraulic System

**Setup:** Consider a hydraulic lift with two pistons of areas $A_1$ (small) and $A_2$ (large) connected by an incompressible fluid.

**Step 1:** A small force $F_1$ is applied on the small piston of area $A_1$.

The pressure generated in the fluid below the small piston:
$$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1}$$

**Step 2:** By Pascal's Law, this pressure $P$ is transmitted **undiminished** throughout the fluid. Therefore, the pressure acting on the large piston (area $A_2$) is also:
$$P = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

**Step 3:** Equating the two expressions for pressure:
$$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

**Step 4:** Solving for the output force $F_2$ (the lifting force):
$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

**Step 5:** Since $A_2 > A_1$, we have $F_2 > F_1$. The ratio $A_2/A_1$ is the **mechanical advantage** (MA) of the hydraulic system:
$$\text{MA} = \frac{F_2}{F_1} = \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

**Step 6:** Numerical example — if $A_1 = 10 \text{ cm}^2$ and $A_2 = 500 \text{ cm}^2$, and $F_1 = 100 \text{ N}$:
$$F_2 = 100 \cdot \frac{500}{10} = 5000 \text{ N} = 5 \text{ kN}$$

A modest **100 N** input force lifts a **5000 N** load — a 50-fold force multiplication.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Conservation of Volume (Displacement Relationship)

**Step 1:** When the small piston moves down by a distance $x_1$, it displaces a volume of fluid:
$$V_{displaced} = A_1 \cdot x_1$$

**Step 2:** Since the fluid is incompressible, this exact volume must appear at the large piston:
$$V_{displaced} = A_2 \cdot x_2$$

**Step 3:** Equating:
$$A_1 \cdot x_1 = A_2 \cdot x_2$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, the displacement of the large piston:
$$x_2 = x_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2}$$

**Step 5:** In the numerical example above, if $x_1 = 10 \text{ cm}$:
$$x_2 = 10 \cdot \frac{10}{500} = 0.2 \text{ cm}$$

The large piston moves only **0.2 cm** while the small piston moves **10 cm** — illustrating the trade-off.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Energy Conservation Verification

**Step 1:** Work done by input force:
$$W_{input} = F_1 \cdot x_1$$

**Step 2:** Work done by output force:
$$W_{output} = F_2 \cdot x_2$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the derived relationships:
$$W_{output} = \left(F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}\right) \cdot \left(x_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right) = F_1 \cdot x_1 = W_{input}$$

**Step 4:** This confirms that **energy is conserved** in an ideal hydraulic system (ignoring friction). The hydraulic system does not create energy — it only **transforms a small force over a large distance into a large force over a small distance**.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Problem Identification Guide

| Problem Type | Given Quantities | Find | Primary Formula |
|--------------|------------------|------|-----------------|
| Find output force | $F_1, A_1, A_2$ | $F_2$ | $F_2 = F_1 (A_2/A_1)$ |
| Find input force | $F_2, A_1, A_2$ | $F_1$ | $F_1 = F_2 (A_1/A_2)$ |
| Find area ratio | $F_1, F_2, A_1$ | $A_2$ | $A_2 = A_1 (F_2/F_1)$ |
| Find displacement | $x_1, A_1, A_2$ | $x_2$ | $x_2 = x_1 (A_1/A_2)$ |
| Mechanical Advantage | $A_1, A_2$ | MA | $A_2/A_1$ |
| Pressure in system | $F_1, A_1$ | $P$ | $P = F_1/A_1$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hydraulic Lift System

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the operational flow of a hydraulic lift system, which is the most common application of Pascal's Law:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[InputForceF1] --> B[SmallPistonA1]
    B --> C[PressureGenerationP]
    C --> D[FluidColumnIncompressible]
    D --> E[PressureTransmissionUndiminished]
    E --> F[LargePistonA2]
    F --> G[OutputForceF2]
    G --> H[LoadLifted]

    I[F1dashA1] --> C
    J[F2dashA2] --> C

    K[ConservationOfVolume] --> L[A1xX1eqA2xX2]
    L --> F

    M[EnergyConservation] --> N[F1X1eqF2X2]
    N --> G

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 State Diagram: Pascal's Law Operational Conditions

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StaticFluidCondition
    StaticFluidCondition --> IncompressibilityAssumption
    IncompressibilityAssumption --> PressureAppliedAtPointA
    PressureAppliedAtPointA --> PressurePropagation
    PressurePropagation --> PressureReceivedAtPointB
    PressurePropagation --> PressureReceivedAtPointC
    PressurePropagation --> PressureReceivedAtPointN
    PressureReceivedAtPointB --> PressureEqualityPBeqPApplied
    PressureReceivedAtPointC --> PressureEqualityPBeqPApplied
    PressureReceivedAtPointN --> PressureEqualityPBeqPApplied
    PressureEqualityPBeqPApplied --> HydraulicSystemActivation
    HydraulicSystemActivation --> ForceMultiplication
    ForceMultiplication --> LoadLifted
    LoadLifted --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Pressure Transmission in a Confined Fluid

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Point1] ---|DistanceL1| FL[StaticIncompressibleFluid]
    P2[Point2] ---|DistanceL2| FL
    P3[Point3] ---|DistanceL3| FL
    P4[Point4] ---|DistanceL4| FL
    FL --> R[ResultP1eqP2eqP3eqP4]
    EXT[ExternalPressureApplied] --> P1

    style FL fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style R fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style EXT fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Identifying Pascal's Law Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([ProblemStatement]) --> Q1{ConfinedFluidPresent?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{IncompressibleFluid?}
    Q1 -->|No| X1[NotPascalLawProblem]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{TwoPistonsOrAreas?}
    Q2 -->|No| X2[UseModifiedGasLaws]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{EquilibriumCondition?}
    Q3 -->|No| X3[SinglePistonHydrostatics]
    Q4 -->|Yes| Apply[ApplyPascalLawF1overA1eqF2overA2]
    Q4 -->|No| X4[ConsiderDynamicEffects]
    Apply --> Solve[SolveForUnknownQuantity]
    Solve --> End([FinalAnswer])

    style Start fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Apply fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Solve fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style End fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1:** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**State Pascal's Law and mention any two of its practical applications.**

**Model Answer:**

**Pascal's Law Statement:** Pascal's Law states that the pressure applied to a confined fluid at rest is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid, and acts perpendicular to the surfaces of the containing vessel.

Mathematically: If pressure $P$ is applied at any point in a confined incompressible fluid, then the pressure at every other point in the fluid becomes $P$ (i.e., $P_1 = P_2 = P_3 = \ldots$).

**Two Practical Applications:**

1. **Hydraulic Lift / Hydraulic Jack:** Used in automobile service stations and industries to lift heavy loads using a small input force. The principle relies on pressure transmission from a small piston to a larger piston, multiplying the force.

2. **Hydraulic Brake System:** Used in all modern automobiles. When the brake pedal is pressed, a small force creates pressure in the brake fluid, which is transmitted to the brake pads at each wheel to stop the vehicle.

*(Alternative valid applications: Hydraulic press, hydraulic crane, dentist's chair, hydraulic steering, hydraulic accumulator)*

---

**Question 2:** [KTU University Exam - December 2023]

**A hydraulic press has a ram diameter of 30 cm and a plunger diameter of 5 cm. Find the force exerted by the ram when a force of 200 N is applied on the plunger.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Diameter of plunger (small piston), $d_1 = 5 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- Diameter of ram (large piston), $d_2 = 30 \text{ cm} = 0.30 \text{ m}$
- Force on plunger, $F_1 = 200 \text{ N}$

**Step 1:** Calculate areas of both pistons:
$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$
$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.30)^2 = 7.0686 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Apply Pascal's Law:
$$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $F_2$:
$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1} = 200 \cdot \frac{7.0686 \times 10^{-2}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}}$$
$$F_2 = 200 \cdot 36 = 7200 \text{ N}$$

**Alternative shortcut using diameter ratio:**
$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \left(\frac{d_2}{d_1}\right)^2 = 200 \cdot \left(\frac{30}{5}\right)^2 = 200 \cdot 36 = 7200 \text{ N}$$

**Final Answer:** The force exerted by the ram is $\boxed{F_2 = 7200 \text{ N} = 7.2 \text{ kN}}$.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - December 2024]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the relationship between input force and output force in a hydraulic system based on Pascal's Law. State clearly the assumptions made in the derivation.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A hydraulic lift is used to lift a car weighing 15,000 N. The area of the large piston is 50 times the area of the small piston. Determine:
   (i) The force that must be applied on the small piston to lift the car.
   (ii) The pressure developed in the hydraulic fluid.
   (iii) The distance the small piston must be pushed to raise the car by 25 cm.

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]

**Assumptions:**
1. The hydraulic fluid is **incompressible** (constant density).
2. The fluid is at **rest** (static condition) except for piston motion.
3. The connecting pipes and cylinder are **rigid** (no expansion).
4. **Frictionless** motion (ideal conditions).

**Derivation:**

Consider a hydraulic system consisting of two cylinders of different cross-sectional areas $A_1$ (small) and $A_2$ (large), connected at the bottom by a pipe and filled with an incompressible fluid.

**Step 1:** Let a force $F_1$ be applied on the smaller piston of area $A_1$.

The pressure generated in the fluid beneath the small piston:
$$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1}$$

**Step 2:** According to **Pascal's Law**, this pressure is transmitted **undiminished and equally in all directions** throughout the confined fluid.

**Step 3:** This same pressure $P$ acts on the larger piston of area $A_2$, producing an upward force $F_2$:
$$P = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

**Step 4:** Equating the two expressions:
$$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

**Step 5:** The output force:
$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

This is the fundamental relationship of hydraulic force multiplication.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- Weight of car (load), $W = F_2 = 15{,}000 \text{ N}$
- Area ratio, $A_2/A_1 = 50$
- Lift height, $x_2 = 25 \text{ cm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$

**Part (i): Force on small piston**

From Pascal's Law:
$$F_1 = F_2 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2} = 15{,}000 \cdot \frac{1}{50} = 300 \text{ N}$$

**Part (ii): Pressure in hydraulic fluid**

$$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

Since the ratio is known but individual areas are not, we express the pressure in terms of $A_1$:
$$P = \frac{300}{A_1} \text{ Pa}$$

If, for instance, $A_1 = 5 \text{ cm}^2 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$ (typical value):
$$P = \frac{300}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 6 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 600 \text{ kPa}$$

**Part (iii): Distance small piston must move**

Using conservation of volume:
$$A_1 \cdot x_1 = A_2 \cdot x_2$$
$$x_1 = x_2 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1} = 0.25 \cdot 50 = 12.5 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $F_1 = 300 \text{ N}$
- (ii) $P = 600 \text{ kPa}$ (assuming $A_1 = 5 \text{ cm}^2$)
- (iii) $x_1 = 12.5 \text{ m}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain with a neat diagram the working principle of a hydraulic brake system. How does Pascal's Law apply to this system?

**(b)** [7 Marks] In a hydraulic press, the diameter of the ram is 25 cm and the diameter of the plunger is 2.5 cm. If a force of 80 N is applied on the plunger, calculate:
   (i) The pressure exerted on the fluid.
   (ii) The force exerted by the ram.
   (iii) The distance moved by the ram when the plunger moves down by 40 cm.

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Hydraulic Brake System [7 Marks]

**Working Principle:**

A hydraulic brake system consists of:
1. A **master cylinder** connected to the brake pedal
2. **Brake fluid** (incompressible, usually glycol-based) filling the system
3. **Wheel cylinders** at each wheel, connected via metal pipes
4. **Brake shoes/pads** attached to the wheel cylinders

**Working:**

When the driver presses the brake pedal, the pedal pushes a piston inside the **master cylinder**, creating pressure $P$ in the brake fluid:

$$P = \frac{F_{pedal}}{A_{master}}$$

By **Pascal's Law**, this pressure is transmitted **undiminished and instantly** through the brake fluid to all wheel cylinders simultaneously. The pressure in each wheel cylinder acts on its piston, pushing the brake shoes/pads against the rotating brake disc or drum.

The braking force at each wheel:
$$F_{wheel} = P \cdot A_{wheel}$$

**Application of Pascal's Law:**

Since the pressure is equal at the master cylinder and all wheel cylinders:
$$\frac{F_{pedal}}{A_{master}} = \frac{F_{wheel}}{A_{wheel}}$$

This ensures **equal braking force** at all four wheels (assuming equal wheel cylinder areas), providing balanced and safe braking. Pascal's Law guarantees **simultaneous** application of brakes at all wheels because pressure transmission in an incompressible fluid is virtually instantaneous.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- Diameter of ram, $D = 25 \text{ cm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$
- Diameter of plunger, $d = 2.5 \text{ cm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$
- Force on plunger, $F_1 = 80 \text{ N}$
- Plunger displacement, $x_1 = 40 \text{ cm} = 0.40 \text{ m}$

**Part (i): Pressure on the fluid**

Area of plunger:
$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

Pressure:
$$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{80}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.630 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} \approx 163 \text{ kPa}$$

**Part (ii): Force exerted by the ram**

Area of ram:
$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.25)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$

By Pascal's Law:
$$F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = 1.630 \times 10^5 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-2}$$
$$F_2 = 8000 \text{ N} = 8 \text{ kN}$$

**Shortcut verification:**
$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \left(\frac{D}{d}\right)^2 = 80 \cdot \left(\frac{25}{2.5}\right)^2 = 80 \cdot 100 = 8000 \text{ N}$$

**Part (iii): Distance moved by the ram**

Using conservation of volume:
$$A_1 \cdot x_1 = A_2 \cdot x_2$$
$$x_2 = x_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2} = 0.40 \cdot \frac{4.909 \times 10^{-4}}{4.909 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.40 \cdot \frac{1}{100} = 0.004 \text{ m} = 4 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $P \approx 163 \text{ kPa}$
- (ii) $F_2 = 8000 \text{ N} = 8 \text{ kN}$
- (iii) $x_2 = 4 \text{ mm}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Always convert cm to m before calculating areas in m². Forgetting this is the #1 cause of wrong answers (off by factors of 100 or 10,000).
> 2. **Confusing Diameter and Radius:** Remember the area formula is $A = \pi d^2/4$, NOT $\pi d^2$. A common error is using $A = \pi r$ or omitting the $/4$.
> 3. **Mixing Up Piston 1 and Piston 2:** Always identify which piston is smaller (input) and which is larger (output) before applying the formula.
> 4. **Forgetting Conservation of Volume:** Many students forget the relationship $A_1 x_1 = A_2 x_2$ when asked about displacement.
> 5. **Pressure Units Confusion:** Express final answers in consistent units (Pa or kPa). $1 \text{ kPa} = 1000 \text{ Pa}$.
> 6. **Missing Assumptions in Derivations:** Always state the assumptions (incompressibility, static fluid, no friction) when deriving — KTU examiners specifically look for these.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pascal's Law** states that pressure applied to a confined incompressible fluid is transmitted **equally and undiminished** in all directions throughout the fluid.
- The law is valid **only for static, incompressible fluids** in **rigid containers**.
- The core equation for hydraulic systems is $\dfrac{F_1}{A_1} = \dfrac{F_2}{A_2}$, leading to force multiplication $F_2 = F_1 \cdot (A_2/A_1)$.
- **Conservation of volume** gives the displacement relationship $A_1 x_1 = A_2 x_2$ — small piston moves far, large piston moves a little.
- **Energy is conserved**: $F_1 x_1 = F_2 x_2$ — hydraulic systems do not create energy, they transform force × distance.
- **Key applications:** Hydraulic lift, hydraulic press, hydraulic brake, hydraulic jack, hydraulic crane, dentist's chair, hydraulic steering.
- **Mechanical Advantage (MA)** of a hydraulic system = $A_2/A_1$ (ratio of large to small piston area).
- **Velocity ratio** = $A_2/A_1$ (large piston moves slower than small piston).
- Always convert all dimensions to **SI units (m, N, Pa)** before calculation.
- The area ratio in terms of diameters simplifies to $(d_2/d_1)^2$ — use this shortcut when only diameters are given.
- **Limitation:** Pascal's Law neglects fluid friction and viscosity; real systems have minor efficiency losses.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Pressure - Piezometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Measurement of Pressure - Piezometer # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **PIEZOMETER (KTU 2024 Definition):** > A piezometer is the simplest form of a **manometer** — a vertical, transparent, open-ended tube connected to a pipe or vessel at the point where the fluid pressure is to be measured. The fluid rises inside the tube to a height (called the **piezometric head**) that is directly proportional to the **gauge pressure** at the connection point. The relationship is governed by the fundamental hydrostatic equation: $P = \rho g h$. The piezometer is a **passive, direct-reading, single-fluid** pressure measurement device. It belongs to the class of **hydrostatic pressure gauges**, where the unknown pressure is balanced against the weight of a column of the same (or compatible) fluid under gravity. ### Key Terminology (Aligned to KTU 2024 Glossary) | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Gauge Pressure** ($P_g$) | Pressure measured relative to local atmospheric pressure. | | **Absolute Pressure** ($P_{abs}$) | Pressure measured relative to perfect vacuum. | | **Piezometric Head** ($h$) | Height of the fluid column in the piezometer tube above the tapping point. | | **Centre Line / Tapping Point** | The exact location on the pipe wall where the piezometer is connected. | | **Datum Head / Elevation Head** ($z$) | Vertical height of the tapping point above an arbitrary horizontal reference (datum). | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Straw in a Juice Box" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Imagine a juice box** filled with a thick, sweet drink. You poke a thin, transparent straw vertically into it from the top. Because the liquid inside the box is under a little pressure (from squeezing or due to the depth of liquid), the juice will rise up inside the straw to a height *higher* than the level in the box. > > **The taller the juice climbs, the more pressure there was inside the box.** > > Now map this to engineering: > - The **juice box** = the pipe/vessel containing the pressurized fluid. > - The **straw** = the piezometer tube. > - The **height of juice rising** = the piezometric head $h$. > - The **atmospheric air pushing down on the open top of the straw** = the atmospheric pressure reference. > > This is *exactly* how a piezometer works. The pressure at the tapping point pushes the fluid up against gravity and the atmosphere, until equilibrium is reached. The **weight of the raised fluid column** is a direct measure of the pressure pushing it up. ### Why Engineers Care About This - Piezometers are the **conceptual foundation** of all pressure measurement. - They are also the **physical definition** of "head" — a quantity that appears in **Bernoulli's equation**, **Darcy-Weisbach friction loss**, and **pump power calculations**. - Whenever a KTU problem says *"find the pressure head at a point"*, it is implicitly asking you to apply the piezometer principle. ### Standard Physical Constants to Remember > [!NOTE] > - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (standard KTU value) > - Density of water (at 4°C): $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ > - Specific weight of water: $\gamma_{water} = \rho g = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$ > - Standard atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 10.3 \text{ m of water column}$ --- ## 1.3 Position in the KTU 2024 Syllabus This topic directly supports: - **CO1**: *Apply the principles of fluid statics to solve problems involving pressure measurement and manometry.* - **Module 1 – Fluid Statics**: Pressure definition, pressure variation, and measurement devices. - It is a **prerequisite** for U-tube manometer, differential manometer, and inclined manometer questions. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Hydrostatic Principle Behind a Piezometer The piezometer operates on **Pascal's Law** combined with the **principle of hydrostatics**: > **Pascal's Law (KTU Formulation):** *The pressure at a point in a static fluid is the same in all directions, and depends only on the depth of the point below the free surface (or reference).* The vertical force balance inside the rising fluid column is the operational core. Consider an elemental fluid cylinder of cross-sectional area $A$ at the base of the column. Three vertical forces act on it: 1. **Upward thrust** = Pressure at tapping point × Area = $P \cdot A$ (this is the unknown we want). 2. **Downward weight** of the fluid column above = $\rho g h \cdot A$. 3. **Atmospheric pressure** acts on the *open top* of the column, pushing downward = $P_{atm} \cdot A$. ### Step-by-Step Logic of the Piezometer - **Step 1 — Tap the Pipe:** Drill a small hole (tapping) into the wall of the pipe at the point of interest. This hole must be smooth, perpendicular to the wall, and free of burrs so it does not disturb the flow. - **Step 2 — Connect the Tube:** Fix a transparent glass (or rigid plastic) tube vertically to this tapping using an airtight, leak-proof fitting. - **Step 3 — Fluid Rises:** The internal pressure of the pipe pushes the fluid up the tube. The fluid climbs until the **weight of the column** exactly balances the pressure difference between the pipe and the atmosphere. - **Step 4 — Equilibrium Achieved:** At equilibrium, no fluid motion occurs. The column is static. - **Step 5 — Read the Height:** The height $h$ of the column above the tapping centre-line is measured in metres. - **Step 6 — Compute Pressure:** Substitute $h$ into $P = \rho g h$ to get the **gauge pressure**. --- ## 2.2 The Governing Equation (Piezometer Equation) Starting from the manometric equation (pressure balance along a static fluid path from the tapping point to the free surface of the piezometer tube): $$ P + \rho g z_1 = P_{atm} + \rho g z_2 $$ Where $z_1$ is the elevation of the tapping point and $z_2$ is the elevation of the free surface in the tube. The difference $z_2 - z_1 = h$ is the **piezometric head**. Rearranging, the **gauge pressure** is: $$ P_{gauge} = P - P_{atm} = \rho g (z_2 - z_1) = \rho g h $$ And the **absolute pressure**: $$ P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm} = \rho g h + P_{atm} $$ The **piezometric head** (pressure expressed as a head of fluid): $$ h = \frac{P_{gauge}}{\rho g} $$ --- ## 2.3 The Pressure Head Triangle (Visual Concept) Pressure in a static fluid increases **linearly with depth** below a free surface. If you draw a graph of pressure vs. depth for a piezometer reading: - The **x-axis** is height $z$ above the tapping point. - The **y-axis** is gauge pressure $P_g$. - The relationship is a **straight line** with slope $-\rho g$. This is the famous **"pressure head triangle"** seen in KTU diagrams. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Applications of the Piezometer - **Standpipes in dams:** Engineers embed vertical tubes in the dam body to monitor internal pore-water pressure. - **Groundwater observation wells:** A simple open tube sunk into an aquifer measures the **water table elevation** (which is a piezometric reading). - **Hydraulic laboratories:** Used to calibrate other pressure-measuring devices. - **Flow visualisation in open channels:** Helps determine the **hydraulic grade line (HGL)** by inserting piezometer taps at various points along the channel. --- ## 2.5 ⚡ KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bookmark this table. Every piezometer problem in KTU exams is solved using one of these formulas.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Gauge pressure | $P_g = \rho g h$ | Pa (N/m²) | Most-used KTU formula | | 2 | Absolute pressure | $P_{abs} = P_g + P_{atm}$ | Pa | Use $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa | | 3 | Piezometric head | $h = \dfrac{P_g}{\rho g}$ | m | Height of fluid column | | 4 | Specific weight | $\gamma = \rho g$ | N/m³ | For water, $\gamma = 9810$ N/m³ | | 5 | Pressure in terms of head | $P = \gamma h$ | Pa | Compact form | | 6 | Pressure in metres of water | $h_{m\ of\ water} = \dfrac{P}{9.81}$ | m | Quick mental conversion | | 7 | Vacuum / Negative gauge | $P_{vac} = -P_g$ | Pa | When $P_{abs} < P_{atm}$ | | 8 | Pressure variation with depth | $\dfrac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$ | Pa/m | Negative sign: $P$ drops as $z$ rises | ### ⚠️ Unit Conversion Cheats for KTU Problems > [!NOTE] > KTU examiners love mixing units. Memorize these: > - $1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 10.3 \text{ m of water} = 760 \text{ mm of Hg}$ > - $1 \text{ bar} = 100 \text{ kPa} = 10.2 \text{ m of water}$ > - $1 \text{ m of water column} = 9.81 \text{ kPa}$ > - $1 \text{ mm of Hg} = 133.32 \text{ Pa}$ --- ## 2.6 Limitations of a Piezometer (Frequently Asked in KTU 2-Mark Questions) 1. **Can only measure positive gauge pressure.** If the pipe pressure is *less* than atmospheric (a vacuum), the fluid will be *pushed down* inside the tube below the tapping point, possibly drawing air in. 2. **Impractical for high pressures.** A 1 MPa pressure requires a water column of $\approx 102$ m — not feasible in a laboratory. 3. **Cannot measure gas pressures.** A gas has very low density; the column would be impractically tall. 4. **Requires a transparent fluid.** The fluid being measured must itself be visible in the tube. 5. **Single-fluid limitation.** Cannot be used if the pipe contains a fluid that is *incompatible* with the tube material (e.g., corrosive chemicals may damage the glass). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Pascal's Law to $P = \rho g h$ We derive the piezometer equation rigorously from first principles. ### Setup Consider a pipe carrying a static liquid of density $\rho$. A piezometer tube of cross-sectional area $A$ is connected at a tapping point **T** located at elevation $z_T$ above an arbitrary horizontal datum. The fluid rises inside the tube to a free surface at elevation $z_F$. The atmosphere acts on this free surface with pressure $P_{atm}$. ### Step 1: Define the Pressure at the Tapping Point Let the pressure at point **T** be $P_T$ (in Pa, absolute). This is the quantity we want to find. ### Step 2: Apply the Fundamental Hydrostatic Equation For any static fluid, the pressure difference between two points is given by: $$ P_2 - P_1 = -\rho g (z_2 - z_1) $$ The negative sign appears because pressure *increases* in the direction of gravity (downward). We apply this between the free surface (point 1) and the tapping point (point 2): $$ P_T - P_{atm} = -\rho g (z_T - z_F) $$ ### Step 3: Explain the Sign in Words The free surface is **above** the tapping point, so $z_T - z_F$ is **negative**. Multiplying by $-\rho g$ makes the right-hand side **positive** — exactly as it should be, since $P_T > P_{atm}$ for a positive gauge pressure. ### Step 4: Rearrange to Isolate the Gauge Pressure $$ P_T - P_{atm} = \rho g (z_F - z_T) $$ ### Step 5: Define the Piezometric Head Let $h = z_F - z_T$ be the height of the fluid column in the tube above the tapping point. By convention, $h > 0$ for a normal piezometer reading. ### Step 6: Write the Final Form $$ P_T - P_{atm} = \rho g h $$ ### Step 7: Identify the Left Side as Gauge Pressure The difference between absolute pressure and atmospheric pressure is, by definition, the **gauge pressure**: $$ \boxed{P_{gauge} = \rho g h} $$ ### Step 8: Express the Absolute Pressure $$ P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm} = \rho g h + P_{atm} $$ ### Physical Interpretation Each step has a clear meaning. Step 1 anchors the unknown. Step 2 invokes Pascal's Law. Step 3–4 perform algebraic manipulation. Step 5 introduces the measured quantity. Step 6–8 convert to the two KTU-asked forms (gauge and absolute). --- ## 3.2 Analytical Example 1 — Standard 3-Mark KTU Question > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]** > *A piezometer is connected to a pipe carrying water. The water column rises to a height of **3.5 m** above the tapping point. Determine (a) the gauge pressure, and (b) the absolute pressure at the tapping point.* **Given:** - Height of water column, $h = 3.5$ m - Density of water, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ - Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.81$ m/s² - Atmospheric pressure, $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa **Solution:** **(a) Gauge Pressure:** We apply the piezometer equation: $$ P_{gauge} = \rho g h $$ Substituting the values: $$ P_{gauge} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 3.5 $$ $$ P_{gauge} = 9810 \times 3.5 $$ $$ P_{gauge} = 34{,}335 \text{ Pa} $$ $$ \boxed{P_{gauge} = 34.335 \text{ kPa}} $$ **(b) Absolute Pressure:** $$ P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm} $$ $$ P_{abs} = 34.335 + 101.325 $$ $$ \boxed{P_{abs} = 135.66 \text{ kPa}} $$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Example 2 — Standard 7-Mark KTU Question > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** > *A pipe carrying oil (specific gravity 0.85) has a piezometer attached to it. The oil rises in the piezometer to a height of **2.8 m** above the centre-line of the pipe. If the tapping point is **1.2 m** below the free surface of a nearby reservoir open to the atmosphere, find: (a) the gauge pressure at the tapping point, and (b) the absolute pressure in kPa.* **Given:** - Specific gravity of oil, $S = 0.85 \Rightarrow \rho_{oil} = 850$ kg/m³ - Height of oil column in piezometer, $h = 2.8$ m - Depth of tapping point below reservoir surface, $d = 1.2$ m - Density of water, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³ - $g = 9.81$ m/s², $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa **Solution:** **(a) Gauge Pressure at the Tapping Point:** The piezometer reading gives the pressure *directly*, regardless of the reservoir. The fluid in the piezometer is the oil from the pipe (since oil is what is in the pipe). We use: $$ P_{gauge} = \rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot h $$ $$ P_{gauge} = 850 \times 9.81 \times 2.8 $$ $$ P_{gauge} = 8338.5 \times 2.8 $$ $$ P_{gauge} = 23{,}347.8 \text{ Pa} $$ $$ \boxed{P_{gauge} \approx 23.35 \text{ kPa}} $$ **(b) Absolute Pressure:** $$ P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm} $$ $$ P_{abs} = 23.35 + 101.325 $$ $$ \boxed{P_{abs} \approx 124.67 \text{ kPa}} $$ **Note for students:** The reservoir depth of 1.2 m is **extra information** (a distractor) for this particular question. KTU examiners use this trick to test whether students can identify *which* fluid column corresponds to *which* pressure difference. The piezometer reading is independent of the reservoir location. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Example 3 — Vacuum / Negative Gauge Pressure > **[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Style]** > *A piezometer connected to a suction line of a pump shows a reading of **0.4 m below** the tapping point. The fluid is water. Find the gauge pressure and the vacuum pressure at the tapping point.* **Given:** - Height, $h = -0.4$ m (negative because it falls *below* the tapping point) - $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s² **Solution:** $$ P_{gauge} = \rho g h = 1000 \times 9.81 \times (-0.4) = -3924 \text{ Pa} $$ $$ \boxed{P_{gauge} = -3.924 \text{ kPa (vacuum)}} $$ The vacuum pressure is the magnitude of the negative gauge pressure: $$ P_{vac} = 3.924 \text{ kPa} $$ The negative sign confirms that the pressure at the tapping point is **below atmospheric** — typical of a pump suction line. --- ## 3.5 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Formula Do I Use?" | Situation in the problem | Fluid type | Formula to apply | |---|---|---| | "Piezometer rises by $h$ m" | Same fluid as in pipe | $P_g = \rho_{pipe} g h$ | | "Oil rises by $h$ m in piezometer" | Oil | $P_g = \rho_{oil} g h = 0.85 \times 1000 \times g \times h$ | | "Piezometer fluid is mercury" (advanced) | Mercury | $P_g = \rho_{Hg} g h = 13600 \times g \times h$ | | "Fluid falls below tapping" | Negative head | $P_g = \rho g (-h)$ (vacuum) | | "Find pressure in metres of water" | Head form | $h_{water} = P / (\rho_w g)$ | | "Absolute pressure asked" | Always add $P_{atm}$ | $P_{abs} = P_g + 101.325$ kPa | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Physical Setup of a Piezometer (Mermaid Block Diagram) The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **functional architecture** of a piezometer measurement system. Note: physical pipe schematics are difficult to render reliably in Mermaid, so this block diagram maps the **component interactions** faithfully. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Pipe with Pressurized Fluid"] --> B["Drilled Tapping Point on Pipe Wall"] B --> C["Airtight Fitting Coupling"] C --> D["Vertical Transparent Tube (Piezometer)"] D --> E["Fluid Column Rising Inside Tube"] E --> F["Free Surface Open to Atmosphere"] F --> G["Atmospheric Pressure P_atm acts Downward"] A --> H["Internal Pressure P at Tapping"] H --> I["Pressure Difference P minus P_atm pushes Fluid Up"] I --> E E --> J["Equilibrium Reached: Weight of Column balances P minus P_atm"] J --> K["Measure Height h of Column above Tapping"] K --> L["Apply Formula P_gauge = rho times g times h"] L --> M["Final Reading: Gauge Pressure in Pascals"] ``` **How to read this diagram (KTU 2024 pedagogical note):** - Follow the **top path**: Pipe → Tapping → Tube → Fluid rises → Free surface → Atmosphere pushes back. - Follow the **right loop**: Internal pressure pushes the fluid up, atmospheric pressure pushes it down, equilibrium gives us the height $h$. - The **final node** "Apply Formula" is the computational step a student performs on the answer sheet. --- ## 4.2 Pressure Head Triangle (Conceptual Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Elevation z_F: Free Surface
Pressure = P_atm"] --> B["Linear Pressure Variation
Slope = minus rho times g"] B --> C["Elevation z_T: Tapping Point
Pressure = P_atm plus rho times g times h"] A -.Gauge Pressure = 0.-> A C -.Gauge Pressure = rho g h.-> C D["Piezometer Tube"] --- A E["Pipe Wall"] --- C D --- F["Vertical Height h
measured here"] E --- F ``` **Interpretation:** The graph on the left is the classical "pressure head triangle." As you go *deeper* (from free surface to tapping point), pressure rises linearly. The slope of the line is $\rho g$, and the **area under the triangle** is the **gauge pressure** $\rho g h$. --- ## 4.3 Component-Level Block Architecture of a Piezometer System ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph S1["Measurement Side"] P1["Pressurized Pipe"] T1["Tapping Point (Drilled Hole)"] F1["Airtight Flange / Coupling"] end subgraph S2["Sensing Element"] T2["Vertical Glass Tube of Length L"] C1["Fluid Column of Height h"] S3["Free Surface (Open to Air)"] end subgraph S3["Readout Side"] R1["Scale (Ruler) Marked in cm or mm"] R2["Visual Indication of h"] end subgraph S4["Computational Stage"] K1["Student / Engineer"] K2["Equation P_gauge = rho g h"] K3["Result in Pa or kPa"] end P1 --> T1 --> F1 --> T2 --> C1 --> S3 C1 --> R1 S3 --> R2 R1 --> K1 --> K2 --> K3 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, Dec 2023]** > **Q:** *What is a piezometer? Mention any two of its limitations.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A piezometer is a simple, vertical, open-ended transparent tube connected to a pipe or vessel at the point where pressure is to be measured. The fluid rises in the tube to a height $h$ above the tapping point, and the gauge pressure is calculated as $P_g = \rho g h$. **Two limitations:** 1. It can measure only **positive gauge pressures** (not vacuums or high pressures). 2. It is **impractical for gases** and **high-pressure liquids** (the required column height becomes excessive). *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for two limitations.)* --- > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, May 2024]** > **Q:** *A piezometer connected to a water pipe shows a column height of 2.4 m. Calculate the gauge pressure at the tapping point.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Given:** $h = 2.4$ m, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s². **Formula:** $$ P_{gauge} = \rho g h $$ **Substitution:** $$ P_{gauge} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 2.4 $$ $$ P_{gauge} = 23{,}544 \text{ Pa} = 23.544 \text{ kPa} $$ *(Award 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final answer.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert units** (e.g., using $g = 981$ cm/s² instead of $9.81$ m/s²) is the **#1 cause of mark deduction**. Always write units explicitly. > 2. **Using density of water (1000 kg/m³) for oil or mercury** is a fatal error. Use $\rho = S \times 1000$ for any fluid with given specific gravity. > 3. **Confusing gauge and absolute pressure** — if the question says "absolute," you MUST add $P_{atm}$. KTU examiners specifically check this. > 4. **Ignoring the sign convention** for vacuum readings: $h$ is negative, so the resulting gauge pressure is negative. > 5. **Writing only the final answer** without showing the substitution step is penalized — KTU 2024 Scheme awards step marks. --- ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, 14-Mark Variant]** > **(a)** Derive the expression for the gauge pressure measured by a piezometer in terms of the height of the fluid column, density, and acceleration due to gravity. List any **four** limitations of a piezometer. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A piezometer attached to a pipe carrying oil (specific gravity 0.8) shows a column of oil rising to 4.2 m above the tapping point. The tapping point is located 1.5 m below the free surface of an open water reservoir. Calculate (i) the gauge pressure at the tapping point, and (ii) the absolute pressure at the tapping point in kPa. **(7 marks)** #### 📝 Model Solution for Question A ##### Part (a) — Derivation and Limitations (7 marks) **Derivation (5 marks):** Consider a piezometer tube connected to a pipe at tapping point **T** where the pressure is $P_T$ (absolute). The fluid of density $\rho$ rises in the tube to a free surface **F** open to the atmosphere ($P_{atm}$). Applying the fundamental equation of hydrostatics between the free surface (point 1) and the tapping point (point 2): $$ P_T - P_{atm} = -\rho g (z_T - z_F) $$ The difference in elevation between the free surface and the tapping point is $h = z_F - z_T$. Substituting: $$ P_T - P_{atm} = \rho g h $$ By definition, the **gauge pressure** is: $$ \boxed{P_{gauge} = \rho g h} $$ The **absolute pressure** is: $$ \boxed{P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm} = \rho g h + P_{atm}} $$ **Four Limitations (2 marks — 0.5 mark each):** 1. Cannot measure **vacuum or negative gauge pressure** (the fluid drops below the tapping, but air leaks in instead of rising). 2. Impractical for **high pressures** (a 1000 kPa pressure needs a ~102 m water column). 3. Cannot be used for **gases** (column would be impractically tall due to low gas density). 4. Requires the **fluid to be transparent and compatible** with the tube material; not suitable for opaque, corrosive, or high-temperature fluids. --- ##### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks) **Given Data:** - Specific gravity of oil: $S = 0.8$ → $\rho_{oil} = 800$ kg/m³ - Piezometer column height: $h = 4.2$ m - Depth of tapping below water surface: 1.5 m (distractor — not needed for the piezometer calculation) - $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa **Step 1: Identify the relevant fluid column.** The piezometer is connected directly to the pipe carrying oil. The fluid rising inside the piezometer is therefore **oil**, not water. The 1.5 m reservoir depth is irrelevant to this part. **Step 2: Compute the gauge pressure.** $$ P_{gauge} = \rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot h $$ $$ P_{gauge} = 800 \times 9.81 \times 4.2 $$ $$ P_{gauge} = 7848 \times 4.2 $$ $$ P_{gauge} = 32{,}961.6 \text{ Pa} $$ $$ \boxed{P_{gauge} \approx 32.96 \text{ kPa}} $$ **Step 3: Compute the absolute pressure.** $$ P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm} $$ $$ P_{abs} = 32.96 + 101.325 $$ $$ \boxed{P_{abs} \approx 134.29 \text{ kPa}} $$ *(Mark distribution: 1 mark for choosing correct fluid density, 2 marks for formula and substitution, 1 mark for gauge pressure answer, 1 mark for adding atmospheric pressure, 1 mark for absolute pressure answer, 1 mark for unit consistency.)* --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, 14-Mark Variant]** > **(a)** Explain the working principle of a piezometer with the help of a neat sketch. State the formula used. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** The pressure at a point in a pipeline carrying water is measured using a piezometer. The height of the water column in the piezometer is 1.8 m. The tapping point is 3 m below the free surface of an open tank. Determine (i) the gauge pressure at the tapping point in kPa, and (ii) the pressure at the same point if the fluid in the pipe were replaced by a liquid of specific gravity 1.2, the piezometer reading remaining the same. **(7 marks)** #### 📝 Model Solution for Question B ##### Part (a) — Working Principle and Sketch (7 marks) A piezometer is a simple device consisting of a **vertical, transparent, open-ended tube** connected to a pipe or vessel at the point where pressure is to be measured. The pressure at the tapping point **T** pushes the fluid up the tube. The fluid rises to a height $h$ above the tapping point, where the weight of the column balances the pressure difference between the pipe and the atmosphere. **Sketch (described in words for 3 marks):** - Draw a horizontal pipe. - Mark the tapping point **T** on the pipe. - Draw a vertical tube rising from **T**. - Show the fluid column of height $h$. - Mark the free surface **F** at the top (open to atmosphere). - Label $P$ (internal pipe pressure), $P_{atm}$ (atmospheric), and $h$ (column height). **Working Equation:** $$ P_{gauge} = P - P_{atm} = \rho g h $$ Where: - $P$ = absolute pressure at the tapping point - $P_{atm}$ = atmospheric pressure - $\rho$ = density of the fluid - $g$ = acceleration due to gravity - $h$ = height of fluid column in the piezometer above the tapping point *(Mark distribution: 2 marks for principle explanation, 3 marks for neat sketch with all labels, 2 marks for the formula.)* --- ##### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks) **Given Data:** - Case 1: Water, $h = 1.8$ m, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³ - Case 2: Liquid with $S = 1.2$, $\rho = 1200$ kg/m³, $h = 1.8$ m (same) - $g = 9.81$ m/s² - Depth of tapping below tank surface: 3 m (distractor) **Step 1: Gauge pressure with water (3 marks).** $$ P_{gauge,1} = \rho_w g h = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 1.8 $$ $$ P_{gauge,1} = 9810 \times 1.8 $$ $$ P_{gauge,1} = 17{,}658 \text{ Pa} $$ $$ \boxed{P_{gauge,1} = 17.658 \text{ kPa}} $$ **Step 2: Pressure with the heavier liquid (4 marks).** Since the piezometer reading $h$ is the **same**, but the density is now 1.2 times that of water: $$ P_{gauge,2} = \rho_{liquid} g h = 1200 \times 9.81 \times 1.8 $$ $$ P_{gauge,2} = 11{,}772 \times 1.8 $$ $$ P_{gauge,2} = 21{,}189.6 \text{ Pa} $$ $$ \boxed{P_{gauge,2} \approx 21.19 \text{ kPa}} $$ **Physical Insight (for 1 bonus mark in valuation):** The same column height produces a **larger pressure** when the fluid is denser. This is why mercury manometers (with $S = 13.6$) can be used for very high pressures in compact tubes — the dense fluid converts a small height into a large pressure reading. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **🎯 KTU 2024 Piezometer — Last-Minute Revision Bullets** - ✅ **Definition:** Piezometer = vertical, open-ended, transparent tube used to measure gauge pressure. - ✅ **Core Equation:** $\mathbf{P_{gauge} = \rho g h}$ — the most important formula. - ✅ **Absolute vs Gauge:** $\mathbf{P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}}$ (use $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa unless stated otherwise). - ✅ **Head form:** $h = P / (\rho g)$ — gives pressure in **metres of fluid column**. - ✅ **Standard values to memorize:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³. - ✅ **Specific gravity rule:** $\rho = S \times 1000$ kg/m³ (water at 4°C is the reference). - ✅ **Sign convention:** Fluid rises above tapping → positive $h$ → positive $P_g$. Fluid falls below tapping → negative $h$ → vacuum. - ✅ **Atmospheric conversions:** 1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 10.3 m H₂O = 760 mm Hg. - ✅ **Distractor trap:** KTU problems often mention a reservoir or tank height — **ignore it** unless the question is about hydrostatics of the reservoir itself. - ✅ **Limitation list (memorize 4):** No vacuum, no high pressure, no gases, requires transparent fluid. - ✅ **Common unit mistake:** Forgetting to convert cm or mm of water column into metres before applying $\rho g h$. - ✅ **Connection to Bernoulli:** Piezometric head appears in the **hydraulic grade line (HGL)** in Bernoulli's equation. - ✅ **Examiner's tip:** Always show **units at every step** of numerical problems — KTU 2024 awards step marks for clear working.

topics.title: Manometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Manometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
A **manometer** is a U-tube (or modified U-tube) device used to measure fluid pressure by balancing the unknown pressure against a column of a known manometric fluid under hydrostatic equilibrium. It is a direct-application instrument of the fundamental hydrostatic equation,

$$P = \rho g h$$

operated on the principle that **fluid pressure at the same horizontal level within a continuous, static fluid is equal**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024, Module 1):** Manometers belong to the core sub-topic *"Fluid Statics – Pressure Measurement"*. KTU examiners frequently test: (i) classification, (ii) piezometer limitations, and (iii) the two-step "go-down + go-up" pressure walk method.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bathroom-Scale Analogy
Imagine a sealed transparent tube bent into a "U" shape, partially filled with mercury. When you blow into one end, the mercury column on the other side **rises**. The harder you blow, the higher it rises. The manometer works on the **exact same idea** — a pressure you cannot see is converted into a **visible liquid column height** that you can directly read with a ruler. The fluid "remembers" the pressure and reports it as a height.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- Standard specific weight of **water at 4 °C**: $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$ (often rounded to **9.81 kN/m³**)
- Standard specific weight of **mercury at 0 °C**: $\gamma_{Hg} = 13.6 \times 9.81 = 133.416 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- Standard **atmospheric pressure**: $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kN/m}^2 \approx 101.3 \text{ kPa}$
- 1 m of water column ≈ **9.81 kN/m²**; 1 cm of Hg ≈ **1.333 kN/m²**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU board problems, students are expected to use **$\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$** (NOT $9810 \text{ N/m}^3$ in many cases) and report final answers in **kN/m²** unless otherwise specified. Always state whether the answer is **gauge** or **absolute**.

### 1.4 Classification of Manometers
| Category | Type | Manometric Fluid |
|----------|------|------------------|
| **Simple** | Piezometer | None (same fluid) |
| **Simple** | U-Tube Manometer | Mercury / Heavy liquid |
| **Differential** | U-Tube Differential | Mercury / Heavy liquid |
| **Differential** | Inverted U-Tube | Light liquid (oil) |
| **Special** | Inclined Manometer | Light liquid (for small $\Delta P$) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Pressure Walk" Method
Every manometer problem in KTU is solved using one universal logic:

1. **Start** at the point of known (or unknown) pressure.
2. **Walk through the fluid**, adding pressure when going *down* ($\gamma h$) and subtracting when going *up* ($-\gamma h$).
3. **Stop** at a horizontal level where pressure is the **same on both limbs** (the manometric fluid interface).
4. **Equate** the two expressions and solve for the unknown.

> The "horizontal-level" rule is the **only** place where pressure must be equal. Anywhere else, the hydrostatic equation applies freely.

### 2.2 Why Mercury is the Default Manometric Fluid
- Mercury is **13.6 times denser** than water → small pressures produce **large readable deflections**.
- Mercury is **immiscible** with water and most oils → clean, stable interface.
- Mercury has a **low vapor pressure** → accurate readings in vacuum service.
- Mercury columns are **short** → manometer is compact and portable.

> [!TIP]
> For very small pressure differences, a **heavier fluid** (e.g., mercury-thallium alloy, SG ≈ 13.6+) is used. For **vacuum** or **low pressure** measurements, a **lighter fluid** (oil, SG < 1) is used in an inverted U-tube.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below assume **uniform fluid columns** in straight vertical segments. For inclined manometers, $h = L \sin\theta$ (where $L$ is the inclined length).

| # | Manometer Type | Governing Equation | Units |
|---|----------------|--------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Piezometer** | $P_A = \gamma_1 \, h$ | kN/m² |
| 2 | **U-Tube (open to atm)** | $P_A = P_{atm} + \gamma_2 \, h - \gamma_1 \, y$ | kN/m² |
| 3 | **U-Tube Differential** | $P_A - P_B = (\gamma_2 - \gamma_1) \, h + \gamma_1 (y_A - y_B)$ | kN/m² |
| 4 | **Inverted U-Tube** | $P_A - P_B = (\gamma_1 - \gamma_o) \, h$ | kN/m² |
| 5 | **Inclined Manometer** | $P_A - P_{atm} = \gamma_2 \, L \sin\theta$ | kN/m² |
| 6 | **Pressure Head Conversion** | $h = P \,/\, \gamma_w$ | m of water |

Where:
- $\gamma_1$ = specific weight of pipe fluid
- $\gamma_2$ = specific weight of manometric fluid (heavier)
- $\gamma_o$ = specific weight of oil in inverted U-tube
- $h$ = difference in manometer-fluid levels
- $y$ = vertical distance from pipe center to lower interface
- $L$ = length read along the inclined tube
- $\theta$ = angle of inclination with the horizontal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute Value Convention:** Whenever a manometer reading represents a *magnitude*, write it as $h = \vert h_1 - h_2 \vert$ or $h = \vert \Delta z \vert$ using $\vert \cdots \vert$ (LaTeX `\vert`) to avoid the markdown table syntax conflict. The *sign* of $P_A - P_B$ is decided by the physical configuration, not the formula.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Hydraulic & Pneumatic Systems:** Field-testing pipe pressure without electronic transducers.
- **Aircraft Altitude Measurement:** Barometric manometers in altimeters.
- **Medical Field:** Sphygmomanometers (blood-pressure cuffs) are manometers calibrated in mmHg.
- **Boiler & Steam Plants:** Steam pressure verification where electronics may fail under heat.
- **Process Industries:** Differential manometers measure flow rate through orifice plates (related to Module 2/3 topics).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Simple U-Tube Manometer (Open Limb)

**Setup:** Pipe A carries a fluid of specific weight $\gamma_1$. A U-tube containing a heavier manometric fluid ($\gamma_2$) connects A to the atmosphere. The distance from A to the mercury interface on the left limb is $y$. The mercury level on the right (open) limb is higher by $h$ than the left interface.

**Step 1 — Identify the equal-pressure level.**
