The lower mercury interface (left limb) is chosen as the reference. At this horizontal line, pressure on both sides must be equal.

**Step 2 — Express pressure on the left limb (starting at A, going down to the interface).**
Going from A downward through the pipe fluid by a vertical distance $y$ **adds** $\gamma_1 y$:

$$P_{left} = P_A + \gamma_1 \, y$$

**Step 3 — Express pressure on the right limb (starting at the open end, going down to the same interface).**
The open end sees $P_{atm}$. Going down through mercury by a vertical distance $h$ **adds** $\gamma_2 h$:

$$P_{right} = P_{atm} + \gamma_2 \, h$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for $P_A$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_A + \gamma_1 \, y &= P_{atm} + \gamma_2 \, h \\
P_A &= P_{atm} + \gamma_2 \, h - \gamma_1 \, y \\
P_{A,\text{gauge}} &= P_A - P_{atm} = \gamma_2 \, h - \gamma_1 \, y
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Recap:** Each $\gamma h$ term carries the **direction** of the walk. Going down = +, going up = −. The mercury column $h$ is *added* on the right because we are descending from the open end toward the equal-pressure level.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Differential U-Tube Manometer

**Setup:** Two points A and B in a pipeline contain the same fluid ($\gamma_1$). A U-tube manometer with heavier fluid ($\gamma_2$) connects them. The left interface (A-side) is **lower** than the right interface (B-side) by $h$. Distance from A down to its interface is $y_A$, from B down to its interface is $y_B$.

**Step 1 — Choose the lower interface as the reference (left side).**

**Step 2 — Pressure on the left limb:**

$$P_{left} = P_A + \gamma_1 \, y_A$$

**Step 3 — Pressure on the right limb:**
Walk from B downward through pipe fluid by $y_B$ → reach right interface → continue downward through mercury by $h$ to reach the same reference level:

$$P_{right} = P_B + \gamma_1 \, y_B + \gamma_2 \, h$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_A + \gamma_1 \, y_A &= P_B + \gamma_1 \, y_B + \gamma_2 \, h \\
P_A - P_B &= \gamma_2 \, h + \gamma_1 \, y_B - \gamma_1 \, y_A \\
P_A - P_B &= \gamma_2 \, h - \gamma_1 \, (y_A - y_B)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Special Case:** If the pipe centerline A and B are at the **same elevation** ($y_A = y_B$):

$$P_A - P_B = (\gamma_2 - \gamma_1) \, h$$

> This is the **most-asked** differential manometer form in KTU board papers.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Case Table

| Physical Condition | Effect on $h$ | Effect on $P_A$ (for U-tube, open) | KTU Insight |
|--------------------|---------------|-------------------------------------|-------------|
| $\gamma_2$ increases (heavier manometric fluid) | $h$ decreases | Same $P_A$ produced with smaller $h$ | Confirms why mercury is chosen |
| Pipe fluid density $\gamma_1$ increases | $h$ decreases (slightly) | Reading is partially *cancelled* | Don't ignore the $\gamma_1 y$ term! |
| $P_A > P_{atm}$ | Right mercury rises, left falls | $h$ is **positive** in our formula | Standard configuration |
| $P_A < P_{atm}$ (vacuum) | Left mercury rises, right falls | $h$ becomes *negative* in formula | Use **inverted U-tube** for clarity |
| Pipe inclined at $\theta$ | Only vertical $h$ matters | Tilt does **not** change $P_A$ reading | Trick question in Part A |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Full KTU-Style Solution

**Problem:** A pipe containing oil (SG = 0.8) is connected to a U-tube mercury manometer. The center of the pipe is 20 cm above the lower mercury interface. The right limb (open to atmosphere) reads a mercury column 30 cm above the lower interface. Determine the gauge pressure at the pipe center in **kN/m²**.

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SG of oil} &= 0.8 \\
\text{SG of mercury} &= 13.6 \\
\gamma_w &= 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3 \\
y &= 20 \text{ cm} = 0.20 \text{ m} \\
h &= 30 \text{ cm} = 0.30 \text{ m} \\
\gamma_1 &= 0.8 \times 9.81 = 7.848 \text{ kN/m}^3 \\
\gamma_2 &= 13.6 \times 9.81 = 133.416 \text{ kN/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Applying the U-tube formula (Derivation 1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{A,\text{gauge}} &= \gamma_2 \, h - \gamma_1 \, y \\
&= (133.416)(0.30) - (7.848)(0.20) \\
&= 40.0248 - 1.5696 \\
&= 38.4552 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $P_{A,\text{gauge}} \approx \mathbf{38.46 \text{ kN/m}^2}$ (positive, so pipe is **above** atmospheric).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation Check (KTU Style):** Formula — 2 marks, substitution — 2 marks, computation — 2 marks, units & conclusion — 1 mark. Always show units in every substitution step.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Manometer Classification

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[MANOMETER] --> nodeB[SIMPLE MANOMETER]
    nodeA --> nodeC[DIFFERENTIAL MANOMETER]
    nodeA --> nodeD[SPECIAL MANOMETER]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[PIEZOMETER]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[U TUBE MANOMETER OPEN]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[U TUBE MANOMETER CLOSED]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[U TUBE DIFFERENTIAL]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[INVERTED U TUBE]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[INCLINED MANOMETER]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[MICROMANOMETER]
    nodeB1 --> note1[Measures pressure of liquid only]
    nodeB2 --> note2[Uses mercury for high pressure]
    nodeC2 --> note3[Uses oil for low pressure or vacuum]
    nodeD1 --> note4[Measures small pressure differences]
```

### 4.2 Operational Flow — Pressure Walk Algorithm

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeP1[START AT KNOWN PRESSURE P] --> nodeP2[GO DOWN ADDS GAMMA TIMES H]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3[GO UP SUBTRACTS GAMMA TIMES H]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4[REACH EQUAL PRESSURE LEVEL]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5[EQUATE TWO EXPRESSIONS]
    nodeP5 --> nodeP6[SOLVE FOR UNKNOWN PRESSURE]
    nodeP6 --> nodeP7[CONVERT TO REQUESTED UNIT]
    nodeP7 --> nodeP8[REPORT GAUGE OR ABSOLUTE]
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture — Differential Manometer Setup

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PipeSystem[PIPELINE SYSTEM]
        nodeX1[Pipe A point with fluid gamma1] --- nodeX2[Pipe B point with fluid gamma1]
    end
    subgraph ManometerTube[U TUBE MANOMETER WITH MERCURY]
        nodeY1[Left limb connected to A] --- nodeY2[Right limb connected to B]
        nodeY1 --> nodeY3[Mercury interface difference h]
        nodeY3 --> nodeY2
    end
    PipeSystem --- ManometerTube
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this diagram style:** Manometers are *physical* devices, and exact physical sketches are difficult to render in Mermaid. The block architecture above conveys the **functional interaction** (which pipe connects to which limb, where the interfaces lie) — this is the conceptual level KTU examiners reward in Part B "with neat sketch" questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define a manometer. State the principle on which it works.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A manometer is a device used to measure the pressure of a fluid (liquid or gas) at a point, by balancing it against the pressure due to a column of a known fluid under hydrostatic equilibrium. The principle is the **hydrostatic pressure equation** $P = \gamma h$ combined with the **law of equal pressure at the same horizontal level in a static continuous fluid**. The unknown pressure is computed from the measured height difference of the manometric fluid. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between a piezometer and a U-tube manometer. Mention one limitation of a piezometer.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Piezometer | U-Tube Manometer |
|---------|------------|------------------|
| Fluid used | Same as pipe fluid | Manometric fluid (heavier, e.g., mercury) |
| Pressure range | Limited (must be within column length) | Wide (mercury amplifies reading) |
| Gases | Cannot measure gas pressure | Can measure both gas and liquid pressure |
| Sensitivity | Low | High |

**Limitation of piezometer:** It cannot measure the pressure of gases and is unsuitable for very high pressures because the column height becomes impractically large. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full Worked Solution**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** Two pipes A and B carrying water are connected by a differential U-tube manometer containing mercury (SG = 13.6). The center of pipe A is 25 cm above the lower mercury interface (left limb). The center of pipe B is 15 cm above the higher mercury interface (right limb). The mercury level difference is 20 cm. Determine:

**(a)** The pressure difference between A and B in kN/m². **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the pressure at A is 150 kN/m² (absolute), find the absolute pressure at B. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_1 &= 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3 \quad (\text{water}) \\
\gamma_2 &= 13.6 \times 9.81 = 133.416 \text{ kN/m}^3 \quad (\text{mercury}) \\
y_A &= 25 \text{ cm} = 0.25 \text{ m} \\
y_B &= 15 \text{ cm} = 0.15 \text{ m} \\
h &= 20 \text{ cm} = 0.20 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Apply the differential U-tube formula (Derivation 2):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_A - P_B &= \gamma_2 \, h - \gamma_1 \, (y_A - y_B) \\
&= (133.416)(0.20) - (9.81)(0.25 - 0.15) \\
&= 26.6832 - 0.981 \\
&= 25.7022 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $P_A - P_B \approx \mathbf{25.70 \text{ kN/m}^2}$ (A is at higher pressure). **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given:** $P_A = 150 \text{ kN/m}^2$ (absolute), $P_A - P_B = 25.70 \text{ kN/m}^2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_B &= P_A - 25.70 \\
P_B &= 150 - 25.70 \\
P_B &= 124.30 \text{ kN/m}^2 \quad (\text{absolute})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $P_B \approx \mathbf{124.30 \text{ kN/m}^2}$ (absolute). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of an **inverted U-tube manometer**. A pipe carrying water is connected to such a manometer using oil of specific gravity 0.75 as the manometric fluid. The difference in oil levels is 35 cm. The pipe fluid level inside the inverted manometer is 10 cm above the higher oil level on one limb.

**(a)** Draw the schematic and explain why the manometric fluid must be **lighter** than the pipe fluid. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the gauge pressure at the pipe center in kN/m². **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Sketch and Explanation (7 marks):**

**Schematic Description (text-based, to be drawn on answer sheet):**
- An inverted U-tube is connected to the pipe at point A.
- The pipe fluid (water, $\gamma_w$) fills both limbs **up to a certain level** $L$.
- Above the water in the connecting top region, a **light manometric fluid** (oil, $\gamma_o = 0.75 \gamma_w$) sits in the middle section, forming a column of height $h$ between the two water-oil interfaces.
- The top of the tube is **open to atmosphere** (or sealed — both are valid).

**Why a lighter fluid?**
In an inverted U-tube, the manometric fluid must be **lighter** than the pipe fluid. The reason is that for a *low pressure* or *vacuum* measurement, the pipe fluid would otherwise flood the entire tube if a heavy fluid were used. The light fluid stays at the top (it floats on water), and the small pressure difference is read as the difference in the light-fluid levels $h$. For vacuum measurement, this avoids the need for a tall mercury column. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_w &= 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3 \\
\gamma_o &= 0.75 \times 9.81 = 7.3575 \text{ kN/m}^3 \\
h &= 35 \text{ cm} = 0.35 \text{ m} \\
y &= 10 \text{ cm} = 0.10 \text{ m} \quad (\text{water column above higher oil interface})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Apply the inverted U-tube formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{A,\text{gauge}} &= (\gamma_w - \gamma_o) \, h - \gamma_w \, y \\
&= (9.81 - 7.3575)(0.35) - (9.81)(0.10) \\
&= (2.4525)(0.35) - 0.981 \\
&= 0.8584 - 0.981 \\
&= -0.1226 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $P_{A,\text{gauge}} \approx \mathbf{-0.123 \text{ kN/m}^2}$ — the **negative sign** indicates the pipe is under **vacuum** relative to atmosphere.

**Vacuum pressure:** $\vert P_{A,\text{gauge}} \vert = \mathbf{0.123 \text{ kN/m}^2}$. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Manometer Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap (–2 marks):** Mixing cm with kN/m³ without converting to meters. Always write $h$ in **m** before substituting.
> 2. **Forgetting the Pipe-Fluid Column (–2 marks):** In U-tube problems, the $\gamma_1 y$ term is the "silent killer" — students often drop it because it is small. KTU examiners *specifically award 2 marks* for correctly including it.
> 3. **Wrong Sign Convention (–2 marks):** Pressure *increases* going **down** and *decreases* going **up**. Drawing a clear arrow on the answer sheet earns 1 mark of "presentation credit".
> 4. **Absolute vs Gauge Confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** Always state explicitly "absolute" or "gauge" in the final line. If the limb is open to atmosphere, the result is **gauge**.
> 5. **Differential Manometer Reference Level (–3 marks):** Choosing the *higher* mercury interface as reference leads to sign errors. Always pick the **lower** interface.
> 6. **Inverted U-Tube Misformulation (–3 marks):** Students often write $P = \gamma h$ for inverted U-tube. Correct: $P = (\gamma_w - \gamma_o) h$ (or equivalent depending on geometry).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Manometer** = pressure-measuring device based on hydrostatic balance of a fluid column.
- **Core equation:** $P = \gamma h$ applied with the "pressure walk" rule.
- **Equal-pressure level** is always at the **lower** interface of the heavier manometric fluid in a U-tube.
- **Open U-tube:** $P_A = P_{atm} + \gamma_2 h - \gamma_1 y$ → gauge form: $P_{A,\text{gauge}} = \gamma_2 h - \gamma_1 y$.
- **Differential U-tube:** $P_A - P_B = \gamma_2 h - \gamma_1 (y_A - y_B)$. If $y_A = y_B$, this reduces to $(\gamma_2 - \gamma_1) h$.
- **Inverted U-tube:** $P_A - P_B = (\gamma_1 - \gamma_o) h$ — used for **vacuum** and **small** pressure differences.
- **Inclined manometer:** $P = \gamma_2 L \sin\theta$ — used for **high sensitivity** small-pressure readings.
- **Piezometer:** Simplest type, uses the *same* fluid, no manometric liquid. Limitation: cannot measure gas pressure or high pressure.
- **Mercury SG = 13.6** and **Water SG = 1.0** are the two most-tested values; memorize them.
- Always end with **units (kN/m²)** and a clear **"gauge" or "absolute"** tag.
- **Sign convention:** $P_A - P_B > 0$ means **A is at higher pressure** than B; $< 0$ means vacuum.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Gauges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Pressure Gauges # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Pressure Gauge** (or pressure measuring instrument) is a mechanical, hydrostatic, or electromechanical device used to measure the intensity of fluid pressure — defined as the normal force exerted per unit area by a fluid at rest or in motion. The standard SI unit is the **Pascal (Pa = N/m²)**, with practical engineering units including **bar**, **psi**, and **metre of water/mercury column**. In KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1, the study of pressure gauges is treated under two broad categories: - **Manometric (Hydrostatic) Gauges** — based on balancing a column of liquid against the unknown pressure. - **Mechanical / Elastic Gauges** — based on the elastic deformation of a sensing element (e.g., Bourdon tube, diaphragm). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Concept — Pressure Types in Fluid Statics** > - **Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$):** Pressure measured from a perfect vacuum (true zero). > - **Gauge Pressure ($P_{gauge}$):** Pressure measured above local atmospheric pressure. > - **Vacuum Pressure ($P_{vac}$):** Pressure measured below local atmospheric pressure. > > **Fundamental Relationship (Universal):** > $$P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$$ > $$P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$$ > > Therefore, at the **International Standard Atmosphere**, $P_{atm} = 101.325$ **kPa** = $1.01325$ **bar** = $10.33$ **m of water** = $760$ **mm of Hg**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Drinking Straw" Intuition Imagine sipping a soft drink through a straw. The liquid rises *not* because the straw "pulls" it, but because **you reduce the pressure inside your mouth below atmospheric pressure**, and the heavier atmospheric pressure pushing down on the drink's surface forces the liquid up the straw. This is the **exact operating principle** of every pressure-measuring device: - **Manometers** balance the unknown fluid pressure against a **known column of liquid** (just like the air pushing the drink up the straw). - **Bourdon gauges** convert the *straightening force* of a curved tube into a *pointer rotation* — much like how a curled paper strip uncurls when you blow into it. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Significance:** Pressure is the most frequently measured variable in process industries, hydraulic machinery, HVAC, and aerospace. KTU examiners frequently test a student's ability to convert between **absolute**, **gauge**, and **vacuum** readings, as well as to read multi-fluid manometer columns without sign error. --- ## 1.3 Classification of Pressure Gauges (KTU Module 1 Scope) | **Category** | **Sub-Type** | **Principle** | **Typical Range** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Hydrostatic (Manometric) | Piezometer | Direct column of test liquid | 0.0 to 0.3 bar | | Hydrostatic (Manometric) | U-Tube Manometer | Liquid column differential balance | 0.0 to 5.0 bar | | Hydrostatic (Manometric) | Inverted U-Tube | For light liquids (oils) | 0.0 to 2.0 bar | | Hydrostatic (Manometric) | Differential Manometer | Measures ΔP between two points | Variable | | Mechanical (Elastic) | Bourdon Tube | Curved metal tube straightening | 0.5 to 7000 bar | | Mechanical (Elastic) | Diaphragm Gauge | Elastic deflection of a disc | 0.001 to 25 bar | | Mechanical (Elastic) | Dead-Weight Tester | Hydraulic loading of a piston | Calibration standard | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Hydrostatic Principle (Why Manometers Work) Every manometric gauge relies on **Pascal's Law of Fluid Statics**, which states that *pressure at a point in a static fluid acts equally in all directions and varies only with depth*: $$P = P_0 + \rho g h$$ Where: - $P_0$ = reference pressure at the free surface (Pa) - $\rho$ = density of the fluid column (kg/m³) - $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²** - $h$ = vertical height of the fluid column (m) > [!NOTE] > The golden rule of manometry: **Pressure at the same elevation in a connected, static, continuous fluid is identical.** This is the single most important concept for solving KTU numerical problems. --- ## 2.2 Manometer Type 1 — The Piezometer A piezometer is the simplest pressure gauge: a transparent tube open to atmosphere, connected directly to the vessel whose pressure is to be measured. - **Limitation:** Cannot measure pressure of heavy liquids (mercury) or pressurized gases safely. - **Equation** (pressure at point A in the vessel): $$P_A = \rho g h$$ Where $h$ is the height of the liquid column rising in the piezometer tube above point A. > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility:** Piezometers are still used in civil engineering for **piezometric head measurement in soil mechanics** and **standpipe piezometers in earth dams** for monitoring pore-water pressure. --- ## 2.3 Manometer Type 2 — The U-Tube Manometer A U-shaped tube containing a **manometric fluid** (typically mercury, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³) is connected to the point of measurement. The unknown pressure pushes the heavy manometric fluid down on the high-pressure side, and the height difference $h$ is read directly. - **Equation** (when vessel fluid is water and manometric fluid is mercury): $$P_A = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot y$$ Where $y$ is the vertical distance from point A to the top of the mercury column on the manometer side. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sign Convention Rule (KTU Valuation Standard):** > - Pressure **increases** as we move **down** in a fluid. > - Pressure **decreases** as we move **up** in a fluid. > - Neglecting atmospheric column when the tube is open to atmosphere (since it acts on both sides equally). --- ## 2.4 Manometer Type 3 — The Inverted U-Tube Manometer Used when the manometric fluid must be **lighter** than the flowing fluid (e.g., measuring pressure in oil pipelines using an air/oil interface). $$P_A - P_B = h \cdot g \cdot (\rho_{oil} - \rho_{air})$$ Since $\rho_{air} \ll \rho_{oil}$, this simplifies to: $$P_A - P_B \approx h \cdot g \cdot \rho_{oil}$$ --- ## 2.5 Manometer Type 4 — The Differential Manometer (Single Column / Two-Fluid) Used to measure the **pressure difference** $P_A - P_B$ between two points in a system (e.g., across an orifice meter, venturi, or pipe section). The general differential equation obtained by tracing the manometer from point A to point B is: $$P_A - P_B = h \cdot g \cdot (\rho_m - \rho_f)$$ Where: - $h$ = differential manometer reading (m) - $\rho_m$ = density of manometric fluid (kg/m³) - $\rho_f$ = density of the flowing fluid in the pipe (kg/m³) --- ## 2.6 Mechanical Gauge — The Bourdon Tube A curved, flattened metallic tube (elliptical cross-section) is sealed at one end and connected to the pressure source at the other. As internal pressure increases, the tube tends to **straighten** (because an elliptical cross-section under internal pressure becomes more circular, increasing the cross-sectional area and forcing the tube to elongate slightly). The tip motion is converted to pointer rotation via a mechanical linkage (gear sector and pinion). - **Sensitivity:** Depends on tube material (phosphor bronze, stainless steel), cross-section (C-type, helical, spiral), and wall thickness. --- ## 2.7 Mechanical Gauge — The Dead-Weight Pressure Balance The **primary standard** for pressure calibration. A known weight $W$ is placed on a piston of known area $A$ floating in oil. The test gauge reads the pressure required to support the piston: $$P = \frac{W}{A}$$ > [!WARNING] > Dead-weight testers are **NOT field instruments** — they are precision laboratory standards. KTU questions may ask you to calculate the calibration pressure or the required weight for a given pressure. --- ## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — KTU 2024 SCHEME MODULE 1** | **Sl. No.** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Absolute pressure from gauge reading | Pa or kPa | | 2 | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ | Vacuum pressure below atmosphere | Pa or kPa | | 3 | $P_A = \rho g h$ | Piezometer pressure equation | Pa | | 4 | $P_A = \rho_m g h - \rho_f g y$ | U-tube manometer (general) | Pa | | 5 | $P_A - P_B = h g (\rho_m - \rho_f)$ | Differential manometer | Pa | | 6 | $P = \dfrac{W}{A}$ | Dead-weight tester calibration | Pa | | 7 | $\gamma = \rho g$ | Specific weight of fluid | N/m³ | | 8 | $h = \dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ | Pressure head (height of fluid column) | m | | 9 | $P_{atm} = 101.325$ **kPa** | Standard atmospheric pressure | kPa | | 10 | $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ **kg/m³** | Density of mercury (manometric fluid) | kg/m³ | | 11 | $\rho_{water} = 1000$ **kg/m³** | Density of water at 4°C | kg/m³ | | 12 | $g = 9.81$ **m/s²** | Acceleration due to gravity | m/s² | > [!TIP] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** > - **Hydraulic systems** use Bourdon gauges (0-700 bar) for pump and accumulator pressure monitoring. > - **Differential manometers** are indispensable in **flow measurement** across orifice plates, venturi meters, and pitot-static tubes. > - **Dead-weight testers** are used by **NABL-accredited calibration labs** in India as the primary standard. > - **Piezo-resistive diaphragm gauges** are used in **medical ventilators** and **avionics altimeters**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure at a Point Below a Free Liquid Surface **Statement:** *The pressure at a depth $h$ below the free surface of a static liquid of density $\rho$ is given by $P = P_0 + \rho g h$.* **Derivation:** Consider an elemental cylindrical column of cross-sectional area $A$ and height $h$ submerged in a static fluid. The forces acting on this element are: 1. Pressure force acting **downward** on the top face = $P_0 \cdot A$ 2. Pressure force acting **upward** on the bottom face = $P \cdot A$ 3. Weight of the fluid element acting **downward** = $mg = (\rho A h) g$ For static equilibrium, sum of vertical forces = 0: $$P \cdot A - P_0 \cdot A - \rho A h g = 0$$ Dividing throughout by $A$: $$P - P_0 - \rho h g = 0$$ Rearranging for $P$: $$P = P_0 + \rho g h$$ **Verification using Dimensional Analysis:** $$[\rho g h] = \left[\frac{kg}{m^3}\right] \cdot \left[\frac{m}{s^2}\right] \cdot [m] = \frac{kg}{m \cdot s^2} = \frac{N}{m^2} = Pa \quad \checkmark$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — U-Tube Manometer Equation (General Case) **Problem Setup:** A closed vessel contains a fluid (e.g., water) under pressure $P_A$. A U-tube manometer using a heavier manometric fluid (e.g., mercury) is connected at point A. The right limb is open to atmosphere $P_{atm}$. The mercury level difference is $h$, and point A is located at a vertical distance $y$ below the lower mercury meniscus on the left side. **Derivation — Tracing the Manometer from A to Atmosphere:** Starting at point A and moving **up** through water by distance $y$ (pressure decreases): $$P_{1} = P_A - \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot y$$ Crossing the **water-mercury interface** (no pressure change at the interface, just a change in fluid density): $$P_{1} = P_{2}$$ Moving **down** through mercury by distance $h$ on the open side (pressure increases): $$P_{3} = P_{2} + \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$$ At the open end, $P_{3} = P_{atm}$. Equating: $$P_{atm} = P_A - \rho_{water} g y + \rho_{Hg} g h$$ Solving for $P_A$: $$\boxed{P_A = P_{atm} + \rho_{water} g y - \rho_{Hg} g h}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note the sign:** If point A is **above** the mercury meniscus, the term $\rho_{water} g y$ becomes **negative** (i.e., we are moving *up* in the denser fluid, but here we move *up* in water — this is the most common KTU sign error). --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Differential Manometer with Two Fluids **Problem Setup:** Two pipes A and B carry a flowing fluid of density $\rho_f$. A U-tube differential manometer is connected between A and B. The manometer uses a heavy manometric fluid of density $\rho_m$ in the lower U-bend and contains a second intermediate fluid (or the same fluid) in the connecting tubes. Let $h$ be the differential mercury reading, and let the centre-line of the pipes be at the same elevation (or at known elevations $z_A$ and $z_B$). **Derivation:** Trace from A, going up or down to mercury level on left, across, then down or up to B. A common KTU variant is when both A and B are at the **same elevation**: $$P_A + \rho_f g \cdot h_1 - \rho_m g \cdot h = P_B + \rho_f g \cdot h_2$$ Where $h_1$ and $h_2$ are the distances from the pipe centre-line to the mercury meniscus on each side. With pipe centre-lines at the same elevation: $h_1 - h_2 = h$, which simplifies to: $$\boxed{P_A - P_B = h g (\rho_m - \rho_f)}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Single Column Differential Manometer (Micrometer Manometer) **Derivation:** A single-column differential manometer uses a large reservoir on one side and a graduated tube on the other. When pressure difference exists, the heavy fluid level in the reservoir drops by a tiny amount $\Delta h$ and rises in the tube by a measurable amount $h$. Since the reservoir cross-section $A_R$ is much larger than the tube cross-section $A_t$, we can write a **volume conservation** equation: $$A_R \cdot \Delta h = A_t \cdot h$$ The actual differential manometer reading is then $(h + \Delta h)$, but since $\Delta h \ll h$: $$\text{Effective manometer reading} \approx h \left(1 + \frac{A_t}{A_R}\right)$$ The corrected pressure difference is: $$P_A - P_B = h \left(1 + \frac{A_t}{A_R}\right) g (\rho_m - \rho_f)$$ > [!NOTE] > When $A_R \gg A_t$ (typical ratio 200:1), the correction term $\frac{A_t}{A_R} \to 0$, and the equation reduces to the standard differential manometer equation $P_A - P_B = hg(\rho_m - \rho_f)$. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Manometer for KTU Problems | **Fluid in Pipe** | **Pressure Range** | **Recommended Manometer** | **Reason** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Water, low pressure | 0 to 0.5 bar | Piezometer / U-tube with CCl₄ | Avoids large mercury column | | Water, high pressure | 0.5 to 10 bar | U-tube with mercury | Hg column reasonable size | | Oil (light) | 0 to 1 bar | Inverted U-tube with air | Air is lighter, gives magnified reading | | Gas (low pressure) | 0 to 0.05 bar | Inclined multi-tube manometer | Inclination magnifies reading by $\frac{1}{\sin\theta}$ | | Steam / corrosive | 0 to 50 bar | Bourdon tube gauge | Safety + remote reading | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Classification Block Diagram — Pressure Measuring Devices ```mermaid flowchart TD A[PRESSURE MEASURING DEVICES]:::main A --> B[Manometric Hydrostatic Gauges]:::cat1 A --> C[Mechanical Elastic Gauges]:::cat2 A --> D[Electronic Gauges]:::cat3 B --> B1[Piezometer Tube]:::sub B --> B2[U-Tube Manometer]:::sub B --> B3[Inverted U-Tube Manometer]:::sub B --> B4[Differential Manometer]:::sub B --> B5[Inclined Tube Manometer]:::sub B4 --> B4A[Single Fluid Type]:::sub B4 --> B4B[Two Fluid Type]:::sub C --> C1[Bourdon Tube Gauge]:::sub C1 --> C1A[C-Type Bourdon]:::deep C1 --> C1B[Helical Bourdon]:::deep C1 --> C1C[Spiral Bourdon]:::deep C --> C2[Diaphragm Gauge]:::sub C --> C3[Bellows Gauge]:::sub C --> C4[Dead Weight Tester]:::sub D --> D1[Strain Gauge Transducer]:::sub D --> D2[Piezoelectric Sensor]:::sub D --> D3[Capacitive Pressure Sensor]:::sub classDef main fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef cat1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef cat2 fill:#385723,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef cat3 fill:#7f6000,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef sub fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef deep fill:#e2efda,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Manometric Measurement System ```mermaid flowchart LR P[PRIMARY SENSOR
Test Fluid]:::node --> T1[TRANSMISSION LINE
Connecting Tube]:::node T1 --> MAN[MANOMETRIC ELEMENT
Manometer U-Tube]:::node MAN --> R[READING STATION
Scale Graduation]:::node R --> C[CALCULATION MODULE
Apply P equals rho g h]:::node C --> O[OUTPUT
Pressure in Pa or bar]:::node ATM[ATMOSPHERIC REFERENCE
P_atm equals 101.325 kPa]:::ref -.-> MAN classDef node fill:#dbe5f1,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ref fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Schematic — Differential Manometer Connecting Two Pipe Sections ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PIPE_A[Pipe Section A] PA[Flow at Pressure P_A]:::pipe end subgraph MANO[U-Tube Manometer] LM[Mercury Left Meniscus]:::hg RM[Mercury Right Meniscus]:::hg H[Differential Height h]:::h LM --- RM H -.- LM H -.- RM end subgraph PIPE_B[Pipe Section B] PB[Flow at Pressure P_B]:::pipe end PA -->|Tapping Tube 1| LM PB -->|Tapping Tube 2| RM classDef pipe fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#385723,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef hg fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef h fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation for KTU Board Exams:** > - If $P_A > P_B$, the mercury on the **left side goes DOWN** and the mercury on the **right side goes UP** by the same volume (assuming equal tube cross-sections). > - The difference in mercury levels $h$ is read directly from the scale. > - The **pipe elevation** $z_A$ and $z_B$ must be carefully included in the tracing equation. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]` **Differentiate clearly between absolute pressure, gauge pressure, and vacuum pressure. A pressure gauge fitted to a steam boiler reads 8.5 bar. If the local barometric pressure is 1.013 bar, determine the absolute pressure inside the boiler in kPa.** **Model Answer:** | **Type** | **Reference Datum** | **Sign Convention** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Absolute Pressure | Perfect vacuum (true zero) | Always positive | | Gauge Pressure | Local atmospheric pressure | Positive if $P > P_{atm}$, negative if $P < P_{atm}$ | | Vacuum Pressure | Local atmospheric pressure | Used when $P < P_{atm}$ (negative gauge) | **Numerical Solution:** Given: $P_{gauge} = 8.5$ bar, $P_{atm} = 1.013$ bar $$P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$$ $$P_{abs} = 8.5 + 1.013 = 9.513 \text{ bar}$$ $$P_{abs} = 9.513 \times 100 = 951.3 \text{ kPa}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to convert **bar to kPa** by multiplying by 100. Marks are deducted in KTU valuation for missing units. --- ### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper]` **A U-tube manometer using mercury (specific gravity = 13.6) is connected to a pipe conveying water under pressure. The mercury column difference is 200 mm. Calculate the gauge pressure at the pipe centre-line, assuming the pipe centre-line is 150 mm below the lower mercury meniscus.** **Model Answer:** Given: - $h_{Hg} = 200$ mm = $0.2$ m - Distance from pipe centre to mercury meniscus on the manometer side, $y = 150$ mm = $0.15$ m - $S_{Hg} = 13.6$, so $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³ - $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³ **Equation for gauge pressure** (manometer open to atmosphere, so $P_{atm}$ cancels): $$P_{A, gauge} = \rho_{water} g y - \rho_{Hg} g h$$ $$P_{A, gauge} = (1000)(9.81)(0.15) - (13600)(9.81)(0.2)$$ $$P_{A, gauge} = 1471.5 - 26683.2$$ $$P_{A, gauge} = -25211.7 \text{ Pa} = -25.21 \text{ kPa (gauge)}$$ The **negative sign** indicates the pipe pressure is **below atmospheric** (i.e., a partial vacuum). The **vacuum pressure** is $25.21$ kPa. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question Choice 1 — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted Model Paper]` **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **Bourdon tube pressure gauge**. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A differential manometer is connected across an orifice meter fitted in a horizontal water pipeline. The manometer uses mercury as the manometric fluid and reads a differential height of 250 mm. Calculate the **pressure difference** between the two tapping points. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to (a):** **Construction of Bourdon Tube:** 1. **Curved metallic tube** — elliptical or flattened cross-section, made of phosphor bronze (low pressure) or stainless steel (high pressure). 2. **Fixed at one end** to the pressure tapping point via a socket. 3. **Free at the other end** — sealed and connected to a mechanical linkage. 4. **Linkage** — a connecting rod, gear sector, and pinion connected to a pointer. 5. **Dial with calibrated scale** — pointer moves over the dial to indicate pressure. **Working Principle:** When fluid under pressure enters the Bourdon tube, the internal pressure tends to make the elliptical cross-section more circular. Since the circumference increases, the tube tends to **straighten**. This straightening motion at the free end is small (a fraction of a millimetre) and is amplified by the **mechanical linkage** to rotate the pointer over a calibrated dial. **Mathematical Behaviour:** For small deflections, the tip displacement $x$ is approximately proportional to the internal pressure $P$: $$x \propto P$$ **Advantages:** - Simple, robust, and inexpensive. - Direct indication on a dial (no need for power). - Wide range (0.5 bar to 7000 bar). - Reasonable accuracy ($\pm 0.5\%$ to $\pm 2\%$ of full scale). **Limitations:** - Hysteresis error due to elastic fatigue of the tube. - Sensitive to temperature and vibration. - Spring constant changes over time — requires periodic calibration. - Cannot measure rapidly fluctuating pressures (pointer oscillation). - Lower accuracy than dead-weight or electronic transducers. **Sketch (textual representation):** ``` Pointer | v [DIAL SCALE] | ___________|___________ | | | [Pin] [Gear Sector] | | \ / | | \ / | | \ / | | [Link] | | \ | | \ | | [Curved Tube] | | (Elliptical) | |___|___________________| | [Pressure Inlet] ``` --- #### **Model Solution to (b):** **Given:** - Differential manometer reading, $h = 250$ mm = $0.25$ m - $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³ - $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³ - $g = 9.81$ m/s² - Pipeline is horizontal, so elevation difference between A and B is zero. **Formula (Differential Manometer):** $$P_A - P_B = h \cdot g \cdot (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_{water})$$ **Substitution:** $$P_A - P_B = (0.25)(9.81)(13600 - 1000)$$ $$P_A - P_B = (0.25)(9.81)(12600)$$ $$P_A - P_B = 30901.5 \text{ Pa} = 30.90 \text{ kPa}$$ **Verification using dimensional analysis:** $$[m] \cdot \left[\frac{m}{s^2}\right] \cdot \left[\frac{kg}{m^3}\right] = \frac{kg}{m \cdot s^2} = Pa \quad \checkmark$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT forget to subtract $\rho_f$** from $\rho_m$ — many students write $P_A - P_B = h g \rho_{Hg}$ which gives a 4% error and loses 1 mark. > 2. **Units** must be consistent: convert mm to m before substituting. > 3. **Direction of $h$:** $h$ is the *difference* in mercury levels, not the height of either column individually. --- ### **Question Choice 2 — Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]` **(a)** Derive the general equation for the **pressure difference indicated by a U-tube differential manometer** when the pipe centre-lines are at the **same elevation**. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A closed tank contains oil (specific gravity = 0.85) under a pressure of 1.5 bar (gauge). The tank is fitted with a U-tube manometer using mercury. The left limb of the manometer is connected to the tank and is filled with oil above the mercury. The right limb is open to atmosphere. If the mercury level in the left limb is 200 mm **below** the centre-line of the tank, and the mercury level in the right limb is 250 mm **above** the mercury level in the left limb, determine the **height of oil column** above the mercury in the left limb. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to (a):** **Statement of Assumptions:** 1. The manometric fluid is **immiscible** with the flowing fluid. 2. The flowing fluid is **incompressible** (constant $\rho_f$). 3. The manometer tubes have **uniform cross-section**. 4. The fluid in the manometer is at **static equilibrium**. 5. **Temperature** is uniform and constant. 6. **Capillary effects** are neglected. **Derivation:** Consider two points A and B in a horizontal pipe carrying a fluid of density $\rho_f$, connected by a U-tube manometer containing a heavier manometric fluid of density $\rho_m$. Let: - $h$ = difference in mercury levels in the two limbs of the manometer - $z$ = vertical distance from pipe centre-line to the **higher** mercury meniscus Tracing from A to B through the manometer (start at A, go up or down to mercury, across, then to B): **Step 1:** Pressure at the mercury meniscus on the A-side (going down by $z$ from A, if A is above the meniscus): $$P_1 = P_A + \rho_f g z$$ **Step 2:** Cross the mercury interface (no pressure change). **Step 3:** Go down through mercury by $h$ on the A-side, then **up** through mercury on the B-side to the B-side meniscus. Net rise from A-side meniscus to B-side meniscus is $h$ (since mercury level on B-side is $h$ higher): $$P_2 = P_1 - \rho_m g h = P_A + \rho_f g z - \rho_m g h$$ **Step 4:** Go down from B-side meniscus to point B (distance $z$, if pipe centre-lines are at the same elevation): $$P_B = P_2 + \rho_f g z = P_A + \rho_f g z - \rho_m g h + \rho_f g z$$ **Step 5:** Since $z$ terms cancel out (A and B at same elevation, and the column $z$ appears on both sides), we get: $$P_B = P_A - \rho_m g h + \rho_f g h$$ **Step 6:** Rearranging: $$\boxed{P_A - P_B = h g (\rho_m - \rho_f)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Notice that the elevation $z$ cancels out completely, which is why **horizontal pipe** differential manometer problems are independent of the pipe elevation — only the **mercury difference $h$** matters. This is the most elegant result in manometry and a frequent KTU 7-mark question. --- #### **Model Solution to (b):** **Given:** - $P_A$ (gauge) = 1.5 bar = $150000$ Pa - $S_{oil} = 0.85$ $\Rightarrow$ $\rho_{oil} = 850$ kg/m³ - $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³ - Vertical distance from tank centre-line to mercury meniscus on left side, $y_1 = 200$ mm = $0.2$ m - Mercury level difference, $h = 250$ mm = $0.25$ m - Let $H$ = height of oil column above mercury in the left limb (in metres). **Manometer Tracing Equation:** Starting at the centre of the tank (point A) and tracing to the atmosphere through the manometer: $$P_A - \rho_{oil} g H - \rho_{Hg} g (h - y_1) - \rho_{air} g \cdot 0 = P_{atm}$$ Wait — we need to be careful with geometry. Let me re-draw: - The tank centre-line is at elevation reference. - The mercury meniscus in the left limb is 200 mm **below** the centre-line (i.e., the oil column from centre-line down to mercury is $200$ mm = $0.2$ m, but we are told to find $H$ = additional oil column above this — but the problem says "200 mm below the centre-line of the tank, and the mercury level in the right limb is 250 mm above the mercury level in the left limb".) **Simplified Setup:** Let us assume the oil column above the mercury in the left limb is the unknown $H$. The centre-line of the tank is 200 mm above the mercury meniscus in the left limb. The mercury in the right limb is 250 mm above the mercury in the left limb. The right limb is open to atmosphere. **Tracing from A (tank centre) to atmosphere:** Going **down** through oil from A to the mercury meniscus on the left (pressure **increases**): $$P_{left meniscus} = P_A + \rho_{oil} g (0.2 + H)$$ Going **up** through mercury from left meniscus to right meniscus (pressure **decreases**): $$P_{right meniscus} = P_{left meniscus} - \rho_{Hg} g h$$ $$P_{right meniscus} = P_A + \rho_{oil} g (0.2 + H) - \rho_{Hg} g (0.25)$$ At the right meniscus, the air column above is at atmospheric pressure (negligible weight). So: $$P_{atm} = P_A + \rho_{oil} g (0.2 + H) - \rho_{Hg} g (0.25)$$ Since $P_{atm}$ is the reference for gauge pressure, and we are using $P_A$ as gauge pressure, we can set $P_{atm} = 0$ in gauge form: $$0 = P_{A, gauge} + \rho_{oil} g (0.2 + H) - \rho_{Hg} g (0.25)$$ **Solving for H:** $$150000 + 850 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (0.2 + H) = 13600 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.25$$ $$150000 + 8338.5 (0.2 + H) = 33354$$ $$8338.5 (0.2 + H) = 33354 - 150000 = -116646$$ $$0.2 + H = \frac{-116646}{8338.5} = -13.99$$ $$H = -14.19 \text{ m}$$ The **negative** result indicates that the gauge pressure of 1.5 bar is **insufficient** to push the mercury down by 250 mm. In a more realistic setup, the oil column would need to be **at least 14.19 m above** the mercury meniscus to support this configuration, OR the gauge pressure reading is incompatible with the stated geometry. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** When tracing, going UP decreases pressure; going DOWN increases pressure. This is the #1 source of errors. > 2. **Forgetting $\rho_f g z$ terms:** If A and B are at different elevations, students often miss the elevation correction term. **Always include $z$ in the trace.** > 3. **Mixing gauge and absolute:** Never add $P_{atm}$ twice. If using gauge pressure, the open end is at 0 (gauge). If using absolute, include $P_{atm}$ at the open end. > 4. **Mercury density:** Use $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³, **NOT** 13.6 kg/m³. SG and density are different — SG is dimensionless. > 5. **Units consistency:** Convert mm → m **before** substituting into the formula. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU MODULE 1: PRESSURE GAUGES** **Core Definitions to Memorize:** - $\square$ Absolute pressure, gauge pressure, vacuum pressure and their inter-relationships - $\square$ Specific weight ($\gamma = \rho g$) and specific gravity (dimensionless ratio) - $\square$ Pressure head $h = \dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ (metres of fluid) **Manometer Types & Their Key Equations:** - $\square$ **Piezometer:** $P_A = \rho g h$ (direct column of test liquid) - $\square$ **U-Tube Manometer:** $P_A = P_{atm} + \rho_f g y - \rho_m g h$ (general form) - $\square$ **Inverted U-Tube:** $P_A - P_B = h g (\rho_f - \rho_{air}) \approx h g \rho_f$ (light fluid flows) - $\square$ **Differential Manometer:** $P_A - P_B = h g (\rho_m - \rho_f)$ (horizontal pipes) **Mechanical Gauges:** - $\square$ **Bourdon Tube:** Elliptical cross-section straightens under pressure; pointer rotated via linkage - $\square$ **Diaphragm Gauge:** Thin metallic disc deflects under pressure; suitable for low pressure - $\square$ **Dead-Weight Tester:** Primary standard; $P = W/A$ **Constants to Remember:** - $\square$ $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa = 760 mm Hg = 10.33 m of water - $\square$ $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³, $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³ - $\square$ $g = 9.81$ m/s² **Golden Rule of Manometry:** - $\square$ **Pressure at the same elevation in a connected, static, continuous fluid is identical.** All manometer equations are derived from this single principle. **Sign Convention:** - $\square$ Going **DOWN** in a fluid → Pressure **INCREASES** (add $\rho g h$) - $\square$ Going **UP** in a fluid → Pressure **DECREASES** (subtract $\rho g h$) - $\square$ Always start from a point of **known pressure** and trace to a point of **known or unknown pressure**. **KTU Examination Strategy:** - $\square$ **Always** draw a clear, well-labelled diagram before starting numerical problems. - $\square$ **Always** mention the assumptions (incompressibility, immiscibility, static equilibrium). - $\square$ **Always** include **units** in the final answer. - $\square$ For Bourdon-type theory questions, draw the C-shaped tube and label all key parts. - $\square$ Practice tracing 5–10 manometer problems to build fluency with sign conventions. > [!NOTE] > **Common KTU Past Question Themes (Last 3 Years):** > 1. Find pressure at a point given manometer column heights. > 2. Calculate absolute/gauge/vacuum pressure conversion. > 3. Differential manometer across an orifice meter / venturi. > 4. Bourdon tube construction and working — theory question. > 5. Dead-weight tester calibration — calculate weight or pressure. > 6. Explain any one type of manometer with a diagram (5 marks).

topics.title: Forces on Planar and Curved Surfaces Immersed in Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Forces on Planar and Curved Surfaces Immersed in Fluids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hydrostatic Force — Formal Definition

When a solid surface (plane or curved) is immersed in a static fluid, the fluid exerts a **pressure force** normal to every elemental area of that surface. The resultant of all these elemental pressure forces is termed the **Hydrostatic Force** (also called the **Thrust Force** or simply **Fluid Force**) acting on the surface.

In its formal, KTU-exam-ready wording:

> **Hydrostatic Force:** The resultant of the distributed pressure loads acting on a wetted surface, obtained by integrating the product of gauge pressure and elemental area over the entire wetted region.

For a plane surface of area $A$ immersed in a static liquid of specific weight $\gamma = \rho g$, the **magnitude of the resultant hydrostatic force** is:

$$F_R = \gamma \, \bar{h} \, A = \rho g \, \bar{h} \, A$$

where $\bar{h}$ is the vertical depth of the **centroid** of the wetted area below the free liquid surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners expect you to use **$\gamma = \rho g$** (specific weight) for fluid forces on submerged bodies. Use $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ and water $\rho = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, so $\gamma_{water} = 9810 \, \text{N/m}^3$ unless stated otherwise.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Underwater Hand"

Imagine you stand in a swimming pool and submerge your palm horizontally below the surface. You feel water pushing **downward** on the back of your palm — the deeper you push it, the harder the push. This is because the water column above your palm weighs more as the depth increases, and pressure grows linearly with depth:

$$p = \gamma \, h$$

Now rotate your palm to face sideways (vertical). The pressure on your palm is no longer uniform — it is **small at the top** and **large at the bottom**. The total push on your palm is the *integral* of this varying pressure. This varying pressure is what gives rise to a concept we will study shortly: the **Center of Pressure** — the point where the *resultant* force effectively acts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Hydrostatic pressure always pushes **perpendicular (normal) into the surface**, never along it. That is why the direction of $F_R$ on a plane surface is always perpendicular to the surface.

---

## 1.3 Planar vs. Curved Surfaces — The Fundamental Distinction

| Surface Type | Geometry of Wetting | Resultant Force Direction |
|---|---|---|
| **Planar** | Flat (horizontal, vertical, or inclined) | Perpendicular to the surface (single resultant) |
| **Curved** | Bent (cylindrical, spherical, etc.) | Computed as two perpendicular components ($F_H$ and $F_V$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For curved surfaces, the resultant force is **NOT** perpendicular to the surface at a single point. Instead, it must be computed in two orthogonal components — horizontal and vertical — and then combined vectorially.

---

## 1.4 Key Definitions You Must Memorise

- **Free Surface:** The upper boundary of the liquid where the absolute pressure equals atmospheric pressure (taken as zero gauge).
- **Submerged Surface:** A solid surface fully in contact with the static fluid.
- **Wetted Area ($A$):** The portion of the solid surface actually in contact with the fluid.
- **Centroid ($\bar{h}$ or $h_c$):** The geometric center of the wetted area, measured vertically below the free surface.
- **Center of Pressure ($h_p$):** The point on the immersed surface through which the *resultant* hydrostatic force effectively acts. It always lies **deeper** than the centroid (because pressure increases with depth, the lower portions experience greater force).
- **Pressure Prism:** A graphical (volumetric) representation of the distributed pressure load; its volume equals the total hydrostatic force and its centroid gives the location of the resultant.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hydrostatic Pressure — The Starting Point

From the basic equation of fluid statics, pressure at any depth $h$ below the free surface is:

$$p(h) = \gamma h$$

where $\gamma = \rho g$ is the specific weight of the fluid. This is **gauge pressure**. If atmospheric pressure is included, we use **absolute pressure** $p_{abs} = p_{atm} + \gamma h$.

For most KTU problems, we deal with **gauge pressure**, since the atmospheric component acts uniformly on both sides of the immersed surface and cancels out (unless specified otherwise, e.g., for a closed tank with pressurised gas above the liquid).

---

## 2.2 Force on a Horizontal Plane Surface (Fully Submerged)

A horizontal plane surface is one whose plane is parallel to the free liquid surface. Every point on this surface lies at the same depth $h$.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. Pressure is uniform across the surface: $p = \gamma h$.
2. Total hydrostatic force: $F = p \cdot A = \gamma h A$.
3. The resultant acts **vertically downward** (normal to the surface) at the **centroid** of the area (since pressure is uniform, the center of pressure coincides with the centroid).

$$\boxed{F = \gamma h A}$$

---

## 2.3 Force on a Vertical Plane Surface

For a vertical plane surface submerged in liquid with its top edge at depth $h_1$ and bottom edge at depth $h_2$:

1. Pressure varies linearly from $p_1 = \gamma h_1$ at top to $p_2 = \gamma h_2$ at bottom.
2. Total force = area of the **trapezoidal pressure prism**:
3. $F = \gamma \bar{h} A$, where $\bar{h} = \dfrac{h_1 + h_2}{2}$ is the depth of the centroid of a rectangle.
4. The resultant acts horizontally at the **center of pressure** $h_p$, which lies *below* the centroid for a vertical surface.

For a rectangle of width $b$ and height $H$ with top at depth $h_1$:

$$\bar{h} = h_1 + \frac{H}{2}$$

$$F = \gamma \, b \, H \left(h_1 + \frac{H}{2}\right)$$

---

## 2.4 Force on an Inclined Plane Surface (General Case)

This is the **most important case** for KTU board examinations.

Consider an inclined plane surface submerged in a liquid. Let:

- $\theta$ = angle between the inclined surface and the **horizontal** free surface.
- $h_c$ = vertical depth of the centroid of the wetted area.
- $A$ = area of the wetted surface.
- $I_{xc}$ = second moment of area of the wetted surface about a horizontal axis through its centroid (parallel to the free surface).
- $y_c$ = slant distance from the free surface to the centroid (along the inclined plane).

**The Three Master Equations:**

### 2.4.1 Magnitude of Resultant Force

$$F_R = \gamma \, h_c \, A = \rho g \, h_c \, A$$

This is the **most fundamental equation** in this topic. You will use it in virtually every problem.

### 2.4.2 Location of Center of Pressure (slant distance from free surface, measured along the plane)

$$y_p = y_c + \frac{I_{xc}}{y_c A}$$

### 2.4.3 Vertical Depth of Center of Pressure

$$h_p = h_c + \frac{I_{xc} \sin^2 \theta}{h_c \, A} = h_c + \frac{I_{xc}}{y_c \, A}$$

(Note: since $y_c = h_c / \sin\theta$, we have $\sin^2\theta / h_c = \sin\theta / y_c$, confirming both forms.)

---

## 2.5 Forces on Curved Surfaces

A curved surface is decomposed into:

- **Horizontal Force Component ($F_H$)** — equal to the force on the *vertical projection* of the curved surface.
- **Vertical Force Component ($F_V$)** — equal to the *weight of the fluid* (real or imaginary) directly above the curved surface up to the free surface.

$$F_H = \gamma \, h_c \, A_v$$

$$F_V = \gamma \, V$$

where $A_v$ is the area of the vertical projection of the curved surface, and $V$ is the volume of fluid (real or imaginary) vertically above the curved surface up to the free liquid level.

The **magnitude of the resultant force** on the curved surface is:

$$F_R = \sqrt{F_H^2 + F_V^2}$$

The **direction** of $F_R$ with respect to the horizontal is:

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{F_V}{F_H}$$

---

## 2.6 The Pressure Prism Method (Graphical Approach)

The pressure prism is a powerful visual tool. Its **volume** equals the resultant hydrostatic force, and its **centroid** indicates the line of action of the resultant.

For a rectangular vertical surface of width $b$ from depth $h_1$ to $h_2$, the pressure prism is a **trapezoid** with parallel sides $\gamma h_1$ and $\gamma h_2$ and width $b$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Volume of Pressure Prism** = $F_R$ (Resultant Force). This is why we say the *area* of the pressure diagram times the width gives the force.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pressure at depth $h$ | $p = \gamma h$ | Pa or N/m² |
| 2 | Specific weight of water | $\gamma_w = 9810$ | N/m³ |
| 3 | Specific weight | $\gamma = \rho g$ | N/m³ |
| 4 | Force on plane surface | $F_R = \gamma h_c A$ | N |
| 5 | Slant depth to centroid | $y_c = h_c / \sin\theta$ | m |
| 6 | Center of pressure (slant) | $y_p = y_c + \dfrac{I_{xc}}{y_c A}$ | m |
| 7 | Center of pressure (vertical) | $h_p = h_c + \dfrac{I_{xc} \sin^2\theta}{h_c A}$ | m |
| 8 | Force on horizontal surface | $F = \gamma h A$ | Acts at centroid |
| 9 | Horizontal force on curved surface | $F_H = \gamma h_c A_v$ | Acts at centroid of $A_v$ |
| 10 | Vertical force on curved surface | $F_V = \gamma V$ | Weight of fluid above |
| 11 | Resultant on curved surface | $F_R = \sqrt{F_H^2 + F_V^2}$ | N |
| 12 | Angle of $F_R$ with horizontal | $\tan \alpha = F_V / F_H$ | degrees or radians |

> [!NOTE]
> In all tables, the notation $\dfrac{I_{xc}}{y_c A}$ uses a **fraction** — make sure you write it explicitly in your KTU answer scripts to avoid mark deductions.

### 2.7.1 Standard $I_{xc}$ Values (Frequently Used)

| Shape | Area $A$ | Centroidal axis $I_{xc}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Rectangle (base $b$, height $h$) | $b h$ | $\dfrac{b h^3}{12}$ |
| Triangle (base $b$, height $h$, vertex up) | $\dfrac{1}{2} b h$ | $\dfrac{b h^3}{36}$ |
| Triangle (base $b$, height $h$, vertex down) | $\dfrac{1}{2} b h$ | $\dfrac{b h^3}{36}$ |
| Circle (radius $R$) | $\pi R^2$ | $\dfrac{\pi R^4}{4}$ |
| Trapezoid | — | Use parallel axis theorem |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding hydrostatic forces on immersed surfaces is essential in designing:

- **Dams** (gravity dams, arch dams) — must resist horizontal water thrust.
- **Tainter gates and sluice gates** in hydraulic projects.
- **Submarine hulls** — pressure distribution and hull thickness.
- **Storage tanks, lock gates, and ship hulls.**
- **Hydraulic presses and accumulators.**
- **Weirs, spillways, and hydro-electric intake structures.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for Students:** In KTU valuation, *every* algebraic step must be shown. Marks are deducted for skipping steps. The derivations below are written in examiner-friendly format.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Force on a Submerged Horizontal Plane Surface

**Setup:** Consider a horizontal plane surface of area $A$ submerged at a constant depth $h$ below the free liquid surface.

**Step 1:** Consider an infinitesimal element of area $dA$ on the surface. Since the surface is horizontal, *every* point lies at the same depth $h$.

**Step 2:** The pressure on this element is uniform:

$$p = \gamma h$$

**Step 3:** The infinitesimal force on $dA$ is:

$$dF = p \, dA = \gamma h \, dA$$

**Step 4:** Integrate over the entire wetted area $A$:

$$F = \int_A \gamma h \, dA = \gamma h \int_A dA = \gamma h A$$

$$\boxed{F = \gamma h A}$$

**Step 5:** Since pressure is uniform, the resultant acts at the geometric centroid of the area (no offset). The direction is **vertically downward** (assuming the surface is below the liquid and faces upward, e.g., the bottom of a tank).

If the surface faces *downward* (e.g., the top of a submerged box from inside), the force is **vertically upward** with the same magnitude.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Force on an Inclined Plane Surface (General)

**Setup:** Consider an inclined plane surface submerged in liquid, making an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal free surface. Let the slant length along the plane from the free surface to the centroid be $y_c$, so the vertical depth of the centroid is:

$$h_c = y_c \sin \theta$$

**Step 1:** Consider an elemental strip on the inclined surface at a slant distance $y$ from the free surface (measured along the plane). The width of this strip is $dA = b(y) \, dy$ where $b(y)$ is the breadth of the strip at position $y$.

**Step 2:** The vertical depth of this strip is $h = y \sin \theta$. The pressure acting on it is:

$$p(y) = \gamma h = \gamma y \sin \theta$$

**Step 3:** The infinitesimal force on the strip (acting normal to the surface) is:

$$dF = p(y) \, dA = \gamma y \sin\theta \cdot b(y) \, dy$$

**Step 4:** Integrate over the entire wetted region:

$$F_R = \int_A \gamma y \sin\theta \, dA = \gamma \sin\theta \int_A y \, dA$$

**Step 5:** Recall the definition of the centroid: the first moment of area about the free surface is:

$$\int_A y \, dA = y_c A$$

Substituting:

$$F_R = \gamma \sin\theta \cdot y_c A$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $h_c = y_c \sin\theta$:

$$F_R = \gamma \, h_c \, A$$

$$\boxed{F_R = \gamma \, h_c \, A = \rho g \, h_c \, A}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Location of Center of Pressure on an Inclined Surface

**Step 1:** The center of pressure is the point where the resultant force effectively acts. By the principle of moments:

$$F_R \cdot y_p = \int_A y \cdot dF$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $dF = \gamma y \sin\theta \, dA$:

$$F_R \cdot y_p = \int_A y \cdot \gamma y \sin\theta \, dA = \gamma \sin\theta \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that $\int_A y^2 \, dA$ is the **second moment of area** about the **free surface line**, denoted $I_x$.

$$F_R \cdot y_p = \gamma \sin\theta \cdot I_x$$

**Step 4:** Use the **parallel axis theorem**:

$$I_x = I_{xc} + y_c^2 A$$

where $I_{xc}$ is the second moment of area about the centroidal axis (parallel to the free surface).

**Step 5:** Substitute $F_R = \gamma y_c A \sin\theta$ and $I_x = I_{xc} + y_c^2 A$:

$$(\gamma y_c A \sin\theta) \, y_p = \gamma \sin\theta \, (I_{xc} + y_c^2 A)$$

**Step 6:** Cancel $\gamma \sin\theta$ from both sides:

$$y_c A \, y_p = I_{xc} + y_c^2 A$$

**Step 7:** Divide by $y_c A$:

$$y_p = \frac{I_{xc}}{y_c A} + y_c$$

$$\boxed{y_p = y_c + \frac{I_{xc}}{y_c A}}$$

**Step 8:** Convert to vertical depth by multiplying both sides by $\sin\theta$:

$$y_p \sin\theta = y_c \sin\theta + \frac{I_{xc} \sin\theta}{y_c A}$$

$$h_p = h_c + \frac{I_{xc} \sin^2\theta}{h_c A}$$

(since $y_c \sin\theta = h_c$ and dividing the rightmost term's numerator and denominator by $\sin\theta$).

$$\boxed{h_p = h_c + \frac{I_{xc} \sin^2\theta}{h_c A}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The term $\dfrac{I_{xc} \sin^2\theta}{h_c A}$ is always **positive**, confirming that the center of pressure is *always* below the centroid. The offset diminishes as the surface is taken deeper (since $h_c$ grows in the denominator).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Horizontal Force on a Curved Surface

**Setup:** Consider a curved surface $S$ submerged in liquid. Let $A_v$ be the projection of $S$ onto a vertical plane.

**Step 1:** By **Pascal's principle**, the pressure at any point in a static fluid depends only on the vertical depth, regardless of the geometry of the surface containing that point.

**Step 2:** Consider two surfaces:
- The actual curved surface $S$.
- Its vertical projection $A_v$.

**Step 3:** Both surfaces contain fluid columns of the *same vertical heights* at corresponding horizontal positions. Therefore, the pressure distribution at corresponding horizontal positions is **identical**.

**Step 4:** The horizontal force is the integral of horizontal pressure components. By the equality of pressure distributions, the horizontal force on $S$ equals the horizontal force on $A_v$:

$$F_H = \gamma \, h_{c,v} \, A_v$$

where $h_{c,v}$ is the vertical depth of the centroid of $A_v$.

$$\boxed{F_H = \gamma \, h_{c,v} \, A_v}$$

The line of action of $F_H$ passes through the center of pressure of the vertical projection $A_v$.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Vertical Force on a Curved Surface

**Setup:** Consider a curved surface $S$ with fluid above it (or with an imaginary volume if fluid is below).

**Step 1:** Consider an elemental area $dA$ on the curved surface. The pressure on it is $\gamma h$ where $h$ is its vertical depth.

**Step 2:** The vertical component of force on $dA$ is:

$$dF_V = p \, dA \cos\phi = \gamma h \, dA \cos\phi$$

where $\phi$ is the angle the outward normal of $dA$ makes with the vertical.

**Step 3:** Observe that $dA \cos\phi$ is the horizontal projection of $dA$, which equals the area $dA_h$ of the vertical projection of $dA$.

So $dF_V = \gamma h \, dA_h$.

**Step 4:** Integrate over the entire surface:

$$F_V = \int_S \gamma h \, dA_h$$

**Step 5:** Note that $h \, dA_h = dV$ — the **elemental volume** of the fluid (real or imaginary) column directly above $dA$ up to the free surface.

Therefore:

$$F_V = \int_V \gamma \, dV = \gamma V$$

$$\boxed{F_V = \gamma V}$$

where $V$ is the volume of fluid (real or imaginary) directly above the curved surface, bounded above by the free liquid surface and below by the curved surface itself.

**Direction of $F_V$:**
- If fluid is *above* the curved surface → $F_V$ is **downward**.
- If fluid is *below* the curved surface → $F_V$ is **upward** (this is the **buoyancy principle**, equal to the weight of displaced fluid).
- For surfaces with fluid on *both sides*, only the **net** volume (fluid above minus fluid below) contributes.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Quick Problem-Solving Guide

| Surface Type | What to Compute | Key Quantity |
|---|---|---|
| Horizontal submerged | $F = \gamma h A$, direction $\downarrow$ or $\uparrow$ | Depth $h$ is constant |
| Vertical plane | $F = \gamma \bar{h} A$, $h_p = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{xc}}{\bar{h} A}$ | $\theta = 90°$, $y_c = \bar{h}$ |
| Inclined plane | $F = \gamma h_c A$, $y_p = y_c + \dfrac{I_{xc}}{y_c A}$ | Use $y_c = h_c / \sin\theta$ |
| Curved surface | $F_H = \gamma h_c A_v$, $F_V = \gamma V$ | Combine vectorially |
| Pressure prism | Volume of prism = $F_R$ | Centroid of prism = line of action |

---

## 3.7 Special Case — Fully Submerged Rectangular Gate (Worked-Out Style)

A rectangular gate of width $b = 2 \, \text{m}$ and height $H = 3 \, \text{m}$ is inclined at $60°$ to the horizontal. The top edge of the gate is $2 \, \text{m}$ below the water surface. Find the magnitude and location of the resultant hydrostatic force.

**Given:** $b = 2 \, \text{m}$, $H = 3 \, \text{m}$, $\theta = 60°$, top depth $h_1 = 2 \, \text{m}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Find the slant length of the gate from top to bottom:

$$L = \frac{H}{\sin 60°} = \frac{3}{0.866} = 3.464 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 2:** Find the slant distance from the free surface to the centroid:

$$y_c = \frac{h_1}{\sin 60°} + \frac{H}{2 \sin 60°} = \frac{2}{0.866} + \frac{3}{2 \times 0.866} = 2.309 + 1.732 = 4.041 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 3:** Find the vertical depth of the centroid:

$$h_c = y_c \sin 60° = 4.041 \times 0.866 = 3.5 \, \text{m}$$

(Alternatively, $h_c = h_1 + H/2 = 2 + 1.5 = 3.5 \, \text{m}$ — direct geometric result for the centroid of a rectangle.)

**Step 4:** Find the area:

$$A = b \times H = 2 \times 3 = 6 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 5:** Compute the resultant force:

$$F_R = \gamma h_c A = 9810 \times 3.5 \times 6 = 206{,}010 \, \text{N} \approx 206 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 6:** Compute the second moment of area of the rectangle about its centroidal axis (parallel to free surface):

$$I_{xc} = \frac{b H^3}{12} = \frac{2 \times 3^3}{12} = \frac{54}{12} = 4.5 \, \text{m}^4$$

**Step 7:** Compute the slant distance to center of pressure:

$$y_p = y_c + \frac{I_{xc}}{y_c A} = 4.041 + \frac{4.5}{4.041 \times 6} = 4.041 + \frac{4.5}{24.246}$$

$$y_p = 4.041 + 0.1856 = 4.227 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 8:** Compute the vertical depth of center of pressure:

$$h_p = h_c + \frac{I_{xc} \sin^2\theta}{h_c A} = 3.5 + \frac{4.5 \times \sin^2 60°}{3.5 \times 6}$$

$$h_p = 3.5 + \frac{4.5 \times 0.75}{21} = 3.5 + \frac{3.375}{21} = 3.5 + 0.1607 = 3.661 \, \text{m}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{F_R = 206 \, \text{kN}, \quad h_p = 3.661 \, \text{m} \text{ below free surface}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Pressure Distribution on a Vertical Submerged Surface

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Free Water Surface<br/>Pressure = 0 gauge] --> A2[Top of Submerged Wall<br/>Depth h1, Pressure gamma h1]
    A2 --> A3[Centroid of Wall<br/>Depth h bar, Pressure gamma h bar]
    A3 --> A4[Bottom of Wall<br/>Depth h2, Pressure gamma h2]
    A1 --- A5[Atmospheric Line<br/>Reference Zero Pressure]
    A2 --- A6[Pressure Prism Top Edge<br/>Trapezoidal Distribution]
    A3 --- A7[Pressure Prism Centroid<br/>Center of Pressure Location]
    A4 --- A8[Maximum Pressure Line<br/>gamma h2 at base]
    A6 --> A9[Resultant Force FR<br/>Acts horizontally at Center of Pressure]
    A7 --> A9
    A8 --> A9
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The vertical wall on the right experiences linearly increasing pressure with depth. The pressure prism (left) is a trapezoid whose **area** × width gives the resultant force and whose **centroid** marks the line of action of $F_R$.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Force Decomposition on a Curved Surface

```mermaid
graph TD
    B1[Curved Submerged Surface<br/>e.g. Cylindrical Gate] --> B2[Vertical Projection Av<br/>Used to compute FH]
    B1 --> B3[Imaginary Fluid Volume V<br/>Above the curved surface]
    B2 --> B4[Horizontal Force FH<br/>Acts at centroid of Av]
    B3 --> B5[Vertical Force FV<br/>Acts through centroid of V]
    B4 --> B6[Resultant Force FR<br/>Magnitude sqrt FH sq plus FV sq]
    B5 --> B6
    B6 --> B7[Direction Angle alpha<br/>tan alpha equals FV by FH]
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Inclined Plane Surface Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    C1[Free Liquid Surface<br/>Horizontal Datum] --> C2[Inclined Surface Angle theta<br/>with Horizontal]
    C2 --> C3[Centroid Location yc<br/>Slant distance from datum]
    C2 --> C4[Center of Pressure yp<br/>Slant distance from datum]
    C3 --> C5[Force Magnitude FR<br/>equals gamma hc A]
    C4 --> C6[Offset Distance<br/>Ixc by yc A]

    C6 --> C7
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Pressure Prism Conceptual Block Flow

```mermaid

```

---

```mermaid
    Q2 -->|Curved| Q6[Split into FH and FV components]
    Q5 --> Q7[Convert slant to vertical if needed]
    Q6 --> Q8[Find Av projection for FH]
    Q6 --> Q9[Find fluid volume V for FV]
    Q8 --> Q10[Combine vectorially]
    Q9 --> Q10
```
