# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Define the term "Center of Pressure" for a submerged plane surface. Why does it not coincide with the centroid?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Center of Pressure** is the point on a submerged plane surface through which the resultant hydrostatic force effectively acts. It is the point where the entire distributed pressure load can be considered to act as a single concentrated force.

> It does not coincide with the centroid because the pressure distribution over the surface is **non-uniform** — it increases linearly with depth. The lower portions of the surface experience higher pressure than the upper portions. This causes the resultant force to act at a point **below** the centroid (for a vertical or inclined surface with the free surface above).

> **Mathematically:** The vertical offset from the centroid to the center of pressure is $\dfrac{I_{xc} \sin^2 \theta}{h_c A}$, which is always positive.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**State Pascal's law. How is it used to determine the horizontal force on a curved submerged surface?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Pascal's Law** states that the pressure at any point in a static fluid depends only on the depth of that point below the free surface and is independent of the shape or orientation of the container or surface at that point:
> $$p = \gamma h$$

> **Application to curved surfaces:** Consider a curved surface $S$ and its vertical projection $A_v$ onto a vertical plane. Since both surfaces span the same horizontal extents and depths, the pressure distribution at corresponding horizontal positions is identical. Therefore, the horizontal force on $S$ equals the hydrostatic force on its vertical projection:
> $$F_H = \gamma \, h_{c,v} \, A_v$$
> This is a key simplification that allows us to compute the horizontal force on a curved surface without performing a complex surface integral.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Module-level ESE questions provide internal choice. Below are **Question A** and **Question B**, each carrying **14 marks** with two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**A vertical rectangular gate of width 3 m and height 4 m is submerged in water with its top edge 2 m below the free water surface.**

**(a)** Determine the magnitude of the resultant hydrostatic force acting on the gate and the depth of the center of pressure. (7 marks)

**(b)** If the gate is rotated about a horizontal hinge at its top edge so that it makes an angle of $45°$ with the horizontal, calculate the new magnitude of force and the new location of the center of pressure along the slant. The top edge remains 2 m below the free surface. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A(a):**

**Given:** Width $b = 3 \, \text{m}$, height $H = 4 \, \text{m}$, top edge depth $h_1 = 2 \, \text{m}$.

**Step 1:** Centroid depth (for a rectangle, centroid is at mid-height):

$$h_c = h_1 + \frac{H}{2} = 2 + \frac{4}{2} = 2 + 2 = 4 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 2:** Area:

$$A = b \times H = 3 \times 4 = 12 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Magnitude of resultant force:

$$F_R = \gamma \, h_c \, A = 9810 \times 4 \times 12 = 470{,}880 \, \text{N} \approx 470.88 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 4:** Second moment of area about centroidal axis (parallel to free surface):

$$I_{xc} = \frac{b H^3}{12} = \frac{3 \times 4^3}{12} = \frac{3 \times 64}{12} = \frac{192}{12} = 16 \, \text{m}^4$$

**Step 5:** Center of pressure depth (vertical surface, so $\theta = 90°$):

$$h_p = h_c + \frac{I_{xc}}{h_c A} = 4 + \frac{16}{4 \times 12} = 4 + \frac{16}{48} = 4 + 0.333 = 4.333 \, \text{m}$$

**Answer:**

$$\boxed{F_R = 470.88 \, \text{kN}, \quad h_p = 4.333 \, \text{m below free surface}}$$

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A(b):**

**Given:** Same gate rotated to $\theta = 45°$ with horizontal. Top edge depth = 2 m.

**Step 1:** Vertical depth of centroid:

$$h_c = h_1 + \frac{H}{2} = 2 + 2 = 4 \, \text{m}$$

(The vertical depth of the centroid is invariant under rotation about the top edge.)

**Step 2:** Area remains $A = 12 \, \text{m}^2$.

**Step 3:** New resultant force:

$$F_R = \gamma \, h_c \, A = 9810 \times 4 \times 12 = 470.88 \, \text{kN}$$

(The magnitude is unchanged because $h_c$ and $A$ are unchanged.)

**Step 4:** Slant distance to centroid:

$$y_c = \frac{h_c}{\sin\theta} = \frac{4}{\sin 45°} = \frac{4}{0.7071} = 5.657 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 5:** Slant distance to center of pressure:

$$y_p = y_c + \frac{I_{xc}}{y_c A} = 5.657 + \frac{16}{5.657 \times 12} = 5.657 + \frac{16}{67.884}$$

$$y_p = 5.657 + 0.2357 = 5.893 \, \text{m along the inclined plane}$$

**Answer:**

$$\boxed{F_R = 470.88 \, \text{kN}, \quad y_p = 5.893 \, \text{m from free surface along the slant}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**A cylindrical gate of radius $R = 2 \, \text{m}$ and width $w = 4 \, \text{m}$ (perpendicular to the page) is used to retain water. The water level is at the top of the cylinder. The cylinder's axis is horizontal.**

**(a)** Determine the horizontal and vertical components of the hydrostatic force acting on the curved surface per unit width. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the magnitude and direction of the resultant force per unit width. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B(a):**

**Setup:** Consider a quarter-cylindrical surface (or half-cylindrical — depending on interpretation). For this problem, assume the gate is a **half-cylinder** retaining water on one side, with water reaching up to the diameter (top of cylinder).

**Per unit width ($w = 1$):**

**Step 1:** Compute the horizontal force. The vertical projection of the curved surface is a rectangle of height $R$ and width $w$. Per unit width:

$$A_v = R \times 1 = 2 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Centroid depth of vertical projection:

$$h_{c,v} = \frac{R}{2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 3:** Horizontal force:

$$F_H = \gamma \, h_{c,v} \, A_v = 9810 \times 1 \times 2 = 19{,}620 \, \text{N per meter width} \approx 19.62 \, \text{kN/m}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the vertical force. The volume of water (per unit width) above the curved surface is a quarter-circle of radius $R$:

$$V = \frac{\pi R^2}{4} \times 1 = \frac{\pi \times 4}{4} = \pi = 3.1416 \, \text{m}^3 \text{ per meter width}$$

**Step 5:** Vertical force:

$$F_V = \gamma \, V = 9810 \times 3.1416 = 30{,}819 \, \text{N/m} \approx 30.82 \, \text{kN/m}$$

**Answer:**

$$\boxed{F_H = 19.62 \, \text{kN/m}, \quad F_V = 30.82 \, \text{kN/m}}$$

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B(b):**

**Step 1:** Magnitude of resultant:

$$F_R = \sqrt{F_H^2 + F_V^2} = \sqrt{(19.62)^2 + (30.82)^2} = \sqrt{385.0 + 949.9} = \sqrt{1334.9}$$

$$F_R = 36.54 \, \text{kN per meter width}$$

**Step 2:** Direction with respect to horizontal:

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{F_V}{F_H} = \frac{30.82}{19.62} = 1.571$$

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(1.571) = 57.5° \text{ with horizontal}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{F_R = 36.54 \, \text{kN/m} \text{ acting at } 57.5° \text{ above horizontal}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Common mistakes students make:
> 1. **Forgetting to use gauge pressure** — including atmospheric pressure leads to incorrect moment calculations.
> 2. **Mixing units** — using $\rho$ in $\text{kg/m}^3$ but forgetting the factor of $g$. Always use $\gamma = \rho g$ in $\text{N/m}^3$ when applying force formulas.
> 3. **Computing center of pressure in slant distance but reporting vertical depth without conversion** — KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** for this mix-up.
> 4. **For curved surfaces, computing $F_V$ as weight of *displaced* fluid instead of fluid *above* the surface** — these are different concepts and lead to opposite signs.
> 5. **Sign convention error on $F_V$** — fluid above pushes **down**, fluid below pushes **up**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Use this before your KTU exam!**

- ✅ Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$: $p = \gamma h = \rho g h$.
- ✅ Force on any submerged plane surface: $F_R = \gamma h_c A$ (only depends on vertical depth of centroid).
- ✅ Center of pressure on inclined surface (slant): $y_p = y_c + \dfrac{I_{xc}}{y_c A}$.
- ✅ Center of pressure on inclined surface (vertical depth): $h_p = h_c + \dfrac{I_{xc} \sin^2\theta}{h_c A}$.
- ✅ For a **vertical** plane surface: $\theta = 90°$, so $h_p = h_c + \dfrac{I_{xc}}{h_c A}$ (since $\sin 90° = 1$).
- ✅ For a **horizontal** plane surface: pressure is uniform, so center of pressure = centroid.
- ✅ Force on curved surface is computed in **two perpendicular components**:
  - Horizontal: $F_H = \gamma h_c A_v$ (on vertical projection).
  - Vertical: $F_V = \gamma V$ (weight of fluid/imaginary volume above surface).
- ✅ Resultant on curved surface: $F_R = \sqrt{F_H^2 + F_V^2}$ at angle $\tan^{-1}(F_V/F_H)$.
- ✅ The center of pressure is **always below** the centroid for any plane surface not horizontal.
- ✅ Second moment of area values to memorise: Rectangle $\dfrac{bh^3}{12}$, Triangle $\dfrac{bh^3}{36}$, Circle $\dfrac{\pi R^4}{4}$.
- ✅ For water, use $\gamma_w = 9810 \, \text{N/m}^3$ and $\rho_w = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$.
- ✅ Pascal's law allows us to equate horizontal force on a curved surface to that on its vertical projection.
- ✅ Pressure prism volume = Resultant force; Pressure prism centroid = Line of action of resultant.
- ✅ Units consistency: $F$ in N, $A$ in $\text{m}^2$, depths in m, $\gamma$ in $\text{N/m}^3$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Centre of Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Centre of Pressure # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Centre of Pressure (CoP)** is defined as the point on the surface of an immersed (submerged) body — typically a plane surface exposed to a hydrostatic pressure field — at which the **resultant hydrostatic pressure force** is considered to act. In other words, it is the point of application of the total hydrostatic thrust. For a plane surface submerged in a static fluid, the pressure distribution is **linear** (since hydrostatic pressure varies linearly with depth). Because the pressure is **not uniform** across the surface, the resultant force does **not** pass through the geometric centroid of the area. Instead, it acts at a point located **below** the centroid (along the line of greatest pressure gradient), and this point is termed the **Centre of Pressure**. Mathematically, the depth of the Centre of Pressure is given by the symbolic expression: $$h^{*} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{G} \sin^{2}\theta}{A \cdot \bar{h}}$$ where each symbol has a precise definition (covered in §2). > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1):** The concept of Centre of Pressure is foundational for deriving total hydrostatic thrust on plane surfaces (horizontal, vertical, and inclined) and is a prerequisite for studying buoyancy, stability of floating bodies, and design of dams/gates in subsequent modules. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine you are pushing a **rectangular wooden plank underwater** in a swimming pool and holding it vertically with one hand at the top edge. The water pushes against the entire back face of the plank, but the push is **stronger at the bottom** (deeper) and **weaker at the top** (shallower). If you were to replace all that distributed push with a **single equivalent force** acting at one point, that point would be **lower than the middle of the plank** — closer to the bottom edge. That single equivalent point is the **Centre of Pressure**. The deeper the plank is submerged, the more uniform the pressure becomes, and the closer the Centre of Pressure moves toward the geometric centre of the plank. > [!TIP] > **Think of it like this:** A heavy toolbox placed on one end of a seesaw makes it tip. The Centre of Pressure is the "balance point" of all the water-pressure "weights" pushing on the surface. Since the bottom of the surface carries heavier "weights" (higher pressure), the balance point is shifted downward. ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics | Quantity | Symbol | Value / Unit | Significance | |---|---|---|---| | Density of water | $\rho$ | $\mathbf{1000 \; kg/m^{3}}$ | Used for fresh water problems | | Density of sea water | $\rho$ | $\mathbf{1025 \; kg/m^{3}}$ | Standard ocean water | | Density of mercury | $\rho_{Hg}$ | $\mathbf{13600 \; kg/m^{3}}$ | For manometer / gauge fluid | | Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | $\mathbf{9.81 \; m/s^{2}}$ | Standard Earth gravity | | Specific weight of water | $\gamma = \rho g$ | $\mathbf{9810 \; N/m^{3}}$ | Often used to simplify expressions | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Physical Setup and Underlying Logic Consider a **plane surface** of arbitrary shape submerged in a static, incompressible fluid. The surface is inclined at an angle $\theta$ to the horizontal free surface of the liquid. The figure (see §4) shows the geometric setup: - **$x$–$y$ plane** = the plane of the submerged surface - **$y$** = distance measured **along the inclined plane** from the point **O** (where the inclined plane intersects the free liquid surface) - **$\bar{y}$** = distance of the **centroid (G)** of the surface from O, measured along the inclined plane - **$\theta$** = angle between the plane surface and the free liquid surface - **$h$** = depth of a typical strip below the free liquid surface = $y \sin\theta$ - **$\bar{h} = \bar{y} \sin\theta$** = depth of the centroid G - **$h^{*}$** = depth of the Centre of Pressure (the point we want to locate) - **$y^{*}$** = slant distance of the Centre of Pressure from O ## 2.2 Logical Step-by-Step Reasoning **Step 1 — Pressure on an elemental strip:** An elemental strip of area $dA$ (width $b$, length $dy$) is located at a slant distance $y$ from O. Its depth below the free surface is $h = y\sin\theta$. The hydrostatic pressure at this depth is: $$p = \rho g h = \rho g y \sin\theta$$ **Step 2 — Force on the elemental strip:** $$dF = p \cdot dA = \rho g y \sin\theta \, dA$$ **Step 3 — Total resultant force (by integration):** $$F = \int_{A} \rho g y \sin\theta \, dA = \rho g \sin\theta \int_{A} y \, dA = \rho g \sin\theta \, A \, \bar{y} = \rho g A \bar{h}$$ This is the classical hydrostatic thrust formula: $F = \rho g A \bar{h}$. **Step 4 — Moment of the pressure forces about the line of intersection (axis through O):** $$M = \int_{A} y \cdot dF = \int_{A} y \cdot (\rho g y \sin\theta) \, dA = \rho g \sin\theta \int_{A} y^{2} \, dA = \rho g \sin\theta \, I_{x}$$ where $I_{x}$ is the **second moment of area** (moment of inertia) of the submerged surface about the axis XX (the line where the surface meets the free liquid surface). **Step 5 — Apply the Parallel Axis Theorem:** $$I_{x} = I_{G} + A\bar{y}^{2}$$ where $I_{G}$ is the moment of inertia about an axis through the **centroid G** parallel to XX. **Step 6 — Locate the Centre of Pressure:** By definition, the Centre of Pressure lies at a slant distance $y^{*}$ such that: $$F \cdot y^{*} = M$$ $$y^{*} = \dfrac{M}{F} = \dfrac{\rho g \sin\theta \, (I_{G} + A\bar{y}^{2})}{\rho g \sin\theta \, A \bar{y}} = \dfrac{I_{G} + A\bar{y}^{2}}{A\bar{y}}$$ $$\boxed{\,y^{*} = \bar{y} + \dfrac{I_{G}}{A\bar{y}}\,}$$ In terms of **vertical depth** from the free surface: $$h^{*} = y^{*} \sin\theta = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{G} \sin^{2}\theta}{A\bar{h}}$$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table must be memorised verbatim for KTU ESE 2024 Scheme examinations. | # | Formula | Description | Special Case | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F = \rho g A \bar{h}$ | Total hydrostatic thrust on a plane surface | Always | | 2 | $h^{*} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{G} \sin^{2}\theta}{A \bar{h}}$ | Depth of Centre of Pressure (general) | Inclined surface | | 3 | $y^{*} = \bar{y} + \dfrac{I_{G}}{A \bar{y}}$ | Slant distance of CoP from O | Inclined surface | | 4 | $h^{*} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{G}}{A \bar{h}}$ | Depth of CoP (vertical surface) | $\theta = 90^{\circ} \Rightarrow \sin\theta = 1$ | | 5 | $h^{*} = 0$ | Centre of pressure on the free surface | Horizontal surface (top face) | | 6 | $h^{*} - \bar{h} = \dfrac{I_{G} \sin^{2}\theta}{A \bar{h}}$ | Eccentricity (offset of CoP below centroid) | Always | | 7 | $I_{G}$ for rectangle ($b \times d$) | $\dfrac{b d^{3}}{12}$ | About centroidal axis parallel to $b$ | | 8 | $I_{G}$ for circle (radius $R$) | $\dfrac{\pi R^{4}}{4}$ | About any diameter | | 9 | $I_{G}$ for triangle (base $b$, height $h$) | $\dfrac{b h^{3}}{36}$ | About centroidal axis parallel to base | | 10 | $I_{G}$ for semicircle (radius $R$) | $0.110 \, R^{4}$ | About centroidal diameter | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Design of Dams and Lock Gates:** The line of action of water thrust determines overturning and sliding stability. - **Ship Hull Design:** Centre of pressure on rudders and hydrofoils governs manoeuvring moments. - **Submarine Pressure Hulls:** Critical for buckling analysis under external hydrostatic loading. - **Sluice Gates and Reservoir Spillways:** Hydrostatic force location dictates hinge/anchor placement. - **Storage Tank Walls:** Side-wall thickness design depends on maximum pressure and its moment arm. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — General Inclined Plane Surface ### Setup A plane surface of area $A$ is immersed in a static liquid of specific weight $\gamma = \rho g$. The surface is inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal free surface. The intersection of the plane with the free surface defines the reference axis **XX**. A point on the surface is located by its slant distance $y$ from XX. The centroid **G** of the surface lies at slant distance $\bar{y}$ from XX. ### Step 1: Elemental Strip Take a thin elemental strip parallel to XX, of width $b$ and thickness $dy$ at slant distance $y$ from XX: $$dA = b \cdot dy$$ ### Step 2: Depth of the Elemental Strip Vertical depth of the strip below the free liquid surface: $$h = y \sin\theta$$ ### Step 3: Hydrostatic Pressure on the Strip By the fundamental equation of hydrostatics: $$p = \rho g h = \rho g y \sin\theta$$ ### Step 4: Force on the Elemental Strip $$dF = p \cdot dA = (\rho g y \sin\theta) \cdot (b \, dy) = \rho g y \sin\theta \, dA$$ ### Step 5: Total Resultant Force Integrate over the entire area $A$: $$F = \int_{A} \rho g y \sin\theta \, dA$$ Since $\rho$, $g$, and $\sin\theta$ are constants for a given setup: $$F = \rho g \sin\theta \int_{A} y \, dA$$ By the definition of the centroid, $\int_{A} y \, dA = A\bar{y}$, hence: $$\boxed{\,F = \rho g A \bar{y} \sin\theta = \rho g A \bar{h}\,}$$ ### Step 6: Moment of the Pressure Force about Axis XX $$M = \int_{A} y \cdot dF = \int_{A} y \cdot \rho g y \sin\theta \, dA = \rho g \sin\theta \int_{A} y^{2} \, dA$$ By the definition of the second moment of area about axis XX, $\int_{A} y^{2} \, dA = I_{x}$. Therefore: $$M = \rho g \sin\theta \, I_{x}$$ ### Step 7: Apply the Parallel Axis Theorem $$I_{x} = I_{G} + A\bar{y}^{2}$$ Substituting: $$M = \rho g \sin\theta \, (I_{G} + A\bar{y}^{2})$$ ### Step 8: Locate the Centre of Pressure By the principle of moments, the resultant force $F$ acting at distance $y^{*}$ from XX produces the same moment: $$F \cdot y^{*} = M$$ $$y^{*} = \dfrac{M}{F} = \dfrac{\rho g \sin\theta \, (I_{G} + A\bar{y}^{2})}{\rho g \sin\theta \, A \bar{y}}$$ After algebraic cancellation: $$y^{*} = \dfrac{I_{G} + A\bar{y}^{2}}{A\bar{y}} = \dfrac{I_{G}}{A\bar{y}} + \bar{y}$$ $$\boxed{\,y^{*} = \bar{y} + \dfrac{I_{G}}{A\bar{y}}\,}$$ ### Step 9: Express the Centre of Pressure in Terms of Vertical Depth The vertical depth of the CoP is: $$h^{*} = y^{*} \sin\theta = \left(\bar{y} + \dfrac{I_{G}}{A\bar{y}}\right) \sin\theta = \bar{y}\sin\theta + \dfrac{I_{G}\sin\theta}{A\bar{y}}$$ Recognising that $\bar{h} = \bar{y}\sin\theta$ and $\sin^{2}\theta = \bar{h}/\bar{y}$: $$\boxed{\,h^{*} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{G} \sin^{2}\theta}{A \bar{h}}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Deduction Check:** Since $I_{G} > 0$, $A > 0$, $\bar{h} > 0$, and $0 < \sin^{2}\theta \le 1$, the term $\dfrac{I_{G}\sin^{2}\theta}{A\bar{h}}$ is **always positive**. Therefore $h^{*} > \bar{h}$, confirming the Centre of Pressure always lies **below** the centroid. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Special Case: Vertical Plane Surface For a **vertical surface**, the plane is perpendicular to the free surface, so $\theta = 90^{\circ}$ and $\sin\theta = 1$. Also, the slant distance $y$ equals the vertical depth $h$. Substituting $\sin\theta = 1$ and $\bar{y} = \bar{h}$ into the general result: $$h^{*} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{G} \cdot (1)^{2}}{A \cdot \bar{h}} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{G}}{A\bar{h}}$$ $$\boxed{\,h^{*} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{G}}{A \bar{h}} \quad \text{(vertical surface)}\,}$$ This is the **most frequently tested formula** in KTU ESE for vertical gates and walls. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Special Case: Horizontal Plane Surface (Top Face) For a **horizontal surface** flush with the free surface (e.g., the lid of a tank), $\theta = 0^{\circ}$, so $\sin\theta = 0$ and $\bar{h} = 0$. However, the formula for $h^{*}$ becomes indeterminate (of the form $0/0$) if we plug in directly. Physically, the pressure is uniform over the surface, so the Centre of Pressure **coincides with the centroid**: $$h^{*} = \bar{h} = 0 \quad \text{(horizontal surface, top face)}$$ --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Standard) ### Problem A circular gate of diameter **1.5 m** is provided at the **vertical end** of a water tank. The centre of the gate is located at a depth of **3 m** below the free water surface. Determine: **(a)** The total hydrostatic thrust on the gate. **(b)** The depth of the Centre of Pressure. **(c)** The position of the Centre of Pressure relative to the centroid. ### Given Data - Diameter of gate, $d = 1.5 \; m \Rightarrow R = 0.75 \; m$ - Depth of centroid, $\bar{h} = 3 \; m$ - Surface orientation: vertical ($\theta = 90^{\circ}$) - Specific weight of water, $\gamma = \rho g = 9810 \; N/m^{3}$ ### Solution — Part (a): Total Hydrostatic Thrust Area of the gate: $$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^{2} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (1.5)^{2} = 1.7671 \; m^{2}$$ Total hydrostatic thrust: $$F = \rho g A \bar{h} = 9810 \times 1.7671 \times 3$$ $$\boxed{\,F = 52{,}008.6 \; N \approx 52.01 \; kN\,}$$ ### Solution — Part (b): Depth of Centre of Pressure Moment of inertia of a circular area about its centroidal axis: $$I_{G} = \dfrac{\pi R^{4}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.75)^{4}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.31641}{4} = 0.2485 \; m^{4}$$ For a vertical surface ($\theta = 90^{\circ}$): $$h^{*} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{G}}{A \bar{h}} = 3 + \dfrac{0.2485}{1.7671 \times 3}$$ $$h^{*} = 3 + \dfrac{0.2485}{5.3014} = 3 + 0.0469$$ $$\boxed{\,h^{*} = 3.0469 \; m \text{ below the free surface}\,}$$ ### Solution — Part (c): Offset from Centroid $$h^{*} - \bar{h} = 0.0469 \; m = 46.9 \; mm \text{ below the centroid}$$ This small offset (compared to the 1.5 m diameter) confirms the physical insight: **the deeper a surface is submerged, the closer the Centre of Pressure moves toward the centroid**. --- # 4. The second chart is a *decision tool* — pick the right formula based on surface orientation. These two flows together summarise the entire topic for rapid revision. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, Part A questions test direct recall and conceptual clarity. Answers should be 4–6 lines with a key formula. ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define the term "Centre of Pressure" for a plane surface submerged in a static liquid. Why does it lie below the centroid of the surface?** **Model Answer:** The Centre of Pressure is the point on the surface of a submerged plane area at which the resultant hydrostatic pressure force is considered to act. It lies below the centroid because the hydrostatic pressure is **not uniform** over the surface; it increases linearly with depth ($p = \rho g h$). The deeper portions of the surface experience greater pressure, so the "weighted average" of the pressure distribution — which is the Centre of Pressure — is shifted toward the deeper (lower) side of the surface. The vertical offset between the centroid and the Centre of Pressure is: $$h^{*} - \bar{h} = \dfrac{I_{G} \sin^{2}\theta}{A\bar{h}} \quad \text{(always positive)}$$ Hence $h^{*} > \bar{h}$, proving that the Centre of Pressure always lies **below** the centroid. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** **A square plane surface of side $a$ is immersed vertically in water with its top edge at the free water surface. Find the depth of the Centre of Pressure.** **Model Answer:** **Given:** Square of side $a$, vertical, top edge at free surface. - Depth of centroid: $\bar{h} = a/2$ - Area: $A = a^{2}$ - Moment of inertia about centroidal axis (parallel to free surface): $I_{G} = a^{4}/12$ For a vertical surface ($\theta = 90^{\circ}$): $$h^{*} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{G}}{A\bar{h}} = \dfrac{a}{2} + \dfrac{a^{4}/12}{a^{2} \cdot (a/2)} = \dfrac{a}{2} + \dfrac{a}{6} = \dfrac{3a + a}{6}$$ $$\boxed{\,h^{*} = \dfrac{2a}{3} \text{ from the free surface}\,}$$ This is a standard KTU result and is often asked directly in Part A. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme ESE mandates an **internal choice** in Part B Module questions. Two complete alternatives (Question A and Question B) are provided below. Each is split into two 7-mark sub-parts. ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **An inclined rectangular gate of width 2 m and height 1.5 m is installed in the side of a dam. The gate is inclined at 60° to the horizontal and the top edge of the gate is 2 m below the free water surface. Calculate:** **(a)** The total hydrostatic thrust acting on the gate and the location of the Centre of Pressure measured along the slant length from the top edge of the gate. **(b)** The position of the Centre of Pressure in terms of vertical depth and the moment of the hydrostatic thrust about the top edge of the gate. #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Given:** - Width of gate, $b = 2 \; m$ - Height of gate (along the slant), $L = 1.5 \; m$ - Angle with horizontal, $\theta = 60^{\circ}$, so $\sin\theta = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.866$ - Depth of top edge of gate, $h_{top} = 2 \; m$ - $\gamma = \rho g = 9810 \; N/m^{3}$ **Step 1: Area of the gate:** $$A = b \times L = 2 \times 1.5 = 3.0 \; m^{2}$$ **Step 2: Slant distance of centroid from the free surface axis XX:** The top edge of the gate is 2 m below the free surface. The depth of the top edge corresponds to a slant distance of $y_{top} = h_{top}/\sin\theta = 2 / 0.866 = 2.309 \; m$ from XX. The centroid is at the **mid-height** of the gate, so its slant distance from the top edge is $L/2 = 0.75 \; m$. $$\bar{y} = y_{top} + \dfrac{L}{2} = 2.309 + 0.75 = 3.059 \; m$$ **Step 3: Vertical depth of the centroid:** $$\bar{h} = \bar{y} \sin\theta = 3.059 \times 0.866 = 2.649 \; m$$ **Step 4: Total hydrostatic thrust:** $$F = \gamma A \bar{h} = 9810 \times 3.0 \times 2.649$$ $$\boxed{\,F = 77{,}962 \; N \approx 77.96 \; kN\,}$$ **Step 5: Moment of inertia of rectangle about centroidal axis (parallel to width $b$):** $$I_{G} = \dfrac{b L^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{2 \times (1.5)^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{2 \times 3.375}{12} = 0.5625 \; m^{4}$$ **Step 6: Slant distance of Centre of Pressure from XX:** $$y^{*} = \bar{y} + \dfrac{I_{G}}{A\bar{y}} = 3.059 + \dfrac{0.5625}{3.0 \times 3.059}$$ $$y^{*} = 3.059 + \dfrac{0.5625}{9.178} = 3.059 + 0.0613 = 3.120 \; m$$ **Step 7: Slant distance of CoP from the top edge of the gate:** $$y^{*} - y_{top} = 3.120 - 2.309$$ $$\boxed{\,y^{*}_{from \; top} = 0.811 \; m \text{ along the slant}\,}$$ #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Step 8: Vertical depth of the Centre of Pressure:** $$h^{*} = y^{*} \sin\theta = 3.120 \times 0.866$$ $$\boxed{\,h^{*} = 2.702 \; m \text{ below the free surface}\,}$$ **Verification using the general formula:** $$h^{*} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{G} \sin^{2}\theta}{A \bar{h}} = 2.649 + \dfrac{0.5625 \times (0.866)^{2}}{3.0 \times 2.649}$$ $$h^{*} = 2.649 + \dfrac{0.5625 \times 0.75}{7.947} = 2.649 + \dfrac{0.4219}{7.947} = 2.649 + 0.0531$$ $$h^{*} = 2.702 \; m \quad \checkmark \text{ (Matches)}$$ **Step 9: Moment of the hydrostatic thrust about the top edge:** The perpendicular distance from the top edge (at depth $h_{top} = 2 \; m$) to the Centre of Pressure is: $$\text{Vertical offset} = h^{*} - h_{top} = 2.702 - 2.0 = 0.702 \; m$$ $$\boxed{\,M_{top} = F \times (h^{*} - h_{top}) = 77{,}962 \times 0.702 = 54{,}729 \; N \cdot m \approx 54.73 \; kN \cdot m\,}$$ --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]** **A vertical triangular gate has its base of 1.2 m at the top, located 1 m below the free water surface, and its apex pointing downward at a depth of 3 m. Determine:** **(a)** The total hydrostatic thrust on the gate and the depth of the Centre of Pressure. **(b)** The position of the Centre of Pressure measured from the free water surface and verify that the resultant thrust passes below the centroid. #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Given:** - Triangular gate, vertical orientation ($\theta = 90^{\circ}$) - Base $b = 1.2 \; m$ at the top - Top of base at depth $h_{top} = 1 \; m$ - Apex at depth $h_{apex} = 3 \; m$ - Height of triangle (along vertical), $h_{tri} = 3 - 1 = 2 \; m$ **Step 1: Area of the triangular gate:** $$A = \dfrac{1}{2} \times b \times h_{tri} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1.2 \times 2 = 1.2 \; m^{2}$$ **Step 2: Depth of the centroid:** For a triangle, the centroid lies at $1/3$ of the height from the base. Since the base is at the top and the apex is at the bottom, the centroid is located at $1/3$ of the height from the base, i.e., $2/3$ of the height from the apex. Distance of centroid from the base (downward): $$\dfrac{h_{tri}}{3} = \dfrac{2}{3} = 0.667 \; m$$ Depth of centroid from free surface: $$\bar{h} = h_{top} + \dfrac{h_{tri}}{3} = 1 + 0.667 = 1.667 \; m$$ **Step 3: Total hydrostatic thrust:** $$F = \gamma A \bar{h} = 9810 \times 1.2 \times 1.667$$ $$\boxed{\,F = 19{,}621 \; N \approx 19.62 \; kN\,}$$ **Step 4: Moment of inertia of triangle about centroidal axis (parallel to base):** $$I_{G} = \dfrac{b h_{tri}^{3}}{36} = \dfrac{1.2 \times (2)^{3}}{36} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 8}{36} = \dfrac{9.6}{36} = 0.2667 \; m^{4}$$ **Step 5: Depth of Centre of Pressure (vertical surface):** $$h^{*} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{G}}{A\bar{h}} = 1.667 + \dfrac{0.2667}{1.2 \times 1.667}$$ $$h^{*} = 1.667 + \dfrac{0.2667}{2.0} = 1.667 + 0.1333$$ $$\boxed{\,h^{*} = 1.800 \; m \text{ below the free water surface}\,}$$ #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Step 6: Position of the Centre of Pressure from the free surface:** $$h^{*} = 1.800 \; m \text{ (already computed in part a)}$$ **Step 7: Offset of CoP below the centroid:** $$h^{*} - \bar{h} = 1.800 - 1.667 = 0.133 \; m = 133 \; mm \text{ below the centroid}$$ **Step 8: Verification that CoP lies below the centroid:** Since the offset $h^{*} - \bar{h} = +0.133 \; m > 0$, the Centre of Pressure is located **below** the centroid. This confirms the general principle that the Centre of Pressure always lies below the centroid for any plane surface submerged in a static liquid, with the offset given by: $$h^{*} - \bar{h} = \dfrac{I_{G} \sin^{2}\theta}{A\bar{h}} = \dfrac{0.2667 \times 1}{1.2 \times 1.667} = 0.133 \; m \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 9: Resultant thrust location summary:** | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Total thrust, $F$ | $19.62 \; kN$ | | Depth of centroid, $\bar{h}$ | $1.667 \; m$ | | Depth of Centre of Pressure, $h^{*}$ | $1.800 \; m$ | | Offset below centroid | $0.133 \; m$ | | Distance of CoP from top of triangle | $1.800 - 1.000 = 0.800 \; m$ | | Distance of CoP from apex of triangle | $3.000 - 1.800 = 1.200 \; m$ | The resultant force of $19.62 \; kN$ acts at a depth of $1.800 \; m$, which is $0.133 \; m$ below the centroid of the triangle — confirming all theoretical predictions. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE Valuation:** > > 1. **Confusing $\bar{y}$ and $\bar{h}$:** Many students substitute $\bar{h}$ where $\bar{y}$ is required, or vice versa. **Remember:** $\bar{y}$ is the slant distance along the inclined surface, and $\bar{h} = \bar{y} \sin\theta$ is the vertical depth. The formula for the slant distance uses $\bar{y}$, while the formula for vertical depth uses $\bar{h}$. > > 2. **Forgetting the $\sin^{2}\theta$ factor:** The general formula $h^{*} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{G} \sin^{2}\theta}{A\bar{h}}$ applies to **all** orientations. The simplified version $h^{*} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{G}}{A\bar{h}}$ is **only** valid for vertical surfaces ($\theta = 90^{\circ}$). Using the simplified form for an inclined surface is a **fatal error** worth 3–4 marks. > > 3. **Using wrong $I_{G}$ formula:** Students often confuse the moment of inertia of a triangle ($\dfrac{bh^{3}}{36}$) with that of a rectangle ($\dfrac{bh^{3}}{12}$). The factor $1/36$ vs $1/12$ is the most-tested trap. > > 4. **Unit errors:** $I_{G}$ must be in $\text{m}^{4}$ and $A$ in $\text{m}^{2}$. Mixing CGS/SI units is a common deduction of 1 mark. > > 5. **Sign convention for offset:** Always state clearly that the Centre of Pressure is **below** the centroid. Just writing the magnitude without indicating direction loses 1 mark. > > 6. **Skipping the verification step:** In 14-mark questions, KTU examiners award up to 2 marks for verifying the result using the alternate form of the formula or by computing the moment equilibrium. **Do not skip this.** --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision — Centre of Pressure** - **Definition:** Centre of Pressure = point of application of the total hydrostatic thrust on a submerged plane surface. **Always lies below the centroid** (never above or at). - **Core Formula (general inclined surface):** $$h^{*} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{G} \sin^{2}\theta}{A\bar{h}} \qquad y^{*} = \bar{y} + \dfrac{I_{G}}{A\bar{y}}$$ - **Special Case — Vertical Surface ($\theta = 90°$):** $$h^{*} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{G}}{A\bar{h}}$$ - **Special Case — Horizontal Surface (top face):** $$h^{*} = \bar{h}$$ (pressure is uniform, CoP coincides with centroid). - **Total Hydrostatic Thrust:** $$F = \rho g A \bar{h} = \gamma A \bar{h}$$ - **Key Insight:** As $\bar{h} \to \infty$ (very deep submersion), the term $\dfrac{I_{G} \sin^{2}\theta}{A\bar{h}} \to 0$, and the CoP **approaches the centroid**. For shallow depths, the CoP is **significantly below** the centroid. - **Key Result for Square (top edge at free surface, vertical):** CoP is at $\dfrac{2a}{3}$ from the free surface (not $a/2$ as intuition suggests). This is the single most-tested numerical in KTU exams. - **Moment of Inertia Quick-Reference:** - Rectangle ($b \times d$): $I_{G} = \dfrac{bd^{3}}{12}$ - Circle (radius $R$): $I_{G} = \dfrac{\pi R^{4}}{4}$ - Triangle (base $b$, height $h$): $I_{G} = \dfrac{bh^{3}}{36}$ - Semicircle (radius $R$): $I_{G} = 0.110 \, R^{4}$ - **Parallel Axis Theorem:** $I_{x} = I_{G} + A\bar{y}^{2}$ — must be used whenever the reference axis is **not** through the centroid. - **Order of Magnitude Check:** For typical KTU problems, the CoP is only a few centimetres below the centroid for surfaces a few metres deep. If your answer gives an offset of metres, suspect a calculation error. - **Always quote three things in the final answer:** (1) the total thrust $F$ in N or kN, (2) the depth of the Centre of Pressure $h^{*}$ in metres, and (3) the **direction** of the thrust (always normal to the surface, pointing into it). - **Pressure Prism Analogy:** The total thrust equals the volume of the pressure prism, and the Centre of Pressure passes through the **centroid of the pressure prism**. This is a useful geometric shortcut for rectangular surfaces. - **Two Formulae — One Answer:** Always cross-verify $h^{*}$ using both $h^{*} = y^{*} \sin\theta$ and the direct general formula. KTU examiners reward this consistency check with 1–2 bonus marks. ---

topics.title: Buoyancy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Buoyancy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **BUOYANCY (Archimedes' Principle):**
> *Buoyancy is the upward vertical force exerted by a fluid on a body that is either fully submerged in it or floating on its surface. According to Archimedes' Principle, this buoyant force is equal in magnitude to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body, and it acts vertically upward through the centroid of the displaced volume (called the Centre of Buoyancy, B).*

Mathematically, the buoyant force is expressed as:

$$F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot \forall$$

where:
- $F_B$ = Buoyant Force (in Newtons, **N**)
- $\rho_f$ = Mass density of the fluid (in **kg/m³**)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $\forall$ = Volume of fluid displaced (in **m³**)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of Buoyancy like this — "The Fluid's Revenge":**
> Imagine you are trying to push a rubber ball underwater in a swimming pool. The deeper you push, the harder the water pushes back up at you. This "push-back" is buoyancy in action. The water is not being mean — it is simply rushing in to fill the space the ball is vacating, and in doing so, it generates an upward force exactly equal to the weight of the water that would have occupied that space.

**Why does a massive iron ship float but a small iron nail sinks?**
- A nail is *solid* iron (density ~ 7874 kg/m³), so it displaces very little water relative to its enormous weight → it sinks.
- A ship is mostly *hollow* (filled with air). The average density of the ship (iron + air) is less than water (1000 kg/m³) → it floats, with the hull displacing a volume of water whose weight equals the entire ship's weight.

> [!NOTE]
> **KEY SYLLABUS HIGHLIGHT (KTU 2024):**
> Module 1 mandates the study of fluid statics, including the rigorous analysis of submerged and floating bodies. The two pillars for exam purposes are:
> 1. **Buoyancy Force & Centre of Buoyancy (B)** — for fully submerged bodies.
> 2. **Metacentre (M), Metacentric Height (GM), and Stability** — for floating bodies.

---

## 1.3 Three Physical States of a Body in a Fluid

| Case | Condition | Net Force | Equilibrium State |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Body floats partially submerged** | Weight of body = Weight of displaced fluid | Net Force = **0** | Stable floating equilibrium |
| **Body is fully submerged and neutrally buoyant** | Density of body = Density of fluid | Net Force = **0** | Neutral equilibrium (e.g., submarines in trim) |
| **Body sinks to the bottom** | Weight of body > Buoyant force | Net Force acts **downward** | No equilibrium — body rests on bed |

---

## 1.4 Specific Gravity & Its Role

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Specific Gravity (SG)** or **Relative Density** is a dimensionless number defined as the ratio of the density of a substance to the density of a standard fluid (water at 4 °C, $\rho_w = 1000$ **kg/m³** for liquids; air at STP for gases).

$$SG = \frac{\rho_{substance}}{\rho_{water}}$$

A body **floats** when its average density is less than the fluid's density (i.e., its specific gravity is less than **1.0**). This is the foundation of naval architecture and ship design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposing the Buoyancy Problem — The Logical Flow

The analysis of buoyancy under the KTU 2024 scheme requires understanding **three distinct conceptual layers**:

1. **Layer 1 — The Force:** Determining the magnitude and direction of the buoyant force using Archimedes' Principle.
2. **Layer 2 — The Point of Application:** Locating the Centre of Buoyancy (B) — the centroid of the displaced fluid volume.
3. **Layer 3 — The Stability:** For floating bodies, analyzing whether the body returns to equilibrium when tilted. This introduces the **Metacentre (M)** and **Metacentric Height (GM)**.

---

## 2.2 The Concept of Centre of Buoyancy (B)

> [!NOTE]
> **Centre of Buoyancy (B):** The point through which the buoyant force is considered to act. It is the **centroid of the displaced fluid volume**. For a fully submerged body, B coincides with the geometric centroid of the body. For a partially submerged (floating) body, B lies on the body's central vertical axis at the centroid of the submerged portion only.

**Crucial Behaviour of B during Tilting:**
When a floating body tilts (rolls) by a small angle $\theta$ about its longitudinal axis, the shape of the displaced volume changes. As a result, **B shifts from the central axis to a new position B'**. This shift is the cornerstone of stability analysis.

---

## 2.3 The Metacentre (M) and Metacentric Height (GM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metacentre (M):** The point where the vertical line through the **new** Centre of Buoyancy (B') intersects the original vertical central axis of the body. For small tilt angles, M is considered a *fixed* point for a given body and load configuration.

**Metacentric Height (GM)** = The vertical distance between the **Centre of Gravity (G)** of the body and the **Metacentre (M)**.

$$GM = BM - BG$$

where:
- $BM$ = Distance between Metacentre and Centre of Buoyancy
- $BG$ = Distance between Centre of Buoyancy and Centre of Gravity

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Archimedes' Buoyant Force | $F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot \forall$ | $F_B$ in **N**, $\forall$ in **m³** |
| 2 | Weight of Body (Floating Equilibrium) | $W = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot \forall_{submerged}$ | Used to find submerged depth |
| 3 | Specific Gravity of Body | $SG = \dfrac{W_{body}}{W_{displaced\_fluid}} = \dfrac{\rho_{body}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Metacentric Radius | $BM = \dfrac{I}{V_{submerged}}$ | $I$ = Second moment of area of waterline plane (**m⁴**), $V$ in **m³** |
| 5 | Metacentric Height | $GM = BM - BG = \dfrac{I}{V} - BG$ | In **metres (m)** |
| 6 | Stability Condition (Floating) | $GM > 0$ (Stable); $GM = 0$ (Neutral); $GM < 0$ (Unstable) | Pure number test |
| 7 | Time Period of Rolling (Oscillation) | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^{2}}{g \cdot GM}}$ | $k$ = radius of gyration, $T$ in **seconds** |
| 8 | Righting Couple / Moment | $M_R = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$ | Restoring moment, $\theta$ = tilt angle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM ALERT:** In numerical problems, KTU examiners often write the second moment of area as $I_{min}$ — this refers to the **minimum** second moment of area of the *waterline plane* (the horizontal cross-section at the water surface). This is because rolling occurs about the axis of *least* moment of inertia.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Naval Architecture:** Designing ship hulls with high $GM$ for rough seas. Cargo ships need small $GM$ for comfortable (less jerky) rolling.
- **Submarine Design:** Ballast tanks are flooded/emptied to change $\rho_{avg}$ of the vessel to control depth.
- **Hydrometer:** A direct application of buoyancy — depth of immersion indicates fluid density.
- **Dam Design:** Uplift pressure on the base of a dam is fundamentally a buoyancy problem.
- **Hot-Air Balloons:** Buoyancy of heated air keeps the balloon aloft.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Buoyant Force from First Principles (Pressure Integration)

Consider a small elemental area $dA$ on the surface of a submerged body. The fluid pressure at depth $h$ is given by the hydrostatic equation:

$$p = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h$$

The force on the elemental area in the direction of the outward normal is:

$$dF = p \cdot dA = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h \cdot dA$$

For a closed body, we integrate the pressure force over the entire surface. The horizontal components cancel out due to symmetry. Only the **vertical** components survive.

> **Step 1:** Resolve $dF$ into vertical component: $dF_z = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h \cdot dA \cos\theta$.
> **Step 2:** Note that $dA \cos\theta$ is the horizontal projection of the elemental area — i.e., the cross-sectional area at that depth, $dA_x$.
> **Step 3:** Substitute:
> $$dF_z = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h \cdot dA_x$$
> **Step 4:** Integrate over the horizontal projected area (which is equivalent to integrating over the volume using $d\forall = h \cdot dA_x$):
> $$F_B = \int dF_z = \int \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h \cdot dA_x = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot \int d\forall$$
> **Step 5:** Recognize that $\int d\forall$ is exactly the total displaced volume $\forall$:
> $$\boxed{F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot \forall}$$

This rigorously proves Archimedes' Principle from pressure integration.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Metacentric Radius (BM)

Consider a floating body of width $2b$ (in the plane of tilting) that tilts by a small angle $\theta$ about its longitudinal axis. The original Centre of Buoyancy is at $B$. After tilting, the new Centre of Buoyancy shifts to $B'$.

> **Step 1:** The wedge of fluid that emerges on one side and submerges on the other side has a small volume, which we call $v$.
> **Step 2:** Taking moments about the original Centre of Buoyancy $B$, the buoyant force on the emerged wedge (acting upward through its centroid) and the buoyant force on the submerged wedge (acting downward through its centroid) form a couple.
> **Step 3:** The horizontal distance between the centroids of the two wedges is approximately $\dfrac{2}{3} \cdot 2b = \dfrac{4b}{3}$.
> **Step 4:** The total moment about $B$ is:
> $$M = (\rho_f \cdot g \cdot v) \cdot \frac{4b}{3}$$
> **Step 5:** Equating this to the restoring moment $W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta \approx W \cdot GM \cdot \theta$ (for small $\theta$):
> $$W \cdot GM \cdot \theta = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot v \cdot \frac{4b}{3}$$
> **Step 6:** Recognize that the wedge volume is $v = A \cdot \bar{x}$, where $A$ is the area of the wedge's base and $\bar{x}$ is the distance of the wedge's centroid from the axis. For a triangular wedge of length $2b$ and height $(2b)\tan\theta$:
> $$v = \frac{1}{2}(2b)(2b \tan\theta) \cdot \bar{x} = 2b^{2}\tan\theta \cdot \bar{x}$$
> **Step 7:** The first moment of the waterline area is $\int x \cdot dA$, and for a triangle, $\int x \cdot dA = 2b^{2}\tan\theta \cdot \bar{x}$. So the moment of the wedge couple becomes $\rho_f \cdot g \cdot \theta \cdot I$, where $I = \int x^{2} dA$ is the second moment of area of the waterline plane.
> **Step 8:** Since $W = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_{submerged}$ (Archimedes), substitute and solve:
> $$\rho_f \cdot g \cdot V \cdot BM \cdot \theta = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot I \cdot \theta$$
> $$\boxed{BM = \frac{I}{V_{submerged}}}$$

This is the **Metacentric Radius**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Time Period of Rolling

For a floating body executing small rolling oscillations, the equation of motion about the metacentre is:

> **Step 1:** The restoring moment for a small angular displacement $\theta$ is:
> $$M_R = -W \cdot GM \cdot \theta$$
> (Negative sign indicates the moment is *opposing* the displacement — i.e., restoring.)
> **Step 2:** The equation of rotational motion is:
> $$I_o \cdot \frac{d^{2}\theta}{dt^{2}} = -W \cdot GM \cdot \theta$$
> where $I_o$ is the mass moment of inertia about the axis of rotation.
> **Step 3:** Rewrite $I_o = m \cdot k^{2}$, where $m = W/g$ is the mass and $k$ is the radius of gyration:
> $$\frac{W}{g} \cdot k^{2} \cdot \frac{d^{2}\theta}{dt^{2}} = -W \cdot GM \cdot \theta$$
> **Step 4:** This is a simple harmonic motion of the form $\ddot{\theta} + \omega^{2}\theta = 0$ with:
> $$\omega^{2} = \frac{g \cdot GM}{k^{2}}$$
> **Step 5:** The natural angular frequency is $\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{g \cdot GM}{k^{2}}}$, so the time period is:
> $$\boxed{T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{k^{2}}{g \cdot GM}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A ship with a *larger* $GM$ rolls faster (shorter period — "stiff" ship, jerky ride). A ship with a *smaller* $GM$ rolls slower (longer period — "tender" ship, smoother ride). This is a classic design trade-off in naval architecture.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem (KTU Style):** A solid cylinder of diameter 0.5 m and height 0.8 m floats in water with its axis vertical. The specific gravity of the cylinder material is 0.7. Determine:
> (a) The depth of immersion.
> (b) Whether the equilibrium is stable, and find the metacentric height. Take $I$ for a circular waterline = $\pi d^{4} / 64$.

**Solution:**

> **Part (a): Depth of immersion**
> For floating equilibrium, Weight of cylinder = Weight of water displaced.
> $$W_{cyl} = \rho_{cyl} \cdot g \cdot V_{cyl} = 0.7 \cdot 1000 \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}(0.5)^{2} \cdot 0.8$$
> $$W_{water\_disp} = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_{sub} = 1000 \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}(0.5)^{2} \cdot h$$
> Equating:
> $$0.7 \cdot 0.8 = h$$
> $$\boxed{h = 0.56 \text{ m}}$$

> **Part (b): Metacentric height**
> **Step 1:** $V_{sub} = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.5)^{2} \cdot 0.56 = 0.11 \text{ m}^{3}$
> **Step 2:** $I = \frac{\pi (0.5)^{4}}{64} = 3.068 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{4}$
> **Step 3:** $BM = \dfrac{I}{V_{sub}} = \dfrac{3.068 \times 10^{-4}}{0.11} = 2.79 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 0.00279 \text{ m}$
> **Step 4:** Find $BG$. $G$ is at the centroid of the cylinder (height $= 0.4$ m from base). $B$ is at the centroid of submerged volume (height $= h/2 = 0.28$ m from base). So:
> $$BG = 0.4 - 0.28 = 0.12 \text{ m}$$
> **Step 5:**
> $$GM = BM - BG = 0.00279 - 0.12 = -0.117 \text{ m}$$
> $$\boxed{GM = -0.117 \text{ m} < 0 \Rightarrow \textbf{UNSTABLE}}$$

**Conclusion:** The cylinder will tip over when placed in water with its axis vertical (because the height is too tall relative to its diameter — $h > 2R$). This is the classic result that *tall, thin* floating objects are unstable.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture of Buoyancy & Stability

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the hierarchical relationships between all key concepts in the Buoyancy module. This is the functional architecture of how the ideas interlock for exam problem-solving.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Buoyancy Module] --> B[Archimedes Principle]
    A --> C[Centre of Buoyancy B]
    A --> D[Stability Analysis]
    
    B --> B1[Force FB equals weight of displaced fluid]
    B --> B2[Acts vertically upward]
    
    C --> C1[Fully submerged body]
    C --> C2[Floating body - B is at centroid of submerged volume]
    
    D --> D1[Floating body - requires Metacentre M]
    D1 --> D2[Metacentric Height GM = BM - BG]
    D2 --> D3[Stability Condition: GM greater than 0]
    
    D1 --> D4[Righting Moment W times GM times sin theta]
    D1 --> D5[Time Period T = 2 pi sqrt of k squared over g GM]
    
    C2 --> F1[Submerged volume V]
    F1 --> D1
    G1[Waterline Plane] --> I1[Second Moment of Area I]
    I1 --> D1
    
    subgraph KTU_Key_Formulas
        K1[FB equals rho times g times V]
        K2[BM equals I over V]
        K3[GM equals BM minus BG]
    end
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Stability Decision Logic

This second Mermaid diagram shows the *decision-flow* that a student must follow in numerical problems to determine the stability of a floating body.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Given: Floating body problem] --> Q{Is body fully submerged?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Apply FB equals rho times g times V for full volume]
    Q -- No --> S[Find depth of immersion h from W equals rho times g times Vsub]
    
    S --> T[Locate Centre of Gravity G]
    S --> U[Locate Centre of Buoyancy B at h over 2]
    T --> V[Compute BG]
    U --> V
    V --> W[Compute BM = I over Vsub]
    W --> X[GM = BM minus BG]
    X --> Y{GM greater than 0?}
    Y -- Yes --> Z[STABLE - body returns to upright]
    Y -- No --> AA[UNSTABLE - body capsizes]
```

## 4.3 Physical Schematic — Tilting Floating Body

Since Mermaid cannot easily render physical 3-D geometry of a ship rolling, the following **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** captures the *kinematic* relationships that occur during the tilting of a floating body. This is precisely the schematic a student should draw in their answer script for stability problems.

```
        ORIGINAL AXIS              TILTED AXIS (after small angle θ)
        ─────────────                ╲
              │                       ╲
              G ●                      G ●  (G does NOT move)
              │                        ╲
              │     ←───  BG  ───→     ╲
              │                         ╲
              B ●                        ╲    ● B'  (New Centre of Buoyancy)
              │                          ╲  ╱
              │            BM             ╲╱
              │  ─────────────────────    ╱╲
              │                          ╱  ╲
              │  ←── θ ───→             ╱    ╲
                                       ╱      ● M  (Metacentre: intersection 
                                       ╱            of vertical through B' 
                                      ╱             with original axis)
        ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ WATERLINE ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
              │                        ╱
              │  ←── I (2nd moment) ──→       (Wedge volume v changes)
              │                        ╱
              W ● (weight)             ╱
                                     ╱
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Schematic:**
> 1. The point **G** stays *fixed* during a small tilt (since mass distribution is unchanged).
> 2. The point **B** shifts sideways to **B'** because the shape of the displaced volume changes.
> 3. The line **B'M** is *vertical* (the new buoyant force is vertical).
> 4. The line through **B** and **G** is the *original* central axis.
> 5. **M** is found at the intersection of the new vertical through B' and the original axis.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1.a]**
> **Q1.** Define buoyancy and state Archimedes' Principle. Mention the conditions for a body to float in a fluid.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> Buoyancy is the upward force exerted by a fluid on a body immersed in it. Archimedes' Principle states that the buoyant force is equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body, and it acts vertically upward through the Centre of Buoyancy.
> A body floats when the weight of the body equals the buoyant force, i.e., $W = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_{submerged}$. Equivalently, the average density of the body must be less than or equal to the density of the fluid.

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1.b]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between the Centre of Gravity (G), Centre of Buoyancy (B), and Metacentre (M) for a floating body.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> - **G (Centre of Gravity):** Point where the body's weight is assumed to act. Fixed relative to the body.
> - **B (Centre of Buoyancy):** Centroid of the displaced fluid volume. Moves when the body tilts.
> - **M (Metacentre):** Point where the vertical line through the *new* Centre of Buoyancy (B') intersects the original vertical axis. For small tilts, M is treated as a fixed point.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### ▶ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, Q2]**
> **(a)** A wooden block of dimensions 0.6 m × 0.4 m × 0.3 m and specific gravity 0.65 floats in water. Find the depth of immersion and the position of the Centre of Buoyancy from the base. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** If the block in part (a) is tilted by 5° about its longitudinal axis, calculate the metacentric height and comment on the stability. Take $I_{min} = \dfrac{bd^{3}}{6}$ where $b$ and $d$ are the waterline dimensions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

> **Step 1:** Volume of block, $V = 0.6 \times 0.4 \times 0.3 = 0.072$ m³
> **Step 2:** Weight of block, $W = 0.65 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.072 = 459.11$ N
> **Step 3:** For floating equilibrium, $W = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_{submerged}$
> Let $h$ = depth of immersion. Assuming the 0.6 m × 0.4 m face is at the waterline:
> $V_{sub} = 0.6 \times 0.4 \times h = 0.24h$
> **Step 4:** Equating:
> $0.24h \times 1000 \times 9.81 = 459.11$
> $\boxed{h = 0.195 \text{ m}}$
> **Step 5:** Centre of Buoyancy is at the centroid of the submerged volume:
> $\boxed{B = h/2 = 0.0975 \text{ m from the base}}$

**Model Solution for (b):**

> **Step 1:** $V_{sub} = 0.24 \times 0.195 = 0.0468$ m³
> **Step 2:** Waterline area: $0.6 \times 0.4$ rectangle. Rolling about the long axis (0.6 m), the relevant dimension for $I_{min}$ is the shorter side. The problem states $I_{min} = \dfrac{bd^{3}}{6}$ — taking $b = 0.6$ m and $d = 0.4$ m:
> $I = \dfrac{0.6 \times (0.4)^{3}}{6} = \dfrac{0.6 \times 0.064}{6} = 6.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{4}$
> **Step 3:** $BM = \dfrac{I}{V_{sub}} = \dfrac{6.4 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0468} = 0.1368$ m
> **Step 4:** $G$ is at the geometric centre of the block: $G = 0.3/2 = 0.15$ m from base
> **Step 5:** $BG = G - B = 0.15 - 0.0975 = 0.0525$ m
> **Step 6:** $GM = BM - BG = 0.1368 - 0.0525 = 0.0843$ m
> $$\boxed{GM = 0.0843 \text{ m} > 0 \Rightarrow \text{STABLE}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Many students mistakenly compute $I$ for the *entire* base of the block, not the *waterline plane* cross-section. If the waterline plane is 0.6 × 0.4, you must use those *exact* dimensions in $I_{min} = bd^{3}/6$ — using the full height (0.3 m) instead of the waterline dimension will lead to **wrong answers and loss of up to 4 marks**. Always re-read the problem to identify which face is at the waterline.

---

### ▶ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1, Q3]**
> **(a)** Derive an expression for the metacentric height of a floating body. State the conditions for stable, unstable, and neutral equilibrium. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A cylindrical buoy 1.5 m in diameter and 2.0 m long weighs 25 kN. It is anchored in sea water of specific gravity 1.03. Find the maximum pull it can withstand before being pulled out of water. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

> Refer to the detailed derivation in **Section 3.2** of these notes. Key result:
> $$GM = \frac{I}{V_{submerged}} - BG$$
> **Conditions:**
> - $GM > 0$ → **Stable** (righting moment restores the body)
> - $GM = 0$ → **Neutral** (body stays at the displaced angle)
> - $GM < 0$ → **Unstable** (body capsizes)

**Model Solution for (b):**

> **Step 1:** Weight of buoy $W = 25$ kN $= 25,000$ N.
> **Step 2:** For the buoy to be on the verge of being pulled out, the *buoyant force equals the weight plus the pulling force P*:
> $F_B = W + P$
> $\rho_{sea} \cdot g \cdot V_{total} = W + P$
> **Step 3:** Total volume of cylinder $V = \frac{\pi}{4}(1.5)^{2} \times 2.0 = 3.534$ m³
> **Step 4:** Buoyant force when fully submerged:
> $F_B = 1.03 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 3.534 = 35,693$ N
> **Step 5:** Maximum pull:
> $$P = F_B - W = 35,693 - 25,000 = 10,693 \text{ N}$$
> $$\boxed{P \approx 10.69 \text{ kN}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students often confuse whether the problem asks for *buoyancy* or *net vertical force*. Here, the *buoyant force* is constant (full volume displaced), and any *extra* downward force on the buoy is what it can "withstand" before submerging. If you compute the net force as $F_B - W$ and then forget to interpret it as the "extra load the buoy can support," you will lose **2–3 marks** in the final answer statement.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — BUOYANCY (Module 1)**

- [x] **Archimedes' Principle:** $F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$ — applies to fully *and* partially submerged bodies.
- [x] **Buoyant force always acts upward**, through the centroid of the displaced volume.
- [x] **Floating equilibrium condition:** Weight = Buoyant force (i.e., average density < fluid density for floating).
- [x] **Three points to remember:** G (fixed), B (moves with tilt), M (fixed for small tilts).
- [x] **Metacentric radius:** $BM = I / V_{submerged}$ — use $I_{min}$ of the *waterline plane*.
- [x] **Metacentric height:** $GM = BM - BG$ — must be **positive** for stability.
- [x] **Stability check:** $GM > 0$ (stable), $GM = 0$ (neutral), $GM < 0$ (unstable).
- [x] **Time period of rolling:** $T = 2\pi \sqrt{k^{2} / (g \cdot GM)}$ — larger $GM$ means *faster* rolling.
- [x] **Righting moment:** $M_R = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$ — for small $\theta$, $\sin\theta \approx \theta$.
- [x] **Common units trap:** $I$ in **m⁴**, $V$ in **m³**, hence $BM$ in **m**.
- [x] **Design rule of thumb:** Tall, slender floating bodies have small $BM$ and large $BG$, so they are usually **unstable**. Flat, wide bodies (rafts) are highly stable.
- [x] **Practical examples to cite in answers:** Hydrometer, submarines, ships, balloons, icebergs (note: 90% of an iceberg is submerged because ice SG ≈ 0.92).
- [x] **Always specify which waterline cross-section** you are using to compute $I$ — this is where most marks are lost in numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equilibrium of Floating Bodies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equilibrium of Floating Bodies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equilibrium of a Floating Body (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A floating body is said to be in **equilibrium** when the vertical forces (Weight acting downward through the Centre of Gravity, *G*, and the Buoyant Force acting upward through the Centre of Buoyancy, *B*) are equal in magnitude, opposite in direction, and collinear along a common vertical line.

For a body *partially immersed* in a fluid, two fundamental conditions must be simultaneously satisfied for static equilibrium:

1. **Force Equilibrium (Translational):**
   $$\sum F_{vertical} = 0 \implies W = F_B$$
   where $W$ is the total weight of the body and $F_B$ is the buoyant force exerted by the displaced fluid.

2. **Moment Equilibrium (Rotational):**
   $$\sum M_{about\ any\ point} = 0$$
   The line of action of the weight (*W*) and the buoyant force ($F_B$) must lie along the **same vertical line**.

### 1.2 Centre of Buoyancy and Centre of Gravity

| Symbol | Term | Physical Meaning |
|--------|------|------------------|
| **G** | **Centre of Gravity** | Point where the entire weight *W* of the body is assumed to act. Fixed for a rigid body. |
| **B** | **Centre of Buoyancy** | Centroid of the volume of fluid *displaced* by the body. Shifts as the body tilts. |
| **M** | **Metacentre** | Point of intersection of the vertical line passing through the *new* Centre of Buoyancy ($B'$) and the original vertical line through *B*, when the body is slightly tilted. |
| **GM** | **Metacentric Height** | The vertical distance between the Centre of Gravity (*G*) and the Metacentre (*M*). The single most important parameter in KTU problems. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cork on Water"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition:**
> Imagine pushing a piece of cork slightly into a bucket of water and then letting it go. Why does it come back upright? Because as the cork tilts, the shape of the displaced water changes, the *Centre of Buoyancy* shifts, and a restoring couple is generated by the weight and buoyant force. This invisible "self-righting" property is governed entirely by the **Metacentric Height (GM)**.
>
> - If you gently place a **tall, narrow pencil** on water → it tips over (unstable).
> - If you place a **wide, flat wooden disc** on water → it stays level (stable).
> - The only difference between them? Their *GM* values.

### 1.4 Three Types of Equilibrium (KTU Favourite Question)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification of Equilibrium of Floating Bodies:**
> 1. **Stable Equilibrium** — Body, when disturbed, returns to its original position. (GM is **positive**, i.e., M lies *above* G).
> 2. **Unstable Equilibrium** — Body, when disturbed, moves further away from its original position. (GM is **negative**, i.e., M lies *below* G).
> 3. **Neutral Equilibrium** — Body, when disturbed, remains in the new position. (GM is **zero**, i.e., M coincides with G).

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Values Used in KTU 2024 Numerical Problems:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$
> - Density of fresh water: $\rho_{water} = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$
> - Specific weight of fresh water: $\gamma_{water} = 9810\ \text{N/m}^3$
> - Density of sea water: $\rho_{sea} = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$
> - Specific weight of sea water: $\gamma_{sea} = 10052\ \text{N/m}^3$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Archimedes' Principle — The Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Archimedes' Principle (KTU 2024 Module 1 Foundation):**
> *"When a body is immersed wholly or partially in a fluid, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body."*

Mathematically:
$$F_B = \rho_{fluid} \cdot g \cdot \forall_{displaced} = \gamma_{fluid} \cdot \forall_{displaced}$$

where $\forall_{displaced}$ is the **volume of fluid displaced** by the submerged portion of the body.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework for Equilibrium Analysis

The following step-by-step logic is the **expected KTU board solution structure** for any "Equilibrium of Floating Body" problem:

- **Step 1 — Compute Weight (W):**
  Identify the total mass ($m$) of the floating body. Compute $W = m \cdot g$.

- **Step 2 — Compute Submerged Volume ($\forall$):**
  Apply the equilibrium condition $W = F_B$ to find the volume submerged. Rearrange:
  $$\forall_{submerged} = \frac{W}{\gamma_{fluid}}$$

- **Step 3 — Locate Centre of Buoyancy (B):**
  The point B is the **centroid of the displaced (submerged) volume**. For symmetric floating bodies (rectangular, cylindrical, spherical), B lies on the vertical axis of symmetry.

- **Step 4 — Locate Centre of Gravity (G):**
  G is the centroid of the **entire body volume**, weighted by its mass distribution. For homogeneous bodies, G coincides with the geometric centre.

- **Step 5 — Compute the Distance $\overline{BG}$:**
  Measure the vertical separation between B and G along the symmetry axis. Sign convention: G above B → **negative**; G below B → **positive** (KTU standard).

- **Step 6 — Compute the Moment of Inertia of the Waterline Plane (I):**
  The waterline plane is the horizontal cross-section of the body at the free water surface. Its second moment of area is $I$.

- **Step 7 — Compute $\overline{BM}$:**
  $$\overline{BM} = \frac{I}{\forall_{submerged}}$$

- **Step 8 — Compute the Metacentric Height $\overline{GM}$:**
  $$\overline{GM} = \overline{BM} \pm \overline{BG}$$
  Sign convention: **+** when G is *below* B (typical floating ship case), **−** when G is *above* B (typical submerged pontoon case).

- **Step 9 — Interpret the Result:**
  - $\overline{GM} > 0$ → Stable
  - $\overline{GM} = 0$ → Neutral
  - $\overline{GM} < 0$ → Unstable

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference for KTU ESE Module 1 — Floating Bodies**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $F_B = \gamma_{fluid} \cdot \forall$ | Archimedes' Principle | All floating body problems |
| 2 | $W = \rho_{body} \cdot g \cdot \forall_{body}$ | Weight of the body | Step 1 of any problem |
| 3 | $\forall_{sub} = \dfrac{W}{\gamma_{fluid}}$ | Submerged volume | Depth of immersion |
| 4 | $\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I}{\forall_{sub}}$ | Distance from B to M | Rolling stability |
| 5 | $\overline{GM} = \overline{BM} \pm \overline{BG}$ | Metacentric height | Stability check |
| 6 | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{g \cdot \overline{GM}}}$ | Time period of oscillation | Rolling motion of ships |
| 7 | $\theta_{max} = \dfrac{M_{disturbing}}{W \cdot \overline{GM}}$ | Maximum angle of heel | Ship stability under couple |
| 8 | $I_{rectangle} = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$ | MOI of rectangular waterline | Ship / pontoon |
| 9 | $I_{circle} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^4}{64}$ | MOI of circular waterline | Cylindrical buoy |
| 10 | $I_{triangle} = \dfrac{b^3 \cdot h}{36}$ | MOI of triangular waterline | Special problems |

where $k$ = radius of gyration of the body about its longitudinal axis through G.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where This Topic Matters in the Industry:**
> - **Naval Architecture:** Design of ships, submarines, and offshore platforms.
> - **Civil Engineering:** Stability of pontoon bridges, floating cranes, and caissons used for deep foundation construction.
> - **Hydraulic Engineering:** Design of floating docks, buoys, ferry terminals, and flood-control gates.
> - **Aerospace:** Stability of floating airship / dirigible envelopes.
> - **Renewable Energy:** Floating solar PV platforms and floating wind turbines — all depend on a *positive GM* to remain upright under wind and wave action.

### 2.5 Stability of Submerged vs Floating Bodies — A Subtle Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
>
> **Submerged Body in Fluid:**
> - The displaced volume is the *entire* body volume, so B is *fixed*.
> - For stability, B and G must **coincide**. If they don't, a moment arises.
> - Stable if B is *above* G (i.e., for a body denser than fluid, equilibrium is impossible — it sinks).
> - Most submerged bodies (e.g., submarines) maintain stability by *dynamic* control or by *intentionally* placing heavy ballast low.
>
> **Floating Body on Fluid Surface:**
> - The displaced volume is *only the submerged portion*, so B **moves** as the body tilts.
> - For stability, M must lie *above* G → $\overline{GM} > 0$.
> - This is the classical ship-stability problem, which dominates KTU numerical questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Metacentric Height Formula $\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I}{\forall}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters:** This is the *single most important derivation* in Module 1. It is asked almost every year in KTU ESE, either as a full 7-mark question or as a sub-part.

**Setup:** Consider a floating body (say, a ship) in its equilibrium position. Let the original Centre of Buoyancy be at $B$. The body is now slightly tilted (heeled) by a small angle $\theta$ about a longitudinal axis. The new Centre of Buoyancy shifts to $B'$.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the Shift:**
When the body tilts, an additional wedge of volume $\Delta \forall$ enters the water on one side, and an equal wedge leaves the water on the other side. The total submerged volume remains constant, but the *centroid* of this new submerged volume shifts.

**Step 2 — Buoyant Force Couple:**
The new buoyant force $F_B$ acts vertically upward through the new Centre of Buoyancy $B'$. To find the position of $B'$ relative to $B$, we apply the **principle of moments**:

$$F_B \cdot \overline{BB'} = (\text{weight of new wedge}) \cdot (\text{moment arm})$$

**Step 3 — Detailed Force-Moment Balance:**

Let the waterline area have a small elemental strip of area $dA$ at a perpendicular distance $y$ from the axis of rotation (the longitudinal axis). When the body tilts by $\theta$, this strip moves vertically by $y \cdot \theta$. The buoyant force contribution from this strip is:

$$dF_B = \rho_{fluid} \cdot g \cdot dA \cdot y \cdot \theta$$

**Step 4 — Moment about the Original Vertical Axis:**
The moment produced by this elemental buoyant force about the original vertical line through $B$ is:

$$dM = dF_B \cdot y = \rho_{fluid} \cdot g \cdot \theta \cdot y^2 \cdot dA$$

**Step 5 — Integrating over the Entire Waterline Area:**
$$M = \int dM = \rho_{fluid} \cdot g \cdot \theta \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

By definition, the **second moment of area** of the waterline plane about the axis of rotation is:
$$I = \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

Therefore:
$$M = \rho_{fluid} \cdot g \cdot \theta \cdot I$$

**Step 6 — Buoyant Force Magnitude:**
$$F_B = \rho_{fluid} \cdot g \cdot \forall_{submerged}$$

**Step 7 — Equating Moments to Find $\overline{BB'}$:**
Since the moment of the buoyant force about $B$ equals the total moment from the wedges:

$$F_B \cdot \overline{BB'} = M$$

$$\rho_{fluid} \cdot g \cdot \forall \cdot \overline{BB'} = \rho_{fluid} \cdot g \cdot \theta \cdot I$$

$$\overline{BB'} = \frac{\theta \cdot I}{\forall}$$

**Step 8 — Locating the Metacentre M:**
By geometry, the metacentre $M$ is on the vertical line through $B'$. The triangle $B B' M$ gives:

$$\tan \theta = \frac{\overline{BB'}}{\overline{BM}} \approx \theta \quad \text{(for small angles)}$$

Therefore:
$$\overline{BM} = \frac{\overline{BB'}}{\theta} = \frac{I}{\forall_{submerged}}$$

**Final Derived Formula:**

$$\boxed{\overline{BM} = \frac{I}{\forall_{submerged}}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Time Period of Oscillation of a Floating Body

**Step 1 — Setting up the Rotational Equation:**
When a floating body is slightly tilted and released, it undergoes simple harmonic motion (SHM) under the action of the restoring couple produced by $W$ and $F_B$.

**Step 2 — Restoring Couple:**
The restoring moment about the metacentre $M$ is:
$$M_{restoring} = W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \sin\theta \approx W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \theta$$

**Step 3 — Moment of Inertia of the Body:**
The moment of inertia of the body about its longitudinal axis through $G$ is:
$$I_{body} = m \cdot k^2 = \frac{W}{g} \cdot k^2$$

where $k$ is the radius of gyration.

**Step 4 — Applying Newton's Second Law for Rotation:**
$$I_{body} \cdot \ddot{\theta} = -W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \theta$$

$$m \cdot k^2 \cdot \ddot{\theta} = -W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \theta$$

$$\ddot{\theta} = -\frac{W \cdot \overline{GM}}{m \cdot k^2} \cdot \theta = -\frac{g \cdot \overline{GM}}{k^2} \cdot \theta$$

**Step 5 — Comparing with SHM Equation $\ddot{\theta} = -\omega^2 \cdot \theta$:**
$$\omega^2 = \frac{g \cdot \overline{GM}}{k^2}$$

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{g \cdot \overline{GM}}{k^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Time Period of Oscillation:**

$$\boxed{T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{k^2}{g \cdot \overline{GM}}}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** A *larger* GM means a *shorter* oscillation period — a ship with high GM rolls quickly and feels "stiff" (sometimes uncomfortably jerky). Naval architects aim for a moderate GM to balance stability with passenger comfort.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Stability Conditions for Different Body Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comprehensive KTU Reference Matrix (Save for Last-Minute Revision):**

| Body Configuration | Position of B | Position of G | Position of M | $\overline{GM}$ | Type of Equilibrium |
|--------------------|---------------|---------------|---------------|------------------|----------------------|
| Floating body, homogeneous | Below free surface | Geometric centre | Above G | $> 0$ | **Stable** |
| Floating body with heavy top deck | Below free surface | Far above B | May fall below G | $< 0$ | **Unstable** |
| Floating body, perfectly balanced (e.g., sphere) | Centroid of submerged cap | Centroid of sphere | Coincides with G | $= 0$ | **Neutral** |
| Submerged body (lighter than fluid) | Below G | Geometric centre | Above G | $> 0$ | **Stable** |
| Submerged body (denser than fluid) | Above G | Geometric centre | Below G | $< 0$ | **Unstable** (sinks) |
| Submerged body held by string | Variable | Fixed | Variable | External constraint | **Neutral** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Equilibrium Concepts Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Floating Body on Fluid Surface"] --> B["Vertical Force Balance W equals FB"]
    A --> C["Moment Equilibrium W and FB collinear"]
    B --> D["Compute Submerged Volume"]
    C --> E["Identify B and G on Vertical Axis"]
    D --> F["Calculate BM equals I over Vsub"]
    E --> G["Measure BG Distance"]
    F --> H["Compute GM equals BM plus or minus BG"]
    G --> H
    H --> I{"Sign of GM"}
    I -->|GM greater than 0| J["Stable Equilibrium"]
    I -->|GM equals 0| K["Neutral Equilibrium"]
    I -->|GM less than 0| L["Unstable Equilibrium"]
    J --> M["Body Returns to Original Position"]
    K --> N["Body Stays in New Position"]
    L --> O["Body Capsizes or Sinks"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: KTU Problem-Solving Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Step 1: Read problem and extract W, geometry, fluid type"] --> P2["Step 2: Compute W equals m times g"]
    P2 --> P3["Step 3: Compute Vsub equals W over gamma"]
    P3 --> P4["Step 4: Locate B as centroid of submerged volume"]
    P4 --> P5["Step 5: Locate G as centroid of full body"]
    P5 --> P6["Step 6: Measure BG with sign convention"]
    P6 --> P7["Step 7: Compute MOI I of waterline area"]
    P7 --> P8["Step 8: Compute BM equals I over Vsub"]
    P8 --> P9["Step 9: Compute GM equals BM plus or minus BG"]
    P9 --> P10["Step 10: Check sign and classify stability"]
    P10 --> P11["Step 11: Optional time period T equals 2 pi sqrt k squared over g times GM"]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Description: Metacentre Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O["Original Vertical Axis"] --> B["B - Original Centre of Buoyancy"]
    B --> G["G - Centre of Gravity on Axis"]
    B -. tilted position .-> Bp["B prime - New Centre of Buoyancy"]
    Bp --> M["M - Metacentre on New Vertical"]
    G --> GM["GM - Metacentric Height"]
    B --> BM["BM equals I over V"]
    B --> BG["BG - Distance between B and G"]
    M --> MM["M above G indicates Stable"]
    style MM fill:#90EE90
```

### 4.4 Block Architecture: Stability Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Body Type Floating or Submerged"] --> S2{"Floating"}
    S2 -->|Yes| S3["Locate B and G"]
    S2 -->|No| S4["Submerged Body Rules"]
    S3 --> S5{"G above B?"}
    S5 -->|Yes| S6["GM equals BM minus BG"]
    S5 -->|No| S7["GM equals BM plus BG"]
    S6 --> S8{"GM greater than 0?"}
    S7 --> S8
    S8 -->|Yes| S9["STABLE"]
    S8 -->|No| S10["UNSTABLE"]
    S4 --> S11["B and G must coincide for Neutral"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Always draw a *rough free-body diagram* in your answer sheet showing the positions of B, G, and M with the directions of W and $F_B$. The examiner awards full marks only when this visual is present alongside the numerical solution.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap
