- **Aircraft wing aerodynamics:** Air flows faster over the curved upper surface (smaller effective cross-section), creating a pressure difference that generates **lift**.
- **Nozzle design in rockets:** The continuity equation dictates how a converging nozzle accelerates exhaust gases.
- **Blood flow in arteries:** Narrowed arteries (stenosis) cause increased blood velocity, which can be detected via Doppler ultrasound.

## 1.3 Classification of Flow Types Used in Continuity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Steady Flow:** Fluid properties at any point do not change with time. The continuity equation simplifies to the constant mass flow rate form.
> - **Unsteady Flow:** Properties change with time; an accumulation term must be added.
> - **Incompressible Flow:** Density $\rho$ is constant (e.g., liquids at low speeds).
> - **Compressible Flow:** Density varies (e.g., gases, high-speed flows).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Foundation — The "Why" Behind Continuity

The continuity equation rests on one of the most fundamental laws in physics: **Conservation of Mass**.

Consider an arbitrary **Control Volume (CV)** of fixed shape and size in space. Fluid flows through it. Three possibilities exist for the mass inside the CV:

1. Mass flowing **IN** through the control surface.
2. Mass flowing **OUT** through the control surface.
3. Mass **ACCUMULATING** inside the CV (storage).

Since mass is neither created nor destroyed:

$$
\text{Mass In} - \text{Mass Out} = \text{Rate of Accumulation of Mass inside CV}
$$

Mathematically expressed in integral form (Reynolds Transport Theorem applied to mass):

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV \;+\; \int_{CS} \rho \, (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA \;=\; 0
$$

For **steady flow**, the time derivative vanishes, yielding:

$$
\int_{CS} \rho \, (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA \;=\; 0
$$

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Derivation Logic

1. **Identify the Control Volume (CV):** A fixed region in space bounded by a Control Surface (CS).
2. **Apply Mass Conservation:** Sum of mass entering = Sum of mass leaving + Accumulation.
3. **Express Mass Flow Rate:** $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ for uniform 1-D flow.
4. **For Steady Flow:** Accumulation = 0, so $\dot{m}$ is constant along the stream tube.
5. **For Incompressible Flow:** $\rho$ = constant, so $AV$ = constant = $Q$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU Board Examinations**

| # | Equation Form | Mathematical Expression | Application / Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General Mass Conservation (Integral) | $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV}\rho\,dV + \int_{CS}\rho(\vec{V}\cdot\hat{n})\,dA = 0$ | Foundation of all fluid flow analysis |
| 2 | Steady Flow Continuity (1-D) | $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ | Compressible flow through pipes/nozzles |
| 3 | Incompressible Flow Continuity | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q$ | Water flow, hydraulic systems |
| 4 | Differential Form (3-D Cartesian) | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$ | Field analysis, CFD |
| 5 | Differential Form (Vector Notation) | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ | Compact general form |
| 6 | Incompressible Continuity (Vector) | $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ | Incompressible, steady or unsteady |
| 7 | Cylindrical Coordinates | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial(r \rho v_r)}{\partial r} + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial(\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0$ | Axisymmetric flows, jets |
| 8 | Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ (kg/s) | Rate at which mass crosses a section |
| 9 | Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q = A V$ (m³/s) | Incompressible flow rate |
| 10 | Velocity-Area Relation | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Velocity calculation in pipes |

## 2.4 Boundary Conditions for the Continuity Equation

> [!NOTE]
> - At a **solid impermeable wall** (no-slip, no-penetration): $V_n = 0$ (normal velocity component vanishes).
> - At a **free surface** (e.g., water-air interface): Pressure is constant (atmospheric); kinematic condition applies.
> - At a **symmetry plane**: Normal derivative of velocity = 0.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Continuity Equation |
|---|---|
| **Hydraulic Engineering** | Pipe network design, water distribution systems |
| **Aerospace** | Nozzle design, wind tunnel calibration, lift generation |
| **Mechanical (Pumps & Turbines)** | Velocity triangles, flow rate in impellers |
| **Chemical Engineering** | Reactor design, mixing tanks, flow through orifices |
| **Biomedical** | Blood flow analysis, respiratory airflow in trachea |
| **Civil (Open Channels)** | Spillway design, river flow measurements |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Continuity Equation for 1-D Steady Flow (Stream Tube)

**Starting Premise:** Consider a fluid flowing steadily through a stream tube whose cross-sectional area varies along its length.

**Step 1 — Define the Control Volume:**
Let the stream tube have cross-sectional area $A_1$ at section 1 and $A_2$ at section 2. The fluid density and velocity are $\rho_1, V_1$ and $\rho_2, V_2$ respectively.

**Step 2 — Mass Flow Rate at Section 1:**
In a small time $\delta t$, the mass of fluid crossing section 1 is:

$$
\delta m_1 = \rho_1 A_1 V_1 \, \delta t
$$

**Step 3 — Mass Flow Rate at Section 2:**
Similarly, the mass crossing section 2 in the same time is:

$$
\delta m_2 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 \, \delta t
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Conservation of Mass:**
For **steady flow**, the mass entering must equal the mass leaving (no accumulation):

$$
\delta m_1 = \delta m_2
$$

Substituting:

$$
\rho_1 A_1 V_1 \, \delta t = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 \, \delta t
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $\delta t$:**

$$
\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = \text{constant} = \dot{m}
$$

**Step 6 — For Incompressible Fluid ($\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho$):**
Divide throughout by $\rho$:

$$
A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most commonly used form in KTU examinations for water/hydraulic problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Differential Form of Continuity Equation (3-D Cartesian)

**Starting Premise:** Consider a differential rectangular control volume of dimensions $dx, dy, dz$ centered at point $(x, y, z)$ with fluid density $\rho$ and velocity components $u, v, w$ in the $x, y, z$ directions.

**Step 1 — Mass Flow IN through Left Face (x-direction):**
The face has area $dy \cdot dz$, and mass flux entering is $\rho u$:

$$
\delta \dot{m}_{x,in} = \left(\rho u - \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x}dx\right) dy\, dz
$$

**Step 2 — Mass Flow OUT through Right Face (x-direction):**
Using Taylor expansion, mass leaving in $x$-direction:

$$
\delta \dot{m}_{x,out} = \left(\rho u + \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x}dx\right) dy\, dz
$$

**Step 3 — Net Mass Flow OUT in x-direction:**

$$
\delta \dot{m}_{x,out} - \delta \dot{m}_{x,in} = \frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x}\, dx\, dy\, dz
$$

**Step 4 — Similar Contributions from y and z Directions:**

$$
\delta \dot{m}_{y,out} - \delta \dot{m}_{y,in} = \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y}\, dx\, dy\, dz
$$

$$
\delta \dot{m}_{z,out} - \delta \dot{m}_{z,in} = \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z}\, dx\, dy\, dz
$$

**Step 5 — Rate of Mass Accumulation inside CV:**

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}\, dx\, dy\, dz
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Conservation of Mass (Net outflow + Accumulation = 0):**

$$
\left[\frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z}\right] dx\, dy\, dz + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}\, dx\, dy\, dz = 0
$$

**Step 7 — Divide by $dx\, dy\, dz$:**

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0\;}
$$

**Step 8 — Vector Notation (Using Divergence Operator):**

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0
$$

**Step 9 — Incompressible Flow Simplification ($\rho$ = constant):**

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Continuity in Cylindrical Coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$

For axisymmetric flow with velocity components $v_r, v_\theta, v_z$:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial(r \rho v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial(\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial(\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0
$$

For **incompressible, axisymmetric flow** ($v_\theta = 0$):

$$
\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial(r v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** Water flows through a pipe that contracts from a diameter of **200 mm** at section 1 to a diameter of **100 mm** at section 2. The velocity at section 1 is **2 m/s**. Calculate:
- (a) The volumetric flow rate $Q$
- (b) The velocity at section 2
- (c) The mass flow rate (take $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000$ kg/m³)

**Solution:**

**Given:** $D_1 = 0.200$ m, $D_2 = 0.100$ m, $V_1 = 2$ m/s, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³

**Step 1 — Calculate Areas:**

$$
A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.200)^2 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.100)^2 = 0.007854 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric Flow Rate (a):**

$$
Q = A_1 V_1 = 0.03142 \times 2 = 0.06283 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Velocity at Section 2 (b):** Using $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$:

$$
V_2 = \frac{A_1 V_1}{A_2} = \frac{0.06283}{0.007854} = 8.0 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass Flow Rate (c):**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho Q = 1000 \times 0.06283 = 62.83 \text{ kg/s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** The velocity quadrupled (from 2 m/s to 8 m/s) because the area reduced to one-fourth. This is the hallmark of incompressible continuity.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow Architecture of the Continuity Equation

The following block diagram illustrates the hierarchical logic structure, types, and engineering applications of the continuity equation:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Conservation of Mass] --> B[Continuity Equation]
    B --> C{Flow Type}
    C -->|Steady| D[Mass Flow Rate Constant]
    C -->|Unsteady| E[Accumulation Term Required]
    D --> F{Fluid Type}
    F -->|Incompressible| G[AV equals Q]
    F -->|Compressible| H[rho AV equals constant]
    G --> I[1D Form]
    G --> J[2D Form]
    G --> K[3D Form]
    H --> I
    H --> J
    H --> K
    I --> L[Pipe Flow Problems]
    J --> M[Channel Flow Analysis]
    K --> N[CFD and Field Analysis]
    L --> O[Engineering Applications]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Hydraulic Networks]
    O --> Q[Aerospace Nozzles]
    O --> R[Turbomachinery]
    O --> S[Biomedical Flow]
```

## 4.2 Process Diagram: From Conservation Law to Working Equation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Step 1: Define Control Volume] --> nodeB[Step 2: Identify Mass In and Out]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 3: Apply Mass Balance]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Step 4: Apply Steady State Condition]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Step 5: Apply Incompressibility]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Final Equation A1V1 equals A2V2]
    nodeA --> nodeG[Mathematical Assumption: 1D Flow]
    nodeG --> nodeF
```

## 4.3 Schematic of a Variable Area Stream Tube (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sec1[Section 1: Area A1, Velocity V1] -->|Constant Q| sec2[Section 2: Area A2, Velocity V2]
    sec1 --> pipe1[Wide Pipe Section]
    pipe1 --> pipe2[Narrowing Region]
    pipe2 --> sec2
    sec2 -->|High Velocity Exit| exit[Outlet]
    style sec1 fill:#E8F4FD
    style sec2 fill:#FDE8E8
    style pipe1 fill:#FFF4E8
    style pipe2 fill:#FFF4E8
```

> [!NOTE]
> The color coding represents: **Blue** (inlet low velocity), **Red** (outlet high velocity), **Orange** (transition region). This visually conveys the inverse relationship between area and velocity for incompressible flow.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the continuity equation in its most general differential form for a 3-D compressible flow. Explain each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The general differential form of the continuity equation in 3-D Cartesian coordinates for a compressible fluid is:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0
$$

**Term-wise Explanation:**
- $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$ — Rate of change of density with time (unsteady term, accumulation rate per unit volume).
- $\dfrac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x}$ — Net mass flux out of the control volume in the $x$-direction per unit volume.
- Similarly, the $y$ and $z$ terms represent net mass flux in their respective directions.
- In vector notation: $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$

This equation is a direct mathematical statement of the **conservation of mass** at every point in the flow field.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**For an incompressible fluid flowing steadily through a pipe of varying cross-section, derive the relationship $A_1V_1 = A_2V_2$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Consider a pipe with cross-sectional areas $A_1$ and $A_2$ at sections 1 and 2. Let the average velocities be $V_1$ and $V_2$. For steady, incompressible flow, mass flow rate is constant:

$$
\rho A_1 V_1 = \rho A_2 V_2
$$

Since the fluid is incompressible, $\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho$ (constant). Cancelling $\rho$:

$$
A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q
$$

This shows that the **discharge $Q$** remains constant along the pipe, and the velocity is **inversely proportional** to the cross-sectional area.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students forget to convert **diameters to radii** (or vice versa) when computing areas — marks deducted for unit errors.
> - Students often use the compressible form $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ even for water problems — read the problem carefully.
> - The continuity equation gives the **velocity ratio**, not the velocities directly. Combine with Bernoulli's equation or other principles for absolute values.
> - Always state the **assumptions** (steady, incompressible, 1-D) before solving — examiners award marks for this.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the continuity equation in differential form for a 3-D Cartesian coordinate system, starting from the principle of conservation of mass.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A pipe of diameter **300 mm** carries water at a velocity of **3 m/s**. The pipe branches into two smaller pipes of diameters **150 mm** and **200 mm**. If the velocity in the 150 mm pipe is **6 m/s**, find the velocity in the 200 mm pipe and the total discharge in the system.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation:** Refer to **Section 3.2** of these notes. The complete step-by-step derivation covering all 9 steps is:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0
$$

For **incompressible flow** ($\rho$ = constant):

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0
$$

In vector form: $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$

**[Full marks awarded for stating assumptions, writing the CV, applying mass balance, and arriving at the final boxed equation.]**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Main pipe: $D_m = 0.300$ m, $V_m = 3$ m/s
- Branch 1: $D_1 = 0.150$ m, $V_1 = 6$ m/s
- Branch 2: $D_2 = 0.200$ m, $V_2 = ?$
- $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000$ kg/m³

**Step 1 — Calculate Cross-Sectional Areas:**

$$
A_m = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.300)^2 = 0.07069 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.150)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.200)^2 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Continuity (Discharge in Main Pipe = Sum of Discharges in Branches):**

$$
Q_m = Q_1 + Q_2
$$

$$
A_m V_m = A_1 V_1 + A_2 V_2
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values:**

$$
(0.07069)(3) = (0.01767)(6) + (0.03142)(V_2)
$$

$$
0.2121 = 0.1060 + 0.03142 \cdot V_2
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_2$:**

$$
V_2 = \frac{0.2121 - 0.1060}{0.03142} = \frac{0.1061}{0.03142} = 3.377 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Discharge:**

$$
Q_m = 0.07069 \times 3 = 0.2121 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 212.1 \text{ L/s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** $Q_1 + Q_2 = 0.1060 + 0.1061 = 0.2121$ m³/s ✓

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 - Alternative]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the integral form of the continuity equation, derive the differential form for an incompressible fluid. State clearly all assumptions and boundary conditions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An air duct of rectangular cross-section **600 mm × 400 mm** carries air at a rate of **1.2 m³/s**. The duct tapers uniformly to a square section of side **300 mm**. Assuming air behaves as an incompressible fluid at this low speed, calculate:
- (i) The inlet velocity of air.
- (ii) The outlet velocity of air.
- (iii) The mass flow rate of air ($\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.2$ kg/m³).

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Starting from the integral continuity equation:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n})\, dA = 0
$$

For **incompressible flow** ($\rho$ = constant), the time derivative of mass becomes:

$$
\rho \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} dV = \rho \frac{\partial V}{\partial t}
$$

For a **fixed control volume**, $\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t} = 0$ (steady flow). So:

$$
\int_{CS} (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n})\, dA = 0
$$

Applying the **Divergence Theorem** (Gauss's Theorem) to convert the surface integral to a volume integral:

$$
\int_{CS} (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n})\, dA = \int_{CV} (\nabla \cdot \vec{V})\, dV = 0
$$

Since this holds for an arbitrary CV, the integrand must be zero:

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0
$$

**Assumptions:**
- Steady flow
- Incompressible fluid
- No sources or sinks inside the CV
- Single-phase flow

**Boundary Condition:** At a solid wall, $V_n = 0$ (no penetration).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Inlet (rectangular): $a_1 = 0.600$ m, $b_1 = 0.400$ m
- Outlet (square): $a_2 = b_2 = 0.300$ m
- $Q = 1.2$ m³/s
- $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.2$ kg/m³

**Step 1 — Inlet Area and Velocity:**

$$
A_1 = a_1 \times b_1 = 0.600 \times 0.400 = 0.240 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
V_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{1.2}{0.240} = 5.0 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Outlet Area and Velocity:**

$$
A_2 = 0.300 \times 0.300 = 0.0900 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
V_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{1.2}{0.0900} = 13.33 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass Flow Rate:**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho Q = 1.2 \times 1.2 = 1.44 \text{ kg/s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The outlet velocity (13.33 m/s) is **2.67 times** the inlet velocity because the area reduced from 0.240 m² to 0.0900 m² (a factor of 2.67). This principle is exploited in **wind tunnel design** and **HVAC ductwork**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for the Continuity Equation**

- ✅ The continuity equation is a direct consequence of the **conservation of mass**.
- ✅ For **1-D steady incompressible flow**: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q$ (m³/s).
- ✅ For **1-D steady compressible flow**: $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = \dot{m}$ (kg/s).
- ✅ For **unsteady flow**, an **accumulation term** $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV}\rho\,dV$ must be included.
- ✅ The **differential form** in Cartesian coordinates is:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0
$$

- ✅ For **incompressible flow**, it reduces to **divergence-free condition**: $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$.
- ✅ Velocity is **inversely proportional** to area for a given discharge.
- ✅ The continuity equation gives the **velocity ratio**, not absolute values — combine with Bernoulli's equation when pressure/energy data is required.
- ✅ Always state the **assumptions** (steady, incompressible, 1-D, inviscid if needed) at the start of any KTU exam answer.
- ✅ Remember the **Divergence Theorem** (Gauss's Theorem) is used to convert surface integrals to volume integrals in derivations.
- ✅ For **branching pipes**: $Q_{\text{main}} = Q_{\text{branch 1}} + Q_{\text{branch 2}} + \dots$
- ✅ For **cylindrical coordinates**, the radial term has an extra $\frac{1}{r}$ factor due to the area element $dA = r\, dr\, d\theta$.
- ✅ **No-slip and no-penetration** at solid walls means $V_n = 0$ at boundaries.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity and Acceleration of Fluid Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Velocity and Acceleration of Fluid Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Velocity and Acceleration in Fluid Flow

In fluid mechanics, **velocity** is defined as the instantaneous rate of change of position of a fluid particle with respect to time, while **acceleration** is the instantaneous rate of change of velocity with respect to time. Since a fluid is a continuum with infinitely many particles in continuous motion, describing its kinematics requires either tracking individual particles (Lagrangian view) or recording field properties at fixed points in space (Eulerian view).

$$ \vec{V} = \frac{d\vec{r}}{dt} \quad \text{and} \quad \vec{a} = \frac{d\vec{V}}{dt} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU board expects students to clearly distinguish between the *Lagrangian* and *Eulerian* methods of describing flow, derive the *material (total) acceleration* expression, and state the difference between *streamlines, pathlines, and streaklines*. These are recurring 7-mark and 14-mark questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on the **Manhattan Bridge in New York City** watching the East River flow beneath you.

- **Eulerian approach** (used 99% of the time in engineering): You stand still with a measuring instrument at one fixed spot on the bridge and record the *river's speed and direction at that exact point* every second. You never follow any particular water molecule. You are measuring a **field**.
- **Lagrangian approach**: You jump into a small red rubber boat with a GPS tracker and let the current carry you. You record how *your boat's velocity changes* as you personally travel downstream. You are tracking an **individual particle**.

> [!NOTE]
> The Eulerian approach is mathematically far more convenient because fluids have billions of moving particles. Therefore, we describe a velocity **field** $\vec{V}(x, y, z, t)$ rather than tracking each particle.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Density of water** at 4 °C: **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
- **Dynamic viscosity of water** at 20 °C: **μ = 1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**
- **Kinematic viscosity of water** at 20 °C: **ν = 1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**
- **Standard gravitational acceleration**: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Standard atmospheric pressure**: **P_atm = 101.325 kPa**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Lagrangian Description of Flow

In the Lagrangian approach, we tag an individual fluid particle (originally at position $\vec{r}_0$ at time $t = 0$) and track its trajectory. Its position at any later instant $t$ is:

$$ \vec{r} = \vec{r}(\vec{r}_0, t) $$

The **Lagrangian velocity** of the particle is:

$$ \vec{V}_L = \frac{\partial \vec{r}}{\partial t}\bigg|_{\vec{r}_0} $$

The **Lagrangian acceleration** is:

$$ \vec{a}_L = \frac{\partial^2 \vec{r}}{\partial t^2}\bigg|_{\vec{r}_0} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}_L}{\partial t}\bigg|_{\vec{r}_0} $$

**Drawback:** Tracking every single particle is mathematically impractical for engineering systems. Hence this method is rarely used directly, except in molecular dynamics and some specialized particle-tracking simulations.

### 2.2 Eulerian Description of Flow

In the Eulerian approach, attention is fixed on a **point in space** through which different fluid particles pass with time. The flow field is expressed as:

$$ \vec{V} = \vec{V}(x, y, z, t) = u\,\hat{i} + v\,\hat{j} + w\,\hat{k} $$

where $u, v, w$ are the velocity components along the $x, y, z$ axes respectively. This is the **velocity field**.

### 2.3 Material (Total) Derivative — The Heart of KTU Module 2

This is the **most important derivation in this module** for KTU 2024. When we differentiate an Eulerian field variable to get the rate of change **following a fluid particle**, we must account for the fact that as the particle moves, both time and position change simultaneously.

The **material derivative** (also called the *substantial* or *total derivative*) is defined as:

$$ \frac{D}{Dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla) $$

> [!NOTE]
> The operator $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)$ is read as "V-del" or "V-grad". It is a **scalar differential operator** that takes a vector or scalar field and returns a directional derivative along the flow direction.

Applying the material derivative to velocity gives the **total acceleration of a fluid particle**:

$$ \boxed{\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \underbrace{\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}}_{\text{Local acceleration}} + \underbrace{(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}}_{\text{Convective acceleration}}} $$

### 2.4 Physical Meaning of Each Acceleration Term

- **Local acceleration** $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}$: This is the **temporal change** of velocity at a *fixed point* in space. It is non-zero only in **unsteady flow**. Example: the accelerating nozzle of a pressure washer, water hammering in a pipe when a valve is suddenly closed.

- **Convective acceleration** $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$: This is the **spatial change** of velocity as the fluid particle *travels* from one point to another. It is non-zero in **non-uniform flow**, even if the flow is steady. Example: flow through a converging pipe duct, water accelerating as it enters a narrow throat.

- **Total acceleration** $\dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt}$: The actual acceleration experienced by a fluid particle, equal to the sum of local and convective parts.

### 2.5 Streamline, Pathline and Streakline

These three concepts are tested every year in Part A (3-mark questions).

| Concept | Definition | Equation / Identification |
|---|---|---|
| **Streamline** | An *imaginary curve* whose tangent at every point is parallel to the instantaneous velocity vector at that point. | $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = \dfrac{dz}{w}$ |
| **Pathline** | The actual *trajectory* traced by a single fluid particle over a time interval. | Solution of $\vec{V} = d\vec{r}/dt$ |
| **Streakline** | The *locus* of all particles that have previously passed through a given fixed point in the flow field (e.g., dye injected continuously from a needle). | Visualized experimentally with dye injection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **steady flow**, streamlines, pathlines, and streaklines **coincide**. In **unsteady flow** they are *different* curves. This is a classic KTU 3-mark question.

### 2.6 Equation of a Streamline

A streamline in 2-D flow is obtained by solving:

$$ \frac{dx}{u(x, y, t)} = \frac{dy}{v(x, y, t)} $$

For steady flow, the streamline pattern does not change with time, and a family of streamlines can be drawn to form a **flow net**.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Eulerian velocity field | $\vec{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$ | m/s | Function of $x, y, z, t$ |
| 2 | Material derivative operator | $\frac{D}{Dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial}{\partial z}$ | s⁻¹ | Total derivative following a fluid particle |
| 3 | Total acceleration | $\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ | m/s² | Sum of local and convective parts |
| 4 | Local acceleration | $\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t}\hat{i} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial t}\hat{j} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial t}\hat{k}$ | m/s² | Non-zero only in unsteady flow |
| 5 | Convective acceleration | $u\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial z}$ | m/s² | Non-zero in non-uniform flow |
| 6 | $x$-component of convective acceleration | $u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}$ | m/s² | Scalar component |
| 7 | Streamline equation (3-D) | $\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w}$ | — | Slope is velocity direction |
| 8 | Steady flow condition | $\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} = 0$ | — | Local acceleration vanishes |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace**: Calculating acceleration terms is essential in the design of rocket nozzles and jet engine inlets, where flow is highly non-uniform and sometimes unsteady.
- **Hydraulic machinery**: In turbines and pumps, the convective acceleration through the impeller blades determines pressure rise and energy transfer.
- **Civil/Hydraulic engineering**: Flood-routing in rivers, surge analysis in penstocks, and dam-break wave propagation all rely on the material derivative form of the governing equations (Navier–Stokes).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Material (Total) Derivative

**Starting Point:** Consider a fluid particle whose position vector at time $t$ is $\vec{r}(t) = x(t)\hat{i} + y(t)\hat{j} + z(t)\hat{k}$. A generic fluid property (say temperature $T$ or velocity component $u$) is an Eulerian field: $T = T(x, y, z, t)$.

**Step 1:** The rate of change of $T$ **as seen by the moving fluid particle** is, by chain rule of multivariable calculus:

$$ \frac{DT}{Dt} = \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{\partial T}{\partial z}\frac{dz}{dt} $$

**Step 2:** Recognize that $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = u$, $\dfrac{dy}{dt} = v$, $\dfrac{dz}{dt} = w$, because the fluid particle is moving with the local flow velocity. Substituting:

$$ \frac{DT}{Dt} = \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial T}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial T}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial T}{\partial z} $$

**Step 3:** This can be written compactly as:

$$ \frac{DT}{Dt} = \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)T $$

**Step 4:** Apply the same operation to the velocity vector $\vec{V}$ itself to obtain the total acceleration:

$$ \vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} \quad \blacksquare $$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Component Form of Total Acceleration

Expanding $\vec{a} = a_x\hat{i} + a_y\hat{j} + a_z\hat{k}$, the $x$-component is:

$$ a_x = \frac{Du}{Dt} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} $$

Similarly for the $y$ and $z$ components:

$$ a_y = \frac{Dv}{Dt} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial v}{\partial z} $$

$$ a_z = \frac{Dw}{Dt} = \frac{\partial w}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial w}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial w}{\partial z} $$

These three equations constitute the **kinematic part of the Euler equations** of motion (before applying Newton's second law).

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Streamline Equation

**Starting Point:** By definition, the tangent vector to a streamline at point $(x, y, z)$ is parallel to the local velocity vector $\vec{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$.

**Step 1:** A differential element $d\vec{r} = dx\,\hat{i} + dy\,\hat{j} + dz\,\hat{k}$ along the streamline is parallel to $\vec{V}$, so:

$$ \frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w} \quad \blacksquare $$

This is the differential equation that must be integrated (with the time held constant) to obtain the streamline pattern.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Given:** The velocity field of a 2-D flow is given by:

$$ u = x^2 + y^2, \quad v = 2xy \quad \text{(units in m/s)} $$

**Find:** (i) The total acceleration of a fluid particle at the point $(1, 1)$ m, and (ii) the streamline passing through $(1, 1)$ at $t = 0$.

**Solution (i) — Total Acceleration:**

Since the velocity field is **independent of time** (steady), $\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = 0$. So the local acceleration is **zero** and only convective acceleration contributes.

Compute the partial derivatives:

$$ \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 2x, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 2y, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 2y, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 2x $$

Evaluate at the point $(1, 1)$:

$$ u(1,1) = 1^2 + 1^2 = 2 \text{ m/s}, \quad v(1,1) = 2(1)(1) = 2 \text{ m/s} $$

$$ \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\bigg|_{(1,1)} = 2, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\bigg|_{(1,1)} = 2, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\bigg|_{(1,1)} = 2, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}\bigg|_{(1,1)} = 2 $$

Now the convective acceleration components:

$$ a_x = u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = (2)(2) + (2)(2) = 8 \text{ m/s}^2 $$

$$ a_y = u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = (2)(2) + (2)(2) = 8 \text{ m/s}^2 $$

Therefore, the magnitude of total acceleration is:

$$ \vert \vec{a} \vert = \sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2} = \sqrt{8^2 + 8^2} = 8\sqrt{2} \approx 11.31 \text{ m/s}^2 $$

**Solution (ii) — Streamline Equation:**

Using $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v}$:

$$ \frac{dx}{x^2 + y^2} = \frac{dy}{2xy} $$

Cross-multiplying:

$$ 2xy\,dx = (x^2 + y^2)\,dy $$

$$ 2xy\,dx - x^2\,dy - y^2\,dy = 0 $$

$$ -(x^2\,dy - 2xy\,dx + y^2\,dy) = 0 $$

$$ -d(x^2 y) + d(y^3) \text{ after rearrangement} \ldots $$

Integrating along the streamline through $(1, 1)$:

$$ \boxed{\,y^2 - x^2 y = 0 \implies y(y - x^2) = 0\,} $$

Discarding the trivial $y = 0$ line, the streamline family is **$y = x^2$**. At $(1, 1)$: $1 = 1^2$ ✓.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Flow Type | $\partial \vec{V} / \partial t$ | $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ | Total Acceleration |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steady, uniform | 0 | 0 | **0** |
| Steady, non-uniform | 0 | ≠ 0 | $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ |
| Unsteady, uniform | ≠ 0 | 0 | $\partial \vec{V} / \partial t$ |
| Unsteady, non-uniform | ≠ 0 | ≠ 0 | Sum of both |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Methods of Describing Fluid Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[FLUID FLOW DESCRIPTION METHODS] --> B[LAGRANGIAN APPROACH]
    A --> C[EULERIAN APPROACH]
    B --> D[Tracks individual fluid particles]
    B --> E[Position vector r as function of r0 and time t]
    B --> F[Velocity VL = partial derivative of r wrt t]
    B --> G[Mathematically complex for practical use]
    C --> H[Studies flow at fixed points in space]
    C --> I[Velocity field V of x y z and t]
    C --> J[Standard engineering approach]
    C --> K[Uses material derivative D by Dt]
    K --> L[Total acceleration D V by Dt]
    L --> M[Local acceleration partial V by partial t]
    L --> N[Convective acceleration V dot grad V]
    M --> O[Time based change at fixed point]
    M --> P[Non zero only in unsteady flow]
    N --> Q[Spatial change as fluid moves]
    N --> R[Non zero in non uniform flow]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Streamline vs Pathline vs Streakline

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[FLUID FLOW VISUALIZATION CONCEPTS] --> B[STREAMLINE]
    A --> C[PATHLINE]
    A --> D[STREAKLINE]
    B --> E[Instantaneous curve tangent to velocity]
    B --> F[Equation dx by u equals dy by v equals dz by w]
    C --> G[Trajectory of a single fluid particle over time]
    C --> H[Found by integrating dr by dt equals V]
    D --> I[Locus of all particles passing through a fixed point]
    D --> J[Visualized with continuous dye injection]
    A --> K[STEADY FLOW SPECIAL CASE]

```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Acceleration Decomposition Engine

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Velocity Field V x y z t] --> B{Material Derivative Operator}
    B --> C[Time Partial Derivative Branch]
    B --> D[Spatial Convective Branch]
    C --> E[Local Acceleration partial V by partial t]
    D --> F[Compute V dot grad]
    F --> G[Convective Acceleration V dot grad V]
    E --> H[Vector Summation Block]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Output Total Acceleration a equals DV by Dt]
    I --> J{Application Layer}
    J --> K[Steady Uniform Flow a equals 0]
    J --> L[Steady Non Uniform Convective only]
    J --> M[Unsteady Uniform Local only]
    J --> N[Unsteady Non Uniform Both terms]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Distinguish between Lagrangian and Eulerian methods of describing fluid flow. Which one is more commonly used in fluid mechanics and why?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Lagrangian method** follows individual fluid particles and traces their trajectories; position is expressed as $\vec{r} = \vec{r}(\vec{r}_0, t)$, where $\vec{r}_0$ is the initial position tag. The **Eulerian method** observes the flow at fixed points in space and expresses properties as fields, $\vec{V} = \vec{V}(x, y, z, t)$.
The **Eulerian method** is preferred in fluid mechanics because tracking every particle in a continuum is practically impossible, and field quantities at fixed spatial locations are directly measurable using instruments like Pitot tubes, hot-wire anemometers, and PIV systems. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**State the condition under which streamline, pathline and streakline coincide. Differentiate between a uniform flow and a non-uniform flow.**

**Model Answer:**
Streamlines, pathlines, and streaklines **coincide in steady flow**, i.e., when $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$. Under unsteady conditions, the three are distinct curves.
- **Uniform flow**: velocity at a given instant is constant in both magnitude and direction along the flow direction (e.g., flow in a long straight pipe of constant diameter, far from entry effects).
- **Non-uniform flow**: velocity changes with position along the flow direction (e.g., flow in a converging nozzle, flow near the entrance region of a pipe). **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **total acceleration of a fluid particle** in a 3-D Eulerian velocity field, clearly identifying the local and convective acceleration components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the given velocity field, $u = 3x + y^2$, $v = 2x - 3y$, $w = 0$, find the magnitude of the **total acceleration** of a fluid particle at the point $(2, 1)$. Is the flow steady or unsteady? Justify. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Total Acceleration

Let the Eulerian velocity field be $\vec{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$, where $u, v, w$ are functions of $(x, y, z, t)$.

The **total derivative** of $u$ following a fluid particle is found by applying the chain rule, accounting for the fact that as the particle moves, both time and the spatial coordinates change:

$$ \frac{Du}{Dt} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\frac{dz}{dt} $$

Since the fluid particle moves with the local flow velocity:

$$ \frac{dx}{dt} = u, \quad \frac{dy}{dt} = v, \quad \frac{dz}{dt} = w $$

Substituting:

$$ a_x = \frac{Du}{Dt} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} $$

By similar expansion, the $y$ and $z$ components are:

$$ a_y = \frac{Dv}{Dt} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial v}{\partial z} $$

$$ a_z = \frac{Dw}{Dt} = \frac{\partial w}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial w}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial w}{\partial z} $$

In compact vector form:

$$ \boxed{\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \underbrace{\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}}_{\text{Local}} + \underbrace{(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}}_{\text{Convective}}} \quad \blacksquare $$

The **local acceleration** $\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}$ is the change of velocity at a fixed spatial point with time, and is **non-zero only in unsteady flow**. The **convective acceleration** $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ arises because the fluid particle moves to a new location where the velocity is different, and is **non-zero in non-uniform flow**, even if steady.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Evaluation

Given: $u = 3x + y^2$, $v = 2x - 3y$, $w = 0$.

**Step 1 — Check steadiness:** None of $u, v, w$ depend explicitly on $t$, so $\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = 0$. The flow is **steady**. Therefore local acceleration is **zero**.

**Step 2 — Required partial derivatives:**

$$ \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 3, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 2y, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} = 0 $$

$$ \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 2, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = -3, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial z} = 0 $$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $u, v$ at point $(2, 1)$:**

$$ u(2,1) = 3(2) + (1)^2 = 7 \text{ m/s} $$

$$ v(2,1) = 2(2) - 3(1) = 1 \text{ m/s} $$

**Step 4 — Evaluate partial derivatives at $(2, 1)$:**

$$ \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\bigg|_{(2,1)} = 3, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\bigg|_{(2,1)} = 2, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\bigg|_{(2,1)} = 2, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}\bigg|_{(2,1)} = -3 $$

**Step 5 — Compute convective acceleration components:**

$$ a_x = u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} = (7)(3) + (1)(2) + 0 = 23 \text{ m/s}^2 $$

$$ a_y = u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial v}{\partial z} = (7)(2) + (1)(-3) + 0 = 11 \text{ m/s}^2 $$

$$ a_z = 0 \text{ m/s}^2 \quad (\text{since } w = 0 \text{ everywhere}) $$

**Step 6 — Magnitude:**

$$ \vert \vec{a} \vert = \sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2} = \sqrt{23^2 + 11^2 + 0^2} = \sqrt{529 + 121} = \sqrt{650} \approx 25.5 \text{ m/s}^2 $$

**Final Answer:** $|\vec{a}| \approx 25.5 \text{ m/s}^2$, flow is **steady**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the difference between **streamlines, pathlines, and streaklines**. Under what flow condition do all three coincide? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The velocity field of a flow is given by $u = 2x$, $v = -2y$, $w = 0$. Find the **equation of the streamline** passing through the point $(2, 4)$ at $t = 0$. Is the flow rotational or irrotational? Show the calculation. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Streamlines, Pathlines, Streaklines

A **streamline** is an imaginary curve in the flow field such that its tangent at every point is parallel to the instantaneous velocity vector at that point. It gives a *snapshot* of the flow direction at a particular instant. Two streamlines can never cross, since at the crossing point the velocity would have two directions simultaneously.

A **pathline** is the actual trajectory traced by a single fluid particle over a finite period of time. It is the *historical record* of where one specific particle has been.

A **streakline** is the locus of all fluid particles that have previously passed through a given fixed point in the flow field. It is what you see as a *continuous dye streak* in a flow-visualization experiment (e.g., smoke in a wind tunnel).

**Steady flow coincidence:** When $\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} = 0$, the flow pattern does not change with time, so the streamline, the pathline of any particle, and the streakline produced by continuous injection at any point **all coincide**. In **unsteady flow**, they are three distinct curves.

#### Part (b) — Streamline Equation and Rotationality

Given: $u = 2x$, $v = -2y$, $w = 0$.

**Step 1 — Streamline equation** uses $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v}$:

$$ \frac{dx}{2x} = \frac{dy}{-2y} $$

**Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate:**

$$ \frac{dx}{x} = -\frac{dy}{y} $$

$$ \int \frac{dx}{x} = -\int \frac{dy}{y} $$

$$ \ln x = -\ln y + C_1 $$

$$ \ln x + \ln y = C_1 $$

$$ \ln(xy) = C_1 $$

$$ xy = C \quad \text{(where } C = e^{C_1}\text{)} $$

**Step 3 — Apply initial point $(2, 4)$:**

$$ (2)(4) = C \implies C = 8 $$

**Streamline equation:**

$$ \boxed{\,xy = 8\,} \quad \text{(rectangular hyperbola)} $$

**Step 4 — Check rotationality.** The $z$-component of vorticity (curl of velocity) is:

$$ \omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} $$

Compute the partial derivatives:

$$ \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial(-2y)}{\partial x} = 0 $$

$$ \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial(2x)}{\partial y} = 0 $$

$$ \omega_z = 0 - 0 = 0 $$

Since vorticity is zero everywhere, the flow is **irrotational**. **(7 marks)**

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the local acceleration term**: Even if the problem statement gives a "steady" velocity field, students sometimes write only the convective term — but they must *verify* the field is steady and *state* this conclusion explicitly in the answer. **(−2 marks)**
> 2. **Conflating streamline with pathline**: In Part A, students often write definitions that are partial or swapped. Streamline = instantaneous direction field; Pathline = actual particle trajectory; Streakline = locus of dye. Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Sign errors in convective acceleration**: In the convective term, the partial derivative is taken with respect to the *spatial* coordinate, not the velocity component index. A common slip is writing $a_x = u \partial u/\partial u$, which is meaningless. Always remember: $a_x = u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}$.
> 4. **Streamline equation sign**: When separating $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v}$, students often drop the negative sign during integration, leading to $xy = $ constant vs. $x/y = $ constant. Re-derive carefully.
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical answers**: KTU examiners are strict. Every final numerical answer must carry units (m/s² for acceleration, m/s for velocity).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Lagrangian vs Eulerian**: Lagrangian tags and tracks individual particles; Eulerian observes field properties at fixed spatial points. **Eulerian is the standard engineering method.**
- ✅ **Material derivative**: $\frac{D}{Dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)$ is the rate of change **following a fluid particle**.
- ✅ **Total acceleration = Local + Convective**:
  $\vec{a} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$
- ✅ **Local acceleration** is non-zero only in **unsteady flow** (e.g., valve closure, pump start-up).
- ✅ **Convective acceleration** is non-zero only in **non-uniform flow** (e.g., nozzle, diffuser, bends).
- ✅ **Streamline equation (3-D)**: $\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w}$
- ✅ **In steady flow**: streamlines, pathlines, and streaklines **coincide**.
- ✅ **In unsteady flow**: the three are **distinct**.
- ✅ **Vorticity / Curl**: $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$. If $\vec{\omega} = 0$ everywhere, the flow is **irrotational**.
- ✅ **Standard physical constants to memorize**: $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $P_{\text{atm}} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$.
- ✅ Always check **steadiness** first when computing acceleration — if steady, the local term vanishes and only convective acceleration remains.
- ✅ Always carry **units** in numerical answers.
- ✅ For streamline integration, treat time $t$ as a parameter; the integration is over space only.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Euler's Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Euler's Equation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Euler's Equation of Motion** is the fundamental differential equation governing the motion of an *inviscid* (frictionless) and *incompressible* fluid along a streamline. It is derived by applying **Newton's Second Law of Motion** to a small fluid element moving along a curved path, and balances the pressure, gravity, and inertial (acceleration) forces acting on that element. Mathematically, in its most widely taught differential form along a streamline: $$\frac{\partial p}{\rho} + g \, dz + V \, dV = 0$$ Where the pressure gradient and gravity terms are balanced against the acceleration of the fluid particle. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Euler's equation serves as the *parent equation* from which **Bernoulli's Equation** (the most frequently examined topic in Fluid Mechanics) is derived by integration. Mastery of Euler's equation is therefore non-negotiable for KTU examinations. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **small marble sliding inside a curved, frictionless transparent tube**: - The tube represents a **streamline** of fluid flow. - The marble's weight pulls it **downward** (gravity force, $g$). - The tube walls push the marble **perpendicular** to its motion (pressure force, $p$). - The marble's **speed changes** as it moves up and down hills (kinetic/inertial term, $V \, dV$). Just as the marble's motion is governed by the balance of weight, normal push, and change in speed, **Euler's equation governs how a fluid particle's pressure, elevation, and velocity must be balanced** as it travels along a streamline. If the fluid rises, it must either speed up or drop in pressure to keep the energy "ledger" balanced. ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants | Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit | |--------|----------|----------------------| | $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** | | $\rho$ | Mass density of fluid | **kg/m³** (e.g., water ≈ **1000 kg/m³**) | | $p$ | Pressure | **Pa (N/m²)** or **kN/m²** | | $V$ | Velocity of fluid particle | **m/s** | | $z$ | Elevation head above datum | **m** | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Logical Breakdown — How Euler's Equation Works The derivation rests on a single physical principle: **For any fluid particle, the net force acting on it equals its mass times its acceleration** (Newton's Second Law). The reasoning chain is: - **Step 1 — Choose a control volume:** Take an infinitesimally small fluid element of cross-section $dA$ and length $ds$ along a streamline. - **Step 2 — Identify the forces:** Two surface forces (pressure on the two end faces) and one body force (gravity acting on the mass of the element). The element is assumed **inviscid**, so no shear stress exists. - **Step 3 — Resolve along the streamline:** Project the gravity vector along the streamline direction using the angle $\theta$ between the streamline tangent and the horizontal. - **Step 4 — Apply Newton's Second Law:** Net force in the streamline direction = mass × acceleration in that direction. - **Step 5 — Simplify:** Divide by mass, take the limit as the element shrinks to a point, and obtain the differential equation. - **Step 6 — Integrate:** (Not done in Euler's equation itself) The integration step *separately* yields **Bernoulli's Equation**. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" behind each term:** > - $g \, dz$ → Work done **against** gravity as the fluid rises by $dz$. > - $\frac{dp}{\rho}$ → Work done **by** the pressure difference as the fluid moves. > - $V \, dV$ → Change in the **kinetic energy** per unit mass of the fluid particle. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Typical Use | |---|---------|-------------|-------------| | 1 | $\dfrac{dp}{\rho} + g\,dz + V\,dV = 0$ | Euler's equation along a streamline (scalar) | Direct exam derivation | | 2 | $\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \rho\left(g_x - a_x\right)$ | Euler's equation in $x$-direction | Vector form proofs | | 3 | $\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial y} = \rho\left(g_y - a_y\right)$ | Euler's equation in $y$-direction | Vector form proofs | | 4 | $\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial z} = \rho\left(g_z - a_z\right)$ | Euler's equation in $z$-direction | Hydrostatics (when $a = 0$) | | 5 | $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} = -\dfrac{1}{\rho}\nabla p + \vec{g}$ | **Vector form** of Euler's equation | Advanced KTU problems | | 6 | $\dfrac{p}{\rho g} + z + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} = \text{constant}$ | **Bernoulli's equation** (integrated form) | Direct application problems | > [!WARNING] > When writing Euler's equation, **do not confuse** $\frac{dp}{\rho}$ with $dp/\rho g$. The form $dp/\rho$ gives units of $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$ (specific energy), while dividing by $g$ gives metres (head). The *Bernoulli head form* comes **after** dividing by $g$. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Euler's equation (and its integrated Bernoulli form) is the workhorse behind: - **Venturi meters and orifice meters** — flow measurement in pipelines. - **Pitot-static tubes** — aircraft airspeed measurement. - **Aerofoil lift** — generation of lift force on aircraft wings. - **Hydraulic machinery** — governing equations for turbines and pumps. - **Open channel flow analysis** — energy grade line and hydraulic grade line computations. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Euler's Equation Along a Streamline ### 3.1.1 Setup — Defining the Fluid Element Consider a small fluid element lying along a **curved streamline** $AB$ in a flow field. Let: - $s$ = direction along the streamline, - $n$ = direction normal to the streamline, - $z$ = vertical elevation, - $\theta$ = angle the streamline makes with the **horizontal**. The element is taken to be a tiny cylindrical/slab shape with: - Cross-sectional area $= dA$ (constant), - Length along streamline $= ds$, - Mass $= \rho \cdot dA \cdot ds$. > The element is **inviscid** (no viscosity, no shear) and **incompressible** (constant $\rho$). ### 3.1.2 Free-Body Diagram — Forces Acting | Force | Origin | Magnitude | Direction | |-------|--------|-----------|-----------| | $F_1$ | Pressure on left face (at $A$) | $p \cdot dA$ | Along $+s$ (pushes forward) | | $F_2$ | Pressure on right face (at $B$) | $\left(p + \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial s}ds\right)dA$ | Along $-s$ (pushes backward) | | $W$ | Weight of element | $\rho \, dA \, ds \cdot g$ | Vertically downward | | $W_s$ | Component of $W$ along streamline | $\rho \, dA \, ds \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | Along $-s$ (if stream is going up) | The relationship between the streamline tilt and the elevation is: $$\sin\theta = \frac{dz}{ds}$$ So the gravity component along the streamline is: $$W_s = \rho \, dA \, ds \, g \, \frac{dz}{ds} = \rho \, g \, dA \, dz$$ ### 3.1.3 Applying Newton's Second Law (in the $s$-direction) Net force along streamline = mass × acceleration along streamline. $$F_1 - F_2 - W_s = (\rho \, dA \, ds) \cdot a_s$$ Substituting the expressions for each force: $$p \, dA - \left(p + \frac{\partial p}{\partial s}ds\right)dA - \rho \, g \, dA \, dz = \rho \, dA \, ds \cdot a_s$$ The $p \, dA$ terms cancel, and dividing throughout by $dA$: $$-\frac{\partial p}{\partial s}ds - \rho \, g \, dz = \rho \, ds \cdot a_s$$ ### 3.1.4 Substituting the Acceleration Term The acceleration of a fluid particle moving along a streamline is purely a function of its position $s$ and time $t$. The total (material) derivative of velocity along the streamline is: $$a_s = \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{\partial V}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}\frac{ds}{dt} = \frac{\partial V}{\partial t} + V\frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$$ For the case most commonly asked in KTU (steady flow, $\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} = 0$): $$a_s = V \, \frac{dV}{ds}$$ Substituting back: $$-\frac{\partial p}{\partial s}ds - \rho \, g \, dz = \rho \, ds \cdot V \, \frac{dV}{ds}$$ Dividing throughout by $\rho \, ds$: $$-\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} - g \, \frac{dz}{ds} = V \frac{dV}{ds}$$ Multiplying through by $-1$: $$\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} + g \, \frac{dz}{ds} + V \frac{dV}{ds} = 0$$ ### 3.1.5 The Final Compact Form Recognising that the partial derivatives along the streamline are simply total differentials in the streamline direction, and multiplying through by $ds$: $$\boxed{\;\frac{dp}{\rho} + g \, dz + V \, dV = 0\;}$$ This is **Euler's Equation of Motion Along a Streamline** — the canonical KTU result. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Verification of Each Term | Term | Physical Meaning | Sign Convention | Units | |------|------------------|-----------------|-------| | $\dfrac{dp}{\rho}$ | Pressure work per unit mass | $+$ when $p$ rises in flow direction | $\text{J/kg} = \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$ | | $g \, dz$ | Gravitational potential change per unit mass | $+$ when $z$ rises | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$ | | $V \, dV$ | Kinetic energy change per unit mass | $+$ when $V$ rises in flow direction | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$ | | $\sum = 0$ | Energy balance (no friction, no pump/turbine) | Always zero for inviscid flow | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$ | > [!NOTE] > The "**zero**" on the right-hand side is the *signature* of inviscid flow. If the fluid had viscosity, a friction term $h_L$ would appear on the right-hand side after integration (this is the **modified Bernoulli / Energy Equation with losses**). ## 3.3 Quick Path From Euler's to Bernoulli's Equation For *steady, incompressible, inviscid* flow, integrate Euler's equation between points 1 and 2 along a streamline: $$\int_1^2 \frac{dp}{\rho} + g\int_1^2 dz + \int_1^2 V \, dV = 0$$ $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + z_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + z_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$ This is **Bernoulli's Equation** — the direct KTU exam favourite, worth understanding as a corollary of Euler's equation. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Free-Body of Fluid Element on Streamline The following block-level schematic maps every force, coordinate, and direction involved in deriving Euler's equation. This serves as the canonical visual reference for the KTU derivation. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Fluid Element on Streamline AB
Cross section dA, Length ds, Mass rho dA ds"] --> B["Identify Forces Acting"] B --> C1["Pressure Force F1 = p dA
Acts in +s direction at point A"] B --> C2["Pressure Force F2 = p + dp dA
Acts in -s direction at point B"] B --> C3["Weight W = rho g dA ds
Acts vertically downward"] C3 --> C4["Resolve along streamline
Ws = rho g dz dA in -s direction"] C1 --> D["Apply Newton Second Law
F1 minus F2 minus Ws equals mass times a"] C2 --> D C4 --> D D --> E["Substitute a equals V dV over ds
for steady incompressible flow"] E --> F["Algebraic Simplification
Cancel p dA and dA terms"] F --> G["Divide by rho ds
Get dp over ds plus g dz over ds plus V dV over ds equals 0"] G --> H["Multiply by ds
Final Euler Equation dp by rho plus g dz plus V dV equals 0"] H --> I["Integrate along Streamline
Between points 1 and 2"] I --> J["Obtain Bernoulli Equation
p by rho g plus z plus V squared by 2g equals constant"] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Sign and Direction Convention Map ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Streamline tilted at angle theta
to horizontal"] --> B["Direction +s
Direction of flow"] A --> C["Direction +z
Vertically upward"] A --> D["Direction -n
Normal to streamline"] B --> E["Pressure force opposes motion
if pressure rises along s"] C --> F["Gravity opposes motion
if stream rises dz greater than 0"] B --> G["Kinetic term V dV opposes motion
if fluid decelerates"] E --> H["Sum of three signed terms equals 0
Euler equilibrium condition"] F --> H G --> H ``` ## 4.3 Engineering Functional Flow (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Inviscid Flow Field
with curved streamlines"] --> B["Euler Equation
dp by rho plus g dz plus V dV equals 0"] B --> C1["Integrate along streamline
Steady incompressible case"] B --> C2["Apply to static fluid
V equals 0, a equals 0"] C1 --> D1["Bernoulli Equation
Used in venturi, pitot, aerofoil"] C2 --> D2["Hydrostatic Equation
p plus rho g z equals constant"] D1 --> E["KTU Application Problems
Flow measurement, machinery"] D2 --> F["KTU Application Problems
Manometry, tank pressure"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Block Architecture Logic:** Euler's equation is the *single source* of the two most-tested sub-topics in Module 2 — *Bernoulli's equation* (dynamic flow) and the *hydrostatic pressure equation* (static flow). Memorising this three-level hierarchy (Euler → Bernoulli / Hydrostatics → Applications) prevents confusion in the exam hall. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **State Euler's equation of motion for a fluid along a streamline and list the assumptions used in its derivation.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Statement (2 marks):** For a steady, incompressible, inviscid fluid flow along a streamline, Euler's equation of motion in differential form is: > > $$\frac{dp}{\rho} + g\,dz + V\,dV = 0$$ > > where $p$ = pressure, $\rho$ = density, $g$ = acceleration due to gravity, $z$ = elevation, and $V$ = velocity of the fluid particle along the streamline. > **Assumptions (1 mark):** > 1. The fluid is **inviscid** (frictionless, $\mu = 0$). > 2. The fluid is **incompressible** ($\rho = \text{constant}$). > 3. The flow is **steady** ($\partial V / \partial t = 0$). > 4. The equation is applied along a **single streamline** (irrotational or rotational flow along $s$). --- ### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **How is Bernoulli's equation obtained from Euler's equation? Mention the conditions of its applicability.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Derivation outline (2 marks):** Bernoulli's equation is obtained by integrating Euler's equation along a streamline between any two points 1 and 2: > > $$\int_1^2 \frac{dp}{\rho} + g\int_1^2 dz + \int_1^2 V\,dV = 0$$ > > For constant $\rho$, this yields: > > $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + z_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + z_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$ > **Conditions of applicability (1 mark):** > 1. Steady, incompressible flow. > 2. Inviscid fluid (no friction losses). > 3. Flow along a single streamline. > 4. No addition of work by pumps or extraction by turbines in the control region. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern ### Question A. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks **Derive Euler's equation of motion in differential form for an ideal fluid flow along a streamline. Hence, obtain Bernoulli's equation as a special case.** **Sub-part (a) — Derive Euler's equation [7 marks]** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider a small fluid element of cross-section $dA$ and length $ds$ lying along a curved streamline $AB$. Mass of element $= \rho \, dA \, ds$. **Step 2 (1 mark):** Let the streamline make an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal. Pressure force at the upstream face $A$ is $p \cdot dA$ in the $+s$ direction. **Step 3 (1 mark):** Pressure force at the downstream face $B$ is $\left(p + \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial s}ds\right) dA$ in the $-s$ direction. **Step 4 (1 mark):** The weight $W = \rho g \, dA \, ds$ acts vertically down. Its component along $-s$ is: $$W_s = \rho g \, dA \, ds \cdot \sin\theta = \rho g \, dA \, dz$$ since $\sin\theta = dz/ds$. **Step 5 (1 mark):** Applying Newton's Second Law along the streamline: $$p\,dA - \left(p + \frac{\partial p}{\partial s}ds\right)dA - \rho g \, dA \, dz = \rho \, dA \, ds \cdot a_s$$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** For steady flow, $a_s = V \dfrac{dV}{ds}$. Substituting and simplifying: $$-\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} - g\frac{dz}{ds} = V\frac{dV}{ds}$$ **Step 7 (1 mark):** Multiplying throughout by $ds$: $$\boxed{\frac{dp}{\rho} + g\,dz + V\,dV = 0}$$ This is **Euler's equation of motion along a streamline**. --- **Sub-part (b) — Obtain Bernoulli's equation [7 marks]** **Step 1 (1 mark):** The equation to integrate is: $$\frac{dp}{\rho} + g\,dz + V\,dV = 0$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Integrating between two arbitrary points 1 and 2 on the same streamline: $$\int_1^2 \frac{dp}{\rho} + g\int_1^2 dz + \int_1^2 V\,dV = 0$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** With $\rho = \text{constant}$: $$\frac{p}{\rho}\bigg|_1^2 + g(z_2 - z_1) + \frac{V^2}{2}\bigg|_1^2 = 0$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Writing the limits explicitly: $$\frac{p_2 - p_1}{\rho} + g(z_2 - z_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} = 0$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Rearranging: $$\frac{p_1}{\rho} + g z_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} = \frac{p_2}{\rho} + g z_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2}$$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** Dividing throughout by $g$ to express in head form: $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + z_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + z_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$ **Step 7 (1 mark):** Hence, for an ideal fluid, the sum of pressure head, elevation head, and velocity head is constant along a streamline. This is **Bernoulli's Equation**. --- ### Question B. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` — 14 Marks **(a) Starting from Newton's second law, derive the vector form of Euler's equation of motion for an ideal fluid. [7 marks]** **(b) For a fluid at rest, simplify Euler's equation to obtain the hydrostatic pressure variation and show that pressure is independent of horizontal coordinates. [7 marks]** **Sub-part (a) — Vector form of Euler's equation [7 marks]** **Step 1 (1 mark):** For a fluid particle, Newton's Second Law states: $$\sum \vec{F} = m \vec{a}$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** The only body force per unit volume in most problems is gravity: $\rho \vec{g}$. The only surface force per unit volume in an inviscid fluid is the **pressure gradient force**: $-\nabla p$. **Step 3 (1 mark):** The equation of motion per unit volume becomes: $$\rho \vec{a} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Acceleration of a fluid particle (material derivative of velocity): $$\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Substituting back: $$\rho\left[\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}\right] = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}$$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** Dividing by $\rho$: $$\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\nabla p + \vec{g}$$ **Step 7 (1 mark):** This is the **vector form of Euler's equation of motion** for an inviscid, compressible (or incompressible) fluid. --- **Sub-part (b) — Hydrostatic simplification [7 marks]** **Step 1 (1 mark):** For a fluid at rest, $\vec{V} = 0$ and $\vec{a} = 0$. Euler's vector equation reduces to: $$0 = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nabla p = \rho \vec{g}$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Writing the gravity vector in Cartesian form $\vec{g} = (0, 0, -g)$ (z positive upward): $$\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\hat{i} + \frac{\partial p}{\partial y}\hat{j} + \frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\hat{k} = \rho(0, 0, -g)$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Equating components: $$\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial p}{\partial y} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial p}{\partial z} = -\rho g$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** The first two equations show that $p$ is **independent of $x$ and $y$** — pressure depends only on depth $z$. **Step 5 (1 mark):** Integrating the third equation with constant $\rho$: $$p = -\rho g z + C$$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** Applying the boundary condition $p = p_0$ at $z = 0$ (free surface): $$p = p_0 - \rho g z$$ **Step 7 (1 mark):** Hence, pressure **increases linearly with depth** below the free surface, and is constant on any horizontal plane — the **hydrostatic pressure law**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Sign errors on $g\,dz$:** The term $g\,dz$ carries a *positive* sign in Euler's equation (energy balance form). Forgetting the sign and writing $-g\,dz$ is the single most common mistake — *deduct 1 to 2 marks* instantly. > 2. **Forgetting $\rho$ in the denominator:** Euler's equation reads $\dfrac{dp}{\rho}$, not $dp$. Mixing the two is an instant 1-mark penalty. > 3. **Conflating streamline and Eulerian forms:** KTU examiners specifically ask for the form *along a streamline*. Writing the vector form $(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V} = \ldots$ without stating "along streamline" loses 1 mark. > 4. **Skipping the "assumptions" sub-question:** In a 7-mark derivation, examiners *expect* a one-line statement of assumptions (inviscid, steady, incompressible). Skipping this costs up to **2 marks**. > 5. **Missing the integration limits:** When going from Euler to Bernoulli, always write $\int_1^2$ explicitly. Writing $\int$ without limits loses 1 mark. > 6. **Unit inconsistency:** Pressure given in kPa and elevation in mm — convert to SI (Pa, m) before substituting. Mixing units is a 1-mark deduction. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Every Exam!** - ✅ **Euler's equation (streamline form):** $\dfrac{dp}{\rho} + g\,dz + V\,dV = 0$ - ✅ **Vector form:** $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} = -\dfrac{1}{\rho}\nabla p + \vec{g}$ - ✅ **Hydrostatic form:** $\dfrac{dp}{dz} = -\rho g$ (derived when $\vec{V} = 0$) - ✅ **Bernoulli's equation:** $\dfrac{p_1}{\rho g} + z_1 + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho g} + z_2 + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g}$ (integrated Euler along a streamline) - ✅ **Core assumptions (4):** Inviscid, incompressible, steady, along a streamline. - ✅ **Each term is energy per unit mass** (units $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$ or $\text{J/kg}$); divide by $g$ to get head form (m). - ✅ **Acceleration along streamline:** $a_s = V \dfrac{dV}{ds}$ (steady flow case). - ✅ **Streamline tilt relation:** $\sin\theta = \dfrac{dz}{ds}$ — required to resolve gravity. - ✅ **Newton's 2nd law link:** Euler's equation is *nothing but* Newton's 2nd law applied to a fluid element with viscous forces removed. - ✅ **Real-world uses:** Venturi meter, Pitot tube, aerofoil lift, free jet, orifice flow, hydraulic machinery. - ✅ **The "zero" on the RHS is sacred:** It is what makes Euler's equation the parent of *conservative* (energy-conserving) flow analysis. Any problem with friction, pump, or turbine uses a *modified* form. - ✅ **Common KTU exam trap:** "Derive Euler's equation for flow along a streamline" — always mention the **streamline restriction** in the final line; otherwise full marks are not awarded. ---

topics.title: Bernoulli's Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Bernoulli's Equation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Bernoulli's Equation** is the fundamental energy conservation equation for an ideal, steady, incompressible, and inviscid flow along a streamline. It establishes a direct mathematical relationship between the pressure energy, kinetic energy (velocity energy), and potential energy (datum/elevation energy) of a flowing fluid at any two points along the same streamline. Mathematically, for a steady, incompressible, frictionless (inviscid) flow along a streamline: $$\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{Constant}$$ Where: - $P/\rho g$ = Pressure head (m) - $V^2/2g$ = Velocity head / Kinetic energy head (m) - $z$ = Elevation head / Datum head (m) - $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid (kg/m³) - $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Bernoulli's equation is the cornerstone of fluid mechanics. It forms the theoretical foundation for **Venturi meters, Orifice meters, Pitot tubes, and free jet flow analysis**. Mastery of its derivation from Euler's equation is mandatory for Part B questions (14 marks). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive Picture Imagine you are standing on a hill and holding a water hose. As water flows **downhill** (elevation $z$ decreases), the water speeds up dramatically at the nozzle exit (velocity $V$ increases), and the pressure at the nozzle mouth drops (you can feel suction if you put your hand near the opening). **Three Energy Forms Working Like a Bank Account:** Think of fluid energy like money in a savings account: - **Elevation head ($z$):** Money saved as "height" — gravity's potential to do work. - **Pressure head ($P/\rho g$):** Money saved as "pressure cash" — the fluid's ability to push. - **Velocity head ($V^2/2g$):** Money saved as "motion cash" — the fluid's kinetic energy. **Bernoulli's Principle:** The *total* money in this account remains *constant*. If you withdraw from one form (e.g., elevation drops), the other forms must compensate (velocity and/or pressure rise). > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Validation:** Place two sheets of paper close together and blow air between them. They come *closer*, not apart. This is because the fast-moving air has *low pressure*, and the higher atmospheric pressure outside pushes them together — a direct demonstration of Bernoulli's principle in action. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value (SI Units) | |---|---|---| | Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | | Density of water (at 4°C) | $\rho_w$ | **1000 kg/m³** | | Specific weight of water | $\gamma_w = \rho g$ | **9810 N/m³** | | Density of air (at STP) | $\rho_{air}$ | **1.225 kg/m³** | | Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** | > [!WARNING] > KTU examiners frequently deduct marks when students forget to convert units consistently. Always express **P in Pa (N/m²)**, **ρ in kg/m³**, **V in m/s**, and **z in meters** to obtain consistent results. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions of Bernoulli's Equation For Bernoulli's equation to be valid in its pure form, the following assumptions **must** hold: 1. **Steady Flow:** Flow parameters (velocity, pressure, density) at any point do not change with time. 2. **Incompressible Flow:** Fluid density $\rho$ is constant throughout the flow field (valid for liquids at low Mach numbers). 3. **Inviscid (Frictionless) Flow:** Fluid viscosity $\mu = 0$; therefore no frictional head losses occur. 4. **Irrotational Flow (or along a streamline):** The equation applies along a single streamline, or throughout the flow field if the flow is also irrotational. 5. **No Energy Addition/Extraction:** No pump, turbine, compressor, or heat transfer exists between the two sections considered. 6. **Single Continuous Fluid:** The same fluid flows continuously between the two sections (no branching or mixing). --- ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic ### Step 1 — Newton's Second Law Foundation The derivation starts with **Euler's Equation of Motion**, which applies Newton's second law ($\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$) to an inviscid fluid particle moving along a streamline. ### Step 2 — Integration Along the Streamline Euler's equation is integrated along the streamline from point **1** to point **2**. The pressure gradient, inertial term, and gravity term combine to yield an exact differential. ### Step 3 — Three-Energy-Head Interpretation The resulting equation is divided by $\rho g$ to express each term as a **head** (energy per unit weight), giving us the three familiar heads. ### Step 4 — The Energy Grade Line (EGL) and Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL) - **Total Energy Line (TEL/EGL):** Plots $\left(\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z\right)$ vs. distance along the flow. - **Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL):** Plots $\left(\frac{P}{\rho g} + z\right)$ vs. distance along the flow. - The **vertical distance** between EGL and HGL at any section equals the **velocity head** $V^2/2g$. --- ## 2.3 Modified (Real-Fluid) Bernoulli Equation For practical engineering applications involving real, viscous fluids, we must add a **head loss term** $h_L$ and optional **pump head** $H_p$ / **turbine head** $H_t$: $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_p = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + H_t + h_L$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Modified Equation is what KTU examiners test in numerical problems.** Always use the modified form whenever friction losses or machines are involved. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Physical Meaning | SI Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = C$ | Bernoulli's equation (ideal flow) | m | | 2 | $P + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 + \rho g z = C$ | Bernoulli's equation (energy per unit volume) | Pa | | 3 | $\dfrac{P}{\rho} + \dfrac{V^2}{2} + gz = C$ | Bernoulli's equation (energy per unit mass) | J/kg | | 4 | $H_p = H_t + h_L + \left(\dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} + z_2 - z_1\right)$ | Energy balance across pump-turbine system | m | | 5 | $Q = A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | Continuity equation (incompressible flow) | m³/s | | 6 | $V = \sqrt{2gH}$ | Torricelli's law (free jet from orifice) | m/s | | 7 | $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2gH}$ | Actual discharge through orifice | m³/s | | 8 | $h_L = \dfrac{4 f L V^2}{2g D}$ | Darcy-Weisbach friction loss (pipes) | m | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace:** Aircraft wing lift generation relies on the pressure differential caused by higher airflow velocity over the curved upper surface. - **Civil Engineering:** Design of spillways, weirs, Venturi flumes, and storm water systems. - **Mechanical Engineering:** Carburetors, atomizers, flow measurement in pipelines. - **Chemical Engineering:** Mixing tanks, ejectors, and process flow instrumentation. - **Hydraulic Machines:** Foundation for analysing pumps, turbines, and draft tubes. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation from Euler's Equation ### Step 1 — Set Up Euler's Equation of Motion Consider a steady, inviscid, incompressible flow.
