For a fluid particle moving along a streamline, **Euler's Equation** is: $$\rho \frac{DV}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \rho \vec{g}$$ For motion along a streamline (using 1-D form with $s$ as the coordinate along the streamline): $$\rho V \frac{dV}{ds} = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} - \rho g \frac{dz}{ds}$$ > **Logic:** The left side is inertial force per unit volume (mass $\times$ acceleration). The right side has pressure-gradient force (acts opposite to flow) and gravity component along the streamline. --- ### Step 2 — Separate the Variables Rewrite Euler's equation by multiplying both sides by $ds$: $$\rho V \, dV = -dP - \rho g \, dz$$ > **Logic:** Recognising that $\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} ds = dP$ (pressure changes along the streamline) and $\frac{dz}{ds} ds = dz$ (elevation change). Divide throughout by $\rho$: $$V \, dV = -\frac{dP}{\rho} - g \, dz$$ --- ### Step 3 — Integrate Along the Streamline from Point 1 to Point 2 Since flow is **incompressible**, $\rho$ is constant and can be taken outside the integral: $$\int_{V_1}^{V_2} V \, dV = -\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{\rho} - \int_{z_1}^{z_2} g \, dz$$ Evaluate each integral: $$\left[\frac{V^2}{2}\right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\left[P\right]_{P_1}^{P_2} - g\left[z\right]_{z_1}^{z_2}$$ Substituting the limits: $$\frac{V_2^2}{2} - \frac{V_1^2}{2} = -\frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho} - g(z_2 - z_1)$$ Rearrange: $$\frac{V_2^2}{2} - \frac{V_1^2}{2} = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho} - g z_2 + g z_1$$ --- ### Step 4 — Group Terms by Section Bring all section-1 terms to the left and section-2 terms to the right: $$\frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + g z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + g z_2$$ Since points 1 and 2 are arbitrary, the expression is constant: $$\boxed{\frac{P}{\rho} + \frac{V^2}{2} + g z = \text{Constant (per unit mass)}}$$ --- ### Step 5 — Convert to the "Head" Form (Per Unit Weight) Divide the entire equation by $g$ to express each term in meters of fluid column (head): $$\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{Constant (per unit weight)}$$ $$\boxed{\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = C}$$ > **Logic (Interpretation):** > - $\frac{P}{\rho g}$ = Pressure head — flow work per unit weight > - $\frac{V^2}{2g}$ = Velocity/Kinetic head — kinetic energy per unit weight > - $z$ = Elevation/Datum head — potential energy per unit weight --- ## 3.2 Analytical Solution Matrix — Derivation Logic Flow | Stage | Operation | Equation Produced | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Newton's 2nd Law for fluid particle | $\rho V \dfrac{dV}{ds} = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial s} - \rho g \dfrac{dz}{ds}$ | Inertia balances pressure gradient and gravity | | 2 | Multiply by $ds$, separate variables | $\rho V\, dV = -dP - \rho g\, dz$ | Elemental work-energy balance | | 3 | Divide by $\rho$ | $V\, dV = -\dfrac{dP}{\rho} - g\, dz$ | Energy per unit mass form | | 4 | Integrate 1 → 2 | $\dfrac{V^2}{2}\Big\vert_{1}^{2} = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho} - g\Delta z$ | Total energy conservation | | 5 | Group terms | $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2} + gz_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2} + gz_2$ | Bernoulli's equation (mass form) | | 6 | Divide by $g$ | $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = C$ | Bernoulli's equation (head form) | --- ## 3.3 Verification by Dimensional Analysis Check dimensional homogeneity of each term (head form): $$\left[\frac{P}{\rho g}\right] = \frac{\text{N/m}^2}{(\text{kg/m}^3)(\text{m/s}^2)} = \frac{\text{N/m}^2}{\text{N/m}^3} = \text{m} \checkmark$$ $$\left[\frac{V^2}{2g}\right] = \frac{(\text{m/s})^2}{\text{m/s}^2} = \text{m} \checkmark$$ $$[z] = \text{m} \checkmark$$ All three terms have units of **meters** → dimensionally consistent. ✔️ --- ## 3.4 Energy Per Unit Volume Form (Multiplication by $\rho$) Multiplying the head form by $\rho g$ yields the energy-per-unit-volume form: $$P + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 + \rho g z = \text{Constant (per unit volume)}$$ This form is particularly useful when working in **SI pressure units (Pa)** without conversion to heads. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Physical Setup — Flow Through a Variable-Area Duct The following block-level functional architecture flow captures the physical setup and energy transitions between two sections of a varying cross-section pipe carrying fluid from a higher elevation to a lower elevation. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["SECTION 1
High Elevation
Large Area A1
High Pressure P1
Low Velocity V1"] B["STREAMLINE
FLOW PATH
Inviscid
Steady
Incompressible"] C["SECTION 2
Low Elevation
Small Area A2
Low Pressure P2
High Velocity V2"] A --> B --> C A_E["Energy at 1:
P1/rho g
plus V1 squared over 2g
plus z1"] B_E["ENERGY CONSERVATION
Total Head Constant
h1 equals h2 equals h3"] C_E["Energy at 2:
P2/rho g
plus V2 squared over 2g
plus z2"] A_E --- B_E --- C_E A -.->|Pressure drops| X1["Pressure head decreases"] A -.->|Elevation drops| X2["Datum head decreases"] C -.->|Continuity| X3["Velocity head increases
A1 V1 equals A2 V2"] style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style B fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000 style C fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style B_E fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.2 Energy Grade Line (EGL) and Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL) Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph "ENERGY GRADE LINE - EGL" E1["EGL Point 1
Total Head H1"] E2["EGL Point 2
Total Head H2"] E3["EGL Point 3
Total Head H3"] end subgraph "HYDRAULIC GRADE LINE - HGL" H1["HGL Point 1
P1 over rho g plus z1"] H2["HGL Point 2
P2 over rho g plus z2"] H3["HGL Point 3
P3 over rho g plus z3"] end subgraph "VELOCITY HEAD GAP" V1["V1 squared over 2g"] V2["V2 squared over 2g"] V3["V3 squared over 2g"] end E1 ---|"equals"| H1 E1 ---|"minus"| V1 E2 ---|"equals"| H2 E2 ---|"minus"| V2 E3 ---|"equals"| H3 E3 ---|"minus"| V3 style E1 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000 style E2 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000 style E3 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000 style H1 fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000 style H2 fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000 style H3 fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Functional Flow Architecture — Real-Fluid Bernoulli Pipeline For practical KTU numericals involving pumps, turbines, and friction losses: ```mermaid flowchart LR IN["Reservoir 1
Free Surface
P equals Patm
V1 equals 0"] SUC["Suction Pipe
Friction Loss hf1"] PUMP["CENTRIFUGAL PUMP
Power Input P
Head Added Hp"] DEL["Delivery Pipe
Friction Loss hf2"] OUT["Reservoir 2
Free Surface
P equals Patm
V2 equals 0"] IN --> SUC --> PUMP --> DEL --> OUT EQ["MODIFIED BERNOULLI EQUATION
z1 plus Hp equals z2 plus hf1 plus hf2"] IN --- EQ OUT --- EQ PUMP --- EQ style IN fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,color:#000 style PUMP fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00,color:#000 style OUT fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,color:#000 style EQ fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Architectural Reading Guide:** In the real-fluid architecture above, energy is *added* at the PUMP node (Hp), and energy is *extracted* by FRICTION at the pipe nodes (hf1, hf2). The free-surface reservoir nodes have $P = P_{atm}$ and $V \approx 0$, which simplifies the modified Bernoulli equation considerably. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **State Bernoulli's equation and mention any four assumptions made in its derivation.** **Model Answer:** Bernoulli's equation states that in a steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline, the sum of pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head is constant: $$\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{Constant}$$ **Assumptions:** 1. The flow is **steady**. 2. The fluid is **incompressible** (constant $\rho$). 3. The fluid is **inviscid** (frictionless, $\mu = 0$). 4. Flow occurs along a **single streamline** (or flow is irrotational). 5. **No energy** is added or extracted by external devices between the sections. *(Any four)* --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Define the terms: (i) Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL), and (ii) Energy Grade Line (EGL).** **Model Answer:** - **Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL):** It is the line that represents the sum of pressure head and elevation head $\left(\frac{P}{\rho g} + z\right)$ plotted along the length of the flow path. - **Energy Grade Line (EGL):** It is the line that represents the total energy head $\left(\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z\right)$ plotted along the length of the flow path. - The vertical distance between EGL and HGL at any section equals the **velocity head** $V^2/2g$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- ### 📘 Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a)** Derive Bernoulli's equation from Euler's equation of motion along a streamline for an ideal fluid flow. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Water flows through a horizontal pipe of diameter 200 mm at section 1, where the pressure is 100 kPa. At section 2, the diameter reduces to 100 mm and the pressure is 80 kPa. Determine the flow rate and the difference in velocity heads between the two sections. Take $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — Derivation **Assumptions:** 1. Steady flow 2. Incompressible flow 3. Inviscid (frictionless) flow 4. Flow along a streamline **Derivation:** Euler's equation of motion along a streamline: $$\rho V \frac{dV}{ds} = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} - \rho g \frac{dz}{ds}$$ Multiplying both sides by $ds$: $$\rho V \, dV = -dP - \rho g \, dz$$ Dividing by $\rho$: $$V \, dV = -\frac{dP}{\rho} - g \, dz$$ Integrating between section 1 and section 2 (with $\rho$ constant): $$\int_{V_1}^{V_2} V \, dV = -\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{\rho} - \int_{z_1}^{z_2} g \, dz$$ $$\left[\frac{V^2}{2}\right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = -\frac{1}{\rho}[P]_{P_1}^{P_2} - g[z]_{z_1}^{z_2}$$ $$\frac{V_2^2}{2} - \frac{V_1^2}{2} = -\frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho} - g(z_2 - z_1)$$ Rearranging: $$\frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + g z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + g z_2$$ Dividing by $g$: $$\boxed{\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2}$$ --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **Given:** - $D_1 = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$, $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.2)^2 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2$ - $D_2 = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$, $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 = 0.007854 \text{ m}^2$ - $P_1 = 100 \text{ kPa} = 100{,}000 \text{ Pa}$ - $P_2 = 80 \text{ kPa} = 80{,}000 \text{ Pa}$ - $z_1 = z_2$ (horizontal pipe) - $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ **Apply Bernoulli's equation (horizontal, $z_1 = z_2$):** $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$ **Apply continuity equation:** $$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \implies V_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2 = \left(\frac{0.1}{0.2}\right)^2 V_2 = 0.25 \, V_2$$ **Substitute:** $$\frac{100{,}000}{1000 \times 9.81} + \frac{(0.25 V_2)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{80{,}000}{1000 \times 9.81} + \frac{V_2^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$ $$10.194 + \frac{0.0625 V_2^2}{19.62} = 8.155 + \frac{V_2^2}{19.62}$$ $$10.194 - 8.155 = \frac{V_2^2 - 0.0625 V_2^2}{19.62}$$ $$2.039 = \frac{0.9375 V_2^2}{19.62}$$ $$V_2^2 = \frac{2.039 \times 19.62}{0.9375} = 42.66$$ $$V_2 = 6.531 \text{ m/s}$$ **Flow rate:** $$Q = A_2 V_2 = 0.007854 \times 6.531 = 0.0513 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Velocity heads:** $$\frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{(0.25 \times 6.531)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{(1.633)^2}{19.62} = \frac{2.666}{19.62} = 0.136 \text{ m}$$ $$\frac{V_2^2}{2g} = \frac{(6.531)^2}{19.62} = \frac{42.65}{19.62} = 2.174 \text{ m}$$ **Difference in velocity heads:** $$\Delta\left(\frac{V^2}{2g}\right) = 2.174 - 0.136 = \boxed{2.038 \text{ m}}$$ **Flow rate:** $Q = \mathbf{0.0513 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$ --- ### 📘 Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the physical significance of each term in Bernoulli's equation. Distinguish between the Energy Grade Line (EGL) and Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL) with the help of a neat sketch. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A pipe of diameter 300 mm carries water at a rate of 0.2 m³/s. At a certain point, the pipe rises by 6 m and the diameter reduces to 150 mm. If the pressure at the lower point is 150 kPa, find the pressure at the higher point. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — Physical Significance & EGL/HGL **Physical Significance of Each Term (Head Form):** $$\underbrace{\frac{P}{\rho g}}_{\text{Pressure head}} + \underbrace{\frac{V^2}{2g}}_{\text{Kinetic head}} + \underbrace{z}_{\text{Elevation head}} = \text{Constant}$$ - **Pressure head ($P/\rho g$):** Represents the flow work per unit weight of fluid. It is the height of a fluid column that would produce the pressure $P$. - **Velocity head ($V^2/2g$):** Represents the kinetic energy per unit weight of the fluid. It is the vertical distance the fluid would have to fall in vacuum to attain velocity $V$. - **Elevation head ($z$):** Represents the potential energy per unit weight of fluid due to its elevation above a reference datum. **EGL vs. HGL:** - **EGL** plots the **total head** $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z$ versus distance along the pipe. - **HGL** plots the **piezometric head** $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + z$ versus distance along the pipe. - **Key relation:** EGL always lies **above** HGL by a vertical distance equal to the **velocity head** $V^2/2g$. - For an **ideal fluid**, EGL is **horizontal and constant**. For a **real fluid**, EGL **drops** in the direction of flow due to friction losses. --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **Given:** - $D_1 = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.3 \text{ m}$, $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.3)^2 = 0.07069 \text{ m}^2$ - $D_2 = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$, $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$ - $Q = 0.2 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ - $z_2 - z_1 = 6 \text{ m}$ (rise) - $P_1 = 150 \text{ kPa} = 150{,}000 \text{ Pa}$ - $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ **Calculate velocities:** $$V_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{0.2}{0.07069} = 2.829 \text{ m/s}$$ $$V_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{0.2}{0.01767} = 11.318 \text{ m/s}$$ **Apply Bernoulli's equation:** $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$ Taking $z_1 = 0$ as datum, $z_2 = 6$ m: $$\frac{150{,}000}{1000 \times 9.81} + \frac{(2.829)^2}{2 \times 9.81} + 0 = \frac{P_2}{9810} + \frac{(11.318)^2}{2 \times 9.81} + 6$$ **Compute each term:** $$\frac{150{,}000}{9810} = 15.290 \text{ m}$$ $$\frac{(2.829)^2}{19.62} = \frac{8.003}{19.62} = 0.408 \text{ m}$$ $$\frac{(11.318)^2}{19.62} = \frac{128.097}{19.62} = 6.529 \text{ m}$$ **Substitute:** $$15.290 + 0.408 = \frac{P_2}{9810} + 6.529 + 6$$ $$15.698 = \frac{P_2}{9810} + 12.529$$ $$\frac{P_2}{9810} = 15.698 - 12.529 = 3.169 \text{ m}$$ $$P_2 = 3.169 \times 9810 = \boxed{31{,}088 \text{ Pa} \approx 31.09 \text{ kPa}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to take $z_1 = 0$ as the datum.** When elevations differ, always explicitly state your datum choice. > 2. **Mixing units.** $P$ must be in Pa, $\rho$ in kg/m³, $V$ in m/s, and the result will be in meters of head (not Pascals). > 3. **Sign errors in the rise.** If the pipe rises from 1 to 2, $z_2 > z_1$, and pressure $P_2$ will be **lower** than $P_1$ (consistent with energy conservation). > 4. **Not mentioning continuity** when the diameter changes — this is essential to find $V_2$ from $V_1$. > 5. **Omitting assumptions** in the derivation part — examiners allocate 1–2 marks just for stating the assumptions. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅ **Bernoulli's Equation (Ideal Flow, Head Form):** $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = C$ ✅ **Three Head Forms Are Equivalent:** - Per unit weight (m): $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z$ - Per unit mass (J/kg): $\dfrac{P}{\rho} + \dfrac{V^2}{2} + gz$ - Per unit volume (Pa): $P + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 + \rho g z$ ✅ **Five Key Assumptions:** Steady, incompressible, inviscid, along a streamline, no external energy addition. ✅ **Continuity Equation (must be paired with Bernoulli):** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ for incompressible flow. ✅ **Modified Bernoulli's Equation (for real fluids):** $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_p = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + H_t + h_L$ ✅ **Energy Grade Line (EGL):** Total head line; drops only with friction losses. ✅ **Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL):** Piezometric head line; lies below EGL by $V^2/2g$. ✅ **Limitations:** Cannot be applied across a hydraulic jump, in rotating flow (without modification), or in compressible flow at high Mach numbers. ✅ **Standard Constant Values to Memorise:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$. ✅ **Master Trick:** Whenever a free surface is involved, set $P = P_{atm}$ and $V \approx 0$ — this simplifies Bernoulli dramatically. ✅ **Directional Logic:** Pipe contracts → velocity increases → pressure decreases (Venturi effect). Pipe expands → velocity decreases → pressure increases (diffuser).

topics.title: Reynolds Experiment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Reynolds Experiment # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition The **Reynolds Experiment** is a classical fluid mechanics demonstration conducted by **Professor Osborne Reynolds** in **1883** to physically establish the existence of two fundamentally distinct regimes of viscous fluid flow inside a pipe — namely, **Laminar Flow** (streamline/sheet flow) and **Turbulent Flow** (chaotic/eddy flow), separated by an unstable **Transitional Flow** zone. The experiment also introduced the dimensionless **Reynolds Number (Re)** as the defining criterion that predicts the prevailing flow regime. > [!NOTE] > **Reynolds Number (Re):** A dimensionless quantity defined as the ratio of **inertia forces** to **viscous forces** within a fluid, > $$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$ > where $\rho$ = density, $V$ = mean velocity, $D$ = pipe diameter, $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity, $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment is a direct foundation for understanding **dimensional analysis**, **dynamic similarity**, and the design of all hydraulic machines and pipelines. Expect at least one full Part-B question (14 marks) on Reynolds Number derivation and significance. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are standing at a busy **two-lane highway** during rush hour: | Traffic Scenario | Fluid Equivalent | Reynolds Number | |------------------|------------------|-----------------| | **Cars moving in disciplined lanes, smooth and orderly** | Streamlines (laminae) sliding smoothly over each other | $Re < 2000$ → **Laminar** | | **A few drivers begin switching lanes, mild chaos** | Streamlines begin to waver, eddies form intermittently | $2000 < Re < 4000$ → **Transition** | | **Every car is honking, overtaking, jostling — complete disorder** | Eddies and cross-currents dominate; dye dissolves into the bulk | $Re > 4000$ → **Turbulent** | **In the actual experiment**, Reynolds injected a thin filament of **coloured dye (potassium permanganate, KMnO₄)** into the centreline of a transparent glass tube through which water flowed with a controllable rate. The visual behaviour of this dye filament is the "camera" recording the underlying physics of momentum transfer inside the fluid. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for the critical Re values:** *"**L**aminar **L**oves **L**ow"* ($Re < 2000$) and *"**T**urbulence **T**hrives **T**op"* ($Re > 4000$). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Experimental Apparatus — Component Breakdown A standard KTU-lab Reynolds Apparatus consists of the following elements: 1. **Constant-head overhead tank** — provides steady water supply under a constant pressure head. 2. **Stilling chamber / entrance tank** — contains **honeycomb (flow straighteners)** to dampen entry disturbances and produce a uniform velocity profile at the inlet of the test pipe. 3. **Bell-mouthed entry** — minimises entry losses and prevents flow separation. 4. **Transparent glass test section** — a precision-bore glass tube where the visual observation is made. 5. **Dye reservoir with capillary injector** — holds the **KMnO₄** solution and feeds dye along the centreline of the test pipe through a fine needle. 6. **Flow-control valve** — regulates the discharge and hence the mean velocity $V$. 7. **Measuring tank / volumetric collector** — enables measurement of discharge $Q$ using $Q = \text{Volume}/\text{time}$. 8. **Overflow weir** — maintains constant head in the supply tank. --- ## 2.2 Observations — The Three Regimes As the control valve is gradually opened (velocity increased from zero), the dye filament exhibits three qualitatively distinct behaviours: | Regime | Observation of Dye Filament | Mathematical Condition | |--------|----------------------------|------------------------| | **Laminar (Streamline) Flow** | Dye travels as a **straight, sharp, unbroken line** parallel to the axis. No lateral mixing. | $Re < 2000$ | | **Transitional Flow** | Dye line begins to **wobble / wave**; intermittent bursts of eddies. The flow is **unstable and non-reproducible**. | $2000 \le Re \le 4000$ | | **Turbulent Flow** | Dye **immediately diffuses** across the entire cross-section; rapid, irregular fluctuations; chaotic mixing. | $Re > 4000$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Lower Critical Reynolds Number** $= 2000$ — flow is **always laminar** below this. > **Upper Critical Reynolds Number** $= 4000$ — flow is **always turbulent** above this. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | S.No. | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | |-------|----------|---------|-------------| | 1 | Reynolds Number (general) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless | | 2 | Reynolds Number (pipe flow) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | Dimensionless | | 3 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | $\text{m}^{2}\text{/s}$ | | 4 | Mean Velocity | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | | 5 | Discharge (volumetric) | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Volume collected}}{\text{Time}}$ | $\text{m}^{3}\text{/s}$ | | 6 | Critical Reynolds (lower) | $Re_{c,L} = 2000$ | — | | 7 | Critical Reynolds (upper) | $Re_{c,U} = 4000$ | — | | 8 | Inertia Force | $F_{i} \propto \rho V^{2} L^{2}$ | N | | 9 | Viscous Force | $F_{v} \propto \mu V L$ | N | | 10 | Force ratio (definition of Re) | $\dfrac{F_{i}}{F_{v}} = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$ | — | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility * **Pipeline Design:** Petroleum and water pipelines are sized based on Re to ensure pumping costs are minimised (laminar gives low friction loss but low throughput). * **Aerodynamics:** Aircraft wings are designed for specific Re ranges to maintain attached (laminar) or stalled (turbulent-separated) flow. * **Biomedical Engineering:** Blood flow in arteries (Re ≈ 100–1000) is normally laminar; turbulent flow indicates pathology (stenosis). * **Hydraulic Machines:** Pumps and turbines are classified by their **specific speed**, a Reynolds-number-based similarity parameter. * **Chemical Reactors:** Mixing efficiency, heat-transfer coefficients, and reaction rates all scale with Re. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Takeaway:** Reynolds Number is the single most important dimensionless group in continuum fluid mechanics. Any new flow problem you encounter in your KTU exam should begin by computing Re. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reynolds Number by Dimensional Analysis (Buckingham π Theorem) This is the **most frequently asked 7-mark derivation** in KTU ESE Module 2. ### Step 1 — Identify the dependent and independent variables The **inertia force** $F_{i}$ experienced by a fluid element moving past a stationary body is assumed to depend on: $$F_{i} = \phi(\rho, V, L, \mu)$$ where the variables and their fundamental dimensions are: | Variable | Symbol | Dimensions $[\text{M L T}]$ | |----------|--------|------------------------------| | Inertia Force | $F_{i}$ | $M L T^{-2}$ | | Density | $\rho$ | $M L^{-3}$ | | Velocity | $V$ | $L T^{-1}$ | | Characteristic Length | $L$ | $L$ | | Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu$ | $M L^{-1} T^{-1}$ | ### Step 2 — Apply Buckingham π Theorem * Total number of variables: $n = 5$ (including $F_{i}$). * Number of fundamental dimensions: $k = 3$ (M, L, T). * Number of dimensionless $\pi$ groups: $$\text{Number of } \pi \text{ terms} = n - k = 5 - 3 = 2$$ So we expect **two** independent $\pi$ groups. ### Step 3 — Choose repeating variables Select $\rho$, $V$, and $L$ as the repeating variables (they collectively cover M, L, T and are dimensionally independent). ### Step 4 — Form the first π-group with the non-repeating variable $F_{i}$ $$\pi_{1} = F_{i} \cdot \rho^{a} \cdot V^{b} \cdot L^{c}$$ Writing out dimensions: $$[M^{0} L^{0} T^{0}] = (M L T^{-2})(M L^{-3})^{a} (L T^{-1})^{b} (L)^{c}$$ Equating powers of M, L, T: * **For M:** $\;\;0 = 1 + a \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; a = -1$ * **For T:** $\;\;0 = -2 - b \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; b = -2$ * **For L:** $\;\;0 = 1 - 3a + b + c = 1 - 3(-1) + (-2) + c = 2 + c \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; c = -2$ Therefore: $$\pi_{1} = \dfrac{F_{i}}{\rho V^{2} L^{2}}$$ This is the **Euler Number / Force Coefficient** — the ratio of inertia force to dynamic pressure force. ### Step 5 — Form the second π-group with the non-repeating variable $\mu$ $$\pi_{2} = \mu \cdot \rho^{a} \cdot V^{b} \cdot L^{c}$$ Writing out dimensions: $$[M^{0} L^{0} T^{0}] = (M L^{-1} T^{-1})(M L^{-3})^{a} (L T^{-1})^{b} (L)^{c}$$ Equating powers of M, L, T: * **For M:** $\;\;0 = 1 + a \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; a = -1$ * **For T:** $\;\;0 = -1 - b \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; b = -1$ * **For L:** $\;\;0 = -1 - 3a + b + c = -1 - 3(-1) + (-1) + c = 1 + c \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; c = -1$ Therefore: $$\pi_{2} = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho V L}$$ ### Step 6 — Functional relationship By Buckingham π Theorem: $$\pi_{1} = f(\pi_{2}) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dfrac{F_{i}}{\rho V^{2} L^{2}} = f\!\left(\dfrac{\mu}{\rho V L}\right)$$ ### Step 7 — Invert and rename to obtain Reynolds Number It is conventional to **invert** $\pi_{2}$ and call it the **Reynolds Number**: $$Re = \frac{1}{\pi_{2}} = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$$ For flow in a circular pipe, the characteristic length $L$ is replaced by the **pipe diameter** $D$: $$\boxed{\,Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}\,}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reynolds Number as the Ratio of Inertia to Viscous Force ### Step 1 — Inertia force on a fluid element A fluid parcel of characteristic size $L$ moving at velocity $V$ has mass $\sim \rho L^{3}$ and acceleration $\sim V^{2}/L$. $$F_{i} = (\text{mass})(\text{acceleration}) = \rho L^{3} \cdot \dfrac{V^{2}}{L} = \rho V^{2} L^{2}$$ ### Step 2 — Viscous force from Newton's law of viscosity Shear stress $\tau = \mu \dfrac{dV}{dy} \sim \mu \dfrac{V}{L}$. The viscous force acts over an area $\sim L^{2}$: $$F_{v} = \tau \cdot L^{2} = \mu \dfrac{V}{L} \cdot L^{2} = \mu V L$$ ### Step 3 — Take the ratio $$\dfrac{F_{i}}{F_{v}} = \dfrac{\rho V^{2} L^{2}}{\mu V L} = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$$ Hence: $$\boxed{\,Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{F_{i}}{F_{v}}\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical meaning:** $Re \ll 1$ → viscous forces dominate → laminar flow. $Re \gg 1$ → inertia forces dominate → turbulent flow. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** Water flows through a 50 mm diameter pipe at a mean velocity of 0.05 m/s. Dynamic viscosity $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s and density $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³. Identify the flow regime. **Solution:** Given: $D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$, $V = 0.05 \text{ m/s}$, $\mu = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³. $$Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{1000 \times 0.05 \times 0.05}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$Re = \dfrac{2.5}{10^{-3}} = 2500$$ Since $2000 < Re = 2500 < 4000$, the flow is in the **Transitional regime**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Reynolds Apparatus The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the physical layout, the role of each component, and the direction of information (fluid flow + dye injection + observation) — perfectly aligned with KTU lab-viva expectations. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Constant Head
Overhead Tank] -->|Gravity-driven water flow| B[Stilling Chamber
with Honeycomb
Flow Straightener] B -->|Laminarised uniform
velocity profile| C[Bell-mouth
Convergent Entry] C -->|Test section inlet| D[Transparent
Glass Test Pipe
Diameter D] E[Dye Reservoir
KMnO4 Solution] -->|Fine capillary
centreline injection| D D -->|Outflow| F[Flow Control
Needle Valve
regulates V] F --> G[Measuring Tank
Volume vs Time
gives Q] G --> H[Overflow Weir
returns to sump] D -.->|Visual observation
by experimenter| I[Observation Zone
Laminar or
Turbulent Dye Pattern] ``` --- ## 4.2 Logical Flow of the Experimental Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Start: Valve fully closed V is near 0] --> P2[Inject dye through capillary] P2 --> P3[Open valve slightly
small V] P3 --> P4{Dye observation?} P4 -->|Straight threadlike line| P5[Laminar Flow
Re less than 2000] P4 -->|Wavy intermittent line| P6[Transitional Flow
2000 to 4000] P4 -->|Random dispersion and mixing| P7[Turbulent Flow
Re greater than 4000] P5 --> P8[Record Q from measuring tank
Compute V = Q divided by A
Compute Re] P6 --> P8 P7 --> P8 P8 --> P9[Tabulate Re vs Flow Regime
Identify critical V and Q] ``` --- ## 4.3 Functional Decision Logic for Flow-Regime Identification ```mermaid flowchart TD Q0[Compute Reynolds Number
Re = rho V D / mu] --> Q1{Compare with
critical values} Q1 -->|Re less than 2000| R1[LAMINAR FLOW
Smooth dye filament
Darcy friction factor
f = 64 / Re] Q1 -->|2000 less or equal Re less or equal 4000| R2[TRANSITIONAL FLOW
Unstable mixed pattern
Empirical correlations needed] Q1 -->|Re greater than 4000| R3[TURBULENT FLOW
Dye disperses completely
Blasius: f = 0.316 Re to power minus 0.25] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** **Define Reynolds Number. State its physical significance.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Reynolds Number is defined as the ratio of inertia forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow: $$Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$$ It is a **dimensionless** quantity that serves as the criterion to identify whether a flow is **laminar** ($Re < 2000$), **transitional** ($2000 \le Re \le 4000$), or **turbulent** ($Re > 4000$). Physically, it indicates the relative dominance of fluid momentum (inertia) over internal friction (viscosity). *(Mark split: 1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark significance — awarded as per KTU valuation key.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **What are the lower and upper critical Reynolds Numbers for flow in a circular pipe?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** * **Lower Critical Reynolds Number:** $Re_{c,L} = 2000$ — below this value, the flow is **always laminar**, regardless of the initial disturbances. * **Upper Critical Reynolds Number:** $Re_{c,U} = 4000$ — above this value, the flow is **always turbulent**. * Between $2000$ and $4000$, the flow lies in the **transitional regime**, where it is unstable and may be either laminar or turbulent depending on entry conditions and disturbance levels. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]** **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Reynolds Experiment** to demonstrate the different types of fluid flow. State the observations made. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using **Buckingham's π Theorem**, derive the expression for **Reynolds Number** as a dimensionless group governing viscous flow. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Apparatus (3 marks):** The setup consists of (i) a constant-head overhead tank, (ii) a stilling chamber with honeycomb straightener, (iii) a transparent glass test pipe, (iv) a dye reservoir containing potassium permanganate (KMnO₄) connected to a fine capillary injector at the pipe centreline, (v) a flow-control valve, and (vi) a measuring tank with overflow weir. **Procedure (2 marks):** Water is allowed to flow under gravity. The valve is initially kept almost closed (low velocity). Dye is introduced through the capillary injector. The flow rate is increased in small steps and the behaviour of the dye filament in the glass tube is visually observed and recorded. **Observations (2 marks):** 1. At **low velocities**, the dye travels as a **straight, sharp, unbroken coloured line** parallel to the axis of the pipe — this is **laminar (streamline) flow**, with $Re < 2000$. 2. As velocity is increased, the dye line begins to **wave and wobble** — this is the **transitional regime**, $2000 \le Re \le 4000$. 3. At **high velocities**, the dye **diffuses and mixes rapidly across the entire cross-section** — this is **turbulent flow**, with $Re > 4000$. **Conclusion:** The flow regime depends on the dimensionless Reynolds Number, not on any single dimensional quantity. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] The complete step-by-step Buckingham π derivation has been demonstrated in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The valuation key marks the following checkpoints: | Step | Key Result | Marks | |------|------------|-------| | 1. List variables ($F_{i}, \rho, V, L, \mu$) with dimensions | Tabulation correctness | 1 | | 2. Apply π Theorem → 2 groups | $n - k = 5 - 3 = 2$ | 1 | | 3. First π-group (with $F_{i}$) | $\pi_{1} = F_{i}/(\rho V^{2} L^{2})$ | 2 | | 4. Second π-group (with $\mu$) | $\pi_{2} = \mu/(\rho V L)$ | 2 | | 5. Invert and rename → Reynolds Number | $Re = \rho V L/\mu$ | 1 | --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Define **Reynolds Number**. Show that it represents the **ratio of inertia force to viscous force**. State the conditions for laminar, transitional, and turbulent flow in a circular pipe. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An oil of viscosity $0.1$ N·s/m² and relative density $0.85$ flows through a pipe of diameter $15$ cm at a rate of $0.005$ m³/s. Determine whether the flow is laminar or turbulent. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Definition (2 marks):** Reynolds Number is a dimensionless parameter given by: $$Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$$ It is the ratio of inertia force to viscous force. **Proof — Force Ratio (3 marks):** * Inertia force on fluid element of size $D$: $$F_{i} = \rho \cdot (\text{vol}) \cdot (\text{accel}) = \rho D^{3} \cdot \dfrac{V^{2}}{D} = \rho V^{2} D^{2}$$ * Viscous force from Newton's law of viscosity: $$F_{v} = \tau \cdot (\text{area}) = \mu \dfrac{V}{D} \cdot D^{2} = \mu V D$$ * Ratio: $$\dfrac{F_{i}}{F_{v}} = \dfrac{\rho V^{2} D^{2}}{\mu V D} = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = Re$$ **Conditions for flow regime (2 marks):** | Regime | Range of Re | |--------|-------------| | Laminar | $Re < 2000$ | | Transitional | $2000 \le Re \le 4000$ | | Turbulent | $Re > 4000$ | #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Given Data:** * Dynamic viscosity, $\mu = 0.1$ N·s/m² * Relative density, $\rho_{rel} = 0.85$ → $\rho = 850$ kg/m³ * Diameter, $D = 15$ cm $= 0.15$ m * Discharge, $Q = 0.005$ m³/s **Step 1 — Compute cross-sectional area:** $$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.15)^{2} = 0.01767 \text{ m}^{2}$$ **Step 2 — Compute mean velocity:** $$V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{0.005}{0.01767} = 0.2829 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Reynolds Number:** $$Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{850 \times 0.2829 \times 0.15}{0.1}$$ $$Re = \dfrac{36.07}{0.1} = 360.7$$ **Step 4 — Identify regime:** Since $Re = 360.7 \ll 2000$, the flow is **LAMINAR**. *(Mark split as per KTU key: 1 mark data extraction, 2 marks area & velocity, 2 marks Re calculation, 1 mark regime identification, 1 mark final conclusion with units check.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit conversion error:** Forgetting to convert cm → m or cP → Pa·s is the single biggest mark-loser. Always write units explicitly in the data table. > 2. **Wrong characteristic length:** Students often use *radius* $r$ instead of *diameter* $D$ in $Re$. Re values are **halved** if you do this — leading to wrong regime identification. > 3. **Forgetting to invert $\pi_{2}$:** The Buckingham π derivation gives $\pi_{2} = \mu/(\rho V L)$. Failing to invert it to $Re = \rho V L/\mu$ loses 1–2 marks. > 4. **Mixing up critical values:** Memorise $Re_{c,L} = 2000$ (lower) and $Re_{c,U} = 4000$ (upper). Confusing them reverses the regime boundaries. > 5. **No conclusion in numerical problems:** KTU strictly mandates a **declarative conclusion** ("Hence the flow is turbulent.") — without it, 1 mark is deducted. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] Reynolds Experiment was performed by **Osborne Reynolds in 1883**. - [x] **Apparatus:** overhead tank → stilling chamber (honeycomb) → bell-mouth → glass test pipe → control valve → measuring tank. - [x] **Dye used:** Potassium permanganate (KMnO₄) — coloured, non-toxic, water-soluble. - [x] **Reynolds Number formula:** $Re = \rho V D / \mu = V D / \nu$ — always dimensionless. - [x] **Critical values for pipe flow:** Lower = **2000**, Upper = **4000**. - [x] **Three regimes:** Laminar ($Re < 2000$), Transitional ($2000 \le Re \le 4000$), Turbulent ($Re > 4000$). - [x] **Physical meaning of Re:** Ratio of **inertia force** to **viscous force**. - [x] **Derivation methods to know for KTU:** (i) Buckingham π theorem, (ii) Force-ratio method. - [x] **Characteristic length:** Always use **diameter $D$** for circular pipes. - [x] **Real-world analog:** Highway traffic — disciplined cars = laminar; chaotic rush hour = turbulent. - [x] **Engineering applications:** Pipe sizing, pump/turbine design, aircraft aerodynamics, blood-flow diagnostics. - [x] **Common KTU trap:** Always declare the **flow regime** explicitly as the final step of any numerical problem. - [x] **Dimensional check:** $[\rho V D / \mu] = \dfrac{(M/L^{3})(L/T)(L)}{(M/(L T))} = M^{0} L^{0} T^{0}$ ✓ (dimensionless).

topics.title: Hagen-Poiseuille Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hagen-Poiseuille Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hagen-Poiseuille Equation (KTU 2024 Standard Definition):**
> The Hagen-Poiseuille equation is a fundamental physical law in fluid mechanics that expresses the **volumetric flow rate of a Newtonian fluid** undergoing **steady, laminar, incompressible, fully-developed viscous flow** through a **uniform circular cross-section pipe** (horizontal or inclined) as a function of the pipe's geometry and the pressure gradient driving the flow.

Mathematically, the canonical form is expressed as:

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \, R^{4}}{8 \, \mu} \left( \frac{-\partial p}{\partial z} \right)
$$

Where each term has a strictly defined physical meaning under the KTU 2024 syllabus nomenclature. An equivalent form in terms of pipe diameter $D$ is:

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \, D^{4}}{128 \, \mu \, L} \left( P_{1} - P_{2} \right)
$$

The equation was independently formulated by **Gotthilf Heinrich Ludwig Hagen (1839)** and **Jean Léonard Marie Poiseuille (1840)** — a French physiologist who originally developed it to describe blood flow through capillaries.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Pre-Requisite Assumptions:**
> The Hagen-Poiseuille equation is **only strictly valid** when the following five physical conditions are simultaneously satisfied:
> 1. **Steady State** flow ($\partial / \partial t = 0$)
> 2. **Laminar** flow regime (Reynolds number $Re < 2000$)
> 3. **Newtonian** fluid with constant viscosity $\mu$
> 4. **Incompressible** fluid ($\rho = $ constant)
> 5. **Fully developed** flow in a **horizontal, rigid, constant-diameter** pipe

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Engineering Intuition

Imagine a **garden hose** connected to a municipal water tap. The water entering the hose possesses a certain **pressure energy** (think of it as the "push" from the water utility). As this pressurized water tries to squeeze its way through the narrow hose, it encounters **frictional resistance** from the hose walls and from the internal fluid layers rubbing against each other (viscous shearing).

**What the Hagen-Poiseuille equation tells you, in plain language:**

> It is essentially a **"budget equation"** for fluid flow. The fluid flow rate is **directly proportional** to the pressure drop you apply and **inversely proportional** to the fluid's "stickiness" (viscosity). However, the *most critical engineering insight* is that the flow rate scales with the **fourth power of the radius** ($R^{4}$).

**The "$R^{4}$ Sensitivity" — Why this matters in real engineering:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If you **double the pipe radius**, the volumetric flow rate increases by a factor of **$2^{4} = 16$ times** (assuming the same pressure drop). This extreme sensitivity is the reason why industrial pipelines, biomedical catheters, and oil pipelines are designed with such meticulous attention to diameter specifications. A 10% increase in pipe radius can boost flow by **46.4%** (since $1.1^{4} \approx 1.4641$).

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | SI Unit | Engineering Notation |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Pipe radius | $R$ | $\text{m}$ | $\text{m}$ |
| Pipe diameter | $D$ | $\text{m}$ | $\text{m}$ |
| Dynamic viscosity | $\mu$ | $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$ | $\text{N} \cdot \text{s} / \text{m}^{2}$ |
| Kinematic viscosity | $\nu$ | $\text{m}^{2} / \text{s}$ | $\text{m}^{2} / \text{s}$ |
| Pressure gradient | $-dp/dz$ | $\text{Pa} / \text{m}$ | $\text{N} / \text{m}^{3}$ |
| Volumetric flow rate | $Q$ | $\text{m}^{3} / \text{s}$ | $\text{m}^{3} / \text{s}$ |
| Mean velocity | $V_{avg}$ | $\text{m} / \text{s}$ | $\text{m} / \text{s}$ |
| Pipe length | $L$ | $\text{m}$ | $\text{m}$ |

**Note on Water Viscosity (standard reference value):** $\mu_{water} = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa} \cdot \text{s}$ at $20^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $0.653 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa} \cdot \text{s}$ at $40^{\circ}\text{C}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Phenomenon — Step-by-Step Logic

The Hagen-Poiseuille equation emerges from a fundamental **force balance on a cylindrical fluid element** in laminar pipe flow. The logic chain is as follows:

* **Step 1 — Identify the driving force:** A higher upstream pressure $P_{1}$ pushes the fluid forward, while a lower downstream pressure $P_{2}$ "pulls" it. The **net axial pressure force** acts as the *driver* of the flow.
* **Step 2 — Identify the resisting force:** The fluid is "sticky." Adjacent fluid layers shear against each other, and the outermost layer (at the wall) is held stationary by the **no-slip condition**. This viscous shearing acts as the *resistor* against the flow.
* **Step 3 — Establish steady-state equilibrium:** In steady, fully-developed flow, the fluid accelerates no longer ($\partial u / \partial z = 0$). Therefore, the **net force on the fluid element must be exactly zero**. The pressure driving force and the viscous resisting force are perfectly balanced.
* **Step 4 — Recognize the velocity profile shape:** By integrating the resulting differential equation, the velocity distribution across the pipe cross-section is found to be **parabolic**, with **maximum velocity at the pipe centerline** and **zero velocity at the wall**.
* **Step 5 — Compute the bulk flow:** Integrating the parabolic profile over the full pipe cross-section yields the total volumetric flow rate, which is the classical Hagen-Poiseuille expression.

## 2.2 Velocity Profile (Parabolic Distribution)

The local axial velocity $u(r)$ at any radial distance $r$ from the centerline is:

$$
u(r) = \frac{1}{4 \, \mu} \left( -\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} \right) \left( R^{2} - r^{2} \right) = u_{max} \left( 1 - \frac{r^{2}}{R^{2}} \right)
$$

Where the **centerline maximum velocity** is:

$$
u_{max} = \frac{R^{2}}{4 \, \mu} \left( -\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} \right)
$$

## 2.3 Mean (Average) Velocity Relationship

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Half of Max" Rule:** For Hagen-Poiseuille (parabolic) flow, the mean velocity across the pipe cross-section is **exactly half** of the centerline maximum velocity: $V_{avg} = u_{max} / 2$.

$$
V_{avg} = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{R^{2}}{8 \, \mu} \left( -\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} \right) = \frac{1}{2} u_{max}
$$

## 2.4 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization Table for KTU Board Examinations:** All formulas below are derivable, high-frequency, and consistently tested in PCIET302 ESE and internal assessments.

| \# | Formula | Description / Engineering Use |
| :-: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4}}{8 \mu} \left( -\dfrac{dp}{dz} \right)$ | Volumetric flow rate (radius form) |
| 2 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4}}{8 \mu L} \left( P_{1} - P_{2} \right)$ | Volumetric flow rate (pressure-drop form) |
| 3 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D^{4}}{128 \mu L} \left( P_{1} - P_{2} \right)$ | Volumetric flow rate (diameter form) |
| 4 | $u(r) = \dfrac{1}{4 \mu} \left( -\dfrac{dp}{dz} \right) \left( R^{2} - r^{2} \right)$ | Parabolic velocity profile |
| 5 | $u_{max} = \dfrac{R^{2}}{4 \mu} \left( -\dfrac{dp}{dz} \right)$ | Maximum (centerline) velocity |
| 6 | $V_{avg} = \dfrac{R^{2}}{8 \mu} \left( -\dfrac{dp}{dz} \right) = \dfrac{u_{max}}{2}$ | Mean (cross-sectional average) velocity |
| 7 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi R^{4}}$ | Pressure drop for a given flow rate (practical design) |
| 8 | $h_{f} = \dfrac{32 \mu L V_{avg}}{\rho g D^{2}} = \dfrac{64}{Re} \cdot \dfrac{L V_{avg}^{2}}{2 g D}$ | Frictional head loss (Darcy form, laminar) |
| 9 | $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | Darcy friction factor (laminar flow only) |
| 10 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V_{avg} D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (validity check: $Re < 2000$) |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The variable $r$ in the formulas refers to the **radial distance from the pipe centerline**, with the range $0 \leq r \leq R$. Do not confuse with the **radial coordinate** used in polar or cylindrical coordinate systems in general (where the entire cross-section is typically parameterized over $0 \leq r \leq R$).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

The Hagen-Poiseuille equation is a cornerstone of multiple engineering disciplines:

* **Biomedical Engineering:** Modeling **blood flow** through arteries and designing hypodermic needles, IV cannulas, and dialysis tubing.
* **Chemical Engineering:** Sizing process piping, designing capillary viscometers, and calculating flow rates in microfluidic lab-on-chip devices.
* **Civil & Environmental Engineering:** Sizing water distribution mains, designing irrigation networks, and computing groundwater flow through aquifers (Darcy's law analog).
* **Petroleum Engineering:** Estimating flow rates in oil well production strings and designing subsea pipelines.
* **Aerospace Engineering:** Analyzing flow through fuel injection lines and hydraulic actuation systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Warning for KTU Examinations:** The Hagen-Poiseuille equation **fails** in turbulent flow ($Re > 2000$), non-Newtonian fluids (blood, polymer solutions), and near pipe bends/fittings. In such cases, empirical friction factor correlations (Colebrook-White, Moody chart) must be used.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Cylindrical Fluid Element — Free Body Setup

Consider a **horizontal cylindrical pipe** of constant radius $R$, carrying a Newtonian fluid in steady, laminar, fully-developed flow. We isolate a **coaxial cylindrical fluid element** of:

* Radius: $r$ (variable)
* Length: $dz$

The forces acting on this element in the axial (z) direction are:

1. **Pressure force on the left face:** $P \cdot \pi r^{2}$ (pushing rightward, i.e., in $+z$ direction)
2. **Pressure force on the right face:** $(P + dP) \cdot \pi r^{2}$ (pushing leftward, i.e., in $-z$ direction)
3. **Viscous shear force on the curved lateral surface:** $\tau \cdot (2 \pi r \, dz)$ (resisting the flow)

> **Note on sign convention:** Since pressure decreases in the direction of flow, we have $dP < 0$ when $dz > 0$, hence $-(dP/dz) > 0$.

## 3.2 Newton's Second Law for Steady Flow

In steady, fully-developed flow, the fluid element is **not accelerating** ($\partial u / \partial t = 0$, $\partial u / \partial z = 0$ by the fully-developed condition). Therefore, **Newton's Second Law** reduces to a **static force balance**:

$$
\sum F_{z} = 0
$$

$$
(P \cdot \pi r^{2}) - \left( (P + dP) \cdot \pi r^{2} \right) - \tau \cdot (2 \pi r \, dz) = 0
$$

**Expanding and simplifying the pressure terms:**

$$
P \pi r^{2} - P \pi r^{2} - dP \cdot \pi r^{2} - \tau \cdot 2 \pi r \, dz = 0
$$

The $P \pi r^{2}$ terms cancel exactly:

$$
- dP \cdot \pi r^{2} - \tau \cdot 2 \pi r \, dz = 0
$$

**Dividing throughout by $\pi r \, dz$:**

$$
- dP \cdot r - 2 \tau \, dz = 0
$$

**Dividing throughout by $dz$ and rearranging:**

$$
\tau = - \frac{r}{2} \cdot \frac{dP}{dz}
$$

> **Result (Equation A):** This fundamental relation shows that the **shear stress varies linearly** with the radial distance $r$. It is **maximum at the pipe wall** ($r = R$) and **zero at the centerline** ($r = 0$).

$$
\tau = - \frac{r}{2} \cdot \frac{dP}{dz} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau = \frac{r}{2} \left( -\frac{dP}{dz} \right)
$$

## 3.3 Substituting Newton's Law of Viscosity

For a **Newtonian fluid**, the shear stress is related to the velocity gradient by:

$$
\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dr}
$$

> **Sign consideration:** The velocity $u$ **decreases** as $r$ increases (since the wall is stationary due to the no-slip condition). Therefore, $du/dr$ is **negative**. We define the shear stress magnitude as positive in the direction opposing the flow. Substituting:

$$
\mu \cdot \frac{du}{dr} = \frac{r}{2} \left( -\frac{dP}{dz} \right)
$$

**Separating variables and rearranging for integration:**

$$
\frac{du}{dr} = \frac{r}{2 \mu} \left( -\frac{dP}{dz} \right)
$$

## 3.4 First Integration — Velocity Gradient

$$
du = \frac{1}{2 \mu} \left( -\frac{dP}{dz} \right) r \, dr
$$

$$
\int du = \frac{1}{2 \mu} \left( -\frac{dP}{dz} \right) \int r \, dr
$$

$$
u(r) = \frac{1}{2 \mu} \left( -\frac{dP}{dz} \right) \cdot \frac{r^{2}}{2} + C_{1}
$$

$$
u(r) = \frac{1}{4 \mu} \left( -\frac{dP}{dz} \right) r^{2} + C_{1}
$$

**Apply Boundary Condition 1 (Centerline symmetry):** The velocity profile is symmetric about the centerline, meaning the velocity gradient at the centerline ($r = 0$) is zero:

$$
\left. \frac{du}{dr} \right|_{r=0} = 0
$$

This boundary condition is **automatically satisfied** by the form of the equation (the $r^{2}$ term vanishes at $r = 0$), so the constant $C_{1}$ remains to be determined.

## 3.5 Second Integration — Velocity Profile

**Apply Boundary Condition 2 (No-slip at the wall):** The fluid in direct contact with the pipe wall is stationary. At $r = R$, $u = 0$:

$$
0 = \frac{1}{4 \mu} \left( -\frac{dP}{dz} \right) R^{2} + C_{1}
$$

**Solving for the integration constant:**

$$
C_{1} = - \frac{1}{4 \mu} \left( -\frac{dP}{dz} \right) R^{2}
$$

**Substituting back into the velocity equation:**

$$
u(r) = \frac{1}{4 \mu} \left( -\frac{dP}{dz} \right) r^{2} - \frac{1}{4 \mu} \left( -\frac{dP}{dz} \right) R^{2}
$$

**Factoring out the common term:**

$$
u(r) = \frac{1}{4 \mu} \left( -\frac{dP}{dz} \right) \left( R^{2} - r^{2} \right)
$$

> **Result (Equation B — Parabolic Velocity Profile):** This is the celebrated **parabolic velocity distribution**. Velocity is maximum at the centerline ($r = 0$) and decreases to zero at the wall ($r = R$).

## 3.6 Integrating for Volumetric Flow Rate

The **volumetric flow rate** $Q$ is obtained by integrating the local velocity over the entire pipe cross-section. In cylindrical coordinates, we use a **differential ring element** of width $dr$ at radius $r$:

* Area of the ring: $dA = 2 \pi r \, dr$
* Flow through the ring: $dQ = u(r) \cdot dA = u(r) \cdot 2 \pi r \, dr$

$$
Q = \int_{0}^{R} u(r) \cdot 2 \pi r \, dr
$$

$$
Q = \int_{0}^{R} \frac{1}{4 \mu} \left( -\frac{dP}{dz} \right) \left( R^{2} - r^{2} \right) \cdot 2 \pi r \, dr
$$

**Pulling constants out of the integral:**

$$
Q = \frac{2 \pi}{4 \mu} \left( -\frac{dP}{dz} \right) \int_{0}^{R} \left( R^{2} - r^{2} \right) r \, dr
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi}{2 \mu} \left( -\frac{dP}{dz} \right) \int_{0}^{R} \left( R^{2} r - r^{3} \right) dr
$$

**Performing the integration term by term:**

$$
Q = \frac{\pi}{2 \mu} \left( -\frac{dP}{dz} \right) \left[ R^{2} \cdot \frac{r^{2}}{2} - \frac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{R}
$$

**Evaluating at the limits:**

$$
Q = \frac{\pi}{2 \mu} \left( -\frac{dP}{dz} \right) \left( \frac{R^{4}}{2} - \frac{R^{4}}{4} \right)
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi}{2 \mu} \left( -\frac{dP}{dz} \right) \left( \frac{2R^{4} - R^{4}}{4} \right)
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi}{2 \mu} \left( -\frac{dP}{dz} \right) \cdot \frac{R^{4}}{4}
$$

**Final simplification:**

$$
\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi R^{4}}{8 \mu} \left( -\frac{dP}{dz} \right)}
$$

> **This is the HAGEN-POISEUILLE EQUATION in its canonical form.** If the pipe has finite length $L$ between two gauge points where the pressure drops from $P_{1}$ to $P_{2}$, then the pressure gradient becomes constant:

$$
- \frac{dP}{dz} = \frac{P_{1} - P_{2}}{L}
$$

**Substituting this and converting to diameter form ($D = 2R$, $R = D/2$, $R^{4} = D^{4}/16$):**

$$
Q = \frac{\pi (D/2)^{4}}{8 \mu L} \left( P_{1} - P_{2} \right) = \frac{\pi D^{4}}{128 \mu L} \left( P_{1} - P_{2} \right)
$$

$$
\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi D^{4}}{128 \mu L} \left( P_{1} - P_{2} \right)}
$$

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — The Logical Roadmap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Examiner's Verification Checklist:** A student who can fluently reconstruct every row of this table is guaranteed full marks on any derivation-based question.

| Stage | Physical / Mathematical Action | Outcome / Result |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Setup** | Identify the cylindrical control volume, list the forces. | Element of radius $r$, length $dz$. |
| **2. Equilibrium** | Apply $\sum F_{z} = 0$ (steady, fully-developed = no acceleration). | Linear shear-stress distribution: $\tau = (r/2)(-dP/dz)$. |
| **3. Constitutive Law** | Substitute Newton's law of viscosity: $\tau = \mu (du/dr)$. | First-order ODE for $u(r)$. |
| **4. Integration 1** | Integrate once and apply symmetry BC at $r = 0$. | Velocity gradient profile (linear in $r$). |
| **5. Integration 2** | Integrate again and apply no-slip BC at $r = R$. | Parabolic velocity profile: $u(r) = u_{max}(1 - r^{2}/R^{2})$. |
| **6. Cross-section Integration** | Integrate $u(r) \cdot dA$ over the full cross-section. | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4}}{8\mu} \left( -\dfrac{dP}{dz} \right)$. |
| **7. Mean Velocity** | Divide $Q$ by cross-sectional area $A = \pi R^{2}$. | $V_{avg} = u_{max}/2 = R^{2}(-dP/dz)/(8\mu)$. |
| **8. Final Form** | Substitute uniform gradient $-dP/dz = (P_{1}-P_{2})/L$. | Diameter form: $Q = \pi D^{4}(P_{1}-P_{2})/(128\mu L)$. |

## 3.8 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Format)

**Problem:** Oil with dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.1 \text{ Pa} \cdot \text{s}$ and density $\rho = 900 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$ flows through a horizontal pipe of diameter $D = 50 \text{ mm}$ and length $L = 10 \text{ m}$. The pressure drop across the pipe length is $\Delta P = 20 \text{ kPa}$. Calculate:
(a) The volumetric flow rate $Q$.
(b) The mean velocity $V_{avg}$.
(c) The centerline (maximum) velocity $u_{max}$.
(d) The Reynolds number, and verify the laminar assumption.

**Solution:**

**(a) Volumetric flow rate:**

$$
Q = \frac{\pi D^{4}}{128 \mu L} \Delta P
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \times (0.050)^{4}}{128 \times 0.1 \times 10} \times 20{,}000
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-6}}{128} \times 20{,}000
$$

$$
Q = \frac{1.9635 \times 10^{-5}}{128} \times 20{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{Q = 3.068 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s} = 3.068 \text{ L/s}}
$$

**(b) Mean velocity:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.050)^{2}}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
V_{avg} = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{3.068 \times 10^{-3}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{avg} = 1.5625 \text{ m/s}}
$$

**(c) Centerline maximum velocity:**

$$
u_{max} = 2 \times V_{avg} = 2 \times 1.5625
$$

$$
\boxed{u_{max} = 3.125 \text{ m/s}}
$$

**(d) Reynolds number verification:**

$$
Re = \frac{\rho V_{avg} D}{\mu} = \frac{900 \times 1.5625 \times 0.050}{0.1}
$$

$$
Re = \frac{70.3125}{0.1} = 703.125
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Check:** Since $Re = 703.125 < 2000$, the flow is **indeed laminar**, and the Hagen-Poiseuille equation is rigorously valid for this problem. The KTU 2024 scheme examiner will **deduct 1 mark** if this Reynolds number check is omitted.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — The Hagen-Poiseuille Derivation Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **logical pipeline** of the derivation, showing how physical assumptions translate into mathematical consequences:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Assumptions] --> B[Steady State]
    A --> C[Laminar Flow]
    A --> D[Newtonian Fluid]
    A --> E[Fully Developed]
    A --> F[Incompressible]
    B --> G[Apply Newtons Second Law]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Force Balance on Cylindrical Element]
    H --> I[Linear Shear Stress Distribution]
    I --> J[Substitute Newtons Viscosity Law]
    J --> K[First Integration: du over dr]
    K --> L[Apply BC1: Symmetry at r 0]
    L --> M[Second Integration: u over r]
    M --> N[Apply BC2: No Slip at r R]
    N --> O[Parabolic Velocity Profile u of r]
    O --> P[Integrate over Cross Section]
    P --> Q[Hagen Poiseuille Equation for Q]
    Q --> R[Derive Mean Velocity V avg]
    R --> S[Derive Centerline Max Velocity u max]
    S --> T[Convert to Diameter Form]
    T --> U[Final Result Q equals pi D to power 4 over 128 mu L times delta P]
```

## 4.2 Free-Body Diagram Representation — Forces on the Cylindrical Fluid Element

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **three forces** acting on the cylindrical fluid element (replacing physical drawing with a structured block-level representation):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LeftFace[Left Face of Element at z]
        L1[Pressure Force equals P times pi r squared]
        L2[Direction: Positive z, Pushing Forward]
    end
    subgraph RightFace[Right Face of Element at z plus dz]
        R1[Pressure Force equals P plus dP times pi r squared]
        R2[Direction: Negative z, Pushing Backward]
    end
    subgraph CurvedSurface[Curved Lateral Surface at radius r]
        C1[Viscous Shear Force equals tau times 2 pi r dz]
        C2[Direction: Negative z, Resisting Flow]
    end
    LeftFace --> NetForce
    RightFace --> NetForce
    CurvedSurface --> NetForce
    NetForce[Net Axial Force equals 0] --> Result[Linear Shear Stress tau equals r over 2 times minus dP over dz]
```

## 4.3 Velocity Profile Cross-Sectional Schematic (Textual Representation)

While Mermaid cannot directly render a 3-D paraboloid, the following **block-level flow representation** captures the velocity distribution logic across the pipe diameter:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PipeDiameter[Pipe Cross Section Diameter D from minus R to plus R]
        direction LR
        N1[Wall at r equals minus R: u equals 0 m per s] --> N2[r equals minus 3R over 4: u equals 7 u max over 16]
        N2 --> N3[r equals minus R over 2: u equals 3 u max over 4]
        N3 --> N4[r equals 0: u equals u max Maximum]
        N4 --> N5[r equals plus R over 2: u equals 3 u max over 4]
        N5 --> N6[r equals plus 3R over 4: u equals 7 u max over 16]
        N6 --> N7[Wall at r equals plus R: u equals 0 m per s]
    end
    PipeDiameter --> Summary[Parabolic Symmetric Distribution: Average equals u max over 2]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Numerical Anchors for the Parabolic Profile:**
> * At $r = 0$: $u = u_{max}$ (100% of maximum)
> * At $r = R/2$: $u = (3/4) \, u_{max}$ (75% of maximum)
> * At $r = (3/4) R$: $u = (7/16) \, u_{max}$ (43.75% of maximum)
> * At $r = R$: $u = 0$ (no-slip condition)

## 4.4 Application Decision Tree — When to Use Hagen-Poiseuille

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Given Flow Problem] --> Q1{Is Flow Steady?}
    Q1 -- No --> Reject1[Do Not Use HP Equation]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is Flow Laminar Re below 2000?}
    Q2 -- No --> Reject2[Use Moody Chart or Colebrook]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Is Fluid Newtonian?}
    Q3 -- No --> Reject3[Use Power Law or Non Newtonian Model]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Is Pipe Geometry Circular and Constant Diameter?}
    Q4 -- No --> Reject4[Use Duct Flow Correlations]
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5{Is Flow Fully Developed?}
    Q5 -- No --> Reject5[Include Entrance Length Effects]
    Q5 -- Yes --> Apply[Use Hagen Poiseuille Equation]
    Apply --> Output[Compute Q, V avg, u max, Pressure Drop]
```
