topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Water Hammer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — WATER HAMMER: A KTU 2024 SCHEME STUDY UNIT

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Water Hammer (Hydraulic Transient)**: A transient pressure surge phenomenon occurring in a closed conduit (penstock or pipeline) when the steady-state flow conditions are suddenly altered — typically due to the rapid closure (or opening) of a valve, sudden pump failure, or turbine wicket-gate closure. The kinetic energy of the moving fluid column is converted into elastic strain energy in the fluid and pipe wall, generating a high-intensity pressure wave that propagates upstream at the **celerity of the pressure wave** (typically **1000 m/s** to **1200 m/s** in metallic pipes).

The phenomenon is governed by the coupled elasticity of the fluid (bulk modulus) and the pipe wall (Young's modulus), and is fundamentally a 1-D unsteady compressible flow problem described by the **Joukowsky equation**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Train of Water"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy (For First-Time Learners)**
> Imagine a long freight train (the water column inside the penstock) moving at high speed toward a closed gate (the valve). If the gate is slammed shut instantaneously, the locomotive has no time to brake — it crashes into the gate with tremendous momentum. This momentum must go *somewhere*, so the engine's kinetic energy momentarily compresses the couplers between cars and the gate itself. The "compression" then ricochets backward through the train as a shock wave.
> In a penstock, the train cars are water molecules, the couplers are fluid elasticity, and the gate is the turbine valve. The compressive shock wave manifests as a **dangerous pressure spike** — a *water hammer*.

### 1.3 Why Water Hammer Matters in KTU Syllabus

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Context (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2)**
> Water Hammer is a **high-weightage, derivation-heavy topic** that directly maps to **Course Outcome CO2**: "Apply the principles of fluid mechanics to analyze transient pipe flow and surge control systems." Questions typically combine the **Joukowsky equation**, **celerity computation**, and **surge tank oscillations**. Memorizing formulas alone is insufficient — KTU examiners expect dimensional rigour, justification of assumptions, and clear identification of *sudden* vs *gradual* closure cases.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants (Standard Values for KTU Problems)

| Constant | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Bulk modulus of water | $K$ | **2.07 × 10⁹** | N/m² |
| Young's modulus of steel | $E$ | **2.0 × 10¹¹** | N/m² |
| Density of water | $\rho$ | **1000** | kg/m³ |
| Wave celerity (steel penstock) | $C$ | **1000 – 1200** | m/s |
| Wave celerity (concrete/AC pipe) | $C$ | **600 – 900** | m/s |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Stages of a Water Hammer Event**

1. **Steady Flow Phase** — Water flows at velocity $V$ through a penstock of length $L$ and diameter $D$ under a head $H$. The fluid is treated as a rigid column (quasi-steady assumption valid for low-frequency events only).
2. **Disturbance Initiation** — A valve at the downstream end is closed in time $t_c$. If $t_c < \frac{2L}{C}$, the closure is *sudden*; otherwise it is *gradual* (where $C$ = wave celerity).
3. **Pressure Wave Propagation** — A high-pressure wave of magnitude $\Delta P$ travels upstream at celerity $C$. Simultaneously, the pipe wall expands elastically, and the fluid compresses — both effects absorbing kinetic energy.
4. **Wave Reflection & Attenuation** — The wave reflects at the reservoir (open end, pressure returns to hydrostatic) and at the closed valve (pressure doubles), gradually attenuating through fluid friction and pipe-wall hysteresis until steady state resumes.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Stage

- **Why compressibility matters**: At low speeds, water behaves as incompressible. However, when a *sudden* valve closure occurs, the deceleration is so abrupt that the local fluid column must compress — this is what stores the kinetic energy and creates the pressure spike.
- **Why pipe elasticity matters**: A rigid pipe would force *all* the kinetic energy into fluid compression, producing a much higher pressure rise. An elastic pipe *shares* the strain energy with the pipe wall, reducing the surge magnitude.
- **Why celerity is finite**: The disturbance cannot propagate instantaneously; it travels as an elastic wave. The combined elasticity of fluid and pipe determines this propagation speed.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Equations for KTU 2024 ESE — Water Hammer problems will almost certainly use at least three of these.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Celerity of pressure wave | $C = \dfrac{\sqrt{K/\rho}}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{KD}{Et}}}$ | $K, \rho, D, E, t$ | All pipe materials |
| 2 | Celerity (water + steel, simplified) | $C \approx \dfrac{9900}{\sqrt{48.3 + K D / E t}}$ | (BWS formula) | When $K$ and $E$ in psi, $D, t$ in inches |
| 3 | Joukowsky pressure rise (instantaneous closure) | $\Delta P = \rho C V$ | $\rho, C, V$ | $t_c < \dfrac{2L}{C}$ |
| 4 | Joukowsky head rise | $\Delta H = \dfrac{C V}{g}$ | $C, V, g$ | Instantaneous closure |
| 5 | Pressure rise (gradual closure) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{\rho L V}{t_c} = \dfrac{2 \rho L V}{T_p}$ | $L, V, t_c, T_p$ | $t_c > \dfrac{2L}{C}$ |
| 6 | Critical time | $t_c = \dfrac{2L}{C}$ | $L, C$ | Boundary between cases |
| 7 | Pipe period | $T_p = \dfrac{4L}{C}$ | $L, C$ | Full round-trip of wave |
| 8 | Simple surge tank — ODE | $\dfrac{d^{2} z}{d t^{2}} + \dfrac{g A_{1}}{L_{1} A_{s}} z = 0$ | $z, A_1, A_s, L_1$ | Frictionless, no inflow change |
| 9 | Surge tank — time period | $T = 2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{L_{1} A_{s}}{g A_{1}}}$ | $L_1, A_s, A_1$ | Simple (cylindrical) tank |
| 10 | Surge tank — max upsurge | $z_{\max} = V_{0} \sqrt{\dfrac{L_{1} A_{s}}{g A_{1}}}$ | $V_0, L_1, A_s, A_1$ | Mass-oscillation method |
| 11 | Surge tank — min downsurge | $z_{\min} = -V_{0} \sqrt{\dfrac{L_{1} A_{s}}{g A_{1}}}$ | $V_0, L_1, A_s, A_1$ | Symmetric oscillation |

### Notation Guide
- $A_1$ = cross-sectional area of pipe
- $A_s$ = cross-sectional area of surge tank
- $D$ = pipe diameter
- $E$ = Young's modulus of pipe material
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity
- $K$ = bulk modulus of fluid
- $L$ = length of pipe
- $L_1$ = length of pipe upstream of surge tank
- $t$ = pipe wall thickness
- $t_c$ = valve closure time
- $T_p$ = pipe period
- $V$ = flow velocity
- $z$ = surge tank water-level deviation from reservoir level

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Where This System/Equation is Used in Practice**

- **Hydroelectric Power Plants** — Surge tanks and pressure relief valves are mandatory design elements upstream of the turbine to prevent penstock rupture during load rejection.
- **Municipal Water Distribution** — Surge analysis is required for long transmission mains; otherwise sudden pump trips can cause pipe bursts and "water hammer" thuds audible to consumers.
- **Oil & Gas Pipelines** — Surge protection prevents catastrophic failure in cross-country crude oil and refined-product pipelines.
- **Cooling Water Systems in Thermal Power Plants** — Pump-discharge surge analysis is critical to avoid condenser-tube failure.
- **Fire-Fighting Networks** — Quick-opening hydrant valves can cause household plumbing damage if surge is unmitigated.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Celerity of the Pressure Wave in an Elastic Pipe–Fluid System

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumptions (KTU Standard)**
> 1. Pipe is prismatic, horizontal, and of constant wall thickness.
> 2. Fluid is slightly compressible (linear bulk modulus).
> 3. Pipe wall is linearly elastic (Hookean).
> 4. Wave motion is 1-D, frictionless, and adiabatic for rapid transients.

### Step 1: Continuity Equation for a Compressible Fluid in an Elastic Pipe

Consider a control volume of length $\delta x$ in the pipe. Due to fluid compressibility, density changes from $\rho$ to $\rho + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x}\delta x$ across the element. The mass-balance yields:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \rho \frac{\partial V}{\partial x} + V \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x} = 0$$

For small perturbations about the steady state, the convective term $V \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x}$ is second-order and is dropped (linearization):

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \rho \frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = 0$$

### Step 2: Momentum Equation (Euler's Equation, Frictionless)

For a fluid element with pressure gradient $\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}$:

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} + V \frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}$$

Linearizing by dropping the convective acceleration:

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}$$

### Step 3: Introducing Fluid Compressibility (Bulk Modulus)

By definition of the bulk modulus $K$:

$$K = \rho \frac{dP}{d\rho} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d\rho}{\rho} = \frac{dP}{K}$$

So:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = \frac{\rho}{K}\frac{\partial P}{\partial t}$$

### Step 4: Relating Pipe Area Strain to Pressure

For a thin-walled pipe of diameter $D$, wall thickness $t$, and Young's modulus $E$, the hoop-stress-induced diameter change under internal pressure $P$ is:

$$\frac{dD}{D} = \frac{P D}{2 t E}$$

(using Barlow's formula $\sigma = PD/2t$ and $\varepsilon = \sigma/E$). Since the pipe area $A \propto D^{2}$, the area strain is:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = 2 \frac{dD}{D} = \frac{P D}{t E}$$

The bulk modulus of the *combined* fluid–pipe system becomes:

$$\frac{1}{K_{eff}} = \frac{1}{K} + \frac{D}{E t}$$

### Step 5: Combining Continuity and Momentum

From the linearized continuity:

$$\frac{\rho}{K_{eff}} \frac{\partial P}{\partial t} + \rho \frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = 0$$

Differentiating with respect to $x$ and substituting from the linearized momentum:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial t^{2}} = \frac{K_{eff}}{\rho} \frac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial x^{2}}$$

### Step 6: Recognizing the Classical Wave Equation

This is the 1-D wave equation with propagation speed $C$ given by:

$$\boxed{C = \sqrt{\frac{K_{eff}}{\rho}} = \frac{\sqrt{K/\rho}}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{K D}{E t}}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary cases for KTU numerics**
> - **Rigid pipe** ($E \rightarrow \infty$): $C = \sqrt{K/\rho} \approx 1430$ m/s in water.
> - **Very flexible pipe** ($E \rightarrow 0$): $C \rightarrow 0$ (no wave propagation).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Joukowsky Pressure Rise Equation

### Step 1: Apply the Momentum Theorem to the Decelerating Fluid Column

When a valve is closed suddenly, the entire fluid column of length $L$ decelerates. By the unsteady momentum equation (impulse–momentum principle):

$$\Delta P \cdot A = \rho A L \cdot \frac{dV}{dt} = \rho A L \cdot \frac{-V}{t_c}$$

The negative sign indicates deceleration. The pressure rise:

$$\Delta P = \frac{\rho L V}{t_c}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Celerity Relation $L = \dfrac{C \, t_c}{2}$ (for critical/sudden closure)

For *instantaneous* closure ($t_c < t_c^{crit}$), the pressure wave travels to the reservoir and returns during $t_c$, so $L = \dfrac{C \, t_c}{2}$. Substituting:

$$\Delta P = \frac{\rho \, (C t_c / 2) \, V}{t_c} = \frac{\rho C V}{2} \cdot 2 = \rho C V$$

$$\boxed{\Delta H = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho g} = \frac{C V}{g}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Insight**: The Joukowsky pressure rise is *independent of pipe length* in the instantaneous closure case. This is a counter-intuitive but crucial result frequently tested.

### Step 3: Maximum Surge Head for Gradual Closure

If $t_c > t_c^{crit} = \frac{2L}{C}$, the valve is closed *after* the reflected wave returns, and the maximum rise is:

$$\boxed{\Delta H = \frac{2 L V}{g \, t_c} = \frac{L V}{g \, t_c / 2}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Simple Surge Tank Mass-Oscillation Equation

### Step 1: Setup

A surge tank of cross-sectional area $A_s$ is connected to a reservoir through a pipe of length $L_1$ and area $A_1$. The tank water level is at height $z$ above (or below) the reservoir level.

### Step 2: Momentum Equation for the Pipe Column

Applying Newton's second law to the fluid column in the pipe (acceleration $a = \frac{dV_1}{dt}$):

$$P_{reservoir} A_1 - P_{tank} A_1 - \text{friction} = \rho L_1 A_1 \frac{dV_1}{dt}$$

For frictionless flow, the net head driving the column is $z$ (the difference in water levels), giving:

$$\rho g A_1 z = \rho L_1 A_1 \frac{dV_1}{dt}$$

$$z = \frac{L_1}{g} \frac{dV_1}{dt}$$

### Step 3: Continuity Equation for the Surge Tank

The flow entering (or leaving) the tank is the difference between upstream flow $Q_0$ (from reservoir) and downstream flow $Q_1$ (in pipe):

$$Q_0 - Q_1 = A_s \frac{dz}{dt}$$

If $Q_0$ is constant and we linearize $Q_1 = A_1 V_1$:

$$\frac{dz}{dt} = -\frac{A_1}{A_s} V_1$$

Differentiating with respect to $t$:

$$\frac{d^{2} z}{dt^{2}} = -\frac{A_1}{A_s} \frac{dV_1}{dt}$$

### Step 4: Eliminate $V_1$

From the momentum equation: $\frac{dV_1}{dt} = \frac{g z}{L_1}$. Substituting:

$$\frac{d^{2} z}{dt^{2}} = -\frac{A_1}{A_s} \cdot \frac{g z}{L_1} = -\frac{g A_1}{L_1 A_s} z$$

$$\boxed{\frac{d^{2} z}{dt^{2}} + \frac{g A_{1}}{L_{1} A_{s}} z = 0}$$

### Step 5: Solution and Time Period

This is a **simple harmonic motion** equation with angular frequency $\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{g A_1}{L_1 A_s}}$.

The general solution is $z(t) = z_{\max} \cos(\omega t)$, giving:

$$\boxed{T = \frac{2 \pi}{\omega} = 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{L_1 A_s}{g A_1}}}$$

### Step 6: Maximum Upsurge

From the energy/initial-condition method (initial velocity $V_0$, initial $z = 0$), the amplitude of oscillation equals:

$$\boxed{z_{\max} = V_0 \sqrt{\frac{L_1 A_s}{g A_1}}}$$

The **downsurge** magnitude is the same (frictionless case):

$$z_{\min} = -V_0 \sqrt{\frac{L_1 A_s}{g A_1}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Friction correction for KTU problems**: In real systems, friction reduces the amplitude. The downsurge is **greater in magnitude** than the upsurge because friction assists downsurge but opposes upsurge. KTU numericals sometimes ask for the *frictionless* case only — read the question carefully.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Table

| Derivation | Input Given | Output Computed | Key Assumption |
|---|---|---|---|
| Celerity $C$ | $K, \rho, D, E, t$ | Pressure wave speed | Linear elasticity |
| Joukowsky (sudden) | $\rho, C, V$ | $\Delta P = \rho C V$ | $t_c < 2L/C$ |
| Joukowsky (gradual) | $\rho, L, V, t_c$ | $\Delta P = \rho L V / t_c$ | $t_c > 2L/C$ |
| Surge tank ODE | $L_1, A_1, A_s, g$ | $T$ and $z_{\max}$ | Frictionless, constant $Q_0$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Water Hammer Mechanism — Block-Level Functional Flow

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure: Block diagram of the water hammer phenomenon and its consequences in a hydroelectric penstock system.**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reservoir with Steady Head H] --> B[Long Penstock of Length L]
    B --> C[Flow at Velocity V]
    C --> D[Turbine Inlet Valve]
    D --> E{Valve Closure Time t_c}
    E -->|t_c less than 2L by C| F[SUDDEN CLOSURE - Joukowsky Case]
    E -->|t_c greater than 2L by C| G[GRADUAL CLOSURE - Riser Formula]
    F --> H[Pressure Wave Travels Upstream at Celerity C]
    G --> I[Partial Pressure Wave - Reduced Magnitude]
    H --> J[Wave Reflects at Reservoir]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Wave Returns to Valve - Doubles Pressure]
    K --> L{Pressure Magnitude Analysis}
    L --> M[Surge Tank Required for Protection]
    M --> N[Mass Oscillation of Water Column in Tank]
    N --> O[Time Period T and Max Upsurge z_max]
    O --> P[System Stabilizes to New Steady State]
```

## 4.2 Surge Tank System — Functional Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure: Block diagram of a simple cylindrical surge tank and the associated water-level oscillation cycle.**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Reservoir] -->|Constant Q0| nodeB[Headrace Pipe of Length L1 Area A1]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Surge Tank Riser with Area A_s]
    nodeC -->|Oscillates by z| nodeD[Penstock to Turbine]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Turbine Load Change]
    nodeE -.->|Sudden demand change| nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeF[Upward Surge Phase]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Downward Surge Phase]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Oscillation with Period T = 2 pi sqrt of L1 A_s over g A1]
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI[Damped steady state via friction]
```

## 4.3 Pressure Wave Propagation Timeline

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure: Time–space diagram of the water hammer pressure wave reflection pattern.**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    t0[Time t=0 - Valve Closed Suddenly] --> t1[Wave Travels Upstream at Celerity C]
    t1 --> t2[At t=L by C - Wave Reaches Reservoir]
    t2 --> t3[Wave Reflects as Rarefaction - Pressure drops to H]
    t3 --> t4[At t=2L by C - Rarefaction Reaches Closed Valve]
    t4 --> t5[Wave Reflects as Positive Surge - Pressure rises to H + delta H]
    t5 --> t6[At t=4L by C - One Full Pipe Period T_p Completed]
    t6 --> t7[Oscillation continues with attenuation]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Summary (Text Diagram)

For KTU board exam drawings, a clean schematic is often required. Here is a text-rendered equivalent:

```
    Reservoir Level
    ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
        |   |              |  |
        |   |              |  |
        |   |  Penstock    |  |  Surge Tank
        |   |     ======== |  |  |  |
        |   |     |      | |  |  |  |
        |   |     |      | |  |  |  |
        |   |     |      | |  |  |  |  <-- z(t) water level
        |   |     |      | |  |  |  |       oscillation
        |   |     |      | |  |  |  |
        |   |     |      | |  |  |  |
        |   |     |      | |  |  |  |
        ======   ======  =================
         Tunnel   Pipe   Surge Tank   Turbine
         (L1)     (L)    (A_s)        Valve
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Define water hammer. What is the difference between *sudden* and *gradual* closure cases?**

**Model Answer (Memorize the Core Statement):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Water Hammer**: It is the phenomenon of a transient pressure surge produced in a pipe when the flow velocity is suddenly changed, causing a steep pressure wave to propagate along the pipe at the celerity $C$.

**Difference Table:**

| Aspect | Sudden Closure | Gradual Closure |
|---|---|---|
| Closure time $t_c$ | $t_c < 2L / C$ | $t_c > 2L / C$ |
| Pressure rise formula | $\Delta P = \rho C V$ | $\Delta P = \rho L V / t_c$ |
| Dependence on $L$ | Independent | Proportional to $L$ |
| Wave reflection | Single pulse dominates | Multiple reflections average out |

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**What is a surge tank? Why is it provided in a hydroelectric power plant?**

**Model Answer:**

> A surge tank is a small reservoir (open or closed) installed at the downstream end of the headrace tunnel and upstream of the penstock in a hydroelectric scheme.
>
> **Functions**:
> 1. **Absorbs pressure surges** caused by sudden turbine valve closure.
> 2. **Improves regulation** by providing a water reserve close to the turbine.
> 3. **Reduces the length of the penstock** that is subject to water hammer effects.
> 4. **Decouples the headrace tunnel** from the fast penstock transients, allowing the long tunnel to remain in steady flow.

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** *Derive the Joukowsky equation for pressure rise due to sudden closure of a valve in a pipe. State all assumptions clearly.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *A steel penstock of length $L = 1500$ m and diameter $D = 1.5$ m carries water at a steady velocity of $V = 4$ m/s. The pipe wall thickness is $t = 25$ mm. Take $E = 2 \times 10^{11}$ N/m² and $K = 2.07 \times 10^{9}$ N/m². Determine:*
  *(i) The celerity of the pressure wave.*
  *(ii) The critical time of closure.*
  *(iii) The maximum rise in pressure head if the valve is closed in 2.5 seconds.* **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) — Joukowsky Derivation (7 Marks)

**Assumptions**:
1. The pipe is horizontal, prismatic, and of constant diameter.
2. The fluid is compressible with bulk modulus $K$; the pipe wall is elastic with Young's modulus $E$.
3. The valve closure is *instantaneous* (or fast enough that $t_c < 2L/C$).
4. The flow is 1-D, frictionless, and adiabatic.

**Derivation**:
Apply the **unsteady impulse–momentum theorem** to the entire fluid column of length $L$ and area $A$:

$$\text{Impulse} = \text{Change in momentum}$$

$$(\Delta P) A \, t_c = \rho A L (V - 0)$$

where $\Delta P$ is the pressure rise, $V$ is the initial velocity, and $t_c$ is the closure time. So:

$$\Delta P = \frac{\rho L V}{t_c}$$

For *sudden* closure, the wave travels to the reservoir and back during $t_c$, so $L = C t_c / 2$. Substituting:

$$\Delta P = \frac{\rho (C t_c / 2) V}{t_c} = \rho C V$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P = \rho C V \quad \text{or} \quad \Delta H = \frac{C V}{g}}$$

##### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given**:
$L = 1500$ m, $D = 1.5$ m, $V = 4$ m/s, $t = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m, $E = 2 \times 10^{11}$ N/m², $K = 2.07 \times 10^{9}$ N/m², $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $t_c = 2.5$ s.

**(i) Celerity**:

$$\frac{K D}{E t} = \frac{2.07 \times 10^{9} \times 1.5}{2 \times 10^{11} \times 0.025} = \frac{3.105 \times 10^{9}}{5 \times 10^{9}} = 0.621$$

$$\sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.07 \times 10^{9}}{1000}} = \sqrt{2.07 \times 10^{6}} = 1438.96 \text{ m/s}$$

$$C = \frac{1438.96}{\sqrt{1 + 0.621}} = \frac{1438.96}{\sqrt{1.621}} = \frac{1438.96}{1.2732}$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 1130.16 \text{ m/s}}$$

**(ii) Critical time**:

$$t_c^{crit} = \frac{2L}{C} = \frac{2 \times 1500}{1130.16} = \frac{3000}{1130.16}$$

$$\boxed{t_c^{crit} \approx 2.654 \text{ s}}$$

**(iii) Maximum rise in pressure head**:
Since $t_c = 2.5$ s $< t_c^{crit} = 2.654$ s, the closure is *sudden*. Use Joukowsky:

$$\Delta H = \frac{C V}{g} = \frac{1130.16 \times 4}{9.81} = \frac{4520.64}{9.81}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta H \approx 460.82 \text{ m of water}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Water Hammer)**
> - **Common Error 1**: Forgetting to compare $t_c$ with $t_c^{crit}$ — if $t_c > t_c^{crit}$, the Joukowsky formula is *invalid*; you must use the gradual closure formula $\Delta H = 2 L V / (g t_c)$.
> - **Common Error 2**: Mixing up the wave celerity (which uses $K$ and $E$) with the fluid bulk speed ($\sqrt{K/\rho} \approx 1430$ m/s). Always check the assumption of *rigid* vs *elastic* pipe.
> - **Common Error 3**: Reporting $\Delta H$ in Pa instead of metres of water. KTU wants $\Delta H = \Delta P / (\rho g)$, in *metres*.
> - **Common Error 4**: Losing 1–2 marks for not stating the assumptions before the derivation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** *Derive the differential equation for water-level oscillation in a simple cylindrical surge tank. Hence obtain expressions for the time period and maximum upsurge.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *A surge tank of diameter 6 m is connected to a headrace pipe of length 800 m and diameter 3 m. The flow velocity in the headrace pipe is 2.5 m/s. Calculate:*
  *(i) The natural time period of oscillation.*
  *(ii) The maximum upsurge of water in the tank.*
  *(iii) Comment on the suitability of a simple surge tank in this case.* **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) — Surge Tank ODE Derivation (7 Marks)

**Setup**: Pipe of length $L_1$, area $A_1$, carries flow $V_1$. Tank area $A_s$, water-level deviation $z$ from reservoir level. Constant inflow $Q_0$ from reservoir.

**Momentum equation** (frictionless) on the pipe column:

$$\frac{L_1}{g} \frac{dV_1}{dt} = z$$

**Continuity equation** at the tank:

$$A_1 V_1 + A_s \frac{dz}{dt} = Q_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dz}{dt} = -\frac{A_1}{A_s} V_1$$

Differentiating the second equation with respect to $t$ and substituting the first:

$$\frac{d^2 z}{dt^2} = -\frac{A_1}{A_s} \frac{dV_1}{dt} = -\frac{A_1}{A_s} \cdot \frac{g z}{L_1}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{d^{2} z}{dt^{2}} + \frac{g A_{1}}{L_{1} A_{s}} z = 0}$$

This is a SHM equation with $\omega^{2} = \dfrac{g A_1}{L_1 A_s}$.

**Time period**:

$$\boxed{T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega} = 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{L_1 A_s}{g A_1}}}$$

**Maximum upsurge** (using initial conditions $z(0)=0$, $V_1(0) = V_0$, i.e. $\dot z(0) = -A_1 V_0 / A_s$):

From the SHM solution $z(t) = z_{\max} \sin(\omega t)$:

$$\dot z(0) = \omega z_{\max} = \frac{A_1 V_0}{A_s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad z_{\max} = \frac{A_1 V_0}{A_s \omega} = \frac{A_1 V_0}{A_s} \sqrt{\frac{L_1 A_s}{g A_1}}$$

$$\boxed{z_{\max} = V_0 \sqrt{\frac{L_1 A_s}{g A_1}}}$$

##### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given**: $D_s = 6$ m, $L_1 = 800$ m, $D_1 = 3$ m, $V_0 = 2.5$ m/s, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Areas**:

$$A_s = \frac{\pi}{4} (6)^2 = 28.274 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} (3)^2 = 7.069 \text{ m}^2$$

**(i) Time period**:

$$T = 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{L_1 A_s}{g A_1}} = 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{800 \times 28.274}{9.81 \times 7.069}}$$

$$\frac{L_1 A_s}{g A_1} = \frac{22619.47}{69.345} = 326.20 \text{ s}^2$$

$$T = 2 \pi \sqrt{326.20} = 2 \pi \times 18.06$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 113.45 \text{ s} \approx 1.89 \text{ min}}$$

**(ii) Maximum upsurge**:

$$z_{\max} = V_0 \sqrt{\frac{L_1 A_s}{g A_1}} = 2.5 \times 18.06$$

$$\boxed{z_{\max} \approx 45.16 \text{ m}}$$

**(iii) Comment on suitability**:
A 45 m upsurge is **excessively high** — the required tank would be impractically tall. A simple cylindrical surge tank is **not suitable** in this case. The recommended alternatives are:
- A **differential (Johnson-type) surge tank**, which damps oscillations rapidly.
- An **orifice-type restricted surge tank**, which dissipates energy through the restriction.
- A **multiple surge tank** system to share the surge volume.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Surge Tank)**
> - **Common Error 1**: Forgetting to convert $D$ to $A$ correctly. KTU gives diameter, not area — always convert first.
> - **Common Error 2**: Using the headrace length $L_1$ as the *penstock* length. The headrace is upstream of the surge tank; the penstock is downstream. The surge tank ODE uses the **headrace length**.
> - **Common Error 3**: Stating that $z_{\max}$ is the *total tank height*. It is only the deviation above the reservoir level — the *total height* is $H_{reservoir} + z_{\max}$.
> - **Common Error 4**: Not commenting on the practical suitability when the numerical produces a very large $z_{\max}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2, Water Hammer**

- ✅ **Water hammer** is a transient pressure surge due to rapid flow-velocity change in a closed conduit.
- ✅ **Celerity** $C = \dfrac{\sqrt{K/\rho}}{\sqrt{1 + K D / (E t)}}$ — the *single most important formula* in this module.
- ✅ **Critical time** $t_c^{crit} = 2L/C$; **pipe period** $T_p = 4L/C$.
- ✅ **Sudden closure** ($t_c < 2L/C$): $\Delta P = \rho C V$ — **independent of pipe length**.
- ✅ **Gradual closure** ($t_c > 2L/C$): $\Delta P = \rho L V / t_c$ — **proportional to pipe length**.
- ✅ Always **compare $t_c$ with $2L/C$ before selecting the formula** — KTU examiners test this.
- ✅ **Joukowsky pressure rise** has no length-dependence because the wave reflects from the reservoir before the valve is fully closed.
- ✅ **Surge tank ODE** is a **simple harmonic motion** — derived from momentum + continuity.
- ✅ **Time period** of surge tank: $T = 2\pi \sqrt{L_1 A_s / (g A_1)}$.
- ✅ **Max upsurge** in frictionless case: $z_{\max} = V_0 \sqrt{L_1 A_s / (g A_1)}$.
- ✅ **Downsurge magnitude equals upsurge** in frictionless case; friction makes downsurge larger.
- ✅ **Simple surge tank** is unsuitable for large $z_{\max}$ — use **differential or restricted** type.
- ✅ Standard values to memorize: $K_{water} = 2.07 \times 10^9$ N/m², $E_{steel} = 2 \times 10^{11}$ N/m², $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- ✅ **1-D wave equation** is the underlying PDE; all formulas are derived from its special solutions.
- ✅ For a *rigid pipe* approximation, use $C = \sqrt{K/\rho} \approx 1430$ m/s as a quick estimate.
- ✅ **Assumptions** to always state in derivations: frictionless, 1-D, linear elasticity, adiabatic for rapid transients.
- ✅ **KTU's favourite trap**: asking you to compute both upsurge and downsurge and asking for the *frictionless* condition — if friction is mentioned, the amplitude is *reduced*, not increased.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transmission of Power through Pipes and Related Practical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Transmission of power through pipes** (also called *hydraulic power transmission*) is the process of conveying mechanical energy from one location to another by means of a fluid (usually water or oil) flowing under pressure through a closed conduit or pipeline. The head difference between the source and the delivery point drives the flow, and the power delivered at the downstream end is utilized to run turbines, hydraulic machines, or industrial equipment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Students must master the **derivation of the condition for maximum power transmission**, the **expression for the economic diameter of the pipe**, and the **efficiency of hydraulic power transmission**. These form the backbone of any 14-mark Part B question in the University ESE.

The key physical quantities involved are:
- **Head difference (H):** Net available head between source and delivery point, in **metres (m)**
- **Discharge (Q):** Volume of fluid flowing per unit time, in **m³/s**
- **Frictional head loss (hf):** Energy dissipated due to pipe friction, in **m**
- **Power transmitted (P):** Useful hydraulic power delivered, in **watts (W)**
- **Friction factor (f):** Dimensionless coefficient (often 0.005 to 0.01 for turbulent flow in commercial pipes)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine two large water tanks placed at different elevations and connected by a long garden hose. If you open the valve at the lower end, water rushes out with a strong jet — this jet can be made to spin a small Pelton wheel or a turbine, doing useful work. This is **hydraulic power transmission in its simplest form**.

In a real engineering setting, think of:
- A **hydroelectric penstock** in Idukki or Mullaperiyar dam, where water from a high-elevation reservoir rushes down a long steel pipe to spin turbines
- An **oil pipeline in Kochi Refinery**, where pressurized oil is transported to drive hydraulic presses in the plant
- A **city water supply main** where pressure energy is tapped to power booster pumps

The trick lies in this: **as discharge Q increases, friction loss also increases** (since hf ∝ Q²). So there is a *sweet spot* — a particular discharge at which the power delivered at the pipe outlet is *maximum*. Pushing more flow actually *reduces* the delivered power beyond this optimum. This is the famous **condition for maximum power transmission** that we will derive rigorously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study this in KTU Module 2?**
> Every hydraulic structure — from penstocks to cooling water mains — is designed using these principles. If the pipe is too small, friction wastes most of the energy; if it is too large, capital cost is wasted. The KTU examiner loves to test the student's ability to balance these two competing effects.

## 1.3 Physical Setup — Schematic Description

The classical configuration consists of:

1. **Source reservoir** at elevation H₁
2. **Delivery reservoir** (or tailrace) at elevation H₂ (with H₁ − H₂ = H)
3. **Long pipe of length L and diameter D** connecting the two
4. **Power take-off point** at the downstream end of the pipe

The total head H supplied by gravity is partly lost to friction (hf) and the remainder is available for useful work (H − hf).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Step-by-Step Logic

The analysis of power transmission through pipes follows a structured logical sequence. Each step builds on the previous one.

**Step 1 — Identify the source of energy:**
The total head **H** available between the source and delivery reservoir is the gross energy per unit weight of fluid. Multiplying by weight discharge ρgQ gives the gross power supplied.

**Step 2 — Account for friction losses:**
As fluid traverses the pipe, energy is lost to wall friction. Using the **Darcy–Weisbach equation**, this head loss is:

$$h_f = \frac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D}$$

where V is the mean flow velocity. Substituting V = Q/A = 4Q/(πD²), we get hf as a function of Q:

$$h_f = \frac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$$

**Step 3 — Define the power delivered:**
The *useful* power at the downstream end of the pipe is the net head available (H − hf) multiplied by the weight discharge ρgQ:

$$P = \rho g Q (H - h_f)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute hf as a function of Q:**
Let **K = 32fL/(π²gD⁵)**, so hf = KQ². Then:

$$P = \rho g Q (H - K Q^2) = \rho g (HQ - K Q^3)$$

**Step 5 — Optimize using calculus (dP/dQ = 0):**

$$\frac{dP}{dQ} = \rho g (H - 3 K Q^2) = 0$$

This yields **3KQ² = H**, i.e., **hf = H/3**. This is the celebrated *condition for maximum power transmission*.

**Step 6 — Compute maximum power and efficiency:**

$$P_{max} = \rho g Q \left( H - \frac{H}{3} \right) = \frac{2}{3} \rho g Q H$$

Efficiency is the ratio of useful head to total head:

$$\eta = \frac{H - h_f}{H} = \frac{H - H/3}{H} = \frac{2}{3} = 66.67\%$$

**Step 7 — Diameter for maximum power transmission:**
Setting hf = H/3 in the friction equation:

$$\frac{H}{3} = \frac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$$

Solving for D:

$$D = \left( \frac{96 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g H} \right)^{1/5}$$

This diameter is called the **economical diameter** because it minimizes the *combined cost* of (a) pipe material and (b) power lost to friction.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is your *ready-reckoner* for KTU Board exams. Memorize every row, especially the highlighted condition **hf = H/3**.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | KTU Frequency |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | Discharge velocity | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | m/s | High |
| 2 | Frictional head loss (Darcy–Weisbach) | $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D}$ | m | Very High |
| 3 | Friction loss in terms of Q | $h_f = \dfrac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$ | m | Very High |
| 4 | Power transmitted | $P = \rho g Q (H - h_f)$ | W (or kW) | Very High |
| 5 | Gross power supplied | $P_{gross} = \rho g Q H$ | W | Medium |
| 6 | Power lost to friction | $P_{lost} = \rho g Q h_f$ | W | Medium |
| 7 | **Condition for maximum power** | $h_f = \dfrac{H}{3}$ | m | **CRITICAL** |
| 8 | **Maximum power transmitted** | $P_{max} = \dfrac{2}{3} \rho g Q H$ | W | **CRITICAL** |
| 9 | **Efficiency at max power** | $\eta = \dfrac{2}{3} \approx 66.67\%$ | % | **CRITICAL** |
| 10 | **Economic diameter** | $D = \left( \dfrac{96 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g H} \right)^{1/5}$ | m | **CRITICAL** |
| 11 | Reynolds number check | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi D \nu}$ | — | High |
| 12 | Chezy formula (alternative) | $V = C \sqrt{m i}$, with $h_f = \dfrac{V^2 L}{C^2 R}$ | m/s | Medium |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This theory is the workhorse of:
- **Hydroelectric penstock design** — to size the pipe that carries water from the reservoir to the turbine
- **Industrial hydraulic power networks** — for transmission of oil at high pressure to operate hydraulic presses, lifts, and machine tools
- **Long-distance water supply mains** — Kerala Water Authority trunk mains between treatment plants and distribution zones
- **Pumping main design** — selecting the optimum pipe size to balance pump running cost vs. pipe capital cost

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The result η = 66.67% is a *theoretical upper limit* achievable only when the pipe is sized for maximum power. In practice, additional losses (bends, valves, sudden contractions, entrance/exit losses) reduce efficiency further to 50–60%.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Condition for Maximum Power Transmission

We start with the power delivered at the downstream end of a long pipe connecting two reservoirs.

**Step 1 — Write the power equation:**
The power delivered is the product of weight discharge (ρgQ) and the net head available at the pipe outlet (H − hf):

$$P = \rho g Q (H - h_f)$$

**Step 2 — Express hf in terms of Q using Darcy–Weisbach:**

$$h_f = \frac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D}$$

Substituting $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$:

$$V^2 = \frac{16 Q^2}{\pi^2 D^4}$$

Therefore:

$$h_f = \frac{4 f L}{2 g D} \cdot \frac{16 Q^2}{\pi^2 D^4} = \frac{64 f L Q^2}{2 g \pi^2 D^5} = \frac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$$

**Step 3 — Combine constants:**
Let $K = \dfrac{32 f L}{\pi^2 g D^5}$, so $h_f = K Q^2$.

The power becomes:

$$P = \rho g Q (H - K Q^2) = \rho g (H Q - K Q^3)$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate P with respect to Q and equate to zero:**

$$\frac{dP}{dQ} = \rho g \left( H - 3 K Q^2 \right) = 0$$

$$H - 3 K Q^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 3 K Q^2 = H$$

**Step 5 — Recognize that KQ² = hf, hence:**

$$3 h_f = H \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{h_f = \frac{H}{3}}$$

**Step 6 — Verify it is a maximum (second derivative test):**

$$\frac{d^2 P}{dQ^2} = \rho g (-6 K Q) = -6 \rho g K Q < 0$$

Since the second derivative is negative, the critical point is indeed a **maximum**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Diameter for Maximum Power Transmission

**Step 1 — Begin with the condition for max power, hf = H/3:**

$$\frac{H}{3} = \frac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-multiply:**

$$H \cdot \pi^2 g D^5 = 3 \times 32 f L Q^2 = 96 f L Q^2$$

**Step 3 — Isolate D⁵:**

$$D^5 = \frac{96 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g H}$$

**Step 4 — Take the fifth root:**

$$\boxed{D = \left( \frac{96 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g H} \right)^{1/5}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Power and Efficiency

**Step 1 — Maximum power delivered:**
At the optimum condition, hf = H/3, so net head = H − H/3 = 2H/3:

$$P_{max} = \rho g Q \left( \frac{2H}{3} \right) = \frac{2}{3} \rho g Q H$$

**Step 2 — Efficiency of transmission:**

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Useful head}}{\text{Total head}} = \frac{H - h_f}{H} = \frac{H - H/3}{H} = \frac{2H/3}{H} = \frac{2}{3}$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 66.67\%}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> The following worked example demonstrates the application of every key formula. KTU examiners expect a similar step-by-step presentation in the answer script.

**Problem Statement:**
A pipeline **15 km long** carries oil (specific gravity = 0.85, kinematic viscosity ν = 0.9 × 10⁻⁴ m²/s) between two reservoirs with a head difference of **50 m**. The friction factor is **f = 0.008**. Find: (a) the diameter for maximum power transmission, (b) the maximum power transmitted, and (c) the corresponding efficiency.

**Given Data:**
- L = 15,000 m
- S = 0.85 ⇒ ρ = 850 kg/m³
- ν = 0.9 × 10⁻⁴ m²/s
- H = 50 m
- f = 0.008

*(Note: This problem has TWO unknowns Q and D in the friction equation, and a third equation from the Reynolds number correction on f. For simplicity at KTU level, f is assumed constant and given — which allows the diameter to be found for an assumed Q, OR the problem is stated in terms of an additional given such as flow velocity or discharge.)*

**Assumption for this part:** Let the discharge Q = 0.05 m³/s.

### Sub-Question (a): Diameter for Maximum Power Transmission

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$D = \left( \frac{96 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g H} \right)^{1/5}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$D = \left( \frac{96 \times 0.008 \times 15000 \times (0.05)^2}{\pi^2 \times 9.81 \times 50} \right)^{1/5}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator:**

$$96 \times 0.008 \times 15000 \times 0.0025 = 28.8$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator:**

$$\pi^2 \times 9.81 \times 50 = 9.8696 \times 9.81 \times 50 = 4841.04$$

**Step 5 — Form the ratio:**

$$D^5 = \frac{28.8}{4841.04} = 5.949 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^5$$

**Step 6 — Take the fifth root:**

$$D = (5.949 \times 10^{-3})^{1/5} = 0.3579 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 0.358 \text{ m} = 358 \text{ mm}}$$

### Sub-Question (b): Maximum Power Transmitted

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$P_{max} = \frac{2}{3} \rho g Q H$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$P_{max} = \frac{2}{3} \times 850 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 50$$

**Step 3 — Compute step by step:**

$$P_{max} = \frac{2}{3} \times 20846.25 = 13897.5 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{max} \approx 13.90 \text{ kW}}$$

### Sub-Question (c): Efficiency

$$\boxed{\eta = 66.67\%}$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Architecture — Power Transmission Through Pipe

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below renders the *functional architecture* of a hydraulic power transmission system, mapping the energy flow from source reservoir to the turbine / power take-off point.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["RESERVOIR A\n(Head H1)"] --> B["PIPE INLET\nEntrance Loss negligible"]
    B --> C["LONG PIPE\nLength L, Diameter D"]
    C --> D["FRICTION ZONE\nhf = 32fLQ^2 divided by pi^2gD^5"]
    D --> E["PIPE OUTLET\nNet Head = H minus hf"]
    E --> F["TURBINE OR\nPOWER LOAD"]
    F --> G["DELIVERY RESERVOIR\nor TAILRACE (Head H2)"]
    H["TOTAL HEAD H = H1 minus H2"] -.-> C
    I["OPTIMUM CONDITION\nhf equals H divided by 3"] -.-> D
    J["EFFICIENCY 66.67 percent"] -.-> F
    styleA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    styleH fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806600
    styleI fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#804000
    styleJ fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- The yellow box marks the *total head H* available between the two reservoirs.
- The orange box marks the *optimum friction condition* (hf = H/3) at which maximum power is delivered.
- The green box marks the *theoretical efficiency ceiling* of 66.67% achievable at this optimum.
- Energy flows from the high-head reservoir through the pipe, loses some energy to friction (D), and the remainder powers the turbine (F) before discharging into the tailrace.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Power vs Discharge Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K["Discharge Q starts to flow"] --> L["Compute hf equals K times Q squared"]
    L --> M{"Is hf less than H by 3 ?"}
    M -- Yes --> N["Power P is increasing with Q\ndP by dQ greater than 0"]
    M -- No --> P["Power P is decreasing with Q\ndP by dQ less than 0"]
    N --> Q["Approach Maximum Power Point\nhf approaches H by 3"]
    Q --> R["MAXIMUM POWER POINT\nhf equals H by 3\nEta equals 66.67 percent"]
    P --> R
    R --> S["If Q increases further\nhf exceeds H by 3\nNet head goes negative\nPower becomes negative"]
    styleK fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064
    styleR fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:3px
    styleS fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- Initially, as Q increases from zero, the power delivered rises (blue region).
- It peaks at the **Maximum Power Point** (green box) where hf = H/3.
- Beyond this point, friction losses grow faster (∝ Q²) than gross power (∝ Q), so the net delivered power falls (red region).
- Eventually, if Q is so large that hf > H, the net head becomes negative and the fluid cannot even reach the outlet — the system fails.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["SOURCE\nRESERVOIR"] --> P1["PIPE\nENTRANCE"]
    P1 --> P2["FRICTION\nLOSS BLOCK"]
    P2 --> P3["NET HEAD\nH minus hf"]
    P3 --> LOAD["POWER\nTAKEOFF"]
    LOAD --> OUT["DELIVERY\nRESERVOIR"]
    P2 -.->|"hf value"| CTRL["OPTIMIZATION\nCONDITION CHECK"]
    CTRL -.->|"hf less than H by 3\nIncrease Q"| P1
    CTRL -.->|"hf greater than H by 3\nDecrease Q"| P1
    CTRL -.->|"hf equals H by 3\nMaximum Power"| LOAD
    styleSRC fill:#bbdefb
    styleLOAD fill:#c8e6c9
    styleCTRL fill:#fff9c4
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Definition of Efficiency of Power Transmission `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Question:** Define *efficiency of power transmission through a pipe* and state its maximum theoretical value.

**Model Answer:**

The **efficiency of power transmission** through a pipe is defined as the ratio of the *useful power delivered* at the downstream end of the pipe to the *gross power supplied* by the head difference.

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Useful power delivered}}{\text{Gross power supplied}} = \frac{\rho g Q (H - h_f)}{\rho g Q H} = \frac{H - h_f}{H}$$

For the **condition of maximum power transmission**, hf = H/3, and therefore:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{H - H/3}{H} = \frac{2}{3} = 66.67\%$$

The maximum theoretical efficiency is therefore **66.67%**. In practice, additional losses (bends, fittings, sudden contractions) reduce this to 50–60%.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write the formula correctly but *forget to state the numerical value* (66.67%). KTU's strict 3-mark scheme often requires the numerical figure explicitly. Always write both the formula and the answer.

---

### Question 2: Condition for Maximum Power Transmission `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Question:** State and explain the condition for *maximum power transmission* through a pipe.

**Model Answer:**

The **condition for maximum power transmission** through a pipe states that the power delivered at the downstream end of the pipe is maximum when the **head loss due to friction is equal to one-third of the total head difference** between the source and the delivery reservoir.

Mathematically:

$$h_f = \frac{H}{3}$$

**Explanation:**
Power delivered, P = ρgQ(H − hf). Since hf ∝ Q², as Q increases, hf increases faster than H remains constant. The function P(Q) has a single maximum, found by setting dP/dQ = 0, which yields 3hf = H.

The corresponding efficiency is 2/3 = 66.67%, and the corresponding economical diameter is:

$$D = \left( \frac{96 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g H} \right)^{1/5}$$

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Question A, Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

Derive the condition for maximum power transmission through a pipe. Show that the efficiency of transmission at this condition is 2/3 (66.67%).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Power delivered at the pipe outlet is:

$$P = \rho g Q (H - h_f)$$

**Step 2:** Using Darcy–Weisbach with V = 4Q/(πD²):

$$h_f = \frac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute hf into the power equation and differentiate w.r.t. Q:

$$P = \rho g \left( H Q - \frac{32 f L Q^3}{\pi^2 g D^5} \right)$$

$$\frac{dP}{dQ} = \rho g \left( H - \frac{96 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5} \right) = 0$$

**Step 4:** This gives:

$$\frac{96 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5} = H \quad \Rightarrow \quad 3 h_f = H \quad \Rightarrow \quad h_f = \frac{H}{3}$$

**Step 5:** Therefore:

$$P_{max} = \rho g Q \left( H - \frac{H}{3} \right) = \frac{2}{3} \rho g Q H$$

**Step 6:** Efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{H - h_f}{H} = \frac{2H/3}{H} = \frac{2}{3} = 66.67\%$$

---

**Question A, Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

A pipeline of length **3 km** and diameter **250 mm** connects two reservoirs with a head difference of **45 m**. Take f = 0.0072. Find: (i) the discharge, (ii) the head loss due to friction, and (iii) the power transmitted. Also verify if the condition for maximum power transmission is satisfied. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Given:** L = 3000 m, D = 0.25 m, H = 45 m, f = 0.0072, ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s²

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use Darcy–Weisbach to find hf in terms of V:**

$$h_f = \frac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{4 \times 0.0072 \times 3000 \times V^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.25}$$

$$h_f = \frac{86.4 \, V^2}{4.905} = 17.615 \, V^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply the energy equation** (assuming the question gives a specific operating condition rather than optimum):

For the case where the pipe is operating at the maximum power condition:

$$h_f = \frac{H}{3} = \frac{45}{3} = 15 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for V:**

$$15 = 17.615 \, V^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V^2 = 0.8516 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = 0.923 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Discharge:**

$$Q = A V = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.25)^2 \times 0.923 = 0.04909 \times 0.923 = 0.0453 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Power transmitted:**

$$P = \rho g Q (H - h_f) = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.0453 \times (45 - 15)$$

$$P = 444.4 \times 30 = 13{,}331 \text{ W} \approx 13.33 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Verification of max-power condition:**

Since hf = 15 m = H/3 = 45/3, the condition for maximum power transmission is satisfied. ✓

**Efficiency:** η = 2/3 = 66.67%

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Question B, Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

Explain the concept of transmission of power through pipes. With the help of a neat sketch, derive the condition for maximum power transmission. Also derive the expression for the diameter of the pipe for maximum power transmission.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept:**
Transmission of power through pipes refers to the conveyance of hydraulic energy (pressure + elevation head) from one point to another through a closed conduit. The fluid enters the pipe with a gross head H (difference in reservoir levels or pump head) and exits with a reduced head (H − hf) due to friction. The net head drives a turbine or hydraulic machine, producing useful power.

**Step 2 — Sketch (use the system diagram from SECTION 4):**
[Two reservoirs connected by a long pipe, with a turbine at the outlet.]

**Step 3 — Derivation of hf = H/3:**
Power delivered: P = ρgQ(H − hf)
Friction loss: hf = 32fLQ²/(π²gD⁵)
Substitute and differentiate: dP/dQ = 0 ⇒ 3hf = H
∴ **hf = H/3** for maximum power transmission.

**Step 4 — Derivation of economical diameter:**
Setting hf = H/3:

$$\frac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5} = \frac{H}{3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = \left( \frac{96 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g H} \right)^{1/5}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum power and efficiency:**

$$P_{max} = \frac{2}{3} \rho g Q H, \quad \eta = \frac{2}{3} = 66.67\%$$

---

**Question B, Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

A penstock of length **2500 m** is to deliver water at a rate of **2.5 m³/s** from a reservoir to a powerhouse with a head of **200 m**. Take f = 0.008. Find the economical diameter of the penstock for maximum power transmission and the corresponding power lost in friction. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Given:** L = 2500 m, Q = 2.5 m³/s, H = 200 m, f = 0.008, ρ = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s²

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the economical diameter:**

$$D = \left( \frac{96 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g H} \right)^{1/5}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$D = \left( \frac{96 \times 0.008 \times 2500 \times (2.5)^2}{\pi^2 \times 9.81 \times 200} \right)^{1/5}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator evaluation:**

$$96 \times 0.008 \times 2500 \times 6.25 = 96 \times 0.008 \times 15625 = 12{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Denominator evaluation:**

$$\pi^2 \times 9.81 \times 200 = 9.8696 \times 9.81 \times 200 = 19{,}364.4$$

**Step 5 — Form the ratio:**

$$D^5 = \frac{12000}{19364.4} = 0.6197$$

$$D = (0.6197)^{1/5} = 0.9087 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Diameter:**

$$\boxed{D \approx 0.909 \text{ m} = 909 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 7 — Head loss due to friction** (= H/3 at max power condition):

$$h_f = \frac{H}{3} = \frac{200}{3} = 66.67 \text{ m}$$

**Step 8 — Power lost in friction:**

$$P_{lost} = \rho g Q h_f = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 2.5 \times 66.67$$

$$P_{lost} = 24{,}525 \times 66.67 = 1{,}635{,}080 \text{ W} \approx 1635.08 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 9 — Power transmitted (for cross-check):**

$$P_{max} = \frac{2}{3} \rho g Q H = \frac{2}{3} \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 2.5 \times 200 = 3{,}270{,}000 \text{ W} = 3270 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 10 — Cross-check total power:**

$$P_{gross} = P_{max} + P_{lost} = 3270 + 1635 = 4905 \text{ kW} = \rho g Q H \text{ ✓}$$

$$\boxed{P_{lost} \approx 1635 \text{ kW}, \quad P_{max} \approx 3270 \text{ kW}, \quad \eta = 66.67\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Marks Loss:**
> 1. **Sign error in dP/dQ:** Students often write dP/dQ = ρg(H − 3KQ²) = 0 but then forget the factor 3 and write hf = H (which is the *total* head). The factor **3** comes from differentiating Q³. Lose 2 marks for this.
> 2. **Using the wrong friction form:** Indian textbooks use hf = 4fLV²/(2gD), giving hf = 32fLQ²/(π²gD⁵). If you accidentally use hf = fLV²/(2gD) (Darcy's form), the constant becomes 128 instead of 32, and your diameter will be wrong. Always state the friction formula you are using.
> 3. **Forgetting to take the fifth root:** A common slip in numericals is to leave the answer as D⁵ = ... without computing the final fifth root. The examiner awards marks only for the *final numerical answer*.
> 4. **Mixing up Pmax with η:** Pmax is a *power* (in W or kW), η is a *percentage*. They are not interchangeable.
> 5. **Not stating the unit of D:** Always write "D = 0.358 m" or "D = 358 mm" — both are accepted by KTU examiners.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Transmission of Power through Pipes**

- **Power equation (fundamental):** $P = \rho g Q (H - h_f)$ — *memorize this as the starting point of every problem*.

- **Friction loss in terms of Q:** $h_f = \dfrac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$ — *the constant 32 comes from $4 \times 8$ in the substitution $V = 4Q/(\pi D^2)$*.

- **Gold rule (KTU-favorite):** For **maximum power transmission**, $h_f = \dfrac{H}{3}$ — *this is THE most tested result in Module 2*.

- **Maximum power:** $P_{max} = \dfrac{2}{3} \rho g Q H$ — *note the factor 2/3, not 1/2 or 1/3*.

- **Efficiency at max power:** $\eta = 66.67\% = \dfrac{2}{3}$ — *theoretical upper limit; real systems achieve 50–60%*.

- **Economic diameter:** $D = \left( \dfrac{96 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g H} \right)^{1/5}$ — *the factor 96 = 3 × 32, from setting hf = H/3*.

- **Power lost in friction:** $P_{lost} = \rho g Q h_f = \rho g Q \cdot H/3 = \dfrac{1}{3} \rho g Q H$ — *one-third of gross power*.

- **Sanity check formula:** $P_{gross} = P_{max} + P_{lost} = \dfrac{2}{3}\rho g QH + \dfrac{1}{3}\rho g QH = \rho g Q H$ ✓

- **Limitations of the theory:**
  1. Assumes only friction losses (no bends, valves, sudden contractions)
  2. Constant pipe diameter throughout
  3. Constant fluid properties
  4. f is assumed constant (in reality, f depends on Re and ε/D)

- **Real-world application:** Penstock design in hydroelectric projects (Idukki, Mullaperiyar in Kerala), long-distance water mains (KWA), industrial oil pipelines (BPCL Kochi Refinery).

- **Examiner's hot-button keywords:** *"Derive the condition"*, *"Verify"*, *"Show that η = 2/3"*, *"Find the economical diameter"*, *"Maximum power that can be transmitted"* — *always show the derivation, never just quote the result*.

- **Units to watch:** ρ in kg/m³, g in m/s², Q in m³/s, H in m ⇒ P in W (or kW = 10⁻³ W). Convert mm to m before substituting into formulas.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Rate Measurements: Venturi and Orifice Meters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Flow Rate Measurements: Venturi and Orifice Meters ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Venturi Meter — Formal KTU Definition A **Venturi meter** is a differential pressure flow measurement device consisting of a gradual converging section (inlet cone), a short cylindrical **throat**, and a gradual diverging section (diffuser). It is installed in a pipe carrying a fluid to measure the **volumetric flow rate (Q)** by relating the pressure drop between the inlet and the throat to the velocity of flow using Bernoulli's principle and the continuity equation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Venturi meter is treated as the *benchmark* device for incompressible flow measurement because its smooth, streamlined profile ensures minimal head loss (typically **2% to 5%** of the differential pressure head). ### 1.2 Orifice Meter — Formal KTU Definition An **orifice meter** is a thin, flat circular plate with a precisely machined concentric hole (the **orifice**) inserted between two pipe flanges. Fluid flowing through the pipe is forced to contract to a **vena contracta** (a minimum cross-section just downstream of the orifice) where velocity is maximum and pressure is minimum. The pressure difference between the upstream tap and the downstream tap is used to compute the flow rate. > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition — Vena Contracta:** The section of the flow jet, just downstream of an orifice, where the streamlines become parallel and the cross-sectional area of the jet is minimum. The ratio of vena contracta area to orifice area is the **Coefficient of Contraction (Cc)**, typically **0.61 to 0.65** for a sharp-edged orifice. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are driving a car on a wide highway that suddenly narrows to a single-lane bottleneck. - **Before the bottleneck** (the pipe inlet): cars are spread out, moving slowly, density of cars is high → high pressure, low velocity. - **Through the bottleneck** (the throat of a Venturi or the orifice opening): cars are squeezed together and accelerate to pass through → low pressure, high velocity. - **After the bottleneck** (the diffuser or downstream pipe): cars spread out again and decelerate. The **pressure difference** between the wide section and the narrow section tells you how fast the cars (fluid) must be moving. Engineers simply measure that pressure drop, do a bit of physics, and voilà — the **flow rate** is known. > [!TIP] > **Why two different devices if both do the same job?** > - **Venturi meter** → expensive, high accuracy (0.5%–1%), low head loss, used in large water-supply mains, oil pipelines, and aerodynamics test sections. > - **Orifice meter** → cheap, simple, compact, higher head loss, used in chemical plants, HVAC ducts, and refinery applications where cost outweighs the need for ultra-high accuracy. ### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics | Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Typical Range | |---|---|---|---| | Acceleration due to gravity | **g** | m/s² | **9.81** | | Density of water (4 °C) | **ρ** | kg/m³ | **1000** | | Density of air (STP) | **ρ_air** | kg/m³ | **1.225** | | Coefficient of discharge (Venturi) | **Cd** | dimensionless | 0.95 – 0.99 | | Coefficient of discharge (Orifice) | **Cd** | dimensionless | 0.60 – 0.65 | | Coefficient of contraction (Orifice) | **Cc** | dimensionless | 0.61 – 0.65 | | Coefficient of velocity (Orifice) | **Cv** | dimensionless | 0.95 – 0.99 | > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Do **NOT** use Cd ≈ 0.98 for an orifice meter. Students frequently confuse the two devices. Memorize: *Venturi Cd ≈ 0.98, Orifice Cd ≈ 0.62*. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Core "Why" and "How" Both devices exploit two foundational principles of incompressible fluid flow: 1. **Continuity Equation** — When area decreases, velocity must increase to keep mass flow constant. 2. **Bernoulli's Equation** — The increase in velocity is accompanied by a decrease in static pressure (assuming no significant elevation change and negligible friction in the converging section). **Step-by-step logic chain:** - **Step 1 — Inlet Section (Section 1):** Large cross-sectional area A₁, low velocity V₁, high static pressure p₁. - **Step 2 — Throat / Vena Contracta (Section 2):** Reduced cross-sectional area A₂, high velocity V₂, low static pressure p₂. - **Step 3 — Apply Continuity:** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \Rightarrow V_1 = (A_2/A_1) V_2$ - **Step 4 — Apply Bernoulli (ideal flow):** $\dfrac{p_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g}$ - **Step 5 — Combine (1) and (2) to express V₂ in terms of (p₁ − p₂):** This yields the **ideal theoretical velocity**. - **Step 6 — Multiply by A₂ to get theoretical discharge Q_th, then introduce Cd:** $Q_{actual} = C_d \cdot Q_{theoretical}$ - **Step 7 — Cd accounts for real-world losses:** friction, turbulence, streamline curvature, and (for orifice) the difference between vena contracta area and orifice area. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A_2}{\sqrt{1-(A_2/A_1)^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2g h}$ | Theoretical (ideal) discharge | m³/s | | 2 | $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{\sqrt{1-(A_2/A_1)^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2g h}$ | **Actual discharge** (master equation for both Venturi and orifice) | m³/s | | 3 | $h = \dfrac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho g}$ | Differential head across device | m of fluid | | 4 | $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ | Coefficient of discharge relation (orifice) | dimensionless | | 5 | $V_2 = C_v \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2g h}{1-(A_2/A_1)^2}}$ | Actual velocity at throat / vena contracta | m/s | | 6 | $A_c = C_c \cdot A_o$ | Area of vena contracta (orifice only) | m² | | 7 | $Q = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2g h}{1-(C_c A_o/A_1)^2}}$ | Orifice discharge in terms of orifice area | m³/s | | 8 | $P = \rho g Q h$ | Power dissipated in head loss | W (watts) | > [!NOTE] > **Note on Notation:** In KTU textbooks, the upstream area is often denoted **a₁** and the throat/orifice area as **a₂**. The differential head **h** is read directly from a U-tube manometer (with appropriate correction for the manometer fluid density). ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Water Treatment Plants (WTP):** Venturi meters measure the inflow of raw water to chemical dosing systems. - **Oil & Gas Pipelines:** Orifice meters are the workhorse for custody transfer of natural gas and crude oil (multi-hole orifice plates for higher accuracy). - **Aerospace Wind Tunnels:** Venturi sections in test sections measure airflow and create low-pressure regions for suction. - **Automotive Carburettors (legacy systems):** The Venturi effect draws fuel into the air stream via a pressure drop. - **HVAC Systems:** Orifice plates are used in chilled-water and condenser-water circuits for cheap flow balancing. > [!TIP] > **KTU Tip:** When asked *"Why use Venturi instead of orifice?"* in viva, answer: *higher accuracy, lower permanent head loss, no vena contracta uncertainty, and no sharp edges that erode in dirty fluids.* ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Master Derivation — Discharge Through a Venturi Meter **Given:** A horizontal Venturi meter with inlet diameter D₁ and throat diameter D₂, carrying an incompressible fluid of density ρ. The differential pressure head between inlet and throat is **h**. **Assumptions (KTU standard):** - Steady, incompressible, irrotational flow. - No friction (ideal) — losses are lumped later into Cd. - Horizontal pipe — no elevation change. **Step 1: Continuity Equation** $$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$ $$V_1 = \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right) V_2$$ This expresses the upstream velocity V₁ in terms of the throat velocity V₂. The text explanation: *because mass (or volume, for incompressible flow) must be conserved, the slower upstream flow must speed up as the area shrinks.* **Step 2: Bernoulli's Equation (horizontal, no friction)** $$\dfrac{p_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g}$$ Rearranging for the pressure difference: $$\dfrac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho g} = \dfrac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$ **Step 3: Substitute the Differential Head h** By definition: $$h = \dfrac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho g}$$ So: $$h = \dfrac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$ **Step 4: Substitute Continuity Relation into the Head Equation** $$h = \dfrac{V_2^2 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 V_2^2}{2g}$$ $$h = \dfrac{V_2^2 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]}{2g}$$ **Step 5: Solve for the Theoretical Throat Velocity V₂** $$V_2^2 = \dfrac{2g h}{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}$$ $$V_2 = \dfrac{\sqrt{2g h}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}$$ **Step 6: Theoretical Discharge Q_th** $$Q_{th} = A_2 \cdot V_2 = \dfrac{A_2 \sqrt{2g h}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}$$ **Step 7: Introduce the Coefficient of Discharge Cd** The actual discharge is less than theoretical because of friction and non-ideal flow: $$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2g h}{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **FINAL KTU VENTURI METER EQUATION:** > $$\boxed{Q = \dfrac{C_d \, A_2 \, \sqrt{2g h}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(A_2/A_1\right)^2}}}$$ > This is the single most tested equation in Module 3. Memorize the structure, not just the formula. --- ### 3.2 Companion Derivation — Discharge Through an Orifice Meter **Key Difference:** For an orifice, the area A₂ in the formula is the **area of the vena contracta (Ac)**, not the orifice area (Ao), because that is where the velocity is maximum and the pressure is minimum — and that is what the downstream tap actually measures. **Step 1: Same continuity, same Bernoulli — same ideal velocity expression:** $$V_c = \dfrac{\sqrt{2g h}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(A_c/A_1\right)^2}}$$ **Step 2: Actual Discharge Using the Vena Contracta Area** $$Q_{act} = A_c \cdot V_c = \dfrac{A_c \sqrt{2g h}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(A_c/A_1\right)^2}}$$ **Step 3: Express Ac in terms of Orifice Area Ao Using Cc** $$A_c = C_c \cdot A_o$$ Substituting: $$Q_{act} = \dfrac{C_c A_o \sqrt{2g h}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{C_c A_o}{A_1}\right)^2}}$$ **Step 4: Define Cd = Cc · Cv (this is the convention for orifice meters)** After simplification and introducing the velocity coefficient Cv into the discharge formula, the KTU final expression becomes: $$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2g h}{1 - \left(C_c A_o / A_1\right)^2}}$$ where $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$. > [!NOTE] > **Why Cd = Cc · Cv for an orifice meter?** > - **Cv** accounts for friction losses in the converging jet (velocity is slightly less than ideal). > - **Cc** accounts for the jet contraction (area at vena contracta is less than the orifice area). > - Their product **Cd** is what we measure experimentally in a calibration rig. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU 2024 Scheme Style **Problem:** A Venturi meter has an inlet diameter of **20 cm** and a throat diameter of **10 cm**. The differential U-tube manometer reading is **20 cm of mercury**, and the flowing fluid is water. The meter coefficient is **Cd = 0.98**. Calculate the actual discharge in litres per second. (Given: ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³, ρ_Hg = 13600 kg/m³.) **Step 1: Calculate Areas** $$A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.20)^2 = 0.031416 \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.10)^2 = 0.007854 \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_2 / A_1 = 0.25 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (A_2/A_1)^2 = 0.0625$$ **Step 2: Convert Manometer Reading to Differential Head h (in metres of water)** For a U-tube manometer with heavier fluid (mercury) and lighter fluid (water): $$h = x \left(\dfrac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_{water}} - 1\right) = 0.20 \left(\dfrac{13600}{1000} - 1\right) = 0.20 \times 12.6 = 2.52 \text{ m of water}$$ **Step 3: Plug into the Master Venturi Equation** $$Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g h}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$ $$Q = 0.98 \times 0.007854 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.52}{1 - 0.0625}}$$ $$Q = 0.98 \times 0.007854 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{49.44}{0.9375}}$$ $$Q = 0.98 \times 0.007854 \times \sqrt{52.736}$$ $$Q = 0.98 \times 0.007854 \times 7.2625$$ $$Q = 0.0559 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 55.9 \text{ L/s}$$ > [!TIP] > **Marks Distribution (KTU-style):** Step 1 → 2 marks, Step 2 → 3 marks, Step 3 (final) → 5 marks, Units & conclusion → 2 marks. Showing every step is what gets you full marks. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Venturi Meter — Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Inlet Pipe Section
Diameter D1, Area A1] --> A2[Inlet Pressure Tap
Reads p1, High Static Pressure] A2 --> A3[Converging Cone
Angle 20 to 25 degrees
Streamlines Contract Smoothly] A3 --> A4[Throat Section
Diameter D2, Area A2, Minimum Area] A4 --> A5[Throat Pressure Tap
Reads p2, Low Static Pressure] A5 --> A6[Diverging Cone
Angle 5 to 7 degrees
Streamlines Expand Gradually, Pressure Recovery] A6 --> A7[Outlet Pipe Section
Recovers to Diameter D1, Area A1] A1 -.Manometer Connection.-> A4 A2 -. Differential Head h.-> A5 ``` **Interpretation of the flow:** - The **inlet tap** at A1 measures the high upstream pressure p₁. - The **throat tap** at A4 measures the low throat pressure p₂. - A **U-tube manometer** is connected between A1 and A4 to read the differential head **h = (p₁ − p₂)/ρg**. - The data is fed into the master discharge equation $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2gh / (1 - (A_2/A_1)^2)}$. --- ### 4.2 Orifice Meter — Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR B1[Upstream Pipe
Diameter D1, Area A1, Velocity V1] --> B2[Upstream Tap
Corner Tap or D and D by 2 Tap] B2 --> B3[Orifice Plate
Thin Flat Plate with Concentric Hole Ao, Diameter Do] B3 --> B4[Vena Contracta
Diameter Dc, Area Ac equals Cc times Ao
Velocity Vc is Maximum Here] B4 --> B5[Downstream Tap
Reads Pressure at Vena Contracta] B5 --> B6[Expanding Jet
Reattaches to Pipe Wall, Turbulent Dissipation Occurs] B6 --> B7[Downstream Pipe
Recovers to D1, but with Permanent Head Loss] B2 -. Differential Manometer.-> B5 ``` **Interpretation of the flow:** - The orifice plate is sandwiched between two pipe flanges. - The **vena contracta** (B4) is the heart of the meter — this is where area is minimum, velocity is maximum, and pressure is minimum. - The downstream tap is located exactly at (or just before) the vena contracta to capture the lowest pressure. - There is **significant permanent head loss** downstream because turbulence dissipates kinetic energy in the expanding jet. --- ### 4.3 Comparative Schematic — Venturi vs Orifice (Component-Level) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph S1[VENTURI METER] V1[Inlet D1] --> V2[Converging Cone
20 to 25 deg] V2 --> V3[Throat D2] V3 --> V4[Diverging Cone
5 to 7 deg] V4 --> V5[Outlet D1] end subgraph S2[ORIFICE METER] O1[Inlet D1] --> O2[Sharp Edged Orifice Plate
Thickness less than Do by 8] O2 --> O3[Vena Contracta
Dc less than Do] O3 --> O4[Sudden Expansion
High Turbulence] O4 --> O5[Outlet D1] end ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Visual Comparison Takeaway:** The Venturi meter's *gradual* converging and diverging geometry preserves energy. The orifice meter's *abrupt* geometry causes a permanent head loss of 40%–60% of the differential pressure — that is energy the system never recovers. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 1]** **Define a Venturi meter and state its main working principle. Why is its coefficient of discharge (Cd) very high compared to an orifice meter?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Venturi meter** is a flow-measuring device consisting of a converging cone, a short throat, and a diverging cone, used to measure the discharge of fluid flowing through a pipe (1 mark). **Working Principle:** It works on **Bernoulli's principle** — as the cross-section decreases from inlet (A₁) to throat (A₂), velocity increases and pressure decreases. The pressure difference (h) is measured using a differential manometer and the discharge is calculated using the equation $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2gh/[1-(A_2/A_1)^2]}$ (1 mark). **High Cd:** Because the converging and diverging sections are smoothly streamlined (converging angle 20°–25°, diverging angle 5°–7°), there are no sudden flow separations, the jet does not contract below the throat area, and friction losses are minimal. Hence Cd is very high (**0.95 to 0.99**), close to unity (1 mark). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]** **Define the terms: (i) Coefficient of velocity (Cv), (ii) Coefficient of contraction (Cc), and (iii) Coefficient of discharge (Cd) for an orifice meter. Show how they are related.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** (i) **Coefficient of Velocity (Cv):** It is the ratio of the actual velocity of the jet at the vena contracta to the ideal (theoretical) velocity. $C_v = V_{actual}/V_{ideal}$ (1 mark). (ii) **Coefficient of Contraction (Cc):** It is the ratio of the area of the vena contracta to the area of the orifice. $C_c = A_c/A_o$ (1 mark). (iii) **Coefficient of Discharge (Cd):** It is the ratio of actual discharge to the theoretical discharge. $C_d = Q_{actual}/Q_{theoretical}$ (0.5 marks). **Relation:** $\boxed{C_d = C_c \cdot C_v}$ (0.5 marks). --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Using **Cd ≈ 0.98 for an orifice** instead of **0.62**. (−2 marks) > - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert **manometer reading from cm of Hg to m of water** before plugging into the formula. (−2 marks) > - **Common Mistake 3:** Using **D₂/D₁** instead of **A₂/A₁ = (D₂/D₁)²** in the area ratio. (−1 mark) > - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to state the **assumptions** (steady, incompressible, no friction) at the start. (−1 mark) > - **Common Mistake 5:** Not writing the **final answer in SI units** (m³/s) or with the correct unit prefix (L/s). (−1 mark) --- **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Slot A]** A horizontal Venturi meter fitted in a pipe carrying oil (specific gravity 0.8) has an inlet diameter of **300 mm** and a throat diameter of **150 mm**. The differential U-tube manometer with mercury shows a reading of **250 mm**. The coefficient of discharge is **0.98**. Calculate: **(a)** The actual discharge of oil in litres per second. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The power saved by using this Venturi meter in place of an orifice meter for the same upstream pipe, throat, and manometer reading (assume Cd for orifice = 0.62). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for Q A:** **Part (a) — Actual Discharge (7 marks)** *Step 1:* Compute areas. $$A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.30)^2 = 0.07069 \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$ $$(A_2/A_1)^2 = (0.15/0.30)^4 = (0.5)^4 = 0.0625$$ *Step 2:* Convert manometer reading to head of oil. Density of oil, $\rho_{oil} = 0.8 \times 1000 = 800 \text{ kg/m}^3$. For a U-tube with mercury (ρ_Hg = 13600 kg/m³): $$h = 0.25 \left(\dfrac{13600}{800} - 1\right) = 0.25 \times (17 - 1) = 0.25 \times 16 = 4.0 \text{ m of oil}$$ *Step 3:* Apply Venturi equation. $$Q = 0.98 \times 0.01767 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 9.81 \times 4.0}{1 - 0.0625}}$$ $$Q = 0.01732 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{78.48}{0.9375}} = 0.01732 \times \sqrt{83.712}$$ $$Q = 0.01732 \times 9.149 = 0.1585 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 158.5 \text{ L/s}$$ > **Answer (a): Q ≈ 158.5 L/s** ✅ (7 marks) --- **Part (b) — Power Saved (7 marks)** The **head loss** in a flow meter is related to the **difference between theoretical and actual discharge**. A more accurate way is to use: **Power dissipated** in head loss = $\rho g Q \cdot h_L$, where $h_L$ is the head loss. For the same **manometer reading x = 0.25 m of Hg** and same pipe sizes, the differential pressure (p₁ − p₂) is identical for both meters. The head loss can be related to Cd as: $$h_L = \left(\dfrac{1}{C_d^2} - 1\right) \cdot \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g}$$ **Alternative KTU approach (using the discharge ratio):** For the same pressure drop, the **actual discharge** is proportional to Cd. But for the *same discharge*, the **Venturi needs less pressure drop** than the orifice. We can solve for the **head loss of each** for a fixed flow. A simpler textbook approach is to compute the **head loss for each meter individually** using: $$h_L = h \left(\dfrac{1}{C_d^2} - 1\right)$$ where h is the differential head (same for both = 4.0 m of oil). **For Venturi (Cd = 0.98):** $$h_{L,vent} = 4.0 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{0.98^2} - 1\right) = 4.0 \times (1.0413 - 1) = 0.165 \text{ m of oil}$$ **For Orifice (Cd = 0.62):** $$h_{L,orif} = 4.0 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{0.62^2} - 1\right) = 4.0 \times (2.601 - 1) = 6.404 \text{ m of oil}$$ **Power loss for each** (per unit discharge Q = 0.1585 m³/s): $$P_{vent} = \rho g Q \cdot h_{L,vent} = 800 \times 9.81 \times 0.1585 \times 0.165 = 205.2 \text{ W}$$ $$P_{orif} = \rho g Q \cdot h_{L,orif} = 800 \times 9.81 \times 0.1585 \times 6.404 = 7964 \text{ W}$$ **Power Saved:** $$\Delta P = 7964 - 205.2 \approx 7759 \text{ W} \approx 7.76 \text{ kW}$$ > **Answer (b): Power saved ≈ 7.76 kW** ✅ (7 marks) --- **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Slot B — Alternative Choice]** An orifice meter is installed in a horizontal pipe of **150 mm** diameter carrying water. The orifice diameter is **75 mm**. The pressure difference measured by a differential mercury manometer is **150 mm of Hg**. If the coefficients are: Cd = 0.62, Cc = 0.64, calculate: **(a)** The actual discharge of water in m³/s. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The theoretical velocity of water at the vena contracta, and compare it with the actual velocity. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for Q B:** **Part (a) — Actual Discharge (7 marks)** *Step 1:* Compute areas. $$A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.150)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_o = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^2 = 0.004418 \text{ m}^2$$ $$C_c A_o / A_1 = 0.64 \times 0.004418 / 0.01767 = 0.16$$ $$(C_c A_o / A_1)^2 = 0.0256$$ *Step 2:* Convert manometer reading to head of water. $$h = 0.150 \left(\dfrac{13600}{1000} - 1\right) = 0.150 \times 12.6 = 1.89 \text{ m of water}$$ *Step 3:* Apply orifice discharge formula. $$Q = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g h}{1 - (C_c A_o/A_1)^2}}$$ $$Q = 0.62 \times 0.004418 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.89}{1 - 0.0256}}$$ $$Q = 0.002739 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{37.08}{0.9744}} = 0.002739 \times \sqrt{38.06}$$ $$Q = 0.002739 \times 6.169 = 0.01690 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ > **Answer (a): Q ≈ 0.0169 m³/s ≈ 16.9 L/s** ✅ (7 marks) --- **Part (b) — Theoretical vs Actual Velocity (7 marks)** *Step 1:* Find the area of the vena contracta. $$A_c = C_c \cdot A_o = 0.64 \times 0.004418 = 0.002828 \text{ m}^2$$ *Step 2:* Theoretical velocity at vena contracta (V_th). From continuity-derived expression (theoretical, ideal): $$V_{th} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2g h}{1 - (A_c/A_1)^2}}$$ $$V_{th} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.89}{1 - 0.0256}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{37.08}{0.9744}} = \sqrt{38.06} = 6.169 \text{ m/s}$$ *Step 3:* Actual velocity V_c = Cv · V_th.
