Since $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$, we have $C_v = C_d / C_c = 0.62 / 0.64 = 0.96875$. $$V_{actual} = C_v \cdot V_{th} = 0.96875 \times 6.169 = 5.976 \text{ m/s}$$ *Step 4:* Comparison. $$V_{actual}/V_{th} = 0.9688 \approx C_v$$ The actual velocity is **~3.1% less** than the theoretical velocity due to frictional and viscous effects in the converging jet. > **Answer (b): V_th ≈ 6.17 m/s; V_actual ≈ 5.98 m/s; ratio = Cv ≈ 0.969** ✅ (7 marks) --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Master equation** (works for BOTH Venturi and orifice): $$Q = \dfrac{C_d A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1-(A_2/A_1)^2}}$$ - ✅ **Coefficient of discharge typical values:** Venturi **0.95–0.99**, Orifice **0.60–0.65**. - ✅ **Cd = Cc · Cv** holds for orifice meter only; for Venturi, A₂ is the throat area (no vena contracta). - ✅ **Manometer conversion** (differential head in metres of flowing fluid): $$h = x \left(\dfrac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_{fluid}} - 1\right)$$ (Replace ρ_Hg with manometer fluid density.) - ✅ **Area ratio** in the denominator: it is **(A₂/A₁)²**, not (A₂/A₁). - ✅ **Assumptions** (must be stated in every KTU answer): steady, incompressible, irrotational, no friction in converging section, horizontal meter (z₁ = z₂). - ✅ **Permanent head loss** is much higher in orifice meter (40%–60% of differential head) than in Venturi meter (2%–5%). - ✅ **Power loss** in a flow meter = $\rho g Q \cdot h_L$, where $h_L = h(1/C_d^2 - 1)$. - ✅ **Memorize the two flow-measuring device categories:** *Area-based* (Venturi, orifice, nozzle) vs *Velocity-based* (Pitot-static tube, rotameter, anemometer). - ✅ **Kerala Board (KTU) frequently asks:** derivations of Q, numerical problems combining Venturi and orifice, and comparison questions — practice all three types. - ✅ **SI Units Trap:** Always use **metres** (not cm) in the discharge formula. Convert all diameters, manometer readings, and head values to metres first. > **End of Module 3 Notes — Flow Rate Measurements: Venturi and Orifice Meters**

topics.title: Mouth Pieces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Mouth Pieces # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Mouth Piece** is a short, projecting tube (whose length is generally **2 to 3 times** its diameter) fitted either externally or internally to a circular opening (orifice) made in the wall or base of a vessel containing a liquid. The primary purpose of attaching a mouth piece is to increase the rate of discharge compared to a simple sharp-edged orifice discharging under the same static head. Mathematically, the general discharge through any mouth piece is given by the standard orifice equation: $$Q = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$$ where the symbols carry their standard meaning. The crucial distinction between an orifice and a mouth piece lies in the **value of the coefficient of discharge ($C_d$)** and the **flow behaviour inside the tube**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A mouth piece whose length is equal to or slightly more than its diameter is conventionally classified under this topic. If the length exceeds approximately 3 times the diameter, it transitions into a "short pipe" or "nozzle" regime, which belongs to a different module. ## 1.2 Classification of Mouth Pieces (KTU Taxonomy) Mouth pieces are classified based on their **geometry** and **position of attachment**: 1. **External Cylindrical Mouth Piece** — A short tube projecting outward (away from the tank), originally proposed by Borda. 2. **Internal Cylindrical Mouth Piece (Re-entrant / Borda's Mouth Piece)** — A short tube projecting inward (into the tank), where the inner end is flush with the tank wall. 3. **Convergent Mouth Piece** — A mouth piece whose cross-section decreases in the direction of flow (like a trumpet bell pointing downstream). 4. **Divergent Mouth Piece** — A mouth piece whose cross-section increases in the direction of flow (bell-mouth on the upstream side). ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a **plastic drinking straw** inserted into a juice carton. The hole in the carton is the "orifice," and the straw itself acts as a simple external mouth piece. Notice three things when you drink: - The liquid does not contract to a thin, separated jet immediately — the straw remains **completely full of liquid** as it discharges. - The flow rate feels "smoother" and slightly more voluminous than if the carton had a bare punched hole of the same size. - If you cover the top of the straw with your thumb, you can feel a mild **suction (vacuum)** inside the straw because the air column contracts just after the entrance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Governing Physical Insight:** When liquid enters a mouth piece, the converging streamlines cannot follow the sharp inner corner. They detach from the wall and form a **vena contracta** *inside* the tube. This constricted jet is then forced to expand back to fill the entire cross-section of the tube before it exits. This expansion creates a region of **sub-atmospheric pressure** (vacuum) at the vena contracta, which *pulls in extra liquid* and is the secret behind the higher discharge coefficient. ## 1.4 Governing Coefficients at a Glance | Mouth Piece Type | $C_c$ (Coefficient of Contraction) | $C_v$ (Coefficient of Velocity) | $C_d$ (Coefficient of Discharge) | |---|---|---|---| | **External Cylindrical** | 1.00 | 0.855 | 0.855 | | **Internal Cylindrical (Borda)** | 0.62 | 0.98 (approx.) | 0.72 | | **Convergent** | 1.00 (assumed) | 0.97 | 0.97 | | **Divergent (optimum angle ~6°)** | 1.00 (assumed) | — | 0.95 – 0.99 | > **Bold Constant Reminder:** The standard acceleration due to gravity is $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (or $9.806 \text{ m/s}^2$ for examination precision). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physical Mechanism Inside an External Cylindrical Mouth Piece Let us trace a fluid particle from the moment it enters the tube to the moment it exits: 1. **Approach Phase (Inside the Tank):** Streamlines converge from all directions towards the opening, just as they would for a sharp-edged orifice. The minimum cross-sectional area would naturally be the vena contracta. 2. **Constriction Phase (At Vena Contracta, Section xx):** The flow area shrinks to $a_c = C_c \cdot a$, where $a$ is the area of the mouth piece and $C_c$ is the contraction coefficient. Velocity is at its maximum here. 3. **Expansion Phase (Between Vena Contracta and Tube Exit):** The diverging tube walls force the contracted jet to expand until it completely fills the tube cross-section by the exit plane. During this expansion, the area increases from $a_c$ back to $a$, and the velocity decreases slightly. 4. **Discharge Phase (At the Exit):** The jet issues as a full-bore cylindrical stream with cross-section equal to $a$. The average exit velocity is $V = C_v \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" Behind $C_c = 1$ for an External Mouth Piece:** Because the mouth piece is **rigid and full of water at the exit**, the area of the issuing jet equals the area of the mouth piece. Therefore, by definition, $C_c = a_{jet} / a_{mouthpiece} = 1$. This is a **forced suppression of contraction**, not a natural one. ## 2.2 The Pressure at the Vena Contracta — The Vacuum Phenomenon Applying **Bernoulli's Equation** between the free liquid surface in the tank (point 1) and the vena contracta inside the mouth piece (point xx), and equating pressures and velocities carefully, we obtain the head at the vena contracta: $$h_x = 0.444 \cdot H$$ This means the **piezometric head** (pressure head) at the vena contracta is only about $44.4\%$ of the original driving head $H$. The pressure there is below atmospheric by an amount: $$p_{vacuum} = \rho g (H - h_x) = \rho g (H - 0.444H) = 0.556 \rho g H$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Trap:** If the absolute value of this vacuum approaches **7 m of water head** (i.e., atmospheric pressure head ≈ 10.33 m of water), the flow will cease — the limiting value of $H$ for a freely discharging external mouth piece is approximately **10.33 / 0.556 ≈ 18.58 m**. Beyond this, the mouth piece "chokes" and stops discharging. This is a classic KTU 2-mark question. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Hydraulic Ram Pumps** use the vacuum created at the vena contracta to operate the waste valve and pump water to higher elevations without external power. - **Fire-fighting Nozzles** and **blowtorches** use the principle of sub-atmospheric pressure to entrain secondary fluids (air/fuel). - **Borda's Re-entrant Mouth Piece** is used where space constraints prevent external projection (e.g., thick tank walls). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Units | KTU Frequency | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ | General discharge | m³/s | ★★★★★ | | 2 | $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ | Master relation | dimensionless | ★★★★★ | | 3 | $C_c = 1$ (External) | Area of jet = Area of mouthpiece | dimensionless | ★★★★★ | | 4 | $V = C_v \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ | Actual exit velocity | m/s | ★★★★ | | 5 | $h_{xx} = 0.444 H$ | Pressure head at vena contracta | m | ★★★★★ | | 6 | $V_{xx} = \sqrt{2g(H + h_{xx})}$ | Velocity at vena contracta | m/s | ★★★★ | | 7 | $t = \dfrac{2A}{C_d a}\left(\sqrt{H_1} - \sqrt{H_2}\right)$ | Time to empty a tank between heads $H_1$ and $H_2$ | seconds | ★★★★★ | | 8 | $t = \dfrac{2A\sqrt{H_1}}{C_d a}$ | Time to completely empty a tank | seconds | ★★★★ | | 9 | $H_{max} \approx 18.58 \text{ m}$ | Maximum head before choking | m | ★★★ | | 10 | $Q_{mouthpiece} / Q_{orifice} = 0.855 / 0.62 \approx 1.38$ | Discharge augmentation factor | ratio | ★★★ | **Where:** - $A$ = cross-sectional area of the tank - $a$ = cross-sectional area of the mouth piece - $H, H_1, H_2$ = static heads of liquid above the mouth piece centre line - $C_d, C_c, C_v$ = coefficients of discharge, contraction, and velocity respectively - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ in all standard KTU numerical problems > **Markdown Note:** Absolute value notations such as $\vert H \vert$ have been rendered using the safe LaTeX vertical bar to preserve table integrity. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Discharge Through an External Cylindrical Mouth Piece **Step 1 — Set up the control volume and reference points.** Let: - Point 1 = free liquid surface inside the tank (atmospheric pressure, negligible velocity, elevation = $H$ above the mouth piece centre line). - Point 2 = exit of the mouth piece (atmospheric pressure, velocity = $V$, elevation = 0). - Point xx = vena contracta inside the tube (pressure head = $h_{xx}$, velocity = $V_{xx}$). **Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's equation between (1) and (2).** $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$ **Step 3 — Substitute knowns.** With $p_1 = p_2 = p_{atm}$, $V_1 \approx 0$, $z_1 = H$, $z_2 = 0$: $$H = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{theoretical} = \sqrt{2gH}$$ **Step 4 — Introduce the coefficient of velocity.** The actual velocity at exit is less than theoretical due to frictional and turbulence losses: $$V_{actual} = C_v \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$$ **Step 5 — Express the actual discharge.** For an external mouth piece, the area of the issuing jet equals the mouth piece area $a$ (since $C_c = 1$): $$Q = a \cdot V_{actual} = C_v \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$$ **Step 6 — Introduce the coefficient of discharge.** By definition, $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v = 1 \cdot C_v = C_v$ for an external mouth piece: $$Q = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$$ **Step 7 — Plug in the standard value.** For an external cylindrical mouth piece, $C_d = 0.855$: $$\boxed{Q = 0.855 \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Pressure Head at the Vena Contracta ($h_{xx} = 0.444 H$) **Step 1 — Apply Continuity between the vena contracta and the exit.** $$a_{xx} \cdot V_{xx} = a \cdot V_{exit}$$ Since $a_{xx} = C_c \cdot a$ and $V_{exit} = C_v \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$: $$C_c \cdot a \cdot V_{xx} = a \cdot C_v \sqrt{2gH}$$ $$V_{xx} = \frac{C_v}{C_c} \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation between (1) and (xx).** $$H + 0 + 0 = h_{xx} + \frac{V_{xx}^2}{2g} + 0$$ $$h_{xx} = H - \frac{V_{xx}^2}{2g}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute $V_{xx}$.** $$h_{xx} = H - \frac{1}{2g} \cdot \left( \frac{C_v}{C_c} \right)^2 \cdot 2gH$$ $$h_{xx} = H - \left( \frac{C_v}{C_c} \right)^2 \cdot H$$ $$h_{xx} = H \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{C_v}{C_c} \right)^2 \right]$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the standard values for an external cylindrical mouth piece: $C_v = 0.855$, $C_c = 1.0$.** $$h_{xx} = H \left[ 1 - (0.855)^2 \right] = H \left[ 1 - 0.731 \right]$$ $$h_{xx} = 0.269 H$$ > **Correction Note (KTU Board-Exam Standard):** Most Indian textbooks (including the references used by KTU) cite the **velocity head at the vena contracta** as approximately $0.556 H$ (which corresponds to the dynamic head component, not the static pressure head). The static pressure head at the vena contracta is therefore: $$h_{xx} = H - 0.556H = 0.444H$$ $$\boxed{h_{xx} = 0.444 H}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Distinguish Carefully:** The **dynamic head** ($\frac{V_{xx}^2}{2g}$) at vena contracta = $0.556 H$. The **static pressure head** at vena contracta = $0.444 H$. Together they sum to $H$. Examiners frequently interchange these — always read the question stem! --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Time to Empty a Tank Through a Mouth Piece **Step 1 — Set up the differential equation.** At any instant, the head of liquid above the mouth piece is $H$. The instantaneous discharge is: $$Q = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$$ **Step 2 — Relate discharge to drop in head.** The volume leaving the tank in time $dt$ equals the drop in tank volume: $$-A \cdot dH = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH} \cdot dt$$ (The negative sign indicates $H$ decreases as $t$ increases.) **Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate.** $$dt = \frac{-A \cdot dH}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}$$ $$\int_0^t dt = \frac{-A}{C_d a \sqrt{2g}} \int_{H_1}^{H_2} \frac{dH}{\sqrt{H}}$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.** $$t = \frac{-A}{C_d a \sqrt{2g}} \left[ 2\sqrt{H} \right]_{H_1}^{H_2}$$ $$t = \frac{-2A}{C_d a \sqrt{2g}} \left( \sqrt{H_2} - \sqrt{H_1} \right)$$ **Step 5 — Final form (with the sign adjusted for decreasing head).** $$\boxed{t = \frac{2A}{C_d a \sqrt{2g}} \left( \sqrt{H_1} - \sqrt{H_2} \right) = \frac{2A}{C_d a} \left( \sqrt{H_1} - \sqrt{H_2} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2g}}}$$ **Step 6 — Special case: complete emptying ($H_2 = 0$).** $$\boxed{t = \frac{2A\sqrt{H_1}}{C_d a \sqrt{2g}}}$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Flow Architecture Through an External Cylindrical Mouth Piece (Block-Level Functional Map) The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture of fluid flow** through an external cylindrical mouth piece, including the formation of the vena contracta, the vacuum region, and the discharge augmentation logic. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Tank Reservoir
Static Head H
Pressure p_atm] --> B[Approach Region
Streamlines Converge
Velocity approx 0] B --> C[Mouthpiece Entry
Circular Sharp Edge
Area a] C --> D[Vena Contracta
Section xx
Area a_xx = Cc x a
Pressure Head h_xx = 0.444H] D --> E[Expansion Zone
Flow Diverges to Fill Tube
Sub atmospheric Vacuum Region] E --> F[Mouthpiece Exit
Full Bore Jet
Area a
Velocity V = Cv x sqrt of 2gH] F --> G[Atmospheric Discharge
Q = Cd x a x sqrt of 2gH
Cd = 0.855] H[Cc = 1.0] -.Forces Jet to fill Tube.-> E I[Cv = 0.855] -.Accounts for Friction Loss.-> F J[Vacuum Delta p = 0.556 rho g H] -.Augments Discharge.-> G style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000 style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style C fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 style D fill:#ffccff,stroke:#990099,color:#000 style E fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style F fill:#e1e1ff,stroke:#000099,color:#000 style G fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#333333,color:#000 style H fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900,color:#000 style I fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900,color:#000 style J fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Classification Flow Chart of Mouth Pieces ```mermaid flowchart TD MP[Mouth Pieces] --> EC[External Cylindrical
Cd = 0.855] MP --> IC[Internal Cylindrical
Borda Type
Cd = 0.72] MP --> CV[Convergent
Cd = 0.97] MP --> DV[Divergent
Optimum Angle 6 deg
Cd approx 0.99] EC --> EC1[Length equals 2 to 3 D] EC --> EC2[Tube projects Outside Tank] EC --> EC3[No Contraction at Exit
Cc = 1] IC --> IC1[Tube projects Inside Tank] IC --> IC2[Used for Thick Walls] IC --> IC3[Jet does not fill Tube
Cc = 0.62] CV --> CV1[Area decreases in Flow Direction] CV --> CV2[High Cv] DV --> DV1[Area increases in Flow Direction] DV --> DV2[Acts as Submerged Nozzle] DV --> DV3[Risk of Flow Separation
if Angle too Large] style MP fill:#ccccff,stroke:#3333cc,color:#000 style EC fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900,color:#000 style IC fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 style CV fill:#ccccff,stroke:#000099,color:#000 style DV fill:#ffccff,stroke:#990099,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Physical Schematic — Annotated Description > *Note:* As per the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards, complex physical free-body sketches of fluid jets are rendered below as a **structured textual schematic** for full KTU examination compatibility. ``` +-------------------------------+ | LIQUID SURFACE | <-- p_atm | (Head = H) | | | | o o o | | o o | | o TANK o | | o o | | o o o | | | | | v (flow) | +-------------|-----------------+ | +---------+---------+ <-- Mouthpiece Entry | | (Area = a) | (Approach) | | \ | | \ | <-- Vena Contracta (xx) | ) | (Area = a_xx) | / <-- p < p_atm (Pressure Head = 0.444H) | / | +------| |---------+ <-- Mouthpiece Exit | | (Jet Area = a, full bore) | | | | | | |v| <-- Q = 0.855 a sqrt(2gH) | | '-' to atmosphere ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]` **Define a mouth piece. How does it differ from a simple orifice?** **Model Answer:** A **mouth piece** is a short cylindrical tube, whose length is generally **2 to 3 times its diameter**, fitted to a circular opening in a tank. It is used to increase the rate of discharge. **Key Differences from a Sharp-Edged Orifice:** | Feature | Sharp-Edged Orifice | Mouth Piece | |---|---|---| | Geometry | Flat plate with a hole | Short tube of length $2D$ to $3D$ | | $C_c$ | 0.62 to 0.65 | 1.0 (external) | | $C_d$ | 0.62 to 0.65 | 0.855 (external) | | Pressure at vena contracta | Atmospheric | Sub-atmospheric (vacuum) | | Discharge for same $a, H$ | Lower | Higher (by factor ≈ 1.38) | The presence of the tube walls forces the contracted jet to expand back and fill the bore, suppressing natural contraction and producing a partial vacuum that draws in extra flow. *(3 marks: 1 definition + 2 difference points)* --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 3]` **State the values of $C_c$, $C_v$, and $C_d$ for an external cylindrical mouth piece. Why is $C_c = 1$?** **Model Answer:** For an **external cylindrical mouth piece**: - $C_c = 1.00$ - $C_v = 0.855$ - $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v = 0.855$ **Reason for $C_c = 1$:** The mouth piece is a rigid tube. Although the jet contracts *inside* the tube at the vena contracta, the diverging walls force it to expand and **completely fill the tube cross-section** at the exit. Since the area of the issuing jet equals the area of the mouth piece, $C_c = a_{jet} / a = 1$. *(3 marks: 1 for values + 2 for explanation)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Module 3 questions of 14 marks include an **internal choice** between two questions. You must attempt **one** complete 14-mark answer. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]` **(a)** Derive an expression for the discharge through an external cylindrical mouth piece. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A tank has a circular external cylindrical mouth piece of diameter **10 cm** fitted to its vertical wall. Find the discharge through the mouth piece when the constant head above the mouth piece is **4.5 m**. Take $C_d = 0.855$. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to A(a):** Refer to the complete derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The final boxed result is: $$Q = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$$ with the following key intermediate steps to be shown in the answer script: 1. Bernoulli between free surface and exit: $H = V^2/2g$ 2. Actual velocity $V = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$ 3. Because $C_c = 1$, jet area at exit = $a$ 4. Discharge $Q = a \cdot V = C_v \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH} = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ *(Award 2 marks for setup, 3 marks for Bernoulli application, 2 marks for final expression.)* #### **Model Solution to A(b):** **Given:** - Diameter $D = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.10 \text{ m}$ - $H = 4.5 \text{ m}$ - $C_d = 0.855$ - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ **Step 1 — Compute the mouth piece area.** $$a = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.10)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Compute the theoretical velocity.** $$V_{th} = \sqrt{2gH} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 4.5}$$ $$V_{th} = \sqrt{88.29} = 9.397 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the discharge formula.** $$Q = C_d \cdot a \cdot V_{th} = 0.855 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.397$$ $$Q = 0.855 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.397 = 0.0631 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$\boxed{Q \approx 63.1 \text{ litres/s}}$$ *(Award 2 marks for area, 2 marks for velocity, 3 marks for final Q. Full marks only with units.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]` **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of an external cylindrical mouth piece. State the values of its coefficients. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A cylindrical tank of diameter **2.0 m** and height **3.5 m** is fitted with an external mouth piece of diameter **8 cm** at its base. The tank is initially filled to a height of **3.0 m**. Find the **time required to bring the water level down to 0.5 m**. Take $C_d = 0.855$. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to B(a):** **Working Principle:** 1. Liquid in the tank exerts hydrostatic pressure at the level of the mouth piece, equivalent to a head $H$. 2. Streamlines converge on entry, forming a **vena contracta** inside the tube at section xx. 3. The tube walls are diverging with respect to the contracted jet, forcing the jet to **expand and fill the entire bore** at the exit (this is why $C_c = 1$). 4. A region of **sub-atmospheric pressure (vacuum)** exists at the vena contracta, equal in head to $0.444H$. 5. This vacuum **sucks additional liquid** into the tube, increasing the discharge. **Coefficient Values:** - $C_c = 1.00$ - $C_v = 0.855$ - $C_d = 0.855$ *(Award 3 marks for sketch description, 2 marks for working principle, 2 marks for coefficient values.)* #### **Model Solution to B(b):** **Given:** - Tank diameter $D_t = 2.0 \text{ m} \Rightarrow$ Tank area $A = \frac{\pi}{4}(2.0)^2 = \pi = 3.1416 \text{ m}^2$ - Mouth piece diameter $d = 0.08 \text{ m} \Rightarrow a = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.08)^2 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$ - Initial head $H_1 = 3.0 \text{ m}$ - Final head $H_2 = 0.5 \text{ m}$ - $C_d = 0.855$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ **Step 1 — Write the time-of-emptying formula (between two heads).** $$t = \frac{2A}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \left( \sqrt{H_1} - \sqrt{H_2} \right)$$ **Step 2 — Compute the constant factor.** $$\frac{2A}{C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2g}} = \frac{2 \times 3.1416}{0.855 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81}}$$ $$= \frac{6.2832}{0.855 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.4294}$$ $$= \frac{6.2832}{0.01904} = 330.0 \text{ s/m}^{1/2}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the head-difference term.** $$\sqrt{H_1} - \sqrt{H_2} = \sqrt{3.0} - \sqrt{0.5} = 1.7321 - 0.7071 = 1.0250 \text{ m}^{1/2}$$ **Step 4 — Final time.** $$t = 330.0 \times 1.0250 = 338.2 \text{ seconds}$$ $$\boxed{t \approx 338.2 \text{ s} \approx 5 \text{ min } 38 \text{ s}}$$ *(Award 2 marks for formula, 2 marks for area/substitutions, 3 marks for arithmetic + units.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to use $C_d = 0.855$ and accidentally using $0.62$ (orifice value):** You will get the *wrong magnitude* of discharge and lose all 7 application marks. Always read the question — it says "mouth piece," not "orifice." > 2. **Confusing static pressure head ($0.444H$) with dynamic head ($0.556H$):** When a question says "vacuum at vena contracta," it means the *static pressure* head is $0.444H$. When it says "velocity head at vena contracta," it means $0.556H$. Mixing these up costs 2 marks. > 3. **Unit slip-ups:** $D$ in cm must be converted to **metres** before computing area. Mark penalty: **−1 mark** per KTU valuation key. > 4. **Time-of-emptying formula misuse:** Some students write $\frac{A}{C_d a}\sqrt{2gH}$ instead of the correct $\frac{2A}{C_d a \sqrt{2g}}\sqrt{H}$. The factor of **2** is critical and frequently omitted. > 5. **Using $g = 10$ instead of $9.81$:** KTU's 2024 Scheme has started deducting ½ mark for imprecise $g$ in numerical problems. Use **$9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$** unless the question explicitly says otherwise. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Mouth piece** = short tube of length $2D$ to $3D$ fitted to an opening. - ✅ **External cylindrical mouth piece:** $C_c = 1$, $C_v = 0.855$, $C_d = 0.855$. - ✅ **Internal (Borda) mouth piece:** $C_c = 0.62$, $C_d = 0.72$. - ✅ **Static pressure head at vena contracta** = $0.444H$. - ✅ **Dynamic (velocity) head at vena contracta** = $0.556H$. - ✅ **Discharge formula** (universal): $Q = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$. - ✅ **Time to empty (between $H_1$ and $H_2$):** $t = \dfrac{2A}{C_d a \sqrt{2g}} \left( \sqrt{H_1} - \sqrt{H_2} \right)$. - ✅ **Time to completely empty:** $t = \dfrac{2A\sqrt{H_1}}{C_d a \sqrt{2g}}$. - ✅ **Maximum safe head** ≈ 18.58 m (above which the mouth piece chokes due to limiting vacuum). - ✅ **Discharge augmentation** vs. simple orifice ≈ **38% higher** for external mouth piece. - ✅ **Use $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$** unless otherwise specified. - ✅ **Convergent mouth piece** has the highest $C_d \approx 0.97$ — used in fire nozzles. - ✅ **Divergent mouth piece** is efficient only up to an included angle of about **6°**; beyond this, flow separation occurs. - ✅ **Vacuum at vena contracta is the *cause* of increased discharge** — remember this for any "explain why" type theory question.

topics.title: Notches and Weirs (Derivation of Discharges Only for Basic Geometries)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Notches and Weirs (Derivation of Discharges Only for Basic Geometries)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Notch?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** A **notch** is an opening made in the side wall of a tank or reservoir, generally of a regulated geometric shape (rectangular, triangular, or trapezoidal), through which **liquid flows under atmospheric pressure** when the liquid surface is above the bottom edge of the opening.

Notches are typically **small in size**, cut into thin plates (metal or masonry), and are primarily used as **measuring devices** in laboratories and small-scale fluid measurement applications (e.g., volumetric flow calibration).

## 1.2 What is a Weir?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** A **weir** is a **low obstruction** (in the form of a barrier or dam) built across an open channel or stream over which water flows. Weirs are generally **larger structures** made of masonry or concrete and are used for flow measurement, flood control, and diversion.

The fundamental hydraulic difference: both operate on the **same governing principle** (overflow under gravity), but **notches are small openings** cut in tank walls while **weirs are large barriers** across flowing streams.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **bathroom washbasin** with a small rectangular cut at its side edge near the bottom — that is essentially a **rectangular notch**. Water flows out due to gravity. Now imagine a **concrete check-dam built across a small stream** in the Western Ghats of Kerala to store water for irrigation — that is essentially a **rectangular weir**. The physics is identical; only the **scale and geometry** differ.

A **V-shaped cut** at the bottom of a measuring tank acts as a **triangular notch** (V-notch). The pointed bottom creates a wider stream at higher heads, making it highly sensitive to small flow changes — useful for laboratory work. A **trapezoidal notch** is a combination (rectangle + two triangles), where the triangular sides are deliberately sloped to **compensate for end contractions** in the rectangular portion.

## 1.4 Classification of Notches and Weirs

**Based on Shape (Geometry):**
- **Rectangular Notch/Weir** — most common, simple straight edge
- **Triangular (V-Notch) Notch/Weir** — V-shaped opening
- **Trapezoidal (Cipolletti) Notch/Weir** — trapezoidal opening
- **Stepped Notch** — combination of rectangular notches at different levels

**Based on the Crest (Top Edge):**
- **Sharp-Crested Weir** — thin plate with a sharp upstream edge
- **Broad-Crested Weir** — flat horizontal crest of substantial length

**Based on End Conditions (Rectangular):**
- **Contracted Weir** — smaller than the channel width (side contractions present)
- **Suppressed Weir** — equal to channel width (no end contractions)

## 1.5 Key Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Head (H):** The vertical height of liquid above the crest (bottom edge) of the notch/weir, measured upstream where the flow is undisturbed.
>
> **Crest:** The lowest edge of the opening through which liquid flows.
>
> **Coefficient of Discharge (Cd):** The ratio of **actual discharge** to **theoretical discharge**. For a sharp-edged orifice/notch, **Cd ≈ 0.62** (notch) and **Cd ≈ 0.611 to 0.65** (sharp-crested weir), depending on geometry.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Physical Principle

The flow of liquid through a notch or over a weir is governed by **Torricelli's Theorem** (a direct consequence of Bernoulli's Equation applied between the free surface and the vena contracta). For any horizontal elemental strip at depth *h* below the free surface:

$$v = \sqrt{2gh}$$

This assumes the velocity at the free surface is negligible (true when the upstream tank/channel is very large compared to the opening).

## 2.2 The Discharge Derivation Logic (Universal 5-Step Method)

Every basic geometry follows this rigorous **5-step framework**:

1. **Identify the geometry of the elemental strip** at depth *h* from the free surface. Determine the width (or radius) of the strip as a function of *h* and the total head *H*.
2. **Compute the elemental area** $dA = (\text{width}) \cdot dh$
3. **Apply Torricelli's velocity:** $v = \sqrt{2gh}$
4. **Write elemental discharge:** $dQ = C_d \cdot dA \cdot v = C_d \cdot (\text{width}) \cdot dh \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$
5. **Integrate** with respect to *h* from 0 to *H* to obtain the total discharge *Q*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vena Contracta:** Just downstream of the notch, the jet contracts to a minimum cross-sectional area known as the vena contracta. This is the region where the coefficient of discharge $C_d$ is applied. The theoretical formula assumes no contraction; the actual discharge is multiplied by $C_d$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Geometry | Theoretical Discharge $Q_{th}$ | Actual Discharge $Q = C_d \cdot Q_{th}$ | Typical $C_d$ Value | Units of H, b |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rectangular Notch/Weir** (width *b*, head *H*) | $\dfrac{2}{3} \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$ | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$ | **0.62 – 0.66** | metres, metres |
| **Triangular (V-Notch)** (full angle $2\theta$, head *H*) | $\dfrac{8}{15} \cdot \tan\theta \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$ | $Q = \dfrac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \tan\theta \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$ | **0.60 – 0.62** | metres, — |
| **Right-Angled V-Notch** ($2\theta = 90°$, $\tan\theta = 1$) | $\dfrac{8}{15} \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$ | $Q = \dfrac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$ | **0.60 – 0.62** | metres, — |
| **Trapezoidal (Cipolletti) Weir** (bottom *b*, slope 1H:4V, head *H*) | $\dfrac{2}{3} \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$ | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$ | **0.62 – 0.63** | metres, metres |
| **Stepped Notch** (rectangular strips at heights $h_1, h_2, h_3$) | $\dfrac{2}{3} \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot (H_2^{3/2} - H_1^{3/2} + H_3^{3/2} - \ldots)$ | $Q = C_d \cdot Q_{th}$ | **0.62** | metres, metres |

**Constants Used:**
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (acceleration due to gravity)
- $\sqrt{2g} = 4.429 \text{ m}^{1/2}\text{/s}$

## 2.4 Engineering Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why These Formulas Matter in Practice:**
> 1. **Calibration of Flow Meters** — Notches and weirs serve as **primary flow standards** in hydraulic laboratories.
> 2. **Irrigation Engineering** — Trapezoidal (Cipolletti) weirs are routinely used in **canal headworks** for measuring irrigation water supply.
> 3. **Dam Spillway Design** — Sharp-crested and broad-crested weirs form the basis of **spillway capacity calculations**.
> 4. **Wastewater Treatment** — V-notch weirs are used in **effluent flow measurement** because they offer superior accuracy at low discharges.
> 5. **KTU Examination Tip** — In KTU 2024 Scheme exams, problems typically ask to derive the discharge for one geometry and then apply it numerically.

## 2.5 Comparison of Basic Geometries

| Geometry | Head Sensitivity | Best Suited For | Numerical Head Power |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangular | Moderate ($H^{3/2}$) | Moderate to high discharges, large tanks | $H^{3/2}$ |
| Triangular (V-notch) | High ($H^{5/2}$) | Low discharges, high-accuracy lab work | $H^{5/2}$ |
| Trapezoidal | Moderate ($H^{3/2}$) | Channels where end contractions must be eliminated | $H^{3/2}$ |
| Stepped | Compound | Variable-head storage tanks | Compound |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Rectangular Notch / Weir

**Setup:** Consider a rectangular notch of width *b* cut into the vertical side wall of a tank. The free surface of the liquid is at a height *H* above the crest of the notch (sill). Liquid flows out through the notch under atmospheric pressure.

**Reference Diagram Setup (Coordinate System):**
- Let the **crest of the notch** be the datum (origin).
- Measure *h* vertically **downward** from the free surface.
- An elemental horizontal strip is located at depth *h* below the free surface, with thickness *dh*.
- The width of the strip equals the notch width *b*.

**Step 1: Geometry of the elemental strip**

Since the strip is horizontal and parallel to the free surface, its width is constant and equal to the notch width *b*.

$$\text{Width of strip} = b$$

**Step 2: Area of the elemental strip**

$$dA = (\text{width}) \times (\text{thickness}) = b \cdot dh$$

**Step 3: Velocity of flow through the strip**

Applying Torricelli's Theorem (a special case of Bernoulli's Equation between the free surface, where pressure is atmospheric and velocity ≈ 0, and the strip location, where pressure is also atmospheric) — the velocity of liquid through the elemental strip is:

$$v = \sqrt{2gh}$$

where *h* is the depth of the strip below the free surface.

**Step 4: Elemental discharge through the strip**

The theoretical elemental discharge is the product of the elemental area and the velocity. To convert theoretical to actual discharge, multiply by the coefficient of discharge $C_d$:

$$dQ = C_d \cdot dA \cdot v = C_d \cdot (b \cdot dh) \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$

**Step 5: Integrate over the full head**

The total discharge *Q* is obtained by integrating *dQ* from $h = 0$ (free surface) to $h = H$ (crest):

$$Q = \int_{0}^{H} C_d \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot h^{1/2} \cdot dh$$

Since $C_d$, $b$, and $\sqrt{2g}$ are constants, they are pulled outside the integral:

$$Q = C_d \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \int_{0}^{H} h^{1/2} \cdot dh$$

**Step 6: Evaluate the integral**

$$\int_{0}^{H} h^{1/2} \cdot dh = \left[ \frac{h^{3/2}}{3/2} \right]_{0}^{H} = \left[ \frac{2}{3} \cdot h^{3/2} \right]_{0}^{H} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot H^{3/2} - 0 = \frac{2}{3} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

**Step 7: Substitute back**

$$Q = C_d \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The discharge is proportional to the **width** of the notch and to the **3/2 power of the head**. This is the foundation of all sharp-crested weir equations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Triangular (V-Notch) Notch

**Setup:** Consider a V-shaped notch with apex at the bottom and a total included angle of $2\theta$. The free surface is at a height *H* above the apex.

**Step 1: Geometry of the elemental strip**

Consider a horizontal elemental strip at a depth *h* below the free surface (and therefore at a vertical distance of $(H - h)$ above the apex of the V-notch).

By similar triangles, the width of the strip at this height is:

$$\text{Width of strip} = 2(H - h)\tan\theta$$

**Step 2: Area of the elemental strip**

$$dA = 2(H - h)\tan\theta \cdot dh$$

**Step 3: Velocity of flow through the strip**

By Torricelli's Theorem:

$$v = \sqrt{2gh}$$

**Step 4: Elemental discharge through the strip**

$$dQ = C_d \cdot dA \cdot v = C_d \cdot 2(H - h)\tan\theta \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \cdot dh$$

**Step 5: Integrate over the full head from 0 to H**

$$Q = 2 \cdot C_d \cdot \tan\theta \cdot \sqrt{2g} \int_{0}^{H} (H - h) \cdot h^{1/2} \cdot dh$$

**Step 6: Expand the integrand**

$$(H - h) \cdot h^{1/2} = H \cdot h^{1/2} - h \cdot h^{1/2} = H \cdot h^{1/2} - h^{3/2}$$

**Step 7: Split the integral**

$$Q = 2 \cdot C_d \cdot \tan\theta \cdot \sqrt{2g} \left[ H \int_{0}^{H} h^{1/2} \, dh - \int_{0}^{H} h^{3/2} \, dh \right]$$

**Step 8: Evaluate each integral separately**

First integral:

$$\int_{0}^{H} h^{1/2} \, dh = \left[ \frac{h^{3/2}}{3/2} \right]_{0}^{H} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

Second integral:

$$\int_{0}^{H} h^{3/2} \, dh = \left[ \frac{h^{5/2}}{5/2} \right]_{0}^{H} = \frac{2}{5} \cdot H^{5/2}$$

**Step 9: Substitute and combine**

$$Q = 2 \cdot C_d \cdot \tan\theta \cdot \sqrt{2g} \left[ H \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot H^{3/2} - \frac{2}{5} \cdot H^{5/2} \right]$$

$$Q = 2 \cdot C_d \cdot \tan\theta \cdot \sqrt{2g} \left[ \frac{2}{3} \cdot H^{5/2} - \frac{2}{5} \cdot H^{5/2} \right]$$

**Step 10: Factor out $H^{5/2}$**

$$Q = 2 \cdot C_d \cdot \tan\theta \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2} \left[ \frac{2}{3} - \frac{2}{5} \right]$$

**Step 11: Simplify the bracket**

$$\frac{2}{3} - \frac{2}{5} = \frac{10 - 6}{15} = \frac{4}{15}$$

**Step 12: Final formula**

$$Q = 2 \cdot C_d \cdot \tan\theta \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2} \cdot \frac{4}{15}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \tan\theta \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}}$$

**Special Case:** For a **right-angled V-notch**, $2\theta = 90°$, so $\theta = 45°$ and $\tan\theta = 1$:

$$Q = \frac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Trapezoidal (Cipolletti) Notch / Weir

**Setup:** A Cipolletti weir is a trapezoidal weir with sides sloping **1 horizontal to 4 vertical**. This specific slope is chosen so that the **discharge increase** due to the two triangular portions exactly **compensates** for the reduction in discharge caused by the end contractions in the rectangular portion.

The discharge through a Cipolletti weir is therefore the discharge through a **rectangular portion** of width *b* (the horizontal bottom) **plus** the discharge through **two identical triangular portions** at the sides.

**Step 1: Decomposition**

$$Q_{Cipolletti} = Q_{Rectangular} + 2 \cdot Q_{Triangular}$$

**Step 2: Discharge through the rectangular portion (width b, head H)**

From the rectangular notch derivation:

$$Q_{Rectangular} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

**Step 3: Discharge through one triangular portion**

For a triangular portion with horizontal length *L* at the top (at free surface), the corresponding angle $\theta$ satisfies:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{L/2}{H}$$

For Cipolletti's 1H:4V slope, the horizontal length at the free surface equals *H/2* (i.e., 1 horizontal per 4 vertical):

$$\tan\theta = \frac{H/4}{H} = \frac{1}{4}$$

**Step 4: Discharge through one triangular portion**

$$Q_{Triangular, \, one} = \frac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \tan\theta \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2} = \frac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \frac{1}{4} \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2} = \frac{2}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$$

**Step 5: Discharge through two triangular portions**

$$2 \cdot Q_{Triangular} = \frac{4}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$$

**Step 6: Total Cipolletti discharge**

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2} + \frac{4}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$$

**Step 7: Simplification for the standard Cipolletti form**

By definition of the Cipolletti weir, the combined formula reduces to the elegant expression:

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}}$$

This is **numerically identical** to the rectangular weir formula but uses the **bottom width *b*** and a **Cipolletti coefficient of discharge** (≈ 0.63), making it free of end contraction corrections.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Stepped Notch

**Setup:** A stepped notch consists of **multiple rectangular notches at different vertical levels**, often used in storage tanks and compound measuring devices.

Consider two stepped rectangular notches of widths $b_1$ and $b_2$. The first notch starts from a height $H_1$ above some datum and the second notch is positioned higher. A stepped notch with multiple horizontal segments behaves as the **sum of discharges** through each rectangular segment independently.

**Generic Form:** For a stepped notch with *n* rectangular sections having widths $b_i$ and corresponding heads $H_i$:

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2g} \left[ b_1 \left(H_2^{3/2} - H_1^{3/2}\right) + b_2 \left(H_3^{3/2} - H_2^{3/2}\right) + \ldots + b_n \left(H_{n+1}^{3/2} - H_n^{3/2}\right) \right]}$$

**Special Case:** For a simple stepped notch with two sections (lower notch of width $b_1$ and upper notch of width $b_2$):

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2g} \left[ b_1 H_1^{3/2} + b_2 \left(H_2^{3/2} - H_1^{3/2}\right) \right]$$

where $H_1$ is the head over the lower step and $H_2$ is the head over the upper step.

---

## 3.5 Summary Derivations Matrix

| Geometry | Final Discharge Formula | Head Power | Notable Coefficient |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangular | $\dfrac{2}{3} C_d b \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2}$ | $H^{3/2}$ | $\dfrac{2}{3}$ |
| Triangular | $\dfrac{8}{15} C_d \tan\theta \sqrt{2g} H^{5/2}$ | $H^{5/2}$ | $\dfrac{8}{15}$ |
| Right-Angle V | $\dfrac{8}{15} C_d \sqrt{2g} H^{5/2}$ | $H^{5/2}$ | $\dfrac{8}{15}$ |
| Cipolletti | $\dfrac{2}{3} C_d b \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2}$ | $H^{3/2}$ | $\dfrac{2}{3}$ (no contraction correction) |
| Stepped | Sum of $\dfrac{2}{3} C_d b_i \sqrt{2g} H_i^{3/2}$ terms | Mixed | $\dfrac{2}{3}$ per term |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Universal Discharge Derivation Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **universal 5-step methodology** that governs every notch/weir geometry derivation in this module:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Notch or Weir Geometry Given] --> B[Step 1: Identify Cross-Section Type at Depth h Below Free Surface]
    B --> B1[Rectangular: Width equals constant b]
    B --> B2[Triangular: Width equals 2 times H minus h times tan theta]
    B --> B3[Trapezoidal: Rectangular bottom plus two triangular sides]
    B --> B4[Stepped: Multiple rectangular segments at different heights]
    B1 --> C[Step 2: Compute Elemental Area dA equal to width times dh]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> D[Step 3: Apply Torricellis Theorem v equals sqrt of 2gh]
    D --> E[Step 4: Write Elemental Discharge dQ equals Cd times dA times v]
    E --> F[Step 5: Integrate dQ from h equals 0 to h equals H]
    F --> G{Geometry Type?}
    G -->|Rectangular| H1[Q equals two-thirds Cd b sqrt 2g H to the power 3 over 2]
    G -->|Triangular| H2[Q equals eight-fifteenths Cd tan theta sqrt 2g H to the power 5 over 2]
    G -->|Cipolletti| H3[Q equals two-thirds Cd b sqrt 2g H to the power 3 over 2]
    G -->|Stepped| H4[Q equals sum of two-thirds Cd bi sqrt 2g Hi to the power 3 over 2]
    H1 --> I[END: Apply C_d and Substitute Numerical Values]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Classification of Notches & Weirs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Notches and Weirs] --> B[Classification by Shape]
    A --> C[Classification by End Condition]
    A --> D[Classification by Crest]
    B --> B1[Rectangular]
    B --> B2[Triangular V Notch]
    B --> B3[Trapezoidal Cipolletti]
    B --> B4[Stepped]
    C --> C1[Contracted Weir Notch smaller than channel]
    C --> C2[Suppressed Weir Notch equal to channel width]
    D --> D1[Sharp Crested Weir thin plate]
    D --> D2[Broad Crested Weir flat horizontal crest]
    B1 --> E1[Head power H to 3 over 2]
    B2 --> E2[Head power H to 5 over 2]
    B3 --> E3[Head power H to 3 over 2 no contraction correction]
    B4 --> E4[Compound head powers]
```

## 4.3 Elemental Strip Schematic (Rectangular Notch)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Free Surface at head H above crest] --> B[Atmospheric Pressure at top]
    B --> C[Elemental Strip located at depth h below free surface]
    C --> D[Strip has width b and thickness dh]
    D --> E[Liquid enters strip with velocity v equals sqrt 2gh]
    E --> F[Strip discharges into atmosphere downstream]
    F --> G[Vena Contracta forms just downstream of crest]
    G --> H[Actual discharge equals Cd times theoretical discharge]
```

## 4.4 Geometry of Triangular Notch Strip

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[V Notch with apex at bottom and full angle 2 theta] --> B[Free Surface at height H above apex]
    B --> C[Elemental Strip at depth h below free surface]
    C --> D[Vertical distance from apex equals H minus h]
    D --> E[Strip half width equals H minus h times tan theta by similar triangles]
    E --> F[Full strip width equals 2 times H minus h times tan theta]
    F --> G[Strip area dA equals 2 H minus h tan theta times dh]
    G --> H[Strip velocity equals sqrt 2gh by Torricelli]
    H --> I[Integrate dQ from 0 to H to obtain total Q]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Differentiate between a notch and a weir. State the assumptions made in the derivation of discharge through a rectangular notch.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Notch | Weir |
|---|---|---|
| Size | Small opening | Large obstruction |
| Plate | Thin metal plate | Masonry or concrete barrier |
| Purpose | Laboratory measurement | Channel/river flow measurement |
| Location | Side of a tank | Across an open channel |

**Assumptions in Rectangular Notch Discharge Derivation:**

1. The liquid surface is horizontal and remains at a constant elevation (steady flow).
2. The velocity of approach at the free surface is negligible (upstream tank is large).
3. The pressure at the free surface and at the notch exit is atmospheric (free discharge).
4. The coefficient of discharge $C_d$ is constant throughout the integration (assumed uniform over the entire head).
5. The flow is one-dimensional and the velocity distribution is uniform across each elemental strip.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** A right-angled V-notch is used to measure a discharge of 25 L/s. If $C_d = 0.62$, find the head over the apex of the notch. Take $\sqrt{2g} = 4.429 \text{ m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$.

**Model Answer:**

For a right-angled V-notch ($2\theta = 90°$, $\tan\theta = 1$):

$$Q = \frac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$$

Given: $Q = 25 \text{ L/s} = 0.025 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $C_d = 0.62$, $\sqrt{2g} = 4.429$

Substituting:

$$0.025 = \frac{8}{15} \times 0.62 \times 4.429 \times H^{5/2}$$

$$0.025 = 1.4652 \cdot H^{5/2}$$

$$H^{5/2} = \frac{0.025}{1.4652} = 0.01706$$

$$H = (0.01706)^{2/5}$$

Computing: $0.01706^{0.4}$:

$$\ln(0.01706) = -4.071$$

$$0.4 \times (-4.071) = -1.6284$$

$$e^{-1.6284} = 0.1964$$

$$\boxed{H \approx 0.196 \text{ m} = 196 \text{ mm}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Format:** Two questions of 14 marks each are provided as **internal choice alternatives**. Solve either Q-A or Q-B in full.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 3]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the discharge through a **triangular (V-notch)** with full included angle $2\theta$ and head $H$ above the apex. Assume coefficient of discharge $C_d$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular notch 1.2 m wide is used to measure the discharge of water. If the head over the notch is 250 mm and $C_d = 0.62$, calculate the discharge in litres per second. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

**Step 1: Consider an elemental horizontal strip** at depth *h* below the free surface with thickness *dh*.

The vertical distance from the apex to this strip is $(H - h)$.

**Step 2: Width of strip by similar triangles:**

$$w = 2(H - h)\tan\theta$$

**Step 3: Elemental area:**

$$dA = 2(H - h)\tan\theta \, dh$$

**Step 4: Velocity by Torricelli's theorem:**

$$v = \sqrt{2gh}$$

**Step 5: Elemental discharge:**

$$dQ = C_d \cdot 2(H - h)\tan\theta \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \, dh$$

**Step 6: Total discharge by integration from h = 0 to h = H:**

$$Q = 2 C_d \tan\theta \sqrt{2g} \int_{0}^{H} (H - h) h^{1/2} \, dh$$

**Step 7: Expand integrand:**

$$(H - h) h^{1/2} = H h^{1/2} - h^{3/2}$$

**Step 8: Split and evaluate integrals:**

$$\int_{0}^{H} H h^{1/2} \, dh = H \cdot \frac{2}{3} H^{3/2} = \frac{2}{3} H^{5/2}$$

$$\int_{0}^{H} h^{3/2} \, dh = \frac{2}{5} H^{5/2}$$

**Step 9: Combine:**

$$Q = 2 C_d \tan\theta \sqrt{2g} \left[ \frac{2}{3} H^{5/2} - \frac{2}{5} H^{5/2} \right]$$

$$Q = 2 C_d \tan\theta \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2} \left[ \frac{10 - 6}{15} \right]$$

$$Q = 2 C_d \tan\theta \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2} \cdot \frac{4}{15}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \tan\theta \sqrt{2g} H^{5/2}}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**Given:**
- Width of rectangular notch: $b = 1.2 \text{ m}$
- Head over the notch: $H = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$
- Coefficient of discharge: $C_d = 0.62$
- $\sqrt{2g} = 4.429 \text{ m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$

**Formula for rectangular notch:**

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d b \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2}$$

**Step 1: Compute $H^{3/2}$:**

$$H^{3/2} = (0.25)^{3/2} = 0.25 \times \sqrt{0.25} = 0.25 \times 0.5 = 0.125 \text{ m}^{3/2}$$

**Step 2: Substitute all values:**

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 1.2 \times 4.429 \times 0.125$$

**Step 3: Multiply sequentially:**

$$\frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 = 0.4133$$

$$0.4133 \times 1.2 = 0.4960$$

$$0.4960 \times 4.429 = 2.1968$$

$$2.1968 \times 0.125 = 0.2746 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4: Convert to litres per second:**

$$Q = 0.2746 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \times 1000 \text{ L/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{Q = 274.6 \text{ L/s}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 3]`

**(a)** Derive the discharge expression for a **rectangular notch** of width *b* with head *H* over the crest. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Cipolletti weir has a crest width of 0.9 m. The head over the weir is 300 mm. If $C_d = 0.63$, calculate the discharge in cubic metres per second. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

**Step 1: Consider an elemental horizontal strip** at depth *h* below the free surface, with thickness *dh* and width *b* (constant).

**Step 2: Elemental area:**

$$dA = b \cdot dh$$

**Step 3: Velocity (Torricelli's theorem):**

$$v = \sqrt{2gh}$$

**Step 4: Elemental discharge:**

$$dQ = C_d \cdot b \cdot dh \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$

**Step 5: Integrate from 0 to H:**

$$Q = C_d \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \int_{0}^{H} h^{1/2} \, dh$$

**Step 6: Evaluate the integral:**

$$\int_{0}^{H} h^{1/2} \, dh = \left[ \frac{2}{3} h^{3/2} \right]_{0}^{H} = \frac{2}{3} H^{3/2}$$

**Step 7: Substitute:**

$$Q = C_d \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \frac{2}{3} H^{3/2}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d b \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2}}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

**Given:**
- Crest width: $b = 0.9 \text{ m}$
- Head: $H = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.3 \text{ m}$
- $C_d = 0.63$ (Cipolletti)
- $\sqrt{2g} = 4.429 \text{ m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$

**Cipolletti weir formula:**

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d b \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2}$$

**Step 1: Compute $H^{3/2}$:**

$$H^{3/2} = (0.3)^{3/2} = 0.3 \times \sqrt{0.3}$$

$$\sqrt{0.3} = 0.5477$$

$$H^{3/2} = 0.3 \times 0.5477 = 0.1643 \text{ m}^{3/2}$$

**Step 2: Substitute all values:**

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.63 \times 0.9 \times 4.429 \times 0.1643$$

**Step 3: Multiply sequentially:**

$$\frac{2}{3} \times 0.63 = 0.42$$

$$0.42 \times 0.9 = 0.378$$

$$0.378 \times 4.429 = 1.6742$$

$$1.6742 \times 0.1643 = 0.2751 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 0.275 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 275 \text{ L/s}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting $C_d$:** Students frequently write the **theoretical discharge** formula without the coefficient of discharge, losing 2 marks. Always check whether the question gives $C_d$ or asks for the **actual discharge**.
> 2. **Confusing $\tan\theta$ and the full angle:** For V-notch problems, ensure you correctly identify $\theta$ as the **half-angle** of the V-notch. If the question says "60° V-notch", then $2\theta = 60°$ and $\theta = 30°$, so $\tan\theta = 0.5774$, **not** $\tan 60° = 1.732$.
> 3. **Unit Conversion Mistakes:** Converting the final answer to L/s requires multiplying by 1000. Forgetting this costs 1 mark. Similarly, head must always be in **metres** before substitution.
> 4. **Integration Errors:** In the V-notch derivation, the most common mistake is **forgetting to expand** $(H-h) \cdot h^{1/2}$ correctly into $H \cdot h^{1/2} - h^{3/2}$. Use the box-by-box integration approach shown in Section 3.2.
> 5. **Cipolletti Confusion:** Many students wrongly apply end-contraction corrections to a Cipolletti weir. Remember: the Cipolletti slope is **specifically designed to eliminate** end contractions, so **no contraction correction** is needed.
> 6. **Numerical Rounding:** Carry at least 4 significant digits through intermediate steps to avoid compounding errors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap: Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Notches and Weirs (KTU Module 3):**

- **Definition:** Notches are **small openings** in tank walls; Weirs are **large obstructions** across open channels. Same physics.
- **Governing Law:** Torricelli's Theorem: $v = \sqrt{2gh}$, derived from Bernoulli's Equation.
- **Universal Method:** All four basic geometries follow the same **5-step framework** (geometry → dA → v → dQ → integrate).
- **Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$):**
  - Rectangular notch: $C_d \approx 0.62$
  - Triangular V-notch: $C_d \approx 0.60$ to $0.62$
  - Cipolletti weir: $C_d \approx 0.63$
- **Rectangular Notch Formula:** $Q = \dfrac{2}{3} C_d b \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2}$
- **Triangular V-Notch Formula:** $Q = \dfrac{8}{15} C_d \tan\theta \sqrt{2g} H^{5/2}$
- **Right-Angled V-Notch Special Case:** $\theta = 45°$, $\tan\theta = 1$, giving $Q = \dfrac{8}{15} C_d \sqrt{2g} H^{5/2}$
- **Cipolletti Formula:** Identical to rectangular, but **no end contraction correction** required.
- **Cipolletti Slope:** 1 horizontal : 4 vertical sides — this slope is not arbitrary; it mathematically cancels end contractions.
- **Stepped Notch:** Sum of discharges through each rectangular section.
- **Head Power:** Rectangular and Cipolletti use $H^{3/2}$; Triangular uses $H^{5/2}$ (more sensitive at low heads).
- **Units:** Always use **SI units** — *b* and *H* in metres, *Q* in m³/s, then convert to L/s if required.
- **Standard Constant:** Use $\sqrt{2g} = 4.429 \text{ m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$ in all numerical problems unless specified otherwise.
- **Vena Contracta:** Always the reason $C_d < 1$; the actual jet contracts just downstream of the crest.
- **Assumptions:** Steady flow, negligible velocity of approach, atmospheric pressure everywhere on the free surface, $C_d$ constant.
- **Numerical Pitfall:** For triangular notches, half-angle $\theta$ enters the formula — read the problem carefully to identify whether the given angle is the **half-angle** or the **full angle**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Velocity Measurements Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flow Velocity Measurements Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Flow Velocity Measurement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> Flow velocity measurement techniques refer to the systematic instrumentation methods employed to quantify the local or average velocity of a fluid (liquid or gas) at a specific point or across a cross-section of a flow domain. These techniques are classified based on the underlying physical principle: **mechanical (obstruction-based)**, **hydraulic (pressure-based)**, **thermal (heat-transfer based)**, and **optical (light-scattering based)**.

The accurate determination of fluid velocity is a cornerstone of experimental fluid mechanics. It is essential for calibrating theoretical models, validating Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations, computing discharge in open channels and pipes, and assessing the performance of hydraulic machinery such as turbines and pumps.

## 1.2 Classification of Velocity Measurement Devices

Velocity measurement devices can be broadly classified into the following categories based on the operating principle:

| Category | Operating Principle | Typical Instruments |
|----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Obstruction-type (Mechanical)** | Convert kinetic energy to measurable pressure head using a body placed in the flow | **Pitot Tube**, **Pitot-Static Tube**, Orifice Meter, Venturimeter |
| **Rotation-type (Mechanical)** | Fluid impinges on rotating vanes/cups; speed is proportional to velocity | **Current Meter (Cup/Paddle type)** |
| **Thermal Anemometry** | Heat loss from a heated sensor is a function of local fluid velocity | **Hot-Wire Anemometer (HWA)**, Hot-Film Probe |
| **Optical / Laser-based** | Use light scattering and frequency shift of reflected light | **Laser Doppler Anemometer (LDA / LDV)**, Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) |
| **Float / Tracer** | Visual observation of surface or introduced tracer | Floats, Dye injection, Hydrogen-bubble technique |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: "Why Do We Need Velocity Probes?"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition (Plain English):**
> Imagine driving a car in fog. You cannot see the speedometer of a passing car directly, but you can *feel* the wind pushing against your hand held out of the window. The stronger the push, the faster the car. **A Pitot tube does exactly this** — it converts the invisible "push" of moving fluid into a measurable column of water in a manometer.
>
> Now imagine stirring sugar into a glass of water. The spinning spoon creates a tiny whirlpool. If you put a tiny light-sensitive dust particle in the water, the **color (frequency) of light bouncing off that particle changes** slightly depending on how fast it moves toward or away from you. This frequency shift is what a **Laser Doppler Anemometer** detects to compute velocity without ever touching the fluid.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$
- **Density of water at 4°C:** $\rho_w = 1000 \ \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Specific weight of water:** $\gamma_w = 9810 \ \text{N/m}^3$
- **Stagnation Pressure:** The pressure attained when a flowing fluid is brought to rest **isentropically** (no losses).
- **Static Pressure:** The thermodynamic pressure of the fluid measured by a sensor moving *with* the fluid.
- **Dynamic Pressure:** The kinetic energy per unit volume, $q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$.

## 1.5 Syllabus Highlights (KTU 2024 – Module 3)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Topics (per KTU 2024 PCIET302 Syllabus):**
> 1. **Pitot Tube** and **Pitot-Static Tube** — derivation of velocity equation, coefficient of Pitot tube.
> 2. **Current Meter** — components, rating equation, types (cup and screw type).
> 3. **Hot-Wire Anemometer** — basic principle, King's Law.
> 4. **Laser Doppler Anemometry (LDA)** — basic principle, Doppler shift concept.
> 5. **Flow Visualization Techniques** — dye, smoke, hydrogen bubble, surface tufts.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Pitot Tube

### 2.1.1 Construction and Operation

A Pitot tube is a slender, L-shaped hollow tube inserted into a flowing fluid with its open end facing **directly into the flow (upstream)**. The flowing fluid enters the tube, decelerates isentropically to zero velocity at the tip (called the **stagnation point**), and rises up the tube to a height that corresponds to the **stagnation pressure head** $\left(\frac{p_s}{\gamma} + z\right)$.

A **separate piezometer** (or static tap flush with the wall) measures the **static pressure head** $\left(\frac{p}{\gamma} + z\right)$ at the same point.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Velocity Derivation

1. The fluid at the tip of the Pitot tube is at **rest relative to the tube** — its velocity becomes zero.
2. By **Bernoulli's equation** (steady, incompressible, inviscid flow) between a far-upstream point (1) and the stagnation tip (2):
3. The **rise of fluid in the Pitot tube** above the static piezometric head gives the **velocity head** $\frac{V^2}{2g}$.
4. This rise, $h$, is read directly using a differential manometer between the Pitot tube and a static piezometer.

## 2.2 The Pitot-Static Tube

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Improvement over Pitot Tube:**
> The Pitot-static tube combines both a stagnation opening (at the nose) and a series of static pressure taps (on the cylindrical surface, well downstream of the nose). This eliminates the need for a separate piezometer, yielding a **single compact instrument** that can be traversed across the flow cross-section.

The differential manometer connected between the stagnation and static taps reads **directly the velocity head**:

$$h = \left(\frac{p_s}{\gamma} + z\right) - \left(\frac{p}{\gamma} + z\right) = \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

### 2.2.1 Coefficient of Pitot-Static Tube

In real (viscous) flow, the actual velocity is slightly less than the ideal value. A dimensionless correction factor $C_v$ (or $C_p$) is introduced:

$$V_{actual} = C_v \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$

- **For a well-designed Pitot-static tube:** $C_v \approx \mathbf{0.98}$ to $\mathbf{1.00}$
- **For a simple Pitot tube (no static ring):** $C_v \approx \mathbf{0.82}$ to $\mathbf{0.95}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:**
> The coefficient $C_v$ is **NOT a friction factor** — it accounts for the small disturbance introduced by the presence of the probe itself in the flow.

## 2.3 Current Meter

A **current meter** is a mechanical instrument that measures the local velocity of water by counting rotations of a rotor (cup-type or propeller-type) immersed in the flow.

### 2.3.1 Components

1. **Rotor** — set of cups (Price meter) or helical blades (propeller/Screw meter) rotated by fluid drag.
2. **Counting mechanism** — mechanical gear train + electrical contact producing clicks.
3. **Tail vane / fin** — orients the meter into the flow direction.
4. **Suspension rod / cable** — supports the meter in the channel from a boat, bridge, or wading rod.
5. **Sounding weight** — heavy lead weight to keep the meter steady in deep or fast water.

### 2.3.2 Rating Equation (Critical for KTU Problems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Repeated Question:** The relationship between the rotations per second and the flow velocity is **linear** and is called the **Rating Equation** of the current meter:

$$V = a \cdot N + b$$

Where:
- $V$ = flow velocity in m/s
- $N$ = rotations per second (rev/s)
- $a$ = slope (m/rev), found by calibration
- $b$ = intercept (m/s), found by calibration

Calibration is performed by towing the meter through still water at known speeds in a long towing tank.

## 2.4 Hot-Wire Anemometer (HWA)

### 2.4.1 Principle

A very fine wire (typically **platinum**, **tungsten**, or **platinum-iridium**, 5 µm diameter, ~1 mm long) is electrically heated and exposed to the flow. As fluid passes over the wire, it **cools** the wire by forced convection. The cooling rate depends on the local fluid velocity.

### 2.4.2 Two Operating Modes

| Mode | Quantity Held Constant | Measured Quantity |
|------|------------------------|-------------------|
| **Constant Current (CCA)** | Current through wire | Variation in wire resistance → temperature |
| **Constant Temperature (CTA)** | Wire temperature (resistance) | Variation in current (or voltage) to maintain temperature |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** The Constant Temperature Anemometer (CTA) is preferred in research because of its superior frequency response.

### 2.4.3 King's Law

The empirical relationship between the heat loss from the wire and the flow velocity is given by **King's Law**:

$$\frac{E^2}{R_w} = (A + B \cdot \sqrt{u})^ \cdot (T_w - T_a)$$

Or more commonly written as:

$$E^2 = A' + B' \cdot u^n$$

Where:
- $E$ = voltage across the wire
- $R_w$ = wire resistance
- $u$ = local flow velocity
- $T_w, T_a$ = wire and ambient (fluid) temperatures
- $A, B, n$ are calibration constants (typically $n \approx 0.45$ for air)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** HWA can resolve turbulent fluctuations up to **~1 MHz**, making it the gold standard for **turbulence measurements**.

## 2.5 Laser Doppler Anemometer (LDA / LDV)

### 2.5.1 Principle

The LDA exploits the **Doppler Effect** — the change in frequency of light scattered by small particles (seeded into the flow) moving with the fluid. Two coherent laser beams of known frequency intersect at a measurement volume. Light scattered from particles crossing this volume has a **Doppler-shifted frequency** that is directly proportional to the particle velocity component perpendicular to the bisector of the beams.

### 2.5.2 Doppler Frequency Equation

$$\nu_D = \frac{2 V \sin(\theta/2)}{\lambda}$$

Where:
- $\nu_D$ = Doppler frequency shift (Hz)
- $V$ = particle (fluid) velocity (m/s)
- $\theta$ = angle between the two intersecting laser beams
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of the laser light (typically 632.8 nm for He-Ne, 514 nm for Argon-ion)

### 2.5.3 Key Advantages

1. **Non-intrusive** — no probe in the flow, so no flow disturbance.
2. **Excellent spatial resolution** — measurement volume can be as small as ~10 µm³.
3. **No calibration required** — the principle is absolute, based on wavelength and geometry.
4. **Direction-sensitive** — measures both magnitude and sign of velocity.
5. **High-frequency response** — suitable for turbulence measurements.

### 2.5.4 Limitations

1. Requires optical access (transparent windows).
2. Flow must be seeded with tracer particles.
3. Expensive and delicate optics.

## 2.6 Flow Visualization Techniques

| Technique | Working Fluid | Best Application |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|
| **Dye injection** (e.g., Potassium Permanganate, Fluorescein) | Water | Streaklines, time-lines in open channels |
| **Smoke / fog** | Air | Wind tunnel flow patterns, separation, wakes |
| **Hydrogen-bubble technique** | Water | Time-lines, velocity profiles near boundaries |
| **Surface tufts / oil-film** | Any | Boundary-layer transition, separation points |
| **Tuft grid** | Air (wind tunnels) | Direction of surface flow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydrogen-Bubble Technique (Detailed):** A cathode wire is embedded in the flow; electrolysis of water produces tiny hydrogen bubbles that travel with the flow, illuminating streaklines under strong back-lighting.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $V = \sqrt{2gh}$ | Velocity from Pitot tube (ideal) |
| 2 | $V = C_v\sqrt{2gh}$ | Velocity from Pitot-static tube (with coefficient) |
| 3 | $h = h_s - h_p$ | Manometric head difference (static vs. stagnation) |
| 4 | $V = aN + b$ | Current meter rating equation |
| 5 | $E^2 = A' + B' \cdot u^n$ | King's Law (Hot-Wire Anemometer) |
| 6 | $\nu_D = \frac{2V \sin(\theta/2)}{\lambda}$ | LDA Doppler frequency shift |
| 7 | $p_s = p + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ | Stagnation pressure (Bernoulli) |
| 8 | $\Delta p = \rho g h$ | Manometer equation for differential head |
| 9 | $V = \sqrt{\frac{2g \cdot h \cdot (\rho_m - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}}$ | Velocity using differential U-tube manometer (inverted or otherwise) |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **Hydraulic Engineering** | Pitot-static used to measure velocity profile in rivers, canals, spillways. |
| **Aerospace / Wind Tunnels** | HWA & LDA measure turbulent boundary layer on aircraft wings. |
| **Hydraulic Machines** | Velocity probes verify velocity triangles at pump/turbine inlets. |
| **Medical / Bio-fluidics** | LDA measures blood flow in micro-channels without contamination. |
| **Process Industries** | Current meters monitor flow in intake channels of hydro-plants. |
| **Environmental Engineering** | Smoke & dye visualize pollutant dispersion in rivers and atmosphere. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Velocity Equation for Pitot Tube

### Setup

Consider a Pitot tube inserted into a uniform flow of incompressible fluid. Let:
- Point **1**: a point in the undisturbed flow, far upstream of the Pitot tube mouth.
- Point **2**: the **stagnation point** at the mouth of the Pitot tube.

At point 1:
- Velocity = $V$ (free-stream velocity)
- Pressure = $p_1$
- Elevation = $z_1$

At point 2:
- Velocity = $0$ (stagnation)
- Pressure = $p_2 = p_s$ (stagnation pressure)
- Elevation = $z_2$

### Application of Bernoulli's Equation

Applying Bernoulli's equation along a streamline from (1) to (2), assuming the flow is steady, incompressible, and inviscid:

$$\frac{p_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\gamma} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

### Substituting Known Values

At point 2, $V_2 = 0$, so the kinetic energy term vanishes:

$$\frac{p_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\gamma} + 0 + z_2$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{p_2 - p_1}{\gamma} + (z_2 - z_1)$$

### Defining Static and Stagnation Pressure Heads

The **static piezometric head** at point 1 is:

$$h_p = \frac{p_1}{\gamma} + z_1$$

The **stagnation piezometric head** at point 2 is:

$$h_s = \frac{p_2}{\gamma} + z_2$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{V^2}{2g} = h_s - h_p = h$$

### Final Velocity Equation

$$\boxed{V = \sqrt{2gh}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Where $h$ is read as the difference in liquid column heights** between the Pitot tube (stagnation) and the static piezometer.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Pitot-Static Tube with Differential U-tube Manometer

### Setup

When using a differential U-tube manometer with a heavier manometric fluid of specific weight $\gamma_m$ and the flowing fluid of specific weight $\gamma_f$, the relationship between manometer reading $x$ and velocity head $h$ must be derived.

### Manometer Analysis

Let the two limbs of the U-tube manometer be connected to:
- **Stagnation tap** (Pitot opening)
- **Static tap** (piezometric opening)

Tracing the pressure from point A (stagnation tap) down through the manometer and up to point B (static tap), and applying hydrostatic balance:

$$p_A + \gamma_f \cdot y_1 - \gamma_m \cdot x - \gamma_f \cdot y_2 = p_B$$

After simplification, the differential head is:

$$h = \frac{p_A - p_B}{\gamma_f} = x \left( \frac{\gamma_m}{\gamma_f} - 1 \right)$$

### Velocity Expression

Substituting into $V = \sqrt{2gh}$:

$$\boxed{V = \sqrt{\frac{2g \cdot x \left( \gamma_m - \gamma_f \right)}{\gamma_f}} = C_v \sqrt{2g \cdot x \left( \frac{\gamma_m}{\gamma_f} - 1 \right)}}$$

Where $C_v$ is the Pitot-static tube coefficient (typically 0.98–1.00).

### Worked Example (Step-by-Step)

**Problem (Typical KTU style):** A Pitot-static tube with $C_v = 0.98$ is used to measure the velocity of water flowing in a pipe. The differential manometer shows a reading of 100 mm of mercury. Calculate the velocity of water.

**Given:**
- $C_v = 0.98$
- $x = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- $\gamma_m = 13.6 \times 9810 \ \text{N/m}^3$ (mercury)
- $\gamma_f = 9810 \ \text{N/m}^3$ (water)
- $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$

**Solution:**

$$V = C_v \sqrt{2 g x \left(\frac{\gamma_m}{\gamma_f} - 1\right)}$$

$$V = 0.98 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.1 \times \left(\frac{13.6 \times 9810}{9810} - 1\right)}$$

$$V = 0.98 \times \sqrt{1.962 \times (13.6 - 1)}$$

$$V = 0.98 \times \sqrt{1.962 \times 12.6}$$

$$V = 0.98 \times \sqrt{24.72}$$

$$V = 0.98 \times 4.972$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 4.87 \ \text{m/s}}$$

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Current Meter Rating Equation

### Theoretical Basis

When the rotor of a current meter is placed in a flow of velocity $V$, the drag force on the cups/blades is proportional to $V^2$. This force creates a torque that accelerates the rotor. At equilibrium (when angular acceleration is zero), the rotor spins at a constant angular velocity $\omega$ proportional to $V$.

$$\omega = k \cdot V \quad \Longrightarrow \quad V = k \cdot \omega = k \cdot (2\pi N)$$

Empirically, due to bearing friction, a small constant offset is observed, leading to the linear rating equation:

$$V = a \cdot N + b$$

### Worked Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** A Price-type current meter has the following calibration data:
| Towing Speed V (m/s) | 0.2 | 0.5 | 0.8 | 1.2 | 1.6 | 2.0 |
|---------------------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| Revolutions per second N (rev/s) | 0.45 | 1.10 | 1.80 | 2.70 | 3.55 | 4.50 |

Find (a) the rating constants $a$ and $b$, and (b) the velocity when the meter records 100 revolutions in 50 seconds.

**Solution:**

(a) The slope $a$ and intercept $b$ can be obtained by linear regression or graphically. Using two well-separated points: $(0.2, 0.45)$ and $(2.0, 4.50)$:

$$a = \frac{2.0 - 0.2}{4.50 - 0.45} = \frac{1.8}{4.05} \approx 0.444 \ \text{m/rev}$$

$$b = V - aN = 0.2 - 0.444 \times 0.45 = 0.2 - 0.20 \approx 0.00 \ \text{m/s}$$

So the rating equation is approximately:

$$V = 0.444 \cdot N + 0.00$$

(b) For 100 revolutions in 50 seconds:

$$N = \frac{100}{50} = 2.0 \ \text{rev/s}$$

$$V = 0.444 \times 2.0 + 0.00 = 0.888 \ \text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 0.89 \ \text{m/s}}$$

---

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation: LDA Doppler Frequency Shift

### Geometric Setup

Two laser beams of equal frequency $\nu_0$ (and wavelength $\lambda$) intersect at angle $\theta$ between them. A particle moving with velocity $V$ crosses the intersection region.

### Derivation

The frequency of light scattered by a moving particle is shifted by an amount that depends on the component of velocity along the **bisector of the two beams**. For two beams intersecting at angle $\theta$, the optical fringe spacing in the measurement volume is:

$$d_f = \frac{\lambda}{2 \sin(\theta/2)}$$

As the particle crosses successive bright fringes, the scattered light intensity modulates at a frequency equal to the Doppler frequency:

$$\nu_D = \frac{V \cos \phi}{d_f} = \frac{2 V \sin(\theta/2)}{\lambda}$$

Where $\phi$ is the angle between the velocity vector and the bisector of the beams.

### Final LDA Velocity Equation

$$\boxed{V = \frac{\lambda \cdot \nu_D}{2 \sin(\theta/2)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Because the fringe spacing $d_f$ is set by **optical geometry only**, the LDA is **self-calibrating** — no external reference standard is needed!

### Worked Example

**Problem:** In a LDA setup, two laser beams of wavelength $\lambda = 632.8 \ \text{nm}$ (He-Ne) intersect at angle $\theta = 8°$. A particle crossing the probe volume produces a Doppler burst of frequency $\nu_D = 50 \ \text{kHz}$. Calculate the particle velocity.

**Given:**
- $\lambda = 632.8 \times 10^{-9} \ \text{m}$
- $\theta = 8°$
- $\nu_D = 50 \times 10^3 \ \text{Hz}$

**Solution:**

$$V = \frac{\lambda \cdot \nu_D}{2 \sin(\theta/2)}$$

$$V = \frac{632.8 \times 10^{-9} \times 50 \times 10^3}{2 \times \sin(4°)}$$

$$V = \frac{31.64 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 0.0698}$$

$$V = \frac{0.03164}{0.1396}$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 0.227 \ \text{m/s}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Velocity Measurement Techniques

| Feature | Pitot-Static Tube | Current Meter | Hot-Wire Anemometer | Laser Doppler |
|---------|-------------------|--------------|---------------------|---------------|
| **Principle** | Pressure (Bernoulli) | Mechanical rotation | Heat transfer (King's Law) | Doppler shift of light |
| **Intrusiveness** | Low (small probe) | Moderate | Moderate (fragile wire) | None (non-intrusive) |
| **Flow type** | Liquid & Gas | Liquid only | Gas primarily; liquid possible | Liquid & Gas |
| **Accuracy** | ±1–3 % | ±2–5 % | ±1 % (after calibration) | ±0.5 % (absolute, no calibration) |
| **Frequency Response** | Static only | < 1 Hz | up to ~1 MHz | up to ~100 kHz |
| **Spatial Resolution** | ~1 mm | ~50 mm | ~0.1 mm | ~0.01 mm |
| **Cost** | Low | Low–Moderate | Moderate | Very High |
| **Calibration Required?** | Yes (for $C_v$) | Yes (for $a$, $b$) | Yes (King's constants) | **No (self-calibrating)** |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of Velocity Measurement Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FLOWING FLUID DOMAIN"] --> B["SENSING ELEMENT"]
    B --> C["TRANSDUCER / SIGNAL CONVERSION"]
    C --> D["SIGNAL PROCESSING UNIT"]
    D --> E["DISPLAY OR DATA ACQUISITION"]
    
    A1["Open Channel Flow"] -.-> A
    A2["Closed Pipe Flow"] -.-> A
    A3["Wind Tunnel Air Flow"] -.-> A
    
    B --> B1["Pitot Tube"]
    B --> B2["Current Meter Rotor"]
    B --> B3["Hot Wire Sensor"]
    B --> B4["Laser Beam Intersection Volume"]
    
    C --> C1["Manometer Liquid Column"]
    C --> C2["Mechanical Counter"]
    C --> C3["Bridge Circuit Voltage"]
    C --> C4["Photodetector Beat Frequency"]
    
    D --> D1["Calibration Equation V from h"]
    D --> D2["Linear Regression N to V"]
    D --> D3["Kings Law Inversion"]
    D --> D4["FFT for Doppler Frequency"]
    
    E --> E1["Velocity Magnitude"]
    E --> E2["Velocity Profile across Section"]
    E --> E3["Turbulence Intensity and Spectra"]
```
