<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Define the term "Impact of a Jet". Explain with a neat sketch the force exerted by a jet of water on a stationary flat plate when the jet strikes perpendicular to the plate.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Impact of a Jet:** The phenomenon of a fluid jet striking a stationary or moving vane and exerting a dynamic force due to the change in momentum of the fluid is called the impact of a jet.

**Derivation (concise):**
- Let jet of area $a$ and velocity $V$ strike a flat plate perpendicularly.
- Mass striking the plate per second: $\dot{m} = \rho a V$
- Initial velocity in direction of jet = $V$; Final velocity = $0$ (water flows along plate)
- Force = rate of change of momentum:
$$F = \dot{m}(V - 0) = \rho a V \cdot V = \rho a V^2$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{F = \rho a V^2 \text{ Newtons}}$

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**State the assumptions made in the analysis of the impact of jets on flat plates. A jet of water 75 mm in diameter moving with a velocity of 20 m/s strikes a flat plate normally. Calculate the force exerted by the jet on the plate. Take ρ = 1000 kg/m³.**

**Assumptions (any 4 valid points):**
1. The fluid is incompressible and steady.
2. The flow is continuous with no air entrainment.
3. The plate is smooth (frictionless tangentially).
4. The jet has uniform velocity across its cross-section.
5. Atmospheric pressure acts on all free surfaces (gauge pressure = 0).
6. The plate is rigid and does not deform.

**Solution:**

Given:
- Diameter $d = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$
- Velocity $V = 20 \text{ m/s}$
- Density $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

Step 1 — Calculate area of jet:
$$a = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.075)^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Step 2 — Apply force equation:
$$F = \rho a V^2 = 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times (20)^2$$

$$F = 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 400$$

$$\boxed{F = 1767.15 \text{ N} \approx 1.767 \text{ kN}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Converting mm to m is the #1 silly mistake. Always convert *all* dimensions to SI units (m, m/s, kg/m³) **before** substituting.
> 2. **Confusing $\theta$ and $\alpha$:** If the problem says "angle with the normal" and you plug in $\sin(\text{normal angle})$, you get **zero marks** for that sub-part. Always re-draw and re-label.
> 3. **Forgetting $\rho$:** Many students write $F = aV^2$. This is **dimensionally wrong**. Force requires $\rho$ to have units of Newtons.
> 4. **Stationary Plate Efficiency:** Do not calculate efficiency for stationary plates — it is **always 0** because no work is done. Examiners will deduct marks for showing $\eta \neq 0$ on a stationary plate.
> 5. **Maximum Efficiency Proof:** When asked to prove $\eta_{\max} = 8/27$, you **must** show the differentiation step $d\eta/du = 0$ leading to $u = V/3$. Skipping the differentiation loses 3 marks.

---

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Internal Choice A]
**A jet of water of diameter 50 mm strikes a flat plate with a velocity of 25 m/s. The plate is moving with a velocity of 10 m/s in the direction of the jet. The jet strikes perpendicular to the plate. Find:**
**(a)** The force exerted by the jet on the plate. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The work done per second by the jet on the plate and the efficiency of the system. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to Part (a): Force Exerted

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- Jet velocity $V = 25 \text{ m/s}$
- Plate velocity $u = 10 \text{ m/s}$ (same direction as jet)
- $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Jet Area:**
$$a = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Relative Velocity of Jet w.r.t. Plate:**
$$V_r = V - u = 25 - 10 = 15 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Mass Striking the Plate per Second:**
$$\dot{m} = \rho a V_r = 1000 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 15$$

$$\dot{m} = 29.4525 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Force Equation (relative velocity reduces to 0):**
$$F = \dot{m} \cdot (V_r - 0) = \rho a (V-u)^2$$

$$F = 1000 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times (15)^2$$

$$F = 1000 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 225$$

$$\boxed{F = 441.79 \text{ N} \approx 442 \text{ N}}$$

#### Solution to Part (b): Work Done and Efficiency

**Step 1 — Work Done per Second:**
The plate moves 10 m every second, so:
$$W = F \cdot u = 441.79 \times 10 = 4417.9 \text{ J/s (Watts)}$$

$$\boxed{W \approx 4417.9 \text{ W} \approx 4.42 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 2 — Kinetic Energy of Jet per Second (Input Power):**
$$\text{KE}_{\text{jet/sec}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho a V^3$$

$$= \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times (25)^3$$

$$= \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 15625$$

$$= 15340.43 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \dfrac{W}{\text{KE}_{\text{jet/sec}}} = \dfrac{4417.9}{15340.43} = 0.288 = 28.8\%$$

**Step 4 — Verification using Direct Formula:**
$$\eta = \dfrac{2u(V-u)^2}{V^3} = \dfrac{2 \times 10 \times (15)^2}{(25)^3} = \dfrac{2 \times 10 \times 225}{15625} = \dfrac{4500}{15625} = 0.288$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 28.8\%}$$

**Step 5 — Check for Maximum Efficiency:**
The plate velocity for maximum efficiency is $u = V/3 = 25/3 = 8.33$ m/s.
Since given $u = 10$ m/s > 8.33 m/s, the plate is moving *faster* than the optimum, so $\eta < \eta_{\max} = 29.63\%$. ✓ **Result is consistent.**

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Internal Choice B]
**A jet of water 100 mm in diameter moving with a velocity of 30 m/s strikes a curved vane moving with a velocity of 15 m/s. The jet strikes the vane at an angle of 30° with the direction of motion. The vane deflects the jet through an angle of 150°. Find:**
**(a)** The force exerted by the jet on the vane in the direction of motion. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The work done per second and the efficiency of the system. **(7 marks)**

*(Note: Although this is a *curved* vane question, KTU frequently tests the **comparison** between flat and curved vanes in the same module — and the **flat-plate formulas are limiting cases** of the curved-vane formulas with $\beta = 180°$. This question is included to test the student's ability to recognize and apply the general framework.)*

#### Solution to Part (a): Force Exerted

**Given:**
- $d = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- $V = 30 \text{ m/s}$, $u = 15 \text{ m/s}$
- Angle of jet with direction of motion: $\alpha = 30°$
- Deflection angle: $\beta = 150°$ (angle through which jet is turned)
- $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Jet Area:**
$$a = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Relative Velocity:**
$$V_r = V - u = 30 - 15 = 15 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Mass Striking per Second:**
$$\dot{m} = \rho a V_r = 1000 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 15 = 117.81 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4 — Resolve Velocities at Inlet and Outlet of Vane (in the vane's frame):**

In the vane's frame:
- **Inlet velocity components:**
  - Along direction of motion: $V_r \cos\alpha = 15 \cos 30° = 12.99$ m/s
  - Perpendicular to direction of motion: $V_r \sin\alpha = 15 \sin 30° = 7.5$ m/s

- **Outlet velocity components** (jet is deflected by $\beta = 150°$):
  - Along direction of motion: $V_r \cos\beta = 15 \cos 150° = -12.99$ m/s
  - Perpendicular: $V_r \sin\beta = 15 \sin 150° = 7.5$ m/s

**Step 5 — Force in Direction of Motion:**
$$F_x = \dot{m} \cdot (V_{\text{inlet},x} - V_{\text{outlet},x})$$

$$F_x = 117.81 \times (12.99 - (-12.99)) = 117.81 \times 25.98$$

$$\boxed{F_x = 3060.5 \text{ N} \approx 3.06 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 6 — Force Perpendicular to Direction of Motion:**
$$F_y = \dot{m} \cdot (V_{\text{inlet},y} - V_{\text{outlet},y}) = 117.81 \times (7.5 - 7.5) = 0$$

(No perpendicular force — symmetric vane.)

#### Solution to Part (b): Work Done and Efficiency

**Step 1 — Work Done per Second:**
$$W = F_x \cdot u = 3060.5 \times 15 = 45907.5 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{W \approx 45.91 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 2 — Kinetic Energy of Jet per Second:**
$$\text{KE}_{\text{jet/sec}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho a V^3 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times (30)^3$$

$$= \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 27000 = 106028.6 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \dfrac{W}{\text{KE}_{\text{jet/sec}}} = \dfrac{45907.5}{106028.6} = 0.4330 = 43.3\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 43.3\%}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with Flat Plate Maximum (8/27 = 29.63%):**

The curved vane achieves **43.3%** efficiency, which is **significantly higher** than the flat plate's maximum of 29.63%. This numerical proof shows why Pelton wheel buckets are *curved* and not flat.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save This Checklist.** These 12 points are the **minimum** you need to memorize before any ESE on this topic.

- [ ] **1. Core Equation (Stationary ⊥):** $F = \rho a V^2$ — the most fundamental result.
- [ ] **2. Inclined Plate:** $F = \rho a V^2 \sin\theta$ where $\theta$ is the angle **with the plate** (not the normal).
- [ ] **3. Moving Plate Perpendicular:** $F = \rho a (V - u)^2$ — the $(V-u)$ is *relative velocity*, not absolute.
- [ ] **4. Moving Plate Inclined:** $F = \rho a (V-u)^2 \sin\theta$.
- [ ] **5. Work Done:** $W = F \cdot u$ — only the **moving** plate produces work.
- [ ] **6. Stationary Plate Work:** $W = 0$ — efficiency is always 0%.
- [ ] **7. Jet KE per second:** $\text{KE}_{\text{jet}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho a V^3$ — the input power benchmark.
- [ ] **8. Maximum Efficiency (Flat Plate):** $\eta_{\max} = \dfrac{8}{27} \approx 29.63\%$ at $u = V/3$.
- [ ] **9. Derivation of $\eta_{\max}$:** Always show the differentiation step: $\dfrac{d\eta}{du} = 0 \Rightarrow V - 3u = 0$.
- [ ] **10. Series of $n$ Plates:** Force per plate is reduced by $n$, but total work remains the same, so **efficiency increases by factor $(n+1)/2$**.
- [ ] **11. Newton's Second Law Foundation:** $F = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta V_{\text{direction}}$ — every force equation in this topic is just this one line.
- [ ] **12. Curved Vane Generalization:** $F = \rho a (V-u)^2 (1 + \cos\beta)$. For flat plate, $\beta = 180°$, so $\cos\beta = -1$, giving $F = 0$ (as expected, since a flat plate creates no net *curved* thrust — only normal force).

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy (14-mark question):** Allocate **3 minutes for setup**, **5 minutes for force calculation**, **3 minutes for work/efficiency**, and **3 minutes for a labeled diagram**. Always **box your final answer with units** — KTU examiners will not award full marks for a unitless number, even if the calculation is perfect.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Curved Symmetrical and Unsymmetrical Plates (Stationary and Moving)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Curved Symmetrical and Unsymmetrical Plates (Stationary and Moving)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Curved Plate in Fluid Mechanics?

A **curved plate** is a solid surface, generally shaped like an arc or a vane, that redirects a fluid jet without enclosing it (unlike a pipe or nozzle). The fluid strikes the plate, changes direction, and leaves the plate as a free jet. These plates are fundamental components in turbines (Pelton wheel buckets), pumps, and fluid power transmission systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A curved plate is a stationary or moving vane that receives a fluid jet at one velocity and discharges it at another velocity, with the change in momentum producing a dynamic force on the plate.

## 1.2 Classification of Curved Plates

Curved plates are classified based on **two criteria**:

### (a) Based on Geometry:
- **Symmetrical Curved Plate:** The angle of the inlet jet (θ₁) equals the angle of the outlet jet (θ₂) measured from the horizontal. Example: θ₁ = θ₂.
- **Unsymmetrical Curved Plate:** The inlet and outlet angles are different (θ₁ ≠ θ₂). Example: Pelton wheel bucket.

### (b) Based on Motion:
- **Stationary Curved Plate:** The plate is fixed. The fluid strikes it and is deflected. All velocity changes are *absolute*.
- **Moving Curved Plate:** The plate itself moves with velocity **u**. The fluid velocities are analyzed in the *relative frame of reference* attached to the moving plate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Intuition):**
> Imagine throwing a tennis ball at a curved wall attached to a skateboard:
> - If the skateboard is **stationary**, the wall receives the full impact force.
> - If the skateboard is **moving away** from you, the ball's *relative velocity* on impact is reduced (since the wall meets it partway), so the force is less.
> - The ball still exerts force on the moving wall and does work on it — this is the principle behind Pelton wheel turbines!

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Density of water (ρ):** **1000 kg/m³** (standard)
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²**
- **Specific weight of water (γ or w):** **9810 N/m³**
- **Mass per unit discharge:** ρQ (kg/s)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Principle: Impulse-Momentum Theorem

The entire theory of curved plates rests on the **Reynolds Transport Theorem** applied to the linear momentum equation:

$$\sum F = \rho Q (V_{out} - V_{in})$$

Where:
- $\sum F$ = Net external force on the fluid by the plate
- $\rho$ = Density of fluid (kg/m³)
- $Q$ = Discharge (m³/s)
- $V_{in}$, $V_{out}$ = Inlet and outlet velocity vectors (m/s)

## 2.2 Stationary Curved Plate — Conceptual Logic

For a **stationary** plate, the fluid enters with velocity $V_1$ at angle $\theta_1$ and leaves with velocity $V_2$ at angle $\theta_2$.

### Key Logical Steps:
1. Fluid enters with momentum $\rho Q V_1$ in direction of $V_1$.
2. Fluid leaves with momentum $\rho Q V_2$ in direction of $V_2$.
3. The plate exerts a force $F$ on the fluid equal to the **rate of change of momentum**.
4. By Newton's Third Law, the fluid exerts an equal and opposite force on the plate.

### Step-by-Step Force Resolution:

**Horizontal Force Component:**

$$F_x = \rho Q [V_1 \cos\theta_1 + V_2 \cos\theta_2]$$

> The sign **(+)** appears at the outlet because the angle $\theta_2$ is conventionally measured such that the horizontal component points in the **same direction** as the inlet jet's horizontal projection.

**Vertical Force Component:**

$$F_y = \rho Q [V_1 \sin\theta_1 - V_2 \sin\theta_2]$$

> The sign **(-)** appears here because the vertical component of the outgoing jet typically opposes the vertical component of the incoming jet for a downward-deflecting curved plate.

### Special Case: Frictionless Curved Plate

If the plate is **smooth (frictionless)** and no energy is lost:

$$V_1 = V_2 = V$$

## 2.3 Moving Curved Plate — Relative Velocity Concept

For a plate moving with velocity **u** in the direction of the jet:

### Step-by-Step Logic:
1. The plate moves in the same direction as the jet at velocity $u$.
2. **Relative velocity of jet w.r.t. plate at inlet:** $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$
3. **Relative velocity of jet w.r.t. plate at outlet:** $V_{r2}$
4. The fluid still leaves the moving plate at the **same relative angle** (no slip).
5. The force is calculated using **relative velocities**, since momentum changes occur in the plate's frame.

### Force on Moving Plate:

**Horizontal Component:**

$$F_x = \rho Q (V_1 \cos\theta_1 - u) [1 + \cos\phi]$$

Where $\phi$ is the angle of turning of the jet relative to the plate.

**Work Done per Second by the Fluid on the Plate:**

$$W = F_x \times u$$

**Efficiency of a Single Moving Curved Plate:**

$$\eta = \frac{2u(V_1 - u)(1 + \cos\phi)}{V_1^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Moving curved plates are the heart of impulse turbines (Pelton wheels). The bucket receives a high-velocity jet, deflects it through ~165°–170°, and extracts maximum kinetic energy by also moving with the jet.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **S.No.** | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Condition** |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | Horizontal Force (Stationary) | $F_x = \rho Q [V_1\cos\theta_1 + V_2\cos\theta_2]$ | N | General |
| 2 | Vertical Force (Stationary) | $F_y = \rho Q [V_1\sin\theta_1 - V_2\sin\theta_2]$ | N | General |
| 3 | Force on Stationary Symmetrical Plate | $F_x = 2\rho Q V\cos\theta$ | N | $\theta_1 = \theta_2 = \theta$, $V_1 = V_2$ |
| 4 | Relative Inlet Velocity | $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$ | m/s | Moving Plate |
| 5 | Relative Outlet Velocity | $V_{r2} = V_{r1}$ (smooth) | m/s | Frictionless Moving Plate |
| 6 | Horizontal Force (Moving Plate) | $F_x = \rho Q (V_{r1} + V_{r2}\cos\phi)$ | N | Moving |
| 7 | Work Done / Second | $W = F_x \cdot u$ | W (J/s) | Moving Plate |
| 8 | Efficiency of Single Plate | $\eta = \dfrac{2u(V_1-u)(1+\cos\phi)}{V_1^2}$ | dimensionless | Frictionless |
| 9 | Discharge | $Q = A \cdot V$ | m³/s | Continuity |
| 10 | Weight of fluid above plate | $W = \rho g \cdot \text{Volume}$ | N | Vertical component |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Sign Convention for KTU Exams:**
> - Always draw the FBD first.
> - Take the **positive x-direction** along the incoming jet.
> - Components acting **along positive x** → positive.
> - Components acting **against positive x** → negative.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Force on Stationary Symmetrical Curved Plate

### Setup:
A jet of velocity $V$ strikes a smooth symmetrical curved plate at angle $\theta$ with the horizontal. The jet is deflected through $180° - 2\theta$ and leaves at angle $\theta$ on the other side (symmetrically).

### Derivation:

**Step 1: Apply continuity equation.**
For an incompressible fluid with no splashing:

$$Q_{in} = Q_{out} \implies Q_1 = Q_2 = Q$$

**Step 2: Apply energy equation (Bernoulli) — no friction, no head loss.**

$$V_1 = V_2 = V$$

**Step 3: Resolve inlet momentum along x-axis.**

$$\text{Momentum}_{in,x} = \rho Q \cdot V \cos\theta$$

**Step 4: Resolve outlet momentum along x-axis.**
The outlet jet also makes angle $\theta$ with the horizontal, but on the *same side* of the plate (because the plate is symmetrical). Its x-component is:

$$\text{Momentum}_{out,x} = \rho Q \cdot V \cos\theta$$

**Step 5: Apply impulse-momentum theorem along x-axis.**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_x &= \rho Q V_{1x} - \rho Q V_{2x} \\
F_x &= \rho Q V \cos\theta - (-\rho Q V \cos\theta) \\
F_x &= \rho Q V \cos\theta + \rho Q V \cos\theta \\
F_x &= 2\rho Q V \cos\theta
\end{aligned}$$

> This is the **standard KTU result** for a smooth, symmetrical, stationary curved plate.

### Step 6: Vertical Force (if required):

$$\begin{aligned}
F_y &= \rho Q V \sin\theta - \rho Q V \sin\theta \\
F_y &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

For a purely symmetrical horizontal plate with no elevation change, $F_y = 0$. This changes if the geometry is **unsymmetrical** or there is **fluid weight** above the plate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Force on Stationary Unsymmetrical Curved Plate

### Setup:
A jet enters at angle $\theta_1$ with velocity $V_1$ and leaves at angle $\theta_2$ with velocity $V_2$ (different from $V_1$ if there is friction).

### Derivation:

**Step 1: Horizontal momentum balance.**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_x &= \rho Q \cdot V_1 \cos\theta_1 - \rho Q \cdot (-V_2 \cos\theta_2) \\
F_x &= \rho Q [V_1 \cos\theta_1 + V_2 \cos\theta_2]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Vertical momentum balance.**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_y &= \rho Q V_1 \sin\theta_1 - \rho Q V_2 \sin\theta_2 \\
F_y &= \rho Q [V_1 \sin\theta_1 - V_2 \sin\theta_2]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Total Resultant Force.**

$$F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2}$$

$$\text{Direction: } \tan\alpha = \frac{F_y}{F_x}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Force and Work on Moving Curved Plate

### Setup:
A jet of velocity $V_1$ strikes a curved vane moving with velocity $u$ in the same direction. The vane deflects the jet through angle $\phi$ (relative to the plate).

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Relative velocity of jet at inlet.**

$$V_{r1} = V_1 - u$$

**Step 2: Apply energy equation in the moving frame (no friction).**

$$V_{r2} = V_{r1} = V_1 - u$$

**Step 3: Apply impulse-momentum in the relative frame along x-direction.**

The fluid enters with horizontal relative velocity $V_{r1}$ and leaves with horizontal relative velocity $V_{r2} \cos\phi$:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_x &= \rho Q V_{r1} - \rho Q (-V_{r2}\cos\phi) \\
F_x &= \rho Q (V_{r1} + V_{r2}\cos\phi) \\
F_x &= \rho Q (V_1 - u) [1 + \cos\phi]
\end{aligned}$$

> Because the plate is moving, the *absolute mass flow rate striking* the plate is $\rho Q$, where $Q = A \cdot V_1$ (absolute discharge). However, the **rate of mass actually reaching the plate per second** is $\rho (V_1 - u) \cdot A$ — the relative mass flux.

**Step 4: Work done per second.**

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= F_x \cdot u \\
W &= \rho Q u (V_1 - u)(1 + \cos\phi)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Work done per second}}{\text{Kinetic energy supplied per second}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{\rho Q u (V_1 - u)(1 + \cos\phi)}{\frac{1}{2} \rho Q V_1^2} \\
\eta &= \frac{2u(V_1 - u)(1 + \cos\phi)}{V_1^2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Condition for maximum efficiency.**
Differentiating $\eta$ with respect to $u$ and setting $\frac{d\eta}{du} = 0$:

$$u = \frac{V_1}{2}$$

This gives $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{1 + \cos\phi}{2}$

For $\phi = 165°$ (Pelton bucket), $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{1 + \cos 165°}{2} \approx 0.991$ or **99.1%**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Edge Cases for KTU

| **Case** | **Scenario** | **Result** |
|----------|--------------|-----------|
| Symmetrical, Smooth | $V_1 = V_2$, $\theta_1 = \theta_2$ | $F_x = 2\rho Q V \cos\theta$ |
| Symmetrical, with Friction | $V_1 \neq V_2$ | $F_x = \rho Q [V_1 + V_2]\cos\theta$ |
| Unsymmetrical, Smooth | $\theta_1 \neq \theta_2$ | $F_x = \rho Q V [\cos\theta_1 + \cos\theta_2]$ |
| Moving Plate, $\phi = 180°$ | Complete reversal | $F_x = 2\rho Q (V_1 - u)$ |
| Moving Plate, $\phi = 90°$ | Quarter turn | $F_x = \rho Q (V_1 - u)$ |
| $u = V_1$ | Plate moves with jet speed | $F_x = 0$, $W = 0$ |
| $u = 0$ | Stationary plate limit | $F_x = \rho Q V_1 (1 + \cos\phi)$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Force Resolution on Stationary Symmetrical Curved Plate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incoming Jet V1 at angle theta1] --> B[Impact Point on Curved Plate]
    B --> C[Horizontal Component V1 cos theta1]
    B --> D[Vertical Component V1 sin theta1]
    C --> E[Momentum Change in X Direction]
    D --> F[Momentum Change in Y Direction]
    E --> G[Force Fx equals rho Q times delta Vx]
    F --> H[Force Fy equals rho Q times delta Vy]
    G --> I[Final Resultant Force F]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Outgoing Jet V2 at angle theta2]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Moving Curved Plate - Relative Frame Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Absolute Frame - Ground Observer] --> B[Jet Velocity V1]
    B --> C[Subtract Plate Velocity u]
    C --> D[Relative Frame - Attached to Plate]
    D --> E[Relative Inlet Velocity Vr1 equals V1 minus u]
    D --> F[Relative Outlet Velocity Vr2 at angle phi]
    E --> G[Apply Impulse Momentum in Relative Frame]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Force Fx on Moving Plate]
    H --> I[Work Done equals Fx times u]
    I --> J[Efficiency Calculation]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Pelton Wheel Bucket (Real-World Application)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[High Pressure Water Jet] --> B[Nozzle Converts Pressure to Velocity]
    B --> C[Jet Strikes Bucket Splitter]
    C --> D[Two Halves Deflected through 165 degrees]
    D --> E[Bucket Moves at Velocity u equals 0.46 V1]
    E --> F[Maximum Kinetic Energy Extracted]
    F --> G[Force on Bucket Drives Turbine Shaft]
    G --> H[Mechanical Power Output equals Fx times u]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Complete Force Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Plate Type] --> B{Stationary or Moving}
    B -->|Stationary| C[Use Absolute Velocities]
    B -->|Moving| D[Subtract Plate Velocity to get Relative Frame]
    C --> E[Resolve Velocities into X and Y Components]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Apply Continuity Q_in equals Q_out]
    F --> G[Apply Energy Equation for V_2]
    G --> H[Apply Impulse Momentum in X Direction]
    G --> I[Apply Impulse Momentum in Y Direction]
    H --> J[Compute Fx]
    I --> K[Compute Fy]
    J --> L[Compute Resultant Force F and Direction]
    K --> L
    L --> M{Plate Moving?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Compute Work Done equals Fx times u]
    M -->|No| O[Final Answer Fx and Fy]
    N --> P[Compute Efficiency]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define a symmetrical curved plate. Why is it called "symmetrical"?**

**Model Answer:**
A symmetrical curved plate is one in which the angle made by the incoming jet with the horizontal at the inlet ($\theta_1$) is equal to the angle made by the outgoing jet with the horizontal at the outlet ($\theta_2$). It is called symmetrical because the geometry is mirror-symmetric about the central vertical axis, leading to identical angle conditions on both sides. For such a plate, $V_1 = V_2$ (smooth) and $\theta_1 = \theta_2 = \theta$, giving $F_x = 2\rho Q V \cos\theta$ and $F_y = 0$.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam - June 2023]
**What is the condition for maximum efficiency of a single moving curved plate?**

**Model Answer:**
The efficiency of a single moving curved plate is given by $\eta = \dfrac{2u(V_1 - u)(1 + \cos\phi)}{V_1^2}$. Differentiating with respect to $u$ and equating to zero:

$$\frac{d\eta}{du} = 0 \implies u = \frac{V_1}{2}$$

Thus, maximum efficiency occurs when the **plate velocity is half the jet velocity**. The corresponding maximum efficiency is:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{1 + \cos\phi}{2}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]

**A jet of water of diameter 75 mm strikes a stationary curved plate with a velocity of 25 m/s at an angle of 30° to the horizontal. The plate deflects the jet through an angle of 120° and discharges it into the atmosphere. Assuming the plate is smooth:**

**(a)** Calculate the horizontal and vertical components of the force exerted by the jet on the plate. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If the plate is moving in the direction of the jet with a velocity of 10 m/s, find the force on the plate, work done per second, and the efficiency of the system. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a): Stationary Plate**

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 75$ mm $= 0.075$ m
- $V_1 = 25$ m/s
- $\theta_1 = 30°$
- Total deflection angle = $120°$
- So $\theta_2 = 120° - 30° = 90°$
- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³

**Step 1: Compute discharge.**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.075)^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \\
Q &= A \cdot V_1 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 25 = 0.1104 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute horizontal force.**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_x &= \rho Q [V_1 \cos\theta_1 + V_2 \cos\theta_2] \\
F_x &= 1000 \times 0.1104 \times [25 \cos 30° + 25 \cos 90°] \\
F_x &= 110.4 \times [25 \times 0.866 + 0] \\
F_x &= 110.4 \times 21.65 \\
F_x &= 2390.16 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute vertical force.**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_y &= \rho Q [V_1 \sin\theta_1 - V_2 \sin\theta_2] \\
F_y &= 1000 \times 0.1104 \times [25 \sin 30° - 25 \sin 90°] \\
F_y &= 110.4 \times [12.5 - 25] \\
F_y &= 110.4 \times (-12.5) \\
F_y &= -1380 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

> The negative sign indicates the vertical force is directed **downward** (jet pushes plate down).

---

#### **Solution (b): Moving Plate**

**Given:** $u = 10$ m/s

**Step 1: Relative inlet velocity.**

$$V_{r1} = V_1 - u = 25 - 10 = 15 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Relative outlet velocity (smooth plate, $\phi = 120°$).**

$$V_{r2} = V_{r1} = 15 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Force on moving plate.**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_x &= \rho Q (V_1 - u) [1 + \cos\phi] \\
F_x &= 1000 \times 0.1104 \times (25 - 10) \times [1 + \cos 120°] \\
F_x &= 110.4 \times 15 \times [1 + (-0.5)] \\
F_x &= 110.4 \times 15 \times 0.5 \\
F_x &= 828 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Work done per second.**

$$W = F_x \cdot u = 828 \times 10 = 8280 \text{ W} = 8.28 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Efficiency.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{2u(V_1 - u)(1 + \cos\phi)}{V_1^2} \\
\eta &= \frac{2 \times 10 \times (25 - 10) \times (1 + \cos 120°)}{25^2} \\
\eta &= \frac{20 \times 15 \times 0.5}{625} \\
\eta &= \frac{150}{625} = 0.24 = 24\%
\end{aligned}$$

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Modified]

**A jet of water with velocity 30 m/s and diameter 50 mm strikes a smooth unsymmetrical curved plate at an angle of 40°. The jet leaves the plate at an angle of 25° with the horizontal in the opposite direction.**

**(a)** Determine the magnitude and direction of the resultant force on the plate. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If the same plate moves at 12 m/s in the direction of the jet, calculate the new force on the plate and the percentage reduction in force compared to the stationary case. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a): Stationary Unsymmetrical Plate**

**Given:**
- $V_1 = 30$ m/s, $d = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m
- $\theta_1 = 40°$, $\theta_2 = 25°$ (opposite side)
- Smooth plate: $V_2 = V_1 = 30$ m/s

**Step 1: Discharge.**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi}{4}(0.05)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \\
Q &= 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 30 = 0.0589 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Horizontal force.**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_x &= \rho Q [V_1 \cos\theta_1 + V_2 \cos\theta_2] \\
F_x &= 1000 \times 0.0589 \times [30\cos 40° + 30\cos 25°] \\
F_x &= 58.9 \times [22.98 + 27.19] \\
F_x &= 58.9 \times 50.17 \\
F_x &= 2955.0 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Vertical force.**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_y &= \rho Q [V_1 \sin\theta_1 - V_2 \sin\theta_2] \\
F_y &= 1000 \times 0.0589 \times [30\sin 40° - 30\sin 25°] \\
F_y &= 58.9 \times [19.28 - 12.68] \\
F_y &= 58.9 \times 6.60 \\
F_y &= 388.74 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Resultant force.**

$$F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2} = \sqrt{2955^2 + 388.74^2} = 2980.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Direction.**

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{F_y}{F_x} = \frac{388.74}{2955} = 0.1315$$

$$\alpha = 7.5° \text{ above horizontal}$$

---

#### **Solution (b): Moving Plate**

**Given:** $u = 12$ m/s

**Step 1: Relative velocity.**

$$V_{r1} = V_1 - u = 30 - 12 = 18 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Determine deflection angle $\phi$.**

The total angle turned relative to the plate:
- Inlet angle relative to plate direction: $\beta_1 = 40°$
- Outlet angle relative to plate direction: $\beta_2 = 180° - 25° = 155°$
- So $\phi = \beta_1 + \beta_2 = 40° + 155° = 195°$

Wait, for unsymmetrical plates moving, we use the angle between inlet and outlet relative velocity directions. Let's re-express:

For an unsymmetrical moving plate, the angle of the relative velocity vector at outlet measured from inlet direction is:

$$\phi = 180° - \theta_2 + \theta_1 = 180° - 25° + 40° = 195°$$

Hmm, this exceeds 180°. Let me reconsider — actually, the standard formulation uses:

$$\phi = 180° - (\theta_2 - \theta_1) \text{ for unsymmetrical}$$

Let me use the standard formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_x &= \rho Q (V_1 - u) [\cos\theta_1 + \cos\theta_2] \\
F_x &= 1000 \times 0.0589 \times (30 - 12) \times [\cos 40° + \cos 25°] \\
F_x &= 58.9 \times 18 \times [0.766 + 0.906] \\
F_x &= 58.9 \times 18 \times 1.672 \\
F_x &= 1772.7 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Reduction in force.**

$$\text{Reduction} = \frac{F_{stationary} - F_{moving}}{F_{stationary}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Reduction} = \frac{2955 - 1772.7}{2955} \times 100 = 40.0\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Confusion:** Forgetting that the outlet velocity's horizontal component adds (not subtracts) in the force equation when the jet reverses direction. Always draw the FBD!
> 2. **Units Mismatch:** Mixing mm with m for diameter. Convert to meters **first**.
> 3. **Forgetting ρ:** Using weight density γ = 9810 N/m³ instead of mass density ρ = 1000 kg/m³. The momentum equation uses **mass density ρ**, not weight density!
> 4. **Relative vs. Absolute:** When plate moves, use $\rho Q$ (absolute discharge) multiplied by $(V_1 - u)$ for relative mass flux in many textbooks. Some use $\rho Q_{rel} = \rho A V_{r1}$ — verify which form your textbook uses.
> 5. **Maximum Efficiency Condition:** Writing $u = V_1$ (which gives zero efficiency) instead of $u = V_1/2$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

✅ **Core Formula (Stationary):** $F_x = \rho Q [V_1 \cos\theta_1 + V_2 \cos\theta_2]$ — memorize this!

✅ **Core Formula (Moving):** $F_x = \rho Q (V_1 - u) [1 + \cos\phi]$ — relative frame analysis is key.

✅ **Work Done:** Always $W = F_x \times u$ for moving plates, never $F_x \times V_1$.

✅ **Symmetrical vs. Unsymmetrical:**
   - Symmetrical: $\theta_1 = \theta_2$, simpler formulas, $F_y = 0$ for horizontal symmetric geometry.
   - Unsymmetrical: $\theta_1 \neq \theta_2$, both $F_x$ and $F_y$ non-zero.

✅ **Maximum Efficiency of Single Plate:** Achieved at $u = V_1/2$, giving $\eta_{max} = (1 + \cos\phi)/2$.

✅ **Frictionless Assumption:** Always gives $V_1 = V_2$ (or $V_{r1} = V_{r2}$ for moving). If friction is present, use $V_2 = V_1 \sqrt{1 - k}$ where $k$ is the friction coefficient.

✅ **Pelton Wheel Bucket:** Real-world example with $\phi \approx 165°$, $u \approx 0.46 V_1$ (slightly less than $V_1/2$ for design margin).

✅ **Vertical Force with Water Weight:** If fluid sits above the plate, add $W = \rho g \times \text{Volume above plate}$ to $F_y$.

✅ **Direction of Resultant:** Always report both magnitude and direction ($\alpha = \tan^{-1}(F_y/F_x)$) in KTU exams for full marks.

✅ **Sign Convention:** Positive x along jet direction, positive y upward. Stick to one convention throughout the problem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In 14-mark problems, KTU examiners expect a **clear free-body diagram** and **step-wise momentum balance**. Show all velocity components, state the continuity and energy equations explicitly, and box the final answer with units.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact of Jet on Hinged Plate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Impact of Jet on Hinged Plate # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Impact of Jet on a Hinged Plate** is defined as the dynamic reaction force produced when a continuous fluid jet, issuing from a nozzle, impinges on a flat plate that is pivoted (hinged) at one of its ends. The hinge permits the plate to undergo angular displacement about the pivot when subjected to the jet's momentum flux, allowing analysis of both the **static force exerted by the jet** and the **rotational moment (torque) generated about the hinge axis**. The fundamental governing principle is the **Euler's Momentum Equation (Linear Momentum Conservation)**, applied along the direction of the jet. The net force exerted by the jet on the plate equals the **rate of change of momentum** of the fluid particles striking the plate per unit time. $$F_{jet} = \dot{m} \times (V_1 - V_2) = \rho \, a \, V \times (V_1 - V_2)$$ where the symbols carry their standard meanings used throughout the KTU module. ## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuitive Understanding) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Garden Door Effect:** > Imagine a lightweight wooden door hinged at one side (say, a bathroom door or a garden gate). When you direct a strong jet of water from a hosepipe onto the door's surface, the door swings open because the water molecules transfer their momentum to the door on impact. The further the water strikes from the hinge, the easier the door swings — exactly the **moment-arm principle** that we use in hinged-plate problems. > > - Water jet ⇒ Carries linear momentum $\rho a V^2$ per unit area > - Door (hinged plate) ⇒ Receives this momentum and converts it into angular motion > - Hinge ⇒ The fixed pivot about which rotation occurs > - Resulting swing ⇒ Proves force was transferred from jet to plate ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants Used > [!NOTE] > - **Density of water, $\rho$** = **1000 kg/m³** (or **9.81 kN/m³** as specific weight) > - **Acceleration due to gravity, $g$** = **9.81 m/s²** > - **Kinematic viscosity of water at 20°C, $\nu$** = **1.003 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** ## 1.4 Types of Hinged Plate Configurations Studied in KTU 2024 The KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 3) typically examines the following variants: 1. **Hinged Flat Plate — Jet Striking Perpendicularly** (Plate normal to jet) 2. **Hinged Flat Plate — Plate Inclined at Angle $\theta$** to jet direction 3. **Hinged Flat Plate — Jet Striking at an Eccentric Point** (force is offset from hinge) 4. **Hinged Curved Vane** (foundational concept for Pelton wheel buckets) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — Structured Logic Steps The phenomenon is governed by the **Impulse-Momentum Theorem**, which states that the net force on a control volume equals the time rate of change of linear momentum of the fluid crossing the boundary. ### Step 1 — Identify the Control Volume - The control volume is the thin layer of fluid that strikes the plate per second. - Inlet velocity = $V$ (jet velocity), inlet area = $a$ (jet cross-section) - Mass flow rate striking plate: $\dot{m} = \rho \, a \, V$ ### Step 2 — Resolve the Jet's Momentum - Initial velocity component normal to plate = $V \sin\theta$ - Initial velocity component along plate = $V \cos\theta$ - For a **smooth, frictionless flat plate**, the tangential (along-plate) component of velocity is **unchanged** because no shear stress acts in that direction. - The **normal component is destroyed** (brought to zero) upon impact since the plate does not allow penetration. ### Step 3 — Apply the Momentum Equation - Change in velocity (normal to plate) = $V \sin\theta - 0 = V \sin\theta$ - Force on plate (normal direction) = mass flow rate × change in velocity - Therefore: $F_n = \rho \, a \, V \times V \sin\theta = \rho \, a \, V^2 \sin\theta$ ### Step 4 — Account for the Hinge - The hinge acts as a **pivot point** — it supplies the reaction force but does no work. - The normal force $F_n$ is applied at the **centroid of the jet's contact patch** on the plate. - The **moment about the hinge** = $F_n \times L$, where $L$ is the perpendicular distance from the hinge to the line of action of $F_n$. ### Step 5 — Static Equilibrium Condition (For a Plate Hanging at Rest) - If the plate's weight $W$ acts at a distance $\bar{x}$ from the hinge (along the plate), the plate remains in equilibrium only if: $$F_n \times L = W \times \bar{x}$$ - This is the **principle of moments** — the **counter-clockwise moment** (jet force) equals the **clockwise moment** (weight). ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** for the "Impact of Jet on Hinged Plate" topic. **Memorize these — they appear in nearly every KTU end-semester paper from this module.** | # | Configuration | Governing Equation | Resulting Force | Direction of Force | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Flat plate held **perpendicular** to jet (no hinge) | $F = \dot{m}(V - 0)$ | $F = \rho \, a \, V^{2}$ | Along the jet (normal to plate) | | 2 | Flat plate **inclined at angle $\theta$** to jet | $F_n = \rho a V (V\sin\theta)$ | $F_n = \rho \, a \, V^{2} \sin\theta$ | Normal to plate surface | | 3 | Flat plate inclined at $\theta$ — **force along jet direction** | $F_x = F_n \sin\theta$ | $F_x = \rho \, a \, V^{2} \sin^{2}\theta$ | Along original jet direction | | 4 | Hinged flat plate — **Moment about hinge** | $M = F_n \times L$ | $M = \rho \, a \, V^{2} \sin\theta \times L$ | Rotational (about hinge) | | 5 | Hinged plate — **Equilibrium with weight $W$** | $F_n L = W \bar{x}$ | $W = \dfrac{\rho a V^{2} \sin\theta \cdot L}{\bar{x}}$ | — | | 6 | **Curved vane (single) hinged** with exit angle $\phi$ | $F = \rho a V^{2}(1 + \cos\phi)$ | $F = \rho a V^{2}(1 + \cos\phi)$ | Along incoming jet direction | | 7 | Hinged plate — **Force when jet splits into two equal streams** (symmetric vane) | $F = 2 \rho a V^{2} \cos\phi$ | $F = 2 \rho a V^{2} \cos\phi$ | Along jet direction | | 8 | **Force component along plate** (inclined plate) | $F_t = \rho a V^{2} \cos\theta$ | $F_t = \rho a V^{2} \cos\theta$ | Along the plate surface (zero for frictionless case) | > [!NOTE] > **Units Reminder for KTU Board Exams:** > - $\rho$ in $\text{kg/m}^{3}$ (or $\text{N·s}^{2}/\text{m}^{4}$ if using weight density) > - $a$ in $\text{m}^{2}$, $V$ in $\text{m/s}$ > - $F$ in **Newtons (N)**, Moment in **N·m** > - Discharge $Q = aV$ in $\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$ ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Where does this topic apply in industry?** > 1. **Pelton Wheel Turbines** — The buckets are essentially curved hinged vanes; their design relies on these exact force equations. > 2. **Firefighting monitors** — Reaction forces from water jets on the nozzle must be calculated to size the swivel mount (hinge). > 3. **Hydraulic Ram (Water Hammer)** — Pipe-end flaps act like hinged plates. > 4. **Jet propulsion rudders & ship stabilizers** — Use jet impact principles. > 5. **Sprinkler irrigation impact heads** — Free-swinging plates rotated by jet impact. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Force on a Flat Plate Held Perpendicular to a Stationary Jet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Setup:** A circular jet of water with velocity $V$ and cross-sectional area $a$ strikes a flat plate held stationary, perpendicular to the jet. **Step 1: Mass flow rate of jet striking the plate per second.** The mass of water striking the plate per second is the product of the mass density, the jet area, and the jet velocity. $$\dot{m} = \rho \times a \times V$$ **Step 2: Initial momentum of the jet in the direction of flow.** The momentum of the water just before striking the plate is: $$M_{initial} = \dot{m} \times V = \rho \, a \, V^{2}$$ This momentum is directed along the jet's original path. **Step 3: Final momentum of the jet after striking the plate.** For a **flat plate held perpendicular to the jet**, the water is brought to rest in the original direction of flow (it splashes sideways radially). The component of velocity in the original jet direction becomes **zero**. $$M_{final} = \dot{m} \times 0 = 0$$ **Step 4: Apply Newton's Second Law (Impulse-Momentum Theorem).** The force exerted by the jet on the plate equals the rate of change of momentum of the fluid: $$F = \frac{dM}{dt} = M_{initial} - M_{final}$$ $$F = \rho \, a \, V^{2} - 0$$ $$\boxed{F = \rho \, a \, V^{2} \quad \text{(Newtons)}}$$ **Step 5: Physical Interpretation.** The force is **independent of the plate's mass or material** — it depends only on the **fluid density, jet area, and the square of the jet velocity**. Doubling the velocity **quadruples** the impact force. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Force on a Flat Plate Inclined at an Angle $\theta$ to the Jet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Setup:** The flat plate is now tilted at an angle $\theta$ to the jet axis. The jet velocity is $V$, jet area is $a$. **Step 1: Resolve the jet velocity into two components.** - Component **normal** to the plate: $V_n = V \sin\theta$ - Component **tangential** (along) the plate: $V_t = V \cos\theta$ **Step 2: Analyze what happens to each component on impact.** - For a **smooth, frictionless plate**, the tangential component $V_t$ is **unchanged** — the water slides along the surface with no shear stress transfer. - The normal component $V_n$ is **completely destroyed** (water cannot penetrate the plate), so the final normal velocity is **zero**. **Step 3: Compute the rate of change of momentum in the normal direction.** Initial normal momentum per second = $\dot{m} \times V_n = (\rho a V)(V \sin\theta)$ Final normal momentum per second = $\dot{m} \times 0 = 0$ **Step 4: Force normal to the plate.** By the momentum equation applied in the normal direction: $$F_n = \rho \, a \, V^{2} \sin\theta$$ This force acts **perpendicular to the plate surface**, pushing the plate away from the jet. **Step 5: Resolve $F_n$ along the original jet direction (often required in KTU problems).** The angle between $F_n$ and the original jet direction is $\theta$ (since the plate is inclined at $\theta$ to the jet, its normal is at $\theta$ to the jet as well, by geometry of complementary angles). $$F_x = F_n \sin\theta = \rho \, a \, V^{2} \sin^{2}\theta$$ **Step 6: Check the limiting case.** When $\theta = 90°$ (plate perpendicular to jet): $F_n = \rho a V^2 \sin 90° = \rho a V^2$ ✓ (matches Derivation 1) When $\theta = 0°$ (plate parallel to jet — no obstruction): $F_n = \rho a V^2 \sin 0° = 0$ ✓ (no impact) $$\boxed{F_n = \rho \, a \, V^{2} \sin\theta \quad ; \quad F_x = \rho \, a \, V^{2} \sin^{2}\theta}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Force and Moment on a Hinged Flat Plate (Central Problem) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Setup:** A flat plate of negligible thickness is hinged at the bottom. The jet strikes the plate at a perpendicular distance $L$ from the hinge, the plate makes an angle $\theta$ with the jet, and the plate's centre of gravity is at a distance $\bar{x}$ from the hinge (along the plate). **Step 1: Force exerted by the jet on the plate (normal to plate).** From Derivation 2: $$F_n = \rho \, a \, V^{2} \sin\theta$$ **Step 2: Determine the point of application of $F_n$.** The force $F_n$ acts at the **centroid of the jet's contact patch on the plate** — which is the centre of the jet cross-section. By geometry, this point lies at perpendicular distance $L$ from the hinge. **Step 3: Calculate the moment (torque) about the hinge.** The moment is the product of the force and its perpendicular distance from the pivot: $$M_{jet} = F_n \times L = \rho \, a \, V^{2} \sin\theta \times L$$ This moment tends to **rotate the plate in the direction of jet flow**. **Step 4: Counter-moment from the plate's weight.** If the plate has weight $W$ acting vertically downward at its centre of gravity, the perpendicular distance from the hinge to the line of action of $W$ is $\bar{x} \cos\theta$ (if $\bar{x}$ is measured along the inclined plate, and $W$ is vertical). For a small plate where $W$ acts effectively horizontally relative to the plate, the moment due to weight is: $$M_W = W \times \bar{x}$$ **Step 5: Equilibrium condition.** For the plate to remain in static equilibrium (i.e., not rotate): $$M_{jet} = M_W$$ $$\rho \, a \, V^{2} \sin\theta \times L = W \times \bar{x}$$ **Step 6: Solve for any unknown (e.g., required jet velocity, jet area, or plate weight).** $$\boxed{W = \frac{\rho \, a \, V^{2} \sin\theta \cdot L}{\bar{x}} \quad \text{or} \quad V = \sqrt{\frac{W \bar{x}}{\rho \, a \, \sin\theta \cdot L}}}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Hinged Curved Vane (Conceptual Step to Pelton Wheel) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Setup:** A single curved vane hinged at one end deflects the jet through an angle $\phi$ such that the jet leaves the vane at angle $\phi$ to its original direction. The vane is smooth (frictionless), so the speed remains $V$ throughout. **Step 1: Initial velocity of jet (in the original flow direction).** $$V_1 = V \quad \text{(horizontal)}$$ **Step 2: Final velocity of jet (after leaving the vane).** The jet leaves at angle $\phi$ from the original direction (curved vane turns flow by $\phi$). The component of final velocity in the **original jet direction** is: $$V_{2x} = V \cos\phi$$ **Step 3: Change in velocity in the original jet direction.** $$\Delta V = V_1 - V_{2x} = V - V \cos\phi = V(1 - \cos\phi)$$ **Step 4: Apply the momentum equation.** $$F = \dot{m} \times \Delta V = (\rho a V) \times V(1 - \cos\phi)$$ $$\boxed{F = \rho \, a \, V^{2} (1 - \cos\phi)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Special Case — Symmetric Curved Vane ($\phi = 180°$):** The jet is reversed completely. > $F = \rho a V^2 (1 - \cos 180°) = \rho a V^2 (1 - (-1)) = 2 \rho a V^2$ — this is the **maximum possible force** and the limiting design principle for Pelton wheel buckets (where the angle is approximately $165°$ to $170°$ for maximum efficiency). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Force Resolution on a Hinged Inclined Plate > [!NOTE] > The diagram below maps the **force resolution logic** of a jet striking a hinged flat plate inclined at angle $\theta$. This is a **functional architecture flow** representing the physics of momentum transfer — it is the exact block used in the KTU board-answer sketch. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Water Jet from Nozzle
Velocity V, Area a] --> B[Jet Strikes Hinged Plate
Inclined at angle theta] B --> C[Resolve Jet Velocity into Two Components] C --> D[Normal Component V sin theta
Perpendicular to Plate] C --> E[Tangential Component V cos theta
Along Plate Surface] D --> F[Normal Force on Plate
Fn = rho times a times V squared sin theta] E --> G[No Tangential Force
Frictionless Plate Assumption] F --> H[Resolve Fn Along Jet Direction] H --> I[Horizontal Component Fx = rho a V squared sin squared theta] F --> J[Moment About Hinge M = Fn times L] J --> K[Equilibrium with Plate Weight W] K --> L[Final Design Equation W times x bar = rho a V squared sin theta times L] ``` ## 4.2 Free-Body Force Triangle (Conceptual Sketch) > [!NOTE] > **For the KTU board exam, draw the following schematic as part of your answer (5–6 marks weightage):** A triangle is formed by three vectors representing the forces at the point of impact: - **Vector $V$ (jet direction, horizontal):** The jet's original momentum direction - **Vector $F_n$ (normal to plate):** The reaction force on the plate, perpendicular to the plate surface - **Vector $F_x$ (horizontal, along original jet):** The resolved force in the original jet direction The angle between $V$ and the plate is $\theta$; the angle between $F_n$ and $F_x$ is also $\theta$ (because $F_n$ is normal to a plate inclined at $\theta$). ``` F_n (normal to plate) ↑ |\ | \ | \ | \ θ | \ | \___________________ | ↗ | F_x (along jet) (plate surface) ──────────────→ ←—————————— θ ——————————→ (jet direction) ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow — Steps to Solve a Hinged Plate Problem ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Step 1 Identify Given Data
V, a, theta, L, x bar] --> P2[Step 2 Compute Mass Flow Rate
m dot equals rho a V] P2 --> P3[Step 3 Apply Momentum Theorem
Fn equals rho a V squared sin theta] P3 --> P4[Step 4 Compute Moment about Hinge
M equals Fn times L] P4 --> P5[Step 5 Set Equilibrium Condition
M jet equals M weight] P5 --> P6[Step 6 Solve for Unknown
Find W or V or a] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Style]** *State and explain the principle of conservation of momentum as applied to the impact of a jet on a hinged flat plate.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Principle of Conservation of Linear Momentum** states that the net force acting on a fluid particle or control volume in a given direction equals the rate of change of linear momentum in that direction. > > Mathematically: > $$F = \frac{d(mV)}{dt} = \dot{m}(V_1 - V_2)$$ > > For a jet of water striking a hinged flat plate: > - The water jet possesses momentum $\dot{m} \times V$ in the direction of flow. > - After striking the plate, the velocity component normal to the plate is **reduced to zero** (frictionless plate, water cannot penetrate). > - The change in momentum per second gives the **force exerted by the jet on the plate**. > - This force, when applied at a distance from the hinge, produces a **moment (torque)** that tends to rotate the plate. > > (3 marks: 1 mark for statement, 1 for formula, 1 for application) ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]** *A jet of water of diameter 75 mm strikes a flat plate normally with a velocity of 20 m/s. Calculate the force exerted by the jet on the plate. Take $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.* **Model Solution (3 marks):** **Given:** - Diameter $d = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$ - Velocity $V = 20 \text{ m/s}$ - Density $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ **Step 1: Compute jet area.** $$a = \frac{\pi}{4} d^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.075)^{2} = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$ **Step 2: Apply the force equation for normal impact.** $$F = \rho \, a \, V^{2} = 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times (20)^{2}$$ $$F = 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 400$$ $$\boxed{F = 1767.1 \text{ N} \approx 1.767 \text{ kN}}$$ ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern for Module 3, students are offered an internal choice. Below are **TWO fully independent 14-mark questions** with complete sub-parts and worked solutions. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Style, Module 3 ESE Pattern]** **(a)** A jet of water having a diameter of **100 mm** strikes a flat plate **hinged at the bottom** with a velocity of **25 m/s**. The plate is **inclined at 30°** to the jet. The jet strikes the plate at a perpendicular distance of **200 mm from the hinge**. Find the **force exerted by the jet on the plate** normal to the plate, and the **force component along the direction of the jet**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** If the plate has a **weight of 150 N** acting at a distance of **150 mm from the hinge** along the plate, find the **angular velocity** at which the plate will start rotating, given the moment of inertia of the plate about the hinge is $I = 0.5 \text{ kg·m}^{2}$. Assume the plate is initially at rest. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Question A:** #### Part (a) — Force Calculation (7 marks) **Given Data:** - Jet diameter $d = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$ - Jet velocity $V = 25 \text{ m/s}$ - Plate inclination $\theta = 30°$ - Distance from hinge to jet strike point $L = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$ - Density $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ **Step 1: Compute jet cross-sectional area.** $$a = \frac{\pi}{4} d^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.1)^{2} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$ **Step 2: Compute the normal force on the plate.** Using the formula derived in Section 3.2: $$F_n = \rho \, a \, V^{2} \sin\theta$$ $$F_n = 1000 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times (25)^{2} \times \sin 30°$$ $$F_n = 1000 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 625 \times 0.5$$ $$\boxed{F_n = 2454.4 \text{ N} \approx 2.454 \text{ kN}}$$ **Step 3: Resolve $F_n$ along the direction of the jet.** $$F_x = F_n \sin\theta = 2454.4 \times \sin 30° = 2454.4 \times 0.5$$ $$\boxed{F_x = 1227.2 \text{ N} \approx 1.227 \text{ kN}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Note:** $F_x$ can also be written as $\rho a V^2 \sin^2\theta$, giving $F_x = 1000 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 625 \times 0.25 = 1227.2 \text{ N}$ ✓ #### Part (b) — Angular Velocity Calculation (7 marks) **Given Data:** - Weight of plate $W = 150 \text{ N}$ - Distance of CG from hinge $\bar{x} = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$ - Moment of inertia $I = 0.5 \text{ kg·m}^{2}$ - Initial state: plate at rest **Step 1: Net moment about the hinge.** Moment due to jet force (tending to rotate plate): $$M_{jet} = F_n \times L = 2454.4 \times 0.2 = 490.88 \text{ N·m}$$ Moment due to weight of plate (opposing rotation): $$M_W = W \times \bar{x} \cos\theta = 150 \times 0.15 \times \cos 30°$$ $$M_W = 22.5 \times 0.866 = 19.485 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 2: Net unbalanced moment (driving moment).** $$M_{net} = M_{jet} - M_W = 490.88 - 19.485 = 471.395 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 3: Angular acceleration of the plate.** Using Newton's second law for rotation: $$\alpha = \frac{M_{net}}{I} = \frac{471.395}{0.5} = 942.79 \text{ rad/s}^{2}$$ **Step 4: Using the kinematic equation to find angular velocity.** Assuming the plate starts rotating through a small angle such that the jet no longer strikes it effectively, or assuming it reaches an angular velocity corresponding to a specific time $t = 1 \text{ s}$ from rest: $$\omega = \omega_0 + \alpha t = 0 + 942.79 \times 1$$ $$\boxed{\omega = 942.79 \text{ rad/s}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **For full marks, students should mention:** "If the plate is assumed to rotate through a time of 1 second starting from rest, the angular velocity attained is 942.79 rad/s. If the problem specifies a particular time interval, use $\omega = \alpha t$." --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice] **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style, Module 3 ESE Pattern]** **(a)** A jet of water with a **diameter of 50 mm** and a **velocity of 30 m/s** strikes a **curved vane hinged at one end**. The vane deflects the jet through an angle of **150°**. Calculate the **force exerted by the jet on the vane** and the **work done per second** by the jet on the vane. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A flat plate is held **perpendicular** to a water jet of **diameter 75 mm** moving with a velocity of **18 m/s**. The plate is now **tilted to 60°** with the jet. Determine the **ratio of the force in the tilted position to the force in the perpendicular position** and the **ratio of the normal force components along the jet direction**. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Question B:** #### Part (a) — Curved Vane Force and Work Done (7 marks) **Given Data:** - Jet diameter $d = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$ - Jet velocity $V = 30 \text{ m/s}$ - Vane deflection angle $\phi = 150°$ - $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ **Step 1: Compute jet area and mass flow rate.** $$a = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^{2} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$ $$\dot{m} = \rho \, a \, V = 1000 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 30 = 58.91 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step 2: Compute force on the curved vane.** Using Derivation 4 (Section 3.4): $$F = \rho \, a \, V^{2} (1 - \cos\phi)$$ $$F = 1000 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times (30)^{2} \times (1 - \cos 150°)$$ Now, $\cos 150° = -\cos 30° = -0.866$ $$F = 1000 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 900 \times (1 - (-0.866))$$ $$F = 1.767 \times 900 \times 1.866$$ $$F = 1589.7 \times 1.866$$ $$\boxed{F = 2966.4 \text{ N} \approx 2.966 \text{ kN}}$$ **Step 3: Work done per second (Power).** Since the vane is stationary and the force $F$ acts on the vane in the direction of jet flow, the work done per second is zero (no displacement of the vane itself). $$\text{Work done per second} = F \times 0 = 0 \text{ W}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Note:** In the **stationary vane** case, the force is maximum but the work done is zero because the vane does not move. To extract useful work, the vane must be allowed to move (e.g., a Pelton wheel bucket). In a moving vane problem, the relative velocity $(V - u)$ is used and work done per second = $F \times u$. #### Part (b) — Force Ratio for Tilted vs Perpendicular Plate (7 marks) **Given Data:** - Diameter $d = 75 \text{ mm}$, so $a = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^{2} = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$ - Velocity $V = 18 \text{ m/s}$ - Tilt angle $\theta = 60°$ **Step 1: Force when plate is perpendicular to jet ($\theta_1 = 90°$).** $$F_{90} = \rho \, a \, V^{2} \sin 90° = \rho \, a \, V^{2}$$ $$F_{90} = 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times (18)^{2} = 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 324 = 1431.4 \text{ N}$$ **Step 2: Force when plate is tilted at $\theta_2 = 60°$.** $$F_{60} = \rho \, a \, V^{2} \sin 60° = 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 324 \times 0.866$$ $$F_{60} = 1431.4 \times 0.866 = 1239.6 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3: Ratio of forces (tilted to perpendicular).** $$\frac{F_{60}}{F_{90}} = \frac{\rho a V^{2} \sin 60°}{\rho a V^{2} \sin 90°} = \frac{\sin 60°}{\sin 90°} = \frac{0.866}{1}$$ $$\boxed{\frac{F_{60}}{F_{90}} = 0.866 \text{ (or } \sin 60°) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}$$ **Step 4: Component of tilted force along jet direction.** $$F_{x,60} = \rho \, a \, V^{2} \sin^{2} 60° = 1431.4 \times (0.866)^{2} = 1431.4 \times 0.75 = 1073.5 \text{ N}$$ **Step 5: Ratio of components along jet direction.** $$\frac{F_{x,60}}{F_{90}} = \frac{\sin^{2} 60°}{\sin 90°} = 0.75 = \frac{3}{4}$$ $$\boxed{\frac{F_{x,60}}{F_{90}} = 0.75 \text{ (or } \frac{3}{4})}$$ ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–4 Marks in KTU Exams (Avoid These!):** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to m:** Students often plug in $d = 100$ instead of $d = 0.1$ in the area formula. **Always convert all units to SI first.** > > 2. **Mixing up $\sin\theta$ and $\sin^{2}\theta$:** KTU examiners deliberately ask for either the *normal force* ($\sin\theta$) or the *force along the jet* ($\sin^{2}\theta$). Read the question twice — note the exact phrase "**normal to plate**" vs. "**along the jet direction**." > > 3. **Ignoring the moment arm:** For hinged plate problems, you **must** multiply the force by the perpendicular distance $L$ from the hinge. Simply computing $F$ without the moment loses **3 marks**. > > 4. **Using $\rho$ in kN/m³ inconsistently:** If you use $\rho = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^{3}$, the answer will be in kN. If you use $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$, the answer will be in N. **Be consistent — mention the unit in the final box.** > > 5. **Not drawing the free-body diagram:** In 14-mark questions, KTU examiners award **1–2 marks** for a clear sketch showing the jet direction, plate inclination $\theta$, hinge location, and force $F_n$. **Always include a diagram.** > > 6. **Sign errors in the curved vane formula:** $F = \rho a V^2(1 - \cos\phi)$ — students often write $(1 + \cos\phi)$ by mistake. **Remember:** the formula is $(1 - \cos\phi)$ when the vane **deflects the jet by angle $\phi$**. Only when $\phi = 0$ (no deflection) does $F = 0$. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Bulletins — Memorize These for the KTU Exam:** > > - **Core principle:** Force on plate = rate of change of momentum = $\dot{m} \Delta V$ > - **Flat plate perpendicular to jet:** $F = \rho a V^2$ > - **Flat plate inclined at $\theta$ to jet (normal force):** $F_n = \rho a V^2 \sin\theta$ > - **Flat plate inclined at $\theta$ (force along jet direction):** $F_x = \rho a V^2 \sin^2\theta$ > - **Hinged plate moment:** $M = F_n \times L = \rho a V^2 \sin\theta \cdot L$ > - **Hinged plate equilibrium with weight:** $F_n \cdot L = W \cdot \bar{x}$ > - **Curved vane (deflection angle $\phi$):** $F = \rho a V^2 (1 - \cos\phi)$ > - **Maximum force on curved vane:** When $\phi = 180°$, $F_{max} = 2 \rho a V^2$ > - **Units check:** $\rho$ in kg/m³, $a$ in m², $V$ in m/s, $F$ in N > - **Density of water:** $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$ (use 9.81 kN/m³ if force in kN) > - **Limiting case 1:** $\theta = 0° \Rightarrow F = 0$ (plate parallel, no obstruction) > - **Limiting case 2:** $\theta = 90° \Rightarrow F = \rho a V^2$ (plate perpendicular) > - **Frictionless assumption:** Tangential velocity unchanged; only normal component destroyed > - **Pelton wheel connection:** Buckets deflect jet by ~165–170° for max force near the ideal 180° > - **Always draw:** Free-body diagram showing jet, plate, $\theta$, $L$, $F_n$, $F_x$ > - **Formula to avoid confusion:** $Q = aV$ (discharge), $\dot{m} = \rho Q = \rho a V$ > - **KTU favourite numerical values:** $d = 50, 75, 100$ mm; $V = 15, 20, 25, 30$ m/s; $\theta = 30°, 45°, 60°$

topics.title: Impact of Jet on Moving Series of Flat and Curved Vanes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Impact of Jet on Moving Series of Flat and Curved Vanes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Impact of Jet** is defined as the force exerted by a fluid jet (typically water) when it strikes a stationary or moving vane, due to the **change in momentum** of the fluid mass. According to Newton's Second Law of Motion applied to a control volume, this force is mathematically equal to the rate of change of momentum of the fluid jet.

In the context of **hydraulic turbines** (e.g., Pelton wheel, Turgo wheel, modern impulse turbines) and **pumps**, the impact of jets on vanes forms the foundational principle of energy conversion between a high-velocity fluid and mechanical rotational work.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** This topic directly links to *Hydraulic Turbines – Impulse Turbines (Pelton Wheel)*. The force on moving vanes derived here is identical to the tangential force on a Pelton wheel bucket. Mastering this means you master the Pelton wheel theory.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in the rain holding a flat plate. The raindrops (analogous to **fluid particles in a jet**) strike your plate with a certain velocity. You will feel a **pushing force** on your palm. This is the *impact of the jet*.

Now, suppose you start **running forward in the same direction as the rain**. What happens? The relative velocity of the raindrops with respect to your hand decreases (because your hand is moving away). The impact force reduces, but you are *doing work* against the rain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** When a vane is stationary, the entire kinetic energy of the jet is wasted as noise, heat, and turbulence. When the vane **moves in the direction of the jet**, part of the kinetic energy is converted into useful mechanical work — this is the heart of every impulse turbine.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

The following standard metrics are used throughout this module:

- **Density of water:** $\rho = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ (often written as $\rho = 1000$)
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$
- **Specific weight of water:** $\gamma = 9810 \; \text{N/m}^3$
- **Mass flow rate:** $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot a \cdot V$ (kg/s), where $a$ is jet area in m² and $V$ is jet velocity in m/s
- **Discharge:** $Q = a \cdot V$ (m³/s)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** In KTU board examinations, $\rho$ is given as **1000 kg/m³** unless otherwise specified. Always substitute this numerical value in the *final* step of your calculation to fetch full marks.

## 1.4 Classification of Vanes

Vanes encountered in this module are classified as:

| Vane Type | Sub-Category | Velocity Consideration |
|---|---|---|
| **Flat Vane** | Stationary / Moving / Inclined | Single or Series |
| **Curved Vane** | Single / Series | With tangential entry / radial exit |
| **Spherical / Hemispherical Vane** | Special case | Used in Pelton buckets |

The shape of the vane determines the **angle of deflection** of the jet and hence the magnitude of momentum change and the resulting force.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundation: Newton's Momentum Equation

Consider a fluid jet of cross-sectional area $a$ moving with absolute velocity $V$ striking a vane. The **mass of fluid striking per second** is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot a \cdot V$$

By Newton's Second Law:

$$F = \dot{m} \times \text{Change in velocity} = \rho a V (V_1 - V_2)$$

This single equation, when applied to specific vane geometries, gives every formula in this module.

## 2.2 Case 1: Force of Jet on Stationary Flat Plate (Perpendicular)

**Setup:** A jet of area $a$ and velocity $V$ strikes a flat plate held perpendicular (at 90°) to the jet. After striking, the water spreads radially along the plate (splashes sideways) with negligible velocity in the original direction.

**Why:** Because the plate is flat, there is no curvature to redirect the flow. The final velocity component along the jet direction becomes zero. So $V_1 = V$ and $V_2 = 0$.

$$F_x = \rho a V (V - 0)$$

$$\boxed{F_x = \rho a V^2}$$

This force acts **normal to the plate** (along the jet direction).

## 2.3 Case 2: Force of Jet on Stationary Inclined Flat Plate

**Setup:** A flat plate is held at an angle $\theta$ to the jet direction. The jet's velocity component normal to the plate is $V \sin\theta$.

**Why:** Only the velocity component *perpendicular to the plate* causes an impulse. The tangential component slides along the plate without contributing to normal force.

$$F_n = \rho a V \cdot V \sin\theta$$

$$\boxed{F_n = \rho a V^2 \sin\theta}$$

The force acts **normal to the plate surface**.

## 2.4 Case 3: Force of Jet on Moving Flat Plate (Perpendicular)

**Setup:** The flat plate moves with velocity $u$ in the same direction as the jet. Since the plate is moving away from the jet, the *relative velocity* of the jet with respect to the plate is $V - u$.

**Why:** From the frame of reference of the moving plate, the jet appears to approach with velocity $V - u$. The mass striking the plate per second is $\rho a (V - u)$.

$$F_x = \rho a (V - u) \cdot (V - u)$$

$$\boxed{F_x = \rho a (V - u)^2}$$

**Work done by the jet on the plate per second:**

$$W = F_x \cdot u = \rho a u (V - u)^2$$

**Efficiency of the system:**

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Work done per second}}{\text{Kinetic energy of jet per second}} = \frac{\rho a u (V - u)^2}{\frac{1}{2} \rho a V^3}$$

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{2u(V - u)^2}{V^3}}$$

**Maximum efficiency condition:** Differentiating and equating to zero gives $u = V/3$, and substituting back:

$$\eta_{\max} = \frac{2 \cdot (V/3) \cdot (2V/3)^2}{V^3} = \frac{2 \cdot V/3 \cdot 4V^2/9}{V^3} = \frac{8}{27} = 0.2963 \text{ or } 29.63\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The maximum efficiency of a single flat vane moving perpendicular to the jet is **$\eta_{\max} = 8/27 \approx 29.63\%$**, occurring at the *speed ratio* $u/V = 1/3$. This is a **favourite 3-mark question** in KTU exams.

## 2.5 Case 4: Force on Moving Inclined Flat Plate

For an inclined moving plate, the velocity component normal to the plate is $(V - u) \sin\theta$. Therefore:

$$F_n = \rho a (V - u)^2 \sin\theta$$

$$W = F_n \cdot u \cdot \cos\alpha$$

where $\alpha$ is the angle between the force direction and the motion of the plate.

## 2.6 Case 5: Series of Flat Vanes (Single Flat Vane to Multiple Vanes)

**Why Series of Vanes?** A single vane cannot capture the entire jet continuously. To extract energy from the *entire* jet, the jet is split and distributed over multiple vanes mounted on a wheel. In **series of flat vanes**, all vanes are identical and equally spaced.

If there are $n$ vanes mounted on the wheel, the jet of area $a$ is split equally. The discharge striking each vane is $Q/n$ and the area of jet striking each vane is $a/n$.

**Relative velocity of jet w.r.t. each vane:** $(V - u)$

**Mass of fluid striking each vane per second:** $\dot{m}_{vane} = \rho \cdot \frac{a}{n} \cdot (V - u)$

**Force on each vane (perpendicular flat plate):**

$$F_{vane} = \rho \frac{a}{n} (V - u)^2$$

**Work done by the jet on each vane per second:**

$$W_{vane} = F_{vane} \cdot u = \rho \frac{a u}{n} (V - u)^2$$

**Total work done by jet (work transferred to wheel):**

$$W_{total} = n \times W_{vane} = \rho a u (V - u)^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** For *flat* vanes in series, the **total work done by the jet is independent of the number of vanes $n$**. Each vane carries $1/n$ of the load, so the total remains the same. This changes dramatically for *curved* vanes.

**Efficiency for series of flat vanes:**

$$\eta = \frac{2u(V - u)^2}{V^3}$$

The efficiency formula is the **same as a single moving flat plate**.

## 2.7 Case 6: Force on a Single Moving Curved Vane (Radial Flow)

A curved vane is designed to **gradually deflect the flow** through a large angle (often close to 180°), reversing the jet direction. This causes a **much larger change in momentum** and therefore a much larger force.

Consider a single curved vane moving with velocity $u$, with the jet entering at velocity $V$ and leaving at a relative velocity $V_{r2} = V - u$ (assuming no friction).

**Velocity triangle at exit of vane:**
- Whirl velocity at exit: $V_{w2} = (V - u) \cos\phi$ (where $\phi$ is the angle the relative velocity makes with the direction of motion at exit)
- For a **single curved vane with no friction and tangential entry (radial exit)**: $\phi = 0$, hence $V_{w2} = V - u$.

**Force on vane in direction of motion (using momentum equation in $x$-direction):**

$$F_x = \rho a V (V_{w1}) - \rho a V (V_{w2})$$

For tangential entry: $V_{w1} = V$. Therefore:

$$F_x = \rho a V^2 - \rho a V \cdot V_{w2}$$

In terms of relative velocity and using continuity:

$$F_x = \rho a (V - u)(V - V_{w2})$$

For a frictionless vane with $\phi = 0$:

$$\boxed{F_x = \rho a (V - u)(V - (V - u)) = \rho a (V - u) \cdot u = \rho a u (V - u)}$$

**Work done on the vane per second:**

$$W = F_x \cdot u = \rho a u^2 (V - u)$$

**Kinetic energy of jet per second:** $KE = \frac{1}{2} \rho a V^3$

**Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{W}{KE} = \frac{2u^2(V - u)}{V^3}$$

**Maximum efficiency condition:** Differentiating and setting to zero:

$$\frac{d\eta}{du} = 0 \Rightarrow u = \frac{2V}{3}$$

$$\eta_{\max} = \frac{2 \cdot (2V/3)^2 \cdot (V/3)}{V^3} = \frac{2 \cdot 4V^2/9 \cdot V/3}{V^3} = \frac{8}{27}$$

Wait — for curved vanes, a more common derivation is for a vane where the relative velocity leaves **backwards** (180° deflection). For a properly designed curved vane where the relative velocity exits in the opposite direction of jet motion, the maximum efficiency can be much higher.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Formula for Single Curved Vane (with $\phi$ being angle of vane at exit):**
> $$F_x = \rho a (V - u)(V - u\cos\phi)$$
> $$W = F_x \cdot u = \rho a u (V - u)(V - u\cos\phi)$$
> $$\eta = \frac{2u(V - u)(V - u\cos\phi)}{V^3}$$
> For $\phi = 0$ (radial exit), this gives the **maximum force case** and the maximum efficiency can approach much higher values depending on $\phi$.

## 2.8 Case 7: Series of Moving Curved Vanes

If the jet of area $a$ strikes a wheel carrying $n$ curved vanes, each vane receives a discharge of $Q/n$, and the area effective on each vane is $a/n$.

**Force on each vane (in direction of motion):**

$$F_{vane} = \rho \frac{a}{n} (V - u)(V - u\cos\phi)$$

**Work done by jet on each vane per second:**

$$W_{vane} = F_{vane} \cdot u = \rho \frac{a u}{n} (V - u)(V - u\cos\phi)$$

**Total work done by jet (work transferred to wheel per second):**

$$W_{total} = n \times W_{vane}$$

$$\boxed{W_{total} = \rho a u (V - u)(V - u\cos\phi)}$$

**Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{2u(V - u)(V - u\cos\phi)}{V^3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Comparison with Flat Vanes:** For curved vanes in series, the *force on each vane* is $1/n$ times the force on a single vane, but the *total work transferred* is the **same as a single vane**. This is the principle that allows Pelton wheels to extract maximum energy from a jet.

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every formula required for board examinations:

| S.No | Vane Configuration | Force (N) | Work Done (W, kW) | Efficiency $\eta$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stationary flat plate (perpendicular) | $F = \rho a V^2$ | $W = 0$ | $0$ |
| 2 | Stationary flat plate (inclined, angle $\theta$) | $F_n = \rho a V^2 \sin\theta$ | $W = 0$ | $0$ |
| 3 | Single moving flat plate (perpendicular) | $F = \rho a (V - u)^2$ | $W = \rho a u (V - u)^2$ | $\dfrac{2u(V - u)^2}{V^3}$, $\eta_{\max} = 8/27$ at $u = V/3$ |
| 4 | Single moving flat plate (inclined, $\theta$) | $F_n = \rho a (V - u)^2 \sin\theta$ | $W = \rho a u (V - u)^2 \sin\theta \cos\alpha$ | Depends on $\alpha$ |
| 5 | Series of moving flat vanes ($n$ vanes) | $F_{vane} = \dfrac{\rho a}{n} (V - u)^2$ | $W_{total} = \rho a u (V - u)^2$ | $\dfrac{2u(V - u)^2}{V^3}$ |
| 6 | Single moving curved vane (exit angle $\phi$) | $F_x = \rho a (V - u)(V - u\cos\phi)$ | $W = \rho a u (V - u)(V - u\cos\phi)$ | $\dfrac{2u(V - u)(V - u\cos\phi)}{V^3}$ |
| 7 | Series of moving curved vanes ($n$ vanes) | $F_{vane} = \dfrac{\rho a}{n} (V - u)(V - u\cos\phi)$ | $W_{total} = \rho a u (V - u)(V - u\cos\phi)$ | $\dfrac{2u(V - u)(V - u\cos\phi)}{V^3}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbols Used:**
> - $\rho$ = density of fluid (kg/m³) — use **1000** for water
> - $a$ = cross-sectional area of jet (m²)
> - $V$ = velocity of jet (m/s)
> - $u$ = velocity of vane (m/s)
> - $\phi$ = angle of relative velocity at exit of vane with direction of motion
> - $\theta$ = angle of inclined plate with jet direction
> - $n$ = number of vanes in series
