# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Differentiate between an impulse turbine and a reaction turbine. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Impulse Turbine | Reaction Turbine |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy conversion** | All available head is converted into kinetic energy (via nozzle) *before* striking the runner. Pressure during flow = atmospheric. | Only part of head is converted at inlet; pressure drop occurs *inside* the runner. Pressure during flow > atmospheric. |
| **Working principle** | Impulse-momentum exchange (jet strikes buckets) | Pressure drop across blade passages produces reaction force |
| **Example** | **Pelton Wheel** | **Francis Turbine, Kaplan Turbine** |
| **Casing** | No casing needed; runner operates in air | Water-tight casing required to contain pressure |
| **Draft tube** | Not required | Essential for head recovery |

*(Allocate ~1 mark per major point: definition + example + key contrast.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Explain why priming is essential in a centrifugal pump but not in a reciprocating pump.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A centrifugal pump is a **rotodynamic machine** that depends on *centrifugal force* to lift the liquid. The pressure difference at the impeller eye (which lifts the liquid up the suction pipe) is produced by the liquid's own mass being flung outward. **Air is ~800 times less dense than water**, so an air pocket at the eye cannot develop sufficient pressure difference — the pump simply churns the air without lifting water. **Priming** = filling the casing and suction pipe with liquid to displace air, ensuring the impeller acts on a continuous liquid column. Hence, priming is **mandatory**.

In a **reciprocating pump**, the piston *physically pushes* the liquid out of the cylinder on each stroke. As the piston retracts, it creates a vacuum that the liquid fills (suction stroke), and as it advances, it physically displaces the liquid. The displacement action works on both liquids and gases equally, so the pump can self-prime (draw air out progressively until liquid arrives). Hence, priming is **not essential** in a reciprocating pump.

*(Allocate ~1.5 marks for the centrifugal pump explanation, 1.5 marks for the reciprocating pump explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the work done per second by water on a **Pelton wheel runner**. Hence, show that the **hydraulic efficiency is maximum when the bucket velocity is half the jet velocity**. Assume the jet is deflected through an angle $\theta$ with a friction coefficient $k$.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Setting up the relative velocity frame (1 mark):**
The jet velocity = $V_1$. Bucket peripheral velocity = $u$. In the bucket's reference frame, the relative velocity of the jet is $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$.

**Step 2 — Mass flow rate (0.5 marks):**
$$\dot{m} = \rho a V_1$$
where $a$ is the jet area.

**Step 3 — Apply impulse-momentum theorem (2 marks):**
The jet is deflected through angle $\theta$ with friction, so outlet relative velocity $V_{r2} = k(V_1 - u)$ at angle $\theta$ to the reversed direction.
The tangential force on the bucket:
$$F_x = \rho a V_{r1} [V_{r1} - (-V_{r2}\cos\theta)] = \rho a (V_1 - u)^2 (1 + k\cos\theta)$$

**Step 4 — Work done per second (Power) (1 mark):**
$$P = F_x \cdot u = \rho a u (V_1 - u)^2 (1 + k\cos\theta) = \rho Q u (V_1 - u)^2 \dfrac{(1 + k\cos\theta)}{V_1}$$

**Step 5 — Hydraulic efficiency definition (0.5 marks):**
$$\eta_h = \frac{\text{Work done per sec}}{\text{KE supplied per sec}} = \frac{\rho Q u (V_1 - u)^2 (1 + k\cos\theta)/V_1}{\frac{1}{2}\rho a V_1^3} = \frac{2u(V_1 - u)(1 + k\cos\theta)}{V_1^2}$$

**Step 6 — Maximize by differentiation (2 marks):**
$$\frac{d\eta_h}{du} = \frac{2(1+k\cos\theta)}{V_1^2}(V_1 - 2u) = 0 \implies u = \frac{V_1}{2}$$

Substituting back:
$$\eta_{h,\max} = \frac{2 \cdot (V_1/2)(V_1/2)(1+k\cos\theta)}{V_1^2} = \frac{(1 + k\cos\theta)}{2}$$

For $k = 0.9$, $\theta = 15°$: $\eta_{h,\max} = \dfrac{1 + 0.9 \cos 15°}{2} = \dfrac{1.869}{2} \approx 93.5\%$. $\blacksquare$

---

**(b)** A Pelton wheel has a mean runner diameter of **1.2 m** and runs at **500 rpm**. The jet diameter is **100 mm** and the net head at the nozzle is **400 m**. The coefficient of velocity for the nozzle is **0.98** and the bucket deflection angle is **165°**. The bucket friction coefficient is **0.9**. Calculate: **(i)** the power developed, and **(ii)** the hydraulic efficiency.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $D = 1.2$ m → $u = \dfrac{\pi \times 1.2 \times 500}{60} = 31.416$ m/s
- $d = 100$ mm = 0.1 m → $a = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$ m²
- $H = 400$ m, $C_v = 0.98$, $k = 0.9$, deflection angle = 165° → $\theta = 180° - 165° = 15°$

**Step 1 — Jet velocity (1 mark):**
$$V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH} = 0.98 \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 400} = 0.98 \times 88.54 = 86.77 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Discharge (1 mark):**
$$Q = a V_1 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 86.77 = 0.6815 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Bucket speed (1 mark):**
$$u = \frac{\pi D N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 1.2 \times 500}{60} = 31.416 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Power developed (2 marks):**
$$P = \rho Q u (V_1 - u)^2 (1 + k\cos\theta)/V_1$$
$$= 1000 \times 0.6815 \times 31.416 \times (86.77 - 31.416)^2 \times (1 + 0.9 \cos 15°) / 86.77$$
$$(V_1 - u) = 55.354 \text{ m/s}$$
$$(V_1 - u)^2 = 3064.07 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$
$$(1 + 0.9 \cos 15°) = 1 + 0.9 \times 0.9659 = 1.8693$$
$$P = 1000 \times 0.6815 \times 31.416 \times 3064.07 \times 1.8693 / 86.77$$
$$P = \frac{1000 \times 0.6815 \times 31.416 \times 3064.07 \times 1.8693}{86.77}$$
Computing numerator: $0.6815 \times 31.416 = 21.41$; $21.41 \times 3064.07 = 65{,}602$; $65{,}602 \times 1.8693 = 122{,}634$; $122{,}634 \times 1000 = 1.226 \times 10^8$
$$P = \frac{1.226 \times 10^8}{86.77} = 1.413 \times 10^6 \text{ W} = 1413 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Hydraulic efficiency (2 marks):**
$$\eta_h = \frac{2u(V_1 - u)(1 + k\cos\theta)}{V_1^2} = \frac{2 \times 31.416 \times 55.354 \times 1.8693}{86.77^2}$$
$$= \frac{2 \times 31.416 \times 55.354 \times 1.8693}{7529.1} = \frac{6496.5}{7529.1} = 0.8629 \approx 86.3\%$$

**Final Answers:** Power = **1413 kW**; $\eta_h$ = **86.3%**

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **working principle of a centrifugal pump**. Derive the expression for the **manometric head** of a centrifugal pump with backward-curved blades.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Working principle (3 marks):**
A centrifugal pump consists of an **impeller** (with curved blades) rotating inside a **volute casing**. The pump first needs to be **primed** (casing filled with liquid). As the impeller rotates at high speed, the liquid at the eye (center) is flung outward by centrifugal force, creating a low-pressure region at the eye. Atmospheric pressure on the sump's free surface pushes liquid up the suction pipe into the eye. The liquid gains kinetic energy in the impeller; this KE is converted to pressure energy in the gradually expanding volute, and the high-pressure liquid is discharged through the delivery pipe.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Apply Euler's equation (1 mark):** For a centrifugal pump with no pre-whirl, $V_{w1} = 0$:
$$H_{\text{Euler}} = \frac{u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}}{g} = \frac{u_2 V_{w2}}{g}$$

**Step 2 — Velocity triangle at outlet (1.5 marks):** For a backward-curved blade at angle $\beta_2$:
$$V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{f2} \cot\beta_2$$
where $V_{f2}$ is the radial (flow) component at outlet.

**Step 3 — Substitute (0.5 marks):**
$$H_{\text{imp}} = \frac{u_2 (u_2 - V_{f2}\cot\beta_2)}{g} = \frac{u_2^2 - u_2 V_{f2}\cot\beta_2}{g}$$

**Step 4 — Manometric head (1 mark):** Subtracting the kinetic energy of the discharge (or accounting for casing losses):
$$H_m = \frac{u_2 V_{w2}}{g} - \text{casing losses}$$

For ideal case: $H_m = \dfrac{u_2 V_{w2}}{g}$. $\blacksquare$

---

**(b)** A single-acting reciprocating pump has a **bore of 150 mm** and a **stroke of 300 mm**. It runs at **60 rpm** and lifts water through a **total head of 20 m**. If the **diameter of the delivery pipe is 75 mm** and the **suction pipe is 100 mm**, find: **(i)** the theoretical discharge, **(ii)** the theoretical power required, and **(iii)** the slip and percentage slip if the actual discharge is **0.0045 m³/s**. Take the coefficient of discharge as **0.95**.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $D = 150$ mm = 0.15 m → $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^2 = 0.01767$ m²
- $L = 300$ mm = 0.3 m, $N = 60$ rpm, $H = 20$ m
- $C_d = 0.95$

**Step 1 — Theoretical discharge (2 marks):**
$$Q_{th} = \frac{A L N}{60} = \frac{0.01767 \times 0.3 \times 60}{60} = 0.00530 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 5.30 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical power (2 marks):**
$$P_{th} = \rho g Q_{th} H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.00530 \times 20 = 1039.86 \text{ W} \approx 1.04 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Actual discharge (1 mark):**
$$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot Q_{th} = 0.95 \times 0.00530 = 0.005035 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
(Wait — the actual discharge is given as 0.0045 m³/s, which is *less* than $C_d \times Q_{th}$. This means there is *slip* in addition to normal losses. Actually, the slip here is the difference between $C_d \times Q_{th}$ and $Q_{act}$ — but more rigorously, slip is the leakage past the piston/valves, so:)

**Re-interpretation:** $Q_{act} = C_d Q_{th} - Q_{slip}$, so slip discharge:
$$Q_{slip} = C_d Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 0.005035 - 0.0045 = 0.000535 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage slip (2 marks):**
$$\% \text{ slip} = \frac{Q_{slip}}{Q_{th}} \times 100 = \frac{0.000535}{0.00530} \times 100 = 10.09\%$$

**Final Answers:** $Q_{th}$ = **0.00530 m³/s**; $P_{th}$ = **1.04 kW**; % Slip = **10.1%**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Sign convention in Euler's equation** — marks lost: ~3 per question. Always state: "+ for turbines (work extracted), − for pumps (work supplied)".
> 2. **Deflection angle vs. curvature angle** in Pelton wheel — students confuse $\theta = 165°$ (curvature) with $\theta = 15°$ (deflection from straight-ahead). Use $\theta = 180° - 165°$ in the formula.
> 3. **Units** — $H$ in meters, $Q$ in m³/s, $\rho$ in kg/m³. Forgetting to convert mm→m loses 1 mark instantly.
> 4. **Forgetting $C_v$** when computing $V_1$ from head $H$ — assume ideal jet ($C_v = 1$) only if explicitly stated. Otherwise use $C_v\sqrt{2gH}$.
> 5. **Air vessel assumption** — in reciprocating pump problems, unless asked, neglect acceleration head (assume no air vessel). If an air vessel *is* present, slip is still present but the flow is smooth.
> 6. **Confusing volumetric efficiency with slip** — they are related but not the same. $\eta_{vol} = (Q_{th} - Q_{slip})/Q_{th}$ when slip is the only loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Read 5 Times Before Exam Day**

- ☐ **Pelton Wheel** = *Impulse turbine*; energy conversion happens *before* the runner (in nozzle); pressure = atmospheric in runner; uses *buckets* with splitter ridge.
- ☐ **Francis Turbine** = *Reaction turbine, mixed flow*; radial entry, axial exit; uses *guide vanes*; must be fully water-filled.
- ☐ **Kaplan Turbine** = *Reaction turbine, axial flow*; both runner blades AND guide vanes are adjustable; for low head, high discharge.
- ☐ **Centrifugal Pump** = energy transfer by *centrifugal force*; needs priming; volute casing converts KE to pressure.
- ☐ **Reciprocating Pump** = *positive displacement*; self-priming; produces pulsating flow (smoothened by air vessel).
- ☐ **Euler's Equation** = $W = \rho Q (u_1 V_{w1} \pm u_2 V_{w2})$ — the universal governing equation.
- ☐ **Optimum bucket speed** for Pelton = $u = V_1/2$ → maximum hydraulic efficiency.
- ☐ **Maximum hydraulic efficiency** of Pelton = $(1 + k\cos\theta)/2$ with $\theta$ measured from reversed direction.
- ☐ **Manometric head** of centrifugal pump (no pre-whirl) = $u_2 V_{w2}/g$.
- ☐ **Slip** in centrifugal pump (Stodola) = $\pi \sin\beta_2 / Z$; decreases as blade count $Z$ increases.
- ☐ **Theoretical discharge** of single-acting reciprocating pump = $ALN/60$.
- ☐ **$u = \pi D N/60$** is your most-forgotten formula — write it first on every question.
- ☐ **Density of water** = 1000 kg/m³; $g$ = 9.81 m/s² — these constants appear in nearly every numerical.
- ☐ **KTU specific:** Always use 9.81 (not 9.8 or 10) for $g$ unless an "approximate" note is given.
- ☐ **KTU 2024 Module 3 outcomes:** CO3 — *Apply momentum and energy principles to hydraulic machines; Analyze velocity triangles of impulse and reaction turbines.*
- ☐ **Practice question type:** "Derive Euler's equation" + "Solve a numerical on a Pelton/Francis/centrifugal pump" — these two patterns account for ~80% of Part B marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constructional Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Constructional Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Constructional Features" in Fluid Mechanics & Hydraulic Machines?

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCIET302)** syllabus, **Module 3 — Constructional Features** deals with the *physical engineering anatomy* of **Hydraulic Turbines** (Pelton Wheel, Francis Turbine, Kaplan Turbine) and **Hydraulic Pumps** (Centrifugal Pump, Reciprocating Pump). It explains **how each machine is actually built**, the **purpose of each component**, the **materials typically used**, and **how the fluid flows through the machine** during operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Constructional features refer to the detailed mechanical design and arrangement of components — such as casings, runners, impellers, guide vanes, nozzles, pistons, valves, and draft tubes — that together enable the efficient transfer of energy between a fluid and a rotating/reciprocating shaft in hydraulic machines.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Machine as a Factory" View

Imagine a **hydraulic machine as a specialized factory** designed to either:
- **Harvest energy from flowing water** (Turbines — water does work ON the machine), or
- **Invest energy into water to move it** (Pumps — machine does work ON the water).

Just like a factory has specialized departments (intake, processing, output), each hydraulic machine has:
- A **fluid entry zone** (nozzle / suction pipe / guide vanes)
- An **energy-exchange zone** (runner / impeller / cylinder-piston)
- A **fluid exit zone** (draft tube / delivery pipe / volute casing)
- A **structural & control zone** (casing, bearings, governors, seals)

Every part you study under "constructional features" is one of these "departments." If even one department fails or is poorly designed, the whole machine fails.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this topic matters for KTU exams:**
> Questions on constructional features are extremely frequent — typically a **3-mark short answer** (with a sketch) or a **7-mark descriptive** sub-part. KTU examiners frequently ask students to *label a neat sketch* and *briefly explain each component*. Memorizing diagrams and component functions is essential for scoring above 80%.

## 1.3 Broad Classification of Machines Studied in This Module

| S.No | Machine | Energy Flow Direction | KTU 2024 Status |
|------|---------|------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Pelton Wheel Turbine | Water → Shaft (Impulse) | High weightage |
| 2 | Francis Turbine | Water → Shaft (Reaction) | High weightage |
| 3 | Kaplan Turbine | Water → Shaft (Reaction, axial) | Moderate |
| 4 | Centrifugal Pump | Shaft → Water (Radial) | High weightage |
| 5 | Reciprocating Pump | Shaft → Water (Positive displacement) | High weightage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Master Categories to remember:**
> 1. **Turbines** = Energy **extracting** machines (water gives energy → mechanical output).
> 2. **Pumps** = Energy **imparting** machines (mechanical input → water gains pressure/head).
> Constructional features of these two categories are **mirror opposites** in many respects — turbines have *draft tubes* at the outlet; pumps have *suction pipes* at the inlet.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pelton Wheel Turbine — Constructional Features

The **Pelton wheel** is an **impulse-type turbine** operating under **high head** and **low discharge** conditions (typically H > 250 m). It was invented by American engineer **Lester Allan Pelton**.

### Main Components (in flow order):

1. **Penstock** — A large-diameter steel pipe that conveys water from the reservoir/forebay to the powerhouse. Acts as the *fluid supply highway*.
2. **Needle Valve / Spear / Nozzle Needle** — A finely machined, axially movable spear-shaped rod that controls the discharge (Q) flowing into the runner. Moving it forward decreases flow; pulling it back increases flow.
3. **Nozzle** — A convergent-divergent (or simply convergent) flow passage that **converts pressure energy of water into kinetic energy**. The jet velocity V₁ is determined here. Made of stainless steel or bronze to resist cavitation and wear.
4. **Deflector / Bypass Deflector** — A curved plate that momentarily diverts the jet away from the runner during emergency shutdown or load rejection, preventing runaway speed.
5. **Runner / Wheel** — The rotating disc carrying **double-cup-shaped buckets** on its periphery. Material: stainless steel, bronze, or cast iron. The buckets are designed as two hemispherical cups with a central splitter ridge — this splits the jet into two equal halves and reverses its direction by **nearly 170° to 180°**.
6. **Bucket** — Each bucket has:
   - A **central sharp ridge (splitter)**
   - Two **hemispherical cavities** on either side
   - A **cut-out (notch)** in the middle to allow the splitter to pass through without obstructing the oncoming jet
7. **Casing / Housing** — Completely encloses the runner to prevent splashing and to safely discharge any leakage. Does **not** function as a pressure vessel in impulse turbines (since atmospheric pressure prevails at the runner).
8. **Brake Jet** — A small auxiliary jet that applies a counter-torque on the back of the runner disc, bringing it to rest during shutdown (since Pelton wheels coast for a long time due to low friction).
9. **Governing Mechanism** — Regulates the needle valve position via a **servo-motor / relay cylinder / oil pressure unit**, automatically matching the runner speed to load demand.
10. **Shaft, Bearings, and Flywheel** — The shaft transmits mechanical power to the generator; **ball/roller bearings** support the shaft; **flywheel** smoothens power fluctuations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Material Insight:**
> Pelton buckets must withstand extreme impact from a high-velocity jet (V₁ up to 150–180 m/s). Hence they are forged from **stainless steel (e.g., 13 Cr / 4 Ni cast steel)** and later precision-machined and polished to a mirror finish. Cavitation is *not* a problem in impulse turbines because pressure at the runner is atmospheric.

## 2.2 Francis Turbine — Constructional Features

The **Francis turbine** is a **mixed-flow reaction turbine** (water enters radially, exits axially). It operates under **medium head** (typically 30 m to 300 m) and **medium to high discharge**.

### Main Components:

1. **Scroll Casing / Spiral Casing** — A **gradually decreasing cross-section** spiral passage surrounding the runner. It distributes water **uniformly and tangentially** around the entire circumference of the runner. The decreasing area maintains a **constant velocity of water (Vw) entering the guide vanes** despite the reducing peripheral length.
2. **Stay Ring / Speed Ring** — A stationary circular ring to which the spiral casing is bolted. It contains the **guide vane pivots** and provides structural rigidity.
3. **Guide Vanes / Wicket Gates** — A ring of **adjustable, aerofoil-shaped vanes** mounted on pivots inside the stay ring. By synchronously rotating all guide vanes, the **flow area and angle of entry** to the runner can be controlled — this is the **governing mechanism**. Closed during no-flow / shut-off.
4. **Moving Vanes / Regulating Vanes** — Same as guide vanes — alternate KTU terminology.
5. **Runner** — A **completely enclosed, fully-filled** wheel with curved, twisted blades. Water enters radially near the outer periphery and exits axially downward through the draft tube. Materials: cast steel, stainless steel, or bronze.
6. **Runner Blades** — Curved inward (or sometimes outward in older designs). Modern Francis runners have **13 to 21 blades**, often machined as a single integrated casting. The blade profile is designed using velocity-triangle analysis.
7. **Draft Tube** — A **gradually expanding pipe** (cone, elbow, or elbow-with-diffuser type) connecting the runner outlet to the tailrace. Its functions are:
   - **Converts the high kinetic energy at the runner exit into pressure energy** (recovery of *kinetic head*).
   - Creates a **suction head** at the runner outlet, allowing the runner to be placed **above the tailrace** without losing net head.
   - Achieves a **pressure below atmospheric** at the runner exit (sub-atmospheric), increasing the effective head on the turbine.
8. **Main Shaft & Bearings** — Connects runner to generator.
9. **Governing Mechanism** — Controls the simultaneous angular movement of all guide vanes via a **regulating ring** (also called a *wearing ring* assembly) connected through links, levers, and an oil-pressure servo motor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Draft Tube Sub-Types (frequently asked in KTU 2-mark questions):**
> - Type 1: **Conical draft tube** (most efficient, simplest)
> - Type 2: **Elbow-type draft tube** (used for horizontal shaft)
> - Type 3: **Moody's diffusion (elbow + diffuser)** (modern, compact)
> - Type 4: **Hydraulic / Circular draft tube** (for very low specific speed)

## 2.3 Kaplan Turbine — Constructional Features

The **Kaplan turbine** is an **axial-flow reaction turbine** with **adjustable runner blades** (in addition to adjustable guide vanes). It operates under **low head** (typically H < 30 m) and **high discharge**.

### Main Components:

1. **Spiral Casing** — Similar to Francis, but typically smaller and made of concrete.
2. **Guide Vanes / Stay Vanes / Wicket Gates** — Same function as Francis, but often more numerous (typically 16–24).
3. **Hub / Boss** — The central rotating element of the runner, made of high-strength cast steel. **Blade roots** are inserted into machined slots in the hub.
4. **Runner Blades** — **4 to 8 twisted, aerofoil-shaped blades** mounted on the hub via **oil-pressure operated servomotors inside the hub**. The blades can be rotated about their own axes during operation — this is called **double regulation** (guide vane + runner blade both adjustable).
5. **Hub Servomotor** — Located inside the runner hub itself; uses pressurized oil from the governor to rotate the blades. The runner is filled with oil for this purpose.
6. **Draft Tube** — Usually a **straight conical or slightly bent draft tube**.
7. **Main Shaft** — Very short vertical shaft connecting the runner hub to the generator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Double Regulation Concept:**
> Kaplan turbine has **two independent governing mechanisms**:
> 1. Guide vane adjustment (controls flow rate Q)
> 2. Runner blade pitch adjustment (controls flow angle and maintains high efficiency across varying loads)
> This makes Kaplan the **most efficient low-head turbine** — flat efficiency curve from 30% to 100% load.

## 2.4 Centrifugal Pump — Constructional Features

The **centrifugal pump** is the **most widely used pump** in industry. It is a **rotodynamic, radial-flow pump** in which fluid enters axially at the eye of the impeller and is flung outward radially by centrifugal force.

### Main Components:

1. **Suction Pipe / Suction Hose** — Connects the sump / well to the pump inlet. Should be short, straight, and airtight.
2. **Foot Valve / Strainer** — A **one-way (non-return) valve** at the bottom of the suction pipe. It:
   - Prevents water from draining back into the sump when the pump stops (keeps pump primed).
   - Contains a **strainer** to filter debris and prevent clogging.
3. **Suction Strainer / Foot Strainer** — Fine mesh that blocks leaves, fish, and stones.
4. **Impeller** — The rotating heart of the pump. Three main types:
   - **Closed impeller** — Has shrouds (front and back) — high efficiency, used for clean water.
   - **Semi-open impeller** — Only back shroud — used for sewage / slurry.
   - **Open impeller** — No shrouds — used for viscous / dirty fluids.
5. **Volute Casing / Volute** — A **spiral-shaped casing** with progressively increasing cross-sectional area. It collects water flung outward by the impeller and **converts kinetic energy into pressure energy** (pressure recovery). The cross-sectional area increases to maintain **constant velocity** of water around the spiral (since the radius is also increasing).
6. **Diffuser (optional)** — Stationary guide vanes surrounding the impeller that perform the same kinetic-to-pressure conversion as the volute. Used in multi-stage pumps.
7. **Casing Wear Ring / Wearing Ring** — Replaceable rings fitted on the impeller and casing at the clearance gap, to maintain a small clearance and minimize recirculation losses.
8. **Stuffing Box / Mechanical Seal** — A sealing arrangement at the point where the shaft exits the casing. Prevents leakage along the shaft and restricts air ingress on the suction side.
9. **Gland Packing** — Soft packing material (graphite-impregnated asbestos / PTFE) wound around the shaft inside the stuffing box.
10. **Shaft** — Transmits torque from motor to impeller.
11. **Bearings** — Support the shaft. Typically ball/roller bearings.
12. **Delivery Pipe / Discharge Pipe** — Carries pressurized water from the volute to the delivery tank.
13. **Delivery Valve** — Throttle / non-return valve on the delivery side.
14. **Air Vessel / Air Cushion** — A pressurized air chamber on the delivery pipe that smoothens pulsations (more critical in reciprocating pumps, but also used in some centrifugal systems).
15. **Priming** — Centrifugal pumps **cannot suck air** — they must be filled with water before starting. This is called **priming**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Priming — the most-asked KTU constructional concept for pumps:**
> A centrifugal pump does not develop suction if air is inside the casing (air is too light to create enough pressure difference). Therefore, before starting, the casing must be completely filled with water — this process is **priming**. Methods include:
> - Manual priming via a funnel and priming cock
> - Vacuum pump priming
> - Self-priming pump designs (built-in water reservoir)

## 2.5 Reciprocating Pump — Constructional Features

The **reciprocating pump** is a **positive-displacement pump** in which a piston (or plunger) moves back and forth inside a cylinder, drawing fluid in during the suction stroke and pushing it out during the delivery stroke.

### Main Components:

1. **Cylinder** — A precision-bored hollow cylinder (typically cast iron with honed inner surface) where the piston reciprocates.
2. **Piston / Plunger** — A tightly-fitted sliding piece that moves inside the cylinder. Piston uses piston rings; plunger is a solid rod (used for high pressure).
3. **Suction Valve / Inlet Valve** — A **one-way valve** at the inlet of the cylinder. Opens during the suction stroke, closes during the delivery stroke.
4. **Delivery Valve / Outlet Valve** — A **one-way valve** at the outlet. Closes during the suction stroke, opens during the delivery stroke.
5. **Suction Pipe** — Connects the sump to the suction valve.
6. **Delivery Pipe** — Connects the delivery valve to the delivery tank.
7. **Crank, Connecting Rod (Crank-Rod), and Piston Rod** — Convert the rotary motion of the drive shaft (motor/engine) into the reciprocating motion of the piston. Crank-pin offset = half the stroke length.
8. **Crankcase** — Houses the crank and connecting rod.
9. **Air Vessel (on suction pipe)** — A closed chamber filled with air at atmospheric pressure. Smoothens suction-side pulsations and prevents separation / column separation in the suction pipe.
10. **Air Vessel (on delivery pipe)** — A closed chamber filled with air at a pressure equal to the mean delivery pressure. Smoothens delivery-side pulsations, provides nearly constant flow, reduces water hammer.
11. **Foot Valve** — Non-return valve at the bottom of suction pipe to retain priming water.
12. **Strainer** — At the foot valve to filter debris.
13. **By-pass / Pressure Relief Valve** — A safety valve on the delivery side that opens if pressure exceeds a set limit (reciprocating pumps can develop dangerously high pressures if the delivery is blocked).
14. **Stuffing Box** — Sealing arrangement around the piston rod where it exits the cylinder.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Constructional-Focused)

> [!NOTE]
> The formulas below are the **engineer's design equations** that govern the constructional sizing decisions. They are not derivations of construction itself, but they constrain *why* certain components are built a certain size.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Application | Units |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | $V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$ | Jet velocity from a nozzle (Pelton) | m/s |
| 2 | $V = \pi D N / 60$ | Peripheral velocity of runner / impeller | m/s |
| 3 | $u_1 = \pi D_1 N / 60$ | Inlet peripheral velocity (Pelton, Francis) | m/s |
| 4 | $u_2 = \pi D_2 N / 60$ | Outlet peripheral velocity (Francis) | m/s |
| 5 | $N_s = N \sqrt{P} / H^{5/4}$ | Specific speed of turbine | rpm, metric |
| 6 | $N_s = N \sqrt{Q} / H^{3/4}$ | Specific speed of pump (metric) | rpm |
| 7 | $Q = A V_1 = \pi d_1^{2} / 4 \cdot V_1$ | Discharge through nozzle (Pelton) | m³/s |
| 8 | $P = \rho g Q H$ | Hydraulic power | W (Watts) |
| 9 | $H_a = H_{atm} - H_{vapor} - NPSH$ | Maximum allowable suction lift (pump) | m |
| 10 | $\eta_{manometric} = \frac{V_w_1 \cdot u_1}{gH}$ | Euler turbine equation (Francis inlet) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\eta_{manometric} = \frac{V_{w1}u_1 \pm V_{w2}u_2}{gH}$ | Euler equation general (radial machines) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $V_{thr} = \sqrt{2gH_a}$ | Theoretical jet velocity with head loss | m/s |
| 13 | $h_f = 4 f L V^{2} / (2 g D)$ | Frictional head loss (Darcy) | m |
| 14 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_h \cdot \eta_m \cdot \eta_v$ | Pump efficiency breakdown | dimensionless |

**Constraint / Boundary Conditions commonly applied during construction:**
- For Pelton: $u_1 \approx 0.46 \, V_1$ (optimum speed coefficient, Kc)
- For Francis: $u_1 \approx 0.45 \sqrt{2gH}$ to $0.65 \sqrt{2gH}$
- For Kaplan: $u \approx 0.6 \sqrt{2gH}$ to $0.9 \sqrt{2gH}$
- For Centrifugal pump: $u_2 \approx 0.95 \sqrt{2gH}$ to $1.10 \sqrt{2gH}$ (impeller tip speed)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Why These Specific Dimensions? — Sizing Logic

This section mathematically justifies the **constructional sizes** of major components — i.e., how an engineer arrives at the diameter of a Pelton wheel, the blade angles of a Francis runner, or the throat diameter of a nozzle.

### 3.1.1 Pelton Wheel Bucket Diameter ($D$) — Step-by-Step Derivation

The Pelton wheel diameter $D$ is set such that the peripheral velocity at the pitch circle equals the **optimum speed coefficient** times the jet velocity.

**Step 1 — Define peripheral velocity:**

$$u = \frac{\pi D N}{60}$$

**Step 2 — Define optimum speed coefficient $K_c$:**

For a Pelton wheel, maximum efficiency occurs when:

$$K_c = \frac{u}{V_1} \approx 0.46$$

(Empirically determined from bucket geometry and 170°–180° reversal.)

**Step 3 — Apply the condition:**

$$\frac{u}{V_1} = 0.46 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; u = 0.46 \, V_1$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $D$:**

$$D = \frac{60 \, u}{\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 0.46 \, V_1}{\pi N}$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of head $H$:**

Since $V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$ and assuming $C_v \approx 0.97$ to $0.99$:

$$D = \frac{60 \times 0.46 \times C_v \sqrt{2gH}}{\pi N}$$

**Step 6 — Use specific-speed substitution:**

For a single-jet Pelton, $N_s$ (metric) $\approx 10$ to $35$ for a single jet. The number of jets $z$ relates:

$$N_s^{\text{total}} = N_s^{\text{single}} \cdot \sqrt{z}$$

For a given head $H$ and required power $P$, the design diameter is uniquely fixed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Constructional takeaway:** A Pelton wheel for $H = 500$ m with $N = 500$ rpm will have a much smaller $D$ than for $H = 50$ m at the same $N$ — that's why Pelton runners for ultra-high-head plants are often as small as 1–2 m diameter.

### 3.1.2 Francis Runner Diameter — Outlet Diameter $D_2$

For Francis turbine, the outlet diameter $D_2$ sets the tip speed $u_2$ that controls the **exit velocity triangle**.

**Step 1 — Define flow velocity component at exit:**

From Euler's equation, for maximum efficiency the **whirl at exit $V_{w2} \approx 0$** (radial exit flow).

**Step 2 — Apply continuity at outlet:**

$$Q = \pi D_2 B_2 \, V_{f2}$$

Where $B_2$ is the outlet blade height and $V_{f2}$ is the flow velocity at exit.

**Step 3 — Express $u_2$:**

$$u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $D_2$:**

$$D_2 = \frac{60 \, u_2}{\pi N}$$

**Step 5 — Choose $V_{f2}$** based on the type of draft tube (typically 1–3 m/s to avoid draft-tube separation):

$$D_2 = \sqrt{\frac{4 Q}{\pi \, V_{f2} \, \frac{B_2}{D_2}}}$$

The blade-height-to-diameter ratio $\dfrac{B_2}{D_2}$ is governed by specific speed: low $N_s$ → small ratio (slender blades); high $N_s$ → large ratio (bulky blades).

### 3.1.3 Centrifugal Pump Impeller Outlet Diameter — Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply Euler's equation to the impeller outlet:**

For a backward-curved vane (most common):

$$H_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{V_{w2} \, u_2}{g}$$

**Step 2 — Express $u_2$:**

$$u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$$

**Step 3 — Express $V_{w2}$ using velocity triangle:**

If the blade makes angle $\beta_2$ with the tangent at exit:

$$V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{f2} \cot \beta_2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$H_{\text{th}} = \frac{u_2 \, (u_2 - V_{f2} \cot \beta_2)}{g} = \frac{u_2^{2}}{g} - \frac{u_2 V_{f2} \cot \beta_2}{g}$$

**Step 5 — Apply continuity for $V_{f2}$:**

$$V_{f2} = \frac{Q}{\pi D_2 B_2}$$

**Step 6 — Combine and solve for $D_2$:**

$$H_{\text{th}} = \frac{1}{g}\left(\frac{\pi D_2 N}{60}\right)^{2} - \frac{1}{g}\left(\frac{\pi D_2 N}{60}\right) \cdot \frac{Q}{\pi D_2 B_2} \cdot \cot \beta_2$$

This is a transcendental equation in $D_2$ and is normally solved **iteratively** for design purposes.

### 3.1.4 Analytical Matrix — Machine-vs-Component Cross-Check

| Component | Pelton | Francis | Kaplan | Centrifugal Pump | Reciprocating Pump |
|-----------|--------|---------|--------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Fluid Entry Zone** | Nozzle + Needle | Spiral Casing + Guide Vanes | Spiral Casing + Guide Vanes | Suction Pipe + Foot Valve | Suction Pipe + Foot Valve + Suction Valve |
| **Energy-Exchange Element** | Runner with buckets | Runner with curved blades | Runner with adjustable blades | Impeller (backward-curved vanes) | Piston / Plunger in Cylinder |
| **Fluid Exit Zone** | Tailrace (open) | Draft Tube | Draft Tube (straight conical) | Volute Casing + Delivery Pipe | Delivery Valve + Delivery Pipe + Air Vessel |
| **Casing Type** | Open housing (atmospheric) | Fully sealed, pressure vessel | Fully sealed, pressure vessel | Volute (pressure vessel) | Cylinder + Crankcase |
| **Speed Range** | High (300–750 rpm) | Medium (75–600 rpm) | Low (70–500 rpm) | 1450–3500 rpm | 60–500 rpm |
| **Pressure at Runner / Cylinder** | Atmospheric | Sub-atmospheric (draft tube) to higher | Sub-atmospheric to higher | Above atmospheric throughout | Above atmospheric throughout |
| **Cavitation Risk** | Nil | High (draft tube) | High (draft tube) | High (suction side) | Moderate |
| **Governing Method** | Needle valve stroke | Guide vane angle | Guide vane + blade pitch | Throttle valve / VFD | Stroke length / by-pass |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The choice of casing type (open vs. sealed) and the presence/absence of a draft tube are dictated entirely by the **impulse vs. reaction classification**:
> - Impulse machines → atmospheric at runner → no draft tube → open casing.
> - Reaction machines → pressurized at runner → need sealed casing + draft tube.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pelton Wheel — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Reservoir / Forebay] --> B1[Penstock<br/>Steel Pipe<br/>High-Pressure Conduit]
    B1 --> C1[Spiral Casing<br/>NOT PRESENT in Pelton]
    B1 --> D1[Needle Valve<br/>Spear Type<br/>Flow Control]
    D1 --> E1[Nozzle<br/>Convergent<br/>Converts P-Energy to K-Energy]
    E1 --> F1[Deflector<br/>Emergency Jet Diverter]
    E1 --> G1[Pelton Runner<br/>Buckets on Disc<br/>170 deg Reversal]
    G1 --> H1[Tailrace<br/>Open Atmosphere Discharge]
    G1 --> I1[Shaft Coupling]
    I1 --> J1[Generator<br/>Electrical Output]
    G1 --> K1[Brake Jet<br/>Auxiliary Nozzle]
    G1 --> L1[Governing Servomotor<br/>Oil Pressure Unit]
    L1 --> D1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice that in a Pelton wheel there is **no spiral casing** and **no draft tube**. The fluid enters through a single nozzle, does its work on the runner in atmospheric conditions, and falls directly into the tailrace. This is the **constructional fingerprint** of an impulse machine.

## 4.2 Francis Turbine — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[Reservoir] --> B2[Penstock]
    B2 --> C2[Spiral Casing<br/>Gradually Decreasing<br/>Cross Section]
    C2 --> D2[Stay Ring<br/>Structural Casing]
    D2 --> E2[Guide Vanes<br/>Adjustable<br/>16 to 24 vanes]
    E2 --> F2[Francis Runner<br/>Mixed Flow<br/>Radial Inlet Axial Outlet]
    F2 --> G2[Draft Tube<br/>Conical Elbow<br/>or Moody Type]
    G2 --> H2[Tailrace]
    F2 --> I2[Main Shaft]
    I2 --> J2[Generator]
    E2 --> K2[Regulating Ring<br/>Synchronises Vanes]
    K2 --> L2[Governor]
    L2 --> M2[Servo Motor]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Compare this with the Pelton diagram — notice the **spiral casing**, **guide vanes**, and **draft tube**. The runner operates submerged in flowing water (not air). This is the constructional fingerprint of a **reaction machine**.

## 4.3 Kaplan Turbine — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A3[Reservoir] --> B3[Penstock]
    B3 --> C3[Spiral Casing<br/>Smaller Concrete Casing]
    C3 --> D3[Guide Vanes<br/>Adjustable Wicket Gates]
    D3 --> E3[Runner Hub<br/>Contains Servomotor]
    E3 --> F3[Runner Blades<br/>4 to 8 Twisted<br/>Adjustable Pitch]
    F3 --> G3[Draft Tube<br/>Straight Conical]
    G3 --> H3[Tailrace]
    E3 --> I3[Hub Servomotor<br/>Oil Pressure Driven]
    I3 --> F3
    D3 --> J3[Guide Vane Linkage]
    J3 --> K3[Governor]
    K3 --> I3
    F3 --> L3[Main Shaft]
    L3 --> M3[Generator]
```

## 4.4 Centrifugal Pump — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A4[Sump / Well] --> B4[Foot Valve<br/>Non Return]
    B4 --> C4[Strainer<br/>Mesh Filter]
    C4 --> D4[Suction Pipe<br/>Short and Straight]
    D4 --> E4[Impeller Eye<br/>Axial Inlet]
    E4 --> F4[Impeller<br/>Closed Semi Open or Open<br/>Backward Curved Vanes]
    F4 --> G4[Volute Casing<br/>Spiral Passage<br/>Increases Pressure]
    G4 --> H4[Delivery Pipe]
    H4 --> I4[Delivery Valve]
    I4 --> J4[Delivery Tank]
    F4 --> K4[Main Shaft]
    K4 --> L4[Motor<br/>Electric / Diesel]
    F4 --> M4[Stuffing Box<br/>Mechanical Seal]
    M4 --> K4
    A4 --> N4[Priming Water<br/>Manual or Vacuum]
```

## 4.5 Reciprocating Pump — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A5[Sump] --> B5[Foot Valve<br/>Non Return]
    B5 --> C5[Strainer]
    C5 --> D5[Suction Pipe]
    D5 --> E5[Suction Air Vessel<br/>Air Cushion]
    E5 --> F5[Suction Valve<br/>One Way]
    F5 --> G5[Cylinder<br/>Precision Bored]
    G5 --> H5[Piston / Plunger<br/>Reciprocating]
    H5 --> G5
    G5 --> I5[Delivery Valve<br/>One Way]
    I5 --> J5[Delivery Air Vessel<br/>Smoothens Pulsation]
    J5 --> K5[Delivery Pipe]
    K5 --> L5[Delivery Tank]
    H5 --> M5[Piston Rod]
    M5 --> N5[Connecting Rod]
    N5 --> O5[Crank]
    O5 --> P5[Drive Shaft]
    P5 --> Q5[Motor]
    K5 --> R5[By Pass Relief Valve<br/>Pressure Safety]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Insight for KTU Diagrams:**
> - **Turbines** flow: top → bottom (water falls down).
> - **Pumps** flow: bottom → top (water lifted up).
> - In **reciprocating pumps**, notice the **two air vessels** (one on each pipe) — these are the constructional reason for **smoother flow** than centrifugal pumps.
> - In **Kaplan**, notice the **hub servomotor** inside the runner — a unique constructional feature among all turbines.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]

**Explain the constructional features of a Pelton wheel runner with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer:**

The Pelton wheel runner consists of a **circular steel disc** mounted on a horizontal shaft. The disc has a number of **doubly-cupped (hemispherical) buckets** bolted or cast around its periphery. Each bucket has:
- Two **symmetrical hemispherical cups** to split the incoming jet into two equal halves.
- A **sharp central ridge (splitter)** that divides the jet cleanly.
- A **central cut-out / notch** that allows the bucket to pass through the jet without obstruction.

The bucket angle is about **165° to 170°** (sometimes up to 180°) for efficient reversal of the jet. Buckets are made of **stainless steel or bronze** for high wear resistance. The jet strikes the splitter and reverses nearly 180°, transferring maximum momentum to the runner.

```
   Jet →
      |  ← Incoming jet (V₁)
      V
    [BUCKET]   Split into two halves,
    /      \  → reversed ~170° → exits
   |   |   |
   |   ←   |
    \      /
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often *forget to draw the central notch/cut-out* on the bucket — losing 1 mark. Always show the **cut-out** between the two cups.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]

**List any six constructional features of a centrifugal pump and explain the need for priming.**

**Model Answer:**

**Six key constructional features of a centrifugal pump:**

1. **Impeller** — Rotating element with curved vanes (backward-curved most common).
2. **Volute casing** — Spiral-shaped casing with increasing cross-sectional area.
3. **Suction pipe with foot valve and strainer** — Non-return valve at the bottom of the sump connection.
4. **Delivery pipe with delivery valve** — Carries pressurized water to the tank.
5. **Shaft, bearings, and stuffing box** — Mechanical seal arrangement at the shaft-casing junction.
6. **Casing / wearing rings** — Replaceable rings for maintaining tight clearance.

**Need for Priming:**

A centrifugal pump cannot develop suction when filled with air because:
- Air has **very low density** (≈ 1.2 kg/m³) compared to water (1000 kg/m³).
- The pressure difference generated is too small to lift water from the sump.
- The pump must be **filled with water before starting** — this process is called **priming**.
- Without priming, the pump will just churn the air and fail to deliver water.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students answer "priming is needed to remove air bubbles" without explaining **why** (low density of air). Always state **the physical reason** for full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **constructional features of a Francis turbine**. List the main components and state the function of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is a **draft tube**? Explain its **types and functions** with diagrams. Why is it **not used** in Pelton wheels? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Part (a):

A Francis turbine is a **medium-head, mixed-flow reaction turbine**. Its main constructional features are:

| S.No | Component | Function |
|------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Spiral Casing** | Distributes water **uniformly and tangentially** around the entire periphery of the runner; gradually decreasing cross-section maintains constant velocity at the guide vane entry. |
| 2 | **Stay Ring / Speed Ring** | Stationary structural ring to which the spiral casing is bolted; holds the guide vane pivots. |
| 3 | **Guide Vanes / Wicket Gates** | Adjustable aerofoil vanes that **regulate the flow rate** entering the runner and the direction of flow (governing). |
| 4 | **Regulating Ring** | Synchronously rotates all guide vanes via linkages. |
| 5 | **Francis Runner** | Curved, twisted blades mounted inside a sealed casing. Water enters **radially near the outer periphery** and exits **axially downward**. |
| 6 | **Runner Blades** | 13 to 21 inward-curved blades; convert pressure + kinetic energy of water into mechanical torque. |
| 7 | **Draft Tube** | Gradually expanding pipe from runner outlet to tailrace; recovers kinetic energy and creates suction head. |
| 8 | **Main Shaft & Bearings** | Transmits mechanical power from runner to generator. |
| 9 | **Governing Mechanism** | Servomotor + regulating ring to control guide vane angle. |

#### Solution to Part (b):

**Draft Tube Definition:** A gradually expanding pipe connecting the runner outlet to the tailrace, designed to **recover kinetic energy** from water exiting the runner and to **create a suction head** allowing the runner to be placed above tailrace level.

**Types of Draft Tubes:**

1. **Conical Draft Tube** — Simple conical frustum. Length = 3 to 4 × outlet diameter $D_2$. Best efficiency. Used for vertical-axis machines.

2. **Elbow-type Draft Tube** — Combines a conical section, a 90° bend, and a horizontal outlet. Used for **horizontal-axis** reaction turbines.

3. **Moody's Diffusion Tube (Elbow with diffuser)** — Elbow + diverging horizontal section. Compact and very efficient. Used in modern installations.

4. **Hydraulic / Circular Draft Tube** — Diverging pipe with a central conical pier. Used for very low specific speeds.

**Functions of Draft Tube:**

1. **Recovers kinetic head** at runner exit → converts it to pressure head.
2. Creates **suction head (vacuum)** at runner outlet → increases net effective head on the turbine.
3. Allows the runner to be installed **above tailrace level** (practical civil engineering convenience).
4. Acts as a **diffuser** — slows down water gradually without separation.

**Why not used in Pelton Wheel:**

In a Pelton wheel, water exits the bucket at **atmospheric pressure** (impulse machine). The bucket is **not submerged** in water. There is **no kinetic energy to recover** at the exit because the jet was completely decelerated by the bucket. Therefore a draft tube would serve no purpose and would actually impede the free fall of water to the tailrace.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Most common marks lost:**
> 1. Forgetting to mention the **stay ring / regulating ring** in the Francis constructional sketch.
> 2. Drawing the draft tube as a straight pipe (must show **gradually increasing** cross-section).
> 3. Saying "draft tube is not used in Pelton because it is an impulse machine" — too vague. State explicitly: *"because the runner operates at atmospheric pressure and there is no kinetic head to recover."*

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2023 / 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the **constructional features of a single-acting reciprocating pump** with a neat labelled sketch. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What are **air vessels**? Why are they provided on the **suction and delivery pipes** of a reciprocating pump? Explain how they **smoothen flow**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Part (a):

A single-acting reciprocating pump has the following constructional features:

1. **Cylinder** — A precision-bored hollow cylinder (cast iron) where the piston reciprocates.
2. **Piston (with piston rings)** — Tightly fitted sliding piece sealing the cylinder bore.
3. **Suction Valve** — One-way valve at the cylinder inlet. **Opens on suction stroke**, closes on delivery stroke.
4. **Delivery Valve** — One-way valve at the cylinder outlet. **Closes on suction stroke**, opens on delivery stroke.
5. **Suction Pipe** — Connects sump → suction valve. Has a foot valve at the sump end.
6. **Delivery Pipe** — Connects delivery valve → delivery tank.
7. **Crank and Connecting Rod** — Convert rotary motion of motor into reciprocating motion of piston. Crank radius = L/2 (stroke).
8. **Crankcase** — Houses the crank and connecting rod.
9. **Stuffing Box** — Sealing arrangement around the piston rod at the cylinder head.
10. **Foot Valve and Strainer** — Non-return valve + filter at the bottom of suction pipe.

**Working:**

- **Suction Stroke:** Piston moves backward → cylinder volume increases → suction pressure drops below atmospheric → suction valve opens → water rushes in.
- **Delivery Stroke:** Piston moves forward → cylinder volume decreases → water pressure rises → suction valve closes → delivery valve opens → water pushed into delivery pipe.

#### Solution to Part (b):

**Air Vessels** are **closed chambers** partially filled with air, installed on the suction and delivery pipes of a reciprocating pump.

**Why Air Vessels are Needed:**

Without air vessels, the flow in a reciprocating pump is **pulsating** — water moves only during the delivery stroke (and only during the suction stroke in the suction pipe). This causes:
- **Vibrations** and noise.
- **Water hammer** during valve closure.
- **Column separation** in long suction pipes.
- Higher energy losses.

**How Air Vessels Smoothen Flow:**

- On the **delivery pipe**, the air vessel is charged with air at the **mean delivery pressure**. During the delivery stroke, when discharge > average, excess water enters the air vessel, compressing the air. When the piston retracts (zero delivery moment), the compressed air pushes water out into the delivery pipe. → Net result: nearly **continuous flow** in the delivery pipe.

- On the **suction pipe**, the air vessel is at **atmospheric pressure**. During the suction stroke, water rushes in from the sump; the air vessel discharges additional water to maintain flow. During the return stroke, water refills the air vessel. → Net result: nearly **continuous flow** in the suction pipe.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Most common marks lost:**
> 1. Confusing the **single-acting** and **double-acting** constructional diagrams. In single-acting, there is **one suction + one delivery valve per cylinder** and delivery happens only on one stroke.
> 2. Failing to mention **foot valve and strainer** in the suction pipe.
> 3. Stating "air vessels remove air" — actually they **store compressed air** that pushes water out. Do not confuse the function.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's General Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Universal Pitfalls across all constructional questions:**
> 1. **Unlabelled sketches** — every line in the diagram should have at least 6–8 labels with **leader lines**.
> 2. **Confusing impulse and reaction** constructional features (e.g., showing a draft tube in a Pelton wheel).
> 3. **Forgetting materials of construction** — Pelton buckets = stainless steel; cylinder = cast iron; runner = cast steel.
> 4. **Not stating why** a component exists. Always pair each component with its **function** (e.g., "Stuffing box — to prevent leakage along the shaft").
> 5. **Missing the cut-out in the Pelton bucket** in the sketch — guaranteed mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Recall Bullet Checklist

- **Pelton Wheel** = Impulse turbine → high head, low discharge → **no draft tube**, **no spiral casing**, **atmospheric at runner**, **bucket has cut-out**, **needle valve governs**.
- **Francis Turbine** = Mixed-flow reaction → medium head → **spiral casing + guide vanes + draft tube**, **governed by guide vane angle**.
- **Kaplan Turbine** = Axial reaction → low head, high discharge → **adjustable runner blades**, **hub servomotor**, **double regulation**.
- **Centrifugal Pump** = Rotodynamic radial → **volute casing**, **impeller**, **needs priming**, **backward-curved vanes** most common, **no air vessel typically needed**.
- **Reciprocating Pump** = Positive displacement → **piston in cylinder**, **suction + delivery valves (one-way)**, **air vessels smoothen pulsation**, **no priming issue** (self-priming).

### Numerical Facts to Memorize

- Pelton optimum speed coefficient: $K_c = u / V_1 = 0.46$
- Pelton bucket angle: 165°–180°
- Francis blade count: 13–21
- Kaplan blade count: 4–8 (adjustable)
- Pelton is governed by: **needle stroke**
- Francis / Kaplan are governed by: **guide vane angle**
- Kaplan additionally has: **runner blade pitch adjustment**

### Memory Trick — "PSFG" for Pump Sequencing

- **P** = Pump inlet (suction side) → Foot valve → Strainer → **Pump body** → **G**lands/stuffing box → Outlet

### Memory Trick — "NDRGS" for Turbine Sequencing (Pelton)

- **N** = Nozzle → **D** = Deflector → **R** = Runner → **G** = Generator → **S** = Shaft to grid

### Memory Trick — "SSDRG" for Francis/Kaplan

- **S** = Spiral casing → **S** = Stay ring → **D** = Draft tube → **R** = Runner → **G** = Generator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute KTU Revision Strategy for Module 3 Constructional Features:**
> 1. Practice drawing **all 5 machine diagrams** from memory (5 minutes each).
> 2. For each diagram, write down **component name + function + material of construction**.
> 3. Revise the formula sheet table above once.
> 4. Read the two air-vessel sections carefully — they appear in **at least one question per KTU exam cycle**.
> 5. Memorize "why no draft tube in Pelton" and "why priming is needed in centrifugal pump" — these two questions alone account for ~6 marks every exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Parameters and Practical Problems on Pelton
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design Parameters and Practical Problems on Pelton

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Pelton Wheel** is a **tangential-flow impulse turbine** in which one or more jets of water (produced by a nozzle) impinge tangentially on a series of hemispherical or double-cup shaped **buckets** mounted on the periphery of a runner. The kinetic energy of the jet is converted into rotational mechanical energy, which is then transmitted to a generator or load through a shaft.

**Design Parameters** are the standardized geometric and kinematic ratios (such as jet-to-wheel diameter ratio, bucket width ratio, bucket count, and speed coefficient) that govern the optimal physical dimensions of a Pelton runner for a given head, discharge, and power output. These parameters ensure maximum hydraulic efficiency, smooth power transmission, and minimal cavitation/erosion losses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A Pelton wheel is the preferred choice for **high-head (above 300 m)** and **low-to-medium discharge** hydroelectric installations. Typical applications in Kerala include the Idukki, Sabarigiri, and Idamalayar hydroelectric projects.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **lawn sprinkler** with curved cups (buckets) attached to its rotating arms. Instead of water spraying outward, a high-velocity water jet strikes these cups tangentially, pushing them sideways. As the cups move, the water bounces off the curved surface and exits in nearly the opposite direction. The "kick" imparted to each cup causes the entire wheel to spin — this is the **Newton's Third Law** principle behind the Pelton wheel.

Think of the design parameters as the **"recipe"** for these cups:
- How **big** should the wheel be? → Wheel diameter **D**
- How **thick** should the water jet be? → Jet diameter **d**
- How **wide** and **deep** should each cup be? → Bucket dimensions **B** and **t**
- How **many** cups are needed to catch every drop? → Number of buckets **Z**
- How **fast** should the wheel spin? → Speed ratio **K_u = u₁/V₁**

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:**
> - **Head range:** 300 m to 2000 m (Impulse class)
> - **Specific speed (N_s):** 10 to 60 (metric, single jet)
> - **Maximum hydraulic efficiency:** ≈ 90% (single wheel, well-designed)
> - **Standard bucket count (Z):** typically **15 to 22** for single-jet Pelton runners

### 1.3 Why Design Parameters Matter

In practice, the Pelton wheel is rarely designed from first principles for every project. Engineers rely on **standard empirical ratios** derived from decades of model testing and field experience. The goal of this topic is to master the procedure of taking a given **H, Q, P, N** and computing all geometric dimensions (**D, d, B, t, Z**) of the runner, plus auxiliary components (nozzle, spear, deflector, casing).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Primary Design Parameters of a Pelton Wheel

A complete design specification for a single-jet Pelton wheel requires the determination of the following quantities, grouped under three categories:

**A. Input (Given) Quantities:**
1. Net head **H** (m) at the nozzle entry
2. Discharge **Q** (m³/s) through the jet
3. Power output **P** (kW or W)
4. Rotational speed **N** (rpm)

**B. Runner Geometry (To be Designed):**
5. Mean wheel (bucket circle) diameter **D** (m)
6. Jet diameter at nozzle exit **d** (m)
7. Bucket width **B** (m)
8. Bucket depth (radial length) **L** (m)
9. Number of buckets **Z**
10. Bucket exit angle **β₂** (typically 10°–15°)

**C. Auxiliary Components (To be Designed):**
11. Nozzle tip diameter, spear stroke, and deflector geometry
12. Casing (scroll-case) dimensions and penstock diameter

### 2.2 Standard Design Ratios (Empirical & KTU-Recommended Values)

These ratios govern the sizing of all geometric quantities. They are derived from manufacturers' catalogues and IS / IEC standards for impulse turbines.

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Range | Typical Value Used in Problems |
|-----------|--------|----------------|---------------------------------|
| Jet ratio | $d / D$ | 1/10 to 1/20 | 1/12 to 1/15 |
| Bucket width ratio | $B / d$ | 3.0 to 6.0 | 4.0 to 5.0 |
| Bucket depth / radial length | $L / d$ | 2.0 to 3.0 | 2.5 to 2.7 |
| Number of buckets (single jet) | $Z$ | 15 to 22 | 15 + (D/2d) approx. |
| Speed ratio | $K_u = u_1 / V_1$ | 0.43 to 0.48 | 0.45 to 0.46 |
| Flow ratio | $K_f = Q / (D^2 \sqrt{gH})$ | 0.02 to 0.10 | — |
| Bucket exit angle | $\beta_2$ | 10° to 15° | 10° |
| Velocity coefficient (nozzle) | $C_v$ | 0.96 to 0.99 | 0.98 |
| Bucket friction coefficient | $K_r$ | 0.92 to 0.98 | 0.95 |
| Deflection angle of jet | $\theta$ | 165° to 170° | 165° |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The **speed ratio $K_u$** is the single most critical parameter. For a *theoretical* maximum efficiency of 100% (no friction), the optimum speed ratio is **0.5**. In practice, with friction losses inside the bucket, the **optimum reduces to 0.45–0.46**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates every formula required to solve KTU-endorsed Pelton-wheel numerical problems.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Jet velocity | $V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$ | $C_v \approx 0.98$ |
| 2 | Wheel peripheral velocity | $u_1 = K_u \sqrt{2gH}$ | $K_u \approx 0.45$ |
| 3 | Jet diameter | $d = \sqrt{\dfrac{4Q}{\pi V_1}}$ | From continuity of jet |
| 4 | Mean wheel diameter | $D = d / (d/D)$ | Use standard ratio |
| 5 | Specific speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | Metric, kW, m |
| 6 | Number of buckets (Z) | $Z \approx 15 + \dfrac{D}{2d}$ | Empirical formula |
| 7 | Bucket width | $B = (B/d) \cdot d$ | Use $B/d \approx 5$ |
| 8 | Bucket depth | $L = (L/d) \cdot d$ | Use $L/d \approx 2.6$ |
| 9 | Relative velocity at inlet | $V_{r1} = V_1 - u_1$ | Vector subtraction |
| 10 | Relative velocity at outlet | $V_{r2} = K_r \cdot V_{r1}$ | Friction factor |
| 11 | Whirl velocity at outlet | $V_{w2} = V_{r2}\cos\beta_2 - u_1$ | Outlet triangle |
| 12 | Work done per unit weight | $W = \dfrac{1}{g}(V_{w1} \pm V_{w2}) \cdot u_1$ | Euler equation |
| 13 | Hydraulic efficiency | $\eta_h = \dfrac{2u_1(V_1 - u_1)}{V_1^2}$ | Ideal, $\beta_2 = 0$ |
| 14 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_o = \dfrac{\text{Shaft power}}{\rho g Q H}$ | Includes mech. + elec. |
| 15 | Runner axial thrust | $F = \rho Q (V_1 - V_{w2})$ | For bearing design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These ratios are not arbitrary. They originate from a *single overriding requirement*: every particle of the jet must strike a bucket and not escape through the gap between two adjacent buckets. This is why **Z increases** with **D/d** — bigger wheels need more buckets to "fill" the periphery.

### 2.4 The Logic Behind the Choice of Speed Ratio

Starting from the Euler equation for an impulse turbine:
$$W = u_1 \cdot V_{w1} \pm u_1 \cdot V_{w2}$$

For a Pelton wheel, the jet impinges tangentially in the direction of wheel motion, hence the absolute whirl velocity at inlet is $V_{w1} = V_1$. At outlet, the water exits opposite to the direction of motion, so $V_{w2}$ carries a negative sign:
$$W = \dfrac{u_1 V_1}{g} - \dfrac{u_1 V_{w2}}{g} = \dfrac{u_1}{g}(V_1 - V_{w2})$$

The hydraulic efficiency becomes:
$$\eta_h = \dfrac{W}{\frac{V_1^2}{2g}} = \dfrac{2 u_1 (V_1 - V_{w2})}{V_1^2}$$

Maximising this with respect to $u_1$ (taking $\partial \eta_h / \partial u_1 = 0$) yields the **optimum speed ratio** $K_u = u_1/V_1 = 0.5$ (frictionless) and ≈ **0.46** with bucket friction.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Optimum Jet Velocity (Work Done per Unit Weight)

Consider a single jet striking a Pelton bucket. Using the linear momentum equation in the tangential direction:

Let $V_1$ = jet velocity, $u_1$ = bucket velocity, $V_{r1} = V_1 - u_1$ = relative velocity at inlet.

The jet, after deflection through the bucket by an angle $(180° - \beta_2)$, leaves with relative velocity $V_{r2} = K_r \cdot V_{r1}$.

**Step 1:** Initial momentum of jet (mass per second × velocity) in direction of $u_1$:
$$\dot{m} V_1 = \rho Q V_1$$

**Step 2:** Final momentum in the direction of $u_1$ (after jet exits with whirl component $V_{w2}$ opposite to bucket motion):
$$\dot{m} V_{w2} = \rho Q V_{w2} \quad (\text{taken positive in original direction})$$

**Step 3:** Force exerted by jet on bucket (Newton's second law):
$$F = \rho Q (V_1 - V_{w2})$$

**Step 4:** Work done per second (Power):
$$P = F \cdot u_1 = \rho Q u_1 (V_1 - V_{w2})$$

**Step 5:** Work done per unit weight of water striking:
$$W = \dfrac{P}{\rho g Q} = \dfrac{u_1(V_1 - V_{w2})}{g}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $V_{w2} = V_{r2}\cos\beta_2 - u_1$ and $V_{r2} = K_r(V_1 - u_1)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \dfrac{u_1}{g}\Big[V_1 - K_r(V_1 - u_1)\cos\beta_2 + u_1\Big] \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{u_1}{g}\Big[(V_1 + u_1) - K_r(V_1 - u_1)\cos\beta_2\Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7:** For maximum $W$, differentiate with respect to $u_1$ and set $\frac{dW}{du_1} = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dW}{du_1} &= \dfrac{1}{g}\Big[(V_1 + u_1) - K_r(V_1 - u_1)\cos\beta_2\Big] \\
&\quad + \dfrac{u_1}{g}\Big[1 + K_r\cos\beta_2\Big] = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8:** Solving for the optimum $u_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(V_1 + u_1) + u_1(1 + K_r\cos\beta_2) &= K_r(V_1 - u_1)\cos\beta_2 \\[3pt]
V_1 + 2u_1 + u_1 K_r\cos\beta_2 &= K_r V_1\cos\beta_2 - K_r u_1\cos\beta_2 \\[3pt]
2u_1 + 2u_1 K_r\cos\beta_2 &= K_r V_1\cos\beta_2 - V_1 \\[3pt]
2u_1(1 + K_r\cos\beta_2) &= V_1(K_r\cos\beta_2 - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9:** Final closed-form result — optimum $u_1$:

$$\boxed{u_{1,\text{opt}} = \dfrac{V_1(K_r\cos\beta_2 - 1)}{2(1 + K_r\cos\beta_2)}}$$

**Step 10:** For the *frictionless* ideal case ($K_r = 1$, $\beta_2 = 0$):
$$u_{1,\text{opt}} = \dfrac{V_1(1 - 1)}{2(1 + 1)} = 0 \quad \text{...apparent contradiction!}$$

*Resolution:* When $\beta_2 = 0$, $V_{w2} = -u_1$, and the standard $V_{w2} = -V_{r2} - u_1$ form must be used, which gives $u_{1,\text{opt}} = V_1/2$. Hence $K_u = 0.5$ for the ideal Pelton wheel.

### 3.2 Derivation: Expression for Optimum Number of Buckets

To avoid a water-free gap between consecutive buckets, the **arc spacing** between two adjacent bucket centres must be just smaller than the **jet circumference slice** that one bucket can capture.

**Step 1:** The arc length between bucket centres on the wheel circle is:
$$s = \dfrac{\pi D}{Z}$$

**Step 2:** For interception without leakage, this must not exceed the **arc width of the jet** (approximately the jet diameter $d$):
$$s \leq d \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{\pi D}{Z} \leq d$$

**Step 3:** Solving for the minimum number of buckets:
$$Z \geq \dfrac{\pi D}{d}$$

**Step 4:** A practical safety margin of 10–20% is added:
$$Z_{\min} \approx 1.15 \cdot \dfrac{\pi D}{d} \approx 3.6 \dfrac{D}{d}$$

**Step 5:** Empirical design formula widely used:
$$\boxed{Z = 15 + \dfrac{D}{2d}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification Example:** For $D = 1.2$ m and $d = 0.1$ m, this gives $Z = 15 + 6 = 21$ buckets — squarely within the KTU-recommended 15–22 range.

### 3.3 Derivation: Minimum Number of Buckets for Jet Interception (Alternate Approach)

A more rigorous approach uses the **kinematic constraint**: as the wheel rotates by the angle $\Delta\theta = 2\pi/Z$ between two successive bucket impacts, the jet cannot move past the second bucket.

**Step 1:** Time between two successive impacts:
$$\Delta t = \dfrac{60}{Z \cdot N} \quad \text{seconds}$$

**Step 2:** Distance travelled by jet (relative to bucket) in this time:
$$\Delta x = (V_1 - u_1) \cdot \Delta t = (V_1 - u_1) \cdot \dfrac{60}{Z N}$$

**Step 3:** This must be less than the **axial length of the jet intercepted by one bucket** (approximately equal to the jet diameter $d$):
$$(V_1 - u_1) \cdot \dfrac{60}{Z N} \leq d$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $Z$:
$$Z \geq \dfrac{60(V_1 - u_1)}{d \cdot N}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $u_1 = \pi D N / 60$ and simplifying:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &\geq \dfrac{60(V_1 - \pi D N/60)}{d \cdot N} \\[3pt]
&= \dfrac{60 V_1}{d N} - \dfrac{\pi D}{d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Final expression (taking $V_1/u_1 = 1/K_u$ from $u_1 = K_u V_1$):
$$Z_{\min} = \dfrac{60 V_1 (1 - K_u)}{d N} = \dfrac{\pi D (1 - K_u)}{K_u d}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall to Avoid:** Do NOT confuse the **minimum** bucket count (from the interference-free condition) with the **recommended** bucket count (from manufacturers' tables). Always state both in your design — first compute $Z_{\min}$, then choose a value 5–10% higher from the table.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Standard KTU Pelton Design Procedure

The following step-by-step procedure is the **canonical KTU 2024 solution template** for any Pelton design problem. Memorise this order.

| Step | Quantity | Formula / Source |
|------|----------|------------------|
| 1 | Jet velocity | $V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$, with $C_v = 0.98$ |
| 2 | Wheel velocity | $u_1 = K_u \sqrt{2gH}$, with $K_u = 0.45$ |
| 3 | Jet diameter | $d = \sqrt{4Q/(\pi V_1)}$ |
| 4 | Wheel diameter | $D = 15d$ (assuming $d/D = 1/15$) |
| 5 | Recompute $N$ from $D$ and $u_1$ | $N = 60 u_1/(\pi D)$ (check against given $N$) |
| 6 | Bucket width | $B = 5d$ |
| 7 | Bucket depth | $L = 2.6 d$ |
| 8 | Min. no. of buckets | $Z_{\min} = 15 + D/(2d)$ |
| 9 | Outlet triangle | $V_{r2} = K_r(V_1 - u_1)$, $V_{w2} = V_{r2}\cos\beta_2 - u_1$ |
| 10 | Hydraulic efficiency | $\eta_h = 2 u_1(V_1 - V_{w2})/V_1^2$ |
| 11 | Shaft power | $P = \rho g Q H \eta_o$ |
| 12 | Specific speed | $N_s = N\sqrt{P}/H^{5/4}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The matrix above is the backbone of the Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) design office methodology for small hydro (up to 25 MW) projects. Mastering it equips you with practical industry-relevant skills.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Pelton Wheel Design Flowchart

The following Mermaid block diagrams the complete KTU-recommended design workflow for a single-jet Pelton wheel. The flowchart captures the logical sequence from input parameters through to component-level deliverables.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given H, Q, P, N] --> B[Compute Jet Velocity V1 = Cv sqrt 2gH]
    B --> C[Compute Wheel Velocity u1 = Ku sqrt 2gH]
    C --> D[Compute Jet Diameter d from Q = pi d2 V1 by 4]
    D --> E[Select d to D Ratio - typically 1 by 15]
    E --> F[Compute Wheel Diameter D = 15 d]
    F --> G[Verify N = 60 u1 by pi D]
    G --> H{Speed N matches given?}
    H -- No --> I[Iterate d or Ku within limits]
    I --> G
    H -- Yes --> J[Design Bucket Width B = 5 d]
    J --> K[Design Bucket Depth L = 2 point 6 d]
    K --> L[Compute Min Bucket Count Zmin = 15 plus D by 2d]
    L --> M[Choose Z between Zmin and 22]
    M --> N[Design Bucket Exit Angle beta2 = 10 to 15 deg]
    N --> O[Compute Velocity Triangles Vr1 Vr2 Vw2]
    O --> P[Compute Hydraulic Efficiency eta h]
    P --> Q[Compute Shaft Power and Specific Speed Ns]
    Q --> R[End: Complete Pelton Design Report]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Pelton Wheel Assembly with Governing Mechanism

The second diagram shows the major assembly components of a typical KSEB-style Pelton installation. It emphasises the *functional interaction* between the hydraulic, mechanical, and electrical subsystems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reservoir Head H] --> B[Penstock Pipe]
    B --> C[Needle Spear Nozzle Assembly]
    C --> D[High Velocity Jet V1]
    D --> E[Pelton Runner with Z Buckets]
    E --> F[Main Shaft]
    F --> G[Governor Mechanism]
    G --> C
    G --> H[Deflector Plate]
    H --> D
    F --> I[Generator Coupling]
    I --> J[Electrical Load Grid]
    E --> K[Tailrace Casing Draft Tube]
    K --> L[Downstream River]
```

### 4.3 Velocity Triangle Schematic (Verbal Description)

Since complex physical drawings are difficult in Mermaid, the velocity triangle for a Pelton bucket is described as a **block-level functional architecture** below. The triangles shown are inlet and outlet velocity triangles for a single Pelton bucket, in the **relative frame** (moving with the bucket at velocity $u_1$).

| Component | Inlet Triangle | Outlet Triangle |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|
| Reference frame | Absolute / Relative (both) | Relative |
| Absolute velocity | $V_1$ (horizontal) | $V_2$ (small, tangential) |
| Bucket velocity | $u_1$ (horizontal) | $u_1$ (horizontal) |
| Relative velocity | $V_{r1} = V_1 - u_1$ | $V_{r2} = K_r V_{r1}$ |
| Angle | 0° with $u_1$ | $\beta_2$ = 10° to 15° |
| Whirl component | $V_{w1} = V_1$ | $V_{w2} = V_{r2}\cos\beta_2 - u_1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visualisation Aid for Students:** At the **inlet**, the triangle is *degenerate* (a straight line) because the jet strikes the bucket splitter edge in the tangential direction. The **outlet** triangle is small and slanted — the relative velocity $V_{r2}$ is nearly opposite to the bucket motion, indicating near-complete energy extraction.
