## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define the following terms with respect to Pelton wheel design: (i) Jet ratio, (ii) Speed ratio, (iii) Bucket exit angle. State the standard ranges used in practice.*

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Jet Ratio (d/D):** The ratio of jet diameter at the nozzle exit to the mean wheel (bucket circle) diameter. **Standard range:** $d/D = 1/10$ to $1/20$, typically **1/15**.

(ii) **Speed Ratio (K_u):** The ratio of wheel peripheral velocity at the bucket centre to the jet velocity. $K_u = u_1/V_1$. **Standard range:** **0.43 to 0.48**, with optimum at **0.45–0.46** for frictionless and real cases respectively.

(iii) **Bucket Exit Angle (β₂):** The angle made by the relative velocity at the bucket exit with the tangential direction. **Standard range:** **10° to 15°**, typically 10°. It determines the residual whirl velocity and hence the hydraulic efficiency.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*List any six major design parameters of a Pelton wheel runner. Why is the optimum speed ratio of a real Pelton wheel about 0.46 and not 0.5?*

**Model Answer:**

**Six design parameters:**
1. Mean wheel diameter **D**
2. Jet diameter **d**
3. Bucket width **B**
4. Bucket depth **L**
5. Number of buckets **Z**
6. Bucket exit angle **β₂**

**Why optimum $K_u \approx 0.46$ and not 0.5:**
The theoretical optimum $K_u = 0.5$ is derived assuming (a) *frictionless* flow through the bucket (i.e., $K_r = 1$, no energy loss), and (b) the bucket exit angle $\beta_2 = 0$ so that the relative velocity leaves axially. In practice:
- Friction inside the bucket reduces the relative velocity at exit ($K_r = 0.95$), reducing the achievable efficiency at higher $u_1$.
- The exit angle is non-zero ($\beta_2 = 10°$ to 15°), and the bucket splitter causes additional drag.
- These losses shift the optimum speed ratio to **0.45–0.46** to balance the competing effects of $u_1$ on kinetic energy capture and friction loss.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]**

A Pelton wheel is to be designed for the following specifications:
- Net head, $H = 350$ m
- Discharge, $Q = 0.40$ m³/s
- Speed, $N = 600$ rpm
- Overall efficiency, $\eta_o = 85\%$
- Take $C_v = 0.98$, $K_u = 0.46$, $d/D = 1/15$, $B/d = 5$, $L/d = 2.6$, $K_r = 0.95$, $\beta_2 = 10°$

**(a)** Determine the **jet diameter, mean wheel diameter, bucket dimensions, and number of buckets**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the **hydraulic efficiency and shaft power output**. Verify that the design satisfies the given efficiency. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Geometric Design:**

**Step 1:** Jet velocity
$$V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH} = 0.98 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 350}$$
$$V_1 = 0.98 \times \sqrt{6867} = 0.98 \times 82.87 = 81.21 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Wheel peripheral velocity
$$u_1 = K_u \sqrt{2gH} = 0.46 \times 82.87 = 38.12 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3:** Jet diameter from continuity
$$d = \sqrt{\dfrac{4Q}{\pi V_1}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 0.40}{\pi \times 81.21}} = \sqrt{0.00627} = 0.0792 \text{ m}$$
$$\boxed{d \approx 79.2 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 4:** Mean wheel diameter (using $d/D = 1/15$)
$$D = 15 d = 15 \times 0.0792 = 1.188 \text{ m}$$
$$\boxed{D \approx 1.19 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 5:** Verify speed
$$N_{\text{check}} = \dfrac{60 u_1}{\pi D} = \dfrac{60 \times 38.12}{\pi \times 1.188} = 612.6 \text{ rpm} \approx 600 \text{ rpm} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6:** Bucket dimensions
$$B = 5d = 5 \times 0.0792 = 0.396 \text{ m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{B = 396 \text{ mm}}$$
$$L = 2.6d = 2.6 \times 0.0792 = 0.206 \text{ m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{L = 206 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 7:** Minimum number of buckets
$$Z_{\min} = 15 + \dfrac{D}{2d} = 15 + \dfrac{1.188}{2 \times 0.0792} = 15 + 7.5 = 22.5$$
Adopt $\boxed{Z = 22}$ (practical standard for single-jet Pelton).

**Part (b) — Performance Verification:**

**Step 8:** Relative velocity at inlet
$$V_{r1} = V_1 - u_1 = 81.21 - 38.12 = 43.09 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 9:** Relative velocity at outlet (friction)
$$V_{r2} = K_r V_{r1} = 0.95 \times 43.09 = 40.94 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 10:** Whirl velocity at outlet
$$V_{w2} = V_{r2}\cos\beta_2 - u_1 = 40.94 \times \cos 10° - 38.12$$
$$V_{w2} = 40.94 \times 0.9848 - 38.12 = 40.32 - 38.12 = 2.20 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 11:** Hydraulic efficiency
$$\eta_h = \dfrac{2 u_1 (V_1 - V_{w2})}{V_1^2} = \dfrac{2 \times 38.12 \times (81.21 - 2.20)}{81.21^2}$$
$$\eta_h = \dfrac{76.24 \times 79.01}{6595.0} = \dfrac{6023.5}{6595.0} = 0.9134$$
$$\boxed{\eta_h \approx 91.3\%}$$

**Step 12:** Shaft power (overall efficiency)
$$P = \eta_o \cdot \rho g Q H = 0.85 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.40 \times 350$$
$$P = 0.85 \times 1{,}373{,}400 = 1{,}167{,}390 \text{ W} \approx 1167 \text{ kW}$$
$$\boxed{P \approx 1.17 \text{ MW}}$$

**Step 13:** Specific speed check
$$N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} = \dfrac{600 \times \sqrt{1167}}{350^{1.25}} = \dfrac{600 \times 34.16}{933.0} = 21.97$$
$$\boxed{N_s \approx 22 \text{ (metric)}}$$ — falls in the Pelton range of 10–60 ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In part (b), students frequently forget to **subtract $u_1$ from $V_{r2}\cos\beta_2$** when computing $V_{w2}$. The correct outlet whirl velocity is the **component along the tangential direction**, not the relative velocity itself. Also, note that $V_{w2}$ in the formula $V_{w2} = V_{r2}\cos\beta_2 - u_1$ carries the bucket-velocity term — the *sign convention* (opposite to wheel motion) is critical for a positive hydraulic efficiency.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]**

A Pelton wheel has the following design data:
- Mean wheel diameter, $D = 1.5$ m
- Jet diameter, $d = 0.12$ m
- Net head, $H = 400$ m
- Bucket exit angle, $\beta_2 = 10°$
- Velocity coefficient, $C_v = 0.97$
- Bucket friction factor, $K_r = 0.95$
- Mechanical efficiency, $\eta_m = 95\%$

**(a)** Determine the **speed ratio, wheel speed, and number of buckets** required. Justify your choice of speed ratio. (7 marks)

**(b)** Calculate the **discharge, power developed, and overall efficiency** of the wheel. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Jet velocity
$$V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH} = 0.97 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 400} = 0.97 \times 88.54 = 85.88 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Optimum speed ratio (frictionless ideal = 0.5, real ≈ 0.46)
$$K_u = 0.46 \quad \text{(chosen for maximum real efficiency)}$$

**Step 3:** Wheel peripheral velocity
$$u_1 = K_u V_1 = 0.46 \times 85.88 = 39.50 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4:** Wheel speed
$$N = \dfrac{60 u_1}{\pi D} = \dfrac{60 \times 39.50}{\pi \times 1.5} = \dfrac{2370}{4.712} = 502.9 \text{ rpm}$$
$$\boxed{N \approx 503 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Step 5:** Number of buckets
$$Z = 15 + \dfrac{D}{2d} = 15 + \dfrac{1.5}{2 \times 0.12} = 15 + 6.25 = 21.25$$
$$\boxed{Z = 22 \text{ (adopted standard value)}}$$

**Part (b):**

**Step 6:** Discharge
$$Q = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot V_1 = \dfrac{\pi (0.12)^2}{4} \times 85.88 = 0.01131 \times 85.88 = 0.9713 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$\boxed{Q \approx 0.97 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$

**Step 7:** Velocity triangles
$$V_{r1} = V_1 - u_1 = 85.88 - 39.50 = 46.38 \text{ m/s}$$
$$V_{r2} = K_r V_{r1} = 0.95 \times 46.38 = 44.06 \text{ m/s}$$
$$V_{w2} = V_{r2}\cos 10° - u_1 = 44.06 \times 0.9848 - 39.50 = 43.39 - 39.50 = 3.89 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 8:** Hydraulic efficiency
$$\eta_h = \dfrac{2 u_1(V_1 - V_{w2})}{V_1^2} = \dfrac{2 \times 39.50 \times (85.88 - 3.89)}{85.88^2}$$
$$\eta_h = \dfrac{79.00 \times 81.99}{7375.4} = \dfrac{6477.2}{7375.4} = 0.8783$$
$$\boxed{\eta_h \approx 87.83\%}$$

**Step 9:** Shaft power
$$P_{\text{water}} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.9713 \times 400 = 3{,}811{,}580 \text{ W} = 3811.6 \text{ kW}$$
$$P_{\text{shaft}} = \eta_m \cdot \eta_h \cdot P_{\text{water}} = 0.95 \times 0.8783 \times 3811.6$$
$$P_{\text{shaft}} = 3179.0 \text{ kW} \approx 3.18 \text{ MW}$$

(Assuming generator efficiency $\eta_g = 0.97$, electrical output $P_{\text{elec}} = 0.97 \times 3179 = 3083.6$ kW.)

**Step 10:** Overall efficiency
$$\eta_o = \eta_h \cdot \eta_m = 0.8783 \times 0.95 = 0.8344$$
$$\boxed{\eta_o \approx 83.4\%}$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024:**

- [x] **Pelton wheels are used for HIGH HEADS (300–2000 m) and LOW discharges.** Always check the head first.
- [x] **Optimum speed ratio:** $K_u = 0.5$ (ideal) and $K_u = 0.46$ (real, with bucket friction).
- [x] **Jet ratio:** $d/D = 1/10$ to $1/20$. **Adopt 1/15** unless otherwise specified.
- [x] **Bucket width:** $B/d = 5$ (typical); **Bucket depth:** $L/d = 2.6$ (typical).
- [x] **Number of buckets:** $Z = 15 + D/(2d)$ is the **standard empirical formula**.
- [x] **Velocity coefficient:** $C_v = 0.98$ (nozzle); **Bucket friction factor:** $K_r = 0.95$.
- [x] **Outlet whirl velocity:** $V_{w2} = V_{r2}\cos\beta_2 - u_1$ (sign convention: opposite to wheel motion).
- [x] **Hydraulic efficiency formula:** $\eta_h = 2 u_1 (V_1 - V_{w2}) / V_1^2$.
- [x] **Specific speed range for Pelton:** $N_s = 10$ to $60$ (metric, kW, m). A value outside this range suggests a different turbine type.
- [x] **Work done per unit weight:** $W = u_1(V_1 - V_{w2})/g$.
- [x] **Governing:** Pelton uses a **double-regulation** system — needle (spear) for steady load variation + deflector for sudden load rejection (avoids water hammer).
- [x] **Tailrace:** Pelton is set **above** the tailwater; the runner discharges into a tailrace channel or casing — no draft tube.
- [x] **Always verify** the computed $N = 60u_1/(\pi D)$ against the given $N$ in the problem. If they differ by more than 5%, iterate $d$ or $K_u$.
- [x] **Units checklist:** $H$ in m, $Q$ in m³/s, $P$ in W or kW, $u_1$ and $V_1$ in m/s, $D$ and $d$ in m, $N$ in rpm.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Francis and Kaplan Turbines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Francis and Kaplan Turbines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Francis Turbine — Formal Definition

A **Francis Turbine** is a **reaction-type, mixed-flow hydraulic turbine** in which water enters the runner radially (or with a small axial component) through a set of guide vanes, passes through the curved runner blades, and discharges axially along the axis of rotation. It is the most widely used hydraulic turbine in the world, designed to operate efficiently under a wide range of **medium head (≈ 30 m to 600 m)** and medium-to-high discharge conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A Francis turbine is *not* purely radial nor purely axial — the name *mixed-flow* comes from the fact that the **relative velocity at inlet is nearly radial**, while the **absolute velocity at outlet is nearly axial**. This is a frequently asked conceptual question in Part A (3 marks).

### 1.2 Kaplan Turbine — Formal Definition

A **Kaplan Turbine** is a **reaction-type, axial-flow hydraulic turbine** equipped with a **propeller-type runner** having **adjustable (pivoting) blades**. Both the guide vanes and the runner blades can be rotated simultaneously to maintain a high efficiency over a wide range of discharges. It is specifically designed for **low head (less than 60 m)** and **high discharge** conditions — the typical choice for run-of-river and tidal power plants.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Note:** A Kaplan turbine is essentially a propeller turbine in which the blade pitch can be adjusted while the machine is running. The unit is called a **Kaplan unit**, and the combination of a variable-pitch runner with a speed-regulating wicket gate is known as **double regulation**.

---

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Spinning Sprinkler + Spiral Slide" Concept

Imagine a giant water slide (the **spiral casing**) that forces water to spin around a central drum. Just before the drum, a set of rotating **sliding doors (guide vanes)** channels the swirling water inward at exactly the right angle. Inside the drum sits a **pincushion-shaped wheel (runner)**. The water pushes the curved blades of this wheel, then drains out through a vertical pipe below (the **draft tube**). This is exactly how a **Francis turbine** works.

For the **Kaplan turbine**, picture instead a **ship's propeller spinning inside a tube**. Water is given a swirling push by guide vanes before it hits the propeller. Because both the swirling vanes *and* the propeller blades can pivot like a *fidget spinner*, the propeller can stay efficient whether water flow is large, small, fast, or slow. The water leaves the runner straight down the axis of the tube.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exams:**
> - **Francis** = *F*or *M*edium head (30 m – 600 m), Mixed flow, Fixed blades.
> - **Kaplan** = *K*eep *A*djusting *P*ropeller blades for *L*ow head, *A*xial flow, *N*eed double regulation.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Francis Turbine | Kaplan Turbine |
|---|---|---|
| Specific Speed (Ns in metric units) | **60 – 450** | **300 – 1000 (or more)** |
| Head Range | **30 m – 600 m** | **< 60 m** |
| Discharge | Medium – High | Very High |
| Runner Type | **Mixed flow (radial → axial)** | **Axial flow (propeller)** |
| Blade Adjustment | Fixed blades | Adjustable blades + guide vanes |
| Discharge Direction | Axial at outlet | Axial throughout |
| Typical Efficiency | **85 % – 92 %** | **88 % – 94 %** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step by Step

#### A. Francis Turbine Operation
1. **Water Entry through Penstock:** High-pressure water from the reservoir is delivered to the turbine via a penstock.
2. **Spiral Casing (Scroll Casing):** The water enters a spiral-shaped casing that distributes it **symmetrically and uniformly** around the entire circumference of the runner. This ensures equal pressure at every guide vane entry.
3. **Stay Vanes and Guide Vanes:** *Stay vanes* are stationary vanes that convert the spiral kinetic energy into pure pressure energy, and *guide vanes* (movable) regulate the discharge and direct water onto the runner at the desired angle $\alpha_1$.
4. **Runner (Mixed-Flow):** Water impinges on the curved blades, transferring momentum. The wheel rotates; hence it is a *reaction* turbine (pressure drop occurs inside the runner itself).
5. **Draft Tube:** The exit pipe converts the residual kinetic energy at the runner outlet into useful suction head (pressure recovery), increasing the net head available between inlet and outlet of the turbine.

#### B. Kaplan Turbine Operation
1. **Scroll Casing:** Concrete (not metal) scroll casing is used because heads are low; it evenly distributes water around the runner.
2. **Guide Vane Mechanism:** Swiveling guide vanes control the magnitude and direction of the **whirl component $V_{w1}$** at inlet.
3. **Runner with Adjustable Blades:** A hub carrying 3 – 6 blades. Each blade can be rotated about its own axis by a **servo-motor inside the hub**, controlled by the **governor**.
4. **Axial Discharge:** Water leaves parallel to the turbine axis and enters a carefully shaped **draft tube** (usually a **conical or elbow type**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** In both Francis and Kaplan turbines, **the draft tube is not just a pipe — it is a critical energy-recovery device**. It recovers the kinetic energy of the exiting water and converts it into useful suction head, thereby increasing the effective head between source and tailrace.

---

### 2.2 Velocity Triangles — The Heart of the Problem

Every hydraulic turbine problem in KTU exams revolves around the **inlet and outlet velocity triangles** of the runner.

For a moving blade, two reference frames are used:
- **Absolute velocity (V)** — velocity of water relative to a fixed observer.
- **Relative velocity (Vr)** — velocity of water relative to the moving blade.
- **Velocity of the blade (U or Vb)** — tangential speed of the blade at that radius.

The vector relation is:

$$
\vec{V} = \vec{V_r} + \vec{U}
$$

#### A. Inlet Velocity Triangle (for Francis and Kaplan)

The velocity triangle at inlet has the following components:
- $V_1$ = absolute velocity of water at inlet
- $V_{w1}$ = whirl component of $V_1$ (tangential direction)
- $V_{f1}$ = flow component of $V_1$ (radial or axial direction depending on turbine)
- $U_1$ = blade velocity at inlet
- $V_{r1}$ = relative velocity at inlet
- $\alpha_1$ = guide vane angle (angle between $V_1$ and $U_1$)
- $\theta$ = blade angle at inlet (angle between $V_{r1}$ and the negative $U_1$ direction)

The following relations hold:

$$
V_{w1} = V_1 \cos \alpha_1
$$

$$
V_{f1} = V_1 \sin \alpha_1
$$

$$
V_{r1} \cos \theta = U_1 - V_{w1}
$$

$$
V_{r1} \sin \theta = V_{f1}
$$

#### B. Outlet Velocity Triangle (for Francis Turbine)

A **standard simplifying assumption** (asked frequently in KTU exams) is that water leaves the Francis runner **radially**, i.e.:

$$
\beta_2 = 90^\circ
$$

This implies that the **whirl at the outlet is zero**:

$$
V_{w2} = 0
$$

The outlet velocity triangle is then a right-angled triangle with:
- $U_2$ = blade velocity at outlet
- $V_{f2}$ = flow component at outlet (axial for Francis)
- $V_{r2} = V_{f2}$ (since $\beta_2 = 90^\circ$)

#### C. Outlet Velocity Triangle (for Kaplan Turbine)

In a Kaplan turbine, the outlet is also axial, but unlike the Francis, the **whirl at outlet is generally not zero**. The absolute velocity at outlet can be decomposed into:
- $V_{w2}$ (small but non-zero)
- $V_{f2}$ (axial flow velocity)

The relative velocity at outlet makes an angle $\phi$ with the axial direction.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat Sheet — Save This for the Exam:** All formulas below are **board-exam approved** and form the backbone of any Francis / Kaplan numerical.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Euler Work Done (per unit weight) | $W = \dfrac{V_{w1} U_1 \pm V_{w2} U_2}{g}$ m·N/N | Head developed by runner |
| 2 | Runner Inlet Blade Angle | $\tan \theta = \dfrac{V_{f1}}{U_1 - V_{w1}}$ | Design of Francis/Kaplan |
| 3 | Runner Outlet Blade Angle (Francis) | $\beta_2 = 90^\circ \Rightarrow \tan \beta_2 = \infty$ | Francis outlet assumption |
| 4 | Runner Outlet Blade Angle (Kaplan) | $\tan \phi = \dfrac{V_{f2}}{U_2 - V_{w2}}$ | Kaplan outlet |
| 5 | Hydraulic Efficiency | $\eta_h = \dfrac{V_{w1} U_1 \pm V_{w2} U_2}{g H}$ | Energy conversion efficiency |
| 6 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{\text{Shaft Power}}{\text{Water Power}}$ | Includes bearing/friction losses |
| 7 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_h \times \eta_m$ | Total system efficiency |
| 8 | Degree of Reaction (R) | $R = \dfrac{V_{w1}^2 - V_{w2}^2 + V_{f1}^2 - V_{f2}^2}{2(V_{w1}U_1 - V_{w2}U_2)}$ | Energy split pressure/kinetic |
| 9 | Specific Speed (Metric) | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | Selection of turbine type |
| 10 | Discharge Through Velocity | $Q = \pi (D_1 + D_2) B \cdot V_{f1}$ | Francis runner capacity |
| 11 | Unit Speed | $N_u = \dfrac{N \sqrt{H^{5/4}}}{\sqrt{H}}$ | Model-prototype similarity |
| 12 | Unit Discharge | $Q_u = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{H}}$ | Model-prototype similarity |
| 13 | Unit Power | $P_u = \dfrac{P}{H^{3/2}}$ | Model-prototype similarity |
| 14 | Draft Tube Suction Head | $H_s = \dfrac{V_2^{\prime 2} - V_3^2}{2g} - \text{losses}$ | Recovery of kinetic energy |

> [!TIP]
> **Sign Convention in Euler's Equation:** Use **'+' for inward radial / axial flow** (radial inward, mixed flow, axial inward) and **'–' for outward radial flow** (centrifugal pumps). For Francis and Kaplan (which are inward flow turbines), the formula uses the **'+'** sign.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Machine | Real-World Application |
|---|---|
| **Francis Turbine** | Hydroelectric dams (e.g., Bhakra Nangal, Idukki), high-head power plants, mid-sized irrigation projects. |
| **Kaplan Turbine** | Tidal power plants, run-of-river schemes, low-head barrages, bulb turbines in canal drops. |
| **Specific Speed Criterion** | Used by design engineers to *select* the correct turbine for a given site (head + discharge). |
| **Draft Tube** | Saves up to **20 % additional head** — economically critical in low-head plants. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Work Done by the Runner (Euler's Equation for Turbines)

The most important derivation in Module 3. Every KTU Part B (14-mark) numerical on Francis / Kaplan will require a direct application of this formula.

#### Starting Premises
- Consider a small mass of water $dm$ entering the runner at the inlet.
- Let the **absolute velocity of water at inlet** be $V_1$ at an angle $\alpha_1$ with the tangential direction (blade direction).
- Let the **blade velocity at inlet** be $U_1 = \dfrac{\pi D_1 N}{60}$, where $D_1$ is the inlet diameter of the runner and $N$ is the rotational speed in rpm.
- The mass is guided by the blade such that its relative velocity at the blade outlet $V_{r2}$ is exactly aligned along the trailing edge of the the blade (this is the *vane condition* or *shockless entry/exit condition*).

#### Step 1: Resolve the Inlet Absolute Velocity
The absolute velocity $V_1$ has two components:
- **Tangential (whirl) component:** $V_{w1} = V_1 \cos \alpha_1$
- **Radial (flow) component:** $V_{f1} = V_1 \sin \alpha_1$

Since the work done on the runner depends only on the tangential component, we focus on $V_{w1}$.

#### Step 2: Apply the Angular Momentum Theorem
The torque on the runner is the **rate of change of angular momentum of the water mass** about the axis of rotation.

If the mass flow rate entering the runner is $\dot{m}$ kg/s, then the rate of change of angular momentum is:

$$
\text{Torque} \; T = \dot{m} (V_{w1} R_1 \pm V_{w2} R_2)
$$

where $R_1$ and $R_2$ are the inlet and outlet radii of the runner, and the sign convention '+' applies for **inward flow** (which is the case for both Francis and Kaplan turbines).

#### Step 3: Compute the Work Done per Second (Power)
Work done per second = Torque × Angular Velocity $\omega$.

$$
P = T \cdot \omega = \dot{m} (V_{w1} U_1 \pm V_{w2} U_2)
$$

since $U_1 = \omega R_1$ and $U_2 = \omega R_2$.

#### Step 4: Convert to Work Done per Unit Weight of Water
Dividing both sides by the weight flow rate $\dot{m} g$:

$$
W = \frac{P}{\dot{m} g} = \frac{V_{w1} U_1 \pm V_{w2} U_2}{g}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;W = \frac{V_{w1} U_1 + V_{w2} U_2}{g} \quad \text{(for Francis and Kaplan)}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **head in metres of water** imparted to the water by the runner. For a **Francis turbine** with radial exit, $V_{w2} = 0$, so the work done simplifies to $W = \dfrac{V_{w1} U_1}{g}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Inlet Blade Angle ($\theta$)

At the runner inlet, the velocity triangle is:

$$
\vec{V_1} = \vec{V_{r1}} + \vec{U_1}
$$

Decomposing in the tangential and flow directions:

- Tangential: $V_{w1} + V_{r1} \cos \theta = U_1$ → (note: $V_{r1}$ has a *negative* tangential component because it points inward against the blade motion)
- Flow (radial/axial): $V_{r1} \sin \theta = V_{f1}$

Dividing the second equation by the first:

$$
\tan \theta = \frac{V_{f1}}{U_1 - V_{w1}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\theta = \tan^{-1} \left( \frac{V_{f1}}{U_1 - V_{w1}} \right)\;}
$$

This is the **design blade angle at the inlet** of the runner, ensuring shockless entry.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Hydraulic Efficiency for Francis Turbine

**Hydraulic efficiency** is defined as the ratio of work done by water on the runner to the energy supplied to the runner per unit weight of water.

$$
\eta_h = \frac{\text{Work done per unit weight}}{\text{Head supplied to runner}}
$$

For a Francis turbine, the head supplied to the runner is given by the **inlet energy equation** (Bernoulli with losses):

$$
H_{\text{runner}} = \frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} - \frac{P_2}{\rho g} - \frac{V_2^2}{2g}
$$

Using the Euler equation and the standard assumption of radial exit:

$$
\eta_h = \frac{V_{w1} U_1 / g}{V_1^2 / 2g}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Euler-Bernoulli** form of hydraulic efficiency — it relates runner work to the kinetic head at the inlet.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Degree of Reaction ($R$)

The **degree of reaction** is the fraction of the energy conversion that occurs due to a **change in pressure** across the runner, as opposed to a change in velocity (kinetic energy).

By the **Euler equation**:
- Total work done per unit weight = $W = \dfrac{V_{w1} U_1 \pm V_{w2} U_2}{g}$

The kinetic energy change of water (per unit weight) is:
- $\Delta KE = \dfrac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g} = \dfrac{(V_{w1}^2 + V_{f1}^2) - (V_{w2}^2 + V_{f2}^2)}{2g}$

The pressure energy change (per unit weight) is:
- $\Delta P = W - \Delta KE$

By definition, the **degree of reaction** is:

$$
R = \frac{\Delta P}{W} = \frac{W - \Delta KE}{W}
$$

$$
R = \frac{(V_{w1}U_1 - V_{w2}U_2) - \dfrac{V_{w1}^2 + V_{f1}^2 - V_{w2}^2 - V_{f2}^2}{2}}{V_{w1}U_1 - V_{w2}U_2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;R = \dfrac{V_{w1}^2 - V_{w2}^2 + V_{f1}^2 - V_{f2}^2}{2(V_{w1}U_1 - V_{w2}U_2)}\;}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Special Case — Pure Impulse Turbine:** $R = 0$ (e.g., Pelton wheel). **Pure Reaction:** $R = 1$ (theoretical, e.g., ideal Francis/Kaplan if $V_1 = V_2$). For **practical Francis**, $R \approx 0.12$ at inlet; for **Kaplan**, $R$ can approach 0.6 – 0.9.

---

### 3.5 Worked-Out Analytical Example (KTU Standard)

> **Problem:** A Francis turbine has an inlet diameter of 1.2 m and an outlet diameter of 0.6 m. The breadth at inlet is 0.15 m. The guide vane angle is 15°, blade angle at outlet is 90°, and the runner speed is 300 rpm. The radial velocity of flow at outlet is 9 m/s. Determine:
> (a) The whirl at inlet.
> (b) The head developed by the runner.
> (c) The hydraulic efficiency.

#### Given Data
- $D_1 = 1.2$ m, $D_2 = 0.6$ m, $B = 0.15$ m
- $\alpha_1 = 15^\circ$, $\beta_2 = 90^\circ$
- $N = 300$ rpm, $V_{f2} = 9$ m/s
- **Assume** no shock at inlet: $V_{r1}$ is tangential to the blade at inlet.

#### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1: Compute the blade velocities.**

$$
U_1 = \frac{\pi D_1 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 1.2 \times 300}{60}
$$

$$
U_1 = 18.85 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
U_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.6 \times 300}{60}
$$

$$
U_2 = 9.42 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the flow velocity at inlet (assuming no shock loss and continuity).**

The discharge is constant: $Q = \pi D_1 B V_{f1} = \pi D_2 B V_{f2}$.

$$
\pi \times 1.2 \times 0.15 \times V_{f1} = \pi \times 0.6 \times 0.15 \times 9
$$

$$
V_{f1} = \frac{0.6 \times 9}{1.2} = 4.5 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the whirl at inlet $V_{w1}$.**

The blade angle at inlet $\theta$ is given by the velocity triangle relation:
- $V_{w1} = U_1 - V_{f1} \cot \theta$ → but $\theta$ is unknown.

Using the velocity triangle directly (since $V_{r1}$ is tangential to the blade):

From the velocity triangle, the whirl component can be found if we know the absolute velocity $V_1$. The guide vane angle gives the *direction* of $V_1$:

$$
\tan \alpha_1 = \frac{V_{f1}}{V_{w1}}
$$

$$
V_{w1} = \frac{V_{f1}}{\tan \alpha_1} = \frac{4.5}{\tan 15^\circ}
$$

$$
V_{w1} = \frac{4.5}{0.2679} = 16.79 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the head developed by the runner.**

For a Francis turbine (radial exit, $V_{w2} = 0$):

$$
W = \frac{V_{w1} U_1}{g} = \frac{16.79 \times 18.85}{9.81}
$$

$$
W = 32.26 \text{ m of water}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the hydraulic efficiency.**

$$
\eta_h = \frac{V_{w1} U_1 / g}{V_1^2 / 2g}
$$

First, compute $V_1$:

$$
V_1 = \sqrt{V_{w1}^2 + V_{f1}^2} = \sqrt{16.79^2 + 4.5^2}
$$

$$
V_1 = \sqrt{281.91 + 20.25} = \sqrt{302.16}
$$

$$
V_1 = 17.38 \text{ m/s}
$$

Now compute efficiency:

$$
\eta_h = \frac{2 \times V_{w1} U_1}{V_1^2} = \frac{2 \times 16.79 \times 18.85}{17.38^2}
$$

$$
\eta_h = \frac{632.78}{302.16} = 2.094
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Hydraulic efficiency > 1?** Yes — this is a *design-stage theoretical efficiency* calculated using only inlet kinetic head. In a real machine, the **head supplied to the runner** includes the full inlet pressure and velocity head *and* accounts for the actual inlet and outlet conditions. Always cross-check the assumed form of $\eta_h$ with what the question provides. If efficiency exceeds 100 %, the formula used is wrong.

> [!TIP]
> **Corrected Efficiency Formula for this Numerical:**
>
> $$
> \eta_h = \frac{V_{w1} U_1}{g H}
> $$
>
> where $H$ is the **gross head supplied to the runner** (from penstock to runner outlet). Without that data, we can only state the **kinetic energy efficiency** as above.

#### Final Tabulated Result

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $U_1$ | **18.85 m/s** |
| $U_2$ | **9.42 m/s** |
| $V_{f1}$ | **4.5 m/s** |
| $V_{w1}$ | **16.79 m/s** |
| $V_1$ | **17.38 m/s** |
| Runner Head $W$ | **32.26 m** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Francis Turbine

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **functional flow** of energy, water, and mechanical motion through a Francis turbine installation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reservoir Head H] --> B[Penstock Inlet]
    B --> C[Spiral Casing Scroll]
    C --> D[Stay Vanes Pressure Recovery]
    D --> E[Guide Vanes Wicket Gates]
    E --> F[Francis Runner Mixed Flow]
    F --> G[Main Shaft Coupling]
    G --> H[Generator Electrical Output]
    F --> I[Draft Tube Kinetic Energy Recovery]
    I --> J[Tailrace Outlet]
    H --> K[Grid Power Distribution]
    J --> L[Downstream River Flow]

    style A fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style C fill:#5f9ea0,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style E fill:#b8860b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style F fill:#8b0000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style I fill:#2e8b57,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style H fill:#4b0082,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style K fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#000
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Kaplan Turbine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Low Head Reservoir] --> R2[Trash Rack Coarse Filter]
    R2 --> R3[Concrete Scroll Casing]
    R3 --> R4[Stay Ring]
    R4 --> R5[Guide Vane Mechanism Double Regulation]
    R5 --> R6[Servo Motor Pitch Control]
    R6 --> R7[Kaplan Runner Hub With Adjustable Blades]
    R7 --> R8[Main Shaft Vertical or Inclined]
    R8 --> R9[Generator Unit]
    R7 --> R10[Elbow Draft Tube]
    R10 --> R11[Tailrace]

    style R1 fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style R3 fill:#6a5acd,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style R5 fill:#daa520,stroke:#000
    style R7 fill:#8b0000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style R9 fill:#4b0082,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style R10 fill:#2e8b57,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Comparative Flow Map — Francis vs Kaplan Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Site Data Head H and Discharge Q] --> Q1{Head less than 60 m}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Q very high}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Head 30 m to 600 m}
    Q2 -- Yes --> K1[Select Kaplan Turbine]
    Q2 -- No --> K2[Consider Propeller or Bulb]
    Q3 -- Yes --> F1[Select Francis Turbine]
    Q3 -- No --> F2{Head more than 600 m}
    F2 -- Yes --> F3[Select Pelton Impulse Turbine]
    F2 -- No --> F4[Re-evaluate head range]

    style K1 fill:#8b0000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style F1 fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style F3 fill:#4b0082,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style P fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#000
```

### 4.4 Velocity Triangle Schematic (Francis — Radial Exit)

```
                  V1 (Absolute Inlet)
                  ↘
                   ↘   α1
                    ↘________  U1 (Blade velocity, horizontal)
                    |       ↘
                    |        ↘
                    |         ↘  Vr1 (Relative inlet)
                    |          ↘
                    |           ↘  θ
                    |     [BLADE INLET]
                    
                    ↓ (water flows inward)
                    
                    [BLADE OUTLET — radial exit β2 = 90°]
                    |\
                    | \
                    |  \  Vr2 (= Vf2, since β2 = 90°)
                    |   \
                    |    \   U2 (horizontal)
                    |_____\___________
                    
                    V2 (radial, no whirl)
```

> [!NOTE]
> This ASCII schematic is the **standard KTU exam-style inlet and outlet velocity triangle** for a Francis turbine with radial exit. The student should reproduce it cleanly with a protractor and ruler for full marks in the 14-mark Part B.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**"What is a mixed-flow turbine? Explain why a Francis turbine is classified as a mixed-flow machine."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **mixed-flow turbine** is a type of reaction turbine in which the water does not flow in a purely radial or purely axial direction through the runner; instead, it enters in a predominantly radial direction and leaves in a predominantly axial direction.

A **Francis turbine** is classified as a mixed-flow machine because:
1. The water **enters the runner radially** (or with a small axial component) through the guide vanes.
2. The water **exits axially** along the axis of rotation of the runner.
3. The blade shape itself follows a **3-D twisted profile** that smoothly redirects the flow from radial to axial — neither purely radial nor purely axial.

Hence, the Francis turbine lies in the family of *mixed-flow* reaction turbines (3 marks).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**"Distinguish between a Francis turbine and a Kaplan turbine in any three aspects."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per aspect):**

| Aspect | Francis Turbine | Kaplan Turbine |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Head Range** | Medium head (30 m – 600 m) | Low head (less than 60 m) |
| **2. Flow Direction** | Mixed flow (radial → axial) | Axial flow throughout |
| **3. Blade Adjustment** | Fixed blades | Adjustable (pivoting) blades with double regulation |

*(Alternative valid aspects: Specific speed, type of scroll casing, draft tube shape, typical applications.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Always draw the velocity triangle** — partial marks (often 2–3 out of 14) are lost by students who skip the diagram.
> 2. **Use SI units consistently** — if $N$ is in rpm, then $U = \pi D N / 60$, not $\pi D N$.
> 3. **State assumptions explicitly** — e.g., "Assuming radial exit, $V_{w2} = 0$." Examiners award 1–2 marks just for clear assumptions.
> 4. **Sign convention matters** — the '+' sign in Euler's equation applies to *inward* flow (Francis and Kaplan), not outward.
> 5. **Distinguish between runner work and total head** — students often confuse $V_{w1} U_1 / g$ with the net head $H$.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

*(a)* A **Francis turbine** has the following design parameters:
- Inlet diameter $D_1 = 1.0$ m, outlet diameter $D_2 = 0.5$ m
- Width of the runner at inlet $B_1 = 0.2$ m
- Guide vane angle $\alpha_1 = 20^\circ$, blade angle at outlet $\beta_2 = 90^\circ$
- Speed $N = 250$ rpm
- Discharge $Q = 6.0$ m³/s
- Mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 92\%$

Calculate:
1. Blade velocities $U_1$ and $U_2$.
2. Flow velocities $V_{f1}$ and $V_{f2}$.
3. Whirl component at inlet $V_{w1}$.
4. Head developed by the runner.
5. Shaft power developed by the turbine.

*(7 marks)*

*(b)* With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a Kaplan turbine**. List its main components and state the function of each. Why are the runner blades of a Kaplan turbine made adjustable?

*(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Blade velocities.**

$$
U_1 = \frac{\pi D_1 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 1.0 \times 250}{60}
$$

$$
U_1 = 13.09 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{... (1 mark)}
$$

$$
U_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.5 \times 250}{60}
$$

$$
U_2 = 6.54 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{... (1 mark)}
$$

**Step 2: Flow velocity at inlet (from continuity).**

$$
Q = \pi D_1 B_1 V_{f1}
$$

$$
6.0 = \pi \times 1.0 \times 0.2 \times V_{f1}
$$

$$
V_{f1} = \frac{6.0}{0.6283} = 9.55 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{... (1 mark)}
$$

**Step 3: Flow velocity at outlet (from outlet area).**

Assuming the outlet width $B_2 \approx B_1 = 0.2$ m:

$$
V_{f2} = \frac{Q}{\pi D_2 B_2} = \frac{6.0}{\pi \times 0.5 \times 0.2}
$$

$$
V_{f2} = 19.10 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4: Whirl at inlet (from guide vane angle).**

$$
V_{w1} = \frac{V_{f1}}{\tan \alpha_1} = \frac{9.55}{\tan 20^\circ}
$$

$$
V_{w1} = \frac{9.55}{0.3640} = 26.24 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{... (1 mark)}
$$

**Step 5: Head developed by the runner (radial exit assumption).**

$$
W = \frac{V_{w1} U_1}{g} = \frac{26.24 \times 13.09}{9.81}
$$

$$
W = 35.02 \text{ m of water} \quad \text{... (1.5 marks)}
$$

**Step 6: Shaft power developed.**

Water power = $\rho g Q W = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 6.0 \times 35.02 = 2,061,277$ W = 2061.3 kW

Shaft power = $\eta_m \times$ Water power = $0.92 \times 2061.3$ = **1896.4 kW ≈ 1.90 MW** *(1.5 marks)*

**Tabulated Result:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $U_1$ | **13.09 m/s** |
| $U_2$ | **6.54 m/s** |
| $V_{f1}$ | **9.55 m/s** |
| $V_{f2}$ | **19.10 m/s** |
| $V_{w1}$ | **26.24 m/s** |
| Runner Head $W$ | **35.02 m** |
| Shaft Power | **1.90 MW** |

---

**Part (b) — Construction and Working of Kaplan Turbine (7 marks)**

**Construction (3 marks):**

1. **Scroll Casing (1 mark):** Made of concrete (since heads are low and dimensions are large). It receives water from the penstock and distributes it symmetrically around the runner circumference.

2. **Guide Vane Mechanism (1 mark):** A series of adjustable guide vanes (wicket gates) mounted on a ring, controlled by a regulating ring. They direct water onto the runner at the desired angle and control discharge.

3. **Runner with Adjustable Blades (1 mark):** A central hub carrying 3 to 6 blades. Each blade can rotate about its own axis (similar to a ship's propeller), and the pitch is changed by an oil-pressure servo-motor inside the hub, controlled by the governor.

**Working (2 marks):**
- Water from the scroll casing passes through the guide vanes, which impart a tangential whirl.
- This swirling water enters the runner hub and is deflected by the runner blades, transferring angular momentum to the runner.
- The runner rotates, driving the generator through a vertical (or sometimes inclined) shaft.
- Water discharges axially into a draft tube, which converts the residual kinetic energy into useful suction head before water returns to the tailrace.

**Why are the blades adjustable? (2 marks):**
- The efficiency of a turbine depends on the **angle at which water meets the blade**.
- In a Kaplan turbine, the **discharge varies widely** with river flow (seasonal, diurnal).
- The guide vane setting and the runner blade pitch are **varied simultaneously** (a process called *double regulation*) so that the relative velocity always enters the blade tangentially.
- This **shockless entry** is maintained across a wide operating range, keeping efficiency high (typically 88 – 94 %) even at part loads.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

*(a)* A **Kaplan turbine** works under a head of 20 m at a discharge of 200 m³/s. The runner diameter is 4.0 m, the hub diameter is 2.0 m, the speed is 150 rpm, and the guide vane angle at the mean radius is 35°. Assume the velocity of whirl at the outlet equals the velocity of whirl at the inlet (i.e., $V_{w1} = V_{w2}$). Determine:

1. The blade velocity at the mean radius.
2. The flow velocity at the mean radius.
3. The runner blade angles at inlet and outlet.
4. The hydraulic efficiency of the turbine.
5. The power developed.

*(7 marks)*

*(b)* What is a **draft tube**? Explain the principle of its working. State the **types of draft tubes** used in Francis and Kaplan turbines, and explain the function of a **draft tube in detail**.

*(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $H = 20$ m, $Q = 200$ m³/s
- $D = 4.0$ m, $D_h = 2.0$ m (hub diameter)
- $N = 150$ rpm, $\alpha_1 = 35^\circ$
- $V_{w1} = V_{w2}$

**Step 1: Mean diameter and blade velocity.**

$$
D_m = \frac{D + D_h}{2} = \frac{4.0 + 2.0}{2} = 3.0 \text{ m}
$$

$$
U = \frac{\pi D_m N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 3.0 \times 150}{60}
$$

$$
U = 23.56 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{... (1 mark)}
$$

**Step 2: Flow velocity at mean radius.**

Annular flow area at the mean radius:

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - D_h^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} (16 - 4) = 9.42 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
V_f = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{200}{9.42} = 21.23 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{... (1.5 marks)}
$$

**Step 3: Whirl velocity at inlet (from guide vane angle).**

$$
V_{w1} = \frac{V_f}{\tan \alpha_1} = \frac{21.23}{\tan 35^\circ}
$$

$$
V_{w1} = \frac{21.23}{0.7002} = 30.32 \text{ m/s}
$$

By assumption, $V_{w2} = V_{w1} = 30.32$ m/s.

**Step 4: Runner blade angles.**

**Inlet blade angle $\theta$:**

$$
\tan \theta = \frac{V_f}{U - V_{w1}} = \frac{21.23}{23.56 - 30.32}
$$

$$
\tan \theta = \frac{21.23}{-6.76} = -3.14
$$

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}(-3.14) \approx -72.3^\circ
$$

The negative sign indicates that the relative velocity makes an obtuse angle with the tangential direction; in practice, blade angle is taken as the **magnitude**: $\theta \approx 72.3^\circ$ on the *other* side. *(1.5 marks)*

**Outlet blade angle $\phi$:**

$$
\tan \phi = \frac{V_f}{U - V_{w2}} = \frac{21.23}{23.56 - 30.32} = -3.14
$$

$$
\phi \approx 72.3^\circ
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 5: Hydraulic efficiency.**

For a Kaplan turbine, the work done per unit weight is:

$$
W = \frac{V_{w1} U + V_{w2} U}{g} = \frac{2 V_{w1} U}{g} = \frac{2 \times 30.32 \times 23.56}{9.81}
$$

$$
W = 145.61 \text{ m}
$$

But this is the runner-developed head, which **exceeds the gross head $H = 20$ m**. This is a *design-stage* inconsistency, indicating that the assumed velocity triangle is **infeasible for a real Kaplan**.

For a real Kaplan, the **hydraulic efficiency** is bounded by:

$$
\eta_h = \frac{W}{H} = \frac{V_{w1} U + V_{w2} U}{g H} = \frac{2 \times 30.32 \times 23.56}{9.81 \times 20} = 7.28
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Note:** This computed efficiency (7.28 = 728 %) is **physically impossible** because the assumption $V_{w1} = V_{w2}$ combined with the given data leads to an over-specification. A typical KTU follow-up question would ask the student to identify the inconsistency and propose a corrected assumption (e.g., $V_{w2} = 0.3 V_{w1}$ or specify the actual outlet whirl).

*(1 mark for correct setup and identification of inconsistency)*

**Step 6: Power developed.**

$$
P = \rho g Q H \eta_h \quad \text{(or use } P = \dot{m} W \text{)}
$$

Without a realistic $\eta_h$, we can only state the *water power*:

$$
P_{\text{water}} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 200 \times 20 = 39.24 \text{ MW}
$$

*(1 mark)*

---

**Part (b) — Draft Tube (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** A **draft tube** is a gradually expanding pipe (or duct) fitted at the outlet of a reaction turbine, which connects the runner outlet to the tailrace. Its primary purpose is to **convert the residual kinetic energy at the runner outlet into useful suction head (pressure head)**, thereby increasing the effective net head available between source and tailrace.

**Principle of Working (2 marks):**
- The water leaves the runner with absolute velocity $V_2$, which has both kinetic and pressure components.
- As the cross-section of the draft tube increases along its length, by **continuity**, the velocity decreases ($V_3 < V_2$ at the tailrace).
- By **Bernoulli's equation**, this decrease in velocity is accompanied by an **increase in pressure** (suction head).
- The pressure at the runner outlet drops below atmospheric, creating a **partial vacuum** (suction effect), which sucks the water out of the runner.
- This allows the turbine to be set **above the tailrace level** without losing head.

**Mathematical Expression (1.5 marks):**

$$
H_s = \frac{V_2^2 - V_3^2}{2g} - h_L
$$

where $H_s$ is the suction head recovered and $h_L$ is the head loss in the draft tube.

**Types of Draft Tubes (2.5 marks):**

| Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Conical Draft Tube** | Simple converging cone; expands gradually; high efficiency (up to 90 %); requires submergence. | Small to medium Francis turbines |
| **Elbow Draft Tube** | Combines a vertical cone with a horizontal bend; allows installation in shallow pits. | Kaplan and large Francis turbines |
| **Moody Spreading Draft Tube** | Long spreading tube; not preferred due to length. | Older installations |

**Functions of a Draft Tube (1 mark):**
1. Converts kinetic energy of water leaving the runner into pressure energy.
2. Creates suction head, enabling the turbine to be set above tailrace.
3. Allows gradual deceleration of water, preventing cavitation and turbulence.
4. Diverts water into the tailrace with minimum loss.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Last-Minute KTU Prep**

- ✅ **Francis Turbine** = Mixed flow, **medium head (30 – 600 m)**, fixed blades, specific speed **60 – 450**.
- ✅ **Kaplan Turbine** = Axial flow, **low head (< 60 m)**, adjustable blades, specific speed **> 300**, double regulation.
- ✅ **Euler Work Equation (inward flow):** $W = \dfrac{V_{w1} U_1 + V_{w2} U_2}{g}$.
- ✅ **Francis Outlet Assumption:** $\beta_2 = 90^\circ \Rightarrow V_{w2} = 0 \Rightarrow W = \dfrac{V_{w1} U_1}{g}$.
- ✅ **Inlet Blade Angle:** $\tan \theta = \dfrac{V_{f1}}{U_1 - V_{w1}}$.
- ✅ **Outlet Blade Angle (Kaplan):** $\tan \phi = \dfrac{V_{f2}}{U_2 - V_{w2}}$.
- ✅ **Hydraulic Efficiency:** $\eta_h = \dfrac{V_{w1} U_1 + V_{w2} U_2}{g H}$.
- ✅ **Degree of Reaction (R):** $R = \dfrac{V_{w1}^2 - V_{w2}^2 + V_{f1}^2 - V_{f2}^2}{2(V_{w1} U_1 - V_{w2} U_2)}$.
- ✅ **Specific Speed:** $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ — used to **select** the right turbine.
- ✅ **Unit Quantities:** $N_u = \dfrac{N}{\sqrt{H}}$, $Q_u = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{H}}$, $P_u = \dfrac{P}{H^{3/2}}$.
- ✅ **Draft Tube** is **not optional** — it recovers up to **20 % of head** in low-head machines.
- ✅ **Double Regulation** = simultaneous adjustment of **guide vanes** and **runner blades** (only in Kaplan).
- ✅ **Sign Convention:** Inward flow (Francis, Kaplan) → '+' sign in Euler's equation.
- ✅ **Always draw velocity triangles** in exams — partial marks heavily depend on this.
- ✅ **Convert rpm to rad/s or use the correct formula:** $U = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ (NOT $\pi D N$).
- ✅ **Continuity in Francis:** $Q = \pi D_1 B_1 V_{f1} = \pi D_2 B_2 V_{f2}$.
- ✅ **Continuity in Kaplan (annulus):** $Q = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - D_h^2) V_f$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Governing of Turbines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Governing of Turbines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

**Governing of a hydraulic turbine** is the process of regulating the **rate of flow (Q)** through the turbine in response to fluctuating load demands so as to maintain the **runner speed (N) within acceptable limits** around its rated (synchronous) value. The device that performs this automatic regulation is called the **governor** (e.g., a centrifugal flyball governor coupled through a servomotor / relay mechanism to the guide-vane / needle / spear valve).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Governing of turbines is studied alongside unit quantities, specific speed, characteristic curves, and cavitation — all of which together describe the **performance, selection, and safe operation** of a hydraulic turbine.

Mathematically, for any hydraulic turbine the kinematic condition at the runner inlet is:

$$ u_1 = \frac{\pi D_1 N}{60} $$

where:
- $u_1$ = tangential velocity of the runner at inlet (m/s)
- $D_1$ = runner inlet diameter (m)
- $N$ = rotational speed (rpm)

Since $N \propto \sqrt{H}$ for a homologous turbine, any change in load that disturbs the torque balance will momentarily alter $N$. Governing restores equilibrium.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pedalling a bicycle (turbine) to power a ceiling fan (generator/load):
- A **gust of head-wind** reduces the load → pedalling speed **shoots up** (N rises).
- A **sudden hill** increases the load → pedalling speed **drops** (N falls).
- The **gear-shifter / brake** acts like a **governor** — it controls the energy input (flow of water) to match the load.

In a hydro-turbine, water is the "energy courier" and the guide vane (or spear valve in Pelton) is the "gear-shifter". A centrifugal **flyball governor** senses speed; if N increases, the balls fly outward, the sleeve rises, the oil-pressurized **servomotor (relay)** closes the guide vanes, reducing Q, which lowers the power output back to match the new (lower) load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** In a 250 MW hydropower station, even a 1 % deviation from 50 Hz means the grid rejects the unit. Governing ensures the **frequency stability** demanded by the Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) and the national grid.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics (in Bold)

- **Acceleration due to gravity, g = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Density of water, ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
- **Atmospheric pressure head, H_atm ≈ 10.3 m of water** (sea level)
- **Synchronous speed (50 Hz, p pole pairs), N_s = 120f/p rpm**
- **Specific gravity of mercury, S_Hg = 13.6**
- **Unit discharge, Q_u (m³/s per √m)**
- **Unit power, P_u (kW per m^(3/2))**
- **Thoma's cavitation parameter, σ (dimensionless)**

> [!NOTE]
> For an **impulse turbine** (Pelton), governing is achieved by **deflecting the jet** using a **deflector plate** and simultaneously **moving the spear/needle** to throttle the jet — this avoids sudden pressure rise ("water hammer") in the penstock.
> 
> For a **reaction turbine** (Francis, Kaplan), governing is done by **rotating the guide vanes** (wicket gate) and, in Kaplan turbines, simultaneously **adjusting the runner blade pitch** (double regulation).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Governing is Necessary

A turbine is coupled to a generator. The load on the generator can change at any instant (lights switched ON/OFF in towns, industries starting motors, etc.). Without governing:

1. **If load decreases** → driving torque > resisting torque → **N rises** → frequency rises → **synchronous machine falls out of step**.
2. **If load increases** → driving torque < resisting torque → **N falls** → frequency falls → lights flicker.

The governor's job is to keep the **steady-state speed rise / drop** within **±2 to ±5 %** of rated speed during sudden load changes.

### 2.2 Operating Principles of a Governor

A typical modern hydraulic-turbine governor is a **PID controller** with three sensing elements:

| Element | Function |
|---|---|
| **Flyball (pendulum) speed sensor** | Translates speed into mechanical position of the sleeve. |
| **Hydraulic servomotor (relay)** | Multiplies the small pilot force into the large force needed to rotate the guide vanes. |
| **Compounding link (dashpot)** | Provides Proportional + Integral + Derivative (PID) action to prevent hunting. |

Working logic (Pelton example):

1. Load drops → N rises → flyballs fly outward → sleeve rises.
2. Sleeve movement uncovers the upper port of the relay piston.
3. Oil is admitted above the relay piston → it moves down.
4. The relay rod, through a lever, **pushes the deflector rapidly into the jet** (instantaneous load rejection without closing the needle).
5. After a brief pause (to allow the penstock pressure to stabilize), the needle is **slowly moved to the new position** corresponding to the new (lower) load.
6. New equilibrium: N ≈ N_rated, Q matches new load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use a deflector in Pelton?** If the needle were closed rapidly, the velocity of the long water column in the penstock would suddenly stop, causing a **pressure surge (water hammer)** that can burst the penstock. The deflector absorbs the excess energy temporarily while the needle closes slowly.

### 2.3 Relation Between Speed, Head and Flow

For a given geometrically similar turbine, dimensional analysis (or Buckingham π theorem) yields three independent dimensionless groups:

**Speed ratio (or head coefficient):**

$$ K_u = \frac{u_1}{\sqrt{2 g H}} = \text{constant for homologous operation} $$

**Flow ratio (or discharge coefficient):**

$$ K_f = \frac{V_{f1}}{\sqrt{2 g H}} = \text{constant} $$

**Power ratio (or output coefficient):**

$$ K_p = \frac{P}{\rho \, D_1^2 \, H^{3/2} \, g^{1/2}} = \text{constant} $$

From $u_1 = \pi D_1 N / 60$ and the constancy of $K_u$:

$$ \frac{N}{\sqrt{H}} = \text{constant} = N_u \quad \text{(Unit Speed)} $$

$$ \frac{Q}{\sqrt{H}} = \text{constant} = Q_u \quad \text{(Unit Discharge)} $$

$$ \frac{P}{H^{3/2}} = \text{constant} = P_u \quad \text{(Unit Power)} $$

### 2.4 Specific Speed (N_s) — The Master Selection Parameter

The most important single number for **classifying, comparing, and selecting** turbines is the **specific speed**:

$$ N_s = \frac{N \, \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} $$

- $N$ in **rpm**
- $P$ in **kW**
- $H$ in **m**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical meaning of N_s:** It is the speed (in rpm) at which a geometrically similar turbine would develop **1 kW** of power under a **head of 1 m**. Two turbines with the same N_s are hydrodynamically similar.

**Range of N_s by turbine type (KTU standard values):**

| Turbine Type | Specific Speed N_s (metric, kW, m) | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Pelton (1 jet) | 8 – 35 (single jet), up to 70 (multi-jet) | Very high head (> 250 m) |
| Francis (slow) | 60 – 180 | High head (60 – 250 m) |
| Francis (medium) | 180 – 350 | Medium head (30 – 60 m) |
| Francis (fast / mixed flow) | 350 – 450 | Medium-low head (15 – 30 m) |
| Kaplan / Propeller | 350 – 1000 | Low head (< 30 m) |

### 2.5 Characteristic Curves of Turbines

A **characteristic curve** is a graphical relationship between the operating parameters (H, N, Q, P, η) at a fixed gate opening (or constant head/speed). Three principal sets are defined:

**1. Main (or Constant-Head) Characteristic:**
- Constant **H** (and constant gate opening)
- Plotted: $N$ vs $P$, $Q$ vs $P$, $\eta$ vs $P$

**2. Operating (or Constant-Speed) Characteristic:**
- Constant **N** (synchronous, as in practice)
- Plotted: $H$ vs $P$, $\eta$ vs $P$, $Q$ vs $P$ for each gate opening.

**3. Muschel (or Constant-Efficiency) Curves / Iso-Efficiency Curves:**
- Curves of **constant efficiency** drawn in the $Q$–$H$ plane (or $N$–$Q$ plane).
- They envelope the "hill of best efficiency" and are essential for **predicting the operating range** of a turbine under varying head and discharge conditions (vital for Idukki, Mullaperiyar type reservoirs in Kerala).

### 2.6 Cavitation in Turbines

**Cavitation** is the formation and violent collapse of vapor bubbles in a region where the **static pressure falls below the vapour pressure** of water. It causes:
- Pitting of runner blades,
- Vibration,
- Loss of efficiency,
- Eventual failure.

**Thoma's Cavitation Parameter (σ):**

$$ \sigma = \frac{H_{atm} - H_{v} - H_s}{H} $$

where:
- $H_{atm}$ = atmospheric pressure head (≈ 10.3 m at sea level; reduces with altitude)
- $H_v$ = vapour pressure head of water (depends on temperature, ≈ 0.24 m at 25 °C)
- $H_s$ = suction head (height of turbine centreline above tail-race; **positive** if above, **negative** if below tail water level)

A turbine operates cavitation-free only if:

$$ \sigma \geq \sigma_c \quad \text{(critical or incipient σ from model tests)} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU standard σ_c values:** Pelton σ_c ≈ 0.0 – 0.1 (impulse, no suction); Francis σ_c ≈ 0.05 – 0.20; Kaplan σ_c ≈ 0.20 – 0.40. Kaplan turbines therefore need to be set **below tail water level** (negative H_s).

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Units | Typical Range / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Runner tip speed | $u_1 = \pi D_1 N / 60$ | m/s | 10 – 80 m/s |
| 2 | Unit speed | $N_u = N / \sqrt{H}$ | rpm/√m | Constant for homolog |
| 3 | Unit discharge | $Q_u = Q / \sqrt{H}$ | m³/s/√m | — |
| 4 | Unit power | $P_u = P / H^{3/2}$ | kW/m^(3/2) | — |
| 5 | **Specific speed** | $N_s = N\sqrt{P} / H^{5/4}$ | rpm·√kW/m^(5/4) | **Master selection parameter** |
| 6 | Speed ratio | $K_u = u_1/\sqrt{2gH}$ | — | 0.45 – 0.55 (Francis) |
| 7 | Flow ratio | $K_f = V_{f1}/\sqrt{2gH}$ | — | 0.15 – 0.30 |
| 8 | Hydraulic power | $P = \rho g Q H$ | W (÷1000 → kW) | — |
| 9 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_o = P_{\text{shaft}} / (\rho g Q H)$ | — | 0.80 – 0.92 |
| 10 | Thoma's σ | $\sigma = (H_{atm} - H_v - H_s)/H$ | — | Safe if $\sigma \geq \sigma_c$ |
| 11 | Setting (TWL - runner centre) | $H_s$ | m | Negative for Kaplan |
| 12 | Synchronous speed | $N = 120f/p$ | rpm | 50 Hz Indian grid |
| 13 | Manometric head (Pelton) | $H_m = H - h_f$ | m | $h_f$ = pipe friction loss |
| 14 | Jet velocity (Pelton) | $V_j = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$ | m/s | $C_v \approx 0.97$–$0.99$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NEVER use `|` (vertical bar) for absolute value in a KTU answer table — use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX. The cheat sheet above obeys this rule.**

### 2.8 Real-World Utility

- **Idukki Hydroelectric Project (Kerala):** 780 MW, head ≈ 169 m, 6 × Francis turbines. Cavitation-free setting required because tail-water level varies 5 m between monsoon and summer. Thoma's σ must be computed for **maximum head AND minimum tail-water level** (worst case).
- **Iravimangalam Moozhiyar Small Hydro:** Kaplan turbines, head ≈ 25 m, set below tail water level.
- **Mullaperiyar:** Proposed to drive Francis units; governing must respond to load swings from the Tamil Nadu grid.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Unit Quantities from Dimensional Analysis (Buckingham π Theorem)

We consider a turbine whose shaft power $P$ depends on the following independent variables:

$$ P = f(D_1, N, H, g, \rho, \mu) $$

To obtain a complete dimensional analysis we include all relevant variables, but for the **principal (Eulerian) similarity** the dominant ones are $D_1, N, H, g, \rho$, and the viscosity term is neglected for fully turbulent flow at high Reynolds numbers (which is the KTU assumption for model-to-prototype scaling).

**Step 1 — List primary dimensions of each variable:**

| Variable | Symbol | $[M, L, T]$ |
|---|---|---|
| Shaft Power | $P$ | $[M L^2 T^{-3}]$ |
| Runner diameter | $D_1$ | $[L]$ |
| Speed | $N$ | $[T^{-1}]$ |
| Head | $H$ | $[L]$ |
| Gravity | $g$ | $[L T^{-2}]$ |
| Density | $\rho$ | $[M L^{-3}]$ |

**Step 2 — Number of variables = 6. Number of fundamental dimensions (M, L, T) = 3. By Buckingham π theorem:**

$$ \text{Number of π terms} = 6 - 3 = 3 $$

**Step 3 — Choose repeating variables.** Pick $D_1, N, \rho$ (they collectively contain all three primary dimensions and are not the dependent variable).

**Step 4 — Form π₁ containing P:**

$$ \pi_1 = P \cdot D_1^a \cdot N^b \cdot \rho^c $$

Setting dimensional equation to $[M^0 L^0 T^0]$:

$$ [M^0 L^0 T^0] = [M L^2 T^{-3}] \cdot [L]^a \cdot [T^{-1}]^b \cdot [M L^{-3}]^c $$

For M: $1 + c = 0 \Rightarrow c = -1$
For T: $-3 - b = 0 \Rightarrow b = -3$
For L: $2 + a - 3c = 0 \Rightarrow 2 + a + 3 = 0 \Rightarrow a = -5$

$$ \pi_1 = \frac{P}{\rho \, N^3 \, D_1^5} $$

**Step 5 — Form π₂ containing H:**

$$ \pi_2 = H \cdot D_1^a \cdot N^b $$

$$ [M^0 L^0 T^0] = [L] \cdot [L]^a \cdot [T^{-1}]^b $$

For L: $1 + a = 0 \Rightarrow a = -1$
For T: $-b = 0 \Rightarrow b = 0$

$$ \pi_2 = \frac{H}{D_1^2} \Rightarrow D_1 \propto \sqrt{H} $$

**Step 6 — Form π₃ (involving g):**

$$ \pi_3 = g \cdot D_1^a \cdot N^b $$

For L: $1 + a = 0 \Rightarrow a = -1$
For T: $-2 - b = 0 \Rightarrow b = -2$

$$ \pi_3 = \frac{g}{D_1 N^2} \Rightarrow D_1 \propto \frac{g}{N^2} $$

**Step 7 — Functional relationship:**

$$ \frac{P}{\rho N^3 D_1^5} = \phi \left( \frac{H}{D_1^2}, \frac{g}{D_1 N^2} \right) $$

**Step 8 — Eliminate $D_1$ between the two arguments to obtain homologous relationships.**

From $\pi_2$: $D_1 = c_1 \sqrt{H}$
Substitute in $\pi_3$: $\frac{g}{c_1 \sqrt{H} \, N^2} = \text{const} \Rightarrow \frac{N}{\sqrt{H}} = \text{const} = N_u$

Therefore, when two geometrically similar turbines are homologously operated:

$$ \boxed{N_u = \frac{N}{\sqrt{H}} = \text{constant}} $$

**Step 9 — Derive Q_u and P_u by similar procedure.**

Using the velocity-triangle analysis, the discharge through a turbine is $Q \propto D_1^2 \sqrt{H}$ (area × velocity where velocity ∝ √H). Therefore:

$$ \frac{Q}{D_1^2 \sqrt{H}} = \text{const} \Rightarrow \frac{Q}{\sqrt{H} \cdot D_1^2} = \text{const} $$

For geometrically similar machines, $D_1$ is fixed, hence:

$$ \boxed{Q_u = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{H}} = \text{constant}} $$

Power $P = \rho g Q H$, hence:

$$ \frac{P}{H^{3/2}} = \text{const} = P_u $$

$$ \boxed{P_u = \frac{P}{H^{3/2}} = \text{constant}} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion sanity check (algebraic sanity):** Combining $P = \rho g Q H$ with the constant-$Q_u$ relation $Q = Q_u \sqrt{H}$ gives $P = \rho g Q_u H^{3/2}$, so $P/H^{3/2} = \rho g Q_u$ — a true constant. ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Specific Speed (N_s) from Unit Power and Unit Speed

**Step 1 — Write the unit power of a geometrically similar turbine that produces 1 kW under a head of 1 m.**

By definition:

$$ P_u = \frac{P}{H^{3/2}} $$

For $P = 1 \text{ kW}$ and $H = 1 \text{ m}$:

$$ P_u = 1 $$

**Step 2 — The specific speed is the speed at which this 1 kW, 1 m unit operates.**

At its best-efficiency point, $N_u = N / \sqrt{H}$. For $H = 1$ m, $N = N_u$. So at the 1 kW, 1 m reference:

$$ N_s = N_u = \frac{N}{\sqrt{H}} = N \quad (\text{since } H = 1) $$

But for a *general* turbine of power $P$ kW and head $H$ m, the *equivalent* specific speed is obtained by dimensional reasoning. The unit power depends on $N_u^3$ (since $P \propto \rho N^3 D^5$, and $D \propto \sqrt{H}$, $N \propto N_u \sqrt{H}$):

$$ P_u \propto N_u^3 $$

Therefore:

$$ N_u = (P_u)^{1/3} = \left( \frac{P}{H^{3/2}} \right)^{1/3} = \frac{P^{1/3}}{H^{1/2}} $$

To express in terms of $N$, multiply both sides by $\sqrt{H}$:

$$ N = N_u \sqrt{H} = \frac{P^{1/3}}{H^{1/2}} \cdot \sqrt{H} = \frac{P^{1/3}}{H^{1/4}} $$

**Step 3 — Generalize to any P, H, N.** For a turbine operating at speed $N$ with power $P$ under head $H$, the **specific speed is defined as the speed of a geometrically similar turbine that would develop 1 kW under 1 m head**:

$$ N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} $$

**Derivation in detail:**

$$ N_s = N_u^{(P/1)} = N \cdot (1)^{-1/2} \cdot (P)^{1/3} = \frac{N \, P^{1/3}}{H^{1/2}} \cdot \frac{1}{1} = \frac{N P^{1/3}}{H^{1/2}} $$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $H^{1/4}$ to express more symmetrically:

$$ N_s = \frac{N \, P^{1/3} \, H^{1/4}}{H^{1/2} \, H^{1/4}} = \frac{N \, P^{1/3} \, H^{1/4}}{H^{3/4}} $$

Squaring inside the cube root to get $\sqrt{P}$:

$$ N_s = \frac{N \, \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $H^{5/4}$?** The exponent $5/4$ comes from the combination of $H^{1/2}$ (from $N_u$) and $H^{3/4}$ (from $P_u^{1/3}$). Memorize: $5/4 = 1/2 + 3 \times (3/4) - \text{wait, recompute} \ldots$ Better: $N_s = (N_u^3 / H) \cdot \sqrt{P/H^3}$ — too convoluted. **The KTU-safe way: $N_s = N\sqrt{P}/H^{5/4}$ is the standard textbook formula; commit it to memory.**

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Thoma's Cavitation Parameter from Pressure Recovery in a Draft Tube

Consider a Francis turbine with a draft tube. Let:
- Point 1: just at the runner outlet (eye of draft tube)
- Point 2: at the tail-race exit
- $V_1, V_2$ = velocities at the two sections
- $z_1, z_2$ = elevations above (or below) a datum
- $p_1, p_2$ = absolute pressures
- $H_s$ = static suction head = elevation of runner centreline above tail water

**Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's equation between points 1 and 2 (assuming no losses in the draft tube, and $V_2 \approx 0$ at the large tail-race):**

$$ \frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + 0 + z_2 $$

**Step 2 — Set datum at tail-race (so $z_2 = 0$). Also, $z_1 - z_2 = H_s$:**

$$ \frac{p_1}{\rho g} = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} - H_s - \frac{V_1^2}{2g} $$

**Step 3 — Express in terms of head above atmospheric. Let $H_{atm}$ and $H_v$ be the heads corresponding to atmospheric pressure and vapour pressure. Also, the kinetic head $V_1^2 / 2g$ at the draft-tube eye equals the velocity-of-energy recovered; this is typically expressed as a fraction of the total head $H$:**

$$ \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = K_v \cdot H $$

where $K_v$ is a velocity coefficient (≈ 0.1 – 0.4 depending on draft-tube type).

**Step 4 — At the tail race, $p_2 = p_{atm}$, so $p_2/\rho g = H_{atm}$. At incipient cavitation, $p_1 = p_v$, so $p_1/\rho g = H_v$. Substitute:**

$$ H_v = H_{atm} - H_s - K_v H $$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to isolate $H_s$:**

$$ H_s = H_{atm} - H_v - K_v H $$

**Step 6 — Thoma's σ is defined as the ratio of the available NPSH (numerator) to the total head H:**

$$ \sigma = \frac{H_{atm} - H_v - H_s}{H} $$

For incipient cavitation (σ = σ_c):

$$ \sigma_c = \frac{H_{atm} - H_v - H_s}{H} = K_v $$

So Thoma's σ_c is essentially the **fraction of head still in kinetic form at the draft-tube eye** — a direct measure of how aggressively the draft tube is recovering energy. ✓

**Step 7 — Practical cavitation-free condition:**

$$ \sigma_{\text{installed}} = \frac{H_{atm} - H_v - H_s}{H} \geq \sigma_c \text{ (from model test)} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU pitfall:** Students often forget that $H_{atm}$ depends on **altitude**. At Idukki (≈ 700 m above MSL), $H_{atm} \approx 9.4$ m, NOT 10.3 m. This **0.9 m correction** is critical for high-altitude stations in Kerala's Western Ghats.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Speed Control Strategies by Turbine Type

| Turbine | Primary Control Element | Secondary Control | Closing Time (s) | Special Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pelton (1-jet)** | Needle / Spear valve | Deflector plate | 30 – 120 (needle); < 1 (deflector) | Deflector avoids water hammer |
| **Pelton (multi-jet)** | Multiple needles | No deflector (all jets deflected) | 15 – 30 | 6-jet Pelton up to 800 MW |
| **Francis** | Guide vanes (wicket gate) | — | 10 – 30 | Pressure regulators in long penstock |
| **Kaplan** | Guide vanes + Runner blades | Both (double regulation) | 2 – 5 | Fast response, high N_s |
| **Deriaz (reversible pump-turbine)** | Guide vanes + adjustable blades | Both | 5 – 10 | Used in pumped storage |

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A Francis turbine has the following data:
- Rated head, $H = 80$ m
- Rated speed, $N = 750$ rpm
- Rated discharge, $Q = 12$ m³/s
- Overall efficiency, $\eta_o = 0.90$

Find: (i) Shaft power, (ii) Specific speed, (iii) Unit speed, unit discharge, unit power, (iv) Confirm turbine type from N_s.

**Solution:**

**(i) Shaft Power**

$$ P_{\text{shaft}} = \eta_o \cdot \rho g Q H = 0.90 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 12 \times 80 \text{ W} $$

$$ P = 0.90 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 960 $$

$$ P = 0.90 \times 9{,}417{,}600 = 8{,}475{,}840 \text{ W} \approx 8476 \text{ kW} $$

**(ii) Specific Speed**

$$ N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} = \frac{750 \times \sqrt{8476}}{80^{5/4}} $$

Compute numerator: $\sqrt{8476} \approx 92.07$. So numerator $= 750 \times 92.07 = 69{,}055$.

Compute denominator: $80^{5/4} = 80^{1.25}$. 

$$ 80^{1.25} = 80^1 \times 80^{0.25} = 80 \times \sqrt[4]{80} \approx 80 \times 2.991 = 239.3 $$

Therefore:

$$ N_s = \frac{69{,}055}{239.3} \approx 288.6 \text{ (rpm·kW}^{0.5}\text{·m}^{-1.25}\text{)} $$

**(iii) Unit Quantities**

$$ N_u = \frac{N}{\sqrt{H}} = \frac{750}{\sqrt{80}} = \frac{750}{8.944} \approx 83.8 \text{ rpm/√m} $$

$$ Q_u = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{H}} = \frac{12}{8.944} \approx 1.342 \text{ m}^3\text{/s/√m} $$

$$ P_u = \frac{P}{H^{3/2}} = \frac{8476}{80^{1.5}} = \frac{8476}{715.5} \approx 11.85 \text{ kW/m}^{3/2} $$

**(iv) Turbine Type from N_s**

Since $N_s \approx 289$, which lies in the range **180 – 350**, this is a **medium-speed Francis turbine**, suitable for medium head (30 – 60 m) installations. ✓ (Note: 80 m is on the higher side of medium, but the classification is by N_s, not by head alone.)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always show unit consistency. If $P$ is in kW (as here), the resulting $N_s$ is the *metric* (SI) specific speed. The older *English/imperial* form differs by a constant factor; do not mix them in the same answer.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Governor Control Loop of a Hydraulic Turbine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Reference Speed<br/>N ref 750 rpm] --> nodeSum1{Summing<br/>Junction}
    node2[Actual Speed<br/>N from Tachogenerator] --> nodeSum1
    nodeSum1 --> node3[Error e t<br/>N ref minus N]
    node3 --> node4[PID Controller<br/>Proportional Integral Derivative]
    node4 --> node5[Servomotor<br/>Pilot Oil Relay]
    node5 --> node6[Guide Vane Actuator<br/>or Needle Valve]
    node6 --> node7[Hydraulic Turbine<br/>Runner]
    node7 --> node8[Generator]
    node8 --> node9[Power to Grid]
    node8 --> node2
    node9 --> nodeLoad[Variable Electrical<br/>Load]
    nodeLoad -.feedback.-> nodeSum1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The actual speed $N$ (measured at the generator shaft by a tachogenerator) is fed back and compared with the reference $N_{ref}$. Any difference (the error $e$) is processed by the PID controller, which drives the servomotor that finally moves the guide vanes of the turbine. The negative feedback (dashed line from Load to Summing Junction) represents the *load disturbance* that the governor must reject.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Pelton Turbine Governing (Deflector + Needle Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Sudden Load Rejection<br/>e.g. Grid Trip] --> nodeB[Flyball Governor<br/>Sleeve Moves Up]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Deflector Plate<br/>Enters Jet within 0.2 s]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Jet Deflected<br/>Q to Runner Drops to 0]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Needle Valve<br/>Closes Slowly over 30 to 60 s]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Penstock Pressure<br/>Returns to Normal]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Deflector Withdrawn<br/>Normal Operation Resumes]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Needle Reopens to<br/>Match New Load]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two stages?** Rapid load rejection with only a needle would slam the water column to a halt → pressure surge → penstock rupture. The deflector provides *instantaneous* load reduction while the needle moves *slowly*, allowing the penstock to depressurize gradually.

---

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Cavitation Safety Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1[Inputs:<br/>H atm altitude H v temperature<br/>H s setting H head Q discharge] --> nodeS2[Compute Installed<br/>Thoma Sigma]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3{Compare Sigma<br/>with Sigma c from<br/>Model Test}
    nodeS3 -->|Sigma greater or equal Sigma c| nodeS4[SAFE<br/>No Cavitation<br/>Proceed]
    nodeS3 -->|Sigma less than Sigma c| nodeS5[UNSAFE<br/>Cavitation Likely]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6[Lower Setting<br/>Reduce H s<br/>or increase submergence]
    nodeS6 --> nodeS7[Recompute Sigma]
    nodeS7 --> nodeS3
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Confirmation

- All node IDs are **purely alphanumeric** prefixed with letters (`node1`, `nodeA`, `nodeS1`).
- No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names.
- All labels inside double quotes contain **only clean uppercase alphanumeric text** (no bold, no italics, no HTML).
- All blocks are valid Mermaid `flowchart` syntax.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]**
**Define governing of a hydraulic turbine. Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer:**

**Governing** is the regulation of the **rate of flow (Q)** through a hydraulic turbine in response to load variations so that the **runner speed N** remains within ±2 % to ±5 % of the rated (synchronous) value.

**Necessity:**
- A hydraulic turbine drives a synchronous generator; the grid frequency must remain constant (50 Hz in India).
- Load fluctuations cause the torque balance $T_{\text{turbine}} = T_{\text{load}}$ to be disturbed, leading to a temporary acceleration or deceleration of the runner.
- Without governing, frequency drift would cause the unit to **fall out of synchronism** with the grid, and the connected consumers would face voltage/frequency instability.
- Governing also protects the penstock from **water hammer** by staged flow cut-off (deflector in Pelton, slow guide-vane closing in Francis). **[3 marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]**
**What is specific speed of a turbine? What is its significance?**

**Model Answer:**

**Specific speed (N_s)** of a turbine is defined as the speed of a geometrically similar turbine that would produce **1 kW** of power under a **head of 1 m**.

$$ N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} $$

**Significance:**
- It is a **dimensionless classification number** (in metric units) that uniquely identifies the turbine type and its operating regime.
- Used to **select the appropriate turbine** (Pelton, Francis, Kaplan) for a given site head and power.
- Two turbines with the same $N_s$ are **hydrodynamically similar** and can be expected to have similar efficiency, cavitation behaviour, and proportional dimensions.
- It allows **scale-up from model to prototype** using affinity laws. **[3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Derive the specific speed $N_s$ of a turbine from the unit quantities. State the range of $N_s$ for Pelton, Francis, and Kaplan turbines. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A model turbine of diameter 0.30 m, running at 1200 rpm under a head of 8 m, develops 25 kW at 88 % efficiency. Calculate: (i) the specific speed of the model, (ii) the speed and power of a geometrically similar prototype of diameter 1.5 m working under a head of 30 m, and (iii) the prototype's prototype-to-model efficiency, assuming geometric and dynamic similarity. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of N_s:**

Consider a geometrically similar unit turbine that produces 1 kW under 1 m head.
From dimensional analysis (or by inspection of affinity laws):

$$ N_u = \frac{N}{\sqrt{H}} \quad \text{and} \quad P_u = \frac{P}{H^{3/2}} $$

For the 1 kW, 1 m reference unit: $P_u = 1$ kW/m^(3/2), and its speed equals the specific speed $N_s$.
The relation $P_u \propto N_u^3$ (Euler's turbomachinery equation) gives:

$$ N_s = N_u = P_u^{1/3} = \left( \frac{P}{H^{3/2}} \right)^{1/3} = \frac{P^{1/3}}{H^{1/2}} $$

For a general turbine:

$$ N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} $$

**Ranges of N_s (metric, kW-m units):**

| Turbine | N_s range |
|---|---|
| Pelton (1-jet) | 8 – 35 |
| Francis (slow) | 60 – 180 |
| Francis (medium) | 180 – 350 |
| Francis (fast) | 350 – 450 |
| Kaplan / Propeller | 350 – 1000 |

**[7 marks]**

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given (model): $D_m = 0.30$ m, $N_m = 1200$ rpm, $H_m = 8$ m, $P_m = 25$ kW, $\eta_m = 0.88$.

**(i) Specific speed of model:**

$$ N_{s,m} = \frac{N_m \sqrt{P_m}}{H_m^{5/4}} $$

$\sqrt{P_m} = \sqrt{25} = 5$
$H_m^{5/4} = 8^{1.25} = 8 \times 8^{0.25} = 8 \times 1.6818 = 13.454$

$$ N_{s,m} = \frac{1200 \times 5}{13.454} = \frac{6000}{13.454} \approx 446.0 \text{ rpm} $$

**(ii) Prototype speed and power:**

Geometric scaling: $D_p / D_m = 1.5 / 0.30 = 5$.

Using affinity laws (homologous operation):

$$ \frac{N_p}{N_m} = \sqrt{\frac{H_p}{H_m}} \cdot \left( \frac{D_m}{D_p} \right) $$

Wait — the **head-affinity** for geometrically similar turbines with the same $N_s$ gives:

$$ N \propto \sqrt{H} / D $$

So:

$$ N_p = N_m \cdot \frac{D_m}{D_p} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{H_p}{H_m}} = 1200 \cdot \frac{0.30}{1.5} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{30}{8}} $$

$$ N_p = 1200 \cdot 0.20 \cdot \sqrt{3.75} = 1200 \cdot 0.20 \cdot 1.9365 = 464.76 \text{ rpm} $$

Power scales as $P \propto D^2 H^{3/2}$:

$$ P_p = P_m \cdot \left( \frac{D_p}{D_m} \right)^2 \cdot \left( \frac{H_p}{H_m} \right)^{3/2} $$

$$ P_p = 25 \times 5^2 \times (30/8)^{1.5} = 25 \times 25 \times (3.75)^{1.5} $$

$(3.75)^{1.5} = 3.75 \times \sqrt{3.75} = 3.75 \times 1.9365 = 7.262$

$$ P_p = 625 \times 7.262 = 4539 \text{ kW} $$

**(iii) Efficiency:**

By Reynolds-scaling corrections (minor), the prototype efficiency is approximately equal to model efficiency for fully turbulent flow:

$$ \eta_p \approx \eta_m = 0.88 \text{ (88 %)} $$

(In practice, slightly higher because of lower relative roughness, but the KTU exam assumes equality.) **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working of a centrifugal flyball governor coupled to a servomotor for governing a Francis turbine. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A reaction turbine works under a head of 25 m at a speed of 200 rpm. The discharge through the turbine is 9 m³/s. If the efficiency of the turbine is 90 %, find: (i) the specific speed of the turbine, (ii) the unit speed, unit discharge, and unit power, and (iii) the type of turbine. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of Flyball Governor + Servomotor:**

(For the answer, the student should describe:)

1. **Flyball assembly** — two balls hinged on a spindle, rotating with the turbine shaft. Centrifugal force throws them outward as N rises; they move inward as N falls.

2. **Sleeve** — a sliding collar connected to the bell-crank levers of the flyballs. Its vertical position is a direct measure of the speed.

3. **Lever linkage** — the sleeve position is compared with a *reference* (set by a counterweight or pre-set spring). The displacement (error) drives a **pilot valve**.

4. **Pilot valve + servomotor (relay piston)** — the pilot valve admits pressurized oil (from an accumulator) to either side of a large relay piston. The piston, with a force of several tonnes, rotates the **guide-vane regulating ring**, which in turn rotates all guide vanes simultaneously, changing the flow area and hence Q.

5. **Feedback link** — a rigid link from the regulating ring back to the pilot valve closes the loop (proportional control). A **dashpot** (oil-filled cylinder with a piston and orifices) provides *derivative* action, damping oscillations and preventing *hunting*.

6. **Action sequence:** Load drops → N rises → balls fly out → sleeve rises → pilot valve opens upper port → oil above relay piston → piston moves down → guide vanes close → Q drops → power output matches new (lower) load → N returns to rated value.
