(For full marks, include a sketch showing flyballs, sleeve, pilot valve, relay piston, dashpot, and guide-vane ring — text description with logical flow is acceptable if a sketch is unrenderable.) **[7 marks]**

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $H = 25$ m, $N = 200$ rpm, $Q = 9$ m³/s, $\eta = 0.90$.

**Shaft power:**

$$ P = \eta \rho g Q H = 0.90 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 9 \times 25 $$

$$ P = 0.90 \times 2{,}207{,}250 = 1{,}986{,}525 \text{ W} \approx 1987 \text{ kW} $$

**(i) Specific speed:**

$$ N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} = \frac{200 \times \sqrt{1987}}{25^{1.25}} $$

$\sqrt{1987} \approx 44.58$
$25^{1.25} = 25 \times 25^{0.25} = 25 \times 2.2361 = 55.90$

$$ N_s = \frac{200 \times 44.58}{55.90} = \frac{8916}{55.90} \approx 159.5 \text{ rpm} $$

**(ii) Unit quantities:**

$$ N_u = \frac{N}{\sqrt{H}} = \frac{200}{\sqrt{25}} = \frac{200}{5} = 40 \text{ rpm/√m} $$

$$ Q_u = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{H}} = \frac{9}{5} = 1.80 \text{ m}^3\text{/s/√m} $$

$$ P_u = \frac{P}{H^{3/2}} = \frac{1987}{25^{1.5}} = \frac{1987}{125} = 15.90 \text{ kW/m}^{3/2} $$

**(iii) Turbine type:**

Since $N_s \approx 159.5$, which lies in the range **60 – 180**, the turbine is a **slow Francis turbine** (suitable for high heads of 60 – 250 m, though here the head is only 25 m, suggesting it's a smaller unit for a low-head site — but the N_s classification says Francis). **[7 marks]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in Governing of Turbines:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $N_s$ ranges.** Memorize: Pelton 8–35, Francis 60–450, Kaplan 350–1000. Do not swap ranges between Francis-slow and Francis-fast.
> 
> 2. **Using the wrong $H$ exponent.** Specific speed is $H^{5/4}$, not $H^{3/2}$ and not $H^{5/2}$. Mix-ups cost 2 marks instantly.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting unit consistency.** $P$ must be in **kW** (not W) when using the standard $N_s$ formula. If you use $P$ in W, the answer is wrong by $\sqrt{1000} \approx 31.6$.
> 
> 4. **Mixing metric and imperial N_s.** Metric $N_s = N\sqrt{P_{kW}} / H_m^{5/4}$. Imperial (US) $N_s = N\sqrt{P_{HP}} / H_{ft}^{5/4}$. They are NOT equal. KTU uses **metric** exclusively.
> 
> 5. **In Thoma's σ calculation**, students often use $H_{atm} = 10.3$ m for all stations. For **Idukki (700 m altitude)** use $H_{atm} \approx 9.4$ m. The 0.9 m difference can flip a design from "safe" to "cavitating".
> 
> 6. **Pelton governing sequence.** Always mention *deflector first, needle second*. If you only mention the needle, you lose 2 marks.
> 
> 7. **Cavitation question:** Many students write $H_s$ as positive when the turbine is set **below** tail water (Kaplan case). The sign convention is: $H_s = z_{\text{tail}} - z_{\text{runner}}$. Positive if runner is above tail water.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Governing** = flow regulation to keep speed stable; carried out by a **flyball governor + servomotor**.
- [ ] **Pelton governing:** **Deflector + needle** (deflector is fast, needle is slow) → avoids water hammer.
- [ ] **Francis governing:** **Guide vanes (wicket gate)** rotate to throttle flow.
- [ ] **Kaplan governing:** **Double regulation** — guide vanes AND runner blades rotate.
- [ ] **Unit speed:** $N_u = N / \sqrt{H}$.
- [ ] **Unit discharge:** $Q_u = Q / \sqrt{H}$.
- [ ] **Unit power:** $P_u = P / H^{3/2}$.
- [ ] **Specific speed:** $N_s = N \sqrt{P} / H^{5/4}$ (with $P$ in kW, $H$ in m, $N$ in rpm).
- [ ] **N_s ranges:** Pelton 8–35 | Francis 60–450 | Kaplan 350–1000.
- [ ] **Main characteristic** = constant H, plotted vs P. **Operating characteristic** = constant N. **Muschel curves** = constant-η loci.
- [ ] **Thoma's σ** = $(H_{atm} - H_v - H_s)/H$. Safe if $\sigma \geq \sigma_c$.
- [ ] Cavitation in Kaplan → set **below tail water** (negative $H_s$).
- [ ] Atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude — Idukki ≈ 9.4 m, not 10.3 m.
- [ ] Vapour pressure head at 25 °C ≈ 0.24 m; at 50 °C ≈ 1.25 m — affects σ by up to 1 m.
- [ ] **Synchronous speed:** $N = 120f / p$ (50 Hz grid, p = pole pairs).
- [ ] **Hydraulic power:** $P = \rho g Q H$ in W; divide by 1000 for kW.
- [ ] **Overall efficiency** $\eta_o = P_{\text{shaft}} / (\rho g Q H)$ — typical 0.85–0.92.
- [ ] **Hydraulic similarity** requires same $K_u, K_f, K_p$ and Reynolds independence (auto-satisfied for $Re > 10^5$).
- [ ] **Setting** of a turbine = elevation of runner centreline with respect to tail water level.
- [ ] **Affinity laws** (model ↔ prototype): $N \propto \sqrt{H}/D$, $Q \propto \sqrt{H} D^2$, $P \propto H^{3/2} D^2$.
- [ ] **Synchronous machine** is **rigidly tied** to grid frequency; hence N of turbine must match $N_s$ of generator.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Draft Tubes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Draft Tubes ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Draft Tube:** A gradually expanding tube (diffuser) attached to the outlet of a reaction turbine (Francis or Kaplan type) that connects the runner exit to the tailrace. Its primary purpose is to (i) convert the **kinetic energy** of water leaving the runner into **pressure energy**, and (ii) create a suction (negative pressure) at the runner outlet so that the turbine may be installed above the tailrace water level. In KTU terminology, the draft tube is referred to as the "tail race tube" or "diffuser tube" of a reaction turbine. It is an essential accessory of every reaction turbine because, without it, a substantial portion (often **10%–20%**) of the gross head would be lost as kinetic energy at the runner exit. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Straw in a Water Tank" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Drinking Straw Effect:** > Imagine drinking a milkshake through a long straw. To make the liquid rise, you create a slight vacuum in your mouth (suction). The taller the straw (greater suction lift), the easier it is to lift the liquid — provided you don't lose too much energy along the length of the straw (friction). > > - The **straw** = the draft tube. > - The **vacuum in your mouth** = the negative pressure (below atmospheric) at the inlet of the draft tube. > - **Friction along the straw wall** = the head loss ($h_f$) inside the draft tube. > - The **exit into the glass** = the tailrace at atmospheric pressure. Just as the straw uses suction to lift fluid, the draft tube uses suction to enable the turbine runner to sit comfortably above the tailrace — saving huge civil engineering excavation costs. ### 1.3 Functions of the Draft Tube > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Five Key Functions of a Draft Tube:** > 1. **Converts kinetic head to pressure head:** $V_1^2 / 2g$ at runner exit is largely recovered as pressure head at tailrace. > 2. **Permits high setting of the runner:** The turbine can be placed **above** the tailrace, reducing civil construction costs. > 3. **Increases the net (effective) head:** The negative pressure (suction) at the runner exit effectively adds a suction head $H_s$ to the gross head $H$. > 4. **Decelerates the flow:** Water leaves the draft tube with a small velocity $V_2$, hence very low kinetic energy is wasted. > 5. **Prevents/reduces cavitation risk:** By maintaining a controlled (but limited) vacuum at the runner outlet. ### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Static suction head | $H_s$ | 1.5 to 6.0 | **m** | | Velocity head at runner exit | $V_1^2 / 2g$ | 1.0 to 8.0 | **m** | | Pressure head (vacuum) at inlet | $-H_s - V_1^2/2g + h_f$ | -1 to -10 | **m of water** | | Permissible absolute pressure | $p_{abs}$ | $\geq 0.2 \times 10^5$ Pa | **Pa** | | Limiting vacuum (water at 20 °C) | $H_{vac, max}$ | 7.6 to **10.3** | **m of water** | > [!NOTE] > The theoretical maximum vacuum is **10.3 m of water** (corresponding to atmospheric pressure at sea level). In practice, KTU/Kerala installations limit it to **7.0–8.5 m** to avoid cavitation. ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Energy Flow Consider a reaction turbine installation where: - **Point 0** = Free surface of head race (reservoir) — at atmospheric pressure, $V_0 \approx 0$, elevation $H$ above tailrace. - **Point 1** = Inlet of the turbine (penstock exit / guide vane entry). - **Point 2** = Outlet of runner / **inlet of draft tube** (this is the *critical* point of analysis). - **Point 3** = Outlet of draft tube (tailrace water surface) — atmospheric pressure, low velocity. **The energy-flow logic is as follows:** - At the runner exit (point 2), water possesses substantial **kinetic energy** $V_2^2/2g$ but very low (often *negative*) pressure energy. - If this water were discharged directly into the tailrace, the entire kinetic energy $V_2^2/2g$ would be **wasted**. - The draft tube acts as a **diverging diffuser**: it gradually decelerates the water, converting its kinetic head into pressure head. - At the draft tube exit (point 3), velocity $V_3$ is small (ideally $\approx 0$), and pressure is atmospheric. - During this deceleration, the **pressure at the inlet of the draft tube drops below atmospheric**, creating a suction effect that effectively increases the net head available to the turbine. > [!TIP] > **Why diverging (not converging) tube?** A converging nozzle would *accelerate* the flow, lowering pressure further. A diverging diffuser *decelerates* flow, raising pressure — but pressure is still below atmospheric at the inlet because of the elevation $H_s$ of the runner above the tailrace. ### 2.2 Bernoulli's Equation Across the Draft Tube Applying Bernoulli's equation between point 2 (inlet) and point 3 (outlet), with the tailrace as datum: $$\frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + H_s = \frac{p_3}{\rho g} + \frac{V_3^2}{2g} + 0 + h_f$$ Where: - $p_3 / \rho g = 0$ (atmospheric, gauge pressure) - $V_3^2 / 2g \approx 0$ (negligibly small in a well-designed draft tube) - $H_s$ = static suction head (vertical distance of runner exit above tailrace) - $h_f$ = head loss due to friction inside the draft tube Solving for $p_2/\rho g$: $$\frac{p_2}{\rho g} = -H_s - \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_f$$ ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates **every formula** that has appeared in KTU past examinations on draft tubes. Memorise them thoroughly. | # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\dfrac{p_2}{\rho g} = -H_s - \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_f$ | Pressure head (gauge) at inlet of draft tube | **m of water** | | 2 | $V_{vacuum} = H_s + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} - h_f$ | Vacuum pressure head at runner exit | **m of water** | | 3 | $H_{net} = H_g - h_f$ (penstock) $-\ h_f$ (draft tube) | Net head available to turbine | **m** | | 4 | $\eta_d = \dfrac{V_2^2/2g - h_f}{V_2^2/2g}$ | Draft tube efficiency (ratio of recovered KE to supplied KE) | dimensionless | | 5 | $\eta_d = 1 - \dfrac{h_f}{V_2^2/2g}$ | Alternative form of draft tube efficiency | dimensionless | | 6 | $\eta_d \approx \dfrac{1}{1 + \tan(\alpha/2)}$ *(conical only)* | Efficiency of conical draft tube (semi-vertical angle $\alpha$) | dimensionless | | 7 | $V_2 / V_3 = (D_3 / D_2)^2$ | Continuity equation for conical tube (inlet $D_2$, outlet $D_3$) | dimensionless | | 8 | $L = \dfrac{D_3 - D_2}{2 \tan \alpha}$ | Length of conical draft tube | **m** | | 9 | $H_{vac, max} \le 7.6$ to **10.3 m** | Limiting vacuum (depends on temperature & altitude) | **m of water** | | 10 | $\eta_o = \eta_h \times \eta_m \times \eta_d$ | Overall efficiency = product of hydraulic, mechanical & draft tube | dimensionless | ### 2.4 Draft Tube Efficiency — Physical Interpretation The draft tube converts a portion of the **kinetic energy at the runner exit** into useful **pressure energy**. This recovered pressure energy can be interpreted in two equivalent ways: - **As kinetic energy recovery** (definition preferred by most KTU papers): $$\eta_d = \frac{\text{Kinetic head recovered}}{\text{Kinetic head supplied at runner exit}} = \frac{V_2^2/2g - h_f}{V_2^2/2g}$$ - **As pressure-head recovery** (alternative textbook formulation): $$\eta_d = \frac{H_s - h_f}{H_s + V_2^2/2g}$$ > [!TIP] > In KTU numerical problems, the **first definition** ($\eta_d = 1 - h_f/(V_2^2/2g)$) is more commonly tested. Always state the definition explicitly to earn full marks. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Draft tubes are critical in: - **Hydropower generation** (Francis, Kaplan, Bulb turbines) — every reaction turbine has one. - **Pumped-storage hydroelectric plants** — draft tubes handle reverse flow during pumping. - **Tidal energy converters** — submerged turbines use similar diffuser concepts. - **Industrial pump sumps** — engineering principle applied to diffuser design. In the Indian context, **major KTU-aligned Kerala hydropower plants** such as Idukki, Pallivasal, and Panniyar rely heavily on draft tubes, and students are expected to appreciate their role in reducing the cost-per-MW of installed capacity. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure at the Inlet of the Draft Tube **Given:** - Static suction head = $H_s$ - Velocity of water at runner exit = $V_2$ - Friction head loss inside draft tube = $h_f$ - Tailrace = atmospheric pressure datum **Goal:** Derive an expression for $p_2 / \rho g$ (the pressure head at inlet of draft tube). **Step 1 — Choose the datum:** Take the tailrace water surface (point 3) as the reference datum, so $z_3 = 0$. The runner exit (point 2) is at a height $H_s$ above the tailrace: $$z_2 = H_s$$ **Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's equation between point 2 and point 3:** $$\frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 = \frac{p_3}{\rho g} + \frac{V_3^2}{2g} + z_3 + h_f$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the known boundary conditions:** $$\frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + H_s = 0 + 0 + 0 + h_f$$ > Note: $p_3 / \rho g = 0$ (atmospheric, gauge pressure), $V_3 \approx 0$ (tailrace is large), $z_3 = 0$. **Step 4 — Rearrange to solve for $p_2 / \rho g$:** $$\frac{p_2}{\rho g} = -H_s - \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_f$$ **Step 5 — Express the vacuum (sub-atmospheric) pressure head:** The **vacuum pressure head** is the magnitude of the negative gauge pressure: $$H_{vac} = - \frac{p_2}{\rho g} = H_s + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} - h_f$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Result:** This negative pressure (vacuum) at the runner exit is what creates the suction effect, effectively *increasing* the net head available to the turbine by an amount equal to $H_s + V_2^2/2g - h_f$. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Draft Tube Efficiency (Conical Type) **Given:** - Conical draft tube with semi-vertical angle $\alpha$ - Inlet diameter $D_2$, outlet diameter $D_3$ ($D_3 > D_2$) - Inlet velocity $V_2$, outlet velocity $V_3$ - Total length of cone = $L$ **Step 1 — Continuity (mass conservation):** $$A_2 V_2 = A_3 V_3$$ $$\frac{\pi D_2^2}{4} V_2 = \frac{\pi D_3^2}{4} V_3$$ $$V_3 = V_2 \left( \frac{D_2}{D_3} \right)^2$$ **Step 2 — Geometric relation for length of cone:** $$\tan \alpha = \frac{(D_3 - D_2)/2}{L}$$ $$L = \frac{D_3 - D_2}{2 \tan \alpha}$$ **Step 3 — Apply momentum equation to a control volume of the cone:** For steady flow in a horizontal cone (simplified, friction neglected for the *ideal* draft tube), the pressure rise equals the change in momentum: $$(p_3 - p_2) A_{avg} = \rho Q (V_2 - V_3)$$ Where $Q$ is the discharge. **Step 4 — Define draft tube efficiency:** The draft tube recovers a fraction of the kinetic energy as pressure energy: $$\eta_d = \frac{\text{Pressure head recovered}}{\text{Kinetic head supplied}} = \frac{(p_3 - p_2)/\rho g}{V_2^2/2g}$$ For an ideal conical draft tube with no losses, the theoretical maximum efficiency reduces to: $$\eta_d = \frac{1}{1 + \tan(\alpha/2)}$$ **Step 5 — Maximise efficiency:** To obtain maximum $\eta_d$, we differentiate with respect to $\alpha$ and set to zero. The maximum occurs at: $$\alpha_{optimum} \approx 4^\circ \text{ to } 6^\circ$$ This explains why **practical conical draft tubes are designed with a half-angle of about 4° to 6°** (some references cite up to 8°) to maximise efficiency while keeping the length reasonable. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** For an ideal conical draft tube, $\eta_d$ depends only on the semi-vertical angle $\alpha$. For $\alpha = 4^\circ$, $\eta_d \approx 96.5\%$; for $\alpha = 6^\circ$, $\eta_d \approx 94.7\%$. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Net Head with vs. without Draft Tube **Case A — Without draft tube (runner at tailrace level):** The water exits the runner directly into the tailrace with velocity $V_2$. The kinetic energy is wasted: $$H_{net, \text{no draft}} = H - h_{f, \text{penstock}} - \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$ **Case B — With draft tube (runner raised by $H_s$):** The draft tube recovers most of $V_2^2/2g$, but at the cost of friction $h_f$ inside the draft tube: $$H_{net, \text{draft}} = H - h_{f, \text{penstock}} - h_{f, \text{draft tube}}$$ **Comparison — Head gained by using draft tube:** $$\Delta H = H_{net, \text{draft}} - H_{net, \text{no draft}} = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} - h_{f, \text{draft tube}}$$ If $\Delta H > 0$ (i.e., $V_2^2/2g > h_{f, \text{draft tube}}$), then **the draft tube increases the net head**, in addition to allowing the runner to be installed above the tailrace. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU Style) **Problem:** A Francis turbine operates under a static suction head of $H_s = 4.5 \text{ m}$. The velocity of water at the runner exit is $V_2 = 8.0 \text{ m/s}$. The head loss in the draft tube is $h_f = 0.8 \text{ m}$. Calculate: **(a)** The pressure head at the inlet of the draft tube (in metres of water, gauge). **(b)** The draft tube efficiency. **(c)** The net increase in head over the no-draft-tube case. **Solution:** **(a) Pressure head at inlet of draft tube:** $$\frac{p_2}{\rho g} = -H_s - \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_f$$ Compute the velocity head: $$\frac{V_2^2}{2g} = \frac{(8.0)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{64.0}{19.62} = 3.262 \text{ m}$$ Substitute: $$\frac{p_2}{\rho g} = -4.5 - 3.262 + 0.8 = -6.962 \text{ m of water}$$ > **Result:** The pressure at the inlet of the draft tube is **-6.962 m of water** (gauge), i.e., a vacuum of about **6.96 m**. Since this is well below the practical limit of 7.6 m, the design is acceptable. **(b) Draft tube efficiency:** $$\eta_d = 1 - \frac{h_f}{V_2^2/2g} = 1 - \frac{0.8}{3.262} = 1 - 0.2452 = 0.7548 \approx 75.5\%$$ **(c) Net head gain over no-draft-tube case:** $$\Delta H = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} - h_f = 3.262 - 0.8 = 2.462 \text{ m}$$ > **Interpretation:** Installing the draft tube recovers **2.462 m of head** that would otherwise have been lost as kinetic energy in the tailrace. For a $500 \text{ kW}$ turbine with a discharge of, say, $Q = 2.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, this corresponds to a power gain of: > $$\Delta P = \rho g Q \Delta H = 9810 \times 2.5 \times 2.462 \approx 60.4 \text{ kW}$$ > — a substantial economic benefit. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Types of Draft Tubes | Feature | Conical | Conical + Sudden Enlargement | Elbow (Moody) | |---|---|---|---| | **Geometry** | Plain diverging cone | Cone followed by a sudden step | Cone + 90° bend | | **Half-angle** | 4°–8° | Cone: 4°–6°; step: any | Cone: small; bend: 90° | | **Length** | Limited (excavation) | Longer | Compact | | **Efficiency** | 85–92% (highest) | 75–85% | 70–80% | | **Application** | High-head Francis turbines | Medium-head | Kaplan / low-head | | **Civil cost** | High | Moderate | Low | | **Cavitation risk** | Lowest | Moderate | Higher (due to bend) | | **KTU exam weight** | **Highest** | Moderate | Moderate | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "discuss types of draft tubes", always draw a *neat labelled sketch*, list *at least 3 types*, and comment on their **relative efficiency and typical applications**. Students who omit the sketch lose 1–2 marks easily. ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Schematic — Reaction Turbine with Draft Tube (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Head Race / Reservoir
Atmospheric Pressure, Elevation H] --> B[Penstock
Head Loss h_f1] B --> C[Guide Vanes / Spiral Casing
Distributes flow tangentially] C --> D[Runner of Reaction Turbine
Extracts Mechanical Energy] D --> E[Inlet of Draft Tube
Point 2: Vacuum Pressure] E --> F[Diverging Diffuser Section
Converts KE to Pressure] F --> G[Outlet of Draft Tube
Point 3: Atmospheric, Low Velocity] G --> H[Tail Race
Atmospheric, z = 0 Datum] style A fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#f4d6d6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#ffe2b3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Energy flows top-to-bottom. The colour-coded nodes highlight (a) **blue** = atmospheric pressure points, (b) **red** = the turbine where mechanical work is extracted, (c) **orange** = the critical *vacuum* point at draft-tube inlet, and (d) **green** = the *diffuser* where KE → PE conversion occurs. --- ### 4.2 Schematic — Detailed Conical Draft Tube Geometry ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Point 2: Runner Exit
Diameter D2, Velocity V2
Pressure p2 negative
Elevation above tailrace = Hs] --> CONE[Conical Diffuser
Half-angle alpha
Length L] CONE --> P2[Point 3: Tailrace
Diameter D3, Velocity V3 approx 0
Pressure atmospheric
Datum Elevation] style P1 fill:#ffe2b3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style CONE fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P2 fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ### 4.3 Functional Flow — Three Standard Draft-Tube Types ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Runner Exit
Vacuum Pressure] --> B{Draft Tube Type?} B -->|Type 1| C[Conical
Smooth Divergence
Alpha = 4 to 8 deg] B -->|Type 2| D[Conical plus Sudden Enlargement
Cone then Step] B -->|Type 3| E[Elbow Type Moody
Cone plus 90 deg Bend] C --> F[Tailrace
Atmospheric] D --> F E --> F style A fill:#ffe2b3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ### 4.4 Energy / Pressure Profile Along the Draft Tube ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Runner Exit
p2 rho g approx minus 7 m
V2 square over 2g approx 3 m
z2 equals Hs approx 4.5 m] --> S2[Mid Cone
Pressure rising
Velocity falling] S2 --> S3[Tailrace Exit
p3 equals 0
V3 approx 0
z3 equals 0] S1 -.- NOTE1[Total energy decreases
due to friction h_f] S2 -.- NOTE2[Pressure head recovers
from negative to atmospheric] S3 -.- NOTE3[Kinetic head dissipated
as draft tube recovers it] style S1 fill:#ffe2b3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style S2 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style S3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!TIP] > **How to read this energy profile:** As water flows from **runner exit (S1)** to **tailrace (S3)**, its **kinetic energy decreases** (good — recovered as pressure), its **pressure rises** from a vacuum to atmospheric, and a small amount of total energy is lost as friction $h_f$. ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]** **Define a draft tube. State its functions.** **Model Answer:** A **draft tube** is a gradually expanding tube (diffuser) fitted at the outlet of a reaction turbine that connects the runner exit to the tailrace. **Functions of a draft tube:** 1. It converts the **kinetic energy** of water leaving the runner into **pressure energy**, which would otherwise be wasted. 2. It creates a **vacuum (suction)** at the runner exit, enabling the turbine to be installed **above** the tailrace water level. 3. It effectively **increases the net head** available to the turbine by an amount equal to $(V_2^2/2g - h_f)$. 4. It **reduces the velocity** of water discharged into the tailrace, minimising tailrace erosion. 5. It helps in **reducing cavitation** risk by controlling the magnitude of vacuum at the runner outlet. > [!TIP] > **Examiner's tip:** Always write *at least 4 functions*. Mentioning only 2 will fetch partial credit only. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]** **List any three types of draft tubes used in reaction turbines, with one specific application of each.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Conical (or Conical-finned) draft tube** — Used in **high-head Francis turbines**. Highest efficiency (≈ 90%), but requires long length. 2. **Conical with sudden enlargement (Hydraulic draft tube)** — Used in **medium-head installations** where excavation cost limits the use of a long cone. 3. **Elbow-type (Moody spreading) draft tube** — Used in **Kaplan turbines** where horizontal outlet and compact design are required. > [!NOTE] > Bonus mark is often awarded if you mention the **hydraulic draft tube** (conical + sudden enlargement), as many students forget it. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]* **(a)** Derive an expression for the **pressure at the inlet of the draft tube** of a reaction turbine, explaining each step clearly. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A Francis turbine has a **static suction head** of $3.6 \text{ m}$ and the velocity of water at the **runner exit is $7.5 \text{ m/s}$**. The head loss in the draft tube is **0.65 m of water**. Determine: - (i) The pressure head at the inlet of the draft tube. - (ii) The vacuum pressure head at the runner exit. - (iii) The **draft tube efficiency**. **[7 Marks]** --- #### **Solution to Q.A (a) — Derivation of Pressure at Inlet of Draft Tube** Let point 2 = inlet of draft tube (just below the runner exit) and point 3 = outlet of draft tube (tailrace surface). Take the tailrace as datum. Applying **Bernoulli's equation** between points 2 and 3: $$\frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 = \frac{p_3}{\rho g} + \frac{V_3^2}{2g} + z_3 + h_f$$ **Boundary conditions:** - $p_3 / \rho g = 0$ (atmospheric, gauge) - $V_3 \approx 0$ (tailrace is large; velocity head negligible) - $z_3 = 0$ (chosen as datum) - $z_2 = H_s$ (height of runner exit above tailrace = static suction head) Substituting: $$\frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + H_s = 0 + 0 + 0 + h_f$$ Rearranging: $$\frac{p_2}{\rho g} = -H_s - \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_f$$ The **vacuum head** at the runner exit is: $$H_{vac} = - \frac{p_2}{\rho g} = H_s + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} - h_f$$ **Conclusion:** The pressure at the inlet of the draft tube is **below atmospheric**, confirming that a vacuum (suction) exists, which is essential for the draft tube's operation. --- #### **Solution to Q.A (b) — Numerical Calculation** **Given:** - $H_s = 3.6 \text{ m}$ - $V_2 = 7.5 \text{ m/s}$ - $h_f = 0.65 \text{ m}$ - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ **Step 1 — Velocity head at runner exit:** $$\frac{V_2^2}{2g} = \frac{(7.5)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{56.25}{19.62} = 2.867 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2 — (i) Pressure head at inlet of draft tube:** $$\frac{p_2}{\rho g} = -H_s - \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_f$$ $$\frac{p_2}{\rho g} = -3.6 - 2.867 + 0.65 = -5.817 \text{ m of water}$$ > **Result:** $p_2/\rho g = -5.82 \text{ m}$ (gauge). **Step 3 — (ii) Vacuum pressure head:** $$H_{vac} = H_s + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} - h_f = 3.6 + 2.867 - 0.65 = 5.817 \text{ m of water}$$ **Step 4 — (iii) Draft tube efficiency:** $$\eta_d = 1 - \frac{h_f}{V_2^2/2g} = 1 - \frac{0.65}{2.867} = 1 - 0.2267 = 0.7733$$ $$\boxed{\eta_d \approx 77.33\%}$$ > **Interpretation:** The draft tube recovers about **77% of the kinetic energy** at the runner exit. Since this is within the typical design range (70–90%), the design is satisfactory. The vacuum head of 5.82 m is well below the safe limit of 7.6 m, so cavitation risk is acceptable. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE* **(a)** Explain the **classification of draft tubes** with neat sketches, highlighting their **relative efficiencies** and **typical applications**. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A Kaplan turbine operates with a **conical draft tube** having an inlet diameter of **1.2 m** and outlet diameter of **2.4 m**. The velocity of water at the **inlet is 9.0 m/s**. The head loss in the draft tube is **1.5 m of water**. Find: - (i) The velocity of water at the **outlet** of the draft tube. - (ii) The **draft tube efficiency**. - (iii) The **pressure rise** in metres of water across the draft tube. **[7 Marks]** --- #### **Solution to Q.B (a) — Classification of Draft Tubes** Draft tubes are broadly classified into **three types**: **1. Conical (Simple Conical) Draft Tube:** - A simple diverging cone of circular cross-section. - The half-angle is kept between **4° and 8°** to avoid flow separation. - Length is determined by $L = (D_3 - D_2)/(2 \tan \alpha)$. - **Efficiency:** Highest, typically **85–92%**. - **Application:** High-head Francis turbines. - **Drawback:** Long length implies heavy civil engineering cost for excavation. **2. Conical with Sudden Enlargement (Hydraulic Draft Tube):** - Consists of a short conical section followed by a sudden (step) enlargement to a larger cross-section. - The sudden enlargement dissipates excess kinetic energy via **turbulent eddies**. - **Efficiency:** Moderate, **75–85%**. - **Application:** Medium-head installations where a long conical tube is impractical. - **Drawback:** Energy is lost in the sudden expansion. **3. Elbow Type (Moody Spreading) Draft Tube:** - A short cone followed by a 90° bend that discharges horizontally into the tailrace. - Used where vertical space is limited. - **Efficiency:** Lower, **70–80%**. - **Application:** Kaplan and Propeller turbines (low-head, large-discharge installations). - **Drawback:** Higher friction in the bend; more prone to cavitation. > **Comparison summary:** Conical > Hydraulic > Elbow (in terms of efficiency). The selection depends on the head, discharge, civil cost, and the type of turbine. --- #### **Solution to Q.B (b) — Numerical Calculation** **Given:** - $D_2 = 1.2 \text{ m}$ (inlet diameter) - $D_3 = 2.4 \text{ m}$ (outlet diameter) - $V_2 = 9.0 \text{ m/s}$ - $h_f = 1.5 \text{ m}$ - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ **Step 1 — (i) Velocity at outlet of draft tube (Continuity):** $$V_3 = V_2 \left( \frac{D_2}{D_3} \right)^2 = 9.0 \times \left( \frac{1.2}{2.4} \right)^2$$ $$V_3 = 9.0 \times \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^2 = 9.0 \times 0.25 = 2.25 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Velocity head at inlet:** $$\frac{V_2^2}{2g} = \frac{(9.0)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{81}{19.62} = 4.128 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Velocity head at outlet:** $$\frac{V_3^2}{2g} = \frac{(2.25)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{5.0625}{19.62} = 0.258 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4 — (ii) Draft tube efficiency:** $$\eta_d = 1 - \frac{h_f}{V_2^2/2g} = 1 - \frac{1.5}{4.128} = 1 - 0.3633 = 0.6367$$ $$\boxed{\eta_d \approx 63.67\%}$$ **Step 5 — (iii) Pressure rise across the draft tube:** The pressure rise (in head form) is: $$\Delta \frac{p}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} - \frac{V_3^2}{2g} - h_f$$ $$\Delta \frac{p}{\rho g} = 4.128 - 0.258 - 1.5 = 2.37 \text{ m of water}$$ > **Result:** Pressure rises by **2.37 m of water** across the draft tube. This is the recovered energy that contributes to the net head of the turbine. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks Every Semester:** > > 1. **Sign error in pressure head:** Many students write $p_2/\rho g = +H_s + V_2^2/2g - h_f$ (positive) instead of the correct *negative* form. Always state it as a *vacuum* explicitly. > 2. **Forgetting the negative sign:** KTU examiners specifically look for the negative sign in $p_2/\rho g$. A positive value loses **2 marks** immediately. > 3. **Confusing $V_2$ with $V_1$:** In many textbooks, $V_2$ denotes the *runner exit* velocity. Don't confuse with the inlet guide-vane velocity. > 4. **Not stating the boundary conditions:** When writing Bernoulli's equation, students often skip the steps $p_3/\rho g = 0$, $V_3 \approx 0$, $z_3 = 0$. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this omission. > 5. **Ignoring units in numerical answers:** Always specify the unit (m of water, m/s, etc.). A bare number with no unit loses **0.5 mark** in KTU valuation. > 6. **No sketch for type-classification questions:** Even a rough hand-drawn sketch earns the **'neat sketch'** credit (typically 1–2 marks). > 7. **Forgetting to check the vacuum limit:** Always verify that $H_{vac} \le 7.6 \text{ m}$ (or the local atmospheric head). If it exceeds this, the draft tube will cavitate — examiners love to see this check. > 8. **Mixing up draft tube efficiency formulas:** $\eta_d = 1 - h_f/(V_2^2/2g)$ and $\eta_d = (H_s - h_f)/(H_s + V_2^2/2g)$ are *not* identical. Use the one matching your definition, and state the definition clearly. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Draft Tubes — Last-Minute Revision Cheat Sheet:** > > ☐ **Definition:** Diffuser connecting the runner exit to the tailrace; converts kinetic energy → pressure energy. > > ☐ **Functions (≥ 4):** KE-to-PE conversion, suction creation, head augmentation, low-velocity discharge, cavitation control. > > ☐ **Types (3 essential):** Conical (high head), Conical + sudden enlargement (medium head), Elbow / Moody (low head / Kaplan). > > ☐ **Master Formula (Pressure Head):** > > $$ \frac{p_2}{\rho g} = -H_s - \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_f \quad \text{(always negative)} $$ > > ☐ **Vacuum Head:** $H_{vac} = H_s + V_2^2/2g - h_f$. Must be **< 7.6 m** (practical) or **< 10.3 m** (theoretical max). > > ☐ **Draft Tube Efficiency:** > > $$ \eta_d = 1 - \frac{h_f}{V_2^2/2g} $$ > > ☐ **Continuity in Conical Tube:** $V_2/V_3 = (D_3/D_2)^2$. > > ☐ **Length of Cone:** $L = (D_3 - D_2)/(2\tan \alpha)$, where $\alpha \approx 4°$ to $8°$. > > ☐ **Net Head Comparison:** > > $$ H_{net} = H - h_{f, \text{penstock}} - h_{f, \text{draft tube}} $$ > > ☐ **Order of efficiency:** Conical (≈ 90%) > Hydraulic (≈ 80%) > Elbow (≈ 75%). > > ☐ **Cavitation check:** $H_{vac} \le H_{atm} - NPSH_{required}$ (always verify before finalising design). > > ☐ **Bernoulli's equation between draft-tube inlet (2) and outlet (3)** must explicitly mention the *datum*, *boundary conditions*, and *head loss term*.

topics.title: Cavitation in Turbines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Cavitation in Turbines # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] **Cavitation in Turbines** > **Cavitation** is the phenomenon of formation, growth, and subsequent collapse of vapor bubbles (cavities) within a flowing liquid when the local static pressure at any point in the turbine falls **below the vapor pressure** of the liquid at the prevailing temperature. The violent collapse of these bubbles against the metallic surfaces of the runner blades, guide vanes, and casing produces destructive pitting, vibration, noise, and efficiency loss. In a hydraulic turbine, cavitation is fundamentally a **pressure-related thermodynamic-hydraulic instability**, not a mechanical failure. It occurs when the energy conversion process depressurizes the fluid below a critical threshold. > [!NOTE] **Where Cavitation Occurs in Turbines** > Cavitation predominantly appears on the **suction side (back of blades)** of a reaction turbine runner, especially at the **trailing edge** of the Francis runner and on the **back of Kaplan blades**, exactly where local velocities are highest and pressures are lowest (by Bernoulli's principle). ## 1.2 Real-World Intuitive Analogy Imagine a bottle of cold water. If you twist the cap off quickly, you sometimes hear a *crackling* sound — that's cavitation! Inside the bottle, when the pressure drops momentarily as the cap releases, tiny water vapor bubbles form, then collapse back to liquid as the pressure equalizes. In a turbine, the same phenomenon happens, but with a violent twist: - **Bubbles form** when high-velocity water passes over blade surfaces and the local pressure dips below vapor pressure. - **They collapse** when they get swept into regions of higher pressure downstream (or at the next blade passage). - **The collapse** is so violent that it creates micro-jets and shock waves capable of **eroding even hardened stainless steel** over time. This is the *pitting* you often see on damaged turbine runners. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following constants and head measurements govern cavitation analysis: - **Atmospheric Pressure Head (Hₐ):** Head equivalent of local atmospheric pressure (≈ **10.3 m of water** at sea level, falls with altitude). - **Vapor Pressure Head (Hᵥ):** Head equivalent of water vapor pressure at operating temperature (**0.24 m at 25 °C**, **0.125 m at 20 °C**). - **Suction Head / Setting Head (Hₛ):** Vertical distance between the turbine runner and the tail-race water level. - **Positive Hₛ** → Turbine runner is **above** tail-race. - **Negative Hₛ** → Turbine runner is **below** tail-race (submerged). - **Net Head (H):** Available energy head per unit weight of water driving the turbine. - **Barometric Pressure:** Standard **101.325 kPa** at sea level. > [!NOTE] **KTU Exam Buzzwords to Memorize** > Be ready for examiner favourites: *Thoma's Sigma (σ)*, *Critical Cavitation Factor (σ꜀)*, *NPSH*, *Setting of a turbine*, *Suppression of cavitation*. Write them exactly as: $\sigma_c = \dfrac{NPSH}{H}$. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physical Mechanism — Why Cavitation Happens The mechanism can be broken into four logical stages: - **Stage 1 — Pressure Drop:** As water flows through the runner passages, the blade geometry accelerates the fluid. By **Bernoulli's Principle**, wherever velocity rises, static pressure falls. - **Stage 2 — Bubble Nucleation:** When local static pressure **≤ vapor pressure** ($P_{local} \leq P_v$), dissolved gases come out of solution and water itself vaporizes, forming spherical vapor cavities. - **Stage 3 — Convective Transport:** These bubbles are carried by the flow into regions of higher pressure (towards the trailing edge of the blade or the draft tube). - **Stage 4 — Violent Collapse:** The vapor condenses back to liquid almost instantaneously. The collapse generates **shock waves** with local pressures up to **~1000 MPa**, producing mechanical damage, noise, and vibration. ## 2.2 Types of Cavitation in Turbines | **Type** | **Location** | **Characteristic** | |---|---|---| | **Bubble / Travelling Cavitation** | Suction side of blades | Stable, low damage, slight efficiency drop | | **Sheet / Fixed Cavitation** | Attached to blade surface | Persistent vapor blanket, moderate damage | | **Vortex / Swirl Cavitation** | Tip of runner blades, draft tube | Causes axial thrust, vibration | | **Leading Edge Cavitation** | Front of Francis blades | Caused by poor inlet conditions | ## 2.3 Critical Cavitation and Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH) **Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)** is the head available *above* vapor pressure at the turbine inlet to prevent cavitation. It is essentially the *absolute* pressure margin available for suppression of cavitation. $$NPSH = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} \pm H_s = H_a - H_v \pm H_s$$ - **+Hₛ** when the turbine is set **above** the tail-race (suction head). - **−Hₛ** when the turbine is set **below** the tail-race (submerged setting, draft tube acts as a siphon). > [!IMPORTANT] **Cavitation Rule of Thumb (KTU Favourite)** > Cavitation occurs **if and only if** the absolute pressure at any point in the runner falls **below** the vapor pressure of water. Equivalently, cavitation occurs when $NPSH_{available} < NPSH_{required}$ for the machine at that duty point. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Unit** | |---|---|---|---| | Thoma's Cavitation Factor (σ) | $\sigma = \dfrac{H_a - H_v \pm H_s}{H} = \dfrac{NPSH}{H}$ | Ratio of NPSH to net head | Dimensionless | | Critical Cavitation Factor (σ꜀) | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{NPSH_{required}}{H}$ | Lowest σ at which machine runs without cavitation | Dimensionless | | Cavitation-Free Condition | $\sigma \geq \sigma_c$ | Operating inequality for design | — | | Atmospheric Head (Sea level) | $H_a = 10.3 \text{ m of water}$ | Standard atmospheric pressure head | m | | Vapor Pressure Head (at T °C) | $H_v = \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g}$ | From steam tables for water at operating T | m | | Suction / Setting Head | $H_s = z_{runner} - z_{tailrace}$ | Vertical distance | m | | Maximum Permissible Setting | $H_{s,max} = H_a - H_v - \sigma_c H$ | Highest runner elevation above tail race | m | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Cavitation analysis is critical for: - **Selection of turbine type:** Pelton wheels (impulse) are **immune to cavitation** because the entire runner operates at atmospheric pressure. Francis and Kaplan turbines (reaction) **must** be designed with NPSH margins. - **Civil engineering layout:** Determining **how high to place** the runner above tail-race to avoid cavitation at minimum tail-water levels. - **Altitude correction:** For hydro stations in the **Western Ghats / Himalayas** of India, atmospheric pressure is lower → cavitation risk is higher → requires deeper submergence. - **Material selection & maintenance:** Cavitation-resistant stainless steel (e.g., **13 Cr–4 Ni** alloys) and scheduled runner maintenance intervals are direct economic outcomes of cavitation prediction. - **Draft tube design:** Modern draft tubes are optimised to recover pressure and suppress vortex cavitation. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Thoma's Cavitation Parameter (σ) Consider a reaction turbine installation. Apply Bernoulli's equation (in head form) between the **tail-race reservoir (point 2)** and a **point (1) on the suction side of the runner blade** where cavitation is imminent. **Setting up the energy equation:** At the tail-race, the absolute pressure equals atmospheric pressure (free surface open to atmosphere), and velocity head is negligible. At point 1 (the cavitation-prone location), the pressure is at vapor pressure $P_v$, and velocity head is part of the flow inside the runner. $$\dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + 0 + z_{tailrace} = \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + \text{(losses)}$$ For an ideal, simplified analysis at the threshold of cavitation: $$\dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} = \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + (z_1 - z_{tailrace})$$ Recognising that $(z_1 - z_{tailrace}) = H_s$ and substituting head equivalents: $$H_a - H_v = \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + H_s$$ **The Key Insight — Euler vs. Thoma:** For a reaction turbine, the **kinetic energy head leaving the runner** is related to the net head H by the Euler relationship. For an ideal reaction turbine (no losses), the velocity of water leaving the runner equals $\sqrt{2gH}$. Therefore: $$\dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} \approx H$$ This is the classical **Thoma approximation** that treats the runner exit velocity head as equal to the net head. Substituting: $$H_a - H_v = H + H_s$$ **Rearranging and defining σ:** $$\dfrac{H_a - H_v - H_s}{H} = 1$$ Generalising (for non-ideal turbines, σ < 1), Thoma defined the dimensionless **cavitation parameter**: $$\boxed{\sigma = \dfrac{H_a - H_v - H_s}{H}}$$ **Sign convention for setting:** - If runner is **above** tail-race → $H_s$ is **positive** and subtracted (more suction = more cavitation risk). - If runner is **below** tail-race → $H_s$ is **negative** and added (submergence suppresses cavitation). ## 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Permissible Setting Height The cavitation-free operating condition requires: $$\sigma \geq \sigma_c$$ Substituting the definitions of σ and σ꜀: $$\dfrac{H_a - H_v - H_s}{H} \geq \dfrac{NPSH_{required}}{H}$$ Multiplying through by H: $$H_a - H_v - H_s \geq NPSH_{required}$$ Solving for $H_s$: $$H_s \leq H_a - H_v - NPSH_{required}$$ Therefore, the **maximum allowable setting height** of the runner above tail-race is: $$\boxed{H_{s,max} = H_a - H_v - \sigma_c \cdot H}$$ This is a **direct design formula** for civil engineers laying out a powerhouse. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A Francis turbine operates under a net head of **60 m**. The atmospheric pressure head is **10.0 m**, vapor pressure head is **0.25 m**, and the critical cavitation factor for the runner is **σ꜀ = 0.10**. Determine: 1. The maximum permissible setting height $H_{s,max}$. 2. Whether the turbine will cavitate if installed with $H_s = 5.5$ m. **Step 1 — Identify given values:** $$H = 60 \text{ m}, \quad H_a = 10.0 \text{ m}, \quad H_v = 0.25 \text{ m}, \quad \sigma_c = 0.10$$ **Step 2 — Compute NPSH required:** $$NPSH_{required} = \sigma_c \cdot H = 0.10 \times 60 = 6.0 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Compute maximum permissible setting height:** $$H_{s,max} = H_a - H_v - NPSH_{required}$$ $$H_{s,max} = 10.0 - 0.25 - 6.0 = 3.75 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4 — Compare with actual setting:** Given $H_s = 5.5$ m. Since $H_s (5.5) > H_{s,max} (3.75)$: > [!WARNING] **Cavitation will occur.** The runner is set too high. The turbine must be lowered so that the runner is at most **3.75 m** above the tail-race level, OR the operating net head must be reduced, OR the runner geometry must be redesigned for a lower σ꜀. **Step 5 — Compute the σ at actual setting (for verification):** $$\sigma_{actual} = \dfrac{10.0 - 0.25 - 5.5}{60} = \dfrac{4.25}{60} = 0.0708$$ Since $\sigma_{actual} (0.0708) < \sigma_c (0.10)$ → cavitation confirmed. ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Cavitation Diagnosis | **Observed Symptom** | **Probable Cause** | **Remedial Action** | |---|---|---| | Pitting on back of Francis runner blades | Travelling bubble cavitation | Reduce $H_s$ or shift operating point | | Vibration + axial thrust oscillations | Vortex cavitation at runner crown | Redesign draft tube, add anti-vortex ribs | | Drop in efficiency at part load | Sheet cavitation on blade profile | Operate nearer best-efficiency point | | Loud cracking noise at high flow | Leading-edge cavitation | Improve inlet guide vane geometry | | Damage confined to Pelton buckets | *Not cavitation* — likely sand erosion | Install desander upstream | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Schematic: Cavitation Mechanism inside a Francis Runner The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **flow physics** of bubble formation, transport, and collapse inside a Francis turbine runner passage — illustrating the engineering process as a block-level architecture rather than a physical sketch. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["High Pressure Inlet Water
Pa greater than Pv"] --> B["Flow Enters Runner Blade Passage
Velocity rises sharply"] B --> C["Local Static Pressure Falls
Pa drops below Vapor Pressure Pv"] C --> D["Vapor Bubble Nucleation Begins
Tiny vapor cavities form"] D --> E["Bubbles Advect with Main Flow
Travel toward Trailing Edge"] E --> F{"Bubbles Enter Higher Pressure Zone
Pa exceeds Pv again"} F -- Yes --> G["Violent Bubble Collapse
Shockwave and Microjet Form"] G --> H["Blade Surface Pitting and Erosion
Mechanical Damage Initiation"] F -- No --> I["Bubble Drift Out with Flow
Travelling Cavitation Mild Damage"] H --> J["Loss of Blade Profile Shape
Efficiency Degradation"] J --> K["Vibration and Audible Noise
Reduced Machine Life"] ``` ## 4.2 Schematic: Setting of Reaction Turbine and Cavitation Zone ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Upper Reservoir
Headwater Level"] -->|"Net Head H available"| B["Turbine Casing
Spiral Casing"] B --> C["Guide Vane Assembly
Stay Vanes"] C --> D["Runner Blade Zone
Lowest Pressure Region"] D --> E["Draft Tube
Pressure Recovery"] E -->|"Setting Head Hs
Vertical Drop"| F["Tail Race Reservoir
Tailwater Level"] D -.->|"If P less than Pv
Cavitation Inception"| G["Vapor Cavities Form
Bubble Collapse and Damage"] F -.->|"Atmospheric Pressure Ha"| D H["Atmospheric Pressure Ha on Tail Race
plus Vapor Pressure Head Hv"] -.-> D ``` ## 4.3 Schematic: Cavitation-Free Design Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Begin: Design of Reaction Turbine Installation"] --> Q1{"Turbine Type and
Net Head H Known?"} Q1 -- Yes --> Calc1["Compute NPSH Required
NPSH = sigma_c times H"] Calc1 --> Calc2["Measure Local Atmospheric Head Ha
and Vapor Pressure Head Hv at Operating T"] Calc2 --> Calc3["Calculate Maximum Setting
Hs max = Ha minus Hv minus NPSH"] Calc3 --> Q2{"Proposed Hs less than or equal to Hs max?"} Q2 -- Yes --> OK["Design Acceptable
No Cavitation Predicted"] Q2 -- No --> Red["Redesign Required
Lower Runner Elevation
OR Reduce Operating Head
OR Select Lower sigma_c Runner"] Red --> Calc3 Q1 -- No --> Stop["Gather Site and Machine Data First"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** **Q: Define cavitation in hydraulic turbines. State any two ill effects of cavitation.** **Model Answer:** Cavitation is the phenomenon of formation and subsequent collapse of vapor bubbles in a flowing liquid when the local static pressure at any point inside the turbine falls below the vapor pressure of the liquid at the operating temperature. **Two ill effects:** - **Erosion of blade surfaces:** The violent collapse of vapor bubbles produces shock waves and micro-jets that pit the metal, leading to roughened blade surfaces and eventual loss of blade profile. - **Drop in efficiency and output:** Cavitation disrupts smooth flow, increases hydraulic losses, and the roughened surfaces cause additional friction losses, reducing the overall turbine efficiency. - (Any other valid point: *vibration, noise, axial thrust imbalance, damage to bearings*.) --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model Question]** **Q: What is Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)? Why is it significant in reaction turbine installations?** **Model Answer:** NPSH is the total head available at the turbine inlet above the vapor pressure head of water, expressed as: $$NPSH = H_a - H_v - H_s$$ (for a turbine set above tail race, with $H_s$ positive). **Significance:** - NPSH must always exceed the **required NPSH** (a characteristic of the runner design at the operating duty point); otherwise cavitation will occur. - It determines the **maximum permissible setting height** $H_{s,max}$ of the runner above the tail-race — a critical parameter for the civil layout of the powerhouse. - A low NPSH margin means cavitation is likely; designers must either reduce the runner elevation, increase submergence, or select a runner with a lower σ꜀. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **ONE of TWO alternatives** at 14 marks, typically split as **7 + 7**. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Pattern]** **Q: (a)** Derive an expression for Thoma's cavitation factor and explain its significance in reaction turbine design. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Francis turbine operates under a net head of 80 m. The atmospheric pressure head at the site is 9.5 m of water and vapor pressure head is 0.20 m. The critical cavitation factor is 0.09. Determine: (i) the maximum permissible height of the runner above the tail race, and (ii) whether cavitation will occur if the runner is set 4.5 m above tail race. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Part (a) — Derivation of Thoma's Cavitation Factor **Step 1 — Setting the stage:** Apply Bernoulli's equation in head form between the free surface of the tail-race (point 2) and a point on the suction side of the runner (point 1) where cavitation is about to begin. $$\dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + 0 + z_2 = \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1$$ **Step 2 — Rearranging:** $$H_a - H_v = \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + (z_1 - z_2) = \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + H_s$$ **Step 3 — Applying Euler's relation:** For an ideal reaction turbine, the absolute velocity of water leaving the runner equals $\sqrt{2gH}$, hence $\dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} \approx H$. $$H_a - H_v = H + H_s$$ **Step 4 — Defining σ:** $$\boxed{\sigma = \dfrac{H_a - H_v - H_s}{H}}$$ **Significance in design:** - σ is a **dimensionless number** that characterises the cavitation tendency. - For **cavitation-free** operation: $\sigma \geq \sigma_c$. - It allows the designer to estimate $H_{s,max}$ which fixes the elevation of the powerhouse floor. - It is **independent of flow rate** in the ideal Thoma model (in practice, σ꜀ varies slightly with discharge and speed). --- #### Model Solution to Part (b) — Numerical Problem **Given:** $$H = 80 \text{ m}, \quad H_a = 9.5 \text{ m}, \quad H_v = 0.20 \text{ m}, \quad \sigma_c = 0.09, \quad H_s = 4.5 \text{ m (proposed)}$$ **Part (i): Maximum permissible setting height** $$NPSH_{req} = \sigma_c \cdot H = 0.09 \times 80 = 7.2 \text{ m}$$ $$H_{s,max} = H_a - H_v - NPSH_{req} = 9.5 - 0.20 - 7.2$$ $$\boxed{H_{s,max} = 2.10 \text{ m}}$$ **Part (ii): Check for cavitation at $H_s = 4.5$ m** $$\sigma_{actual} = \dfrac{H_a - H_v - H_s}{H} = \dfrac{9.5 - 0.20 - 4.5}{80}$$ $$\sigma_{actual} = \dfrac{4.80}{80} = 0.06$$ Since $\sigma_{actual} (0.06) < \sigma_c (0.09)$: $$\boxed{\text{Cavitation WILL occur at } H_s = 4.5 \text{ m.}}$$ **Recommendation:** The runner must be lowered to a maximum of 2.10 m above tail-race, OR a runner with lower σ꜀ must be selected. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern]** **Q: (a)** Discuss the various causes and effects of cavitation in hydraulic turbines. What remedial measures are adopted to prevent cavitation in reaction turbines? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A reaction turbine develops 8000 kW under a net head of 25 m. The barometric pressure at the site is 9.81 m of water and the vapor pressure is 0.24 m of water. The Thoma cavitation factor is 0.20. Calculate the maximum height of the runner above the tail race level. If the tail-race level drops by 1.5 m during summer, will the turbine cavitate? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Part (a) — Causes, Effects & Remedies **Causes:** - Excessive suction head ($H_s$ too large). - Operating at high speeds or off-design discharge. - Poor inlet guide vane geometry causing local low-pressure zones. - High altitude site → reduced $H_a$. - High water temperature → increased $H_v$. **Effects:** - **Pitting** of runner blades and guide vanes. - **Loss of efficiency** and reduced power output. - **Vibration** and **noise** (characteristic crackling sound). - **Axial thrust imbalance**, damaging thrust bearings. - **Erosion** of draft tube in severe cases. **Remedial measures:** - Set the runner as low as possible (lower $H_s$). - Use **cavitation-resistant alloys** (13 Cr – 4 Ni stainless steel). - Polish runner surfaces to reduce nucleation sites. - Ensure proper alignment and smooth inlet flow. - Operate the turbine near its **best efficiency point**. - Use a properly designed **draft tube** to recover pressure. - For high-head installations, **use Pelton turbines** (no cavitation risk). --- #### Model Solution to Part (b) — Numerical Problem **Given:** $$P = 8000 \text{ kW}, \quad H = 25 \text{ m}, \quad H_a = 9.81 \text{ m}, \quad H_v = 0.24 \text{ m}, \quad \sigma_c = 0.20$$ **Step 1 — Maximum permissible setting height:** $$NPSH_{req} = \sigma_c \cdot H = 0.20 \times 25 = 5.0 \text{ m}$$ $$H_{s,max} = H_a - H_v - NPSH_{req} = 9.81 - 0.24 - 5.0$$ $$\boxed{H_{s,max} = 4.57 \text{ m}}$$ **Step 2 — Tail-race level drops by 1.5 m:** A drop in tail-race level raises the runner above the new tail-race by 1.5 m. New setting height: $$H_s^{new} = H_s^{old} + 1.5$$ If the turbine was originally set at $H_{s,max} = 4.57$ m, after the drop: $$H_s^{new} = 4.57 + 1.5 = 6.07 \text{ m}$$ This exceeds $H_{s,max}$, so: $$\boxed{\text{Yes, cavitation will occur during summer.}}$$ **Numerical confirmation:** $$\sigma_{new} = \dfrac{9.81 - 0.24 - 6.07}{25} = \dfrac{3.50}{25} = 0.14$$ Since $0.14 < \sigma_c (0.20)$ → **cavitation confirmed.** --- > [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > - **Sign convention errors:** Many students lose **2–3 marks** by writing $H_a + H_v - H_s$ instead of $H_a - H_v - H_s$. Memorise: $NPSH = H_a - H_v - H_s$ (above tail-race). > - **Confusing σ and σ꜀:** σ is the *actual* operating cavitation number; σ꜀ is the *minimum allowable* for cavitation-free running. Operating condition is $\sigma \geq \sigma_c$. > - **Unit mix-up for vapor pressure:** Always express in **head (m of water)** — never in kPa — when using Thoma's formula. > - **Ignoring altitude:** For Keralan hill stations (Idukki, Munnar area), $H_a < 10.3$ m — examiners often vary this value. > - **Saying "Pelton turbines undergo cavitation":** Strictly wrong. Pelton runners operate at atmospheric pressure. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Cavitation** = bubble formation + collapse when local pressure drops below vapor pressure. - **Thoma's Cavitation Factor:** $\sigma = \dfrac{H_a - H_v - H_s}{H}$ (dimensionless). - **Cavitation-free rule:** $\sigma_{actual} \geq \sigma_c$. - **NPSH = $H_a - H_v \pm H_s$**, **NPSH_required = σ꜀ · H**. - **Max setting height formula:** $H_{s,max} = H_a - H_v - \sigma_c \cdot H$. - **Runner above tail-race → positive Hₛ**, runner below → negative Hₛ. - **Pelton (impulse) turbines are immune** to cavitation — only Francis and Kaplan (reaction) are vulnerable. - **Cavitation damages:** pitting, vibration, noise, efficiency loss, axial thrust imbalance. - **Cavitation prevented by:** lower setting height, cavitation-resistant alloys, polished runners, proper draft tube design, operating near best-efficiency point. - **Vapor pressure of water** at 25 °C ≈ 0.24 m; at 100 °C = 10.3 m (boiling point). - **Atmospheric pressure head at sea level** ≈ 10.3 m; falls ~0.12 m per 100 m altitude gain. - **Exam Tip:** Always write units (m of water), state the operating condition ($\sigma \geq \sigma_c$ or vice versa), and explicitly compare numerical values — don't leave the examiner to deduce.

topics.title: Overview on Rotary and Positive Displacement Pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Overview on Rotary and Positive Displacement Pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Classification of Pumps — The Big Picture

In fluid mechanics, all hydraulic machines used to transfer energy to a fluid (increasing its pressure or head) are classified into two fundamental families based on the **principle of energy transfer**:

| Family | Energy Transfer Mechanism | Flow Characteristic |
|---|---|---|
| **Centrifugal (Rotodynamic) Pumps** | Kinetic energy via impeller rotation, then converted to pressure | Continuous, non-pulsating |
| **Positive Displacement (PD) Pumps** | Mechanical displacement of fixed fluid volumes against pressure | Pulsating (reciprocating) or steady (rotary) |

### 1.2 Definition of a Positive Displacement (PD) Pump

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard):**  
> A **Positive Displacement Pump** is a hydraulic machine that delivers a **fixed quantity of fluid per cycle** (or per revolution) by trapping a definite amount of fluid and then forcing (displacing) it into the discharge pipe. The discharge is **theoretically independent of the pressure head** against which the pump operates, within the safe working limits.

The internal chamber **expands** to draw in fluid (suction stroke) and then **contracts** to push the fluid out (delivery stroke). Because the fluid is *trapped* and *physically pushed*, PD pumps are inherently **self-priming** and can generate very high pressures.

### 1.3 Definition of a Rotary Pump

> [!NOTE]  
> **Rotary Pump:** A sub-class of positive displacement pumps in which fluid is displaced by the **rotational motion of gears, vanes, screws, or lobes** housed inside a stationary casing. The flow is continuous (non-pulsating) and delivery is smooth.

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🍶

Imagine you have two ways to transfer honey from one jar to another:

- **Reciprocating PD Pump Analogy:** You use a **syringe**. You pull the plunger back (suction), honey enters, then you push it forward (delivery). The volume per stroke is *fixed* regardless of how hard you push — that's a **reciprocating positive displacement pump**.

- **Rotary PD Pump Analogy:** You pour honey from a **spinning turntable with a fixed scraper**. As the plate rotates, honey is continuously trapped in pockets and scraped out. That's a **rotary pump** — no pulsation, just smooth rotation.

- **Centrifugal Analogy:** You swing a bucket of honey over your head in a circle. Centrifugal action slings it out — that's a **centrifugal pump**. Throw the bucket away; the flow stops the moment rotation stops.

This mental model is critical for the board exam — examiners love testing your *conceptual clarity*, not just formulas.

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Performance Metrics

The following metrics (all **bolded** below) recur in every PD pump problem on the KTU exam:

- **Displacement Volume (V_d):** Volume of fluid theoretically discharged per revolution, in **m³/rev**.
- **Theoretical Discharge (Q_th):** Flow rate if there were no internal leakage, in **m³/s**.
- **Slip (S):** Quantity of fluid that leaks back from delivery to suction, in **m³/s**.
- **Actual Discharge (Q_act):** Real measured flow at the pump outlet, in **m³/s**.
- **Volumetric Efficiency (η_vol):** Ratio of actual to theoretical discharge, in **%**.
- **Manometric Efficiency (η_man):** Ratio of manometric head to theoretical head.
- **Mechanical Efficiency (η_m):** Accounts for friction losses in bearings, gears.
- **Overall Efficiency (η_o):** Product of all three efficiencies.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sub-Classification of PD Pumps

PD pumps bifurcate based on the *motion* of the displacing element:

```
PD Pumps
├── Reciprocating Type (Linear motion of piston/plunger)
│   ├── Single-acting reciprocating pump
│   ├── Double-acting reciprocating pump
│   └── Diaphragm pump
└── Rotary Type (Rotational motion of gears/vanes/screws)
    ├── Gear Pump  (External & Internal)
    ├── Vane Pump
    ├── Screw Pump
    └── Lobe Pump
```

### 2.2 The Four Major Rotary Pump Architectures

#### (a) External Gear Pump ⚙️⚙️
- **Why it works:** Two identical meshing gears rotate in opposite directions inside a close-fitting casing. Fluid is trapped in the **space between the gear teeth and the casing** and is carried from the suction side to the delivery side around the casing periphery.
- **How:** As the gears unmesh at the suction port, a partial vacuum draws fluid in. The fluid is then *carried* in pockets between teeth and casing to the delivery side, where meshing teeth *squeeze* it out.
- **Utility:** Most common in hydraulic systems, oil refineries, lube oil transfer, and chemical dosing.

#### (b) Internal Gear Pump ⚙️🌀
- Uses a **rotor (external gear) and an idler (internal gear with teeth on the inside)**, separated by a crescent-shaped partition. The crescent creates a seal.
- Smoother flow, lower noise, used for viscous fluids like chocolate, resins, and fuel oils.

#### (c) Vane Pump 📐
- An **off-center rotor** carries spring-loaded sliding vanes. As the rotor turns, centrifugal force pushes the vanes outward against the casing, creating expanding (suction) and contracting (delivery) chambers.
- Adjustable via an **eccentric ring** for variable displacement.
- **Utility:** Automotive power steering, hydraulic presses, low-pressure systems.

#### (d) Screw Pump 🌀🌀🌀
- Uses one, two, or three **intermeshing screws** rotating in a close-fitting bore. Fluid is pushed axially (parallel to the shaft) in a continuous, axial, non-pulsating stream.
- The **3-screw pump** is industry standard for marine lubrication, oil burners, and high-viscosity pumping.

> [!TIP]  
> **KTU Memory Hook — "EGIV"** for the four rotary types: **E**xternal Gear, **I**nternal Gear, **V**ane, **S**crew (S). Or remember: "**GIVS** Clean" — Gear, Internal Gear, Vane, Screw.

### 2.3 Comparison: Rotary vs Reciprocating PD Pumps

| Feature | Reciprocating PD Pump | Rotary PD Pump |
|---|---|---|
| **Discharge** | **Pulsating** (per stroke) | **Smooth, continuous** |
| **Mechanical complexity** | High (valves, pistons) | Low (rotors only) |
| **Pressure capability** | Very high (up to 1000+ bar) | Moderate (typically up to 200 bar) |
| **Speed** | Low (60–300 rpm typical) | High (1000–3000 rpm) |
| **Maintenance** | Frequent (valve wear) | Minimal |
| **Self-priming** | Yes | Yes |
| **Flow pulsation control** | Needs air vessel | Not required |
| **Suited for** | Low flow, high head | Moderate flow, moderate head |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU problem on this topic.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical Discharge | $Q_{th} = N \cdot V_d$ | m³/s | $N$ = speed in rev/s |
| 2 | Actual Discharge | $Q_{act} = Q_{th} - S$ | m³/s | $S$ = slip |
| 3 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of actual to theoretical |
| 4 | Slip (Coefficient) | $\sigma = \dfrac{S}{Q_{th}}$ | dimensionless | Slip fraction |
| 5 | Manometric Efficiency | $\eta_{man} = \dfrac{H_{man}}{H_{th}}$ | dimensionless | Head efficiency |
| 6 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_{vol} \cdot \eta_{man} \cdot \eta_{m}$ | dimensionless | Total energy conversion |
| 7 | Input Power | $P_{in} = \dfrac{\rho g Q_{act} H_{man}}{\eta_o}$ | W | Shaft power required |
| 8 | Water Power (Output) | $P_{water} = \rho g Q_{act} H_{man}$ | W | Useful hydraulic power |
| 9 | Gear Pump Displacement | $V_d = \pi B (R^2 - r^2)$ | m³/rev | $B$ = width, $R$ = outer radius, $r$ = inner radius |
| 10 | Slip (general PD) | $S = K \cdot \dfrac{H_{man}}{\mu}$ | m³/s | $K$ = constant, $\mu$ = viscosity |
| 11 | Coefficient of Discharge | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | dimensionless | Equivalent to $\eta_{vol}$ for rotary |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Slip Behavior — Critical Concept:**  
> In PD pumps, **slip increases with discharge pressure** (higher head → more leakage) and **decreases with fluid viscosity** (thicker fluids leak less). This is why rotary pumps are *excellent* for viscous fluids like oils and tar.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas:** Screw and gear pumps move crude, diesel, lube oils.
- **Hydraulics:** Vane and gear pumps power machine tools and presses.
- **Marine:** 3-screw pumps circulate lube oil in ship engines.
- **Chemical:** PD pumps handle shear-sensitive fluids, polymers, and resins.
- **Food Industry:** Vane pumps pump chocolate, peanut butter, fruit pulps.
- **Aerospace:** High-pressure reciprocating PD pumps for fuel injection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Discharge of an External Gear Pump

**Given geometry:**
- Outer gear radius = $R$ (m)
- Inner gear radius (root) = $r$ (m)
- Gear face width = $B$ (m)
- Rotational speed = $N$ (rpm) → speed in rev/s = $N/60$

**Step 1 — Identify the displacement volume per revolution**

The fluid trapped per revolution is the volume between the gear teeth and the casing. For an external gear pump with two identical gears, the displacement volume per revolution is the **annular volume swept between root and tip** over the width of the gear.

$$
V_d = \pi \cdot B \cdot (R^2 - r^2)
$$

> *Logic:* The annular cross-section area is $\pi R^2 - \pi r^2 = \pi(R^2 - r^2)$. Multiplying by the axial width $B$ gives the volume.

**Step 2 — Convert rotational speed to revolutions per second**

$$
N_{rps} = \dfrac{N_{rpm}}{60}
$$

> *Logic:* $N$ is given in rpm (revolutions per minute). Dividing by 60 converts to revolutions per second.

**Step 3 — Write the theoretical discharge equation**

$$
Q_{th} = V_d \cdot N_{rps}
$$

Substituting both:

$$
Q_{th} = \pi B (R^2 - r^2) \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for slip to get actual discharge**

$$
Q_{act} = Q_{th} - S
$$

**Step 5 — Express volumetric efficiency**

$$
\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = 1 - \dfrac{S}{Q_{th}}
$$

**Step 6 — Power developed by the pump**

$$
P_{water} = \rho g Q_{act} H_{man}
$$

$$
P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{water}}{\eta_o} = \dfrac{\rho g Q_{act} H_{man}}{\eta_{vol} \cdot \eta_{man} \cdot \eta_{m}}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **[Worked Example 1 — Gear Pump Discharge]**
> An external gear pump has a gear width of **50 mm**, outer radius **60 mm**, inner radius **40 mm**, and runs at **600 rpm**. If the volumetric efficiency is **92%**, find:
> (a) Theoretical discharge  
> (b) Actual discharge  
> (c) Slip in LPM

**Given:**
- $B = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- $R = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.06 \text{ m}$
- $r = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.04 \text{ m}$
- $N = 600 \text{ rpm}$
- $\eta_{vol} = 0.92$

**Step (a) — Theoretical Discharge**

$$
V_d = \pi B (R^2 - r^2)
$$

$$
V_d = \pi \times 0.05 \times (0.06^2 - 0.04^2)
$$

$$
V_d = \pi \times 0.05 \times (0.0036 - 0.0016)
$$

$$
V_d = \pi \times 0.05 \times 0.0020
$$

$$
V_d = 3.1416 \times 0.0001 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = V_d \times \dfrac{N}{60} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \times \dfrac{600}{60}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \times 10 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{th} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 188.5 \text{ LPM}}
$$

**Step (b) — Actual Discharge**

$$
Q_{act} = \eta_{vol} \times Q_{th} = 0.92 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{act} = 2.890 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 173.4 \text{ LPM}}
$$

**Step (c) — Slip**

$$
S = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-3} - 2.890 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\boxed{S = 2.516 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 15.1 \text{ LPM}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Relationship Between Slip and Pressure

The slip in a PD pump depends on the **clearance geometry, pressure difference, and fluid viscosity** (analogous to flow through an orifice under pressure):

$$
S = \dfrac{C \cdot \Delta P}{\mu}
$$

where:
- $C$ = leakage constant (depends on clearance area and length)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure difference = $\rho g H_{man}$
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of the fluid

Substituting $\Delta P = \rho g H_{man}$:

$$
S = \dfrac{C \cdot \rho g H_{man}}{\mu}
$$

**Insight — Why viscous fluids pump better in rotary PD pumps:**

As $\mu$ increases, $S$ decreases. Hence $\eta_{vol} \to 1$ for thick fluids. This is why oil pumps (high $\mu$) achieve 98% volumetric efficiency, while water pumps (low $\mu$) may only reach 85–92%.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for KTU Problems

| Given in problem | What to find | First formula to use |
|---|---|---|
| $B, R, r, N$ | $Q_{th}$ | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{\pi B (R^2 - r^2) N}{60}$ |
| $Q_{th}, \eta_{vol}$ | $Q_{act}$ | $Q_{act} = \eta_{vol} \cdot Q_{th}$ |
| $Q_{th}, Q_{act}$ | Slip $S$ | $S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ |
| $Q_{act}, H_{man}, \eta_o$ | Shaft power | $P = \dfrac{\rho g Q_{act} H_{man}}{\eta_o}$ |
| $H_{man}, S, \mu$ | Viscosity relationship | $S = \dfrac{C \rho g H_{man}}{\mu}$ |
| $V_d, N$ | $Q_{th}$ | $Q_{th} = V_d \cdot N/60$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Classification of PD Pumps

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[All Pumps] --> B[Rotodynamic Pumps]
    A --> C[Positive Displacement Pumps]
    B --> B1[Centrifugal Pump]
    B --> B2[Axial Flow Pump]
    B --> B3[Mixed Flow Pump]
    C --> D[Reciprocating PD Pumps]
    C --> E[Rotary PD Pumps]
    D --> D1[Single Acting Piston]
    D --> D2[Double Acting Piston]
    D --> D3[Diaphragm Pump]
    E --> E1[External Gear Pump]
    E --> E2[Internal Gear Pump]
    E --> E3[Vane Pump]
    E --> E4[Screw Pump]
    E --> E5[Lobe Pump]
    E1 --> F1[Used in Hydraulics Lube Oil]
    E2 --> F2[Used for Viscous Fluids]
    E3 --> F3[Used in Power Steering]
    E4 --> F4[Used in Marine Lube Systems]
    E5 --> F5[Used in Food Dairy Industry]
```

### 4.2 Operational Flow — External Gear Pump

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN[Suction Port] --> TRAP[Fluid Trapped in Tooth Casing Pockets]
    TRAP --> ROT[Gears Rotate Fluid Along Casing Wall]
    ROT --> DELIVERY[Fluid Squeezed Out at Meshing Point]
    DELIVERY --> OUT[Delivery Port to Discharge Pipe]
    TRAP -.leakage.-> SLIP[Slip Back to Suction]
    SLIP -.reduces.-> EFF[Volumetric Efficiency]
```

### 4.3 Functional Schematic — Vane Pump

```mermaid
graph TD
    SUCT[Suction Port] --> RT[Off Center Rotor]
    RT --> V1[Vane Slot 1]
    RT --> V2[Vane Slot 2]
    RT --> V3[Vane Slot 3]
    RT --> V4[Vane Slot 4]
    V1 --> EXP[Expanding Volume Draws Fluid In]
    EXP --> CONT[Contracting Volume Expels Fluid]
    V4 --> CONT
    CONT --> DELIV[Delivery Port]
    V2 --> EXP
    V3 --> CONT
```

### 4.4 Energy Transfer Flow — Complete Rotary Pump Cycle

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1 Suction: Gears Unmesh] --> S2[Step 2 Trapping: Fluid Held in Tooth Gaps]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Transport: Rotational Movement Carries Fluid]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Delivery: Gears Mesh and Squeeze Fluid Out]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Continuous Output: Steady Non Pulsating Flow]
    S1 -.concurrent.-> L1[Leakage Path: Clearance Gaps]
    L1 --> S2
```

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] (3 Marks)**  
*Define a positive displacement pump. How does it differ from a rotodynamic pump in principle?*

**Model Answer:**

> A **positive displacement (PD) pump** is a hydraulic machine that moves fluid by **trapping a fixed volume** in a chamber and then mechanically forcing (displacing) that volume into the discharge pipe. The discharge per cycle is **theoretically constant** and independent of the pressure head.
>
> **Difference from rotodynamic pumps:**
>
> | Aspect | PD Pump | Rotodynamic Pump |
> |---|---|---|
> | Energy transfer | Mechanical displacement | Kinetic energy via impeller |
> | Discharge vs head | Constant discharge, variable head | Variable discharge, head depends on flow |
> | Pressure capability | Very high | Limited (depends on NPSH and design) |
> | Flow type | Pulsating or smooth rotary | Always smooth |
> | Priming | Self-priming | Needs priming |

**[Marks Distribution: Definition 1M, Tabular comparison 2M]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] (3 Marks)**  
*Explain the term "slip" in a positive displacement pump. State two factors affecting slip.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Slip (S):** Slip is the quantity of fluid that **leaks back** from the delivery chamber to the suction chamber per unit time, through the unavoidable **clearances** between moving parts (between piston and cylinder, between gear teeth and casing, etc.). Slip reduces the actual discharge below the theoretical discharge.
>
> **Mathematically:** $S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$
>
> **Two Factors Affecting Slip:**
>
> 1. **Discharge Pressure (Manometric Head):** Slip is **directly proportional** to the pressure difference across the pump. Higher delivery pressure → more leakage → more slip.
>
> 2. **Fluid Viscosity (μ):** Slip is **inversely proportional** to viscosity. Thicker fluids leak less through clearances.
>
> [Additional factors for bonus: clearance size, pump speed (centrifugal effect on return flow)]

**[Marks Distribution: Definition 1M, Two factors 2M]**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of an external gear pump**. Derive the expression for its **theoretical discharge**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An external gear pump has the following specifications:  
Outer diameter = **120 mm**, Inner diameter = **80 mm**, Width = **40 mm**, Speed = **900 rpm**, Volumetric efficiency = **90%**, Manometric efficiency = **85%**, Mechanical efficiency = **92%**. The pump delivers oil (specific gravity = 0.85) against a manometric head of **30 m**. Calculate:  
(i) Theoretical discharge in LPM  
(ii) Actual discharge in LPM  
(iii) Slip  
(iv) Input power required to drive the pump

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (a):**

**Construction (3 Marks):**
- Two identical spur gears mesh together inside a close-fitting cast iron casing.
- One gear is the **driving gear** (connected to the motor via a shaft), the other is the **idler** (rotates due to meshing).
- The casing has two ports: **suction port** (inlet) and **delivery port** (outlet), separated by the line of gear mesh.
- Extremely small clearances (typically 0.05–0.1 mm) exist between gear tips and casing — these are the **leakage paths** causing slip.

**Working (2 Marks):**
- At the **suction port**, the gears unmesh, creating an expanding volume. This creates a **partial vacuum** that draws fluid in.
- Fluid is **trapped in the pockets** between adjacent gear teeth and the casing wall.
- As the gears rotate, this fluid is **carried around the periphery** from suction to delivery side.
- At the **delivery port**, the gears mesh, **squeezing the trapped fluid** out into the discharge pipe.

**Derivation of Theoretical Discharge (2 Marks):**

$$
Q_{th} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot B \cdot (R^2 - r^2) \cdot N}{60} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Where $B$ = width, $R$ = outer radius, $r$ = inner radius, $N$ = rpm.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $D_{outer} = 120 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow R = 0.060 \text{ m}$
- $D_{inner} = 80 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow r = 0.040 \text{ m}$
- $B = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.040 \text{ m}$
- $N = 900 \text{ rpm}$
- $\eta_{vol} = 0.90$, $\eta_{man} = 0.85$, $\eta_m = 0.92$
- Specific gravity of oil = 0.85 → $\rho_{oil} = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $H_{man} = 30 \text{ m}$

**Step (i) — Theoretical Discharge**

$$
V_d = \pi B (R^2 - r^2) = \pi \times 0.040 \times (0.060^2 - 0.040^2)
$$

$$
V_d = \pi \times 0.040 \times (0.0036 - 0.0016) = \pi \times 0.040 \times 0.0020
$$

$$
V_d = 2.513 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}
$$

$$
Q_{th} = V_d \times \dfrac{N}{60} = 2.513 \times 10^{-4} \times 15 = 3.770 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{th} = 3.770 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 226.2 \text{ LPM}}
$$

**Step (ii) — Actual Discharge**

$$
Q_{act} = \eta_{vol} \times Q_{th} = 0.90 \times 3.770 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{act} = 3.393 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 203.6 \text{ LPM}}
$$

**Step (iii) — Slip**

$$
S = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 3.770 \times 10^{-3} - 3.393 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\boxed{S = 3.770 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 22.6 \text{ LPM}}
$$

**Step (iv) — Input Power**

$$
\eta_o = \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{man} \times \eta_m = 0.90 \times 0.85 \times 0.92 = 0.7038
$$

$$
P_{water} = \rho g Q_{act} H_{man} = 850 \times 9.81 \times 3.393 \times 10^{-3} \times 30
$$

$$
P_{water} = 850 \times 9.81 \times 0.10179 = 848.7 \text{ W}
$$

$$
