> **Why this matters for constructional features:** The *length, taper angle, and surface finish* of the nozzle directly govern $C_v$. A shorter nozzle → lower $C_v$. A long conical nozzle with $\approx 12°$–$15°$ taper gives the highest $C_v$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Draft Tube Pressure Recovery (Francis/Kaplan)

**Statement:** A draft tube has inlet velocity $V_2$ at the runner exit and outlet velocity $V_3$. Derive the suction head $H_s$ recovered.

### Step 1: Apply Bernoulli's Equation Between Runner Exit (point 2) and Draft Tube Exit (point 3)

$$
\frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 = \frac{p_3}{\rho g} + \frac{V_3^2}{2g} + z_3
$$

### Step 2: Identify Reference Conditions

- $p_3$ = atmospheric pressure (tailrace level, gauge = 0) → $\frac{p_3}{\rho g} = 0$
- $z_3 - z_2 = H_s$ (the suction head or vertical drop from runner to tailrace)
- Rearranging for $p_2$:

$$
\frac{p_2}{\rho g} = \frac{p_3}{\rho g} + \frac{V_3^2 - V_2^2}{2g} + (z_3 - z_2)
$$

### Step 3: Substitute and Simplify

$$
\frac{p_2}{\rho g} = 0 + \frac{V_3^2 - V_2^2}{2g} + H_s
$$

Since the runner exit pressure $p_2$ must **not fall below the vapor pressure** (else cavitation occurs), the limiting case is $p_2 = p_v$ (vapor pressure).

For maximum pressure recovery, $V_3 \ll V_2$, so the kinetic-energy term dominates:

$$
H_s \approx \frac{V_2^2 - V_3^2}{2g}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constructional Implication:** To *maximize* $H_s$ (and hence the draft tube's recovery), the draft tube must have a **gradually expanding cross-section** so that $V_3 \ll V_2$. The taper angle is limited to **≤ 7° to 8°** to prevent flow separation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Constructional Comparison

| Feature | Pelton Wheel | Francis Turbine | Kaplan Turbine |
|---|---|---|---|
| Turbine type | Impulse | Reaction (mixed flow) | Reaction (axial flow) |
| Head range | High (> 300 m) | Medium (30–300 m) | Low (< 30 m) |
| Specific speed $N_s$ | 10–35 | 60–300 | 300–1000 |
| Casing | Open (no pressure vessel) | Spiral / scroll casing | Scroll casing |
| Water entry | Jet (no pressure at runner) | Guide vane → radial inward | Guide vane → axial |
| Runner flow | Tangential | Radial → axial | Axial throughout |
| Blade adjustment | Not adjustable | Only guide vanes adjustable | Guide vanes + runner blades |
| Draft tube | Not used | Used | Used (often elbow-type) |
| Number of blades | 16–26 (bucket pairs) | 12–24 | 4–8 (large) |
| Pressure at runner inlet | Atmospheric | Above atmospheric | Above atmospheric |
| Cavitation risk | Low | Medium | High (low head → need careful $\sigma$) |
| Typical materials | Cast iron/steel buckets, bronze nozzles | Cast steel runner, steel casing | Stainless steel or bronze blades |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Since physical drawings of turbines cannot be perfectly rendered in Mermaid, the diagrams below show **block-level functional architecture** of each turbine's construction, with arrows indicating flow direction and functional grouping.

## 4.1 Pelton Wheel — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[RESERVOIR / FOREBAY] --> A2[INTAKE GATE]
    A2 --> A3[PENSTOCK: Long steel pipe carrying pressurized water]
    A3 --> A4[NEEDLE NOZZLE ASSEMBLY: Needle valve plus tapered nozzle]
    A4 --> A5[HIGH VELOCITY JET: Velocity equals CV times root 2gH]
    A5 --> A6[RUNNER WITH DOUBLE CUP BUCKETS: Bucket angle 170 deg, splitter ridge in middle]
    A6 --> A7[BRAKE JET / DEFLECTOR: For emergency shutdown]
    A6 --> A8[TAILRACE: Casing encloses runner to prevent splashing]
    A9[GOVERNOR: Controls needle stroke for speed regulation] --> A4
    A10[SHAFT plus BEARINGS: Transmits mechanical power to generator] --> A6
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** Water flows from A1 → A2 → A3 → A4 → A5 → A6 (energy transfer) → A8 (discharge). The governor A9 regulates the jet, and A10 extracts useful work.

---

## 4.2 Francis Turbine — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[RESERVOIR] --> F2[INTAKE plus TRASHRACK]
    F2 --> F3[PENSTOCK]
    F3 --> F4[SPIRAL SCROLL CASING: Constant velocity flow distribution]
    F4 --> F5[STAY VANES: Anti-whirl fixed vanes]
    F5 --> F6[GUIDE VANES WICKET GATES: Adjustable flow control]
    F6 --> F7[FRANCIS RUNNER: Radial inlet axial outlet mixed flow]
    F7 --> F8[DRAFT TUBE: Conical or elbow, expanding section]
    F8 --> F9[TAILRACE]
    F10[GOVERNOR] --> F6
    F11[MAIN SHAFT plus COUPLING] --> F7
    F12[SURGE TANK: Placed on penstock to absorb water hammer] -.-> F3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constructional Key:** The **spiral casing** is the heart of the Francis design. Its volute geometry (Archimedean spiral) ensures water enters the guide vanes with **uniform velocity from all directions**.

---

## 4.3 Kaplan Turbine — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1[LOW HEAD RESERVOIR OR RIVER] --> K2[INTAKE CHANNEL]
    K2 --> K3[PENSTOCK: Short and large diameter]
    K3 --> K4[SCROLL CASING: Smaller radius than Francis]
    K4 --> K5[ADJUSTABLE GUIDE VANES: Pre-swirl control]
    K5 --> K6[KAPLAN RUNNER HUB: Houses oil servomotor for blade pitch]
    K6 --> K7[ADJUSTABLE RUNNER BLADES: 4 to 8 blades twist during operation]
    K7 --> K8[ELBOW TYPE DRAFT TUBE: Curved gradually expanding]
    K8 --> K9[TAILRACE]
    K10[SERVOMOTOR: Adjusts runner blade pitch in real time] --> K6
    K11[GOVERNOR] --> K10
    K11 --> K5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Constructional Highlight:** The Kaplan runner is **doubly regulated** (guide vanes + runner blades move together), which is why it maintains a flat efficiency curve over a wide load range.

---

## 4.4 Cross-Component Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Pelton: High Head] -->|Convert| E1[Kinetic Energy Jet]
    Fr[Francis: Medium Head] -->|Convert| E2[Pressure plus Kinetic Energy]
    Kp[Kaplan: Low Head] -->|Convert| E3[Pressure plus Kinetic Energy]
    E1 -->|Strike| B1[Buckets: Impulse]
    E2 -->|Pass Through| B2[Curved Blades: Mixed Reaction]
    E3 -->|Pass Through| B3[Propeller Blades: Axial Reaction]
    B1 -->|Discharge to| T1[Tailrace at Atmospheric Pressure]
    B2 -->|Pass Through| D1[Draft Tube: Conical]
    B3 -->|Pass Through| D2[Draft Tube: Elbow]
    D1 --> T1
    D2 --> T1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the function of the draft tube in a reaction turbine. Why is it not used in Pelton wheels?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A draft tube is a **gradually expanding tube** fitted at the outlet of a reaction turbine runner. Its functions are:

1. **Converts residual kinetic energy** at the runner exit into pressure energy, recovering a suction head of $H_s = \frac{V_2^2 - V_3^2}{2g}$. This typically saves **15–20%** of available head.
2. **Allows the runner to be set above tailrace level** — easing maintenance access.
3. **Prevents cavitation** by maintaining runner exit pressure above vapor pressure.

It is **not used in Pelton wheels** because Pelton is an impulse turbine — water at the runner is already at atmospheric pressure, and the entire energy transfer has already occurred via the jet. There is no residual pressure head to recover.

*(1 mark for each function, plus 0.5 for the "not used in Pelton" reason. Avoid the pitfall of confusing impulse and reaction — see warning below.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a stay vane? How is it different from a guide vane in a Francis turbine?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- A **stay vane** is a **fixed vane** placed between the scroll casing and the guide vanes in a Francis turbine.
- Its functions are:
  1. **Prevents whirling** of water inside the spiral casing.
  2. **Transmits structural loads** (weight of runner + water pressure) from the upper bearing assembly to the foundation.
- A **guide vane** (wicket gate), in contrast, is **adjustable** — it regulates the flow rate entering the runner and controls power output.
- Stay vanes = fixed (structural), Guide vanes = movable (operational). *(1 mark for the stay vane definition, 1 for functions, 1 for the difference.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, students answer **one question from Module 4** with internal choice. Each question carries **14 marks** split into sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **constructional features of a Pelton wheel**. Discuss the role of the **needle valve** and **splitter ridge** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Pelton wheel has a mean runner diameter of **1.2 m** and is driven by a jet of diameter **100 mm** having a velocity coefficient of **0.98**. The available head at the nozzle is **400 m**. The bucket deflects the jet through **170°**, and the speed ratio is **0.46**. Determine:
  (i) The discharge through the jet
  (ii) The force exerted by the jet on the bucket
  (iii) The power developed by the wheel **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Constructional Features of Pelton Wheel (7 marks)**

*Sketch: Refer to SECTION 4.1 — Pelton Wheel Architecture diagram.*

Key constructional components (2 marks):
- **Runner with buckets** — elliptical double-cup buckets mounted on a circular disc.
- **Nozzle assembly** — needle valve + tapered nozzle.
- **Casing** — open type, prevents splashing, supports bearings.
- **Brake jet** — for emergency shutdown.
- **Deflector** — deflects jet during load rejection.

**Role of Needle Valve (2 marks):**
- The needle is a tapered spear that slides axially inside the nozzle.
- It regulates the **annular flow area** and thus controls discharge $Q$.
- Closing the needle reduces flow → reduces power output.
- Operated by the **governor** to maintain constant speed under varying load.

**Role of Splitter Ridge (1.5 marks):**
- A sharp central **ridge** running along the middle of the bucket.
- Splits the jet into **two equal halves** to prevent the jet from sliding sideways off the bucket at high speeds.
- Also reduces lateral thrust on the bearing.

**Why a double cup (1.5 marks):**
- Reverses the jet direction by ~170°, giving maximum **change of momentum ($V_w$)** and hence maximum force.
- Reduces residual kinetic energy leaving the bucket → higher efficiency.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Mean runner diameter $D = 1.2$ m
- Jet diameter $d = 100$ mm $= 0.10$ m
- Velocity coefficient $C_v = 0.98$
- Net head $H = 400$ m
- Bucket deflection angle $\beta = 170°$
- Speed ratio $k_u = 0.46$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³

**Step 1: Jet velocity**

$$
V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH} = 0.98 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 400}
$$

$$
V_1 = 0.98 \times \sqrt{7848} = 0.98 \times 88.59 = 86.82 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2: Jet area and discharge (i)**

$$
a = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.10)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
Q = a \cdot V_1 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 86.82 = 0.682 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3: Force on bucket (ii)**

For a Pelton bucket with deflection angle $\beta$:

$$
F = \rho Q V_1 (1 - \cos \beta)
$$

$$
\cos 170° = -0.9848
$$

$$
1 - \cos 170° = 1 - (-0.9848) = 1.9848
$$

$$
F = 1000 \times 0.682 \times 86.82 \times 1.9848
$$

$$
F = 1000 \times 0.682 \times 86.82 \times 1.9848 \approx 1.175 \times 10^5 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F \approx 117.5 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4: Power developed (iii)**

First find runner velocity $u$:

$$
u = k_u \cdot \sqrt{2gH} = 0.46 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 400} = 0.46 \times 88.59 = 40.75 \text{ m/s}
$$

Shaft power $P = F \cdot u = 1.175 \times 10^5 \times 40.75$

$$
P = 4.79 \times 10^6 \text{ W} \approx 4787 \text{ kW} \approx 4.79 \text{ MW}
$$

> **Final Answers:**
> (i) $Q = 0.682$ m³/s
> (ii) $F = 117.5$ kN
> (iii) $P = 4.79$ MW

*(Mark split: Step 1 — 1 mark, Step 2 — 1.5 marks, Step 3 — 2 marks, Step 4 — 1.5 marks, presentation — 1 mark)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **constructional features of a Francis turbine** with a neat sketch. Discuss why the **scroll casing is spiral** and how **guide vanes** assist in flow regulation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Francis turbine working under a head of **60 m** develops **500 kW** at a rotational speed of **750 rpm**. The hydraulic efficiency is **85%** and the overall efficiency is **78%**. Calculate:
  (i) The discharge through the turbine
  (ii) The specific speed of the turbine
  (iii) Comment on the suitability of the turbine type **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Constructional Features of Francis Turbine (7 marks)**

*Sketch: Refer to SECTION 4.2 — Francis Turbine diagram.*

Components (2 marks):
- Spiral casing, stay vanes, guide vanes (wicket gates), runner, draft tube, main shaft, bearings.

**Why spiral casing? (2.5 marks):**
- The scroll casing has a **volute (Archimedean spiral) shape** with progressively decreasing cross-section.
- This design ensures that the **flow velocity entering the guide vanes remains constant** along the periphery.
- Flow rate at any radius $Q(r) = \int_0^r V \cdot dA = $ const, achieved because the rate of area decrease compensates for the circumference increase.

**Role of guide vanes (2.5 marks):**
- Adjustable vanes located between stay vanes and runner.
- **Functions:**
  1. Regulate the **flow rate** entering the runner.
  2. Adjust the **inlet flow angle** to match the runner blade entry angle, minimizing shock losses.
  3. Permit efficient **partial-load operation**.
- Operated synchronously by a **regulating ring** linked to the governor.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $H = 60$ m, $P = 500$ kW, $N = 750$ rpm
- $\eta_h = 0.85$, $\eta_o = 0.78$

**Step 1: Discharge (i)**

$$
\eta_o = \frac{P}{\rho g Q H}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{P}{\rho g H \eta_o} = \frac{500 \times 10^3}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 60 \times 0.78}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{500000}{459108} = 1.089 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2: Specific speed (ii)**

$$
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}
$$

$H^{5/4} = 60^{1.25} = 60 \times 60^{0.25} = 60 \times 2.783 = 166.98$

$$
N_s = \frac{750 \times \sqrt{500}}{166.98} = \frac{750 \times 22.36}{166.98}
$$

$$
N_s = \frac{16770}{166.98} \approx 100.4 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 3: Comment on suitability (iii)**

- $N_s \approx 100$ falls within the **Francis range (60–300)**. **(1 mark)**
- Head of 60 m is in the **medium-head range** consistent with Francis. **(1 mark)**
- Therefore, **Francis turbine is the correct choice** for this application. **(0.5 mark)**
- The relatively low specific speed (100) suggests this Francis unit operates **closer to the high-head end** of the Francis spectrum, with a more **radial flow** character. **(0.5 mark)**

> **Final Answers:**
> (i) $Q = 1.089$ m³/s
> (ii) $N_s = 100.4$ rpm
> (iii) Francis turbine is appropriate.

*(Mark split: Step 1 — 2 marks, Step 2 — 2 marks, Step 3 — 2 marks, units/presentation — 1 mark)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones — KTU Board Examiner's Perspective:**
>
> 1. **Conflating impulse and reaction** — A Pelton runner is at **atmospheric pressure**; do not mention scroll casing or draft tube for Pelton. **(–2 marks if confused)**
> 2. **Missing the splitter ridge role** — A sketch without the splitter ridge labelled will lose **1 mark** in constructional drawings.
> 3. **Forgetting velocity coefficient in Pelton numerics** — If $C_v$ is given, students often write $V_1 = \sqrt{2gH}$ directly. **Full marks demand** $V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$.
> 4. **Specific speed units confusion** — KTU uses the **metric convention** (kW, m, rpm). Do **NOT** use the imperial ($N_s = N\sqrt{P}/H^{5/4}$ with HP and ft) version.
> 5. **Draft tube pressure recovery formula** — Use $H_s = (V_2^2 - V_3^2)/2g$, not the inverted form.
> 6. **Thoma's cavitation parameter** — When asked, compute $\sigma = (H_{atm} - H_v - H_s)/H$. A common error is to forget $H_s$ (suction head).
> 7. **Diagrams are mandatory** — A 14-mark answer on constructional features **without a labelled diagram** typically loses **2–3 marks** automatically. Always include one, even if rough.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — High-Yield Points**

- **Pelton Wheel:**
  - Impulse turbine, high head (> 300 m), atmospheric pressure inside.
  - Bucket angle ≈ 170°, splitter ridge in middle, $k_u \approx 0.46$.
  - No draft tube, no scroll casing; needle valve controls flow.
  - $V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$, $F = \rho Q V_1 (1 - \cos\beta)$, $P = F \cdot u$.

- **Francis Turbine:**
  - Mixed-flow reaction turbine, medium head (30–300 m).
  - **Spiral casing** (volute) → uniform velocity at guide vane entry.
  - **Stay vanes** (fixed, structural) vs. **Guide vanes** (movable, flow control).
  - **Draft tube** recovers $\approx 15$–$20\%$ of head; $H_s = (V_2^2 - V_3^2)/2g$.
  - $N_s$ range: **60–300 rpm** (metric).

- **Kaplan Turbine:**
  - Axial-flow reaction turbine, low head (< 30 m).
  - **Double regulation** (guide vanes + runner blades).
  - **Elbow-type draft tube** common.
  - $N_s$ range: **300–1000 rpm** (metric).

- **Cavitation Rule of Thumb:** $\sigma_{\text{actual}} > \sigma_{\text{Thoma}}$ (critical) to avoid cavitation.

- **Specific Speed Decision Tree:**
  - $N_s < 35$ → **Pelton**
  - $35 \le N_s \le 300$ → **Francis**
  - $N_s > 300$ → **Kaplan / Propeller**

- **Why Draft Tube?**
  - Recovers kinetic energy.
  - Increases effective head.
  - Allows runner above tailrace.
  - Reduces cavitation risk.

- **Why Spiral Casing?**
  - Maintains constant inlet velocity.
  - Equal distribution to all guide vanes.
  - Smooth flow, minimum eddy losses.

- **Constant Reminders for Numerical Problems:**
  - State $C_v$ and $C_c$ assumptions.
  - Use $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³.
  - Draw a velocity triangle wherever applicable.
  - Final answer with **units**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Hydraulic Machines (Turbines & Pumps)** covered under Module 4 of KTU's PCIET302 syllabus, **Design Considerations** refer to the systematic engineering methodology used to proportion the principal geometric and kinematic parameters of a hydraulic machine (such as its **runner/wheel diameter, blade angles, jet diameter, number of blades, width, and shaft speed**) so that the machine operates at its **best efficiency point (BEP)** for a specified **net head (H)**, **discharge (Q)**, and **power output (P)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Design considerations of hydraulic turbines and pumps encompass the analytical determination of main dimensions using velocity triangles, specific speed, head-flow-power relationships, and cavitation-free operating zones, ensuring mechanical robustness, hydraulic efficiency, and structural integrity.*

Mathematically, the design problem reduces to solving the **Euler's turbine equation** coupled with the **velocity triangle relationships**:

$$
\frac{P}{\rho g Q} = U_1 V_{w1} - U_2 V_{w2} \quad \text{(for Turbines)}
$$

$$
H_{\text{pump}} = \frac{V_{w2} U_2 - V_{w1} U_1}{g} \quad \text{(for Pumps)}
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Designing a Ceiling Fan**
> 
> Imagine you are an engineer asked to design a ceiling fan for a room. Before sketching a single blade, you must ask:
> - **Purpose:** Cooling? Decoration? Industrial ventilation?
> - **Air volume (Q):** How much air must be moved per second?
> - **Rotational speed (N):** Fast breeze or slow rotation?
> - **Power (P):** Energy budget allowed?
> - **Noise, blade shape, diameter:** All derived *after* the core numbers.
>
> **Hydraulic machine design is exactly the same logic, applied to water.**
> 
> - **Q (discharge)** ↔ Volume of air the fan moves.
> - **H (head)** ↔ Pressure the fan must overcome.
> - **P (shaft power)** ↔ Electrical power consumed.
> - **D (runner diameter)** ↔ Blade tip diameter.
> - **Blade angle (β)** ↔ Blade tilt/pitch.
> 
> You *cannot* draw the blade before fixing **Q, H, N, η**. The same principle governs Pelton wheels, Francis runners, Kaplan propellers, and Centrifugal pumps.

## 1.3 Why "Design Considerations" is a High-Yield KTU Topic

| Aspect | Significance in KTU Exam |
|---|---|
| **Numerical weightage** | 7–14 mark problems every semester |
| **Conceptual weightage** | Definition-type questions in Part A |
| **Industry relevance** | Real-world pump/turbine selection in KSEB, KWA, BHEL recruitments |
| **CO Mapping** | CO4–CO5 of PCIET302 |

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants must be memorized for KTU exams:
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Density of water: **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
> - Specific weight of water: **γ = ρg = 9810 N/m³**
> - 1 HP = **745.7 W** ≈ **0.746 kW**
> - 1 kW = **1.341 HP**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Design Philosophy — A 7-Step Logical Framework

> [!NOTE]
> Every KTU design problem follows this **universal 7-step framework**, regardless of machine type:

1. **Identify machine type** (Impulse / Reaction turbine / Centrifugal pump).
2. **Specify input data:** Net head H (m), Discharge Q (m³/s), Speed N (rpm), Efficiency η.
3. **Compute specific speed (Ns)** to validate the chosen machine category.
4. **Compute runner peripheral speed U** from velocity ratio (Ku).
5. **Resolve velocity triangles** to find blade angles and absolute velocities.
6. **Compute principal dimensions:** D (diameter), d (jet/bore), B (width), n (number of blades).
7. **Check for cavitation (σ)** and mechanical safety.

## 2.2 Machine-Type Identification via Specific Speed

The **specific speed (Ns)** is the single most powerful tool for design classification:

$$
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} \quad \text{(for Turbines, metric units)}
$$

$$
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}} \quad \text{(alternative form using Q)}
$$

$$
N_s' = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}} \quad \text{(for Pumps)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick — Specific Speed Range:**
> 
> | Ns Range | Machine Type |
> |---|---|
> | 8 to 30 (or 10–35) | Pelton Wheel (single jet) |
> | 30 to 60 | Pelton (multi-jet) |
> | 60 to 300 | Francis Turbine |
> | 300 to 900 | Kaplan / Propeller Turbine |
> | 90–400 (pump form) | Centrifugal Pump |

## 2.3 Design Considerations — Pelton Wheel

A Pelton wheel is an **impulse turbine** with the following design sequence:

**Step 1: Jet velocity**
$$
V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH}
$$
where Cv = 0.98 to 0.99 (velocity coefficient).

**Step 2: Wheel (runner) peripheral speed**
$$
U = \phi \cdot V_1 \quad \text{where } \phi = \frac{U}{V_1} \approx 0.43 \text{ to } 0.48
$$

**Step 3: Wheel diameter**
$$
D = \frac{60 U}{\pi N}
$$

**Step 4: Jet diameter (d)**
$$
Q = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 V_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad d = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi V_1}}
$$

**Step 5: Number of jets**
$$
n_{\text{jets}} = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{Q_{\text{per jet}}}
$$

**Step 6: Bucket dimensions (proportional to jet diameter)**
- Bucket width at splitter: **B = 5d**
- Bucket depth: **T = 1.2d**
- Bucket radial length: **L = 2.5d to 3d**
- Splitter ridge angle: kept sharp

## 2.4 Design Considerations — Francis Turbine

Francis is a **mixed-flow reaction turbine**. Design differs because flow enters and leaves under pressure.

**Step 1: Runner peripheral speed**
$$
U_1 = \pi D_1 N / 60
$$
where D1 is the outer runner diameter (at inlet).

**Step 2: Flow velocity (radial component)**
$$
V_{f1} = K_f \sqrt{2gH} \quad \text{where } K_f \approx 0.15 \text{ to } 0.20
$$

**Step 3: Velocity ratios**
$$
K_u = \frac{U_1}{\sqrt{2gH}} \approx 0.65 \text{ to } 0.75
$$
$$
K_{u2} = \frac{U_2}{\sqrt{2gH}} \approx 0.20 \text{ to } 0.40
$$

**Step 4: Inlet blade angle (β1)**
$$
\tan \beta_1 = \frac{V_{f1}}{U_1 - V_{w1}}
$$

**Step 5: Outlet blade angle (β2)**
$$
\tan \beta_2 = \frac{V_{f2}}{V_{w2}}
$$
For radial exit (best efficiency): **V_{w2} = 0**, hence β2 = 90°.

## 2.5 Design Considerations — Kaplan Turbine

Kaplan is **axial-flow reaction** with adjustable blades.

- Hub diameter: **Dh = 0.4 D** (typical)
- Outer diameter: **D** (from specific speed)
- Guide vane angle governs flow pre-swirl
- Runner blade angle (φ) is adjustable: usually 15°–30° at design point

## 2.6 Design Considerations — Centrifugal Pump

For pumps, the design **output** is the impeller delivering a required head H against flow Q.

**Step 1: Impeller peripheral speed at outlet**
$$
U_2 = K_u \sqrt{2gH_m} \quad K_u \approx 0.95 \text{ to } 1.10
$$

**Step 2: Outlet diameter**
$$
D_2 = \frac{60 U_2}{\pi N}
$$

**Step 3: Width at outlet**
$$
B_2 = \frac{Q}{\pi D_2 V_{f2}} \quad \text{where } V_{f2} \approx 0.04 \sqrt{2gH}
$$

**Step 4: Inlet diameter (eye)**
$$
D_1 = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi V_{f1}}} \quad V_{f1} \approx 2 \text{ to } 3 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5: Manometric head**
$$
H_m = \frac{V_{w2} U_2}{g}
$$
(for Vw1 = 0, no pre-whirl)

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — At a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD KTU FORMULA TABLE — Module 4 Design**

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Applicable To |
|---|---|---|---|
| Jet velocity | $V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$ | m/s | Pelton |
| Wheel speed | $U = \phi \sqrt{2gH}$ | m/s | Pelton |
| Wheel diameter | $D = 60U/(\pi N)$ | m | All turbines |
| Jet diameter | $d = \sqrt{4Q/(\pi V_1)}$ | m | Pelton |
| Specific speed (turbine) | $N_s = N\sqrt{P}/H^{5/4}$ | (SI metric) | All |
| Specific speed (pump) | $N_s' = N\sqrt{Q}/H^{3/4}$ | (SI metric) | Pumps |
| Runaway speed | $N_r = 1.8$ to $2.2 \times N$ | rpm | All turbines |
| Thoma's σ (cavitation) | $\sigma = (H_{sv} - H_s)/(H_s - H_v)$ | — | Reaction turbines |
| Euler head (turbine) | $H = (U_1 V_{w1} - U_2 V_{w2})/g$ | m | All turbines |
| Euler head (pump) | $H_m = (V_{w2} U_2 - V_{w1} U_1)/g$ | m | Pumps |
| Inlet blade angle | $\tan \beta_1 = V_{f1}/(U_1 - V_{w1})$ | rad/° | Francis |
| Outlet blade angle | $\tan \beta_2 = V_{f2}/V_{w2}$ | rad/° | Francis |
| Bucket width (Pelton) | $B = 5d$ | m | Pelton |
| Bucket depth (Pelton) | $T = 1.2d$ | m | Pelton |
| Bucket length (Pelton) | $L = 2.5d$ | m | Pelton |

## 2.8 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> These design equations are used by:
> - **Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB)** — Idukki, Mullapperiyar turbine selection
> - **BHEL Bhopal** — Francis runners for 700 MW units
> - **KSIDC Pump Industries** — Irrigation pump sizing
> - **L&T Construction** — Hydro-project tender specifications
> 
> A single miscalculation in **specific speed** can lead to a **wrong machine selection**, causing catastrophic efficiency loss (up to 40%) and cavitation damage worth crores.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimum Velocity Ratio for a Pelton Wheel

### Statement
Show that the **maximum hydraulic efficiency** of a single-jet Pelton wheel occurs when **U/V1 = 0.5 cos φ**, where φ is the bucket exit angle (typically 15°).

### Full Derivation

**Step 1: Euler's equation for Pelton wheel (only one wheel circumference):**
$$
H = \frac{U (V_{w1} - V_{w2})}{g}
$$

**Step 2: Bucket geometry — the jet strikes the splitter and turns through 180° − 2φ:**
The relative velocity leaving the bucket makes angle φ with the tangential direction (opposite to Vw1). Therefore:

$$
V_{w1} = V_1 \quad \text{(jet velocity)} \\
V_{w2} = U - V_r \cos \phi
$$

**Step 3: Relativistic loss — relative velocity reduces after impact:**
$$
V_r = k V_r' = k (V_1 - U)
$$
where k = friction coefficient of bucket (0.95 to 0.98).

For an **ideal smooth bucket (k = 1, no shock loss)**:
$$
V_r = V_1 - U
$$

**Step 4: Substitute into Euler head:**
$$
H = \frac{U \cdot V_1 - U(U - V_r \cos\phi)}{g}
$$
$$
H = \frac{U \cdot V_1 - U \cdot U + U \cdot V_r \cos\phi}{g}
$$
$$
H = \frac{U(V_1 - U + V_r \cos\phi)}{g}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute V_r = V_1 − U:**
$$
H = \frac{U [V_1 - U + (V_1 - U)\cos\phi]}{g}
$$
$$
H = \frac{U (V_1 - U)(1 + \cos\phi)}{g}
$$

**Step 6: Hydraulic efficiency:**
$$
\eta_h = \frac{2gH}{V_1^2} = \frac{2U(V_1 - U)(1+\cos\phi)}{V_1^2}
$$

**Step 7: Maximize ηh w.r.t. U by setting dηh/dU = 0:**

Let x = U/V1 (the velocity ratio). Then:

$$
\eta_h = 2 x (1 - x) (1 + \cos\phi)
$$

$$
\frac{d\eta_h}{dx} = 2 (1 + \cos\phi)(1 - 2x) = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{1 - 2x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac{U}{V_1} = 0.5}
$$

**Step 8: Maximum efficiency value (substituting x = 0.5):**
$$
\eta_{h,\max} = 2 (0.5)(1 - 0.5)(1 + \cos\phi) = 0.5 (1 + \cos\phi)
$$

For **φ = 15°**:
$$
\eta_{h,\max} = 0.5 (1 + \cos 15°) = 0.5 (1 + 0.966) = 0.983 \approx 98.3\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This 98.3% is the **ideal hydraulic efficiency** — actual overall efficiency is always lower due to mechanical, bearing, windage, and splash losses (typical overall efficiency: 85–90%).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Speed for Turbines

### Statement
Derive the relationship **Ns = N√P / H^(5/4)** for turbines.

### Derivation

**Step 1: Define specific speed:**
Specific speed is the speed of a geometrically similar turbine that produces **unit power (1 kW)** under **unit head (1 m)**.

$$
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}
$$

**Step 2: From Euler's equation:**
$$
P = \rho g Q H \eta
$$

**Step 3: For geometrically similar machines, Q ∝ D²√H:**
$$
Q = K_1 D^2 \sqrt{H}
$$

**Step 4: Dimensional analysis — U ∝ √(2gH):**
$$
U = \phi \sqrt{2gH}
$$

**Step 5: Since U = πDN/60:**
$$
D = \frac{60 U}{\pi N} = \frac{60 \phi \sqrt{2gH}}{\pi N}
$$

**Step 6: Substitute into Q:**
$$
Q = K_1 \left(\frac{60 \phi \sqrt{2gH}}{\pi N}\right)^2 \sqrt{H} = \frac{K_2 H^{3/2}}{N^2}
$$

where K2 = (60φ)²·2g·K1/π².

**Step 7: Power becomes:**
$$
P = \rho g \eta \cdot \frac{K_2 H^{3/2}}{N^2} \cdot H = \frac{K_3 H^{5/2}}{N^2}
$$

**Step 8: Rearrange for N:**
$$
N = \frac{K_3' H^{5/4}}{\sqrt{P}}
$$

**Step 9: Express as specific speed (per unit H, P):**
$$
\boxed{N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cavitation Parameter (Thoma's σ)

### Statement
Derive Thoma's cavitation coefficient σ.

### Derivation

**Step 1: Pressure at the runner eye (using Bernoulli, datum at tail race):**
$$
\frac{P_e}{\rho g} = \frac{P_a}{\rho g} - H_s
$$
where Hs = static suction head (positive if above tail water level).

**Step 2: Pressure at vapor pressure for cavitation inception:**
$$
\frac{P_v}{\rho g} = \frac{P_e}{\rho g} - H_{\text{net suction}}
$$

**Step 3: Net Positive Suction Head available (NPSHa):**
$$
\text{NPSH}_a = \frac{P_e - P_v}{\rho g} = H_s + \frac{P_a - P_v}{\rho g}
$$

**Step 4: Express as a non-dimensional ratio:**
$$
\sigma = \frac{\text{NPSH}_a}{H} = \frac{H_s + (P_a - P_v)/(\rho g)}{H}
$$

**Step 5: Solving for maximum allowable Hs:**
$$
\boxed{H_{s,\text{critical}} = \sigma_c H - \frac{P_a - P_v}{\rho g}}
$$

**Step 6: At sea level, Pa = 10.3 m of water, Pv (20°C) = 0.24 m:**
$$
H_{s,\text{max}} = \sigma_c H - 10.06 \text{ m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> If actual Hs exceeds Hs,max → **cavitation occurs** → blade pitting, vibration, efficiency drop.

---

## 3.4 Design Numerical Example — Pelton Wheel

**Given:**
- Net head H = 200 m
- Discharge Q = 0.5 m³/s
- Speed N = 300 rpm
- Cv = 0.98, Ku = 0.46, φ (bucket exit angle) = 15°
- ηoverall = 85%

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Jet velocity**
$$
V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH} = 0.98 \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 200}
$$
$$
V_1 = 0.98 \sqrt{3924} = 0.98 \times 62.64 = 61.39 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2: Wheel peripheral speed**
$$
U = K_u V_1 = 0.46 \times 61.39 = 28.24 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3: Wheel diameter**
$$
D = \frac{60 U}{\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 28.24}{\pi \times 300} = \frac{1694.4}{942.48} = 1.798 \text{ m} \approx 1.8 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Jet diameter**
$$
d = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi V_1}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.5}{\pi \times 61.39}}
$$
$$
d = \sqrt{\frac{2}{192.85}} = \sqrt{0.01037} = 0.1018 \text{ m} \approx 102 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5: Bucket dimensions**
$$
B = 5d = 0.509 \text{ m}, \quad T = 1.2d = 0.122 \text{ m}, \quad L = 2.5d = 0.255 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6: Power developed**
$$
P = \rho g Q H \eta = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 \times 200 \times 0.85
$$
$$
P = 833{,}850 \text{ W} = 833.85 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 7: Specific speed (verification)**
$$
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} = \frac{300 \times \sqrt{833.85}}{(200)^{1.25}}
$$
$$
H^{5/4} = 200^{1.25} = 379.27
$$
$$
N_s = \frac{300 \times 28.876}{379.27} = \frac{8662.9}{379.27} = 22.84
$$

Since **Ns ≈ 23**, this confirms a **Pelton wheel design** ✓ (range 8–30 for single jet).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Machine Selection

| Parameter | Pelton | Francis | Kaplan | Centrifugal Pump |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Head (m) | High (>250) | Medium (30–400) | Low (<60) | Up to 100 |
| Specific speed | 8–30 | 60–300 | 300–900 | 90–400 (pump form) |
| Flow direction | Tangential | Mixed (radial→axial) | Axial | Radial outward |
| Pressure at inlet | Atmospheric | Above atm | Above atm | Above atm |
| Adjustable blades | No | No | Yes | No |
| Cavitation risk | None | High | High | High |
| Best efficiency | 88–90% | 90–92% | 92–94% | 80–85% |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Design Workflow Diagram — Hydraulic Machines

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Define Q, H, N, eta"] --> B["Compute Specific Speed Ns"]
    B --> C{Ns Decision}
    C -->|Ns less than 30| D["Select Pelton Wheel"]
    C -->|Ns 60 to 300| E["Select Francis Turbine"]
    C -->|Ns 300 to 900| F["Select Kaplan Turbine"]
    C -->|Pump form| G["Select Centrifugal Pump"]
    D --> H["Compute V1, U, D, d, bucket dims"]
    E --> I["Resolve velocity triangles"]
    F --> J["Compute hub and outer diameters"]
    G --> K["Compute D2, B2, blade angles"]
    H --> L["Check runaway speed"]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M["Check cavitation sigma"]
    M --> N["Finalize geometric design"]
    N --> O["End: Report dimensions"]
```

## 4.2 Pelton Wheel Velocity Triangle — Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Absolute Velocity V1"] --> B["Whirl component Vw1 equals V1"]
    B --> C["Peripheral velocity U at bucket tip"]
    C --> D["Relative velocity Vr1 equals V1 minus U"]
    D --> E["Bucket deflects flow by 180 minus 2 phi"]
    E --> F["Exit relative velocity Vr2 at angle phi"]
    F --> G["Whirl component Vw2 equals U minus Vr2 cos phi"]
    G --> H["Compute Euler Head: H equals U times Vw1 minus Vw2 over g"]
```

## 4.3 Francis Turbine — Inlet and Outlet Velocity Triangles

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Inlet Triangle At D1"] --> B["Vw1 from guide vane"]
    B --> C["Vf1 radial component"]
    C --> D["U1 tangential blade speed"]
    D --> E["Beta1 inlet blade angle"]
    E --> F["Outlet Triangle At D2"]
    F --> G["Vw2 ideally equals zero for radial exit"]
    G --> H["Beta2 equals 90 degrees at best efficiency"]
    H --> I["Euler Head H equals U1 times Vw1 over g"]
```

## 4.4 Centrifugal Pump — Design Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input: Q, H_m, N"] --> B["Compute Manometric Head from Euler"]
    B --> C["Select Ku ratio between 0.95 and 1.10"]
    C --> D["Compute U2 equals Ku times sqrt of 2gHm"]
    D --> E["Compute D2 equals 60 times U2 over pi times N"]
    E --> F["Compute Vf2 equals 0.04 times sqrt of 2gHm"]
    F --> G["Compute B2 equals Q over pi times D2 times Vf2"]
    G --> H["Compute Vw2 equals g times Hm over U2"]
    H --> I["Compute Beta2 from tan inverse of Vf2 over Vw2 minus U2"]
    I --> J["Compute inlet eye D1 using Vf1 equals 2 to 3 m/s"]
    J --> K["Finalize Impeller Geometry"]
```

## 4.5 Specific Speed Selection Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Specific Speed Ns"] --> B{Ns less than 30}
    A --> C{Ns 30 to 60}
    A --> D{Ns 60 to 300}
    A --> E{Ns above 300}
    B --> F["Pelton single jet"]
    C --> G["Pelton multi jet"]
    D --> H["Francis Turbine"]
    E --> I["Kaplan Turbine"]
```

## 4.6 Mermaid Compilation Safety Notes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - All node IDs use **purely alphanumeric labels** (A, B, C… step1, step2).
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) used as node names.
> - Labels are written in **plain uppercase alphanumeric text only** — no markdown bold, italics, or HTML tags inside quoted labels.
> - This guarantees Mermaid syntax compliance for all KTU digital platforms.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define **specific speed** of a turbine. How is it used in selecting the type of turbine?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Specific speed of a turbine is defined as the speed of a geometrically similar turbine which would develop a **unit power (1 kW)** when working under a **unit head (1 m)**. Mathematically:

$$
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}
$$

It is a non-dimensional classification parameter that helps in selecting the type of turbine for given operating conditions:
- Low Ns (8–30) → Pelton wheel
- Medium Ns (60–300) → Francis turbine
- High Ns (300–900) → Kaplan turbine

**(Mark distribution: 1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark selection ranges)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What are the **design considerations** for the bucket dimensions of a Pelton wheel?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The bucket dimensions of a Pelton wheel are designed in proportion to the **jet diameter (d)** to ensure smooth flow deflection without spillage. The standard proportional design considerations are:

- **Width of bucket (B)** = 5d — to fully intercept the jet
- **Depth of bucket (T)** = 1.2d — to contain the deflected jet
- **Radial length of bucket (L)** = 2.5d to 3d — sufficient turning length
- **Splitter ridge** — sharp-edged to split the jet equally
- **Bucket exit angle (φ)** = 10° to 15° — to maximize efficiency

**(Mark distribution: 1 mark intro, 2 marks proportional relations)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** [7 marks] A Pelton wheel is to be designed for the following specifications:
- Net head H = 150 m
- Discharge Q = 0.4 m³/s
- Speed N = 320 rpm
- Coefficient of velocity Cv = 0.97
- Speed ratio Ku = 0.45
- Overall efficiency ηo = 86%

Determine: (i) Wheel diameter D, (ii) Jet diameter d, (iii) Number of jets if minimum jet diameter = 50 mm.

**(b)** [7 marks] For a Francis turbine working under a head of 60 m, speed 700 rpm, develop the design procedure showing how you would determine the inlet and outlet blade angles from the velocity triangles. State clearly all standard velocity ratios used in design.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Pelton Wheel Design [7 marks]

**Step 1: Jet velocity (1 mark)**
$$
V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH} = 0.97 \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 150}
$$
$$
V_1 = 0.97 \sqrt{2943} = 0.97 \times 54.25 = 52.62 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2: Peripheral velocity (1 mark)**
$$
U = K_u V_1 = 0.45 \times 52.62 = 23.68 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3: Wheel diameter (1 mark)**
$$
D = \frac{60 U}{\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 23.68}{\pi \times 320} = \frac{1420.8}{1005.31} = 1.413 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Jet diameter (1 mark)**
$$
d = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi V_1}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.4}{\pi \times 52.62}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.6}{165.30}} = \sqrt{0.00968} = 0.0984 \text{ m} = 98.4 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5: Check minimum jet diameter constraint (1 mark)**
Required d = 98.4 mm > minimum allowed 50 mm. Hence **single jet is sufficient**.
$$
\boxed{n_{\text{jets}} = 1}
$$

**Step 6: Bucket dimensions (1 mark)**
$$
B = 5d = 492 \text{ mm}; \quad T = 1.2d = 118 \text{ mm}; \quad L = 2.5d = 246 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7: Power output (1 mark)**
$$
P = \rho g Q H \eta = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.4 \times 150 \times 0.86 = 506.2 \text{ kW}
$$

#### Part (b) — Francis Turbine Velocity Triangles [7 marks]

**Step 1: Inlet velocity triangle parameters (2 marks)**
At runner inlet (diameter D1):
- Peripheral speed: $U_1 = \pi D_1 N / 60$
- Radial (flow) velocity: $V_{f1} = K_f \sqrt{2gH}$, with $K_f \approx 0.15$ to $0.20$
- Whirl velocity: $V_{w1} = K_{u1} \sqrt{2gH}$, with $K_{u1} \approx 0.65$ to $0.75$

**Step 2: Inlet blade angle (1 mark)**
$$
\tan \beta_1 = \frac{V_{f1}}{U_1 - V_{w1}}
$$
This angle is measured relative to the tangential direction at the inlet.

**Step 3: Outlet velocity triangle (2 marks)**
At runner outlet (diameter D2):
- $U_2 = \pi D_2 N / 60$
- $V_{f2}$ is taken equal to $V_{f1}$ (continuity)
- For best efficiency (radial exit): $V_{w2} = 0$
- Hence $K_{u2} = 0.20$ to $0.40$

**Step 4: Outlet blade angle (1 mark)**
$$
\tan \beta_2 = \frac{V_{f2}}{V_{w2}}
$$
With $V_{w2} = 0$, $\beta_2 = 90°$ (radial exit).

**Step 5: Euler head verification (1 mark)**
$$
H = \frac{U_1 V_{w1} - U_2 V_{w2}}{g} = \frac{U_1 V_{w1}}{g}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** [7 marks] A centrifugal pump is to deliver **0.05 m³/s** of water against a manometric head of **25 m** at a speed of **1450 rpm**. Calculate:
- (i) Impeller diameter D2
- (ii) Width of impeller at outlet B2
- (iii) Vane angle at outlet β2

Take velocity ratio Ku = 1.0 and flow ratio Kf = 0.04.

**(b)** [7 marks] Explain with a neat sketch the **design considerations for the guide mechanism** of a Kaplan turbine. Why is the Kaplan turbine preferred for low-head, high-discharge applications?

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Centrifugal Pump Design [7 marks]

**Step 1: Manometric head and velocities (2 marks)**
$$
\sqrt{2gH_m} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 25} = \sqrt{490.5} = 22.15 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2: Peripheral speed at outlet (1 mark)**
$$
U_2 = K_u \sqrt{2gH_m} = 1.0 \times 22.15 = 22.15 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3: Impeller diameter (1 mark)**
$$
D_2 = \frac{60 U_2}{\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 22.15}{\pi \times 1450} = \frac{1329}{4555.3} = 0.2918 \text{ m} \approx 292 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4: Flow velocity at outlet (1 mark)**
$$
V_{f2} = K_f \sqrt{2gH_m} = 0.04 \times 22.15 = 0.886 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5: Width at outlet (1 mark)**
$$
B_2 = \frac{Q}{\pi D_2 V_{f2}} = \frac{0.05}{\pi \times 0.2918 \times 0.886} = \frac{0.05}{0.8123} = 0.0616 \text{ m} = 61.6 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6: Whirl velocity at outlet (1 mark)**
$$
V_{w2} = \frac{g H_m}{U_2} = \frac{9.81 \times 25}{22.15} = 11.07 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 7: Vane angle at outlet (1 mark)**
$$
\tan \beta_2 = \frac{V_{f2}}{V_{w2} - U_2} = \frac{0.886}{11.07 - 22.15} = \frac{0.886}{-11.08} = -0.0800
$$
$$
\beta_2 = \tan^{-1}(-0.0800) = -4.57°
$$

Negative sign indicates **backward-curved vanes** (as expected for centrifugal pumps).

---

#### Part (b) — Kaplan Turbine Guide Mechanism [7 marks]

**Step 1: Concept (2 marks)**
The **guide mechanism** (wicket gate assembly) of a Kaplan turbine consists of a ring of movable guide vanes surrounding the runner. These vanes regulate the flow rate entering the runner and create the desired **pre-whirl** (Vw1) at the runner inlet.

**Step 2: Design considerations (3 marks)**
- **Number of guide vanes:** Typically 16 to 24, depending on design discharge.
- **Guide vane profile:** Airfoil-shaped (NACA profile) to minimize flow separation.
- **Synchronizing link:** Connects all guide vanes so they move simultaneously.
- **Servomotor control:** Hydraulic servomotor rotates the runner blades by 90° relative to guide vane movement (linkage ratio).
- **Sealing rings:** At guide vane tips to minimize leakage losses.
- **Material:** Stainless steel (CA6NM) for cavitation resistance.

**Step 3: Why preferred for low-head applications (2 marks)**
- **High specific speed (300–900)** — handles large discharges efficiently.
- **Adjustable runner blades** maintain high efficiency across varying loads (90–94% over wide flow range).
- **Compact runner diameter** even at high Q — economical civil structures.
- **No draft tube cavitation issues** at low heads when properly designed.
- **Ideal for Kerala's low-head sites** — Idukki, Idamalayar.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES — WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS:**
> 
> 1. **Mixing up speed ratio formulas:** For Pelton, Ku = U/V1 ≈ 0.46; for Francis, Ku = U1/√(2gH) ≈ 0.65–0.75. Students often interchange these.
> 
> 2. **Specific speed formulas:** Turbine formula has H^(5/4); Pump formula has H^(3/4). Confusing these leads to wrong machine selection.
> 
> 3. **Unit conversions:** H = 200 m, g = 9.81 m/s² → √(2gH) has units m/s. Forgetting to square-root properly is a common error.
> 
> 4. **Sign convention in velocity triangles:** Direction of Vw2 (with or against rotation) determines the sign of the Euler head term.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting the bucket proportions:** Pelton bucket width = 5d, NOT 2d or 10d.
> 
> 6. **Not stating assumptions:** KTU examiners deduct marks if Cv, Ku, Kf are not clearly stated.
> 
> 7. **Skipping cavitation check:** For Francis/Kaplan/pumps, Thoma's σ must always be computed.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION — DESIGN CONSIDERATIONS MODULE 4**
> 
> ✅ **Pelton Wheel Design:**
> - Jet velocity: V1 = Cv·√(2gH)
> - Wheel speed: U = Ku·V1, Ku ≈ 0.43–0.48
> - Wheel diameter: D = 60U/(πN)
> - Jet diameter: d = √(4Q/(πV1))
> - Bucket width = 5d; Depth = 1.2d; Length = 2.5d
> - Best efficiency condition: U/V1 = 0.5
> - Maximum theoretical ηh = 0.5(1 + cos φ)
> 
> ✅ **Francis Turbine Design:**
> - Mixed flow, inward reaction
> - Ku1 ≈ 0.65–0.75; Ku2 ≈ 0.20–0.40
> - Kf ≈ 0.15–0.20
> - Outlet blade angle β2 = 90° (radial exit for max efficiency)
> - Inlet angle: tan β1 = Vf1/(U1 − Vw1)
> - Ns range: 60–300
> 
> ✅ **Kaplan Turbine Design:**
> - Axial flow, fully reaction
> - Adjustable runner blades + adjustable guide vanes
> - Ns range: 300–900
> - Hub diameter ≈ 0.4 D
> - Preferred for low-head, high-discharge sites
> 
> ✅ **Centrifugal Pump Design:**
> - Outward radial flow
> - Ku ≈ 0.95–1.10
> - Kf ≈ 0.04
> - Vw1 = 0 (no pre-whirl at inlet)
> - Manometric head: Hm = Vw2·U2/g
> - Backward-curved vanes (β2 negative)
> 
> ✅ **Universal Design Steps:**
> 1. Specify Q, H, N, η
> 2. Compute Ns → select machine type
> 3. Compute U from velocity ratio
> 4. Compute principal diameters
> 5. Resolve velocity triangles
> 6. Check cavitation (Thoma's σ)
> 7. Verify mechanical strength (runaway speed = 1.8N to 2.2N)
> 
> ✅ **Cavitation Check (Thoma's σ):**
> - σ = NPSHa/H
> - σ_critical from Moody chart
> - Hs,max = σc·H − (Pa − Pv)/(ρg)
> - At sea level: Hs,max ≈ σc·H − 10.06 m
> 
> ✅ **Runaway Speed:**
> - Nrunaway = (1.8 to 2.2) × Ndesign
> - Generator must withstand this without damage.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accessories
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Accessories

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Hydraulic Machine Accessories

**Hydraulic Machine Accessories** are auxiliary components and devices that are installed alongside the main hydraulic machines (turbines and pumps) to enhance their operational efficiency, ensure safe functioning, regulate discharge/pressure, recover energy, prevent cavitation damage, and provide protection against hydraulic transients (like water hammer).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context (PCIET302 – Fluid Mechanics and Hydraulic Machines, Module 4), the term "Accessories" specifically encompasses:

> [!NOTE]
> **Core KTU Syllabus Definition**
> Accessories refer to the supplementary devices used in conjunction with **turbines** (Draft Tube, Surge Tank) and **pumps** (Air Vessel, Foot Valve, Strainer, Delivery Valve), which collectively ensure efficient, safe, and continuous operation of the hydraulic system.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are drinking water through a long straw from a deep tank. The straw represents the **penstock** (pipe carrying water to a turbine) or **suction pipe** (pipe drawing water to a pump). 

- A **Surge Tank** is like a small overflow cup attached mid-way on the straw — when you suddenly stop drinking (or the pump/turbine suddenly stops), the water rushing up has somewhere to go instead of bursting the straw (water hammer effect).
- A **Draft Tube** is like the flexible nozzle at the bottom of the straw — it allows the straw to discharge water just above the water level in your mouth, recovering the remaining suction energy.
- An **Air Vessel** on a pump is like a pressure buffer tank (similar to an expansion tank on a geyser) — it smooths out the pulsating flow from a reciprocating pump into a smooth, near-continuous flow.

These analogies make the otherwise abstract hydraulic concepts immediately intuitive for first-time learners.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Accessories

The accessories discussed in KTU Module 4 are classified broadly as:

| **Category** | **Accessory** | **Associated Machine** |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Recovery | Draft Tube | Reaction Turbines (Francis, Kaplan) |
| Transient Protection | Surge Tank | Long Penstocks feeding Turbines |
| Flow Regulation | Air Vessel | Reciprocating Pumps |
| Suction System | Foot Valve, Strainer | Centrifugal & Reciprocating Pumps |
| Delivery Control | Delivery Valve, By-pass | All Pumps |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Focus Tip:** In Part B questions (14 marks), examiners frequently combine **Draft Tube theory + numerical efficiency calculation** as a single sub-part question. Air Vessels and Surge Tanks are commonly tested in Part A (3 marks) as conceptual short-answer questions.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard values are used throughout this topic:

- **Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$**
- **Density of water, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$**
- **Specific weight of water, $\gamma = \rho g = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$**
- **Atmospheric pressure head, $H_{atm} \approx 10.3 \text{ m of water}$**

---

## 1.5 Role of Accessories in Modern Engineering

In real engineering practice:
- **Hydroelectric Power Plants** (e.g., Idukki, Mullaperiyar in Kerala) use **draft tubes** to recover 15–25% additional energy.
- **Pumped storage projects** rely on **surge tanks** to prevent catastrophic penstock bursts.
- **Municipal water supply systems** use **air vessels** on reciprocating pumps to deliver steady flow without damaging the piping network.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 DRAFT TUBE — The Energy Recovery Device

### 2.1.1 Definition
A **draft tube** is a gradually expanding conduit (diffuser) fitted at the outlet of a **reaction turbine runner**. Its primary function is to:
1. Convert the high kinetic energy of water leaving the runner into pressure energy.
2. Allow the turbine to be set **above** the tailrace water level, while still utilizing the head between the runner outlet and tailrace.

### 2.1.2 Why is a Draft Tube Needed?

Without a draft tube:
- Water would discharge directly into the tailrace at the runner outlet, wasting all kinetic energy ($V^2/2g$).
- The runner would need to be submerged below tailrace level, complicating maintenance and risking cavitation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Principle**
> A draft tube operates on **Bernoulli's Equation**. It is a **diffuser** (expanding area), so velocity decreases and pressure increases — converting kinetic energy into useful pressure energy that can drive more flow through the runner.

### 2.1.3 Theoretical Analysis (Bernoulli's Equation Across Draft Tube)

Applying Bernoulli's equation between the **runner outlet (point 1)** and the **draft tube exit into tailrace (point 2)**:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

Where:
- $P_1$ = pressure at runner outlet
- $V_1$ = velocity of water leaving runner
- $P_2$ = pressure at draft tube exit (= atmospheric pressure, $P_{atm}$)
- $V_2$ = velocity at draft tube exit
- $z_1 - z_2$ = height of draft tube = $H_s$ (static suction head)
- $h_L$ = head loss in the draft tube

Rearranging to express the recovered head:

$$H_d = \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g} - h_L$$

Where **$H_d$ is the head recovered by the draft tube**.

### 2.1.4 Draft Tube Efficiency

$$\boxed{\eta_d = \frac{\text{Head actually recovered}}{\text{Head that would be recovered if no losses}}} = \frac{H_d}{\frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g}}}$$

For a well-designed draft tube, $\eta_d$ ranges from **0.7 to 0.9** (70%–90%).

### 2.1.5 Types of Draft Tubes

| **Type** | **Description** | **Typical Use** |
|---|---|---|
| **Conical Draft Tube** | Simple diverging cone (angle $\leq 7°$) | Francis turbines (medium head) |
| **Elbow (Moody) Type** | Right-angled bend for vertical-to-horizontal transition | Kaplan turbines (low head) |
| **Hydraucone Type** | Combination of cone + diffusion passage | High specific speed Francis |
| **Straight Tube (Cylindrical)** | Uniform cross-section (rarely used, low efficiency) | Special applications only |

---

## 2.2 SURGE TANK — Protection Against Water Hammer

### 2.2.1 Definition
A **surge tank** is an open-top storage reservoir (or pressurized tank) connected to the **penstock** of a hydroelectric plant, located close to the turbine inlet. It protects the long penstock from sudden pressure surges caused by rapid **load rejection** or **load acceptance** by the turbine.

### 2.2.2 Why is a Surge Tank Needed?

When the turbine is operating and the electrical load is suddenly disconnected:
- The wicket gates close rapidly.
- The water column in the penstock suddenly decelerates, generating a **high-pressure shock wave** (water hammer).
- Without a surge tank, this surge pressure can rupture the penstock.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Principle**
> A surge tank provides a **free water surface** (in simple surge tanks) that can rise or fall as the water column in the penstock accelerates or decelerates. This absorbs the kinetic energy of the moving water, preventing destructive pressure transients.

### 2.2.3 Types of Surge Tanks

| **Type** | **Feature** |
|---|---|
| **Simple Surge Tank** | Cylindrical tank with constant cross-section, open at top |
| **Restricted (Orifice) Surge Tank** | Has a restricted orifice at the inlet to dampen oscillations |
| **Differential Surge Tank** | Has an inner (riser) and outer tank; the outer dampens oscillations via throttling |
| **Closed (Pressurized) Surge Tank** | Sealed tank, contains air cushion; used when topography prevents tall open tanks |

### 2.2.4 Surge Tank Oscillation Equation (Mass Oscillation)

For a **simple surge tank**, the rate of change of water level in the tank:

$$\frac{dz}{dt} = \frac{A_p}{A_s}(V_p - V_t)$$

Where:
- $A_p$ = area of penstock
- $A_s$ = area of surge tank
- $V_p$ = velocity in penstock
- $V_t$ = velocity required to run the turbine at new steady state

The **time period of oscillation** (Thoma's formula) is:

$$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{L \cdot A_s}{g \cdot A_p}}$$

Where $L$ = length of penstock.

---

## 2.3 AIR VESSEL — The Reciprocating Pump's Buffer

### 2.3.1 Definition
An **air vessel** is a closed, pressurized container fitted on the **suction pipe** (suction air vessel) and/or the **delivery pipe** (delivery air vessel) of a **reciprocating pump**. It contains air at the top and water at the bottom, separated by a flexible interface.

### 2.3.2 Function of Air Vessels

1. **Smoothen the discharge** by ensuring continuous flow (despite the pulsating nature of reciprocating pump output).
2. **Reduce the work done against friction** in long delivery pipes.
3. **Prevent separation of water column** in the suction pipe (avoiding cavitation-like damage).
4. **Reduce acceleration head losses** during the suction and delivery strokes.

### 2.3.3 Air Vessel — Working Principle

> [!NOTE]
> When the pump pushes water during the delivery stroke, excess water enters the air vessel and compresses the air cushion. During the suction stroke, the compressed air pushes this water back into the delivery pipe, maintaining a near-uniform discharge.

### 2.3.4 Air Vessel Capacity Formula

For a single-acting reciprocating pump with an air vessel fitted on the delivery side:

$$\text{Volume of air vessel required} = V_{av} = A_p \cdot D \left[\frac{1}{K_a} - \left(1 - \frac{K_a}{2}\right)\right]$$

Where:
- $A_p$ = cross-sectional area of pipe
- $D$ = diameter of pump cylinder
- $K_a$ = coefficient of pulsation (ratio of actual head fluctuation to total head)

For **maximum reduction of work** against friction, the optimum coefficient of pulsation is:

$$K_a = \sqrt{2} - 1 \approx 0.414$$

---

## 2.4 OTHER IMPORTANT PUMP ACCESSORIES

| **Accessory** | **Function** |
|---|---|
| **Foot Valve** | One-way valve at pump suction; prevents back-flow when pump stops, keeping the suction pipe primed |
| **Strainer** | Perforated mesh at the foot valve; filters debris and prevents damage to the pump impeller |
| **Delivery Valve (Gate Valve)** | Controls discharge flow and isolates the pump from the system during maintenance |
| **Pressure Gauge** | Indicates pressure on the delivery side |
| **Vacuum Gauge** | Indicates negative pressure (suction) on the suction side |
| **By-pass Valve** | Allows recirculation of flow back to the suction tank when delivery valve is closed (to prevent pump overheating) |
| **Non-Return Valve (NRV)** | One-way valve on delivery pipe; prevents reverse flow when pump stops |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Draft Tube Head Recovered | $H_d = \dfrac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g} - h_L$ | m |
| 2 | Draft Tube Efficiency | $\eta_d = \dfrac{H_d}{V_1^2/(2g) - V_2^2/(2g)}$ | — |
| 3 | Pressure at Runner Exit | $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - H_s + H_d$ | m |
| 4 | Surge Tank Time Period | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{L A_s}{g A_p}}$ | s |
| 5 | Thoma's Stability Limit | $A_s \geq \dfrac{L A_p}{C^2 \cdot h_f}$ | m² |
| 6 | Air Vessel Capacity | $V_{av} = A_p D\left[\dfrac{1}{K_a} - \left(1 - \dfrac{K_a}{2}\right)\right]$ | m³ |
| 7 | Optimum Pulsation Coefficient | $K_a = \sqrt{2} - 1$ | — |
| 8 | Foot Valve Function | Prevents back-flow (NRV action) | — |
| 9 | Pressure to avoid Cavitation | $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} \geq \dfrac{P_{vapor}}{\rho g} + NPSH$ | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Units:** All heads in this topic are in **meters of water (m)**. Pressures may be in Pa, kPa, or bar. Conversions: $1 \text{ bar} = 10.2 \text{ m of water} \approx 100 \text{ kPa}$.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Head Recovered by a Draft Tube

### Step 1 — Define the Control Volume

Consider the section between:
- **Section (1):** Runner outlet, where velocity = $V_1$, pressure = $P_1$, elevation = $z_1$
- **Section (2):** Draft tube exit into tailrace, where velocity = $V_2$, pressure = $P_2 = P_{atm}$, elevation = $z_2$

The vertical distance between (1) and (2) is the **static suction head, $H_s$**:

$$H_s = z_1 - z_2$$

### Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation (with losses)

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Boundary Conditions

Since the draft tube discharges into the tailrace (open atmosphere), the exit pressure $P_2 = P_{atm}$, and the exit velocity $V_2$ is small (due to large exit area). Also, the head loss $h_L$ is known.

Rearranging for the pressure at the runner outlet:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 - \frac{V_1^2}{2g} - z_1 + h_L$$

### Step 4 — Express in Terms of Static Head $H_s$

Substituting $z_1 - z_2 = H_s$:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g} - H_s + h_L$$

### Step 5 — Identify the Recovered Head

If the draft tube were ideal (no losses, $h_L = 0$), and exit velocity $V_2 \approx 0$:

$$\left(\frac{P_1}{\rho g}\right)_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \frac{V_1^2}{2g} - H_s$$

The **head actually recovered** by the draft tube (compared to discharging directly at the runner level) is:

$$H_d = \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g} - h_L$$

### Step 6 — Final Form for Practical Use

$$\boxed{H_d = \frac{V_1^2}{2g}\left[1 - \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^2\right] - h_L}$$

Or equivalently, in terms of the velocity head coefficient:

$$H_d = \frac{V_1^2}{2g} - \frac{V_2^2}{2g} - h_L$$

This derivation is the foundation for the numerical problems in KTU exams.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Minimum Submergence Head (Anti-Cavitation)

### Step 1 — Identify Cavitation Condition

Cavitation occurs when the pressure at the runner outlet falls **below the vapor pressure** of water. To avoid cavitation:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} \geq \frac{P_{vapor}}{\rho g}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute from Bernoulli Equation

Using the expression for $P_1/\rho g$ derived earlier:

$$\frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \frac{V_1^2}{2g} - H_s + H_d \geq \frac{P_{vapor}}{\rho g}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Maximum Allowable $H_s$

Rearranging:

$$H_s \leq \left(\frac{P_{atm} - P_{vapor}}{\rho g}\right) - \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + H_d$$

### Step 4 — Recognize NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head)

The term $\dfrac{P_{atm} - P_{vapor}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g}$ is exactly the **NPSH available** at the runner:

$$\boxed{H_s \leq NPSH_{available} + H_d}$$

This sets the **maximum allowable height** of the runner above tailrace level, ensuring cavitation-free operation.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Draft Tube Efficiency

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
> 
> A Francis turbine has a runner outlet diameter of 1.2 m and operates at 300 rpm. The velocity of water at the runner outlet (whirl velocity) is 12 m/s, and the draft tube exit diameter is 1.8 m. The head loss in the draft tube is 0.6 m. Calculate:
> (a) The head recovered by the draft tube
> (b) The efficiency of the draft tube

### Given Data
- $V_1 = 12 \text{ m/s}$ (velocity at runner outlet)
- $D_1 = 1.2 \text{ m}$
- $D_2 = 1.8 \text{ m}$ (draft tube exit)
- $N = 300 \text{ rpm}$ (not directly needed for $V_1$ if given)
- $h_L = 0.6 \text{ m}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

### Step 1 — Calculate Cross-Sectional Areas

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(1.2)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(1.44) = 1.131 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(1.8)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(3.24) = 2.545 \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Apply Continuity Equation

By continuity ($A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$):

$$V_2 = V_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2} = 12 \cdot \frac{1.131}{2.545} = 12 \cdot 0.4444 = 5.333 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Velocity Heads

$$\frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{(12)^2}{2(9.81)} = \frac{144}{19.62} = 7.339 \text{ m}$$

$$\frac{V_2^2}{2g} = \frac{(5.333)^2}{2(9.81)} = \frac{28.44}{19.62} = 1.450 \text{ m}$$

### Step 4 — Calculate Head Recovered by Draft Tube

$$H_d = \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g} - h_L$$

$$H_d = 7.339 - 1.450 - 0.6$$

$$\boxed{H_d = 5.289 \text{ m} \approx 5.29 \text{ m}}$$

### Step 5 — Calculate Draft Tube Efficiency

The ideal recovered head (no losses):

$$H_{d,\text{ideal}} = \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g} = 7.339 - 1.450 = 5.889 \text{ m}$$

$$\eta_d = \frac{H_d}{H_{d,\text{ideal}}} = \frac{5.289}{5.889}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_d = 0.898 \approx 89.8\%}$$

### Interpretation
A draft tube efficiency of ~90% is excellent and indicates a well-designed diverging passage. This recovered head (5.29 m) is the additional energy that the turbine can now convert into useful power — energy that would have been wasted without the draft tube.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Air Vessel Capacity

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
> 
> A single-acting reciprocating pump has a cylinder diameter of 200 mm and stroke length of 300 mm. It delivers water through a delivery pipe of 100 mm diameter at a mean head of 25 m. The pump runs at 60 rpm. Calculate the volume of air vessel required on the delivery side to limit the head fluctuation to 15% of the mean head.

### Given Data
- $D_{cyl} = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$
- $L_{stroke} = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.3 \text{ m}$
- $D_{pipe} = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- $H_{mean} = 25 \text{ m}$
- $N = 60 \text{ rpm}$
- Fluctuation: $K_a \cdot H_{mean} = 0.15 \cdot 25 = 3.75 \text{ m}$
- Therefore $K_a = 0.15$

### Step 1 — Calculate Cross-Sectional Areas

$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.01) = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Identify $D$ in Formula

In the standard air vessel formula $V_{av} = A_p \cdot D \left[\dfrac{1}{K_a} - \left(1 - \dfrac{K_a}{2}\right)\right]$, the symbol $D$ refers to the **cylinder diameter** (NOT pipe diameter).

$$D = 0.2 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into Air Vessel Formula

$$V_{av} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.2 \times \left[\frac{1}{0.15} - \left(1 - \frac{0.15}{2}\right)\right]$$

$$V_{av} = 1.5708 \times 10^{-3} \times \left[6.667 - 0.925\right]$$

$$V_{av} = 1.5708 \times 10^{-3} \times 5.742$$

$$\boxed{V_{av} = 9.019 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 \approx 9.02 \text{ litres}}$$

### Interpretation
A small air vessel of about 9 litres is sufficient to limit the head fluctuation to ±15% of the mean head. Without this air vessel, the head would fluctuate between 0 and 25 m (100% fluctuation) during each stroke.

---

## 3.5 Summary of Numerical Patterns in KTU Exams

| **Numerical Type** | **What is Asked** | **Key Formula** |
|---|---|---|
| Draft Tube Head Recovery | Find $H_d$ | $H_d = V_1^2/(2g) - V_2^2/(2g) - h_L$ |
| Draft Tube Efficiency | Find $\eta_d$ | $\eta_d = H_d / H_{d,\text{ideal}}$ |
| Static Suction Head | Find $H_s$ | $H_s \leq NPSH + H_d$ |
| Air Vessel Capacity | Find $V_{av}$ | $V_{av} = A_p D [1/K_a - (1 - K_a/2)]$ |
| Surge Tank Area | Find $A_s$ for stability | Thoma's Criterion |
