# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define a draft tube. Why is it used in a reaction turbine?**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

A draft tube is a **gradually expanding passage (diffuser)** fitted at the outlet of a reaction turbine runner.

It is used to:
1. Convert the kinetic energy of water leaving the runner into pressure energy, thereby **increasing the effective head** on the turbine (1.5 marks).
2. Allow the turbine to be set **above the tailrace level**, making maintenance easier and reducing cavitation risk (1 mark).
3. Reduce the velocity of water discharging into the tailrace, minimizing kinetic energy loss (0.5 marks).

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**List any three accessories of a reciprocating pump and state their function.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

1. **Foot Valve** (1 mark): One-way valve at the pump suction that prevents back-flow of water when the pump stops, keeping the suction pipe always primed.
2. **Strainer** (1 mark): Perforated mesh fitted before the foot valve that filters debris and prevents solid particles from entering the pump.
3. **Air Vessel** (1 mark): Closed vessel containing air and water that smooths the pulsating discharge of the reciprocating pump into a near-uniform flow.

*(Alternative valid accessories: Delivery Valve, Pressure Gauge, Vacuum Gauge, By-pass Valve — 1 mark each)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Unit mismatch:** Forgetting to convert diameters from mm to m. Many students lose 2–3 marks on this single error.
> - **Wrong $D$ in air vessel formula:** Students often use the pipe diameter instead of the **cylinder diameter** in $V_{av} = A_p \cdot D \cdot [...]$, losing full marks.
> - **Atmospheric pressure omission:** In cavitation-related questions, students must explicitly use $H_{atm} = 10.3$ m of water as a datum.
> - **No continuity equation application:** Skipping the calculation of $V_2$ from $V_1$ via area ratio is a frequent error.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Derive the expression for the **head recovered by a draft tube** fitted to a reaction turbine, clearly stating the underlying assumptions.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
A Francis turbine develops 900 kW under a net head of 30 m. The velocity of water at the runner outlet is 9 m/s. The draft tube exit diameter is 1.5 times the runner outlet diameter. The head loss in the draft tube is 0.4 m. Calculate:
1. The head recovered by the draft tube
2. The efficiency of the draft tube
3. The pressure head at the runner outlet if the draft tube is set 2.5 m above the tailrace

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Explain with a neat sketch the **construction and working of an air vessel** on the delivery side of a single-acting reciprocating pump. State its advantages.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
A single-acting reciprocating pump has a cylinder diameter of 150 mm and a stroke length of 250 mm. The delivery pipe diameter is 75 mm. The mean velocity in the delivery pipe is 1.2 m/s. If an air vessel is fitted on the delivery side such that the head fluctuation is limited to 10% of the mean head, calculate:
1. The mean discharge through the pump (without air vessel)
2. The volume of air vessel required
3. The percentage saving in work done against friction due to the air vessel

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Solution: Derivation of Head Recovered by Draft Tube

**Given:** Reaction turbine with draft tube fitted at the runner outlet.

**Step 1 — Define sections:**
- Section 1: Runner outlet (velocity $V_1$, pressure $P_1$, elevation $z_1$)
- Section 2: Draft tube exit into tailrace (velocity $V_2$, pressure $P_2 = P_{atm}$, elevation $z_2$)

**Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation between sections 1 and 2:**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

**Step 3 — Substitute known boundary conditions:**
- $P_2 = P_{atm}$ (atmospheric, since discharging into tailrace)
- $z_1 - z_2 = H_s$ (static suction head)

**Step 4 — Rearrange to express pressure head at runner outlet:**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 - \frac{V_1^2}{2g} - z_1 + h_L$$

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g} - H_s + h_L$$

**Step 5 — Identify the recovered head:**

$$\boxed{H_d = \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g} - h_L}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, incompressible flow
2. Friction losses in the draft tube are lumped into $h_L$
3. Atmospheric pressure acts at the tailrace

**Marks Distribution:** (1 mark diagram/setup + 2 marks Bernoulli + 2 marks substitution + 1 mark final formula + 1 mark assumptions = 7 marks)

---

#### Part (b) — Solution: Numerical Problem

**Given Data:**
- $V_1 = 9 \text{ m/s}$
- $D_2 = 1.5 \cdot D_1$
- $h_L = 0.4 \text{ m}$
- $H_s = 2.5 \text{ m}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Power and net head are extra information (not needed for draft tube calculation)

**Step 1 — Calculate area ratio:**

$$\frac{A_1}{A_2} = \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{1}{1.5}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{2.25} = 0.4444$$

**Step 2 — Apply continuity to find $V_2$:**

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \implies V_2 = V_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2} = 9 \cdot 0.4444 = 4.0 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate velocity heads:**

$$\frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{(9)^2}{2(9.81)} = \frac{81}{19.62} = 4.128 \text{ m}$$

$$\frac{V_2^2}{2g} = \frac{(4)^2}{2(9.81)} = \frac{16}{19.62} = 0.8155 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Head Recovered (Part 1):**

$$H_d = \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g} - h_L = 4.128 - 0.8155 - 0.4$$

$$\boxed{H_d = 2.912 \text{ m} \approx 2.91 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Draft Tube Efficiency (Part 2):**

$$H_{d,\text{ideal}} = \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g} = 4.128 - 0.8155 = 3.313 \text{ m}$$

$$\eta_d = \frac{H_d}{H_{d,\text{ideal}}} = \frac{2.912}{3.313}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_d = 0.879 \approx 87.9\%}$$

**Step 6 — Pressure Head at Runner Outlet (Part 3):**

Using Bernoulli with $P_{atm}/\rho g = 10.3$ m of water:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g} - H_s + h_L$$

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} = 10.3 - 3.313 - 2.5 + 0.4$$

$$\boxed{\frac{P_1}{\rho g} = 4.887 \text{ m of water (absolute)}}$$

**Marks Distribution:** (1 mark area ratio + 1 mark continuity + 1 mark velocity heads + 1 mark $H_d$ + 1 mark efficiency + 1 mark pressure + 1 mark unit consistency = 7 marks)

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Solution: Air Vessel Construction and Working

**Construction:** (3.5 marks)

An air vessel is a **closed, cylindrical/ellipsoidal vessel** made of cast iron or fabricated steel. It is fitted on the delivery pipe of a single-acting reciprocating pump, very close to the pump cylinder. It contains:
- **Compressed air at the top** (at a pressure approximately equal to the delivery pressure)
- **Water at the bottom**, in contact with the air through a free surface (or via a flexible diaphragm in modern designs)
- A **flanged connection** to the delivery pipe
- An **air charging valve** at the top (for periodic air replenishment, since air gradually dissolves into water)

**Working:** (2.5 marks)

During the **delivery stroke**, the pump pushes water into the delivery pipe. Excess water enters the air vessel and compresses the trapped air cushion. During the **suction stroke**, the pump sucks water from the sump, but the compressed air pushes the stored water back into the delivery pipe, maintaining flow.

This results in:
- Continuous flow in the delivery pipe (instead of zero flow during suction stroke)
- Reduced acceleration head losses
- Lower friction losses (because velocity is more uniform)
- Prevention of water column separation in long pipes

**Advantages:**
1. Flow becomes continuous → smoother delivery
2. Saves up to 80–85% of work done against friction
3. Prevents water hammer in long delivery pipes
4. Allows the pump to operate efficiently even with very long delivery mains

---

#### Part (b) — Solution: Numerical Problem

**Given Data:**
- $D_{cyl} = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- $L_{stroke} = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$
- $D_{pipe} = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$
- $V_{pipe} = 1.2 \text{ m/s}$ (mean velocity in delivery pipe)
- $K_a = 0.10$ (head fluctuation = 10% of mean head)

**Step 1 — Mean Discharge (Part 1):**

$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.005625) = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q = A_p \cdot V_{pipe} = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.2$$

$$\boxed{Q = 5.301 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 5.301 \text{ L/s}}$$

**Step 2 — Volume of Air Vessel Required (Part 2):**

Using the formula (with $D$ = cylinder diameter = 0.15 m):

$$V_{av} = A_p \cdot D_{cyl} \left[\frac{1}{K_a} - \left(1 - \frac{K_a}{2}\right)\right]$$

$$V_{av} = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.15 \times \left[\frac{1}{0.10} - \left(1 - \frac{0.05}{1}\right)\right]$$

$$V_{av} = 6.627 \times 10^{-4} \times [10 - 0.95]$$

$$V_{av} = 6.627 \times 10^{-4} \times 9.05$$

$$\boxed{V_{av} = 5.998 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 \approx 6.0 \text{ litres}}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Saving in Work (Part 3):**

For a single-acting reciprocating pump without an air vessel, the work done against friction is proportional to:

$$W_{no} \propto 1$$

With an air vessel, the work is proportional to:

$$W_{av} \propto K_a^2$$

**Percentage saving:**

$$\% \text{ Saving} = \frac{W_{no} - W_{av}}{W_{no}} \times 100 = (1 - K_a^2) \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Saving} = (1 - 0.01) \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\% \text{ Saving} = 99\%}$$

**Interpretation:** This extraordinarily high saving demonstrates why air vessels are mandatory in industrial reciprocating pump installations with long delivery lines. The 99% reduction occurs because most of the work done against friction in a pulsating flow is dissipated in the acceleration/deceleration cycle, which the air vessel eliminates.

**Marks Distribution:** (1 mark discharge + 1.5 marks air vessel capacity + 1.5 marks saving formula + 1 mark substitution + 1 mark interpretation + 1.5 marks setup/diagram = 7 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 Accessories**

### 📌 DRAFT TUBE
- ✔️ A diffuser fitted at the outlet of a reaction turbine to **recover kinetic energy**.
- ✔️ Operates on **Bernoulli's principle**: expanding area → velocity decreases → pressure increases.
- ✔️ Formula: $H_d = \dfrac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g} - h_L$
- ✔️ Efficiency: $\eta_d = \dfrac{H_d}{H_{d,\text{ideal}}}$ — typically **70% to 90%**.
- ✔️ Common types: **Conical, Elbow (Moody), Hydraucone**.
- ✔️ Static suction head must satisfy: $H_s \leq NPSH_{available} + H_d$ (anti-cavitation check).
- ✔️ Conical draft tube diverging angle should be **≤ 7°** for efficient flow separation prevention.

### 📌 SURGE TANK
- ✔️ Open/pressurized tank on the penstock, close to the turbine inlet.
- ✔️ Protects the long penstock from **water hammer** during sudden load changes.
- ✔️ Provides a **free water surface** that rises/falls to absorb kinetic energy of water column.
- ✔️ Time period of oscillation: $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{L A_s}{g A_p}}$.
- ✔️ **Thoma's stability criterion** sets minimum surge tank area.
- ✔️ Types: **Simple, Restricted, Differential, Closed**.

### 📌 AIR VESSEL
- ✔️ Closed vessel on suction/delivery pipe of a **reciprocating pump**.
- ✔️ Contains air cushion above water — provides **flow smoothing** and reduces friction losses.
- ✔️ Capacity formula: $V_{av} = A_p \cdot D \left[\dfrac{1}{K_a} - \left(1 - \dfrac{K_a}{2}\right)\right]$.
- ✔️ Note: **$D$ = cylinder diameter**, NOT pipe diameter.
- ✔️ Optimum $K_a$ for maximum work saving: $\sqrt{2} - 1 \approx 0.414$.
- ✔️ Percentage saving in friction work: $(1 - K_a^2) \times 100\%$.

### 📌 OTHER PUMP ACCESSORIES
- ✔️ **Foot Valve**: One-way valve at suction; prevents back-flow, keeps pipe primed.
- ✔️ **Strainer**: Filters debris before the foot valve.
- ✔️ **Delivery Valve (Gate Valve)**: Controls discharge, isolates pump for maintenance.
- ✔️ **By-pass Valve**: Recirculates flow back to sump when delivery is shut (prevents overheating).
- ✔️ **NRV (Non-Return Valve)**: Prevents reverse flow when pump stops.
- ✔️ **Pressure Gauge / Vacuum Gauge**: Indicate pressures on delivery and suction sides.

### 📌 EXAM STRATEGY QUICK TIPS
- ✔️ Always **convert mm → m** in numerical problems (most common error).
- ✔️ For draft tube: use $V_2 = V_1 (A_1/A_2) = V_1 (D_1/D_2)^2$ (continuity).
- ✔️ For air vessel formula, **clearly label which $D$** you are using.
- ✔️ Atmospheric pressure head = **10.3 m of water** (use when $P_{atm}$ is required).
- ✔️ Always state **assumptions** in derivations — examiners allocate 1–2 marks for this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Performance Characteristics and Practical Problems on Centrifugal Pumps and Reciprocating Pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Performance Characteristics and Practical Problems on Centrifugal Pumps and Reciprocating Pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Centrifugal Pumps — Performance Characteristics

### Formal Definition
**Centrifugal Pump Performance Characteristics** are the graphical and analytical relationships between the principal operating parameters — *head (H)*, *discharge (Q)*, *power (P)*, and *efficiency ($\eta$)* — under steady-state operating conditions. These characteristics describe how the pump behaves when operating at a *constant speed* and *constant impeller diameter*, and how it responds to changes in system resistance (static head, friction losses).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** The "constant-speed characteristic curves" of a centrifugal pump form a **high-weightage (14-mark)** question in Module 4. Mastery of the three standard curves (H–Q, $\eta$–Q, P–Q) is non-negotiable.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine riding a bicycle. When you pedal at a **constant pedaling speed** (constant rotational speed *N*), but ride **uphill**, the effort (power) required increases while your speed (discharge *Q*) decreases. Conversely, riding **downhill** makes pedaling easier and you go faster. The bicycle's *gear size* (impeller diameter *D*) and *pedal gear ratio* (blade angle *$\beta_2$*) are fixed — only the *road slope* (system head) changes. This is exactly how a centrifugal pump behaves at constant *N* and constant *D*: the operating point shifts along the H–Q curve depending on the resistance the downstream system offers.

## 1.2 Reciprocating Pumps — Performance Characteristics

### Formal Definition
The **performance of a reciprocating pump** is quantified by the *coefficient of discharge (Cd)*, *slip*, *separation head*, and *theoretical vs. actual indicator diagrams*. The **indicator diagram** is the pressure–volume (p–V) loop traced by an *indicator* instrument fitted to the pump cylinder, which reveals the *actual work done per stroke* (area of the diagram).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Centrifugal pumps deliver *continuous flow*; reciprocating pumps deliver *pulsating, intermittent* flow. Hence the indicator diagram (a *closed loop*) is meaningful for reciprocating pumps only.

### Conceptual Analogy
A reciprocating pump behaves like a *medical syringe* dipped in water. As the plunger is pulled back, water rushes in; as it is pushed, water is expelled. However, real water has *inertia* (acceleration head) and the *non-return valves* are not perfect (slip). A *cardiogram* (which records pressure vs. time) is analogous to the indicator diagram (which records pressure vs. volume). The "missing area" between ideal and actual diagrams is exactly the *energy lost* — the same way a noisy, irregular heartbeat indicates a stressed heart.

## 1.3 Factors Affecting Performance — Quick Overview

| Pump Type | Primary Factors Affecting Performance |
|---|---|
| Centrifugal | Speed (N), Impeller Diameter (D), Blade angle ($\beta_2$), Manometric efficiency |
| Reciprocating | Slip, Friction in suction/delivery pipes, Acceleration of fluid, Head, Speed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Density of water: **$\rho$ = 1000 kg/m³**
> - Specific weight of water: **w = $\rho g$ = 9810 N/m³**
> - Standard KTU notations: *N* in rpm, *D* in m, *Q* in m³/s or L/s, *H* in m, *P* in W or kW, *$\eta$* as a decimal or percentage.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Centrifugal Pump Performance — Detailed Theory

### 2.1.1 The Three Standard Constant-Speed Characteristic Curves

When a centrifugal pump is tested at **constant speed N** and **constant impeller diameter D**, the following three curves are plotted from experimental data:

1. **Head vs. Discharge (H–Q curve):** Monotonically *decreasing*. As Q rises, the head H falls due to increased internal circulation and hydraulic losses.
2. **Efficiency vs. Discharge ($\eta$–Q curve):** *Bell-shaped (parabolic)*. Efficiency is zero at zero discharge and zero at maximum discharge, reaching a **maximum** at the *Best Efficiency Point (BEP)*.
3. **Power vs. Discharge (P–Q curve):** *Slightly increasing and nearly linear* (for backward-curved blades typical in centrifugal pumps). Power at *Q = 0* is *not* zero because some power is consumed to overcome disk friction and pre-rotation.

### 2.1.2 Factors Affecting Centrifugal Pump Performance

**(A) Effect of Change of Speed (N) at Constant D:**

Using the *homologous (or affinity) laws*, when the speed is changed from N₁ to N₂ at constant impeller diameter:

$$
Q \propto N, \quad H \propto N^2, \quad P \propto N^3
$$

The shape of the H–Q curve remains the *same*, but the *ordinates scale up* according to the laws above. The *efficiency* remains approximately the *same* (small change).

**(B) Effect of Change of Impeller Diameter (D) at Constant N:**

Trimming the impeller (reducing D) reduces the manometric head according to:

$$
\frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2, \quad \frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{D_1}{D_2}
$$

This is the basis of *impeller trimming* in industry to meet a new duty point without changing the motor.

**(C) Effect of Blade Angle ($\beta_2$):**
Larger outlet blade angle increases the *manometric head* and *discharge* but may reduce *manometric efficiency* due to increased eddying and shock losses.

**(D) Effect of Manometric Efficiency ($\eta_{man}$):**
The **manometric head** is given by the Euler equation applied between inlet and outlet of the impeller:

$$
H_{man} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}
$$

If inlet has no pre-whirl (*Vw1 = 0*), the head is $\frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$. The manometric efficiency is:

$$
\eta_{man} = \frac{H_{man}}{H_{euler}} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{u_2^2 - u_1^2}
$$

**Real-world utility:** These relationships are used in *pump selection*, *pump affinity calculations during VFD speed control*, and *series/parallel pump operations* in water supply schemes and HVAC systems.

## 2.2 Reciprocating Pump Performance — Detailed Theory

### 2.2.1 Indicator Diagram

The *ideal indicator diagram* is a **rectangle** of height $(H_s + H_d)$ (suction head + delivery head) and length corresponding to the swept volume. The *actual diagram* differs because of:

- **Slip:** Leakage past the valves, reducing the actual discharge.
- **Friction in suction and delivery pipes:** Pressure drops cause the actual curve to dip/rise from the ideal.
- **Acceleration head:** During stroke reversal, the fluid column must be accelerated/decelerated, causing a pressure variation.

### 2.2.2 Slip, Coefficient of Discharge, and Separation

**Slip (in volume per stroke):**

$$
\text{Slip} = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = A \cdot L \cdot N - Q_{act}
$$

Where *A* = piston area, *L* = stroke length, *N* = crank rpm (in rps), $Q_{act}$ = actual discharge.

**Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$):**

$$
C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{Q_{act}}{A \cdot L \cdot N}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The value of $C_d$ in KTU problems is typically given as **0.90 to 0.97**. Always read whether slip is to be neglected ($C_d = 1$).

### 2.2.3 Work Done and Power for Reciprocating Pump

**Work done per second (Power) by the pump against the total head H:**

$$
P = \rho g A L N \left( H_s + H_d + \frac{h_f}{2} + \frac{h_a}{2} \right)
$$

Where:
- $H_s$ = static suction head
- $H_d$ = static delivery head
- $h_f$ = friction head loss in pipes
- $h_a$ = acceleration head

**Acceleration head** (for a single-acting pump with long suction/delivery pipes):

$$
h_a = \frac{L_p \cdot A \cdot N^2 \cdot r}{g \cdot A_p}
$$

Where *Lp* = pipe length, *A* = piston area, *r* = crank radius, *Ap* = pipe area, *N* in rps.

> **Note:** In KTU problems, acceleration head is often *neglected* unless explicitly stated. Always check the problem statement.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$ | Manometric head (no pre-whirl) |
| 2 | $\eta_{man} = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{u_2^2 - u_1^2}$ | Manometric efficiency |
| 3 | $\eta_{o} = \eta_{man} \times \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{vol}$ | Overall efficiency |
| 4 | $P_{water} = \rho g Q H$ | Water power (W) |
| 5 | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{o}}$ | Shaft power (W) |
| 6 | $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | Affinity law — discharge |
| 7 | $\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$ | Affinity law — head |
| 8 | $\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$ | Affinity law — power |
| 9 | $\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2$ | Affinity law — head vs diameter |
| 10 | $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{D_1}{D_2}$ | Affinity law — discharge vs diameter |
| 11 | $Q_{th} = A L N$ | Theoretical discharge of reciprocating pump (per second) |
| 12 | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | Coefficient of discharge |
| 13 | $\% \text{Slip} = \dfrac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100$ | Percentage slip |
| 14 | $h_a = \dfrac{L_p A N^2 r}{g A_p}$ | Acceleration head (long pipes) |
| 15 | $P = \rho g A L N H$ | Power for reciprocating pump (no losses) |
| 16 | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = C_d$ | Volumetric efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units in KTU exam:** Always express *N* in **rps** in acceleration head and power formulas, even if the problem states *rpm*. Convert *rpm → rps* by dividing by 60. Marks are lost if units are inconsistent.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Affinity Laws for Centrifugal Pump (From First Principles)

### Setup
Consider a geometrically similar pump running at two different speeds $N_1$ and $N_2$. Let the corresponding discharge be $Q_1$ and $Q_2$, head be $H_1$ and $H_2$, power be $P_1$ and $P_2$.

**Step 1: Discharge scales with peripheral velocity and flow area.**

The discharge through the impeller is $Q = A \cdot V_f$, where $V_f$ is the *flow velocity* and *A* is the impeller outlet area. Both *A* and *V_f* scale with impeller diameter *D* and impeller tip speed *u*. Since $u \propto N D$:

$$
Q \propto A \cdot V_f \propto D^2 \cdot (N D) = N D^3
$$

**Step 2: Head scales with the square of velocity (Bernoulli principle).**

The manometric head is $H \propto V^2$ (from $\dfrac{V^2}{2g}$). Since velocity $V \propto N D$:

$$
H \propto (N D)^2 = N^2 D^2
$$

**Step 3: Power = $\rho g Q H$, so it scales as the product of Q and H.**

$$
P \propto Q \cdot H \propto (N D^3) \cdot (N^2 D^2) = N^3 D^5
$$

**Step 4: Eliminate D (constant D, varying N case).**

With *D* constant, dividing the two states:

$$
\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}, \quad \frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2, \quad \frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3
$$

**Step 5: Eliminate N (constant N, varying D case).**

With *N* constant:

$$
\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{D_1}{D_2}, \quad \frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2, \quad \frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^5
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The *P ∝ N³* law is why *VFD-controlled* (Variable Frequency Drive) pumps are **highly energy-efficient**. A 20% speed reduction saves nearly *50% power*. This is the foundation of modern *HVAC energy conservation* in green buildings.

## 3.2 Derivation: Work Done per Stroke by a Reciprocating Pump (No Losses)

### Setup
For a single-acting reciprocating pump, the water column is lifted by a height $H = H_s + H_d$ during the *delivery stroke* (suction stroke does zero useful work).

**Step 1:** Volume of water pumped per stroke = $A \cdot L$.

**Step 2:** Work done against gravity per stroke = weight × height.

$$
W_{stroke} = (\rho g \cdot A \cdot L) \cdot H
$$

**Step 3:** Number of strokes per second = *N* (rps).

**Step 4:** Work done per second (power) = Work per stroke × strokes per second.

$$
P = W_{stroke} \times N = \rho g A L N H
$$

This is the **ideal water power**. For actual pumps, the power input is higher and we use the overall efficiency:

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{\rho g A L N H}{\eta_o}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Practical Problem-Solving Workflow

| Step | Action | Formula / Reference |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify pump type from problem statement | Centrifugal or Reciprocating |
| 2 | For centrifugal: identify what is varied — N, D, or both? | Apply Affinity Laws (rows 6–10) |
| 3 | For reciprocating: identify whether slip is to be considered | $C_d$ given or $C_d = 1$ (no slip) |
| 4 | Compute theoretical discharge first | $Q_{th} = A L N$ |
| 5 | Apply $C_d$ to get actual discharge | $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot Q_{th}$ |
| 6 | Compute water power | $P_{water} = \rho g Q_{act} H$ |
| 7 | Compute shaft power (or input power) | $P_{shaft} = P_{water} / \eta_o$ |
| 8 | Cross-check units — convert N from rpm → rps, Q from L/s → m³/s | Unit consistency |
| 9 | Express final answer with proper unit (W or kW) | KTU convention |
| 10 | For multi-part question: present all intermediate steps | KTU valuation scheme awards step marks |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Centrifugal Pump (Affinity Laws)

**Problem:** A centrifugal pump delivers 0.05 m³/s against a head of 20 m at 1450 rpm. The pump is now run at 1750 rpm. Calculate the new discharge, head, and power. Assume overall efficiency is 70% in both cases. (Density of water = 1000 kg/m³.)

**Given:** $Q_1 = 0.05$ m³/s, $H_1 = 20$ m, $N_1 = 1450$ rpm, $N_2 = 1750$ rpm, $\eta_o = 0.70$

**Step 1: New Discharge (Q₂) using Affinity Law.**

$$
\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}
$$

$$
Q_2 = Q_1 \times \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 0.05 \times \frac{1750}{1450}
$$

$$
Q_2 = 0.05 \times 1.2069 = 0.0603 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2: New Head (H₂) using Affinity Law.**

$$
H_2 = H_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = 20 \times (1.2069)^2
$$

$$
H_2 = 20 \times 1.4566 = 29.13 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Original Power (for reference):**

$$
P_1 = \frac{\rho g Q_1 H_1}{\eta_o} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 20}{0.70}
$$

$$
P_1 = \frac{9810}{0.70} = 14014.3 \text{ W} = 14.01 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4: New Power (P₂) using Affinity Law (cube law):**

$$
\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_2 = P_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3
$$

$$
P_2 = 14014.3 \times (1.2069)^3 = 14014.3 \times 1.7577
$$

$$
P_2 = 24634.0 \text{ W} \approx 24.63 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** A 20.7% increase in speed produced a *76% increase in power*. This vividly demonstrates the *P ∝ N³* relationship and explains why speed control is the most energy-efficient way to regulate pump output.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Reciprocating Pump (with Slip)

**Problem:** A single-acting reciprocating pump has a bore of 150 mm and a stroke of 300 mm. It runs at 60 rpm and delivers water against a total head of 20 m. If the coefficient of discharge is 0.92, calculate: (a) the theoretical discharge, (b) the actual discharge, (c) the percentage slip, and (d) the power required at 75% overall efficiency.

**Given:** Bore = 0.150 m, Stroke L = 0.300 m, N = 60 rpm = 1 rps, H = 20 m, $C_d$ = 0.92, $\eta_o$ = 0.75

**Step 1: Piston Area.**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.150)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0225 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Theoretical Discharge.**

$$
Q_{th} = A \cdot L \cdot N = 0.01767 \times 0.300 \times 1
$$

$$
Q_{th} = 0.005301 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 5.301 \text{ L/s}
$$

**Step 3: Actual Discharge.**

$$
Q_{act} = C_d \times Q_{th} = 0.92 \times 0.005301
$$

$$
Q_{act} = 0.004877 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 4.877 \text{ L/s}
$$

**Step 4: Percentage Slip.**

$$
\% \text{Slip} = \frac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100 = \frac{0.005301 - 0.004877}{0.005301} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{Slip} = \frac{0.000424}{0.005301} \times 100 = 8.0\%
$$

**Step 5: Shaft Power Required.**

$$
P = \frac{\rho g Q_{act} H}{\eta_o} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.004877 \times 20}{0.75}
$$

$$
P = \frac{956.85}{0.75} = 1275.8 \text{ W} \approx 1.276 \text{ kW}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Centrifugal Pump Performance Mapping

<!-- Mermaid Block 1: Centrifugal Pump Performance Flow -->
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Centrifugal Pump Operating Point] --> B{System Resistance Curve}
    A --> C{Pump Characteristic Curve}
    B --> D[Duty Point Intersection]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Operating Discharge Q]
    D --> F[Operating Head H]
    D --> G[Operating Efficiency eta]
    D --> H[Required Shaft Power P]
    
    I[Factor 1: Speed N] --> J[Affinity Laws Applied]
    K[Factor 2: Impeller Dia D] --> J
    L[Factor 3: Blade Angle beta2] --> J
    M[Factor 4: Manometric Efficiency] --> J
    
    J --> C
    
    N[VFD Control] --> I
    O[Impeller Trimming] --> K
    P[Blade Redesign] --> L
    Q[Surface Finish] --> M
    
    style A fill:#1e40af,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#16a34a,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#ea580c,stroke:#9a3412,color:#ffffff
    style N fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#4c1d95,color:#ffffff
    style O fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#4c1d95,color:#ffffff
    style P fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#4c1d95,color:#ffffff
    style Q fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#4c1d95,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The pump operating point is the *intersection* of the *system resistance curve* (rising) and the *pump characteristic curve* (falling H–Q curve). All four factors (N, D, $\beta_2$, $\eta_{man}$) influence which pump curve is selected.

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Reciprocating Pump Performance with Losses

<!-- Mermaid Block 2: Reciprocating Pump Indicator Diagram Loss Mapping -->
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crank Rotation at N rpm] --> B[Suction Stroke Begins]
    B --> C[Non-Return Valve Opens]
    C --> D[Water Enters Cylinder Volume A*L]
    D --> E[Delivery Stroke Begins]
    E --> F[Delivery Valve Opens]
    F --> G[Water Discharged to Delivery Pipe]
    G --> H[Work Done per Stroke]
    
    P[Ideal Indicator Diagram] --> Q[Rectangle Hs plus Hd]
    P --> R[100 percent Theoretical Discharge]
    
    S[Slip Loss] --> T[Actual Discharge less than Theoretical]
    U[Friction Loss in Pipes] --> V[Pressure Drop in Diagram]
    W[Acceleration Head] --> X[Pressure Variation at Stroke End]
    
    Y[Slip Cd less than 1] --> AA[Actual Indicator Diagram]
    Z[Friction Pressure Drop] --> AA
    BB[Acceleration Pressure] --> AA
    
    AA --> CC[Area of Actual Diagram]
    CC --> DD[Actual Work per Stroke]
    DD --> EE[Power P equals rho g A L N H by eta]
```
