# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCIET302 Model Paper]**
> Define a peristaltic pump. Mention **any two** distinguishing features that differentiate it from a centrifugal pump.

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

A **peristaltic pump** is a positive-displacement pump that moves fluid by alternately compressing and relaxing a flexible tube using rotating rollers or shoes, causing the fluid to be propelled in a wave-like motion identical to biological peristalsis.

**Two distinguishing features compared to a centrifugal pump:**

1. **Flow Mechanism:** A peristaltic pump produces flow by *positive displacement* (fixed volume per cycle), whereas a centrifugal pump produces flow by *kinetic energy conversion* (impeller throws fluid outward). Therefore, peristaltic flow is essentially independent of discharge pressure, while centrifugal flow drops sharply with pressure.

2. **Fluid Contact:** In a peristaltic pump, the fluid is **completely contained within the tube** and never touches the rotor, rollers, or seals — making it inherently sterile and ideal for pharmaceutical/food applications. In a centrifugal pump, the fluid contacts the impeller, casing, and mechanical seals, posing contamination risks.

3. *(Bonus third point for extra credit:)* Peristaltic pumps are **self-priming** with suction lifts up to 9 m, while centrifugal pumps typically prime only to 4–5 m.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCIET302 Retest]**
> List **any four** advantages and **any two** disadvantages of peristaltic pumps.

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

**Four Advantages:**
1. **Sterile / Contamination-Free Pumping** — fluid is sealed in the tube.
2. **Self-Priming** — can evacuate air from the suction line, suction lift up to 9.5 m.
3. **Reversible Flow** — change rotor direction to reverse flow; no check valve needed.
4. **Handles Highly Viscous and Abrasive Fluids** — slurries up to 80% solids, viscosities up to 2,00,000 cP.

**Two Disadvantages:**
1. **Pulsating Flow** — discrete slugs cause pressure pulsations, requiring dampeners for sensitive applications.
2. **Tube Wear and Limited Life** — elastomeric tubes fatigue and must be replaced every 1000–10000 hours.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper aligned to 2024 Scheme, Module 4, PCIET302]**
> (a) **[7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a tube-type peristaltic pump. Clearly identify the role of the rotor, rollers, tube, and casing.
>
> (b) **[7 Marks]** A peristaltic pump has a tube of internal diameter **15 mm**, the centre-to-centre distance between two adjacent rollers is **50 mm**, and the rotor carries **2 rollers**. The rotor rotates at **300 rpm**. The fluid has density 1100 kg/m³ and viscosity 1.5 cP. Calculate the (i) theoretical discharge, (ii) actual discharge for a slip factor of 0.95, and (iii) Reynolds number of flow.

### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 Marks):**

> **Sketch Description (no diagram in text answer — descriptive):**
> *A peristaltic pump consists of a circular rotor mounted inside a casing. The rotor carries rollers (or shoes) that orbit eccentrically and press against a flexible elastomeric tube bent into a U-shape inside the casing. As the rotor turns, the rollers squeeze the tube progressively, forcing the trapped fluid toward the discharge port.*

**Component Roles (each ≈ 1 mark, totalling 4 marks):**

- **Rotor:** Carries the rollers and transmits the rotational motion of the drive shaft to the rollers. Provides the kinematic input for the peristaltic wave.
- **Rollers:** Compress the tube against the casing to create a moving occlusion (sealing point). As the rotor rotates, each roller successively compresses and releases the tube.
- **Flexible Tube:** The *only* component in contact with the fluid. Made of chemically compatible elastomer. Stores elastic energy during compression and releases it to push the fluid forward.
- **Casing (Track):** Rigid metal housing that constrains the outer surface of the tube. The interior shape matches the roller profile, ensuring uniform and effective compression at the occlusion point.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
> The fluid is trapped between two consecutive rollers, forming a "bolus". As the rotor turns, the trailing roller releases its seal (drawing fluid in from the suction port due to vacuum), while the leading roller advances, pushing the bolus toward the discharge port. The bolus is ejected at the discharge end, and a new cycle begins. This produces a unidirectional, pulsating flow with the fluid completely isolated from mechanical parts.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Internal tube diameter $D = 15 \text{ mm}$, so $R = 7.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- Roller spacing $L = 50 \text{ mm} = 50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- Number of rollers $n_r = 2$
- Rotational speed $N = 300 \text{ rpm}$
- Slip factor $S = 0.95$
- $\rho = 1100 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.5 \text{ cP} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$

**(i) Theoretical Discharge:**

$$
Q_{\text{theo}} = \frac{\pi R^2 L \, n_r \, N}{60}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{theo}} = \frac{\pi \times (7.5 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times (50 \times 10^{-3}) \times 2 \times 300}{60}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{theo}} = \frac{\pi \times 5.625 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.1 \times 600}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 3.375 \times 10^{-3}}{60}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{theo}} = \frac{1.0603 \times 10^{-2}}{60} \approx 1.767 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 10.60 \text{ L/min}
$$

**(ii) Actual Discharge:**

$$
Q_{\text{actual}} = S \times Q_{\text{theo}} = 0.95 \times 10.60 = 10.07 \text{ L/min} = 1.678 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**(iii) Reynolds Number:**

$$
\text{Re} = \frac{2 \rho Q_{\text{actual}}}{\pi \mu R} = \frac{2 \times 1100 \times 1.678 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 7.5 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\text{Re} = \frac{0.3692}{3.534 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 10,447
$$

> **Conclusion:** Flow is **turbulent** (Re > 4000). For most KTU problems, flow is assumed laminar, but here turbulence is real.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Alternative Module Choice]**
> (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the phenomenon of *slip* in a peristaltic pump. How does it affect the volumetric efficiency? Mention **two methods** to minimize slip.
>
> (b) **[7 Marks]** A peristaltic hose pump handles a slurry of density 1400 kg/m³ at a rate of 20 m³/h against a head of 30 m. The pump has 2 rollers, tube diameter 75 mm, and runs at 80 rpm. Calculate (i) the theoretical discharge per revolution, (ii) the volumetric efficiency, and (iii) the shaft power required if the mechanical efficiency is 75%.

### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Slip Phenomenon (7 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** *Slip* is the back-leakage of fluid from the high-pressure (discharge) side to the low-pressure (suction) side through the residual gap at the occlusion point. Because the tube is not perfectly rigid and the roller does not achieve an absolute zero-radius pinch, a thin leakage path always exists.

> **Effect on Volumetric Efficiency (3 marks):** Slip reduces the *net forward flow* delivered by the pump. Since volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = Q_{\text{actual}} / Q_{\text{theo}}$, any increase in slip directly reduces $\eta_v$. For a new pump, $\eta_v \approx 90$–$98\%$. As the tube wears, slip grows and $\eta_v$ may drop below 70%, signalling the need for tube replacement.

> **Two Methods to Minimize Slip (2 marks):**
> 1. **Use a stiffer tube material** (e.g., reinforced hose with textile braid) to reduce elastic deformation under pressure.
> 2. **Increase the roller contact length** by using crowned or larger-diameter rollers that compress a longer axial segment of tube, reducing the average leakage gap.
> 3. *(Bonus:)* Operate at the **manufacturer-recommended back-pressure** to avoid over-stressing the occlusion zone; use **silicone lubricant** on the outer tube surface to reduce roller friction and ensure clean rolling.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $Q_{\text{actual}} = 20 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = \dfrac{20}{3600} = 5.556 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $H = 30 \text{ m}$, $\rho = 1400 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $n_r = 2$, $D = 75 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow R = 0.0375 \text{ m}$, $N = 80 \text{ rpm}$

**(i) Theoretical Discharge per Revolution:**

$$
Q_{\text{theo/rev}} = \frac{\pi R^2 L \, n_r}{?}
$$

> Note: We first need to find $L$. Since the rotor has 2 rollers, and the tube wraps around the inside of a circular casing of (typical) radius $R_c$, the centre-to-centre roller distance is half the casing circumference. However, for this problem we are not given $R_c$ explicitly, so we compute $\eta_v$ using the *given actual flow* and the *theoretical flow* implied by the formula. Let $L = 0.10 \text{ m}$ (assumed standard, KTU board often supplies $L$ separately).

$$
Q_{\text{theo}} = \frac{\pi \times (0.0375)^2 \times 0.10 \times 2 \times 80}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 1.406 \times 10^{-3} \times 16}{60} \approx 1.178 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{theo/rev}} = \frac{Q_{\text{theo}}}{N} = \frac{1.178 \times 10^{-3}}{80/60} = 8.83 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}
$$

**(ii) Volumetric Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_v = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{theo}}} = \frac{5.556 \times 10^{-3}}{1.178 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 4.72
$$

> **Wait — this is greater than 1, indicating an inconsistency in the assumed $L$.** The correct procedure: re-derive $L$ from the given $Q_{\text{actual}}$ and a typical $\eta_v$ of 0.90:
> $$L = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}} \times 60}{\pi R^2 n_r N \eta_v} = \frac{5.556 \times 10^{-3} \times 60}{\pi \times 1.406 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 80 \times 0.90} \approx 5.24 \text{ m}$$
>
> Such a long effective tube length corresponds to a **multi-loop hose arrangement** typical of heavy-duty industrial pumps. The KTU board will normally supply $L$ in the problem statement; here we have derived it as a consistency check.

**(iii) Shaft Power Required:**

$$
P_{\text{hyd}} = \rho g Q_{\text{actual}} H = 1400 \times 9.81 \times 5.556 \times 10^{-3} \times 30 \approx 2289 \text{ W} \approx 2.29 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{P_{\text{hyd}}}{\eta_{\text{mech}}} = \frac{2.29}{0.75} \approx 3.05 \text{ kW}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Peristaltic Pump Questions (Examiner's Perspective):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $n_r$ (number of rollers):** Students frequently write $Q = \pi R^2 L N / 60$ and forget to multiply by the number of rollers. Each missed roller costs **2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
>
> 2. **Mixing up slip factor and volumetric efficiency:** The slip factor $S$ is **less than 1** (it represents retained flow fraction). The volumetric efficiency is **less than 1** (same meaning). Some students write $Q_{\text{actual}} = Q_{\text{theo}} / S$, which is wrong — it should be $Q_{\text{actual}} = S \times Q_{\text{theo}}$.
>
> 3. **Confusing peristaltic pumps with centrifugal pumps:** The flow rate of a peristaltic pump is *independent* of pressure, whereas for a centrifugal pump, $Q$ drops with $H$. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this conceptual error.
>
> 4. **Unit conversion errors:** Tube diameter is usually given in **mm** in KTU problems. Convert to metres **before** squaring. $D = 12 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow R = 0.006 \text{ m}$, **not** $0.006 \times 10^3$ m.
>
> 5. **Wrong formula for Reynolds number:** Use $\text{Re} = 2 \rho Q / (\pi \mu R)$ for peristaltic tube flow, **not** $\text{Re} = \rho v D / \mu$ (which is for open-pipe flow). The $2/(\pi)$ factor is non-negotiable.
>
> 6. **Ignoring pulsation:** When asked about "flow characteristics", always mention that peristaltic flow is **pulsating** with frequency $f = n_r \times N / 60$ Hz. This single sentence often earns 1–2 bonus marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Peristaltic Pump**

- A **peristaltic pump** is a **positive-displacement** pump that moves fluid through a flexible tube by **roller-induced peristaltic waves**, mimicking the human esophagus.

- **Working cycle (4 phases):** **Occlusion → Bolus Trapping → Transport → Discharge Ejection**.

- **Theoretical Discharge (MOST IMPORTANT FORMULA):**
  $$Q_{\text{theo}} = \dfrac{\pi R^2 L \, n_r \, N}{60} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{s}]$$

- **Actual Discharge (with slip):**
  $$Q_{\text{actual}} = S \times Q_{\text{theo}}, \quad S = \eta_v \in [0.5, 0.98]$$

- **Slip Phenomenon:** Back-leakage through the non-ideal occlusion point; increases with $\Delta P$, tube wear, and lower tube stiffness.

- **Wave Speed in Tube Wall:**
  $$c = \sqrt{\dfrac{E h}{2 \rho R}}$$
  Sets the upper limit on rotor speed before wave-breaking.

- **Pressure Rise Model:** $\Delta P \propto \dfrac{\mu Q L}{R^4}$ — strong inverse-fourth-power dependence on tube radius.

- **Reynolds Number for Tube Flow:**
  $$\text{Re} = \dfrac{2 \rho Q}{\pi \mu R}$$

- **Classification:** Tube-type (low P, 2 bar), Hose-type (high P, 16 bar), Linear (OEM/microfluidic).

- **Top 4 Advantages:** Sterile, self-priming, reversible, handles viscous/abrasive fluids.

- **Top 2 Disadvantages:** Pulsating flow output, limited tube life (elastomer fatigue).

- **Top 5 Applications:** (1) Haemodialysis blood pumps, (2) Pharmaceutical sterile filling, (3) Wastewater sludge transfer, (4) Mining slurry pumping, (5) IV infusion and laboratory dosing.

- **Comparison Anchors:** *PD* (peristaltic) vs. *PD* (gear/lobe) vs. *Rotodynamic* (centrifugal) — peristaltic wins on **sterility** and **abrasion handling**, loses on **pressure** and **efficiency**.

- **Pulsation Frequency:** $f_{\text{pulse}} = \dfrac{n_r \times N}{60} \text{ Hz}$ — critical for selecting pulsation dampeners in precision dosing.

- **Reversible Operation:** Achieved simply by reversing motor phase (3-phase) — no internal valve changes required.

- **Self-Priming Limit:** Suction lift up to **9.5 m water column** (~0.93 bar vacuum) — superior to most centrifugal pumps.

- **Tube Material Hierarchy (by chemical resistance):** Silicone (general) < EPDM (alkali) < Viton® (acid) < PTFE (universal, but stiff).

- **Board-Exam Golden Rule:** Always convert $D$ from mm to m **before** any calculation, and always state units in the final answer.

> **End of Module 4 — Peristaltic Pump Notes | KTU 2024 Scheme | PCIET302**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Progressive Cavity Pump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Progressive Cavity Pump

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Progressive Cavity Pump (PCP)**, also known as an **Eccentric Screw Pump** or **Moyno Pump**, is a type of **positive displacement rotary pump** that utilizes a single helical rotor rotating eccentrically within a double-helical elastomeric stator to transport fluids. The pump moves fluid through a series of sealed, progressing cavities formed between the rotor and stator — fluid is displaced axially from the suction end to the discharge end without pulsation or turbulence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *"A progressive cavity pump is a positive displacement pump used for handling viscous, abrasive, and shear-sensitive fluids. It consists of a helical metal rotor turning inside a rubber/moulded elastomer stator of double internal helix. Fluid is carried in cavities between the rotor and stator, progressing from suction to discharge."*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cork Screw Inside a Soft Rubber Tube"

Imagine holding a **solid metal corkscrew (rotor)** and inserting it inside a **flexible rubber tube molded into a double-spiral shape (stator)**. When you rotate the corkscrew:

1. The metal spiral doesn't slip because the rubber grips it tightly.
2. Small **"pockets" or "cavities"** form in the spiral spaces along its length.
3. Each pocket traps a small quantity of liquid at the **suction (inlet)** end.
4. As you keep rotating, each pocket **slides forward** like a sealed bubble along the tube.
5. At the **discharge (outlet)** end, the cavity collapses and squeezes the liquid out.

This is precisely how a PCP operates — except instead of a cork screw held in your hand, an **electric motor** drives the rotor, and instead of a rubber tube, a precision-moulded **elastomer stator** is used.

> [!NOTE]
> **The word "Progressive"** means *gradually moving forward*.
> **The word "Cavity"** means *the sealed pocket of fluid trapped between rotor and stator*.
> Together: cavities progress from suction to discharge, one screw pitch at a time.

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics and Identification

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Rotor Eccentricity | $\mathbf{e = D_s - D_r}$ where $D_s$ = stator bore, $D_r$ = rotor OD | $e$ |
| Stator Pitch Length | **Typically 2× to 4× the rotor pitch** | $L_s$ |
| Rotor Pitch Length | Equal to one full 360° helix turn | $L_r$ |
| Standard Stator-Phase Offset | **2 pitches ahead** (creates sealed cavities) | $\Delta L$ |
| Specific Speed Range | **Low specific speed** (positive displacement family) | $N_s$ |
| Common Rotor Speeds | **100 – 500 RPM** (never high!) | $N$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Unlike centrifugal pumps, PCPs operate at **low rotational speeds** because high speeds cause elastomer overheating, stator wear, and cavitation. KTU examiners LOVE this contrast question.

---

### 1.4 Primary Classification of PCPs (KTU-Standard)

1. **Single-Stage PCP** — One rotor-stator pair (most common).
2. **Multi-Stage PCP** — Multiple rotors and stators in series for higher pressure.
3. **Down-Hole PCP (ESPCP – Electric Submersible PCP)** — Used in oil wells with long drive shafts.
4. **Surface Installation PCP** — Used in chemical, food, and wastewater industries.
5. **Open vs. Bushed-Drive PCP** — Open has exposed drive shaft; bushed uses a sealed protective rod.

---

### 1.5 Real-World Engineering Significance

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PCPs Matter in Industry:**
> - **Oil & Gas**: 60% of artificial lift systems in stripper wells use PCPs.
> - **Wastewater**: Handles raw sewage with rags and solids (up to 30 mm spheres).
> - **Food Industry**: Pumps chocolate, peanut butter, fruit pulp without damaging texture.
> - **Chemical Industry**: Pumps acids, polymers, adhesives, and abrasive slurries.
> - **Mining**: Slurry transport with up to 80% solids by weight.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Anatomy — Component-by-Component Breakdown

A Progressive Cavity Pump consists of the following key elements:

#### A. **The Rotor (Male Helix)**
- Material: **Hardened alloy steel** (often chrome-plated for corrosion resistance).
- Geometry: **Single-start external helix** with high surface finish.
- Cross-section: Usually **circular** (true circular profile is mathematically impossible; close approximations are used).
- Length: **Long and slender** (slenderness ratio L/D can be 5:1 or more).
- Surface treatment: Polished to **Ra ≤ 0.4 μm** to minimize stator wear.

#### B. **The Stator (Female Helix)**
- Material: **Vulcanized elastomer** — natural rubber, nitrile, EPDM, Viton, or polyurethane.
- Geometry: **Double-internal helix** — exactly **twice the pitch** of the rotor.
- Bonded inside a **steel tube housing** for structural rigidity.
- Internal bore profile: Matches the rotor's circular cross-section.
- The stator pitch is **double** the rotor pitch: $L_s = 2 L_r$

#### C. **The Drive Shaft (Connecting Rod)**
- Connects the surface drive motor to the down-hole rotor (in ESPCP applications).
- Protected by **tubing** in deep-well installations.

#### D. **Universal Joint (U-Joint)**
- Allows the rotor's **eccentric motion** (it sweeps a circle around the stator's central axis) to be transmitted smoothly.
- Heavy-duty designs because the rotor traces a **circular path** of radius equal to the eccentricity.

#### E. **Suction and Discharge Ports**
- Suction: Usually at one end; discharge at the opposite end.
- **No valves** are needed (unlike reciprocating pumps) because cavity sealing is mechanical.

#### F. **Elastomer Selection (Critical Engineering Decision)**

| Fluid Type | Recommended Elastomer | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Water / Sewage | Natural Rubber / Nitrile | Cost-effective, abrasion resistant |
| Oils & Hydrocarbons | Nitrile / Viton (FKM) | Oil-resistant |
| Acids / Caustics | EPDM / Viton | Chemical inertness |
| High Temperature | Viton / PTFE | Thermal stability |
| Abrasive Slurries | Polyurethane | Maximum wear resistance |

---

### 2.2 Operational Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The PCP works on a **continuous sealed-cavity displacement** mechanism. Here is the detailed logical flow:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind PCP Operation:**
> Because the stator has **TWICE the pitch** of the rotor, every point along the rotor is **always engaged** with the stator at a fixed angular position. This geometric interlock creates a **continuous seal line** that prevents backflow and ensures one-directional fluid movement.

**Step-by-Step Operation:**

1. **Suction Phase**: At the suction end, as the rotor rotates, the cavity volume **increases** (expanding cavity), creating a partial vacuum that draws fluid in.

2. **Transport Phase**: The fluid-filled cavity **moves axially** along the axis of the pump at exactly one rotor pitch per revolution. Fluid does NOT rotate with the rotor — it translates linearly.

3. **Discharge Phase**: At the discharge end, the cavity volume **decreases** (contracting cavity), compressing and ejecting the fluid at high pressure.

4. **Continuous Action**: Because of the helical geometry, **multiple cavities exist simultaneously** along the pump length, giving continuous, **pulsation-free flow**.

---

### 2.3 Mathematical Foundation — Slip and Leakage Analysis

A real PCP is **NOT 100% efficient** because fluid leaks back from the discharge to the suction through the clearance between the rotor and stator. This phenomenon is called **SLIP**.

**Defining Slip Mathematically:**

$$Q_{slip} = K \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\mu / \rho}$$

Where:
- $Q_{slip}$ = Volumetric slip (back-leakage flow rate)
- $K$ = Slip constant (depends on rotor-stator clearance geometry)
- $\Delta P = P_d - P_s$ = Pressure rise across the pump
- $\mu/\rho$ = Kinematic viscosity of the fluid

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU**: Slip **increases** with pressure difference and **decreases** with fluid viscosity. This is why PCPs handle HIGH VISCOSITY fluids more efficiently than low-viscosity fluids!

**Discharge Flow Rate Equation (Real Pump):**

$$Q_{actual} = Q_{theoretical} - Q_{slip}$$

**Volumetric Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{vol} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{slip}}{Q_{theoretical}}$$

For a **single-stage, single-rotor PCP**:

$$Q_{theoretical} = 4 \cdot e \cdot D_r \cdot L_s \cdot N$$

Where:
- $e$ = Eccentricity = $D_s - D_r$ (where $D_s$ is the maximum internal stator diameter)
- $D_r$ = Rotor diameter
- $L_s$ = Stator pitch length (axial length for one full stator helix turn)
- $N$ = Rotational speed (rev/sec)

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory formulas for any PCP problem in KTU ESE — memorize this table!**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{th} = 4 e D_r L_s N$ | Theoretical discharge (single-stage) | m³/s |
| 2 | $Q_{act} = Q_{th} - Q_{slip}$ | Actual discharge | m³/s |
| 3 | $\eta_{vol} = Q_{act}/Q_{th}$ | Volumetric efficiency | dimensionless |
| 4 | $e = D_s - D_r$ | Eccentricity | m |
| 5 | $P_{shaft} = Q_{act} \cdot \Delta P / \eta_{overall}$ | Shaft power input | W |
| 6 | $H = \Delta P / (\rho g)$ | Developed head | m |
| 7 | $P_{fluid} = \rho g Q_{act} H$ | Hydraulic power output | W |
| 8 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{vol} \cdot \eta_{mech}$ | Overall efficiency | dimensionless |
| 9 | $Slip\% = (Q_{th} - Q_{act})/Q_{th} \times 100$ | Percentage slip | % |
| 10 | $L_s = 2 L_r$ | Geometric pitch relationship | m |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Typical Application | Why PCP is Preferred |
|---|---|---|
| Oil & Gas (Upstream) | Artificial lift in stripper wells | Handles sand, gas, viscous crude |
| Municipal Sewage | Sludge pumping | Passes rags, wipes, solids |
| Mining | Tailings and slurry transport | Abrasive resistance |
| Pulp & Paper | Stock pumping | Shear-sensitive material |
| Chemical | Polymer, resin transfer | No pulsation, no foaming |
| Food Processing | Chocolate, jam, dough | Gentle handling, sanitary designs |

---

### 2.6 Advantages vs. Limitations — KTU Comparison Table

| Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|
| Handles highly viscous fluids (up to 100,000 cP) | Limited to low RPM (elastomer wear) |
| Self-priming (up to 8.5 m suction lift) | Lower discharge pressure per stage (~10 bar) |
| Constant, non-pulsating flow | Stator is a wear item (replace every 1-3 years) |
| Reversible flow (just reverse rotation) | Sensitive to dry running |
| Passes solids up to rotor-stator clearance size | High initial cost vs. centrifugal |
| Low NPSH requirement | Sensitive to fluid temperature extremes |
| Bi-directional operation | Not suitable for high-temperature fluids (>150 °C) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Theoretical Discharge $Q_{th}$

This derivation is the **most frequently asked** derivation in KTU Module 4. We will derive it from **first principles** using geometry.

---

#### **Geometric Setup**

Consider a single cavity at one instant. When the rotor makes **one complete revolution**, each cavity advances by **exactly one rotor pitch length $L_r$** along the axis. The volume of fluid contained in one cavity equals the **displacement volume per pitch**.

---

#### **Step 1 — Identify the Displaced Volume per Cavity**

For a single-stage PCP, the theoretical volume displaced per revolution (across the entire pump) is given by:

$$V_{per\,rev} = 4 \cdot e \cdot D_r \cdot L_s$$

Where:
- $e$ = Eccentricity (m)
- $D_r$ = Rotor diameter (m)
- $L_s$ = Stator pitch length (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the factor "4"?** It emerges from the geometric integration of the eccentric circular rotor sweeping inside the doubly-helical stator. The full derivation uses the **Pappus-Guldinus theorem**: Volume = Area swept × distance traveled by centroid. Two seal lines × two pitch relationships = **4×**.

---

#### **Step 2 — Convert Rotation Rate to Volumetric Flow**

If the rotor rotates at $N$ revolutions **per second**, the theoretical discharge is:

$$Q_{theoretical} = V_{per\,rev} \times N$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = 4 \cdot e \cdot D_r \cdot L_s \cdot N}$$

> **Units check:**
> $[e] = m, [D_r] = m, [L_s] = m, [N] = s^{-1}$
> $\Rightarrow Q_{th} = m \cdot m \cdot m \cdot s^{-1} = m^3/s$ ✓

---

#### **Step 3 — Derive Actual Discharge Considering Slip**

The **slip flow** $Q_{slip}$ is back-leakage from discharge to suction through the rotor-stator clearance. Empirically:

$$Q_{slip} = \frac{K \cdot \Delta P}{\mu / \rho} = \frac{K \cdot \rho \cdot \Delta P}{\mu}$$

Where:
- $K$ = Slip coefficient (function of geometry, m³)
- $\Delta P = P_d - P_s$ = Pressure rise (Pa)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\rho$ = Fluid density (kg/m³)

Therefore:

$$Q_{actual} = Q_{theoretical} - Q_{slip}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = 4 e D_r L_s N - \frac{K \rho \Delta P}{\mu}}$$

---

#### **Step 4 — Derive Volumetric Efficiency**

By definition:

$$\eta_{vol} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$$

Substituting both expressions:

$$\eta_{vol} = 1 - \frac{Q_{slip}}{Q_{th}} = 1 - \frac{K \rho \Delta P / \mu}{4 e D_r L_s N}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{vol} = 1 - \frac{K \rho \Delta P}{4 e D_r L_s N \mu}}$$

---

#### **Step 5 — Derive Shaft Power and Overall Efficiency**

Hydraulic power delivered to fluid:

$$P_{fluid} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{act} \cdot H = Q_{act} \cdot \Delta P$$

Shaft power input (from motor):

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{fluid}}{\eta_{overall}} = \frac{Q_{act} \cdot \Delta P}{\eta_{overall}}$$

Overall efficiency:

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{fluid}}{P_{shaft}} = \eta_{vol} \cdot \eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{hydraulic}$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Derivations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following problems are **archetype problems** for KTU Module 4 PCP section. Practice these until you can solve them cold.

---

#### **Problem 1: Basic Theoretical Discharge Calculation**

**Statement:**
A progressive cavity pump has a rotor of diameter $D_r = 80$ mm and an eccentricity $e = 6$ mm. The stator pitch length is $L_s = 200$ mm. The pump operates at 300 RPM. Determine the theoretical discharge.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert all quantities to SI base units.

$$D_r = 80\,mm = 0.080\,m$$

$$e = 6\,mm = 0.006\,m$$

$$L_s = 200\,mm = 0.200\,m$$

$$N = 300\,RPM = \frac{300}{60}\,rev/s = 5\,rev/s$$

**Step 2:** Apply the theoretical discharge formula.

$$Q_{th} = 4 \cdot e \cdot D_r \cdot L_s \cdot N$$

Substituting the values:

$$Q_{th} = 4 \times 0.006 \times 0.080 \times 0.200 \times 5$$

$$Q_{th} = 4 \times 0.006 \times 0.080 = 0.001920$$

$$Q_{th} = 0.001920 \times 0.200 = 0.000384$$

$$Q_{th} = 0.000384 \times 5 = 0.001920\,m^3/s$$

**Step 3:** Convert to litres per minute for industrial readability.

$$Q_{th} = 0.001920\,m^3/s \times 1000\,L/m^3 \times 60\,s/min = 115.2\,L/min$$

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = 0.00192\,m^3/s = 115.2\,L/min}$$

---

#### **Problem 2: Volumetric Efficiency and Slip Calculation**

**Statement:**
For the pump in Problem 1, the actual discharge measured is $Q_{act} = 100\,L/min$ against a pressure rise of $\Delta P = 8$ bar. Calculate:
(a) Volumetric efficiency
(b) Slip flow in L/min
(c) Mechanical efficiency if overall efficiency is 75%

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert units.

$$Q_{act} = 100\,L/min = \frac{100 \times 0.001}{60}\,m^3/s = 0.001667\,m^3/s$$

$$\Delta P = 8\,bar = 8 \times 10^5\,Pa = 800{,}000\,Pa$$

**Step 2 (a): Volumetric Efficiency**

$$\eta_{vol} = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{0.001667}{0.001920} = 0.8682$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{vol} = 86.82\%}$$

**Step 3 (b): Slip Flow**

$$Q_{slip} = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 0.001920 - 0.001667 = 0.000253\,m^3/s$$

Converting to L/min:

$$Q_{slip} = 0.000253 \times 60 \times 1000 = 15.2\,L/min$$

$$\boxed{Q_{slip} = 15.2\,L/min}$$

**Step 4 (c): Mechanical Efficiency**

Given $\eta_{overall} = 0.75$ and assuming $\eta_{hydraulic} \approx 1$ (typical for PCPs):

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech}$$

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{\eta_{overall}}{\eta_{vol}} = \frac{0.75}{0.8682} = 0.8638$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{mech} = 86.38\%}$$

---

#### **Problem 3: Multi-Stage Pressure Calculation**

**Statement:**
A 3-stage PCP with single-stage pressure capability of 10 bar is used to pump oil. If the required delivery head is 240 m of oil, determine:
(a) Whether 3 stages are sufficient
(Specific gravity of oil = 0.85)

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert oil head to pressure.

$$\Delta P_{required} = \rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot H = 0.85 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 240$$

$$\Delta P_{required} = 850 \times 9.81 \times 240 = 2{,}001{,}240\,Pa$$

$$\Delta P_{required} = 20.01\,bar$$

**Step 2:** Maximum pressure from 3 stages.

$$\Delta P_{max} = 3 \times 10 = 30\,bar$$

**Step 3:** Compare.

$$\Delta P_{max} = 30\,bar > \Delta P_{required} = 20.01\,bar$$

$$\boxed{\text{3 stages are sufficient. Head margin} = (30 - 20.01)/20.01 = 49.9\%}$$

---

### 3.3 Slip Behavior with Viscous Fluids — Physical Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Conceptual Question Favourite:**
> *"Why does a PCP's volumetric efficiency increase with fluid viscosity?"*

**Answer (from the slip equation):**

$$Q_{slip} = \frac{K \rho \Delta P}{\mu}$$

As viscosity $\mu$ **increases**, $Q_{slip}$ **decreases** (inversely proportional). Therefore:

$$\eta_{vol} = 1 - \frac{Q_{slip}}{Q_{th}}$$

Slip becomes negligible at high viscosity, so $\eta_{vol} \rightarrow 1$ (100%). This is the **fundamental engineering reason** why PCPs are the pump of choice for viscous crudes, polymers, and food pastes.

Conversely, with water (low $\mu$), slip is significant — this is why PCPs are not preferred for clean water pumping.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture of a Progressive Cavity Pump system**, including all major subsystems and their interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Drive Motor - Electric or Hydraulic] --> B[Gear Reducer - Speed Reduction to 100-500 RPM]
    B --> C[Universal Joint - Accommodates Eccentric Motion]
    C --> D[Drive Shaft - Transmits Torque to Rotor]
    D --> E[Rotor - Single Helical Hardened Steel]
    E --> F[Stator - Double Helical Elastomer Lined Steel Tube]
    F --> G[Cavity Formation - Sealed Pockets Form Along Axis]
    G --> H[Suction Port - Inlet for Fluid Entry]
    G --> I[Discharge Port - Outlet for Pressurized Fluid]
    F --> J[Elastomer Cooling - Bypass Line for Hot Slurries]
    J --> F
    F --> K[Bearing Housing - Supports Eccentric Loading]
    K --> L[Seal Assembly - Prevents Leakage to Atmosphere]
    L --> M[Mounting Frame - Anchors the Pump Assembly]

    style A fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#f4b084,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.2 Cavity Progression Schematic — Conceptual Block Diagram

This diagram illustrates how cavities form, progress, and collapse along the pump length during one rotor revolution.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Suction End - Low Pressure] --> B[Cavity 1 - Expanding - Drawing Fluid In]
    B --> C[Cavity 2 - Full - Transporting Fluid]
    C --> D[Cavity 3 - Full - Transporting Fluid]
    D --> E[Cavity 4 - Contracting - Compressing Fluid]
    E --> F[Discharge End - High Pressure - Fluid Ejected]

    G[Rotor Rotating Counter Clockwise] -.-> B
    G -.-> C
    G -.-> D
    G -.-> E

    style A fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define a Progressive Cavity Pump. List any FOUR advantages it has over a centrifugal pump.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
A Progressive Cavity Pump is a positive displacement rotary pump consisting of a single helical metal rotor turning eccentrically inside a double-helical elastomeric stator. Fluid is trapped in sealed cavities between the rotor and stator, which progress axially from suction to discharge as the rotor rotates.

**Advantages over Centrifugal Pump (4 × 0.375 = 1.5 marks):**

1. Handles highly viscous fluids (up to 100,000 cP) efficiently.
2. Provides constant, non-pulsating discharge regardless of pressure.
3. Self-priming capability with high suction lift.
4. Can pass solids and abrasive particles without damage.

---

#### **Question A2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Why are Progressive Cavity Pumps operated at LOW rotational speeds? What happens if they are run at high RPM?*

**Model Answer:**

**Reason for Low RPM (1.5 marks):**
PCPs use an **elastomeric stator** (rubber/elastomer) which is a viscoelastic, heat-sensitive material. At high RPM, frictional heating between rotor and stator rapidly degrades the elastomer, causing swelling, hardening, and ultimate failure.

**Consequences of High RPM (1.5 marks):**

1. **Stator overheating** → Reduced elastomer life (typically reduces from 3 years to weeks).
2. **Severe slip increase** → Volumetric efficiency drops sharply.
3. **Cavitation at suction** → NPSH requirements are violated at high speeds.
4. **Mechanical vibration** due to unbalanced eccentric forces at high frequency.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Many students write "PCPs run at low RPM because they are small pumps." This is **WRONG** and will cost marks. The correct reason is **elastomer thermal limitations and cavitation NPSH constraints**.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a Progressive Cavity Pump. Explain the role of the elastomer stator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-stage PCP has rotor diameter $D_r = 100$ mm, stator pitch $L_s = 250$ mm, and eccentricity $e = 5$ mm. It operates at 400 RPM. If the volumetric efficiency is 88%, calculate the actual discharge in L/s and the slip flow rate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION A:**

### **Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks)**

**Construction (3.5 marks):**

The PCP consists of three main elements:

**1. Rotor:** A single-start external helical shaft made of **hardened chrome-plated steel**, with a circular cross-section and a high surface polish to minimize stator wear.

**2. Stator:** A double-start internal helix moulded from **vulcanized elastomer** (natural rubber, nitrile, EPDM, or polyurethane depending on fluid compatibility), bonded inside a rigid steel tube.

**3. Drive System:** Consists of a motor, gearbox, universal joint, and connecting rod that drives the rotor.

**Geometric Relationship:** The stator has **twice the pitch** of the rotor ($L_s = 2L_r$). The rotor is positioned with its axis offset from the stator axis by an **eccentricity $e$**. This creates a **circular eccentric motion** of the rotor inside the stator.

**Working (3.5 marks):**

As the rotor turns inside the stator, sealed cavities form between the contacting surfaces. At any instant, there are **multiple cavities** along the length of the pump.

- **At the suction end**: The cavity volume increases → partial vacuum → fluid enters.
- **Along the length**: Each cavity moves axially at the rate of one rotor pitch per revolution, carrying the fluid forward without rotation.
- **At the discharge end**: The cavity volume decreases → fluid is pressurized and expelled.

This continuous, sealed-cavity action provides **pulsation-free, constant discharge** even when handling viscous or solids-laden fluids.

**Role of Elastomer Stator (built into discussion):**
The elastomer provides:
1. **Sealing** — Its elasticity conforms to the rotor profile, preventing backflow.
2. **Cushioning** — Absorbs vibration and allows passage of solids.
3. **Self-compensating wear** — Minor wear is compensated by elastomer elasticity.
4. **Fluid compatibility** — Different elastomers allow handling of corrosive fluids.

---

### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**

$$D_r = 100\,mm = 0.100\,m$$

$$L_s = 250\,mm = 0.250\,m$$

$$e = 5\,mm = 0.005\,m$$

$$N = 400\,RPM = \frac{400}{60}\,rev/s = 6.667\,rev/s$$

$$\eta_{vol} = 88\% = 0.88$$

**Step 1: Calculate Theoretical Discharge**

$$Q_{th} = 4 \cdot e \cdot D_r \cdot L_s \cdot N$$

$$Q_{th} = 4 \times 0.005 \times 0.100 \times 0.250 \times 6.667$$

$$Q_{th} = 4 \times 0.005 = 0.0200$$

$$Q_{th} = 0.0200 \times 0.100 = 0.00200$$

$$Q_{th} = 0.00200 \times 0.250 = 0.000500\,m^3/rev\text{-}group$$

Wait — recomputing carefully:

$$Q_{th} = 4 \times 0.005 \times 0.100 \times 0.250 \times 6.667$$

Multiplying 4 × 0.005 × 0.100 = 0.002
Multiplying 0.002 × 0.250 = 0.000500
Multiplying 0.000500 × 6.667 = 0.003334 m³/s

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = 0.003334\,m^3/s = 3.334\,L/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Actual Discharge**

$$Q_{act} = \eta_{vol} \times Q_{th} = 0.88 \times 0.003334$$

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = 0.002934\,m^3/s = 2.934\,L/s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Slip Flow**

$$Q_{slip} = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 0.003334 - 0.002934$$

$$\boxed{Q_{slip} = 0.000400\,m^3/s = 0.400\,L/s}$$

**Step 4: Express in L/min for clarity**

$$Q_{act} = 2.934 \times 60 = 176.04\,L/min$$

$$Q_{slip} = 0.400 \times 60 = 24.00\,L/min$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **slip** in a Progressive Cavity Pump. Derive the expression for the theoretical discharge $Q_{th}$ and develop the slip correction equation to obtain the actual discharge. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PCP pumps oil of specific gravity 0.9 and viscosity 0.5 Pa·s at 250 RPM. The pressure rise across the pump is 6 bar. If the slip coefficient $K = 2.5 \times 10^{-9}$ m³ and theoretical discharge is 2.5 L/s, determine: (i) actual discharge, (ii) volumetric efficiency, (iii) hydraulic power. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION B:**

### **Part (a) — Slip Concept and Derivation (7 marks)**

**Concept of Slip (2 marks):**

In an ideal PCP, all fluid entering the suction should be delivered at the discharge. However, due to the **finite clearance** between the rotor and the elastomer stator, a small portion of fluid leaks back from the high-pressure discharge side to the low-pressure suction side. This back-leakage is called **SLIP**.

**Causes of slip:**
- Clearance between rotor and stator due to elastomer compliance.
- Pressure differential between discharge and suction.
- Low fluid viscosity (slip is higher for low-viscosity fluids).

**Derivation of $Q_{th}$ (3 marks):**

For a single-stage PCP with rotor diameter $D_r$, eccentricity $e$, stator pitch $L_s$, and speed $N$ rev/s:

$$Q_{theoretical} = 4 \cdot e \cdot D_r \cdot L_s \cdot N$$

**Derivation of Actual Discharge (2 marks):**

Including the slip back-flow $Q_{slip}$ which depends on pressure rise and fluid viscosity:

$$Q_{slip} = \frac{K \cdot \rho \cdot \Delta P}{\mu}$$

Therefore:

$$Q_{actual} = Q_{theoretical} - Q_{slip} = 4 e D_r L_s N - \frac{K \rho \Delta P}{\mu}$$

And:

$$\eta_{vol} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$$

---

### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**

$$\rho = 0.9 \times 1000 = 900\,kg/m^3$$

$$\mu = 0.5\,Pa \cdot s$$

$$N = 250\,RPM = 4.167\,rev/s$$

$$\Delta P = 6\,bar = 6 \times 10^5\,Pa$$

$$K = 2.5 \times 10^{-9}\,m^3$$

$$Q_{th} = 2.5\,L/s = 0.0025\,m^3/s$$

**Step (i): Actual Discharge**

First compute the slip flow:

$$Q_{slip} = \frac{K \cdot \rho \cdot \Delta P}{\mu}$$

$$Q_{slip} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 900 \times 6 \times 10^5}{0.5}$$

Numerator: $2.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 900 = 2.25 \times 10^{-6}$

$$2.25 \times 10^{-6} \times 6 \times 10^5 = 2.25 \times 6 \times 10^{-1} = 1.35$$

Denominator: $0.5$

$$Q_{slip} = \frac{1.35}{0.5} = 2.7\,m^3/s$$

Wait — this is too large. Recheck units: $K$ must have proper scaling.

Recalculating with proper dimensional analysis. Standard slip coefficient has units $\text{m}^3$ when used with SI. Using the conventional PCP formula in industry units (Q in L/min, ΔP in bar, μ in cP):

$$Q_{slip\,[L/min]} = K \cdot \frac{\Delta P\,[bar]}{\mu\,[cP]}$$

Converting: $\mu = 0.5\,Pa \cdot s = 500\,cP$, $\Delta P = 6\,bar$.

$$Q_{slip} = 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \times \frac{6}{500}\,m^3/s$$

$$Q_{slip} = 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.012\,m^3/s$$

$$Q_{slip} = 3.0 \times 10^{-11}\,m^3/s = 3.0 \times 10^{-5}\,L/s$$

$$Q_{act} = Q_{th} - Q_{slip} = 2.5 - 0.00003 \approx 2.5\,L/s$$

$$\boxed{Q_{act} \approx 2.49997\,L/s \approx 2.5\,L/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is slip so low here?** Because oil has HIGH viscosity (500 cP). This demonstrates the KTU principle: **PCPs are nearly 100% volumetrically efficient with viscous fluids.**

**Step (ii): Volumetric Efficiency**

$$\eta_{vol} = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{2.5}{2.5} = 1.000$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{vol} \approx 100\%}$$

**Step (iii): Hydraulic Power**

$$P_{hydraulic} = \Delta P \times Q_{act}$$

$$P_{hydraulic} = 6 \times 10^5 \times 0.0025 = 1500\,W$$

$$\boxed{P_{hydraulic} = 1.5\,kW}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion (Most common — 2-3 marks lost)**: Always convert mm to m, RPM to rev/s, bar to Pa **BEFORE** substituting into the formula. Many students substitute mixed units and get answers off by 1000× or 60×.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Slip**: When a problem gives you "volumetric efficiency," students sometimes forget to apply it. Always read the question twice.
>
> 3. **Stator Pitch vs. Rotor Pitch Confusion**: The formula uses $L_s$ (stator pitch), NOT $L_r$ (rotor pitch). Remember $L_s = 2 L_r$.
>
> 4. **Wrong Power Formula**: Hydraulic power is $P = \Delta P \times Q$, NOT $P = \rho g Q H$ alone (though they are equivalent if $\Delta P = \rho g H$).
>
> 5. **Confusing PCP with Centrifugal Pump**: Don't write $Q \propto \sqrt{H}$ or use affinity laws — PCPs are positive displacement and obey $Q \propto N$ linearly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section before the exam. It is your survival kit.**

#### **Must-Know Facts**

- ✅ PCP = **Positive displacement** pump (NOT centrifugal).
- ✅ Components: **Rotor (single helix, steel) + Stator (double helix, elastomer)**.
- ✅ Geometric rule: **$L_s = 2 L_r$** (stator pitch is double rotor pitch).
- ✅ Eccentricity: $e = D_s - D_r$ (offset between rotor and stator axes).
- ✅ Standard speed: **100–500 RPM** (low, due to elastomer thermal limits).
- ✅ Suction principle: **Self-priming** (high suction lift possible).
- ✅ Discharge: **Constant, pulsation-free**, independent of pressure.
- ✅ Best for: **Viscous, abrasive, solids-laden, shear-sensitive fluids**.

#### **Must-Know Formulas (in order of importance)**

1. **Theoretical Discharge**: $Q_{th} = 4 e D_r L_s N$
2. **Actual Discharge**: $Q_{act} = Q_{th} - Q_{slip}$
3. **Volumetric Efficiency**: $\eta_{vol} = Q_{act}/Q_{th}$
4. **Slip Flow**: $Q_{slip} = K \rho \Delta P / \mu$
5. **Shaft Power**: $P_{shaft} = Q_{act} \Delta P / \eta_{overall}$
6. **Hydraulic Power**: $P_{fluid} = \rho g Q_{act} H$
7. **Overall Efficiency**: $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech}$

#### **Must-Know Comparative Statements (for 3-mark questions)**

| Comparison | Statement |
|---|---|
| PCP vs Centrifugal (viscosity) | PCP **wins** for viscous fluids |
| PCP vs Centrifugal (flow) | PCP gives **constant** flow; centrifugal gives **variable** flow |
| PCP vs Centrifugal (efficiency) | PCP efficiency **rises** with viscosity; centrifugal efficiency **falls** |
| Slip vs Viscosity | Slip is **inversely** proportional to viscosity |
| Slip vs Pressure | Slip is **directly** proportional to $\Delta P$ |
| Flow vs Speed | Flow is **directly proportional** to N (linear) |

#### **Must-Avoid Errors in Exam**

- ❌ Don't say "PCP works on centrifugal force."
- ❌ Don't write "$L_s = L_r$" — always remember the **factor of 2**.
- ❌ Don't forget to convert **RPM to rev/s** in $Q_{th}$ formula.
- ❌ Don't say "PCPs can run at 3000 RPM" — this is **wrong**, maximum is around 500 RPM.
- ❌ Don't confuse **stator pitch** with **stator length** (the pump may have multiple stator pitches along its length).

#### **Last-Minute Memory Hooks**

1. **"Two-for-One"**: One rotor turn moves fluid by **one rotor pitch**; **stator has twice the pitch** → continuous sealing.
2. **"Eccentric Inside Elastic"**: Rotor is hard and eccentric; stator is soft and concentric to a different axis → sealed cavities.
3. **"Viscous is Victorious"**: High viscosity = low slip = high efficiency. **PCP LOVES viscous fluids.**
4. **"Wear Item"**: The stator is the **consumable part** — must be replaced every 1–3 years depending on service.
5. **"No Valves, No Pulse"**: Unlike reciprocating pumps, PCPs need **no check valves** and produce **no pressure pulsation**.

---

> **End of Module 4 — Progressive Cavity Pump Notes**

topics.title: Drum Pump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Drum Pump

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Drum Pump** (also termed a **Barrel Pump** or **Container Pump**) is a compact, portable, positive-displacement (or in some variants, centrifugal) pumping device specifically engineered to evacuate or transfer fluids—primarily low to medium viscosity liquids—directly from standardized industrial containers such as **200 L steel drums**, **plastic barrels (IBC totes)**, or **carboys**. The pump's suction tube is sized to reach the bottom of these containers, and its discharge assembly is mounted on the container's mouth, allowing controlled delivery without tilting, spilling, or manual scooping.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, drum pumps are classified as **special-purpose positive-displacement pumps** belonging to the family of **rotodynamic/reciprocating hydraulic machines**, characterized by:
- A vertically mounted pump tube inserted into the container
- A drive unit (electric motor, pneumatic motor, or manual crank) coupled at the top
- A sealing arrangement that prevents vapor escape and contamination
- Direct immersion of the pumping element in the fluid

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Drum pumps are studied as a representative example of *specialized hydraulic machines* used in fluid transfer applications. Students must understand the constructional features, working principle, and discharge/head relationships of drum pumps. The topic is often assessed as a 3-mark conceptual question in Part A and occasionally as part of a 14-mark Part B question comparing positive-displacement pumps.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

**The "Straw in a Juice Box" Analogy:**

Imagine you have a sealed juice box and you want to drink it without lifting or tipping the box. You insert a long straw all the way to the bottom of the box. When you suck at the top, the liquid rises through the straw and out to your mouth. Now, replace your mouth with a small mechanical impeller, and the straw with the rigid pump tube of a drum pump. The impeller "sucks" fluid continuously and pushes it out through a discharge nozzle.

**Real-World Analogy — The Hand Operated Hand-Pump at Construction Sites:**

Picture a hand-cranked water pump used on construction sites to draw diesel from a 200 L barrel. As the operator rotates the crank, a geared mechanism inside the pump drives a small rotor/impeller at the bottom of the suction tube. The rotor creates a low-pressure zone that lifts the diesel up through the tube and out through a flexible hose into a smaller container or vehicle tank. This is exactly how a **manual or electric drum pump** functions in industries handling lubricants, coolants, solvents, and mild chemicals.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following standard engineering values govern drum pump design and selection in industrial practice.

| Parameter | Standard Range | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Container Capacity | 50 – 220 (typical 200) | L |
| Suction Tube Length | 700 – 1200 | mm |
| Tube Outer Diameter | 28 – 42 | mm |
| Drive Power Rating | 0.18 – 1.5 (0.25 – 2) | kW (HP) |
| Flow Rate (Q) | 8 – 250 | L/min |
| Maximum Delivery Head | 10 – 40 | m |
| Maximum Viscosity Range | 1 – 1500 (motor) / 1 – 500 (manual) | cP (mPa·s) |
| Specific Gravity (SG) Tolerance | Up to 1.9 | – |
| Operating Temperature | -20 to +180 | °C |
| Pressure Rating (Outlet) | 2 – 8 | bar |

**Bold Constants for KTU Recall:**
- **200 L** = standard drum capacity
- **1 m³ = 1000 L** = conversion factor for flow rate
- **g = 9.81 m/s²** = standard acceleration due to gravity
- **ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³** = reference density for SG calculations
- **1 HP = 0.7457 kW** = standard motor power conversion

### 1.4 Classification of Drum Pumps

Drum pumps are categorized based on their **drive mechanism** and **pumping principle**:

**A. By Drive Mechanism:**
1. **Manual (Hand-Operated) Drum Pumps** — Crank or lever-driven; portable, no power required
2. **Electric Motor-Driven Drum Pumps** — AC or DC motor coupled to the pump tube
3. **Pneumatic (Compressed-Air) Drum Pumps** — Driven by compressed air; explosion-proof, used for flammable fluids
4. **Battery-Powered Drum Pumps** — Cordless, portable electric variants

**B. By Pumping Principle:**
1. **Centrifugal-Type Drum Pumps** — Use an impeller; suited for low-viscosity, clean fluids
2. **Positive-Displacement (PD) Rotary Drum Pumps** — Use eccentric screw, gear, or vane mechanisms
3. **Reciprocating Piston Drum Pumps** — Use oscillating piston; suited for high-viscosity fluids

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The working of a **typical rotary positive-displacement drum pump** (most common industrial type) follows a precise four-stage operational sequence:

**Step 1 — Immersion and Priming**
The pump tube (suction tube) is inserted into the container until its inlet port sits approximately **10–20 mm** above the container bottom. This prevents suction of settled sludge or sediment. The pump is **self-priming** because the pumping element (impeller/rotor) is submerged in the fluid, eliminating the need for an external priming step.

**Step 2 — Suction (Intake) Phase**
As the drive motor rotates the pumping element (typically at **1400–2900 rpm** for motor-driven units), a **low-pressure region** is created at the inlet. The atmospheric pressure acting on the free surface of the liquid in the container forces the fluid up the suction tube. For rotary PD types, the meshing of gears/eccentric screws creates discrete sealed cavities that expand on the inlet side, drawing fluid in.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Self-Priming?** Because the impeller or rotor is *physically immersed* in the fluid, the air-cavity problems that plague dry-mounted centrifugal pumps are eliminated. The pump can start delivering fluid from the very first revolution.

**Step 3 — Transfer and Pressurization**
The fluid moves axially up the tube through the rotation of the pumping element. In centrifugal-type drum pumps, the kinetic energy imparted by the impeller is converted to pressure energy within the narrow discharge passage. In PD-type pumps, the trapped fluid is mechanically carried upward by the rotating cavities.

**Step 4 — Discharge (Delivery)**
The pressurized fluid exits through the discharge nozzle, which is typically fitted with a **hose spout, trigger nozzle, or flow meter**. The discharge head (H) is determined by the pump's pressure rating — typically **5 to 30 m** of fluid column.

### 2.2 Constructive Components — Detailed Architecture

The drum pump comprises six essential sub-assemblies:

1. **Drive Head (Motor Housing):** Contains the motor/gearbox; provides rotational input
2. **Coupling Shaft:** Connects the motor to the pumping element via the suction tube
3. **Pump Tube (Suction Tube):** Rigid stainless-steel or polypropylene tube; the structural backbone
4. **Pumping Element:** Impeller (centrifugal) or rotor/stator (eccentric screw) or gear pair
5. **Mechanical Seal:** Prevents leakage of fluid up the shaft; critical for hazardous fluids
6. **Discharge Assembly:** Outlet port with integrated hose barb and shut-off valve

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all exam-relevant formulas** for drum pump problems. Master these for the KTU board examination.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Q = A \times v$ | Volumetric flow rate (continuity) | m³/s |
| 2 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2} \times v$ | Flow through the discharge pipe | m³/s |
| 3 | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}}$ | Fluid velocity in the discharge line | m/s |
| 4 | $Q_{PD} = N \times V_{d}$ | Theoretical discharge of PD pump (speed × displacement) | m³/s |
| 5 | $Q_{actual} = Q_{PD} \times \eta_{vol}$ | Actual discharge accounting for volumetric efficiency | m³/s |
| 6 | $H = \dfrac{p_{d} - p_{s}}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_{d}^{2} - v_{s}^{2}}{2g} + (z_{d} - z_{s})$ | Total head developed by the pump (Bernoulli-based) | m |
| 7 | $P_{water} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{1000}$ | Water power (output hydraulic power) | kW |
| 8 | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{water}}{\eta_{overall}} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{1000 \cdot \eta_{overall}}$ | Required shaft input power | kW |
| 9 | $P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{motor}} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{1000 \cdot \eta_{overall} \cdot \eta_{motor}}$ | Electric motor nameplate power | kW |
| 10 | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Specific gravity relation | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{hyd}}$ | Overall efficiency decomposition | dimensionless |
| 12 | $N_{s} = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{5/4}}$ | Specific speed (for classification) | rpm·m⁻³ᐟ⁴·s⁻¹ᐟ² |
| 13 | $T = \dfrac{P_{shaft} \times 60}{2\pi N}$ | Torque transmitted by the shaft | N·m |
| 14 | $\Delta p = \rho g H$ | Pressure rise across the pump | Pa |
| 15 | $W = \rho g H$ | Specific work done per unit weight | m |

> **Notation:** $Q$ = discharge, $A$ = cross-sectional area, $D$ = pipe diameter, $v$ = velocity, $N$ = rotational speed (rev/s or rpm), $V_d$ = displacement volume per revolution, $H$ = head, $P$ = power, $\eta$ = efficiency, $SG$ = specific gravity, $T$ = torque, $N_s$ = specific speed, $W$ = work per unit weight.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Drum pumps are indispensable in the following industrial sectors:

- **Petrochemical Industry:** Transfer of lubricating oils, diesel, kerosene from 200 L drums to machinery sumps
- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Sterile transfer of solvents, alcohols, and API solutions
- **Food & Beverage Industry:** Hygienic transfer of edible oils, syrups, and flavoring concentrates (using food-grade stainless steel)
- **Automotive Workshops:** Extraction of engine oil, coolant, and gear oil from storage drums
- **Chemical Processing:** Safe handling of acids, alkalis, and corrosive chemicals (using PP/PVDF construction)
- **Military & Defense:** Field-deployable fuel transfer from standard NATO drums
- **Printing Industry:** Transfer of inks and solvents from bulk containers

The engineering significance lies in **eliminating manual handling hazards**, ensuring **accurate volumetric dispensing**, and providing **closed-loop transfer** that prevents contamination, spillage, and VOC emissions.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Discharge of a Positive-Displacement Drum Pump

**Problem Statement:** A positive-displacement drum pump has a displacement volume per revolution of $V_d = 50 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$. The motor drives the pump at $N = 1450 \text{ rpm}$. The volumetric efficiency of the pump is $\eta_{vol} = 0.92$. Calculate the actual discharge in L/min and the fluid velocity at the discharge nozzle (nozzle diameter = 12 mm).

**Step 1 — Identify the Governing Equation**

For a positive-displacement pump, the theoretical discharge is the product of the number of displacement cycles per unit time and the volume displaced per cycle.

$$Q_{theoretical} = N \times V_{d}$$

where $N$ must be in **revolutions per second** (rev/s) for SI consistency.

**Step 2 — Convert Rotational Speed to rev/s**

$$N = \frac{1450 \text{ rpm}}{60 \text{ s/min}} = 24.1667 \text{ rev/s}$$

**Explanation:** We divide by 60 because 1 minute = 60 seconds, converting revolutions per minute to revolutions per second.

**Step 3 — Convert Displacement Volume to SI Units (m³)**

$$V_{d} = 50 \text{ cm}^3 = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3 = 5.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}$$

**Explanation:** 1 m = 100 cm, so 1 m³ = 10⁶ cm³. Therefore, 1 cm³ = 10⁻⁶ m³.

**Step 4 — Calculate the Theoretical Discharge**

$$Q_{theoretical} = 24.1667 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$Q_{theoretical} = 1.2083 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Explanation:** Each revolution displaces 50 cm³ of fluid, and the pump completes 24.1667 revolutions every second.

**Step 5 — Convert to L/min for Industrial Readability**

$$Q_{theoretical} = 1.2083 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \times \frac{1000 \text{ L}}{1 \text{ m}^3} \times \frac{60 \text{ s}}{1 \text{ min}}$$

$$Q_{theoretical} = 1.2083 \times 10^{-3} \times 60000 = 72.5 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Volumetric Efficiency to Obtain Actual Discharge**

$$Q_{actual} = Q_{theoretical} \times \eta_{vol}$$

$$Q_{actual} = 72.5 \times 0.92 = 66.7 \text{ L/min}$$

**Explanation:** The volumetric efficiency accounts for internal leakage past the sealing clearances and through the relief path in a PD pump. The remaining 8% of fluid recirculates internally and does not reach the discharge.

**Step 7 — Calculate Discharge Velocity at the Nozzle**

The cross-sectional area of the 12 mm nozzle is:

$$A_{nozzle} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.012)^{2}$$

$$A_{nozzle} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 1.44 \times 10^{-4} = 1.13097 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

The actual flow in m³/s:

$$Q_{actual} = 66.7 \text{ L/min} = \frac{66.7 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 1.1117 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Therefore, the discharge velocity:

$$v = \frac{Q_{actual}}{A_{nozzle}} = \frac{1.1117 \times 10^{-3}}{1.13097 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$v = 9.83 \text{ m/s}$$

**Final Result Summary:**
- $Q_{actual} = 66.7 \text{ L/min}$
- $v_{discharge} = 9.83 \text{ m/s}$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Power Required to Drive a Drum Pump

**Problem Statement:** A drum pump delivers lubricating oil (SG = 0.88, kinematic viscosity = 90 cSt) from a 200 L drum at a rate of $Q = 80 \text{ L/min}$. The total delivery head (vertical lift + friction) is $H = 8 \text{ m}$. The overall efficiency of the pump is $\eta_{overall} = 0.55$ and the motor efficiency is $\eta_{motor} = 0.85$. Calculate: (a) the water power, (b) the shaft power, and (c) the required motor nameplate power.

**Step 1 — Identify the Densities**

$$\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3 \quad \text{(standard reference)}$$

$$\rho_{oil} = SG \times \rho_{water} = 0.88 \times 1000 = 880 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Convert the Discharge to SI Units (m³/s)**

$$Q = 80 \text{ L/min} = \frac{80 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 1.3333 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Water (Hydraulic) Power**

The water power is the useful energy imparted to the fluid per unit time:

$$P_{water} = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{1000}$$

$$P_{water} = \frac{880 \times 9.81 \times 1.3333 \times 10^{-3} \times 8}{1000}$$

Let us evaluate the numerator step by step:

$$880 \times 9.81 = 8632.8$$

$$8632.8 \times 1.3333 \times 10^{-3} = 11.5104$$

$$11.5104 \times 8 = 92.0832$$

$$P_{water} = \frac{92.0832}{1000} = 0.09208 \text{ kW} \approx 92.08 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the Shaft Power Input to the Pump**

The shaft power accounts for all losses within the pump (hydraulic, volumetric, and mechanical):

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{water}}{\eta_{overall}}$$

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{0.09208}{0.55} = 0.1674 \text{ kW} \approx 167.4 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the Required Motor Nameplate Power**

The motor must overcome the pump's shaft power demand plus its own internal losses:

$$P_{motor} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{motor}}$$

$$P_{motor} = \frac{0.1674}{0.85} = 0.1969 \text{ kW} \approx 196.9 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6 — Apply a Standard Safety Factor for Real-World Selection**

Industrial practice dictates selecting the next standard motor size **above** the calculated value. A safety margin of **20–25%** is added:

$$P_{motor,design} = P_{motor} \times 1.25 = 0.1969 \times 1.25 = 0.2462 \text{ kW}$$

The next standard fractional kW motor size is **0.25 kW (or 1/3 HP)**.

**Final Result Summary:**
- $P_{water} = 0.0921 \text{ kW} = 92.1 \text{ W}$
- $P_{shaft} = 0.1674 \text{ kW} = 167.4 \text{ W}$
- $P_{motor} = 0.1969 \text{ kW} \approx 0.20 \text{ kW}$
- **Selected standard motor: 0.25 kW (1/3 HP)**

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Head Developed by a Centrifugal-Type Drum Pump Using Euler's Equation

**Problem Statement:** A centrifugal-type drum pump has an impeller with inlet diameter $D_1 = 40 \text{ mm}$ and outlet diameter $D_2 = 100 \text{ mm}$. The impeller rotates at $N = 2900 \text{ rpm}$. The widths at inlet and outlet are $b_1 = 8 \text{ mm}$ and $b_2 = 6 \text{ mm}$. The flow is **radial at exit** (i.e., $\alpha_2 = 90°$, so the whirl component at exit is $V_{w2} = V_{r2}$). Assuming **no pre-whirl** at inlet ($V_{w1} = 0$) and no slip, determine the theoretical head developed.

**Step 1 — Apply Euler's Turbomachinery Equation**

$$H_{th} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}$$

Since $V_{w1} = 0$ (no pre-whirl), the equation simplifies to:

$$H_{th} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Blade (Peripheral) Speed at the Outlet**

$$u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.100 \times 2900}{60}$$

$$u_2 = \frac{3.1416 \times 0.100 \times 2900}{60} = \frac{910.99}{60} = 15.183 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Continuity Equation to Find the Radial (Meridional) Velocity at the Outlet**

The radial velocity is related to the flow rate by continuity. The pump is rated at $Q = 200 \text{ L/min}$ for this design.

Convert discharge to m³/s:

$$Q = 200 \text{ L/min} = \frac{200 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 3.3333 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

The flow area at the impeller outlet is:

$$A_2 = \pi D_2 b_2 = \pi \times 0.100 \times 0.006 = 1.8850 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Therefore, the radial velocity at the outlet is:

$$V_{r2} = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{3.3333 \times 10^{-3}}{1.8850 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.7683 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Use the Radial Exit Condition**

Since $\alpha_2 = 90°$ (radial exit), the relative velocity is entirely radial, which means the tangential component of absolute velocity equals the blade speed minus the relative velocity's tangential projection. In this special case:

$$V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2} \cdot \tan\beta_2$$

However, the problem specifies the **radial exit condition** which by the velocity triangle gives:

$$V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2} \cdot \cot\beta_2$$

For the simplest "no slip" radial exit configuration (where the relative velocity leaves along the blade), and using the design simplification for radial exit:

$$V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Values**

$$V_{w2} = 15.183 - 1.7683 = 13.415 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Theoretical Head**

$$H_{th} = \frac{V_{w2} \cdot u_2}{g} = \frac{13.415 \times 15.183}{9.81}$$

$$H_{th} = \frac{203.66}{9.81} = 20.76 \text{ m}$$

**Final Result Summary:**
- $H_{theoretical} \approx 20.76 \text{ m}$ of fluid column

This represents the **ideal head** developed by the impeller. In practice, hydraulic, mechanical, and volumetric losses reduce this to the actual head, typically **60–80%** of the theoretical value.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance

| Parameter | Manual Drum Pump | Electric Drum Pump | Pneumatic Drum Pump |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Drive Source | Human effort | AC/DC motor | Compressed air |
| Max Flow Rate | 10 – 25 L/min | 50 – 250 L/min | 30 – 200 L/min |
| Max Head | 5 – 8 m | 15 – 30 m | 20 – 40 m |
| Viscosity Limit | ~500 cP | ~1500 cP | ~2000 cP |
| Power Source Dependency | None | Electricity | Air compressor |
| Explosion-Proof Suitability | Excellent | Limited (ATEX needed) | Excellent |
| Typical Efficiency | 30 – 50% | 50 – 70% | 40 – 60% |
| Cost (Relative) | Low | Medium | High |
| Best Use Case | Field/remote sites | Workshop/indoor | Hazardous/explosive areas |
