## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions in KTU 2024 scheme ESE (End Semester Examination) carry **3 marks** and require precise, definition-oriented or short-derivation answers of **40–60 words** with supporting diagrams where relevant.

---

**Question 1:** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper Style)]**

Define a **drum pump**. List any **four** typical industrial applications of drum pumps.

**Model Answer:**

A drum pump is a **portable, self-priming pumping device** designed to transfer fluids from standardized containers such as 200 L drums, barrels, and IBC totes. The pump tube is inserted into the container, and a drive mechanism (electric, pneumatic, or manual) energizes a submerged pumping element that lifts the fluid and discharges it through a nozzle.

**Four typical industrial applications:**
1. Transfer of lubricating oils and coolants in automotive workshops
2. Extraction of diesel and kerosene from storage drums in petrochemical industries
3. Sterile transfer of solvents and alcohols in pharmaceutical manufacturing
4. Dispensing of edible oils and syrups in food processing plants

**[Marks Distribution: Definition 1.5 + Applications 1 × 4 × 0.375 = 1.5 → Total 3 marks]**

---

**Question 2:** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper Style)]**

Explain why a drum pump is called a **"self-priming" pump**. How does this differ from a dry-mounted centrifugal pump?

**Model Answer:**

A drum pump is called **self-priming** because its pumping element (impeller, rotor, or piston) is **physically immersed in the fluid** inside the drum. Since the suction passage is already filled with liquid at the moment of start-up, no air-cavity formation occurs, and the pump begins delivering flow from the **very first revolution** without any external priming step.

In contrast, a **dry-mounted centrifugal pump** has its impeller in air, requiring the suction casing to be manually filled with fluid (priming) before operation. Failure to prime causes the pump to run dry, overheat, and suffer cavitation damage.

**[Marks Distribution: Self-priming explanation 1.5 + Comparison 1.5 = Total 3 marks]**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** with **internal choice** (i.e., students answer EITHER OR from the two alternatives). Each question has two sub-parts, typically **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**. Solutions must be **step-by-step with proper units and clear logic**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working principle of a positive-displacement rotary drum pump**. Discuss the role of the mechanical seal. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A positive-displacement drum pump has a displacement of $V_d = 75 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$ and operates at $N = 1440 \text{ rpm}$. If the volumetric efficiency is $\eta_{vol} = 0.90$ and the pump transfers oil of specific gravity 0.86 to a height of $H = 10 \text{ m}$ through a 15 mm nozzle, calculate: (i) the actual discharge in L/min, (ii) the discharge velocity, and (iii) the water power in watts. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Construction and Working [7 Marks]:**

A **positive-displacement rotary drum pump** consists of the following constructional elements:

1. **Drive Motor:** A 0.25–1.5 kW electric motor (or pneumatic motor) provides the rotational input. [1 Mark]
2. **Coupling Shaft:** A long, rigid shaft transmits torque from the motor down through the suction tube to the pumping element. [1 Mark]
3. **Suction Tube:** A stainless-steel or polypropylene tube of length 700–1200 mm and diameter 28–42 mm. It houses the shaft and provides the fluid flow path. [1 Mark]
4. **Pumping Element:** A rotor (eccentric screw, gear, or vane type) at the bottom of the tube that creates sealed moving cavities which transfer fluid upward. [1 Mark]
5. **Mechanical Seal:** A face-type seal (typically SiC vs. carbon with Viton or PTFE secondary seals) mounted at the top of the pump tube. It prevents fluid from leaking up the shaft and prevents atmospheric contamination of the fluid. [1.5 Marks]
6. **Discharge Assembly:** An outlet nozzle with a trigger valve and hose barb. [0.5 Marks]

**Working Principle:**
The motor rotates the rotor inside the stator. As the rotor turns, it creates **expanding cavities on the inlet side** (low pressure) and **contracting cavities on the discharge side** (high pressure). Atmospheric pressure acting on the free surface of the liquid in the drum forces fluid up into the expanding cavities. The fluid is then **mechanically carried upward** by the rotor's rotation and discharged through the nozzle at high pressure. [1 Mark]

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given Data:**
- $V_d = 75 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev} = 75 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}$
- $N = 1440 \text{ rpm} = 24 \text{ rev/s}$
- $\eta_{vol} = 0.90$
- $SG = 0.86$, so $\rho = 860 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $H = 10 \text{ m}$
- $D_{nozzle} = 15 \text{ mm} = 0.015 \text{ m}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**(i) Actual Discharge in L/min:**

$$Q_{theoretical} = N \times V_d = 24 \times 75 \times 10^{-6} = 1.80 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to L/min:

$$Q_{theoretical} = 1.80 \times 10^{-3} \times 60 \times 1000 = 108 \text{ L/min}$$

Apply volumetric efficiency:

$$Q_{actual} = 108 \times 0.90 = 97.2 \text{ L/min}$$

**[2 Marks]**

**(ii) Discharge Velocity:**

$$Q_{actual} \text{ in m}^3/\text{s} = \frac{97.2 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 1.62 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$A_{nozzle} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.015)^2 = 1.7671 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$v = \frac{Q_{actual}}{A_{nozzle}} = \frac{1.62 \times 10^{-3}}{1.7671 \times 10^{-4}} = 9.167 \text{ m/s}$$

**[2 Marks]**

**(iii) Water Power in Watts:**

$$P_{water} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$$

$$P_{water} = 860 \times 9.81 \times 1.62 \times 10^{-3} \times 10$$

Step-wise evaluation:
- $860 \times 9.81 = 8436.6$
- $8436.6 \times 1.62 \times 10^{-3} = 13.6673$
- $13.6673 \times 10 = 136.673 \text{ W}$

$$P_{water} \approx 136.67 \text{ W} = 0.1367 \text{ kW}$$

**[3 Marks]**

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Actual Discharge | 97.2 L/min |
| Discharge Velocity | 9.17 m/s |
| Water Power | 136.67 W |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Compare **centrifugal-type** and **positive-displacement-type** drum pumps in terms of working principle, viscosity handling, discharge characteristics, and typical applications. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An electric motor-driven drum pump delivers water at $Q = 120 \text{ L/min}$ against a head of $H = 12 \text{ m}$. The overall efficiency of the pump is $\eta_{overall} = 0.60$ and the motor efficiency is $\eta_{motor} = 0.88$. Calculate: (i) the water power, (ii) the shaft power, and (iii) the electric power consumed by the motor. Also determine the most economical standard motor rating in HP. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Comparative Analysis [7 Marks]:**

| Parameter | Centrifugal-Type Drum Pump | Positive-Displacement Drum Pump |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Working Principle** | Uses a spinning impeller to impart kinetic energy to the fluid, which is then converted to pressure energy in the volute. | Uses a rotor/stator or piston to mechanically trap and displace fixed volumes of fluid per cycle. |
| **Viscosity Handling** | Poor for high-viscosity fluids; performance drops sharply beyond 100 cP. | Excellent for high-viscosity fluids; handles up to 1500–2000 cP with minimal efficiency loss. |
| **Discharge Characteristic** | Discharge **decreases** as head **increases** (sensitive to pressure variations). | Discharge is **nearly constant** regardless of head (within the pump's pressure limit). |
| **Self-Priming** | Yes (since impeller is immersed). | Yes (since rotor is immersed). |
| **Typical Applications** | Low-viscosity fluids: water, diesel, light solvents, coolants. | High-viscosity fluids: heavy oils, grease, adhesives, resins, chemical slurries. |
| **Flow Pulsation** | Smooth, continuous flow. | Pulsating flow (in piston types) or slightly rippled (in rotary types). |
| **Pressure Capability** | Limited head (5–20 m typical). | Higher head capability (20–40 m typical). |

**[7 marks — 1 mark per row with at least 6 well-explained rows + bonus 0.5 mark for concluding remark]**

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given Data:**
- $Q = 120 \text{ L/min} = \frac{120 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $H = 12 \text{ m}$
- $\eta_{overall} = 0.60$
- $\eta_{motor} = 0.88$
- $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**(i) Water Power:**

$$P_{water} = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{1000}$$

$$P_{water} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 12}{1000}$$

Step-wise:
- $1000 \times 9.81 = 9810$
- $9810 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} = 19.62$
- $19.62 \times 12 = 235.44 \text{ W} = 0.23544 \text{ kW}$

$$P_{water} = 235.44 \text{ W} = 0.2354 \text{ kW}$$

**[2 Marks]**

**(ii) Shaft Power:**

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{water}}{\eta_{overall}} = \frac{0.2354}{0.60} = 0.3924 \text{ kW} = 392.4 \text{ W}$$

**[2 Marks]**

**(iii) Electric Power Consumed by Motor:**

$$P_{electric} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{motor}} = \frac{0.3924}{0.88} = 0.4459 \text{ kW} = 445.9 \text{ W}$$

**[2 Marks]**

**Economical Standard Motor Rating in HP:**

$$P_{shaft} = 0.3924 \text{ kW} \times \frac{1 \text{ HP}}{0.7457 \text{ kW}} = 0.5262 \text{ HP}$$

Applying a **25% safety factor:**

$$P_{design} = 0.5262 \times 1.25 = 0.6578 \text{ HP}$$

The next standard fractional HP motor size is **0.75 HP (or 0.5–0.55 kW)**. Selecting a 0.75 HP motor provides adequate margin for transient loads and voltage fluctuations. **[1 Mark]**

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Water Power | 235.44 W |
| Shaft Power | 392.4 W |
| Electric Power | 445.9 W |
| Selected Motor | 0.75 HP (≈ 0.56 kW) |

---

### 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Drum Pump Problems (KTU Board Examiner Observations):**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (Most Frequent Deduction: 1–2 marks):** Students frequently fail to convert **cm³ to m³** (factor: 10⁻⁶) or **L/min to m³/s** (factor: 1/60,000). Always write the conversion factor explicitly. Examiners WILL deduct 1 mark for every missing conversion factor in a multi-step problem.
>
> 2. **Confusing Density with Specific Gravity:** SG is dimensionless, but ρ is in kg/m³. Mixing them up leads to **wrong numerical answers by an order of magnitude** — a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Volumetric Efficiency:** Many students compute the theoretical discharge but forget to multiply by η_vol. The examiner will deduct **0.5–1 mark** if the actual discharge is not computed.
>
> 4. **Not Selecting the Next Standard Motor Size:** A 0.5 kW calculated requirement means you select a **0.55 kW or 0.75 kW** standard motor, not 0.5 kW exactly. Industrial practice requires the safety margin. Failure to do so = **0.5 mark deduction**.
>
> 5. **Missing the "Why" in Conceptual Questions:** A 3-mark question on "why is a drum pump self-priming" expects the keyword **"physically immersed pumping element"**. Vague answers like "it can start easily" will receive only **1 of 3 marks**.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the Mechanical Seal's Role:** In construction-based questions, students often list the seal as just a "part" but do not explain its **dual function** (fluid containment + contamination prevention). Examiners allocate **1.5 marks** specifically to the seal's role.
>
> 7. **No Diagram in 7-Mark Sub-Part:** A 7-mark sub-part on "construction and working" without a labelled block diagram will be capped at **5 of 7 marks maximum**, regardless of textual content.

---

### 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- **Definition:** Drum pump = portable, immersed, self-priming pump for standardized containers (200 L typical).
- **Self-Priming:** Achieved because the pumping element is **physically immersed** in the fluid.
- **Primary Drive Types:** Manual (crank), Electric (AC/DC), Pneumatic (air motor), Battery.
- **Pumping Principles:** Centrifugal (low viscosity) OR Positive-Displacement (high viscosity).
- **Key Constructional Parts:** Motor → Coupling → Shaft → Mechanical Seal → Suction Tube → Rotor/Impeller → Discharge Nozzle.
- **Discharge Equation:** $Q_{actual} = N \times V_d \times \eta_{vol}$ (for PD type).
- **Velocity at Nozzle:** $v = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$.
- **Water Power:** $P_{water} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{1000}$ in kW.
- **Shaft Power:** $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{water}}{\eta_{overall}}$.
- **Motor Power:** $P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{motor}}$.
- **Euler's Head (Centrifugal type):** $H_{th} = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}$.
- **Standard Drum Capacity:** 200 L (industrial convention).
- **Standard Gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.
- **Standard Water Density:** $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
- **Unit Conversions to Memorize:** 1 HP = 0.7457 kW; 1 L/min = 1/60,000 m³/s; 1 cm³ = 10⁻⁶ m³; 1 bar = 10.2 m of water column.
- **PD Type vs Centrifugal Type:** PD → constant flow, handles viscosity; Centrifugal → variable flow, viscosity-sensitive.
- **Efficiency Hierarchy:** $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{hyd}$.
- **Application Domains:** Oils, fuels, chemicals, solvents, food-grade liquids, pharmaceuticals, inks.
- **Mechanical Seal Function:** Dual role — (1) prevent fluid leakage up shaft, (2) prevent external contamination of the fluid.
- **Exam Tip:** Always draw a labelled block diagram for 7-mark "construction" questions — it secures **at least 2 marks** even if textual content is weak.
- **Numerical Tip:** Show every unit conversion as a separate step; KTU examiners reward clarity and deduct heavily for skipped conversions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Submersible Pump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Submersible Pump # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Submersible Pump**: A **submersible pump** is a hermetically sealed, multi-stage **centrifugal pumping machine** in which the complete pumping assembly — including the **electric motor**, **impellers**, and **diffusers** — is fully immersed in the fluid (typically water) being pumped. The motor and pump form a single integrated unit, with the pump operating below the free liquid surface and discharging the fluid vertically upward through a riser pipe. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCIET302 – Module 4), submersible pumps belong to the family of **rotodynamic (turbo) machines** and are specifically classified as **vertical-shaft, single/multi-stage, radial-flow centrifugal pumps** designed for sub-surface installation. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction from a Normal Centrifugal Pump**: A conventional centrifugal pump sits *outside* the sump and fluid is drawn in via suction. A submersible pump sits *inside* the fluid and pushes it upward. This eliminates the **cavitation problem** associated with high suction lifts (since NPSH is naturally satisfied by submergence). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Underwater Blender" Intuition Imagine you need to lift water from the bottom of a deep swimming pool to the surface. If you keep a blender (electric motor with rotating blades) at the surface and try to *suck* the water up, you will fail — atmospheric pressure (~10.3 m of water) limits how much you can lift. **Solution?** Drop the entire blender (motor + spinning blades = impeller) **inside the pool**, near the bottom, and let it **push** the water up through a hose. The motor is waterproof, the blades spin and fling water outward (centrifugal force), and the water rises through the pipe. That underwater blender **is** a submersible pump. - **Motor** → Watertight electric motor (sealed with O-rings, oil chamber, or water-filled) - **Blades** → Multi-stage impellers - **Hose** → Riser pipe (delivery pipe) - **Pool bottom** → Borewell / openwell / sump ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Reference Values | Parameter | Typical Value | Units | |---|---|---| | Submergence depth | **50 – 400 m** | m | | Specific speed ($N_s$) range | **300 – 600 (metric)** | rpm | | Pump efficiency (overall) | **65 – 85 %** | – | | Power rating | **0.37 kW – 1500 kW** | kW | | Operating frequency | **50 Hz / 60 Hz** | Hz | | Synchronous speed (2-pole) | **3000 rpm** (at 50 Hz) | rpm | --- # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Constructional Architecture of a Submersible Pump A submersible pump is an integrated **motor-pump stack** that hangs vertically inside the well. From bottom to top: 1. **Suction Strainer / Screen** — Prevents debris and stones from entering the impeller. Sets the minimum submergence. 2. **Suction Casing / Inlet** — Guides water axially into the first-stage impeller. 3. **Multi-Stage Impellers (Radial / Mixed Flow)** — Usually **6 to 30 stages** stacked on a common shaft. Each impeller adds a finite head. The total head is the sum of all stages. $$H_{total} = H_{stage,1} + H_{stage,2} + H_{stage,3} + \dots + H_{stage,n} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} H_{stage,i}$$ 4. **Intermediate Diffusers / Bowls** — Convert the high-velocity kinetic energy from the impeller into pressure energy (static head) using the diffuser's expanding cross-section. 5. **Thrust Bearing Assembly** — Carries the weight of the rotating assembly (shaft + impellers + motor rotor) plus the hydraulic axial thrust. Often a Kingsbury-type tilting-pad bearing. 6. **Water-Filled / Oil-Filled Induction Motor** — Submersible motors are typically squirrel-cage, 3-phase induction type. Stator windings are insulated with water-proof varnish and sealed inside a stainless-steel shell. 7. **Cable Entry / Sealing Chamber** — A double-mechanical seal (silicon-carbide faces) prevents water ingress into the motor. 8. **Check (Non-Return) Valve** — Prevents backflow when the pump stops, protecting against water hammer. 9. **Discharge Head / Delivery Bend** — Mounted at the well top, connects the riser pipe to the surface delivery line. ## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Flow - **Step 1 — Energization:** When 3-phase AC supply is given to the stator, a rotating magnetic field is produced. - **Step 2 — Rotation:** The rotor (caged in motor) rotates, dragging the common vertical shaft with all the impellers mounted on it. Speed is typically **N ≈ 2900 rpm** (2-pole, 50 Hz). - **Step 3 — Impeller Entry:** Water from the well/sump enters the **eye of the first impeller** axially through the strainer. - **Step 4 — Centrifugal Acceleration:** The spinning impeller imparts kinetic energy to water. Water exits the impeller tip with absolute velocity $V_2$ having a tangential whirl component $V_{w2}$ and radial component $V_{f2}$. - **Step 5 — Diffuser Energy Conversion:** In the diffuser (stationary casing ring), the kinetic energy is **decelerated and converted into pressure energy** (Bernoulli's principle in stationary frame). - **Step 6 — Multi-Stage Staging:** Water moves into the next impeller (mounted directly above), which adds more energy. After n stages, the total head becomes **n times** the head per stage. - **Step 7 — Discharge:** Water finally exits through the top check valve and flows up the riser pipe to the surface. > [!NOTE] > **Why Multi-Stage?** A single-stage pump can develop only ~10–15 m of head (limited by impeller tip speed). For a 100 m deep well, you would need **8–12 stages** in series. This is the fundamental design trade-off of submersible pumps. ## 2.3 Euler's Head Equation — Applied to a Submersible Pump Stage The work done by the impeller per unit weight of water (Euler head) is: $$H_{euler} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}$$ For a **radial entry** (no pre-whirl), $V_{w1} = 0$, so: $$H_{euler} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$$ For backward-curved vanes (typical for submersibles), $V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{f2} \cot \beta_2$, where $\beta_2$ is the vane exit angle. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table contains every formula you must memorize for the KTU ESE on this topic.** Pay close attention to the units column. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $H_{euler} = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}$ | Euler head per stage | m | | 2 | $H_m = H_{euler} \times n$ | Total manometric head (n stages) | m | | 3 | $P = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_m}{1000}$ | Water Power (shaft input to water) | kW | | 4 | $\eta_{man} = \dfrac{g H_m}{V_{bw2} \cdot u_2}$ | Manometric efficiency | – (decimal) | | 5 | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | Volumetric efficiency | – | | 6 | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{\text{Impeller power}}{\text{Shaft power}}$ | Mechanical efficiency | – | | 7 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{man} \times \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech}$ | Overall efficiency | – | | 8 | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | Specific speed (metric, P in kW, H in m) | rpm | | 9 | $H \propto N^2$ | Affinity law — head | m | | 10 | $Q \propto N$ | Affinity law — discharge | m³/s | | 11 | $P \propto N^3$ | Affinity law — power | kW | | 12 | $H_{total} = H_{static} + h_f + \dfrac{V_{del}^2}{2g}$ | Total system head | m | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility**: Submersible pumps are the workhorse of **agricultural irrigation in Kerala** (Kasaragod, Palakkad), **domestic water supply** in apartment bore-wells, **mine dewatering**, **fountain installations**, and **drainage in metro projects**. The multi-stage stacked architecture is the design template that enables India's deep-tube-well revolution (Mark II handpumps were later upgraded to submersible versions under the Jal Jeevan Mission). --- # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Euler's Head Equation for a Pump Impeller We derive the work transferred to the fluid per unit weight by the impeller using a control-volume and angular-momentum approach. ### Assumptions - Steady, incompressible flow - Uniform velocities at impeller inlet (1) and outlet (2) - Flow is axisymmetric - No pre-whirl at inlet ($V_{w1} = 0$ for radial entry) ### Derivation Steps **Step 1 — Apply the angular momentum theorem to a control volume enclosing the impeller.** The torque $T$ exerted by the impeller on the fluid equals the rate of change of angular momentum: $$T = \dot{m} (V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1)$$ where $\dot{m} = \rho Q$ is the mass flow rate, $r_1$ and $r_2$ are the impeller inlet and outlet radii. **Step 2 — Express the blade (tangential) speeds.** The impeller rotates at angular velocity $\omega$, so: $$u_1 = \omega r_1 \quad \text{and} \quad u_2 = \omega r_2$$ **Step 3 — Substitute $r_1$ and $r_2$ in terms of blade speeds.** $$T = \dot{m} \left( \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{\omega} - \dfrac{V_{w1} u_1}{\omega} \right) = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\omega} (V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1)$$ **Step 4 — Work done per second (Power transferred to fluid).** $$P_{fluid} = T \omega = \dot{m} (V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1)$$ **Step 5 — Work done per unit weight of fluid (Head).** Divide by weight flow rate $\dot{W} = \dot{m} g$: $$H_{euler} = \dfrac{P_{fluid}}{\dot{m} g} = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}$$ This is the celebrated **Euler Turbomachine Equation** for pumps. **Step 6 — Simplify for radial entry (no pre-whirl).** Setting $V_{w1} = 0$: $$H_{euler} = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$$ **Step 7 — For multi-stage submersible pump with $n$ identical stages:** $$H_{total,manometric} = n \cdot \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Pattern) > **Problem**: A multi-stage submersible pump delivers **0.05 m³/s** of water against a total head of **120 m**. The pump runs at **1450 rpm** with an overall efficiency of **75%**. Calculate: > (a) The shaft power input > (b) The power transferred to water > (c) The manometric efficiency if $V_{w2} = 25$ m/s and $u_2 = 30$ m/s > (d) Number of stages, given that each stage develops a manometric head of 12 m. ### Solution **Part (a) — Shaft Power Input:** $$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_{total}}{\eta_{overall}}$$ $$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 120}{0.75}$$ $$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{58{,}860}{0.75}$$ $$\boxed{P_{shaft} = 78{,}480 \text{ W} = 78.48 \text{ kW}}$$ **Part (b) — Power Transferred to Water (Water Power):** $$P_{water} = \rho g Q H_{total} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 120$$ $$\boxed{P_{water} = 58{,}860 \text{ W} = 58.86 \text{ kW}}$$ **Part (c) — Manometric Efficiency:** Euler head per stage: $$H_{euler} = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g} = \dfrac{25 \times 30}{9.81} = 76.45 \text{ m}$$ This represents the energy transferred per stage. The manometric head developed per stage uses this Euler head corrected by the manometric efficiency. Manometric efficiency is defined as the ratio of manometric head to Euler head: $$\eta_{man} = \dfrac{H_{m,stage}}{H_{euler,stage}} = \dfrac{12}{76.45} = 0.157$$ Wait — this gives an unrealistic value, indicating the given blade speeds correspond to design conditions. For a properly designed pump, $\eta_{man} = 0.85 - 0.95$. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall**: Do not blindly plug $H_{euler}$ into the $\eta_{man}$ formula without verifying that the given $V_{w2}$ and $u_2$ correspond to the *actual* operating point. In KTU problems, the given manometric head (12 m) is the **true** $H_m$ per stage. Use it directly with the actual discharge. **Part (d) — Number of Stages:** $$n = \dfrac{H_{total}}{H_{m,per stage}} = \dfrac{120}{12}$$ $$\boxed{n = 10 \text{ stages}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Choose a Submersible Pump | Application Scenario | Recommended Pump Type | Reason | |---|---|---| | Depth > 8 m, clean water | **Submersible (borewell)** | Avoids suction cavitation | | Depth < 7 m, clean water | Mono-block centrifugal | Easier maintenance | | Muddy / sewage water | **Submersible (dewatering type)** | Sealed motor, vortex impeller | | High discharge, low head | Axial/mixed flow | Higher $N_s$ is suitable | | Low discharge, high head | **Submersible (multi-stage radial)** | Exactly our topic | --- # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Component Block Diagram (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[3-Phase AC Power Supply] --> B[Cable Entry Seal
Double Mechanical Seal] B --> C[Submersible Induction Motor
Water-Filled Stator] C --> D[Common Vertical Shaft
Rotates at 2900 rpm] D --> E[Stage 1 Impeller
Radial Backward Curved] E --> F[Stage 1 Diffuser
Converts KE to Pressure] F --> G[Stage 2 Impeller] G --> H[Stage 2 Diffuser] H --> I[... n Stages ...] I --> J[Top Bowl with Check Valve] J --> K[Riser Pipe to Surface] K --> L[Discharge Head and Delivery Tank] M[Suction Strainer] --> E N[Thrust Bearing
Kingsbury Type] --> D O[Sand Surrounding Borewell] --> M ``` **Reading the diagram**: - The **left vertical chain** (A → B → C → D → E → F → ... → K → L) is the **flow of energy + fluid** from the surface power source down to the impeller assembly and back up to delivery. - **M, N, O** are auxiliary support subsystems (suction, mechanical, environmental). ## 4.2 Velocity Triangle at Impeller Outlet (Schematic Description) At the impeller exit, three velocity components interact. Below is a description that complements a Mermaid block. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Absolute Velocity V2
at impeller exit] --> B[Whirl Component Vw2
tangent to impeller] A --> C[Flow Component Vf2
radial direction] D[Blade Velocity u2
tangential to impeller] --> E[Relative Velocity Vr2] B --> E C --> E D --> E ``` **Key geometric relation** (from velocity triangle): $$V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{f2} \cot \beta_2$$ where $\beta_2$ is the vane outlet angle measured from the tangential direction. ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ElectricalSubsystem["ELECTRICAL INPUT SUBSYSTEM"] S1[3-Phase 415V Supply] S2[Starter Panel DOL Star Delta] S3[Submersible Cable V4 Grade] S1 --> S2 --> S3 end subgraph MotorSubsystem["SEALED MOTOR SUBSYSTEM"] M1[Stator Windings] M2[Rotor Squirrel Cage] M3[Thrust Bearing Assembly] M4[Mechanical Seal Pair] M1 --> M2 M2 --> M3 M4 --> M1 end subgraph HydraulicSubsystem["MULTI-STAGE HYDRAULIC SUBSYSTEM"] H1[Suction Strainer] H2[Stage 1 Impeller and Diffuser] H3[Stage 2 Impeller and Diffuser] H4[Stage n Impeller and Diffuser] H5[Check Valve] H1 --> H2 --> H3 --> H4 --> H5 end subgraph OutputSubsystem["DELIVERY SUBSYSTEM"] O1[Riser Pipe Column] O2[Surface Discharge Head] O3[Consumer Storage Tank] O1 --> O2 --> O3 end S3 --> M1 M3 --> H2 H5 --> O1 ``` --- # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define a submersible pump and state any two advantages over a conventional centrifugal pump.** **Model Answer** (3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for advantages): A submersible pump is a **multi-stage centrifugal pump** in which the **pump and driving motor** are housed in a single, hermetically sealed unit and operate **fully submerged** in the fluid to be pumped. **Two advantages over a conventional centrifugal pump:** 1. **No priming required** — because the pump casing is always filled with liquid, the suction line is never empty. 2. **No cavitation risk** — submergence provides positive NPSH naturally, so high suction lifts do not cause vapor bubble formation. 3. *(Bonus 3rd point)* **Footprint / space saving** — no need for a separate pump house. > [!NOTE] > Any two advantages carry 1 mark each; the definition carries 1 mark. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional II]** **Why are submersible pumps designed as multi-stage machines? What limits the head developed by a single stage?** **Model Answer** (3 marks): A submersible pump is designed **multi-stage** because a single impeller stage can develop only a limited head (typically 10–15 m) restricted by the maximum permissible **blade tip speed** $u_2 = \pi D_2 N / 60$. To overcome high static heads (e.g., 100 m deep wells), multiple impeller-diffuser stages are stacked on a common shaft, and their heads add algebraically: $H_{total} = n \cdot H_{stage}$. The head per stage is limited by: - **Material strength** of the impeller (centrifugal stress $\propto u_2^2$) - **Cavitation** if $u_2$ is too high relative to NPSH - **Mechanical losses** that grow with rotational speed. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice) > **Module 4 Question Paper Format** (per KTU 2024 ESE pattern): Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. Internal choice is provided. --- ### **Question A — 14 Marks (OR) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4, Set A]** > **(a)** A multi-stage submersible pump runs at **1450 rpm** and delivers **0.04 m³/s** of water against a head of **90 m**. The impeller has an outer diameter of **0.30 m**, outlet width **0.025 m**, and vane exit angle $\beta_2 = 30°$. The manometric efficiency is **88%**. Determine: > 1. The manometric head per stage. > 2. The number of stages. > 3. The blade velocity $u_2$. > **(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the **construction and working of a submersible pump**. List four specific applications. ### Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Given:** - $N = 1450$ rpm - $Q = 0.04$ m³/s - $H_{total} = 90$ m - $D_2 = 0.30$ m, $B_2 = 0.025$ m, $\beta_2 = 30°$, $\eta_{man} = 0.88$ **Step 1 — Blade velocity at outlet:** $$u_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.30 \times 1450}{60}$$ $$u_2 = \dfrac{1366.55}{60} = 22.78 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Radial (flow) velocity at outlet:** Assume no axial pre-swirl, flow area at outlet $\approx \pi D_2 B_2$: $$V_{f2} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi D_2 B_2} = \dfrac{0.04}{\pi \times 0.30 \times 0.025}$$ $$V_{f2} = \dfrac{0.04}{0.02356} = 1.698 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 3 — Whirl component from velocity triangle:** $$V_{w2} = u_2 - \dfrac{V_{f2}}{\tan \beta_2} = 22.78 - \dfrac{1.698}{\tan 30°}$$ $$V_{w2} = 22.78 - \dfrac{1.698}{0.5774} = 22.78 - 2.941 = 19.84 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 4 — Euler head per stage (no pre-whirl):** $$H_{euler,stage} = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g} = \dfrac{19.84 \times 22.78}{9.81} = 46.08 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5 — Manometric head per stage:** $$H_{m,stage} = \eta_{man} \times H_{euler,stage} = 0.88 \times 46.08 = 40.55 \text{ m}$$ **Step 6 — Number of stages:** $$n = \dfrac{H_{total}}{H_{m,stage}} = \dfrac{90}{40.55} = 2.22$$ Since $n$ must be an integer and we need $H_{total} \geq 90$ m, round up: $$\boxed{n = 3 \text{ stages}}$$ Actual head per stage = 90/3 = **30 m** (slight over-design is acceptable). #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Construction (4 marks):** A submersible pump consists of a **sealed electric motor** (water-cooled, squirrel-cage induction type) directly coupled to a **vertical multi-stage centrifugal pump assembly**. From bottom to top: (1) **suction strainer**, (2) **multi-stage impellers and diffusers** mounted on a common shaft, (3) **thrust bearing**, (4) **sealed motor unit**, (5) **cable entry**, (6) **discharge head with check valve** at the top. **Working (2 marks):** When energized, the motor rotates the shaft and impellers. Water enters the strainer, passes through successive impeller-diffuser stages (each adding head), and is discharged up the riser pipe. The check valve prevents backflow. **Four applications (1 mark):** 1. **Borewell water supply** for domestic/agricultural use 2. **Mine dewatering** 3. **Sewage and stormwater pumping stations** 4. **Industrial process water circulation** --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Internal Choice) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, Set B]** > **(a)** With the help of a velocity triangle, derive the **Euler head equation** for a centrifugal pump impeller. State clearly all assumptions. > **(b)** A submersible pump with **12 stages** operates at **2900 rpm** and delivers **900 L/min** against a head of **150 m**. The overall efficiency is **70%**. Calculate: > 1. The input power to the motor. > 2. The manometric head per stage. > 3. The specific speed of the pump (in metric units). ### Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — 7 Marks Refer to the **full derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes. Summarize the key steps in your answer: 1. Apply **angular momentum theorem** to fluid in the impeller: $T = \dot{m}(V_{w2}r_2 - V_{w1}r_1)$. 2. Relate $r_2, r_1$ to blade speeds $u_2, u_1$ using $u = \omega r$. 3. Power = $T\omega = \dot{m}(V_{w2}u_2 - V_{w1}u_1)$. 4. Head (work per unit weight) = Power / ($\dot{m} g$): $$\boxed{H_e = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}}$$ 5. **Assumptions**: steady, incompressible, axisymmetric flow; uniform velocities at inlet/outlet; radial entry gives $V_{w1} = 0$. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** - $n = 12$ stages - $N = 2900$ rpm - $Q = 900$ L/min = 900/60000 = 0.015 m³/s - $H = 150$ m - $\eta_{overall} = 0.70$ **Step 1 — Input Power:** $$P_{input} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{overall}} = \dfrac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 \times 150}{0.70}$$ $$P_{input} = \dfrac{22{,}072.5}{0.70}$$ $$\boxed{P_{input} = 31{,}532 \text{ W} \approx 31.53 \text{ kW}}$$ **Step 2 — Manometric Head per Stage:** $$H_{m,stage} = \dfrac{H_{total}}{n} = \dfrac{150}{12}$$ $$\boxed{H_{m,stage} = 12.5 \text{ m}}$$ **Step 3 — Specific Speed (metric):** $$N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$$ where $P$ is in **kW** and $H$ in m: $$N_s = \dfrac{2900 \times \sqrt{31.53}}{(150)^{1.25}}$$ Computing piece by piece: - $\sqrt{31.53} = 5.615$ - $150^{1.25} = 150 \times 150^{0.25} = 150 \times 3.5006 = 525.09$ $$N_s = \dfrac{2900 \times 5.615}{525.09} = \dfrac{16{,}283.5}{525.09}$$ $$\boxed{N_s \approx 31 \text{ rpm (metric)}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This low $N_s$ (around 30) is consistent with a **radial-flow, high-head, low-discharge** pump — exactly the design space of multi-stage submersibles. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Read Carefully!)** > 1. **Do not forget the factor of 1000** when converting water power (W) to kW. A frequent mistake is reporting 58,860 W instead of 58.86 kW. > 2. **Unit consistency** in $N_s$: $H$ must be in **metres per stage** (NOT total head), and $P$ must be in **kW**. The most common error: using $H_{total}$ instead of $H_{stage}$, which gives a $N_s$ off by orders of magnitude. > 3. **Velocity triangle signs**: $V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{f2}\cot\beta_2$ — the minus sign applies for backward-curved vanes ($\beta_2 < 90°$). For forward-curved vanes, the sign flips. Submersible pumps always use **backward-curved** vanes for stable operation. > 4. **Number of stages rounding**: Always round **up** to the next integer. A pump designed for 2.22 stages must be built as 3; otherwise it cannot deliver the required head. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - [x] A **submersible pump** is a **multi-stage, hermetically sealed centrifugal pump-motor assembly** that operates **fully submerged** in the pumped fluid. - [x] **No priming required**, **no cavitation**, **no suction lift limit** — these are the three flagship advantages. - [x] Multi-stage architecture is necessary because **head per stage** is limited by **blade tip speed**, material strength, and cavitation margins. - [x] The motor is **squirrel-cage induction**, **water-filled or oil-filled**, **3-phase**, **2-pole** (≈ 2900 rpm at 50 Hz). - [x] Each impeller adds Euler head $H_e = V_{w2}u_2/g$ (for radial entry). With manometric efficiency: $H_m = \eta_{man} \cdot H_e$. - [x] Total head $H_{total} = n \times H_{m,stage}$, where $n$ = number of stages. - [x] Water Power = $\rho g Q H$ in watts; **Shaft Power** = Water Power / $\eta_{overall}$. - [x] **Affinity laws**: $H \propto N^2$, $Q \propto N$, $P \propto N^3$ — vital for variable-speed operation. - [x] **Specific speed** $N_s = N\sqrt{P}/H^{5/4}$ — for submersibles, $N_s$ is **low (≈ 25–60 metric)** indicating radial-flow design. - [x] Construction includes **strainer → multi-stage impellers + diffusers → thrust bearing → sealed motor → check valve → discharge head**. - [x] **Applications**: borewell water supply (Kerala agriculture), mine dewatering, sewage pumping, industrial water circulation, fountains. - [x] **Key design checks** before KTU exam: (i) round up $n$ to integer, (ii) verify $\beta_2 < 90°$ (backward-curved), (iii) ensure NPSH margin is positive (automatic for fully submerged pumps). ---

topics.title: Fire Fighting Pumps and Micro Pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET302 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fire Fighting Pumps and Micro Pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fire Fighting Pumps

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
A *fire fighting pump* is a specially engineered, high-reliability centrifugal (or positive displacement) pump assembly, complete with its driver, controller, and auxiliary piping, designed exclusively to deliver water at the required flow rate and pressure to a building's sprinkler system, hydrant network, or standpipe system under emergency fire-suppression conditions. These pumps are governed by the **NFPA 20** (National Fire Protection Association Standard for the Installation of Stationary Pumps for Fire Protection) and **IS 15105** standards, and are characterized by their ability to operate continuously for extended durations (typically **2 to 4 hours**) without failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** Fire fighting pumps are *not* ordinary centrifugal pumps. They are required to deliver **150% of rated flow at not less than 65% of rated head**, and the shut-off head must not exceed **140% of the rated head** to prevent pipe network rupture. This is a frequent 3-mark and 14-mark question in KTU ESE.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a fire fighting pump as the "heart" of a building's emergency water supply system — just as a human heart keeps blood (life) flowing even under extreme stress, a fire fighting pump keeps water flowing to sprinklers even when grid power fails, even at peak demand, and even during prolonged use. To make the analogy even sharper:
- The **main electric pump** is like the primary, healthy heart.
- The **diesel-driven standby pump** is the *backup* — a defibrillator that kicks in when the main heart fails (power outage).
- The **jockey pump** is the small "trickle pump" that keeps the arteries pressurized at all times so the main heart does not have to start every time a tap opens.

### 1.2 Micro Pumps

**Formal Definition:**
A *micro pump* (or *micropump*) is a fluid-handling device with characteristic dimensions typically **below 10 mm** (often in the range of **10–1000 µm**), designed to deliver very small flow rates (typically **µL/min to mL/min** range) at modest pressure heads. Micro pumps form the fluidic backbone of **MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems)**, lab-on-chip devices, drug-delivery implants, and micro-scale cooling systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core KTU Point:** At micro-scale, **Reynolds number (Re) is very low (Re < 1 to Re ~ 100)**, so flow is overwhelmingly **laminar and dominated by viscous forces**, not inertia. This fundamentally changes pump design philosophy compared to macroscopic pumps.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
A micro pump is like a **tiny programmable syringe driver** — imagine the precision of a wristwatch combined with the ability to push microliters of fluid. Whereas a centrifugal fire pump moves *tons* of water per minute using a spinning impeller, a micro pump might move just a few *drops* per hour using a vibrating diaphragm the size of a rice grain. They are the "precision pharmacists" of the fluid world.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Notes |
|-----------|---------------|-------|
| Water density (ρ) | **1000 kg/m³** | At standard temperature (4°C) |
| Gravitational acceleration (g) | **9.81 m/s²** | For head-to-pressure conversion |
| Atmospheric pressure | **101.325 kPa** | Used for NPSH calculations |
| Vapor pressure of water (at 20°C) | **2.34 kPa** | Critical for NPSH_A |
| Specific weight of water (γ) | **9810 N/m³** | γ = ρg |
| Fire pump rated test duration | **≥ 2 hours** | Continuous, per NFPA 20 |
| Micro pump typical flow | **1 µL/min – 1 mL/min** | Order-of-magnitude |
| Micro pump Reynolds number | **Re < 100** | Laminar regime |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fire Fighting Pump — Operational Logic

A fire fighting installation consists of three distinct pumps working in concert:

**A. Jockey Pump (Pressure Maintenance Pump)**
- Small capacity (typically 3–5% of main pump flow)
- Keeps the system pressurized above the main pump's cut-in pressure
- Prevents the main pump from cycling on/off due to minor leaks
- Electrically driven; runs frequently during normal (non-fire) conditions

**B. Main Fire Pump (Electric Motor Driven)**
- Sized for the full fire-protection demand (sprinklers + hydrants)
- Starts automatically when system pressure drops below the cut-in setting
- Driven by a high-performance electric motor (typically TEFC — Totally Enclosed Fan Cooled)
- Sized for the building's hydraulic demand as per **IS 15105 / NFPA 20**

**C. Standby Fire Pump (Diesel Engine Driven)**
- Required by code because grid power may fail during a fire
- Starts within **10–30 seconds** of a start signal (battery-powered)
- Has its own dedicated fuel supply for **≥ 4 hours** of full-load operation
- Takes over automatically if the electric main pump fails to start

**Why three pumps?** — The logic is a *redundant, hierarchical response*:
1. Normal condition → jockey pump maintains pressure (no main pump running).
2. Minor leak → jockey pump keeps up; main pump stays off.
3. Fire event (large demand) → jockey pump cannot keep up → pressure drops → main electric pump starts.
4. Power failure → main electric pump fails → diesel standby pump starts within 30 s.

### 2.2 Performance Requirements of a Fire Pump (NFPA 20 Curves)

A centrifugal fire pump's head-flow (H–Q) curve has a specific shape mandated by code. Three critical operating points define the acceptability:

- **Rated Point:** $(Q_r, H_r)$ — the design operating point.
- **150% Flow Point:** At $Q = 1.5 Q_r$, the developed head must satisfy $H \geq 0.65 H_r$.
- **Shut-off Point:** At $Q = 0$, the head must satisfy $H \leq 1.40 H_r$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** A *steep* H–Q curve (non-overloading) is preferred for fire pumps because it limits the shut-off head, protecting the discharge piping from overpressure damage. This is the opposite of a *flat* curve preferred for process pumps.

### 2.3 Micro Pumps — Classification by Actuation Principle

Micro pumps are broadly classified into two families:

**A. Mechanical Micro Pumps** (use a moving solid surface)
- Piezoelectric diaphragm micropumps
- Electromagnetic micropumps
- Thermopneumatic micropumps
- Peristaltic micropumps
- Centrifugal MEMS pumps (rare)

**B. Non-Mechanical (Dynamic) Micro Pumps** (no moving solid parts)
- Electroosmotic pumps (EOF)
- Electromagnetic (Lorentz-force) pumps
- Electrohydrodynamic (EHD) pumps
- Capillary-driven pumps (passive)

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

#### Fire Fighting Pump Formulas

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta}$ | Hydraulic power input to pump | W (watts) |
| 2 | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{overall}}$ | Brake power (shaft power) | W |
| 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{P_{shaft}}$ | Pump efficiency (overall) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | Specific speed (metric) | — |
| 5 | $NPSH_A = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_f - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g}$ | Net Positive Suction Head available | m |
| 6 | $NPSH_R = f(Q)$ (from manufacturer) | Net Positive Suction Head required | m |
| 7 | $H \geq 0.65 H_r \text{ at } Q = 1.5 Q_r$ | NFPA 20 condition at 150% flow | m |
| 8 | $H \leq 1.40 H_r \text{ at } Q = 0$ | NFPA 20 shut-off head limit | m |
| 9 | $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}; \dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$ | Affinity laws for pump scaling | — |
| 10 | $V_{elbow} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \times 30 \text{ s}$ | Minimum pipe volume for diesel pump priming | m³ |

#### Micro Pump Formulas

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 11 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D_h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number in microchannel | — |
| 12 | $D_h = \dfrac{4 A}{P_{wetted}}$ | Hydraulic diameter | m |
| 13 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille (circular tube) | Pa |
| 14 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D^4 \Delta P}{128 \mu L}$ | Volumetric flow rate (circular) | m³/s |
| 15 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \mu L Q}{w h^3}$ | Rectangular channel (h ≪ w approx.) | Pa |
| 16 | $\delta = d_{31} \cdot \dfrac{V \cdot L}{t}$ | Piezoelectric diaphragm deflection | m |
| 17 | $Q_p = \pi r^2 \cdot \delta \cdot f$ | Peristaltic/piezo pump theoretical flow | m³/s |
| 18 | $V_{EOF} = \mu_{EO} \cdot E$ | Electroosmotic velocity | m/s |
| 19 | $\mu_{EO} = \dfrac{\epsilon \zeta}{\eta_f}$ | Electroosmotic mobility | m²/(V·s) |
| 20 | $\tau_{wall} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot D_h}{4 L}$ | Wall shear stress in microchannel | Pa |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

**Fire Fighting Pumps:**
- **High-rise buildings**: Required by National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 4) for any building above 15 m.
- **Industrial plants**: Oil refineries, chemical plants, power stations (where water is the primary fire suppressant).
- **Airports & metro stations**: Where water mains may be unreliable.
- **Tunnels & underground structures**: Critical life-safety infrastructure.

**Micro Pumps:**
- **Medical**: Implantable insulin pumps, drug-delivery patches, intraocular pressure control.
- **Lab-on-chip**: PCR analysis, point-of-care diagnostics, DNA sequencing.
- **Cooling**: Microchannel coolers for CPUs, lasers, and high-power electronics.
- **Aerospace**: Micro-propulsion systems, satellite micro-thrusters.
- **Analytical chemistry**: Micro-HPLC, micro-reactors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Net Positive Suction Head Available (NPSH_A)

The NPSH available is the total head available at the pump suction flange above the vapor pressure of the liquid, accounting for atmospheric pressure, suction lift (or head), friction losses, and vapor pressure.

**Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's equation between the source reservoir (point 0) and the pump suction flange (point s):**

$$
\dfrac{P_0}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_0^2}{2g} + z_0 = \dfrac{P_s}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_s^2}{2g} + z_s + h_{f,0 \to s}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify with reservoir assumptions:**

The reservoir is large, so $V_0 \approx 0$. Also, $P_0 = P_{atm}$ (open reservoir) or $P_0 = P_{tank}$ (pressurized tank). The elevation difference $z_0 - z_s = \pm h_s$, where $h_s$ is positive for *suction lift* (pump above source) and negative for *suction head* (pump below source, flooded suction).

$$
\dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + z_0 = \dfrac{P_s}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_s^2}{2g} + z_s + h_f
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the pressure head at the suction flange:**

$$
\dfrac{P_s}{\rho g} = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - h_f \pm h_s - \dfrac{V_s^2}{2g}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to absolute pressure head above vapor pressure (this defines NPSH_A):**

$$
NPSH_A = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_f - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} - \dfrac{V_s^2}{2g}
$$

**Step 5 — Velocity head simplification:**

In most KTU textbook problems, the velocity head at the suction flange is either included in $h_f$ or neglected if the suction pipe is large. The standard KTU form used in exam problems is:

$$
\boxed{\,NPSH_A = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_f - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g}\,}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Each term is in *meters of head*. We subtract $P_v/\rho g$ because cavitation begins when local pressure drops to the vapor pressure; the margin above that is what NPSH measures.

### 3.2 Derivation: Hagen–Poiseuille Flow in a Microchannel (Circular Cross-Section)

This is the foundational equation for micro pump and microchannel analysis.

**Step 1 — Geometry & assumptions:**
- Steady, laminar, incompressible, fully-developed flow.
- Newtonian fluid (viscosity μ constant).
- Circular tube of diameter $D$ and length $L$.
- Pressure drop $\Delta P$ drives the flow.

**Step 2 — Apply the Navier–Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates:**

For fully-developed flow in the $z$-direction, $V_z = V_z(r)$ only, and the equation reduces to:

$$
\dfrac{1}{r} \dfrac{d}{dr} \left( r \dfrac{dV_z}{dr} \right) = \dfrac{1}{\mu} \dfrac{dP}{dz}
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate once with respect to r:**

$$
r \dfrac{dV_z}{dr} = \dfrac{1}{2\mu} \dfrac{dP}{dz} r^2 + C_1
$$

$$
\dfrac{dV_z}{dr} = \dfrac{1}{2\mu} \dfrac{dP}{dz} r + \dfrac{C_1}{r}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary condition at centerline ($r = 0$, $V_z$ is finite):**

The term $C_1/r$ would blow up at $r = 0$ unless $C_1 = 0$.

$$
\dfrac{dV_z}{dr} = \dfrac{1}{2\mu} \dfrac{dP}{dz} r
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate a second time:**

$$
V_z(r) = \dfrac{1}{4\mu} \dfrac{dP}{dz} r^2 + C_2
$$

**Step 6 — Apply no-slip condition at the wall ($r = R$, $V_z = 0$):**

$$
0 = \dfrac{1}{4\mu} \dfrac{dP}{dz} R^2 + C_2 \;\Rightarrow\; C_2 = -\dfrac{1}{4\mu} \dfrac{dP}{dz} R^2
$$

**Step 7 — Final velocity profile (parabolic):**

$$
V_z(r) = -\dfrac{1}{4\mu} \dfrac{dP}{dz} \left( R^2 - r^2 \right)
$$

**Step 8 — Compute volumetric flow rate by integrating over the cross-section:**

$$
Q = \int_0^R V_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = -\dfrac{\pi}{2\mu} \dfrac{dP}{dz} \int_0^R (R^2 - r^2) r \, dr
$$

$$
\int_0^R (R^2 r - r^3) dr = \left[ \dfrac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \dfrac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R = \dfrac{R^4}{2} - \dfrac{R^4}{4} = \dfrac{R^4}{4}
$$

**Step 9 — Substitute and use $dP/dz = -\Delta P / L$ (pressure drop over length L):**

$$
Q = -\dfrac{\pi}{2\mu} \cdot \left( -\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} \right) \cdot \dfrac{R^4}{4} = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}
$$

**Step 10 — Convert $R = D/2$ to get the final form:**

$$
Q = \dfrac{\pi D^4 \Delta P}{128 \mu L}
$$

> **Conversion Logic / Engineering Insight:** The fourth-power dependence on diameter is the *single most important fact* about microchannels — halving the channel diameter reduces flow by a factor of 16 for the same pressure drop. This is why micro pumps use *many parallel channels* instead of one large one.

### 3.3 Derivation: Piezoelectric Diaphragm Deflection

**Step 1 — Piezoelectric constitutive relation:**
A piezoelectric material generates strain $S$ proportional to applied electric field $E$:

$$
S = d_{31} \cdot E
$$

where $d_{31}$ is the *piezoelectric strain coefficient* (m/V).

**Step 2 — Convert field to voltage:**
For a diaphragm of thickness $t$ and applied voltage $V$:

$$
E = \dfrac{V}{t}
$$

**Step 3 — Strain relates to deflection:**
If the diaphragm is clamped at the edge and has length $L$ (e.g., a cantilever or simply-supported circular plate), the tip/center deflection $\delta$ is:

$$
\delta = S \cdot L = d_{31} \cdot \dfrac{V}{t} \cdot L
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\delta = d_{31} \cdot \dfrac{V \cdot L}{t}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Resulting pumped flow per cycle:**
For a circular diaphragm of radius $r$ deflecting by $\delta$, the displaced volume per stroke is the volume of a spherical cap (approx.):

$$
V_{stroke} \approx \pi r^2 \cdot \delta
$$

If the diaphragm oscillates at frequency $f$ (Hz), the theoretical flow rate is:

$$
Q_{pump} = \pi r^2 \cdot \delta \cdot f = \pi r^2 \cdot d_{31} \cdot \dfrac{V L}{t} \cdot f
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Increasing $f$ linearly increases flow but introduces resonance limits; typical piezoelectric micropumps operate at **10 Hz to 1 kHz**, balancing flow rate against diaphragm fatigue and backflow through the check valves.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> **Problem (Model KTU Part B Question, 14 Marks):**
> A fire pump delivers **1500 L/min** at a rated head of **60 m**. The pump is driven by a **30 kW** electric motor. Calculate: (a) the overall efficiency of the pump, and (b) check whether the pump satisfies the NFPA 20 condition at 150% flow (assume a parabolic H–Q curve, and shut-off head is 1.30 times the rated head).

**Solution (Part a):**

Step 1 — Convert flow rate:

$$
Q = 1500 \, \dfrac{L}{min} = 1500 \times \dfrac{10^{-3}}{60} \, m^3/s = 0.025 \, m^3/s
$$

Step 2 — Water power (output):

$$
P_{water} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.025 \times 60 = 14{,}715 \, W \approx 14.72 \, kW
$$

Step 3 — Overall efficiency:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P_{water}}{P_{input}} = \dfrac{14.72}{30} = 0.491 \approx 49.1\%
$$

**Solution (Part b):**

Step 1 — Identify 150% flow condition:

$$
Q_{1.5} = 1.5 \times 1500 = 2250 \, L/min
$$

Step 2 — For a *parabolic* H–Q curve, the shut-off head is $H_0$, the rated head is $H_r$ at $Q_r$, and the relation is:

$$
H = H_0 \left[ 1 - \left( \dfrac{Q}{Q_{max}} \right)^2 \right]
$$

Given $H_0 = 1.30 H_r = 1.30 \times 60 = 78 \, m$ and at $Q_r$, $H_r = 60 \, m$:

$$
60 = 78 \left[ 1 - \left( \dfrac{1500}{Q_{max}} \right)^2 \right]
$$

$$
1 - \dfrac{2.25 \times 10^6}{Q_{max}^2} = \dfrac{60}{78} = 0.7692
$$

$$
\dfrac{2.25 \times 10^6}{Q_{max}^2} = 0.2308
$$

$$
Q_{max}^2 = \dfrac{2.25 \times 10^6}{0.2308} = 9.75 \times 10^6
$$

$$
Q_{max} = 3122 \, L/min
$$

Step 3 — Compute head at $Q = 2250 \, L/min$:

$$
H_{1.5} = 78 \left[ 1 - \left( \dfrac{2250}{3122} \right)^2 \right] = 78 \left[ 1 - (0.7207)^2 \right]
$$

$$
H_{1.5} = 78 \times (1 - 0.5194) = 78 \times 0.4806 = 37.5 \, m
$$

Step 4 — Check NFPA condition:

$$
H_{1.5} = 37.5 \, m \quad \text{vs.} \quad 0.65 \times H_r = 0.65 \times 60 = 39 \, m
$$

**Result:** $37.5 < 39$ → **Pump FAILS the NFPA 20 condition at 150% flow.** A larger impeller or higher specific speed is required.

> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** This type of "check NFPA 20 condition" question is extremely common as a 7-mark sub-part. Students must remember to **convert units carefully** (L/min → m³/s) and to **interpret the result correctly** ("fails" vs. "passes").

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fire Fighting Pump System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Water Source: Tank, Reservoir, City Main]
    B[Jockey Pump: 3-5 percent capacity, Electric]
    C[Main Fire Pump: Electric Motor Driven, 100 percent capacity]
    D[Standby Fire Pump: Diesel Engine Driven, 100 percent capacity]
    E[Fire Pump Controller: Automatic, NFPA 20 compliant]
    F[Fire Protection System: Sprinklers, Hydrants, Standpipes]
    G[Pressure Switch / Pressure Sensor]
    H[Diesel Fuel Tank: 4 hour minimum capacity]
    I[Building Power Supply: Grid electricity]
    J[Diesel Battery Bank: 30 second cranking]

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    B --> F
    C --> F
    D --> F
    I --> C
    I --> B
    H --> D
    J --> D
    E --> B
    E --> C
    E --> D
    G --> E
    F --> G

    classDef source fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef pump fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#a94442,color:#000
    classDef aux fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41,color:#000

    class A source
    class B,C,D pump
    class E control
    class F load
    class G,H,I,J aux
```

**Figure 1 — Functional Architecture of a Fire Fighting Pump Installation.**
The diagram shows the three-pump hierarchy (jockey, main electric, standby diesel), the pressure-sensing feedback loop, and the auxiliary supplies (grid power, diesel fuel, cranking battery).

### 4.2 Fire Pump H–Q Characteristic Curve vs. NFPA 20 Limits

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Rated Point: Qr, Hr"]
    P2["150 percent Flow Point: 1.5 Qr, must be greater than or equal to 0.65 Hr"]
    P3["Shut-off Point: Q=0, must be less than or equal to 1.40 Hr"]
    P4["Unstable Region: Below 0.65 Hr line, NOT acceptable"]
    P5["Overpressure Region: Above 1.40 Hr line, NOT acceptable"]

    P1 --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P5

    classDef ok fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000
    classDef bad fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#a94442,color:#000
    classDef neutral fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000

    class P1 ok
    class P2 ok
    class P3 ok
    class P4 bad
    class P5 bad
```

**Figure 2 — NFPA 20 Acceptable Operating Envelope on the H–Q Curve.**
The pump must pass through the "OK" zone — delivering rated head at rated flow, staying above the 0.65 Hr line up to 150% flow, and not exceeding 1.40 Hr at shut-off.

### 4.3 Micro Pump Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Micro Pumps]
    A --> B[Mechanical Micro Pumps: moving solid surface]
    A --> C[Non Mechanical Micro Pumps: no moving solid]

    B --> B1[Piezoelectric Diaphragm]
    B --> B2[Electromagnetic]
    B --> B3[Thermopneumatic]
    B --> B4[Peristaltic]
    B --> B5[Static Surface MEMS]

    C --> C1[Electroosmotic EOF]
    C --> C2[Electromagnetic Lorentz]
    C --> C3[Electrohydrodynamic EHD]
    C --> C4[Capillary Driven Passive]

    B1 --> D1[Used in: Drug delivery, Lab on chip]
    C1 --> D2[Used in: High pressure, no pulsation]

    classDef mech fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef nmech fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef app fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000

    class A,B,B1,B2,B3,B4,B5 mech
    class C,C1,C2,C3,C4 nmech
    class D1,D2 app
```

**Figure 3 — Classification of Micro Pumps by Actuation Principle.**
Mechanical pumps dominate low-pressure biomedical applications; non-mechanical (dynamic) pumps excel where pulsation-free flow or high pressure is needed.

### 4.4 Piezoelectric Micro Pump — Working Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Piezoelectric Disc: 5 to 20 mm diameter]
    B[Diaphragm: Silicon or Polymer, 10 to 50 micrometer thick]
    C[Pump Chamber: Volume 1 to 100 microliter]
    D[Inlet Check Valve: Forward direction only]
    E[Outlet Check Valve: Forward direction only]
    F[Inlet Reservoir]
    G[Outlet: To Microfluidic Network]

    F --> D
    D --> C
    C --> E
    E --> G
    A --> B
    B --> C

    H[AC Drive Signal: 10 Hz to 1 kHz, 50 to 200 V] --> A

    classDef actuator fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef chamber fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef valve fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000
    classDef flow fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#a94442,color:#000

    class A,B actuator
    class C chamber
    class D,E valve
    class F,G,H flow
```

**Figure 4 — Cross-Functional Schematic of a Piezoelectric Diaphragm Micro Pump.**
The piezoelectric disc vibrates the diaphragm at high frequency; check valves rectify the oscillating flow into a net DC output.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
Define a *fire fighting pump* and state the **NFPA 20** performance criteria it must satisfy at (i) 150% rated flow and (ii) shut-off condition.

**Model Answer:**

A fire fighting pump is a dedicated, high-reliability centrifugal pump assembly — complete with driver, controller, and suction/discharge piping — used to supply water to fire protection systems (sprinklers, hydrants, standpipes) under emergency conditions. It must comply with **NFPA 20** (Standard for the Installation of Stationary Pumps for Fire Protection) and must satisfy the following two performance criteria:

- **(i) At 150% rated flow:** The pump must develop a head **greater than or equal to 65%** of its rated head, i.e., $H_{1.5} \geq 0.65 H_r$.
- **(ii) At shut-off (Q = 0):** The developed head must **not exceed 140%** of the rated head, i.e., $H_0 \leq 1.40 H_r$, to prevent overpressure damage to the discharge network.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1) + NFPA 20 (1) + two conditions (1).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
List any **four types of micro pumps** based on actuation principle and state one typical application of each.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Micro Pump Type | Actuation Principle | Typical Application |
|---|-----------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Piezoelectric diaphragm | Vibration of piezo-ceramic disc | Implantable insulin delivery |
| 2 | Electroosmotic pump (EOF) | Tangential electric field on charged walls | Lab-on-chip high-pressure generation |
| 3 | Thermopneumatic pump | Heat-induced gas expansion/contraction | Disposable diagnostic cartridges |
| 4 | Peristaltic micro pump | Sequential compression of flexible tube | Continuous drug infusion |

**Marks Distribution:** 4 types × 0.5 = 2 marks + 4 applications × 0.25 = 1 mark.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU ESE Model – Module 4, 14 Marks]**
**Question A (14 Marks):**

(a) **[7 Marks]** A centrifugal fire fighting pump has a rated capacity of **1800 L/min** at a head of **55 m**. The pump is driven by an electric motor rated **22 kW** with a mechanical efficiency of 95%. Calculate the overall efficiency of the pump-set. If the same pump is now re-rated to run at 20% higher speed (using affinity laws), determine the new flow rate, head, and power input (assuming the efficiency remains unchanged).

(b) **[7 Marks]** Explain the role and working of a **jockey pump** in a fire fighting installation. Why is a *diesel-driven* standby pump mandatory in addition to the main electric pump? List the **NFPA 20 requirements** the standby pump must satisfy.

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Convert flow rate to SI units:**

$$
Q = 1800 \, L/min = 1800 \times \dfrac{10^{-3}}{60} = 0.030 \, m^3/s
$$

**Step 2 — Compute water power (output):**

$$
P_{water} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.030 \times 55 = 16{,}186.5 \, W \approx 16.19 \, kW
$$

**Step 3 — Compute shaft power input to pump:**

The motor output power is the pump input power (shaft power). However, the problem gives motor rated power = 22 kW with mechanical efficiency 95%, so the shaft power delivered to the pump:

$$
P_{shaft} = \eta_{mech} \times P_{motor} = 0.95 \times 22 = 20.9 \, kW
$$

**Step 4 — Overall efficiency of pump set:**

$$
\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{P_{water}}{P_{motor}} = \dfrac{16.19}{22} = 0.736 \approx 73.6\%
$$

**Step 5 — Apply affinity laws at 20% higher speed:**

New speed $N_2 = 1.20 \, N_1$. Affinity laws state:

$$
\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = 1.20
$$

$$
\dfrac{H_2}{H_1} = \left( \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \right)^2 = (1.20)^2 = 1.44
$$

$$
\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left( \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \right)^3 = (1.20)^3 = 1.728
$$

**Step 6 — Compute new values:**

$$
Q_2 = 1.20 \times 1800 = 2160 \, L/min
$$

$$
H_2 = 1.44 \times 55 = 79.2 \, m
$$

$$
P_2 = 1.728 \times 16.19 = 27.98 \, kW
$$

**Final Answer (a):** $\eta_{overall} = 73.6\%$; $Q_2 = 2160 \, L/min$, $H_2 = 79.2 \, m$, $P_2 = 27.98 \, kW$.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Jockey Pump:**
A jockey pump is a small, electrically-driven centrifugal pump (typically **3% to 5%** of the main fire pump capacity) installed in parallel with the main fire pump. Its role is to **maintain the system pressure** slightly above the main pump's cut-in pressure during normal (non-fire) conditions. By doing so, it compensates for minor leaks and prevents the main pump from cycling on/off, which would otherwise cause wear, motor heating, and unnecessary power consumption.

**Why a Diesel Standby Pump is Mandatory:**
A diesel-driven standby pump is mandatory because, in the event of a fire, the **grid electrical supply may fail** — either due to the fire damaging cables, intentional power cut-off for safety, or the building's electrical system being compromised. The main electric pump will then be unable to start or run. The diesel standby pump is **independent of grid power**, equipped with its own **battery bank** (capable of at least 6 successive cranking attempts of 15 seconds each) and dedicated **fuel tank** (rated for **at least 4 hours** of full-load operation). It must start automatically within **30 seconds** of receiving the start signal.

**NFPA 20 Requirements for the Standby Pump:**
1. Independent dedicated fuel supply for ≥ 4 hours of full-load operation.
2. Automatic starting on pressure drop (when main electric pump fails to start).
3. Battery banks sized for **6 cranking attempts of 15 seconds each**, plus 30 minutes of cooling-down operation.
4. Same head–flow performance as the main electric pump.
5. Located in a separate fire-rated room/area to ensure survival.
6. Weekly automatic test run for **30 minutes** (logging the run).

---

**Question B (Alternative 14 Marks):**

(a) **[7 Marks]** A microchannel of circular cross-section with diameter **D = 200 µm** and length **L = 50 mm** carries water at a flow rate of **Q = 1 µL/min**. Calculate: (i) the average velocity, (ii) the Reynolds number (µ = 1 mPa·s, ρ = 1000 kg/m³), (iii) the pressure drop required, and (iv) the wall shear stress. Comment on the flow regime.

(b) **[7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **working principle of a piezoelectric micro pump**. Derive an expression for the volumetric flow rate in terms of the diaphragm radius, peak deflection, and drive frequency. State **two advantages** and **two limitations** of piezoelectric micro pumps.

**Model Solution to (a) — Alternative:**

**Step 1 — Convert units:**

$$
D = 200 \times 10^{-6} \, m, \quad L = 50 \times 10^{-3} \, m, \quad Q = 1 \times 10^{-9} \, m^3/60 \, s = 1.667 \times 10^{-11} \, m^3/s
$$

**Step 2 — Average velocity:**

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (200 \times 10^{-6})^2}{4} = 3.142 \times 10^{-8} \, m^2
$$

$$
V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{1.667 \times 10^{-11}}{3.142 \times 10^{-8}} = 5.305 \times 10^{-4} \, m/s \approx 0.53 \, mm/s
$$

**Step 3 — Reynolds number:**

$$
Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{1000 \times 5.305 \times 10^{-4} \times 200 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.106
$$

**Step 4 — Pressure drop (Hagen–Poiseuille):**

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4} = \dfrac{128 \times 10^{-3} \times 50 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.667 \times 10^{-11}}{\pi (200 \times 10^{-6})^4}
$$

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{1.067 \times 10^{-13}}{\pi \times 1.6 \times 10^{-18}} = \dfrac{1.067 \times 10^{-13}}{5.027 \times 10^{-18}} = 21{,}225 \, Pa \approx 21.2 \, kPa
$$

**Step 5 — Wall shear stress:**

$$
\tau_{wall} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4 L} = \dfrac{21{,}225 \times 200 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 50 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{4.245}{0.2} = 21.2 \, Pa
$$

**Step 6 — Flow regime comment:**
$Re = 0.106 \ll 2300$ → flow is **deeply laminar** and **viscosity-dominated**. Inertia is negligible; viscous forces govern the entire flow field. This validates the use of Hagen–Poiseuille equation.

**Marks Distribution (a):** 4 sub-parts (1.5 each) + comment (1).

**Model Solution to (b) — Alternative:**

Refer to **Figure 4** (Section 4.4) for the schematic. The working principle is described in the schematic: an AC voltage applied to the piezoelectric disc causes it to vibrate, which deflects the bonded diaphragm. The diaphragm deflection alternately increases and decreases the pump chamber volume, creating suction (intake through the inlet check valve) and discharge (expulsion through the outlet check valve). The two check valves rectify the oscillating flow into a unidirectional net output.

**Derivation of flow rate:**

Step 1 — Peak diaphragm deflection (assuming center of a clamped circular plate of radius $r$):

$$
\delta = d_{31} \cdot \dfrac{V_{pp} \cdot L}{t}
$$

Step 2 — Volume displaced per stroke (spherical cap, small deflection approximation):

$$
V_{stroke} \approx \pi r^2 \cdot \delta
$$

Step 3 — At drive frequency $f$ (Hz), the theoretical (no-leakage) flow rate is:

$$
Q_{pump} = \pi r^2 \cdot \delta \cdot f = \pi r^2 \cdot d_{31} \cdot \dfrac{V_{pp} \cdot L \cdot f}{t}
$$

**Two advantages:**
1. **No moving mechanical parts** (apart from the diaphragm and valves) → high reliability, long life, low wear.
2. **Fast dynamic response** (millisecond-scale actuation) → precise flow control, suitable for pulsatile biomedical delivery.

**Two limitations:**
1. **High drive voltage required** (typically 50–200 V) → requires bulky driver electronics, limiting miniaturization.
2. **Limited flow rate** (typically µL/min range) → not suitable for high-throughput applications.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** Students frequently forget to convert L/min to m³/s in pump power calculations. **1 mark lost** in nearly every KTU paper if the conversion is skipped.
> 2. **NFPA 20 mix-up:** Several students write "$H \geq 1.65 H_r$" instead of "$H \geq 0.65 H_r$ at 150% flow." This is a **direct 2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Sign error in NPSH:** Suction lift must be **subtracted** (positive when pump is above source), and suction head (flooded suction) must be **added** — students routinely invert this sign.
> 4. **Hagen–Poiseuille formula:** The constant in the denominator is **128** (not 32, not 8π). Using the wrong constant gives wrong pressure drop.
> 5. **Piezoelectric equation:** Students sometimes write $d_{33}$ instead of $d_{31}$. For a diaphragm in bending mode, the relevant coefficient is $d_{31}$ (transverse), not $d_{33}$ (longitudinal).
> 6. **Micro pump flow rate order-of-magnitude:** Stating mL/min or L/min is **wrong** — micro pumps are characterized by **µL/min**, not mL/min.

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU prep):**

- [ ] A **fire fighting pump** is a dedicated, code-compliant pump (NFPA 20 / IS 15105) used to feed sprinklers and hydrants.
- [ ] **Three pumps in a fire installation:** Jockey (small, pressure maintenance) + Main (electric, full capacity) + Standby (diesel, full capacity).
- [ ] **NFPA 20 Critical Criteria:** $H(1.5Q_r) \geq 0.65 H_r$ and $H(0) \leq 1.40 H_r$.
- [ ] Jockey pump capacity = **3% to 5%** of main pump.
- [ ] Standby diesel pump must have **≥ 4 hours** of fuel and **start within 30 seconds**.
- [ ] Pump power formula: $P = \rho g Q H / \eta$ (water power); $P_{shaft} = P / \eta_{overall}$.
- [ ] Affinity laws: $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$.
- [ ] NPSH available: $NPSH_A = P_{atm}/\rho g \pm h_s - h_f - P_v/\rho g$.
- [ ] **Micro pumps** handle **µL/min to mL/min** flow at **low Re** (laminar, viscous-dominated).
- [ ] Micro pumps are classified into **mechanical** (piezo, peristaltic, electromagnetic, thermopneumatic) and **non-mechanical** (electroosmotic, EHD, capillary).
- [ ] **Hagen–Poiseuille** (circular tube): $Q = \pi D^4 \Delta P / (128 \mu L)$ — fourth-power dependence is critical.
- [ ] **Piezoelectric deflection:** $\delta = d_{31} V L / t$.
- [ ] **Piezo pump flow rate:** $Q = \pi r^2 \delta f$.
- [ ] Electroosmotic velocity: $V_{EOF} = \mu_{EO} \cdot E$, with $\mu_{EO} = \epsilon \zeta / \eta_f$.
- [ ] In microchannels, friction factor follows $f = 64 / Re$ (Poiseuille flow).
- [ ] At micro-scale, **surface tension, electrokinetics, and diffusion** dominate — inertia becomes negligible.
- [ ] **Key applications:** Fire pumps → high-rise buildings, refineries, tunnels; Micro pumps → insulin delivery, lab-on-chip, micro-cooling.
